# Now copy the entire anihpj project
COPY . .

# ── Stage 7: Collect static files ───────────────────────────
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# ── Stage 8: Expose port ────────────────────────────────────
# Document which port the app listens on (doesn't publish it)
EXPOSE 8000

# ── Stage 9: Run the application ────────────────────────────
# Use gunicorn for production (not manage.py runserver!)
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", \
     "--workers", "3"]
⚠️ Common Mistake

Never use python manage.py runserver in production! It's single-threaded and not designed for real traffic. Use gunicorn (or uvicorn for async) with multiple workers.

Multi-Stage Build (Advanced)

For even smaller images, use a multi-stage build. Build everything in a "builder" stage, then copy only the final artifacts to a clean slim image:

Dockerfile.multistage 
# ── Build stage: install dependencies with build tools ──
FROM python:3.11-slim AS builder
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y gcc libpq-dev
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# ── Production stage: clean, small image ────────────────
FROM python:3.11-slim
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libpq5 \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
WORKDIR /app
COPY . .
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# 🔒 Run as non-root user (security best practice)
RUN adduser --disabled-password --no-create-home appuser
USER appuser

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "3"]
🔒 Docker Security Checklist
  • ✅ Run as non-root — Always add USER appuser (never run as root in production)
  • ✅ No secrets in images — Never put passwords or API keys in ENV or COPY. Use K8s Secrets instead
  • ✅ Pin base image versions — Use python:3.11.7-slim not python:latest
  • ✅ Scan for vulnerabilities — Run docker scout cves anihpj:v1 before deploying
  • ✅ Minimal base image — Use -slim or -alpine to reduce attack surface
💡 Why python:3.11-slim?
  • python:3.11 — Full Debian image (~900 MB) — everything included, but huge
  • python:3.11-slim — Stripped Debian (~120 MB) — great balance ✅
  • python:3.11-alpine — Tiny (~50 MB) — but musl libc can cause issues with some packages

2.5 The .dockerignore File

Just like .gitignore keeps junk out of your repo, .dockerignore keeps junk out of your image. Smaller images = faster builds, faster pulls, less attack surface.

.dockerignore 
# Version control
.git
.gitignore

# Python
__pycache__
*.pyc
*.pyo
*.egg-info
env/
venv/
.venv/

# Database (don't ship SQLite into the image!)
db.sqlite3

# IDE
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp

# Docker
Dockerfile
docker-compose*.yml
.dockerignore

# OS
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Documentation
*.md
docs/

Impact on Image Size

Without .dockerignore
~450 MB (includes .git, env/, __pycache__)
With .dockerignore
~160 MB (only what's needed)

2.6 Docker Commands You Need to Know

Here are the essential Docker commands organized by what you're doing:

🏗️ Build & Tag

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker buildBuild image from Dockerfiledocker build -t anihpj:v1 .
docker tagAdd another tag to an imagedocker tag anihpj:v1 registry/anihpj:v1
docker imagesList local imagesdocker images

🚀 Run & Manage

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker runCreate + start a containerdocker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1
docker run -dRun in background (detached)docker run -d -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1
docker psList running containersdocker ps
docker ps -aList ALL containers (incl. stopped)docker ps -a
docker stopGracefully stop containerdocker stop <id>
docker startRestart a stopped containerdocker start <id>

🔍 Inspect & Debug

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker logsView container outputdocker logs <id>
docker logs -fFollow logs (live tail)docker logs -f <id>
docker exec -itOpen shell inside containerdocker exec -it <id> bash
docker inspectFull JSON detail of containerdocker inspect <id>

🧹 Cleanup

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker rmRemove stopped containerdocker rm <id>
docker rmiRemove imagedocker rmi anihpj:v1
docker system pruneRemove all unused datadocker system prune -a

🗄️ Registry

CommandWhat It DoesExample
docker loginAuthenticate to registrydocker login docker.io
docker pushUpload image to registrydocker push registry/anihpj:v1
docker pullDownload image from registrydocker pull nginx:latest

2.7 Building and Running anihpj Container

Let's put it all together — here's the step-by-step to containerize and run anihpj:

📝
Step 1
Write Dockerfile

Create the Dockerfile in the anihpj/ project root

🏗️
Step 2
Build Image

docker build -t anihpj:v1 .

🚀
Step 3
Run Container

docker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v1

🌐
Step 4
Access App

Visit http://localhost:8000/jobs/

terminal — Build & Run anihpj 
# Step 1: Make sure you're in the project directory
cd anihpj/

# Step 2: Build the Docker image
docker build -t anihpj:v1 .
# ✅ Output: Successfully tagged anihpj:v1

# Step 3: Run the container
docker run -d -p 8000:8000 --name anihpj-app anihpj:v1
# ✅ Output: a1b2c3d4e5f6... (container ID)

# Step 4: Open your browser → http://localhost:8000/jobs/

# Step 5: Check the logs to see Django output
docker logs anihpj-app
# [INFO] Starting gunicorn 21.2.0
# [INFO] Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000 (1)
# [INFO] Using worker: sync
# [INFO] Booting worker with pid: 7, 8, 9

# Step 6: Peek inside the running container
docker exec -it anihpj-app bash
# root@a1b2c3d4:/app# ls
# anihpj/  jobpost/  manage.py  requirements.txt

# Step 7: Stop and remove when done
docker stop anihpj-app
docker rm anihpj-app
💡 Flags Explained
  • -d — Detached mode (runs in background)
  • -p 8000:8000 — Map host port 8000 to container port 8000
  • --name anihpj-app — Give the container a human-friendly name
  • -t anihpj:v1 — Tag the image with name and version
Port Mapping: -p HOST:CONTAINER
   YOUR MACHINE                          DOCKER CONTAINER
  ┌──────────────┐                      ┌──────────────────┐
  │              │                      │  anihpj (Django)  │
  │  Browser     │   -p 8000:8000      │                  │
  │  localhost ──────────────────────▶  │  gunicorn :8000  │
  │  :8000       │   host → container  │                  │
  │              │                      │  0.0.0.0:8000    │
  └──────────────┘                      └──────────────────┘

  You could also map to a different host port:
  -p 80:8000    →  access at localhost:80, container still on 8000
  -p 9090:8000  →  access at localhost:9090

2.8 Docker Compose — Running Multiple Containers

Real apps need more than one container. anihpj needs Django + PostgreSQL + Redis. Docker Compose lets you define and run multi-container setups with a single YAML file:

docker-compose.yml 
version: "3.9"

services:
  # ── Django Application ─────────────────────────
  web:
    build: .
    command: gunicorn anihpj.wsgi:application --bind 0.0.0.0:8000
    ports:
      - "8000:8000"
    environment:
      - DATABASE_URL=postgres://anihpj:secret@db:5432/anihpj_db
      - REDIS_URL=redis://cache:6379/0
    depends_on:
      - db
      - cache

  # ── PostgreSQL Database ────────────────────────
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: anihpj_db
      POSTGRES_USER: anihpj
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    volumes:
      - postgres_data:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  # ── Redis Cache ────────────────────────────────
  cache:
    image: redis:7-alpine

volumes:
  postgres_data:
terminal — Docker Compose Commands 
# Start all services in background
docker compose up -d

# Check status
docker compose ps

# View logs from all services
docker compose logs -f

# Run Django migrations inside the web container
docker compose exec web python manage.py migrate

# Stop everything
docker compose down

# Stop and remove volumes (database data!)
docker compose down -v
🐳 Docker Compose

✅ Simple YAML file
✅ Great for local dev
✅ One command: docker compose up
❌ Single host only
❌ No auto-scaling
❌ No self-healing across nodes

☸️ Kubernetes

✅ Multi-host clusters
✅ Auto-scaling (HPA)
✅ Self-healing across nodes
✅ Rolling updates & rollbacks
⚠️ More complex configuration
⚠️ Steeper learning curve

📌 The Natural Progression

Most projects start with Docker Compose for local development, then graduate to Kubernetes for production. You'll keep using Compose on your laptop — but deploy to K8s when it's time to go live.

2.9 Container Registries

Container registries are where Docker images live. When Kubernetes needs to run anihpj, it pulls the image from a registry. Here are your options:

Registry Provider Free Tier Best For
Docker Hub Docker Inc. 1 private repo Open-source, public images
GitHub GHCR GitHub 500 MB free (private) GitHub Actions CI/CD
Amazon ECR AWS 500 MB / 12 months EKS clusters on AWS
Google GCR / Artifact Registry Google Cloud 500 MB free GKE clusters on GCP
Azure ACR Microsoft Basic tier ($5/mo) AKS clusters on Azure
terminal — Push to Docker Hub 
# 1. Login to Docker Hub
docker login

# 2. Tag with your Docker Hub username
docker tag anihpj:v1 yourusername/anihpj:v1

# 3. Push to Docker Hub
docker push yourusername/anihpj:v1

# 4. Anyone (or Kubernetes) can now pull it:
docker pull yourusername/anihpj:v1
💡 For This Guide

In later chapters, when Kubernetes pulls the anihpj image, it will fetch it from whichever registry you push to. For local development with Minikube, you can even load images directly without a registry: minikube image load anihpj:v1

⏱️ 8 minutes
Scenario: Multi-Stage Build & Image Management

You need to optimize the anihpj Docker image for production. Currently it's 1.2GB. Complete these tasks:

  1. Write a multi-stage Dockerfile that separates the build stage from the runtime stage
  2. Use python:3.11-slim as the final base image
  3. Ensure the container runs as a non-root user
  4. Tag the image appropriately for a private registry
💡 Hint: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM instructions. Only the final stage ends up in the output image.
📝 Click to reveal solution 
# ── Stage 1: Build dependencies ──
FROM python:3.11 AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# ── Stage 2: Runtime (slim) ──
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app

# Create non-root user
RUN groupadd -r django && useradd -r -g django django

# Copy only installed packages from builder
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

# Switch to non-root user
USER django

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
# Tag for private registry
docker build -t registry.example.com/anihpj:v1.0.0 .

# CKAD Key Points:
# - Multi-stage: ~200MB vs 1.2GB (83% reduction)
# - Non-root: Required by Pod Security Standards
# - imagePullPolicy: "IfNotPresent" for tagged images
# - For "latest" tag, K8s defaults to "Always"

On the CKAD, you won't build Docker images but you'll frequently need to specify image details in pod specs. Memorize this pattern:

  • kubectl run nginx --image=nginx:1.25 — Create pod with specific image
  • kubectl set image deployment/web web=nginx:1.26 — Update image in deployment
  • kubectl get pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].image}' — Check current image

Chapter 2 — Practice Questions

Q1
Write the command to create a pod named web using the image nginx:1.25 in the production namespace.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 -n production
Explanation

kubectl run creates a standalone pod (not a deployment). The --image flag specifies the container image with tag. Always include the image tag — using :latest or omitting the tag is bad practice and can cause unexpected behavior with imagePullPolicy.

Q2
What is the difference between a Docker image and a container? How does this relate to Kubernetes pods?
Show Answer
Answer

An image is a read-only template (blueprint). A container is a running instance of that image. A Kubernetes pod wraps one or more containers, providing shared networking and storage.

Explanation

Think: Image = Class, Container = Object instance. You can run multiple containers from the same image. In Kubernetes, pods are the smallest deployable unit — you never deploy a container directly, you deploy a pod that contains containers.

Q3
What does a multi-stage Dockerfile achieve? Write a minimal 2-stage Dockerfile for a Python application.
Show Answer
Answer
# Stage 1: Build
FROM python:3.11 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Stage 2: Production (smaller image)
FROM python:3.11-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages /usr/local/lib/python3.11/site-packages
COPY . .
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Explanation

Multi-stage builds produce smaller production images by separating build dependencies from runtime. The final image only contains what's needed to run. This reduces attack surface and speeds up pod startup. On the CKAD you won't write Dockerfiles, but you need to understand how images work for troubleshooting.

Q4
What are the three possible values for imagePullPolicy in a container spec, and when is each used by default?
Show Answer
Answer

Always — Pull the image every time (default when tag is :latest or omitted).
IfNotPresent — Pull only if not already on the node (default when a specific tag like :1.25 is used).
Never — Never pull; only use local images.

Explanation

This is a common CKAD trap. If you use image: nginx (no tag), K8s defaults to :latest tag with imagePullPolicy: Always. This means every pod restart pulls from the registry. Always specify a tag like nginx:1.25 to get predictable behavior with IfNotPresent.

Q5
How do you check which image a running pod is currently using?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: JSONPath
kubectl get pod web -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].image}'

# Method 2: Describe
kubectl describe pod web | grep Image:

# Method 3: YAML output
kubectl get pod web -o yaml | grep image:
Explanation

The JSONPath method is fastest on the CKAD exam. {.spec.containers[*].image} returns images for ALL containers in the pod (important for multi-container pods). kubectl describe provides more context but is more verbose.

Q6
Update the image of a container named app in a deployment called web-deploy to nginx:1.26. What command do you use?
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl set image deployment/web-deploy app=nginx:1.26
Explanation

The syntax is kubectl set image RESOURCE/NAME CONTAINER=IMAGE. The container name (app) must match the container name in the deployment spec. This triggers a rolling update. Alternatively, you can kubectl edit deployment web-deploy and change the image field directly.

Q7
What is the difference between ENTRYPOINT and CMD in a Dockerfile? How do they map to Kubernetes pod spec fields?
Show Answer
Answer

ENTRYPOINT → Kubernetes command field (the executable)
CMD → Kubernetes args field (arguments to the executable)
In Kubernetes, command overrides ENTRYPOINT and args overrides CMD.

Explanation

This is a high-frequency CKAD question. Remember: Docker ENTRYPOINT = K8s command, Docker CMD = K8s args. If you set command in a pod spec, it completely replaces the image's ENTRYPOINT. If you only set args, the image's ENTRYPOINT is preserved.

Q8
A pod spec has image: myapp:latest and no imagePullPolicy specified. The image exists locally on the node but is outdated. What happens when the pod is created?
Show Answer
Answer

Kubernetes pulls the latest version from the registry because :latest tag defaults to imagePullPolicy: Always. The outdated local image is not used.

Explanation

This is why :latest is problematic — it always pulls, which is slow and requires registry access. It also means "latest" at build time, not "latest available." Use explicit tags like :1.25.3 for reproducible deployments. If the registry is unavailable, the pod will be stuck in ErrImagePull.

Q9
What is a container registry? Name three common registries and write the full image reference for pulling from a private registry at registry.example.com.
Show Answer
Answer

A container registry stores and distributes container images.
Common registries: Docker Hub, Google Container Registry (gcr.io), Amazon ECR.
Private image reference: registry.example.com/myteam/myapp:1.0

Explanation

When you specify image: nginx, K8s pulls from Docker Hub by default (docker.io/library/nginx:latest). For private registries, you need the full path AND an imagePullSecret in the pod spec. On the CKAD, you may need to create secrets for private registries: kubectl create secret docker-registry.

Q10
Write the Kubernetes pod spec fields to override a container's default command. The container should run python with arguments manage.py runserver 0.0.0.0:8000.
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    command: ["python"]
    args: ["manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Explanation

command replaces the image's ENTRYPOINT — it's the executable. args replaces CMD — the arguments. Both use JSON array syntax ["item1", "item2"]. A common CKAD pattern is overriding a busybox container: command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo hello; sleep 5; done"].

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Securing Control Plane Components

CLUSTER SETUP 15% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Control Plane Security Checklist

ComponentSecurity ConcernHardening
API ServerCentral entry pointTLS, RBAC, audit logging, disable anonymous
etcdStores ALL secrets in plaintext by defaultEncryption at rest, TLS peer certs, restrict access
SchedulerCan be manipulated to place pods on specific nodesSecure port binding, RBAC
Controller ManagerCreates/deletes cluster resourcesSecure port, service account key rotation
KubeletNode agent with full pod accessDisable anonymous auth, webhook authn/authz

etcd Encryption at Rest

# /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
    - secrets
    providers:
    - aescbc:
        keys:
        - name: key1
          secret: 
    - identity: {}  # fallback: read unencrypted
# API Server flag:
--encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml

# Verify encryption is working:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret | hexdump -C
# Should show encrypted data, not plaintext

Chapter 2 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
How do you enable encryption at rest for Secrets in etcd? Show the EncryptionConfiguration and API server flag.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create EncryptionConfiguration
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
    - secrets
    providers:
    - aescbc:
        keys:
        - name: key1
          secret: $(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)
    - identity: {}
# 2. Add to kube-apiserver manifest
# /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
#   --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml

# 3. Mount the config as volume
# 4. Restart API server (it's a static pod — auto-restarts)

# 5. Re-encrypt existing secrets:
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# 6. Verify:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret
Explanation

By default, Secrets are stored base64-encoded (NOT encrypted) in etcd. The EncryptionConfiguration tells the API server to encrypt before storing. aescbc is the standard provider. The identity fallback allows reading old unencrypted secrets. On the CKS exam, you MUST know EncryptionConfiguration — it's a very common question.

CKS-2
How do you secure the kubelet? Name 4 hardening measures.
Show Answer
Answer
# Kubelet security flags:
# 1. Disable anonymous authentication
--anonymous-auth=false

# 2. Enable webhook authorization (API server decides)
--authorization-mode=Webhook

# 3. Rotate kubelet certificates
--rotate-certificates=true

# 4. Protect the kubelet read-only port
--read-only-port=0    # Disable entirely

# 5. Restrict kubelet API access
# Only API server should talk to kubelet port 10250

# Verify kubelet config:
kubectl get --raw /api/v1/nodes/worker-1/proxy/configz | jq
Explanation

The kubelet exposes an API on port 10250. Without --anonymous-auth=false, anyone can exec into pods, read logs, and access node data. Port 10255 (read-only) leaks pod info. On the CKS exam, know how to check and fix kubelet configuration — either via command flags or the kubelet config file at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml.

CKS-3
How do you verify the integrity of Kubernetes binaries after download?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Download binary and checksum
curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/v1.30.0/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl"
curl -LO "https://dl.k8s.io/release/v1.30.0/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl.sha256"

# 2. Verify checksum
echo "$(cat kubectl.sha256)  kubectl" | sha256sum --check
# kubectl: OK

# 3. For kubelet and kubeadm, same pattern:
sha256sum kubelet
# Compare against published checksum on k8s release page

# If verification fails → DO NOT USE the binary (may be tampered)
Explanation

Binary verification prevents supply chain attacks where an attacker replaces official binaries with backdoored versions. On the CKS exam, you may be asked to verify platform binaries. Always compare SHA256 checksums against the official Kubernetes release page. This falls under the "Cluster Setup" domain.

CKS-4
What API server flags should you set to harden authentication and authorization?
Show Answer
Answer
# Critical kube-apiserver security flags:

# Authentication:
--anonymous-auth=false                    # No anonymous access
--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction  # Limit kubelet permissions
--client-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt  # Client cert auth

# Authorization:
--authorization-mode=Node,RBAC            # Never use AlwaysAllow

# Audit:
--audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
--audit-log-maxage=30

# Network:
--insecure-port=0                         # Disable insecure port
--secure-port=6443                        # HTTPS only

# Check in static pod manifest:
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep -E "anonymous|authorization-mode|insecure"
Explanation

The CKS exam frequently asks you to inspect and fix API server flags. The manifest is at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml. After editing, the static pod auto-restarts. Key: never AlwaysAllow, always Node,RBAC. The insecure-port (deprecated/removed) should always be 0.

CKS-5
How do you restrict etcd access? Why is etcd security critical?
Show Answer
Answer
# etcd security measures:

# 1. TLS client certificates (only API server can talk to etcd)
--cert-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt
--key-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
--trusted-ca-file=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt
--client-cert-auth=true
--peer-client-cert-auth=true

# 2. Network isolation — etcd only listens on localhost + cluster peer
--listen-client-urls=https://127.0.0.1:2379

# 3. Firewall: block port 2379/2380 from worker nodes

# 4. Why it matters:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get "" --prefix --keys-only | head
# /registry/secrets/...       ← All secrets
# /registry/serviceaccounts/  ← SA tokens
# /registry/clusterroles/     ← RBAC rules
Explanation

etcd contains EVERY object in the cluster — Secrets, RBAC rules, ServiceAccount tokens. Direct etcd access bypasses ALL Kubernetes auth/authz. On the CKS exam, know how to verify etcd uses TLS (--client-cert-auth=true), and ensure only the API server can reach etcd ports (2379/2380).

Chapter 3: Kubernetes Architecture (How It All Fits Together)

Now that you understand why Kubernetes exists and how Docker packages apps, let's look under the hood. This chapter dissects the architecture — control plane, worker nodes, and how every component collaborates to keep your anihpj pods running.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1.5 hours 🏗️ Architecture 🧠 Conceptual

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What a Kubernetes cluster is and its minimum requirements
  • ✅ The 5 control plane components and what each one does
  • ✅ The 3 worker node components that run your containers
  • ✅ The full 8-step journey of how a pod gets created
  • ✅ The declarative model — desired state vs actual state
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Foundation ALL DOMAINS
Architecture Knowledge on the CKAD

You won't be directly asked to "name the control plane components," but understanding architecture helps you troubleshoot faster during the exam:

  • API Server — Every kubectl command hits this. If API server is down, nothing works.
  • etcd — All cluster state is here. Backing it up = backing up the cluster.
  • Scheduler — Decides WHERE pods run. Affected by nodeSelector, affinity, taints (Ch33 topics).
  • kubelet — Agent on each node that actually runs pods. If it fails, pods on that node stop.
  • kube-proxy — Manages networking rules. Explains why Services can route to Pods.

Cluster Component Commands — Exam Awareness

What You NeedCommand
Check cluster infokubectl cluster-info
List all nodeskubectl get nodes
Node detailskubectl describe node <name>
Check all API resourceskubectl api-resources
Check API versionskubectl api-versions
Current contextkubectl config current-context
Switch contextkubectl config use-context <name>
Set default namespacekubectl config set-context --current --namespace=myns
View all contextskubectl config get-contexts
Check component statuskubectl get componentstatuses

Context Switching — #1 Exam Time Waster

  • Every CKAD question specifies a context — you MUST switch before answering
  • ❌ Forgetting to switch context → creating resources in the wrong cluster = 0 marks
  • ✅ First command for every question: kubectl config use-context <context-name>
  • Many questions also specify a namespace — always check and set it: kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=target-ns
  • Verify before working: kubectl config current-context then kubectl get ns

3.1 The Cluster — The Big Picture

A Kubernetes cluster is a set of machines (physical or virtual) that work together to run containerized applications. Every cluster has two types of machines:

🧠
Control Plane (The Brain)

Makes all the decisions: what runs where, when to scale, how to recover. In production, you run 3+ control plane nodes for high availability.

💪
Worker Nodes (The Muscles)

Actually run your application containers. Each worker runs your anihpj pods, handles traffic, and reports status back to the control plane.

Complete Kubernetes Cluster Architecture
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        KUBERNETES CLUSTER                           │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                   🧠 CONTROL PLANE (The Brain)                │  │
│  │                                                               │  │
│  │  ┌─────────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌───────────┐ ┌──────────────┐  │  │
│  │  │ API Server  │ │  etcd  │ │ Scheduler │ │  Controller  │  │  │
│  │  │ 🛡️ Front    │ │ 💾 Data │ │ 📍 Assign │ │ 🔄 Manager   │  │  │
│  │  │    Door     │ │  Store │ │    Pods   │ │    Loops     │  │  │
│  │  └──────┬──────┘ └────────┘ └───────────┘ └──────────────┘  │  │
│  └─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│            │  kubectl / API calls                                   │
│  ┌─────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │         ▼                                                     │  │
│  │  💪 WORKER NODE 1              💪 WORKER NODE 2               │  │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────────┐         │  │
│  │  │ kubelet | kube-proxy │   │ kubelet | kube-proxy │         │  │
│  │  ├──────────────────────┤   ├──────────────────────┤         │  │
│  │  │ ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  │   │ ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  │         │  │
│  │  │ │📦  ││📦  ││📦  │  │   │ │📦  ││📦  ││📦  │  │         │  │
│  │  │ │web ││redis││nginx│  │   │ │web ││web ││ db │  │         │  │
│  │  │ └────┘└────┘└────┘  │   │ └────┘└────┘└────┘  │         │  │
│  │  └──────────────────────┘   └──────────────────────┘         │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📌 Minimum Cluster

For local development (Minikube, kind), you get a single node that acts as both control plane and worker. In production, you'd have at least 3 control plane nodes (for high availability) and 2+ worker nodes (for capacity).

3.2 Control Plane Components (The Brain)

The control plane makes all cluster-level decisions. It has five components, each with a specific job:

a) kube-apiserver — The Front Door

🛡️
What It Does

The only component that everything talks to. Every kubectl command, every internal communication, every dashboard click goes through the API Server. It validates requests, authenticates users, and routes commands.

🔑
Key Responsibilities

• REST API endpoint (HTTPS)
• Authentication & authorization
• Admission control (mutating & validating)
• Gateway to etcd (only component that reads/writes etcd)

API Server as the Central Hub
    kubectl ──────┐
    Dashboard ────┤
    CI/CD ────────┤──▶  🛡️ API Server  ──▶  💾 etcd
    kubelet ──────┤      (authenticates,
    Scheduler ────┤       validates,
    Controllers ──┘       stores)

b) etcd — The Cluster's Database

💾
What It Does

A distributed key-value store that holds all cluster state — every pod, service, config, secret. Uses the Raft consensus algorithm to ensure data consistency across replicas.

📁
What It Stores

• Which pods exist and their status
• Deployment configurations
• Secrets and ConfigMaps
• Node registrations
• Service endpoints

🚨 Critical Warning

If etcd data is lost and there's no backup, your entire cluster state is gone. Always back up etcd in production. Everything else in Kubernetes is stateless — etcd is the single source of truth.

c) kube-scheduler — The Pod Placement Engine

📍
What It Does

Watches for newly created pods with no assigned node. Evaluates each node's resources, constraints, and affinities, then picks the best fit. Example: "anihpj pod needs 256Mi RAM → Node 2 has 512Mi free → assign to Node 2."

📦
New Pod Unscheduled
→
🔍
Filter Which nodes can fit?
→
⚖️
Score Rank candidates
→
📍
Bind Assign to best node

d) kube-controller-manager — The Reconciliation Loops

🔄
What It Does

Runs multiple controller loops that continuously compare desired state to actual state, and take action to fix any drift. Each controller handles one type of resource.

🔁 Deployment Controller

Ensures the correct number of ReplicaSets exist for each Deployment.

📋 ReplicaSet Controller

Maintains the desired number of pod replicas. Creates or deletes pods as needed.

💻 Node Controller

Monitors node health. Marks unhealthy nodes and reschedules their pods.

⏰ Job Controller

Watches for Job objects and creates pods to run tasks to completion.

Controller Loop Pattern (Watch → Compare → Act)
     ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐
     │  Desired State   │         │  Actual State    │
     │  replicas: 3     │───?───▶│  2 pods running  │
     └─────────────────┘  match? └─────────────────┘
                                          │
                                     ❌ NO!
                                          │
                                          ▼
                                  🔄 Controller creates
                                     1 more pod → ✅ 3 pods

e) cloud-controller-manager

☁️
What It Does

Integrates Kubernetes with cloud provider APIs (AWS, GCP, Azure). Creates cloud load balancers when you create a LoadBalancer Service, manages cloud routes, and handles node lifecycle in cloud environments. Not present in local clusters like Minikube or kind.

3.3 Worker Node Components (The Muscles)

Worker nodes are where your application containers actually run. Each node has three essential components:

a) kubelet — The Node Agent

🤖
What It Does

An agent running on every node. Receives pod specifications from the API Server, ensures the containers described in the spec are running and healthy, and reports status back.

📋
Responsibilities

• Pull container images
• Start/stop containers
• Run health checks (liveness, readiness)
• Report pod & node status to API Server

b) kube-proxy — The Network Plumber

🔀
What It Does

A network proxy on every node that maintains network rules (iptables or IPVS). When a Service is created, kube-proxy ensures traffic to that Service's ClusterIP is forwarded to the correct backend pods. This is how anihpj-service routes to the right pod, even if pods restart with new IPs.

c) Container Runtime

⚙️
What It Does

The software that actually creates and runs containers. Kubernetes itself doesn't run containers — it delegates to a runtime via the Container Runtime Interface (CRI).

RuntimeStatusNotes
containerd✅ DefaultMost common. Used by Docker, EKS, GKE, AKS.
CRI-O✅ SupportedLightweight, OCI-compliant. Popular with OpenShift.
Docker Engine⚠️ DeprecatedRemoved as a runtime in K8s 1.24+. Docker images still work — only the runtime shim was removed.
💡 Docker Images Still Work!

When people say "Docker was removed from Kubernetes," they mean the Docker runtime shim (dockershim) was removed. Your Docker-built images are OCI-compliant and work perfectly with containerd or CRI-O.

How Worker Node Components Communicate

Worker Node Internal Flow
  🧠 Control Plane (API Server)
         │
         │ Pod spec: "run anihpj:v2 on port 8000"
         │
    ┌────▼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  💪 WORKER NODE                                       │
    │                                                       │
    │  🤖 kubelet                                           │
    │    │  "I received a pod spec, let me handle it"       │
    │    │                                                  │
    │    ├──▶ ⚙️ Container Runtime (containerd)             │
    │    │      │  Pull anihpj:v2 from registry             │
    │    │      │  Create & start the container            │
    │    │      └──▶ 🐍 anihpj container running :8000     │
    │    │                                                  │
    │    └──▶ Reports status back to API Server             │
    │                                                       │
    │  🔀 kube-proxy                                        │
    │    │  "Service anihpj-svc created, update rules"      │
    │    └──▶ iptables/IPVS rules updated                  │
    │         Traffic to ClusterIP → forwarded to pod      │
    └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

3.4 How a Pod Gets Created — The Full Journey

When you run kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml, here's the complete 8-step chain of events:

The Pod Creation Journey — 8 Steps
  👨‍💻 You                   🛡️ API Server            💾 etcd
   │                        │                       │
   │── ① kubectl apply ───▶│                       │
   │                        │── ② validate ────────▶│
   │                        │   + store             │── ③ saved!
   │                        │                       │
  🔄 Controller              │                       │
   │── ④ sees new deploy ─▶│                       │
   │   creates ReplicaSet   │                       │
   │── ⑤ creates Pod obj ─▶│                       │
   │                        │                       │
  📍 Scheduler               │                       │
   │── ⑥ picks best node ─▶│                       │
   │   "Node 2 has space"   │                       │
   │                        │                       │
  💪 Node 2 (kubelet)        │                       │
   │── ⑦ pulls anihpj:v1   │                       │
   │── ⑧ starts container  │                       │
   │                        │                       │
  ✅ Pod Running!             │                       │
①
kubectl apply

You send your YAML to the API Server

②③
Validate & Store

API Server authenticates, validates, stores in etcd

④⑤
Controllers Act

Deployment controller creates ReplicaSet, which creates Pod objects

⑥⑦⑧
Schedule & Run

Scheduler assigns node, kubelet pulls image, container starts

📌 Key Insight

No single component "runs" a pod. It's a relay race: API Server → etcd → Controller → Scheduler → kubelet → Container Runtime. Each component watches for changes and does its part. This decoupled design is what makes Kubernetes resilient.

Verify Each Step With kubectl

terminal — Tracing the Journey 
# After applying anihpj-deployment.yaml, trace each step:

# ① Check the Deployment was created
kubectl get deployment anihpj-deployment

# ④ Check the ReplicaSet was created by the controller
kubectl get replicaset

# ⑤ Check the Pods were created by the ReplicaSet
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj

# ⑥ See which NODE each pod was scheduled on
kubectl get pods -o wide

# ⑦⑧ See events — the full story: scheduling, pulling, starting
kubectl describe pod <pod-name>
# Events:
#   Scheduled → Pulling → Pulled → Created → Started

3.5 Control Plane vs Worker Node

Aspect 🧠 Control Plane 💪 Worker Node
Purpose Manage cluster state & make decisions Run application pods
Components API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager kubelet, kube-proxy, Container Runtime
User workloads? Generally no (tainted by default) Yes — this is where anihpj pods run
Count (dev) 1 (Minikube combines both) 1 (same node)
Count (prod) 3+ (for high availability) 2+ (scale as needed)
If it fails? Cluster can't schedule or heal (critical!) Pods reschedule to other nodes

3.6 Kubernetes API and API Groups

Every resource in Kubernetes belongs to an API group. This organizes the hundreds of resource types and controls versioning:

API GroupVersionResourcesExample
Core (legacy) v1 Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, Namespace, Node apiVersion: v1
apps apps/v1 Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet apiVersion: apps/v1
batch batch/v1 Job, CronJob apiVersion: batch/v1
networking networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress, NetworkPolicy, IngressClass apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
rbac rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
autoscaling autoscaling/v2 HorizontalPodAutoscaler apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
📌 API Versioning

APIs go through maturity stages: alpha (v1alpha1 — experimental) → beta (v1beta1 — mostly stable) → stable (v1 — production-ready). Always use stable versions in production YAML files.

3.7 Understanding Desired State vs Actual State

This is the most important mental model in Kubernetes. You don't tell Kubernetes how to do things — you tell it what you want, and it continuously works to make reality match your description.

📝 Declarative (Kubernetes Way)

"I want 3 replicas of anihpj:v2 running with 256Mi memory each."

You declare the end state. Kubernetes figures out the steps and continuously enforces it — even if a pod crashes at 3 AM.

⌨️ Imperative (Traditional Way)

"SSH to server, pull the latest code, restart gunicorn, check if it's running, configure nginx..."

You specify every step. If something breaks, you have to detect it and fix it manually.

The Reconciliation Loop in Action
┌──────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐
│    DESIRED STATE     │            │    ACTUAL STATE       │
│    (your YAML)       │            │    (what exists)      │
│                      │            │                       │
│  replicas: 3         │    ≠?      │  🟢 pod-1 Running     │
│  image: anihpj:v2    │ ────────▶  │  🟢 pod-2 Running     │
│  memory: 256Mi       │  compare   │  🔴 pod-3 Crashed!    │
└──────────────────────┘            └──────────┬────────────┘
                                               │
                                        🔄 Controller detects
                                           mismatch!
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                      Creates new pod-3
                                               │
                                               ▼
                                    🟢 pod-3 Running ✅
                                    All 3 replicas healthy!
🎉 What This Means for anihpj
  • ✅ You write a YAML file saying "I want 3 copies of anihpj running"
  • ✅ If pod crashes → Controller creates a replacement automatically
  • ✅ If node dies → Scheduler moves pods to healthy nodes
  • ✅ If you change the image tag → Deployment controller does a rolling update
  • ✅ You never SSH into servers — you just update the YAML and kubectl apply
💡 The Golden Rule

Never make manual changes to a cluster. Always edit your YAML files and re-apply. That way, your YAML is always the single source of truth, and you can reproduce your entire deployment from scratch.

Chapter 3 — Practice Questions

Q1
Name all the components of the Kubernetes control plane and briefly describe each one's role.
Show Answer
Answer

kube-apiserver — Front door to the cluster; all requests go through it.
etcd — Key-value store holding all cluster state.
kube-scheduler — Assigns pods to nodes based on resources/constraints.
kube-controller-manager — Runs controllers (Deployment, ReplicaSet, Node, etc.).
cloud-controller-manager — Integrates with cloud provider APIs (optional).

Explanation

On the CKAD, you mainly interact with the API server via kubectl. Understanding the flow: kubectl → API server → etcd (store) → scheduler (assign node) → kubelet (run pod) helps you troubleshoot why pods are stuck in Pending (scheduler issue) vs CrashLoopBackOff (application issue).

Q2
What components run on every worker node? What happens to existing pods if the kubelet process on a node crashes?
Show Answer
Answer

Worker node components: kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime (containerd/CRI-O).
If kubelet crashes: existing containers keep running (managed by the container runtime), but the node stops reporting to the control plane. After the node-monitor-grace-period (default 40s), the node is marked NotReady and pods may be rescheduled.

Explanation

The kubelet is the agent on each node that receives pod specs from the API server and ensures containers are running. The container runtime does the actual work of running containers. kube-proxy manages network rules for Service routing. Knowing this helps diagnose node-level issues on the CKAD.

Q3
You run kubectl get nodes and see a node in NotReady status. What are 3 possible causes?
Show Answer
Answer

1. Kubelet is not running on the node (service crashed or stopped).
2. Network connectivity — the node can't reach the API server.
3. Resource exhaustion — disk pressure, memory pressure, or PID pressure.

Explanation

Debug with: kubectl describe node NODE_NAME → check Conditions section (MemoryPressure, DiskPressure, PIDPressure, Ready). Also check: systemctl status kubelet on the node. On the CKAD, node debugging is tested — use kubectl get nodes first, then describe for details.

Q4
Trace the lifecycle of a pod creation from kubectl apply -f pod.yaml to the container running. What happens at each step?
Show Answer
Answer

1. kubectl sends the pod spec to the API server.
2. API server validates and stores it in etcd.
3. Scheduler watches for unassigned pods, selects a node, and updates the pod's nodeName.
4. Kubelet on the selected node watches for pods assigned to it.
5. Kubelet instructs the container runtime to pull the image and start the container.
6. Kubelet reports pod status back to the API server.

Explanation

This flow is called the "watch-and-react" pattern. Components don't directly call each other — they watch the API server for changes relevant to them. This loose coupling is why K8s is so resilient. If the scheduler is down, step 3 fails and the pod stays Pending.

Q5
What is the difference between the control plane and the data plane in Kubernetes?
Show Answer
Answer

Control plane — Makes decisions: scheduling, scaling, detecting failures. Runs on master nodes (API server, scheduler, controller-manager, etcd).
Data plane — Does the work: runs containers, handles networking. Runs on worker nodes (kubelet, kube-proxy, container runtime, your pods).

Explanation

In managed K8s (EKS, AKS, GKE), the cloud provider manages the control plane — you only manage worker nodes. On the CKAD exam, you interact with the control plane via kubectl commands. Your applications run on the data plane (worker nodes).

Q6
What is kube-proxy and how does it handle Service traffic routing?
Show Answer
Answer

kube-proxy runs on every node and maintains network rules (iptables/IPVS) that route traffic from Service ClusterIPs to pod endpoints. When you access my-service:80, kube-proxy's rules route it to one of the backend pods.

Explanation

kube-proxy doesn't actually proxy traffic in most modes — it just sets up networking rules. In iptables mode (default), it creates iptables rules for load balancing. In IPVS mode, it uses kernel-level load balancing. On the CKAD, know that Services work because of kube-proxy's networking rules on each node.

Q7
How do you check the Kubernetes version of your cluster and the API resources available?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cluster version
kubectl version --short

# API resources available
kubectl api-resources

# API versions available
kubectl api-versions

# Cluster info
kubectl cluster-info
Explanation

kubectl api-resources is invaluable on the CKAD — it shows every resource type, its short name, API group, and whether it's namespaced. Use it to find the correct apiVersion for your YAML. Example: kubectl api-resources | grep deploy shows apps/v1 for Deployments.

Q8
What is a controller in Kubernetes? Name 4 built-in controllers and what each manages.
Show Answer
Answer

Deployment controller — Manages ReplicaSets and rolling updates.
ReplicaSet controller — Ensures desired number of pod replicas.
Job controller — Ensures a task runs to completion.
Node controller — Monitors node health, marks unhealthy nodes.

Explanation

Controllers implement the reconciliation loop: observe current state → compare with desired state → take action. They all run inside the kube-controller-manager on the control plane. On the CKAD, you create the desired state (YAML), and controllers do the rest.

Q9
You need to check if the control plane components are healthy. Which commands would you use?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check component status
kubectl get componentstatuses    # or: kubectl get cs

# Check control plane pods (if running as pods)
kubectl get pods -n kube-system

# Check node status
kubectl get nodes

# Detailed cluster info
kubectl cluster-info dump | head -50
Explanation

On the CKAD exam, if things aren't working, always start with kubectl get nodes and kubectl get pods -n kube-system. The kube-system namespace contains control plane components when they run as pods (which is most clusters). Check that all are Running with correct restart counts.

Q10
If the kube-apiserver goes down, what still works and what breaks?
Show Answer
Answer

Still works: Already-running containers continue to run (kubelet keeps them alive locally).
Breaks: ALL management operations — kubectl commands fail, no new pods can be scheduled, no scaling, no service discovery updates, no self-healing (deleted pods won't be recreated).

Explanation

The API server is the single gateway to the cluster. Without it, the cluster is effectively frozen. Controllers can't reconcile, the scheduler can't schedule, and kubelet can't report status. This is why production clusters run multiple API server replicas behind a load balancer for high availability.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Pod Security Admission & Namespace Isolation

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Pod Security Standards (PSS) — Three Profiles

ProfileDescriptionWhat's Blocked
PrivilegedUnrestricted (default)Nothing — wide open
BaselineMinimally restrictivehostNetwork, hostPID, privileged containers
RestrictedHeavily restricted (CKS target)All of baseline + must run as non-root, drop ALL caps, seccomp required

Pod Security Admission (PSA) — Enforcement Modes

# Apply PSA labels to a namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: production
  labels:
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted   # REJECT violating pods
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit: restricted      # Log violations
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn: restricted       # Warn users
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version: latest

Namespace as Security Boundary

Namespaces are the primary security boundary in Kubernetes. Always combine:

  • RBAC: Role/RoleBinding per namespace
  • NetworkPolicy: Default deny ingress/egress per namespace
  • ResourceQuota: Prevent resource exhaustion
  • PSA labels: Enforce Pod Security Standards

Chapter 3 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Apply the "restricted" Pod Security Standard to namespace "production" in enforce mode. What happens when a pod violating the policy is created?
Show Answer
Answer
# Label the namespace
kubectl label namespace production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest

# Verify
kubectl get ns production -o yaml | grep pod-security

# When a violating pod is created:
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n production
# Error: pods "test" is forbidden: violates PodSecurity "restricted:latest"
# The pod is REJECTED (never created)
Explanation

In enforce mode, PSA rejects pods that violate the standard. The "restricted" profile requires: runAsNonRoot, drop ALL capabilities, seccomp profile set, no privilege escalation. This is a very common CKS exam task.

CKS-2
Write a pod spec that is compliant with the "restricted" Pod Security Standard.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: production
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true           # Required by restricted
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault       # Required by restricted
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false   # Required by restricted
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]            # Required by restricted
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true      # Best practice
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
Explanation

The restricted PSS requires: runAsNonRoot: true, seccompProfile, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, capabilities.drop: ["ALL"]. Without ALL of these, the pod will be rejected in an enforce=restricted namespace. Memorize this pattern for the CKS exam.

CKS-3
Create a default-deny NetworkPolicy for all ingress AND egress traffic in namespace "secure-ns".
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: secure-ns
spec:
  podSelector: {}     # Select ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
  # No ingress/egress rules = deny all
# Apply
kubectl apply -f deny-all.yaml

# Verify — pods in secure-ns can't communicate
kubectl exec -n secure-ns test-pod -- wget -qO- http://other-svc --timeout=2
# wget: download timed out
Explanation

An empty podSelector: {} selects ALL pods. Listing Ingress and Egress in policyTypes without any rules means deny all. This is the CKS best practice: start with default-deny, then add specific allow rules. Without a CNI that supports NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium), this has no effect.

CKS-4
What are the three PSA modes and when would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
ModeLabelBehaviorUse Case
enforcepod-security.kubernetes.io/enforceRejects violating podsProduction namespaces
auditpod-security.kubernetes.io/auditLogs violations (pod still created)Migration — discover violations
warnpod-security.kubernetes.io/warnWarning to user (pod still created)Dev envs — educate users
 
# Recommended: combine all three
kubectl label ns my-ns \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted
Explanation

Best practice: enforce baseline + audit/warn restricted. This blocks dangerous configs while warning about ideal state. On the CKS exam, remember: enforce=reject, audit=log, warn=display warning. Use -version labels to pin to a specific Kubernetes version.

CKS-5
How do you create a ResourceQuota to prevent namespace resource exhaustion as a security measure?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: security-quota
  namespace: tenant-a
spec:
  hard:
    pods: "20"                      # Max pods
    requests.cpu: "4"               # Max CPU requests
    requests.memory: "8Gi"          # Max memory requests
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: "16Gi"
    services.nodeports: "0"         # Block NodePorts (security!)
    count/secrets: "20"             # Limit secrets
# Verify:
kubectl get resourcequota -n tenant-a
kubectl describe quota security-quota -n tenant-a
Explanation

ResourceQuota prevents resource exhaustion (DoS). Security-wise, setting services.nodeports: "0" blocks direct node port exposure. Limiting pod count prevents fork bombs. On CKS, ResourceQuota is tested as part of namespace isolation strategy — combine with RBAC, NetworkPolicy, and PSA.

Chapter 4: Core Kubernetes Objects (The Building Blocks)

Kubernetes manages your applications through objects — persistent entities that represent the desired state of your cluster. This chapter covers the essential building blocks: Pods, Deployments, Services, Namespaces, Labels, and how they all connect.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🧱 Core Objects 📝 YAML

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The anatomy of every Kubernetes object (apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec)
  • ✅ Pods — what they are, their lifecycle, and why you never create them directly
  • ✅ The Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod hierarchy
  • ✅ Service types and how pods are exposed to traffic
  • ✅ Namespaces, Labels, Selectors, and Annotations
  • ✅ How all objects connect in a real application
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Application Design & Build 20% Application Deployment 20%
Core Objects — Your CKAD Foundation

Almost every CKAD question involves creating or modifying core objects. Master these imperative shortcuts — they save 2-3 minutes per task:

  • kubectl run nginx --image=nginx — Create a Pod (fastest method)
  • kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx --replicas=3 — Create a Deployment
  • kubectl expose deployment web --port=80 --type=ClusterIP — Create a Service
  • kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml — Generate YAML template

⚡ Pro Strategy: Use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate base YAML, then edit it for complex requirements.

Complete Pod Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create podkubectl run nginx --image=nginx
Pod + labelskubectl run nginx --image=nginx --labels="app=web,env=prod"
Pod + portkubectl run nginx --image=nginx --port=80
Pod + commandkubectl run busybox --image=busybox -- sleep 3600
Pod + env varskubectl run nginx --image=nginx --env="DB_HOST=postgres" --env="DB_PORT=5432"
Pod + requests/limitskubectl run nginx --image=nginx --requests="cpu=100m,memory=64Mi" --limits="cpu=200m,memory=128Mi"
Pod + ServiceAccountkubectl run nginx --image=nginx --serviceaccount=my-sa
Generate YAML onlykubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml
Generate + save to filekubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
Temporary debug podkubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never -- sh
Delete pod fastkubectl delete pod nginx --force --grace-period=0
Execute into podkubectl exec -it nginx -- /bin/sh

Minimal Pod YAML — Type in 30 Seconds

This is the base template you'll modify for EVERY question. Memorize this structure cold.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: myapp
  labels:
    app: myapp
spec:
  containers:
  - name: myapp
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "64Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
      limits:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "500m"

kubectl explain — Find Any YAML Field Instantly

During the exam, use kubectl explain to discover field names you've forgotten. This is faster than browsing docs:

kubectl explain pod.spec                              # All pod spec fields
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers                   # Container fields
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.ports              # Port definitions
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.env                # Environment variables
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources          # Requests & limits
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts       # Volume mounts
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes                       # Volume types (emptyDir, etc.)
kubectl explain pod.spec.initContainers                # Init containers
kubectl explain pod.spec.serviceAccountName            # Service account
kubectl explain pod.spec.securityContext               # Pod-level security
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.securityContext    # Container-level security
kubectl explain pod.spec.nodeSelector                  # Node selection
kubectl explain pod.spec.tolerations                   # Tolerations

# Recursive (shows ALL nested fields):
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | grep -i volume  # Find volume-related fields
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive | head -80        # Overview

Pod Creation Traps on the CKAD

  • ❌ Forgetting the namespace → resource created in default instead of target namespace = 0 marks
  • ✅ Always set namespace first: kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=TARGET
  • Double dash (--): Arguments after -- go to the container, not kubectl. Compare:
    • kubectl run bb --image=busybox -- sleep 3600 (correct — sleep is the container command)
    • kubectl run bb --image=busybox sleep 3600 (wrong — kubectl tries to parse "sleep")
  • containerPort is informational only — it doesn't actually open a port. You need a Service to expose the pod
  • Pod vs Deployment: kubectl run creates a Pod. kubectl create deployment creates a Deployment. Know the difference — a Pod won't self-heal
  • --restart=Never ensures a bare Pod (not managed). Use this for one-time tasks in the exam

After Creating a Pod — Verify Checklist

  • kubectl get pod myapp → STATUS should be Running
  • kubectl get pod myapp -o wide → Check NODE assignment and IP
  • kubectl describe pod myapp → Scroll to Events for errors
  • kubectl logs myapp → Check application output
  • kubectl get pod myapp --show-labels → Verify labels match requirements
  • kubectl get pod myapp -o yaml | grep -A5 resources → Verify requests/limits
  • kubectl get pod myapp -n target-ns → Confirm correct namespace

🗺️ How Core Kubernetes Objects Connect

☸️
Cluster
📦
Pod

Smallest deployable unit. Runs your container(s).

↑ created by Deployment
🔄
ReplicaSet

Maintains desired number of pod copies.

↑ managed by Deployment
🚀
Deployment

Declares desired state. Handles updates & rollbacks.

→ creates ReplicaSet → Pod
🌐
Service

Stable network endpoint for pods. Load balances traffic.

→ routes to Pods via labels
📁
Namespace

Virtual cluster. Isolates resources by team/env.

→ contains all objects
🏷️
Labels

Key-value pairs. The glue connecting everything.

→ used by Selectors
1
Write YAML

Define desired state in a manifest file

2
kubectl apply

Send manifest to API Server

3
Store in etcd

API Server saves desired state

4
Controller Acts

Controllers create ReplicaSets & Pods

5
Scheduler Assigns

Pods placed on suitable nodes

6
Running! ✅

kubelet starts containers on the node

❌ Common YAML Mistakes
  • Using tabs instead of spaces
  • Wrong apiVersion for the resource kind
  • Mismatching selector.matchLabels and template.metadata.labels
  • Forgetting metadata.name (required field)
  • Incorrect indentation on container specs
✅ YAML Best Practices
  • Always use 2-space indentation
  • Use kubectl explain <resource> to find field names
  • Generate templates with --dry-run=client -o yaml
  • Validate with kubectl apply --dry-run=server
  • Keep labels consistent: app, env, version

4.1 What are Kubernetes Objects?

Every resource in Kubernetes — a Pod, a Deployment, a Service — is an object. Each object is a "record of intent": you tell Kubernetes what you want, and the system continuously works to ensure that state exists.

Anatomy of a Kubernetes Object

Every object has four required fields:

📋
apiVersion

Which API group and version to use. Example: v1 for Pods, apps/v1 for Deployments.

🏷️
kind

The type of object. Example: Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap.

📛
metadata

Identifying info: name, namespace, labels, annotations. Must be unique within a namespace.

⚙️
spec

The desired state — what you want. Varies by object type. This is where you define containers, replicas, ports, etc.

Every K8s object follows this structure 
apiVersion: v1              # ← Which API version
kind: Pod                   # ← What type of object
metadata:                   # ← Identity & organization
  name: anihpj-pod
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: backend
spec:                       # ← Desired state (varies by kind)
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
# status: ...              # ← Set by Kubernetes (read-only)

4.2 Pods — The Smallest Deployable Unit

a) What is a Pod?

A Pod is a wrapper around one or more containers that share the same network and storage. Think of it as an apartment building:

🏠 Pod = Apartment

Shared address (IP), shared hallways (network namespace), shared utilities (volumes). Containers inside the pod communicate via localhost.

🚪 Container = Room

Each container has its own process, filesystem, and resources. But it shares the pod's IP address and mounted volumes with its neighbors.

Single-Container vs Multi-Container Pod
  SINGLE-CONTAINER POD (most common)     MULTI-CONTAINER POD (sidecar pattern)
  ┌─────────────────────┐                ┌────────────────────────────────┐
  │  📦 Pod              │                │  📦 Pod                        │
  │  ┌────────────────┐ │                │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  │
  │  │  🐍 anihpj      │ │                │  │ 🐍 anihpj │  │ 📊 log   │  │
  │  │  Django app    │ │                │  │  Django   │  │ collector│  │
  │  │  port 8000     │ │                │  │  :8000    │  │  sidecar │  │
  │  └────────────────┘ │                │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  │
  │  IP: 10.244.1.5     │                │  IP: 10.244.1.6               │
  │  Volume: /app/data  │                │  Shared volume: /var/log      │
  └─────────────────────┘                └────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Rule of Thumb

If two containers must be deployed, scaled, and stopped together — put them in one pod. Otherwise, use separate pods. For anihpj, the Django app gets its own pod. PostgreSQL gets a separate pod.

b) Pod Lifecycle and Phases

Every pod goes through a lifecycle with distinct phases:

Pod Lifecycle State Machine
                    ┌─────────────┐
                    │  ⏳ Pending   │ ← Image pulling, scheduling
                    └──────┬──────┘
                           │
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              ▼                         ▼
       ┌─────────────┐          ┌─────────────┐
       │  🟢 Running   │          │  ❌ Failed   │ ← Image not found,
       └──┬────────┬──┘          └─────────────┘   node down, etc.
          │        │
          ▼        ▼
   ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────────┐
   │✅ Succeed │ │🔄 CrashLoop  │ → restartPolicy
   │ ed       │ │  BackOff     │   decides next
   └──────────┘ └──────────────┘   action
PhaseMeaningCommon Cause
PendingPod accepted, waiting to be scheduled or image pulledImage downloading, no node has capacity
RunningAt least one container is runningNormal operation
SucceededAll containers exited successfully (exit code 0)Jobs, batch tasks
FailedAt least one container exited with an errorApp crash, misconfiguration
CrashLoopBackOffContainer keeps crashing; K8s retries with increasing delayBug in app, missing env vars, wrong command
📌 Restart Policies
  • Always (default) — Always restart, even on success. Used for web servers like anihpj.
  • OnFailure — Only restart if exit code ≠ 0. Used for Jobs.
  • Never — Never restart. Used for debugging.

c) Pod YAML for anihpj

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    tier: backend
    version: v1
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
          protocol: TCP
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        - name: PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE
          value: "1"
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "100m"
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
  restartPolicy: Always
⚠️ Don't Create Bare Pods in Production!

This YAML creates a single pod. If it crashes and restartPolicy can't fix it (e.g., the node dies), it's gone forever. In production, always use a Deployment — which manages pods through a ReplicaSet and can recreate them on other nodes.

d) When Pods Die

💀
Pods are Ephemeral

Pods are mortal. They are not resurrected after deletion. They get new IPs when restarted. Their local filesystem is wiped. Treat pods like cattle, not pets.

🛡️
Deployments are Durable

Deployments ensure the right number of pods are always running. Pod dies? Deployment creates a replacement. Node dies? Pods are rescheduled elsewhere.

4.3 ReplicaSets — Ensuring Availability

A ReplicaSet ensures a specified number of pod replicas are running at all times. If a pod dies, the ReplicaSet creates a new one to maintain the desired count.

ReplicaSet Maintaining 3 anihpj Pods
   ReplicaSet: "maintain 3 replicas of app=anihpj"

   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-3 (anihpj)
   Running               Running               Running
                                                     │
                                                  💥 Crashes!
                                                     │
   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🔴 pod-3 (dead)
                                                     │
                                              🔄 ReplicaSet detects!
                                                     │
   🟢 pod-1 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-2 (anihpj)     🟢 pod-4 (anihpj) ← NEW!
   Running               Running               Running ✅
📌 You Rarely Create ReplicaSets Directly

In practice, you create Deployments, which automatically create and manage ReplicaSets for you. The Deployment adds rolling updates and rollback capabilities on top of what ReplicaSets provide.

4.4 Deployments — Managing Application Updates

The Deployment is the most commonly used workload resource. It manages the full lifecycle: creating pods, scaling, rolling updates, and rollbacks.

The Object Hierarchy: Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     🏢 Deployment                              │
│          "I want 3 replicas of anihpj:v2"                     │
│                      manages ▼                                 │
│                ┌────────────────────┐                          │
│                │   📋 ReplicaSet    │                          │
│                │  "Maintain 3 pods" │                          │
│                └──┬──────┬──────┬──┘                          │
│                   │      │      │                              │
│              manages  manages  manages                        │
│                   ▼      ▼      ▼                              │
│                ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐                           │
│                │📦 1│ │📦 2│ │📦 3│  ← anihpj Pods            │
│                └────┘ └────┘ └────┘                           │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  🏢 Deployment  = CEO ("We need 3 workers running anihpj:v2")
  📋 ReplicaSet  = Manager ("I'll maintain exactly 3 pods")
  📦 Pod         = Worker (actually runs the container)
anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                    # ← Run 3 copies of our app
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj               # ← Find pods with this label
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate          # ← Zero-downtime updates
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1               # ← Create 1 extra pod during update
      maxUnavailable: 0          # ← Never have fewer than 3 running
  template:                      # ← Pod template (what each pod looks like)
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj             # ← Must match selector above!
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v2
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"

Update Strategies

🔄 RollingUpdate (Default)

Gradually replaces old pods with new ones. At no point are all pods down. Users experience zero downtime.

v1 → mix of v1+v2 → v2

💥 Recreate

Kill ALL old pods first, then create new ones. Causes downtime but guarantees no two versions run simultaneously.

v1 → all down → v2

Rolling Update In Action (3 replicas, v1 → v2)
  Step 1:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🟢v1              ← All running v1
  Step 2:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🟢v1  🔵v2        ← New v2 pod created (maxSurge: 1)
  Step 3:  🟢v1  🟢v1  🔵v2  ✅v2 ready   ← v2 passes health check
  Step 4:  🟢v1  🔵v2  🔵v2              ← Old v1 pod terminated
  Step 5:  🔵v2  🔵v2  🔵v2              ← All running v2 ✅

Essential Deployment Commands

terminal — Managing Deployments 
# Create or update a deployment
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml

# Check rollout status (watch it happen live)
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Undo last deployment (instant rollback!)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Scale to 5 replicas
kubectl scale deployment/anihpj-deployment --replicas=5

# Update image (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-deployment anihpj=anihpj:v3

4.5 Services — Exposing Your Application

a) Why Services Exist

😰
The Problem

Pods get random IP addresses that change every time they restart. You can't hardcode 10.244.1.5 because that pod might be replaced by one at 10.244.2.9 tomorrow.

✅
The Solution: Services

A Service provides a stable IP and DNS name that never changes. It load-balances traffic across all pods matching its selector, regardless of their actual IPs.

b) Service Types

🔒
ClusterIP (Default)

Internal-only access. Other pods can reach it, but external users cannot. Perfect for internal services like databases.

IP: 10.96.x.x (cluster-internal)
Use: Inter-service communication

🔓
NodePort

Exposes the service on a static port (30000-32767) on every node. Good for development and testing.

Access: <NodeIP>:30080
Use: Dev/testing environments

🌐
LoadBalancer

Cloud provider creates an external load balancer with a public IP. The standard for production traffic.

Access: 52.x.x.x (public IP)
Use: Production deployments

📎
ExternalName

Maps a Service to an external DNS name (CNAME). No proxying — just DNS aliasing.

Maps to: mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
Use: External services like RDS

Service Type Traffic Flow Comparison
  ClusterIP:      Internal pod ──▶ ClusterIP:80 ──▶ anihpj pod:8000
                  (inside cluster only)

  NodePort:       Browser ──▶ NodeIP:30080 ──▶ ClusterIP:80 ──▶ pod:8000
                  (accessible from outside via node IP)

  LoadBalancer:   Browser ──▶ PublicIP:80 ──▶ NodePort ──▶ ClusterIP ──▶ pod:8000
                  (cloud LB in front, production-ready)

  ExternalName:   pod ──▶ my-db-svc ──DNS──▶ mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
                  (just a DNS alias, no proxying)
anihpj-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
spec:
  type: ClusterIP              # ← Internal access (default)
  selector:
    app: anihpj                # ← Route to pods with label app=anihpj
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                 # ← Service listens on port 80
      targetPort: 8000         # ← Forward to container port 8000

c) Service Discovery

Kubernetes provides built-in DNS. Every Service gets a DNS name following this pattern:

Service DNS Format
  <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

  Example: anihpj-service.default.svc.cluster.local

  Short forms (within same namespace):
    • anihpj-service                          ← Most common
    • anihpj-service.default                  ← Explicit namespace
    • anihpj-service.default.svc.cluster.local ← Fully qualified
💡 Practical Example

In your Django settings.py, instead of hardcoding a PostgreSQL IP, you'd use the Service name:
DATABASES = {'HOST': 'postgres-service'}
Kubernetes DNS resolves this to the PostgreSQL pod's IP, no matter where it runs.

4.6 Namespaces — Organizing Resources

Namespaces are virtual clusters within your physical cluster. They let you divide resources by team, environment, or project:

Cluster Divided into Namespaces
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     KUBERNETES CLUSTER                          │
│                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐                   │
│  │  📁 default       │  │  📁 kube-system   │                   │
│  │  (your apps)     │  │  (K8s internals) │                   │
│  │  • anihpj pods   │  │  • CoreDNS       │                   │
│  │  • anihpj-svc    │  │  • kube-proxy    │                   │
│  │  • postgres pods │  │  • metrics-server│                   │
│  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘                   │
│                                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐                   │
│  │  📁 staging       │  │  📁 production    │                   │
│  │  (test env)      │  │  (live env)      │                   │
│  │  • anihpj:v2-rc  │  │  • anihpj:v1     │                   │
│  │  • test database │  │  • prod database │                   │
│  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘                   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
NamespacePurposeCreated By
defaultWhere your objects go if you don't specify a namespaceKubernetes
kube-systemKubernetes internal components (DNS, proxy, metrics)Kubernetes
kube-publicPublicly readable data (cluster info)Kubernetes
kube-node-leaseNode heartbeat leases for health monitoringKubernetes
terminal — Working with Namespaces 
# List all namespaces
kubectl get namespaces

# Create a namespace
kubectl create namespace staging

# Deploy anihpj to the staging namespace
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment.yaml -n staging

# List pods in a specific namespace
kubectl get pods -n staging

# List pods in ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

# Set default namespace (avoid typing -n every time)
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=staging

# Delete a namespace (⚠️ deletes EVERYTHING inside it!)
kubectl delete namespace staging
⚠️ Namespace Scope

Not all objects are namespaced. Namespaced: Pods, Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, Secrets. Cluster-scoped: Nodes, PersistentVolumes, ClusterRoles, Namespaces themselves. Run kubectl api-resources --namespaced=true to see the full list.

4.7 Labels and Selectors — The Glue

Labels are key-value pairs attached to objects. Selectors are how you query objects by their labels. This is how Services find their pods, and how Deployments manage their ReplicaSets.

Labels in action 
# Pod labels
metadata:
  labels:
    app: anihpj          # Which application
    tier: backend        # Which tier (frontend/backend/database)
    version: v2          # Which version
    env: production      # Which environment

# Service selector — "route traffic to pods with app=anihpj"
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj

# Deployment selector — "manage pods with app=anihpj"
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
Labels Connect Everything
 🏢 Deployment                  📡 Service
 selector:                     selector:
   app: anihpj  ──────────┐      app: anihpj  ──────────┐
                          │                             │
                          ▼                             ▼
                    ┌──────────┐                  ┌──────────┐
                    │ 📦 Pod    │                  │ 📦 Pod    │
                    │ labels:  │  ← SAME POD! →   │ labels:  │
                    │  app:    │                  │  app:    │
                    │  anihpj  │                  │  anihpj  │
                    └──────────┘                  └──────────┘

   Deployment manages it ──── AND ──── Service routes traffic to it
📌 Two Types of Selectors
  • Equality-based: app=anihpj, tier!=frontend
  • Set-based: tier in (frontend, backend), env notin (test)
terminal — Querying with Labels 
# Show all pods with their labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels

# Filter pods by label
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
kubectl get pods -l "tier in (backend,frontend)"
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,version=v2

# Add a label to an existing pod
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod env=production

# Remove a label (note the dash at the end)
kubectl label pod anihpj-pod env-

# Filter ALL resource types by label
kubectl get all -l app=anihpj
💡 Label Best Practices

Use the recommended labels from the Kubernetes docs for consistency across tools:
app.kubernetes.io/name: anihpj
app.kubernetes.io/version: "2.0"
app.kubernetes.io/component: backend
app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: kubectl

4.8 Annotations — Metadata for Machines

Unlike labels (used for selection), annotations store non-identifying metadata that tools and libraries read. They're not used for querying.

Annotation examples 
metadata:
  annotations:
    # Deployment tracking
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "Updated jobpost views for pagination"
    
    # Build info
    app.kubernetes.io/version: "2.1.0"
    git.commit/sha: "abc123f"
    
    # Ingress configuration
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    
    # Contact info
    team: "backend-team"
    oncall: "dev@example.com"
🏷️ Labels

• Used for selection and grouping
• Must be short (63 chars max value)
• Used by Services, Deployments, kubectl
• Example: app: anihpj

📝 Annotations

• Used for metadata only
• Can be large (256 KB total)
• Used by tools, controllers, humans
• Example: git.commit/sha: "abc123"

4.9 Object Relationships — The Big Picture

Here's how all the core objects connect in a real application like anihpj:

Complete Object Relationship Map for anihpj
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     📁 Namespace: default                             │
│                                                                      │
│   🌐 Ingress ──── routes ────▶ 📡 Service ──── selects ────▶ 📦 Pods  │
