│   (anihpj.com)               (anihpj-svc)               (app=anihpj)│
│   /jobs/ → anihpj-svc:80     ClusterIP                      │       │
│                                                     ┌────────┘       │
│                                                     │                │
│   📝 ConfigMap ──── env ────▶  Pod reads config     │ managed by     │
│   (django-config)             DJANGO_DEBUG=False     │                │
│                                                     │                │
│   🔐 Secret ────── env ────▶  Pod reads secrets     │                │
│   (db-credentials)            DB_PASSWORD=***       │                │
│                                                     │                │
│   💾 PVC ───────── mount ──▶  Pod mounts volume     │                │
│   (media-storage)             /app/media/           │                │
│                                                ┌────┴─────┐         │
│                                                │ReplicaSet│         │
│                                                ├──────────┤         │
│                                                │Deployment│         │
│                                                └──────────┘         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💡 Summary of Chapter 4

Think of it as a chain: Deployment manages a ReplicaSet, which manages Pods. A Service gives pods a stable address. Namespaces organize everything. Labels are the glue that connects objects. ConfigMaps and Secrets inject configuration. This is the foundation for everything else in this guide.

Quick Reference — All Core Objects at a Glance

ObjectapiVersionPurposeCreated By
Podv1Run one or more containersReplicaSet (usually)
ReplicaSetapps/v1Maintain desired pod countDeployment
Deploymentapps/v1Manage rolling updates & rollbacksYou (kubectl apply)
Servicev1Stable networking & load balancingYou (kubectl apply)
Namespacev1Virtual cluster isolationYou or Kubernetes
ConfigMapv1Non-sensitive configurationYou (kubectl apply)
Secretv1Sensitive data (passwords, keys)You (kubectl apply)
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1HTTP/HTTPS routing & TLSYou (kubectl apply)
⏱️ 12 minutes (exam-paced)
Scenario: Core Objects Speed Drill

Complete all tasks using imperative commands. Time yourself — on the CKAD you'll average 6-8 minutes per task:

  1. Create a namespace called ckad-drill
  2. In that namespace, create a pod named web running nginx:1.25 with label app=web
  3. Create a deployment named api with 3 replicas running python:3.11-slim in namespace ckad-drill
  4. Expose the api deployment as a ClusterIP service on port 8000
  5. List all pods in ckad-drill showing labels
  6. Get the YAML of the web pod without connecting to the cluster
📝 Click to reveal solution 
# 1. Create namespace
kubectl create namespace ckad-drill

# 2. Create pod with label
kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 -n ckad-drill --labels=app=web

# 3. Create deployment with 3 replicas
kubectl create deployment api --image=python:3.11-slim --replicas=3 -n ckad-drill

# 4. Expose deployment as ClusterIP
kubectl expose deployment api --port=8000 -n ckad-drill

# 5. List pods with labels
kubectl get pods -n ckad-drill --show-labels

# 6. Generate YAML without cluster (dry-run)
kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 --dry-run=client -o yaml

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# CKAD SCORING: Each task ~2 min, 6 tasks
# Target: Under 12 min = exam-ready pace
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

CKAD Imperative Command Cheat Sheet — Memorize these generators for the exam:

  • kubectl run NAME --image=IMG — Pod
  • kubectl create deployment NAME --image=IMG — Deployment
  • kubectl expose TYPE NAME --port=P — Service
  • kubectl create configmap NAME --from-literal=key=val — ConfigMap
  • kubectl create secret generic NAME --from-literal=key=val — Secret
  • kubectl create namespace NAME — Namespace
  • Add --dry-run=client -o yaml > file.yaml to ANY of the above to generate YAML

Chapter 4 — Practice Questions

Q1
Create a pod named busybox-test using image busybox that runs the command sleep 3600 and outputs the YAML to a file called pod.yaml without actually creating it.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl run busybox-test --image=busybox --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  --command -- sleep 3600 > pod.yaml
Explanation

--dry-run=client validates the resource without creating it. -o yaml outputs YAML format. --command -- separates the command from kubectl flags. This pattern is essential for the CKAD — generate YAML quickly, then edit if needed with vi pod.yaml.

Q2
What is the correct apiVersion for each of these resources: Pod, Deployment, Service, Ingress, NetworkPolicy, CronJob?
Show Answer
Answer
Pod:           v1
Deployment:    apps/v1
Service:       v1
Ingress:       networking.k8s.io/v1
NetworkPolicy: networking.k8s.io/v1
CronJob:       batch/v1
Explanation

Don't memorize all of these — use kubectl api-resources | grep RESOURCE to look them up during the exam. Core resources (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret) use v1. Workloads (Deployment, StatefulSet) use apps/v1. Networking uses networking.k8s.io/v1. Batch uses batch/v1.

Q3
You need to add a label env=production to an existing pod named web. Then list all pods that have the label env=production.
Show Answer
Answer
# Add label
kubectl label pod web env=production

# List pods with that label
kubectl get pods -l env=production

# Or equivalently:
kubectl get pods --selector=env=production
Explanation

Labels are added/modified with kubectl label. To overwrite an existing label, add --overwrite. To remove: kubectl label pod web env- (note the minus sign). The -l flag filters by label selector. You can combine with -l 'env=production,tier=frontend' (AND logic).

Q4
Create a namespace called exam-ns, then create a deployment named web with image nginx:1.25 and 3 replicas in that namespace — all using imperative commands.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl create namespace exam-ns
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=3 -n exam-ns
Explanation

Speed is everything on the CKAD. These two commands take 10 seconds vs. writing YAML which takes 2+ minutes. Always check if a task can be done imperatively first. Verify with: kubectl get deployment web -n exam-ns — ensure READY shows 3/3.

Q5
What is the difference between kubectl apply -f and kubectl create -f? When should you use each?
Show Answer
Answer

kubectl create -f — Creates the resource. Fails if it already exists.
kubectl apply -f — Creates if not exists, updates if it does. Declarative, idempotent.
Use create for one-time creation, apply for ongoing management.

Explanation

On the CKAD, use kubectl apply -f for everything — it's safer because it won't error on re-runs. apply records the last-applied configuration as an annotation, enabling three-way merges. create is purely imperative with no history tracking.

Q6
Write a minimal Pod YAML manifest for a pod named myapp running container nginx:1.25 with port 80 exposed and label app: myapp.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: myapp
  labels:
    app: myapp
spec:
  containers:
  - name: myapp
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
Explanation

This is the most fundamental YAML you must memorize. Key points: containerPort is informational (it doesn't actually open the port — containers listen on whatever port the app uses). Labels are critical because Services and other resources select pods by labels. The container name can be anything but should be descriptive.

Q7
How do you delete all pods with the label tier=test in all namespaces?
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl delete pods -l tier=test --all-namespaces
Explanation

-l filters by label selector. --all-namespaces (or -A) searches all namespaces. Be careful — if these pods are managed by a Deployment/ReplicaSet, they'll be recreated immediately. To actually remove them, delete the controlling resource: kubectl delete deployment NAME.

Q8
You run kubectl get pods and see a pod in CrashLoopBackOff state. What does this mean and how do you debug it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check pod events
kubectl describe pod POD_NAME

# Check application logs
kubectl logs POD_NAME

# Check previous container's logs (if restarted)
kubectl logs POD_NAME --previous
Explanation

CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, K8s restarts it, it crashes again — in an exponential backoff loop. Common causes: wrong command/args, missing environment variables, application errors, wrong image. --previous shows logs from the last crashed instance — crucial since the current container may not have logs yet.

Q9
What is an annotation in Kubernetes? How is it different from a label? Give an example use case for each.
Show Answer
Answer

Labels: key-value pairs used for selection/filtering. Selectors can query them.
Example: app: web (used by Service selectors).

Annotations: key-value pairs for non-identifying metadata. Cannot be used in selectors.
Example: kubernetes.io/change-cause: "Updated to v2" (used by rollout history).

Explanation

Labels are for Kubernetes (selection). Annotations are for humans and tools (metadata). Annotations can hold larger values (up to 256KB). Common annotations: description, version, change-cause, Ingress controller configurations like nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target.

Q10
Generate the YAML for a Deployment named api with image python:3.11-slim and 2 replicas without creating it. Then apply it.
Show Answer
Answer
# Generate YAML
kubectl create deployment api --image=python:3.11-slim \
  --replicas=2 --dry-run=client -o yaml > api-deploy.yaml

# Review and edit if needed
vi api-deploy.yaml

# Apply
kubectl apply -f api-deploy.yaml
Explanation

This is the CKAD workflow pattern: generate → edit → apply. The --dry-run=client -o yaml combo is your best friend on the exam. It creates valid YAML templates in seconds that you can customize with vi. This is much faster than writing YAML from scratch. Practice this flow until it's muscle memory.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Pod SecurityContext Deep Dive

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

SecurityContext — Pod vs Container Level

FieldPod LevelContainer LevelCKS Exam Importance
runAsUser✅✅ (overrides pod)🔴 High
runAsNonRoot✅✅🔴 High
runAsGroup✅✅🟡 Medium
capabilities❌✅ only🔴 High
allowPrivilegeEscalation❌✅ only🔴 High
readOnlyRootFilesystem❌✅ only🟡 Medium
seccompProfile✅✅🔴 High
fsGroup✅ only❌🟡 Medium

Linux Capabilities — CKS Must-Know

# Drop ALL, add only what's needed
securityContext:
  capabilities:
    drop: ["ALL"]
    add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]  # Only if binding to port < 1024

# Dangerous capabilities to NEVER allow:
# SYS_ADMIN  — mount filesystems, load kernel modules
# NET_RAW    — craft raw packets (ARP spoofing)
# SYS_PTRACE — debug/trace other processes
# DAC_OVERRIDE — bypass file permissions

Seccomp and AppArmor Profiles

# Seccomp — restrict syscalls
securityContext:
  seccompProfile:
    type: RuntimeDefault     # Docker/containerd default profile
    # type: Localhost        # Custom profile
    # localhostProfile: profiles/my-profile.json

# AppArmor — annotation-based (container-level)
metadata:
  annotations:
    container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/mycontainer: runtime/default
    # or: localhost/my-custom-profile

Chapter 4 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create a pod that runs as non-root user 1000, drops all capabilities, and uses RuntimeDefault seccomp profile.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: hardened-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx
    - name: run
      mountPath: /var/run
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: run
    emptyDir: {}
Explanation

This is the gold-standard secure pod. readOnlyRootFilesystem prevents file writes (need emptyDir for temp dirs). drop: ["ALL"] removes all Linux capabilities. seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault limits syscalls. On the CKS exam, you'll be asked to create or fix pods to meet these requirements.

CKS-2
A pod is running as root. Identify and fix all security issues:
Show Answer
Answer
# BEFORE (insecure):
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    securityContext:
      privileged: true           # ❌ Full host access
      runAsUser: 0               # ❌ Running as root
      capabilities:
        add: ["SYS_ADMIN"]      # ❌ Dangerous capability

# AFTER (secure):
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25            # ✅ Pinned version
    securityContext:
      privileged: false          # ✅ Not privileged
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # ✅ No escalation
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]            # ✅ All dropped
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true     # ✅ Read-only
Explanation

privileged: true gives the container full host access — equivalent to root on the node. SYS_ADMIN capability allows mounting filesystems and loading kernel modules. The CKS exam will give you insecure pod specs to fix. Always: remove privileged, set runAsNonRoot, drop ALL caps, add seccomp.

CKS-3
Apply an AppArmor profile to a container. What annotation format is used?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: apparmor-pod
  annotations:
    # Format: container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/CONTAINER_NAME: PROFILE
    container.apparmor.security.beta.kubernetes.io/secure-container: runtime/default
spec:
  containers:
  - name: secure-container    # Must match annotation key
    image: nginx:1.25
# Check available AppArmor profiles on node:
ssh node01 "aa-status"

# Load a custom profile:
ssh node01 "apparmor_parser -q /etc/apparmor.d/my-profile"

# Verify pod has AppArmor applied:
kubectl get pod apparmor-pod -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
Explanation

AppArmor is applied via annotations (not spec fields — it's still beta). The annotation key must include the exact container name. Available values: runtime/default, localhost/profile-name, or unconfined. On the CKS exam, you need to know how to apply profiles and verify they're loaded on the node.

CKS-4
What is a seccomp profile and how do you create a custom one?
Show Answer
Answer
// Custom seccomp profile — /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/profiles/audit.json
{
  "defaultAction": "SCMP_ACT_LOG",
  "architectures": ["SCMP_ARCH_X86_64"],
  "syscalls": [
    {
      "names": ["read", "write", "exit", "exit_group", "openat"],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ALLOW"
    },
    {
      "names": ["unshare", "mount", "reboot"],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ERRNO"
    }
  ]
}
# Use in pod spec:
securityContext:
  seccompProfile:
    type: Localhost
    localhostProfile: profiles/audit.json
# Path relative to /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/
Explanation

Seccomp restricts which syscalls a container can make. RuntimeDefault blocks ~44 dangerous syscalls. Custom profiles go in /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/. Actions: SCMP_ACT_ALLOW (permit), SCMP_ACT_ERRNO (deny), SCMP_ACT_LOG (allow + log). On the CKS exam, know how to use both RuntimeDefault and Localhost profiles.

CKS-5
Why is allowPrivilegeEscalation: false critical? What attack does it prevent?
Show Answer
Answer
# Without allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
# A process can gain MORE privileges than its parent:

# Attack: SUID binary exploitation
# 1. Container has a SUID binary (owned by root, setuid bit)
# 2. Normal user runs it → gets root privileges
# 3. Container escape possible via root + other vulns

# With allowPrivilegeEscalation: false:
# - Sets no_new_privs kernel flag
# - SUID bits are ignored
# - Processes cannot gain more privileges

# Always set in container securityContext:
containers:
- name: app
  securityContext:
    allowPrivilegeEscalation: false  # Blocks SUID attacks
    runAsNonRoot: true               # Don't run as root
    capabilities:
      drop: ["ALL"]                  # Remove all capabilities
Explanation

allowPrivilegeEscalation maps to Linux no_new_privs flag. When true (default!), a process can gain privileges via SUID binaries or capabilities. This is a container escape vector. The restricted PSS requires false. On CKS, always set this to false unless you have a specific reason not to.

🛠️ Part 2: Hands-On Chapters 5-8

Chapter 5: Setting Up Your Kubernetes Environment

Time to get your hands dirty. In this chapter you'll install a local Kubernetes cluster, learn to talk to it with kubectl, and run your very first pod — all on your own machine before touching any cloud infrastructure.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~1 hour 🛠️ Hands-On 💻 Local Setup

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Compare 5 local Kubernetes development tools and pick the right one
  • ✅ Install Minikube on Windows, macOS, or Linux
  • ✅ Install and configure kubectl — the K8s command-line tool
  • ✅ Start your first cluster and verify it's running
  • ✅ Deploy and access your first pod (nginx hello-world)
  • ✅ Manage cluster lifecycle (start, stop, pause, delete)
  • ✅ Work with multiple clusters using contexts
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Exam Environment SETUP
The CKAD Exam Environment

The CKAD exam runs in a browser-based terminal with pre-configured clusters. You won't install anything, but understanding the environment is critical:

  • Multiple clusters — You'll switch between clusters using kubectl config use-context
  • Pre-set aliases — k is usually aliased to kubectl
  • Access to docs — You can open kubernetes.io/docs during the exam
  • One terminal — Practice efficient terminal use (tmux-like pane management is available)

⚡ Set up these aliases before every practice session to build muscle memory:

~/.bashrc — CKAD Exam Aliases 
# Essential CKAD aliases
alias k=kubectl
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgd='kubectl get deployments'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdel='kubectl delete'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kl='kubectl logs'
alias kex='kubectl exec -it'

# Fastest way to generate YAML templates
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
# Usage: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml

# Quick namespace switch
alias kn='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'
# Usage: kn my-namespace

# Auto-completion (HUGE time saver)
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k
1
Install Tool

Minikube, kind, k3s, or Docker Desktop

2
Install kubectl

CLI to talk to the cluster

3
Start Cluster

minikube start

4
Verify

kubectl get nodes

5
Deploy First Pod

kubectl run nginx --image=nginx

6
Explore! 🎉

Access your app and experiment

👨‍💻 Your Code anihpj Django app, Dockerfile, YAML manifests
manage.pyDockerfiledeployment.yaml
☸️ Kubernetes Orchestrates containers, manages desired state
kubectlAPI Serveretcd
🐳 Container Runtime Runs containers — containerd, CRI-O, or Docker
containerdCRI-O
🖥️ Your Machine Local laptop or VM running the cluster
Minikube VMDocker driver

5.1 Local Development Options

You don't need a cloud account to learn Kubernetes. Several tools let you run a full cluster right on your laptop. Here's how they compare:

⭐ Minikube

Recommended for beginners

🟢 Beginner
💾 RAM: 2-4 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac / Linux

Full K8s in a VM/container. Addons, dashboard, and multi-node support.

🐳 Docker Desktop

1-Click Enable

🟢 Easiest
💾 RAM: 4 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac

Toggle in Docker settings. Limited config compared to Minikube.

📦 kind

K8s in Docker

🟡 Lightweight
💾 RAM: 1 GB
🖥️ Win / Mac / Linux

Uses containers as nodes. Popular for CI/CD testing.

🔧 k3s

Rancher Lightweight

🟡 Edge / IoT
💾 RAM: 512 MB
🖥️ Linux only

Single binary, production-ready. Great for resource-constrained environments.

🔮 MicroK8s

Canonical Snap

🟡 Linux Focus
💾 RAM: 1 GB
🖥️ Linux (snap)

Snap install, built-in add-ons. Good for Ubuntu users.

Feature ⭐ Minikube Docker Desktop kind k3s MicroK8s
Difficulty🟢 Easy🟢 Easiest🟡 Medium🟡 Medium🟡 Medium
Multi-node✅ Yes❌ No✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes
Dashboard✅ Built-in❌ No❌ No❌ No✅ Add-on
Addons✅ 30+❌ Limited❌ None✅ Helm✅ 20+
CI/CD Use🟡 OK❌ No✅ Best🟡 OK🟡 OK
Production❌ Dev only❌ Dev only❌ Testing✅ Yes✅ Yes
💡 Our Recommendation

For this guide, we use Minikube. It gives you a complete Kubernetes cluster with built-in dashboard, add-ons, and multi-node support — the closest experience to a real production cluster without any cloud spending.

5.2 Installing Minikube

Minikube creates a single-node (or multi-node) Kubernetes cluster on your machine. It can use Docker, VirtualBox, Hyper-V, or other drivers as its backend.

5.2a Prerequisites

🔧 Hardware Requirements
  • 2+ CPUs
  • 2 GB+ free RAM (4 GB recommended)
  • 20 GB+ free disk space
  • Internet connection (for image pulls)
📦 Software Requirements
  • A container/VM runtime (pick one):
  • 🐳 Docker (recommended)
  • 📦 VirtualBox
  • 🖥️ Hyper-V (Windows Pro/Enterprise)

5.2b Windows Installation

PowerShell (Administrator) 
# Option 1: Using Chocolatey
choco install minikube

# Option 2: Using Scoop
scoop install minikube

# Option 3: Direct download from GitHub
# Download the latest .exe from:
# https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube/releases/latest
# Move minikube.exe to a folder in your PATH

# Verify installation
minikube version
# output: minikube version: v1.34.0

5.2c macOS Installation

Terminal 
# Option 1: Using Homebrew (recommended)
brew install minikube

# Option 2: Direct download (Apple Silicon / M1+)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-darwin-arm64
sudo install minikube-darwin-arm64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Option 2b: Direct download (Intel)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-darwin-amd64
sudo install minikube-darwin-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Verify
minikube version

5.2d Linux Installation

Terminal 
# Option 1: Direct download (x86_64)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-linux-amd64
sudo install minikube-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/minikube

# Option 2: Using apt (Debian/Ubuntu)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube_latest_amd64.deb
sudo dpkg -i minikube_latest_amd64.deb

# Option 3: Using rpm (Fedora/RHEL)
curl -LO https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube-latest.x86_64.rpm
sudo rpm -Uvh minikube-latest.x86_64.rpm

# Verify
minikube version

5.2e Verifying Installation

Terminal — Expected Output 
$ minikube version
minikube version: v1.34.0
commit: 210b148df93a80eb872ecbeb7e35281b3c582c61

$ which minikube        # macOS/Linux
$ where minikube        # Windows
C:\ProgramData\chocolatey\bin\minikube.exe
📝 Verification Checklist

If minikube version prints a version number, you're good! If you get "command not found", make sure the install directory is in your system PATH environment variable.

5.3 Installing kubectl

kubectl (pronounced "kube-control" or "kube-C-T-L") is the command-line tool you use to communicate with any Kubernetes cluster — local or cloud.

How kubectl Connects to a Cluster

  ┌──────────────┐         ┌───────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
  │  👨‍💻 You       │  ──▶   │  kubectl CLI  │  ──▶   │  API Server      │
  │  (terminal)  │  HTTP   │  reads        │  REST   │  (control plane) │
  │              │         │  ~/.kube/     │  API    │                  │
  └──────────────┘         │  config       │         │  ┌────────────┐  │
                            └───────────────┘         │  │ etcd       │  │
                                                      │  │ scheduler  │  │
                                                      │  │ controller │  │
                                                      │  └────────────┘  │
                                                      └──────────────────┘
Installing kubectl — All Platforms 
# ── Windows ──
choco install kubernetes-cli          # Chocolatey
scoop install kubectl                 # Scoop

# ── macOS ──
brew install kubectl                  # Homebrew

# ── Linux (Debian/Ubuntu) ──
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y kubectl

# ── Verify (all platforms) ──
kubectl version --client
# Client Version: v1.31.0
💡 kubectl Is Separate From Your Cluster

kubectl doesn't run Kubernetes — it's just the CLI tool that talks to a cluster. You can use the same kubectl binary to connect to Minikube, EKS, GKE, AKS, or any cluster. It reads ~/.kube/config to know which cluster to target.

Shell Completion (optional but very useful) 
# Bash
echo 'source <(kubectl completion bash)' >> ~/.bashrc

# Zsh
echo 'source <(kubectl completion zsh)' >> ~/.zshrc

# PowerShell
kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

5.4 Starting Your First Cluster

With Minikube and kubectl installed, let's spin up your first Kubernetes cluster:

OptionDefaultDescriptionExample
--driverdockerVM/container runtimeminikube start --driver=hyperv
--cpus2Number of CPUsminikube start --cpus=4
--memory2200mbRAM allocationminikube start --memory=4096
--disk-size20000mbDisk spaceminikube start --disk-size=40g
--kubernetes-versionlatest stableK8s version to installminikube start --kubernetes-version=v1.30.0
--nodes1Multi-node clusterminikube start --nodes=3
-pminikubeProfile/cluster nameminikube start -p dev-cluster
Starting your cluster 
# Start a cluster (uses Docker driver by default)
$ minikube start
😄  minikube v1.34.0 on Darwin 14.0 (arm64)
✨  Using the docker driver based on existing profile
👍  Starting "minikube" primary control-plane node
🐳  Preparing Kubernetes v1.31.0 on Docker 27.2.0 ...
🔎  Verifying Kubernetes components...
🌟  Enabled addons: storage-provisioner, default-storageclass
🏄  Done! kubectl is now configured to use "minikube" cluster

# Check cluster status
$ minikube status
minikube
type: Control Plane
host: Running
kubelet: Running
apiserver: Running
kubeconfig: Configured

# Cluster details
$ kubectl cluster-info
Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:55000
CoreDNS is running at https://127.0.0.1:55000/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system/services/kube-dns:dns/proxy

# See your node
$ kubectl get nodes
NAME       STATUS   ROLES           AGE   VERSION
minikube   Ready    control-plane   45s   v1.31.0

What Just Happened

  minikube start
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. Creates a VM or Docker container         │
  │  2. Installs Kubernetes inside it            │
  │  3. Starts the control plane components:     │
  │     • API Server    • etcd                   │
  │     • Scheduler     • Controller Manager     │
  │  4. Configures kubectl (~/.kube/config)       │
  │  5. Enables default add-ons                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
  ✅ You now have a running Kubernetes cluster!
Open the Dashboard (Web UI) 
# Open Kubernetes Dashboard in your browser
$ minikube dashboard
🔌  Enabling dashboard ...
🤔  Verifying dashboard health ...
🚀  Launching proxy ...
🤔  Verifying proxy health ...
🎉  Opening http://127.0.0.1:57683/api/v1/namespaces/kubernetes-dashboard/...
📝 The Dashboard

The Kubernetes Dashboard is a web-based UI that shows all running workloads, services, and cluster health. It's a great way to visually verify what's happening while you learn the CLI commands.

5.5 Your First Pod — Hello World

Before we deploy anihpj, let's run a quick hello-world to prove your cluster works:

Running your first pod 
# Create a single pod running nginx
$ kubectl run my-first-pod --image=nginx
pod/my-first-pod created

# Watch it come to life
$ kubectl get pods -w
NAME           READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
my-first-pod   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          3s
my-first-pod   1/1     Running             0          8s

# Forward port so you can access it from your browser
$ kubectl port-forward my-first-pod 8080:80
Forwarding from 127.0.0.1:8080 -> 80

Now open http://localhost:8080 in your browser — you'll see the nginx welcome page!

🎉 Congratulations!

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                               │
  │   You just ran your first Kubernetes Pod!     │
  │                                               │
  │   kubectl run  ──▶  Pod Created  ──▶  🌐      │
  │                                               │
  │   nginx is running inside a container,        │
  │   inside a pod, on your Minikube cluster.     │
  │                                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Exploring your pod 
# Detailed info about the pod
$ kubectl describe pod my-first-pod

# View pod logs
$ kubectl logs my-first-pod

# Shell into the running container
$ kubectl exec -it my-first-pod -- /bin/bash
root@my-first-pod:/# curl localhost
# outputs the nginx HTML

# Clean up when done
$ kubectl delete pod my-first-pod
pod "my-first-pod" deleted

5.6 Cluster Lifecycle Commands

Minikube gives you full control over your local cluster. Here are the commands you'll use daily:

Command What It Does When to Use
minikube startCreates or starts the clusterBeginning of your work session
minikube stopStops the cluster (preserves state)End of your work session
minikube pausePauses K8s processes (saves CPU)Taking a break but keeping state
minikube unpauseResumes paused clusterReturning from a break
minikube deleteDestroys the cluster entirelyStarting fresh or cleaning up
minikube statusShows cluster healthChecking if cluster is running
minikube dashboardOpens web dashboardVisual cluster inspection
minikube addons listShows available add-onsEnabling extras (metrics, ingress)
💡 Daily Workflow

Start your day with minikube start, do your work, then minikube stop when done. Your deployments, services, and config are preserved between stop/start cycles. Only minikube delete destroys everything.

AddonWhat It DoesEnable Command
dashboardWeb-based cluster management UIminikube addons enable dashboard
metrics-serverCPU/memory metrics for kubectl topminikube addons enable metrics-server
ingressNGINX Ingress Controller for HTTP routingminikube addons enable ingress
storage-provisionerDynamic PersistentVolume provisioningminikube addons enable storage-provisioner
registryPrivate container image registryminikube addons enable registry

5.7 Multiple Clusters and Contexts

As you advance, you'll work with multiple clusters (dev, staging, production). Kubernetes uses a kubeconfig file to manage connections.

The kubeconfig File (~/.kube/config)

  ~/.kube/config
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  clusters:           ◄── Where to connect            │
  │    - minikube  →  https://127.0.0.1:55000            │
  │    - prod-eks  →  https://eks.amazonaws.com/...      │
  │    - dev-gke   →  https://gke.googleapis.com/...     │
  │                                                      │
  │  users:              ◄── How to authenticate          │
  │    - minikube-user   (client certificate)            │
  │    - aws-admin       (token)                         │
  │    - gke-dev         (oauth)                         │
  │                                                      │
  │  contexts:           ◄── cluster + user combos        │
  │    - minikube  =  minikube cluster + minikube-user   │
  │    - prod      =  prod-eks cluster + aws-admin       │
  │    - dev       =  dev-gke cluster  + gke-dev         │
  │                                                      │
  │  current-context: minikube  ◄── active right now     │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Managing Contexts 
# List all contexts
$ kubectl config get-contexts
CURRENT   NAME       CLUSTER    AUTHINFO        NAMESPACE
*         minikube   minikube   minikube-user   default

# See current context
$ kubectl config current-context
minikube

# Switch to a different context
$ kubectl config use-context prod
Switched to context "prod".

# Set default namespace for a context
$ kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-app
⚠️ Always Check Your Context Before Running Commands

The most common Kubernetes mistake is running commands against the wrong cluster. Before running any destructive command (delete, scale to 0, etc.), always verify: kubectl config current-context. Some teams protect production by requiring explicit --context=prod flags.

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Environment Setup Speed Run

⏱️ Target: 3 minutes

You've just started the CKAD exam. Complete these setup tasks as fast as possible:

  1. Set up the alias k=kubectl and enable bash completion for it
  2. Create a variable do="--dry-run=client -o yaml" for quick YAML generation
  3. List all available contexts and switch to context named k8s-cluster2
  4. Set the default namespace to ckad-prep for the current context
  5. Verify you're on the right cluster and namespace
💡 Hint: kubectl completion bash, export, kubectl config subcommands
View Solution 
# 1. Alias + completion
alias k=kubectl
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# 2. Dry-run variable
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"

# 3. List contexts and switch
kubectl config get-contexts
kubectl config use-context k8s-cluster2

# 4. Set default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=ckad-prep

# 5. Verify
kubectl config current-context
kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace
Context Switching — The #1 Exam Mistake

Every CKAD question tells you which context to use. Forgetting to switch = wrong cluster = zero marks. Build this habit:

# ALWAYS run this FIRST for every question
kubectl config use-context <context-name>

# Then verify
kubectl config current-context

Chapter 5 — Practice Questions

Q1
You need to set up a local Kubernetes environment for CKAD practice. Compare the three most popular local K8s tools and explain which one the CKAD exam environment most closely resembles.
Show Answer
Answer
ToolNodesRuntimeBest For
minikubeSingle (multi optional)Docker / VMFeature-rich local dev
kindMulti-node in DockerDocker containersCI pipelines, quick clusters
k3sMulti-node, lightweightNative binaryEdge, production-lite

The CKAD exam uses kubeadm-based clusters, which is closest to a full cluster (not minikube). However, kind or minikube with multi-node is best for practice.

Explanation

The CKAD exam provides pre-configured clusters accessed via kubectl config use-context. You don't install or manage clusters during the exam. For practice, use any tool that gives you a working kubectl connection. minikube start is the fastest way to get started.

Q2
After installing minikube, you run kubectl get nodes and get "The connection to the server localhost:8080 was refused". What's wrong and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer

The kubeconfig is not set up or minikube isn't running. Fix:

# Start minikube first
minikube start

# Verify kubeconfig is set
kubectl config current-context
# Should show: minikube

# If still failing, check KUBECONFIG env var
echo $KUBECONFIG
# Should point to ~/.kube/config
Explanation

The error localhost:8080 means kubectl has no valid kubeconfig and falls back to the default. This happens when: (1) minikube isn't started, (2) ~/.kube/config doesn't exist, or (3) KUBECONFIG env var points to a wrong path. On the CKAD, clusters are pre-configured — but you must switch contexts correctly.

Q3
What are the three components of a kubeconfig file? Write a command to view them.
Show Answer
Answer
# View full kubeconfig
kubectl config view

# The three components:
# 1. clusters   — API server endpoints + CA certs
# 2. users      — Authentication credentials
# 3. contexts   — Binds a cluster + user + namespace together
Explanation

A context = cluster + user + (optional) namespace. The CKAD exam has multiple contexts (one per question cluster). Always verify your context before answering: kubectl config current-context. The kubeconfig lives at ~/.kube/config by default.

Q4
You have two clusters configured: dev-cluster and prod-cluster. Write the commands to: (a) list all contexts, (b) switch to dev-cluster, (c) set the default namespace for that context to app-ns.
Show Answer
Answer
# (a) List all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts

# (b) Switch to dev-cluster
kubectl config use-context dev-cluster

# (c) Set default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=app-ns
Explanation

On the CKAD, every question specifies which context to use. Forgetting to switch context is the #1 exam mistake. Setting the namespace with set-context --current --namespace= saves you from typing -n namespace on every command. Always verify after switching.

Q5
What is the purpose of minikube addons? Enable the metrics-server addon and verify it's running.
Show Answer
Answer
# List available addons
minikube addons list

# Enable metrics-server
minikube addons enable metrics-server

# Verify it's running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
kubectl top nodes  # Should work after ~60s
Explanation

Minikube addons extend your local cluster with optional components. metrics-server is critical for CKAD because it powers kubectl top and HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler). Without it, kubectl top pods returns "Metrics API not available". The CKAD exam clusters have metrics-server pre-installed.

Q6
You want to access a web application running inside minikube from your browser. What are the two main ways to expose it, and which one creates a tunnel?
Show Answer
Answer

Method 1: minikube service (creates a tunnel)

minikube service my-service --url

Method 2: kubectl port-forward (direct forwarding)

kubectl port-forward svc/my-service 8080:80

minikube service creates a tunnel from host to the NodePort. port-forward directly proxies through the API server.

Explanation

On the CKAD exam, you won't use minikube service (it's a minikube-specific command). You'll use kubectl port-forward or create Services of type NodePort. Know both methods for practice, but focus on kubectl port-forward for exam-style tasks, as it works on any cluster.

Q7
What is minikube ssh and when would you use it? Give an example of debugging a networking issue using it.
Show Answer
Answer
# SSH into the minikube node
minikube ssh

# Inside the node — debug networking
docker ps                          # See containers
curl http://10.96.0.1:443 -k      # Test cluster IP
iptables -t nat -L | grep my-svc  # Check service rules
cat /etc/resolv.conf               # Check DNS config
exit
Explanation

minikube ssh drops you into the underlying node. It's useful for debugging at the node level — checking container runtime, network rules (iptables), storage mounts, etc. On the CKAD, you may be asked to troubleshoot from within a pod using kubectl exec, which is the equivalent technique on real clusters.

Q8
What is the KUBECONFIG environment variable? How do you merge multiple kubeconfig files?
Show Answer
Answer
# Set KUBECONFIG to multiple files (merged automatically)
# Linux/Mac:
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/cluster2-config

# Windows PowerShell:
$env:KUBECONFIG="$HOME\.kube\config;$HOME\.kube\cluster2-config"

# Verify merged contexts
kubectl config get-contexts

# Permanently merge into one file
kubectl config view --flatten > ~/.kube/merged-config
Explanation

The CKAD exam pre-configures KUBECONFIG for you with all cluster credentials. Understanding how kubeconfig merging works helps if you practice with multiple clusters locally. The colon-separated list (semicolon on Windows) tells kubectl to merge all files into a unified view.

Q9
You're setting up your CKAD exam environment. List five kubectl aliases or shell configurations you should set up in the first 60 seconds of the exam.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Short alias for kubectl
alias k=kubectl

# 2. Enable bash completion
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# 3. Dry-run shortcut
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"

# 4. Force delete shortcut
export now="--force --grace-period=0"

# 5. Default editor
export KUBE_EDITOR=nano   # or vim
Explanation

These aliases can save 5-10 minutes over a 2-hour exam. With them, creating a pod YAML becomes: k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml. Bash completion lets you tab-complete resource names. Practice these so they're muscle memory on exam day. The exam provides a pre-configured terminal but you still need to set up your own shortcuts.

Q10
You run minikube start and get an error: "Exiting due to PROVIDER_DOCKER_NOT_RUNNING". What does this mean and how do you fix it on different operating systems?
Show Answer
Answer

Docker Desktop (or the Docker daemon) is not running. minikube needs a container runtime.

# Linux — start Docker daemon
sudo systemctl start docker

# Mac/Windows — open Docker Desktop app
# Then retry:
minikube start --driver=docker

# Alternative: use a different driver
minikube start --driver=virtualbox
minikube start --driver=hyperv      # Windows
Explanation

minikube uses a "driver" to create the cluster node. The docker driver (default) runs the node as a Docker container, so Docker must be running. If you can't use Docker, alternative drivers include VirtualBox, Hyper-V, or Podman. On the CKAD exam, you never deal with cluster setup — but knowing this helps for practice environments.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — CIS Benchmarks & Secure Cluster Setup

CLUSTER SETUP 15%

CIS Kubernetes Benchmark

The CIS (Center for Internet Security) Benchmark is the industry standard for Kubernetes security hardening. The CKS exam tests your ability to run and interpret these benchmarks.

kube-bench — CIS Benchmark Scanner

# Run kube-bench on master node
kube-bench run --targets=master

# Run on worker node
kube-bench run --targets=node

# Run specific check
kube-bench run --targets=master --check=1.2.6

# Run as a Kubernetes Job
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench/main/job-master.yaml
kubectl logs job/kube-bench

Key CIS Checks to Know

Check IDDescriptionFix
1.2.6--kubelet-certificate-authority setAdd to API server manifest
1.2.16Admission plugin NodeRestriction enabled--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction
1.2.18--insecure-bind-address not setRemove from manifest
1.2.20--audit-log-path setAdd audit log path flag
4.2.1Kubelet anonymous auth disabled--anonymous-auth=false
4.2.2Kubelet authorization mode not AlwaysAllow--authorization-mode=Webhook

Chapter 5 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Run kube-bench against the master node and fix a failing check for API server audit logging.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Run kube-bench
kube-bench run --targets=master --check=1.2.20,1.2.21,1.2.22

# Output shows FAIL for audit logging:
# [FAIL] 1.2.20 Ensure that the --audit-log-path argument is set

# 2. Fix — edit API server manifest:
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml

# Add to .spec.containers[0].command:
- --audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
- --audit-log-maxage=30
- --audit-log-maxbackup=10
- --audit-log-maxsize=100
- --audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml

# 3. Mount the audit log directory:
# Add volumeMount and volume for /var/log/kubernetes

# 4. Create basic audit policy:
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: Metadata

# 5. Wait for API server to restart, then re-run kube-bench
kube-bench run --targets=master --check=1.2.20
Explanation

kube-bench checks CIS Kubernetes Benchmark compliance. Audit logging is commonly tested on CKS. You must add flags to the API server static pod manifest AND create an audit policy file. The API server auto-restarts as a static pod when the manifest changes.

CKS-2
A kubeadm cluster was set up with insecure defaults. Identify and fix 3 security issues.
Show Answer
Answer
# Issue 1: Anonymous auth enabled on kubelet
# Fix on EACH worker node:
vi /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Set: authentication.anonymous.enabled: false
# Set: authorization.mode: Webhook
systemctl restart kubelet

# Issue 2: API server allows anonymous access
vi /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
# Add: --anonymous-auth=false

# Issue 3: No encryption at rest
# Create EncryptionConfiguration, add flag:
# --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml

# Verify all fixes:
kube-bench run --targets=master,node
Explanation

Default kubeadm installations have several insecure defaults. The CKS exam expects you to know where config files are: API server manifest at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml, kubelet config at /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml. After fixing kubelet config, restart it with systemctl restart kubelet.

CKS-3
How do you verify that the Kubernetes cluster uses TLS for all component communication?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check API server TLS certs
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | grep -E "tls|cert|key|ca"
# Should have: --tls-cert-file, --tls-private-key-file, --client-ca-file

# 2. Check etcd TLS
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml | grep -E "cert|key|ca"
# Should have: --cert-file, --key-file, --trusted-ca-file

# 3. Check kubelet-to-apiserver
cat /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml | grep -A2 "tlsCertFile\|tlsPrivateKeyFile"

# 4. Verify no insecure ports
netstat -tlnp | grep -E "8080|10255"
# Port 8080 (insecure API) should NOT be listening
# Port 10255 (kubelet read-only) should NOT be listening

# 5. Check certificate expiry
kubeadm certs check-expiration
openssl x509 -in /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver.crt -noout -dates
Explanation

All Kubernetes components should communicate over TLS. On CKS, verify: API server serves HTTPS (6443), etcd uses peer/client TLS (2379/2380), kubelet uses authenticated HTTPS (10250). Insecure ports (8080, 10255) must be disabled. Check certificate expiry regularly.

CKS-4
What is the NodeRestriction admission plugin and why is it important?
Show Answer
Answer
# Enable NodeRestriction in API server:
--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction

# What it does:
# - Limits what kubelets can modify
# - Kubelet can ONLY modify pods bound to its own node
# - Kubelet can ONLY modify its own Node object
# - Kubelet CANNOT modify pods on other nodes
# - Kubelet CANNOT modify labels with node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix

# Without NodeRestriction:
# A compromised kubelet could:
# 1. Modify pods on other nodes
# 2. Add labels to attract workloads
# 3. Escalate privileges cluster-wide

# Verify it's enabled:
kubectl exec -n kube-system kube-apiserver-master -- \
  kube-apiserver --help | grep admission-plugins
# Or check the pod spec:
kubectl get pod -n kube-system kube-apiserver-master -o yaml | grep admission
Explanation

NodeRestriction is a critical admission plugin that limits kubelet permissions. Without it, a compromised worker node could manipulate the entire cluster. CIS Benchmark check 1.2.16 requires it. On the CKS exam, know how to verify and enable admission plugins in the API server manifest.

CKS-5
How do you secure a kubeadm cluster during initial setup vs after deployment?
Show Answer
Answer
# During setup — secure kubeadm config:
apiVersion: kubeadm.k8s.io/v1beta3
kind: ClusterConfiguration
apiServer:
  extraArgs:
    anonymous-auth: "false"
    enable-admission-plugins: "NodeRestriction,PodSecurity"
    audit-log-path: "/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log"
    audit-policy-file: "/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml"
    encryption-provider-config: "/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml"
etcd:
  local:
    extraArgs:
      client-cert-auth: "true"
# After deployment — fix file permissions:
# CIS requires specific permissions on key files:
chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/etcd.yaml
chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt
chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key
chown root:root /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf

# Rotate certificates:
kubeadm certs renew all
Explanation

It's easier to secure at setup time using kubeadm config. Post-deployment, you must edit static pod manifests and fix file permissions. CIS Benchmark checks file permissions on all manifests and PKI files. On CKS, you may need to fix permissions using chmod and chown.

Chapter 6: kubectl — Your Command Center

kubectl is the single most important tool in your Kubernetes toolkit. Every operation — creating pods, scaling deployments, debugging failures — goes through this CLI. Master it, and you master Kubernetes.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 🛠️ Hands-On ⌨️ CLI Mastery

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The kubectl command syntax and how every command is structured
  • ✅ Imperative vs Declarative — two approaches and when to use each
  • ✅ 30+ essential commands organized by category
  • ✅ Output formats (-o wide, -o yaml, -o json, jsonpath)
  • ✅ Resource abbreviations to speed up your workflow
  • ✅ Filtering with labels, fields, and namespaces
  • ✅ Practice exercises using the anihpj project
🎓 CKAD Relevance
ALL DOMAINS CRITICAL Observability & Maintenance 15%
kubectl IS the CKAD Exam

Every single CKAD question requires kubectl. The difference between passing and failing is how fast you can type commands. Key exam patterns:

  • Imperative > Declarative for speed — Use kubectl run, kubectl create, kubectl expose to generate resources instantly
  • Generate, don't write — kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
  • kubectl explain is your cheat sheet — kubectl explain pod.spec.containers shows all fields with descriptions
  • Output formatting saves time — -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' for quick extraction
  • Resource abbreviations — po, deploy, svc, ns, cm, sa, pv, pvc, netpol, ing

The 20 kubectl Commands That Cover 90% of the Exam

CategoryCommandWhen to Use
Createkubectl run nginx --image=nginxQuick pod creation
Createkubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3Deployment with replicas
Createkubectl create cm myconfig --from-literal=k=vConfigMap from literal
Createkubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=pass=123Secret from literal
Createkubectl create sa mysaServiceAccount
Createkubectl create ns mynsNamespace
Createkubectl create job myjob --image=busybox -- echo helloJob
Createkubectl create cronjob mycj --image=busybox --schedule="*/1 * * * *" -- echo hiCronJob
Exposekubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --type=ClusterIPService for deployment
Exposekubectl expose pod nginx --port=80 --name=nginx-svcService for pod
Scalekubectl scale deploy web --replicas=5Scale replicas
Updatekubectl set image deploy/web web=nginx:1.26Rolling update
Rollbackkubectl rollout undo deploy/webUndo last change
Debugkubectl describe pod nginxDetailed status + events
Debugkubectl logs nginx -c sidecarContainer logs
Debugkubectl exec -it nginx -- shShell into container
Labelkubectl label pod nginx env=prodAdd/update labels
Annotatekubectl annotate pod nginx desc="web server"Add annotations
Editkubectl edit deploy webEdit live resource in vim
Deletekubectl delete pod nginx --force --grace-period=0Fast deletion

kubectl Output Format Traps

  • JSONPath queries — Always wrap in single quotes: -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
  • ❌ kubectl get pods -o jsonpath="{.items}" → fails on many shells (double quotes conflict)
  • ✅ kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
  • Custom columns — Great for quick tables: kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,IMAGE:.spec.containers[0].image
  • Sort by — kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp (useful for finding latest pod)
  • -o wide — Shows NODE and IP columns. Use when question asks "which node" or "pod IP"
  • Field selectors — kubectl get pods --field-selector=status.phase=Running (filter by status)

Label Selectors — The Glue of Kubernetes

Labels connect everything: Services → Pods, Deployments → ReplicaSets, NetworkPolicies → target Pods. Master these selector patterns:

# List pods with specific label
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx
kubectl get pods -l 'app in (nginx, web)'
kubectl get pods -l app=nginx,env=prod        # AND logic
kubectl get pods -l 'app notin (test,dev)'     # NOT IN
kubectl get pods -l '!env'                     # Label key does NOT exist

# Show labels as columns
kubectl get pods --show-labels
kubectl get pods -L app,env                    # Show specific labels as columns

# Add/update/remove labels
kubectl label pod nginx env=prod               # Add label
kubectl label pod nginx env=staging --overwrite # Update
kubectl label pod nginx env-                   # Remove label (note the minus)

# Use labels to select for deletion
kubectl delete pods -l env=test                # Delete all test pods

kubectl Command Categories at a Glance

➕
Create run, create, apply
👁️
Read get, describe, logs
✏️
Update edit, set, patch, label
🗑️
Delete delete, prune
🔍
Debug exec, port-forward, top
📐
Scale scale, autoscale
🔄
Rollout rollout status/undo/history
⚙️
Config config, cluster-info
🐢 Imperative (Ad-hoc)

Run commands directly. Fast for quick tasks and the CKAD exam.

  • kubectl run nginx --image=nginx
  • kubectl create deploy web --image=nginx
  • kubectl scale deploy web --replicas=5

Best for: Exam, quick tests, one-off tasks

📝 Declarative (YAML)

Define desired state in files. Best for production and version control.

  • kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
  • kubectl apply -f ./manifests/
  • kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml

Best for: Production, GitOps, team workflows

40+
kubectl commands
20
cover 90% of exam
12
resource abbreviations
5
output formats

6.1 kubectl Syntax Structure

Every kubectl command follows the same pattern. Once you learn this structure, you can construct any command from memory:

Command Anatomy

  kubectl       get        pods      anihpj-pod     -n prod
  ──┬──        ─┬─        ─┬──      ───┬─────     ──┬───
    │           │          │          │             │
  🔵 CLI     🟢 VERB    🟡 TYPE    🟠 NAME      🔴 FLAGS
  (tool)    (action)   (resource)  (which one)   (options)
🟢 Common Verbs (Actions)
  • get — list resources
  • describe — detailed info
  • create — create new
  • apply — create or update
  • delete — remove
  • edit — edit live
  • logs — container logs
  • exec — run command in pod
🟡 Common Types (Resources)
  • pods (po) — running containers
  • services (svc) — networking
  • deployments (deploy) — workloads
  • namespaces (ns) — isolation
  • configmaps (cm) — config
  • secrets — sensitive data
  • nodes (no) — cluster machines
  • ingress (ing) — HTTP routing
Pattern in Action — Examples 
# kubectl  VERB     TYPE         NAME            FLAGS
kubectl    get      pods                                      # all pods
kubectl    get      pod          anihpj-pod                   # specific pod
kubectl    get      pods                         -n production # in namespace
kubectl    get      pods                         -o wide       # extra columns
kubectl    describe deployment   anihpj-deploy                # full details
kubectl    delete   service      anihpj-svc                   # remove service
kubectl    logs                  anihpj-pod-abc                # view logs
kubectl    exec     -it          anihpj-pod-abc  -- /bin/bash  # shell in

6.2 Imperative vs Declarative

There are two fundamental ways to work with Kubernetes. Understanding the difference is crucial:

🗣️ Imperative — "Do This Now"

You tell Kubernetes exactly what action to take, step by step.

# Create a pod directly
kubectl run anihpj --image=anihpj:v1

# Scale a deployment
kubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5

# Expose a service
kubectl expose deploy anihpj --port=8000

✅ Pros: Quick, great for learning & debugging

❌ Cons: No version control, not reproducible, hard to track

📄 Declarative — "Make It Like This" ⭐

You describe the desired state in a YAML file, and K8s figures out how to get there.

# Apply from a file
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Apply an entire directory
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# Update (just change YAML and re-apply)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

✅ Pros: Version controlled, reproducible, team-friendly

❌ Cons: More setup, need to learn YAML

💡 Rule of Thumb

Use imperative commands for quick tests and debugging. Use declarative YAML files for anything that goes to production. In this guide we'll learn both, but all our anihpj deployment files will be declarative.

6.3 Essential kubectl Commands — Categorized

Here are the commands you'll use 90% of the time, organized by what you're trying to do:

6.3a 🔍 Viewing / Inspecting

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl get <resource>List resourceskubectl get pods
kubectl get allList pods, services, deployments, etc.kubectl get all -n anihpj-app
kubectl describe <resource> <name>Detailed info with eventskubectl describe pod anihpj-abc
kubectl logs <pod>View container outputkubectl logs anihpj-abc -f
kubectl logs <pod> --previousLogs from crashed containerkubectl logs anihpj-abc --previous
kubectl top podsCPU/memory usagekubectl top pods -n anihpj-app
kubectl get eventsCluster event logkubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

6.3b ➕ Creating / Applying

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl create -f <file>Create from YAML (fails if exists)kubectl create -f pod.yaml
kubectl apply -f <file>Create or update from YAMLkubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f <dir>/Apply all files in directorykubectl apply -f k8s/
kubectl run <name> --image=<img>Quick pod (imperative)kubectl run nginx --image=nginx
kubectl create deploy <name> --image=<img>Quick deployment (imperative)kubectl create deploy anihpj --image=anihpj:v1
📝 create vs apply

create fails if the resource already exists. apply creates if missing, updates if it exists. For declarative workflows, always use apply.

kubectl apply Lifecycle

  kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml
           │
           ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐
  │ Resource exists? │────▶│ YES → Update  │
  └──────────────────┘     │ (patch diff)  │
           │               └───────────────┘
           ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │ NO → Create      │
  │ (new resource)   │
  └──────────────────┘

6.3c ✏️ Modifying

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl edit <resource> <name>Edit resource live in your editorkubectl edit deploy anihpj
kubectl scale deploy <name> --replicas=NChange replica countkubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5
kubectl set image deploy/<n> <c>=<img>Update container imagekubectl set image deploy/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2
kubectl label <resource> <name> key=valAdd or update a labelkubectl label pod anihpj-abc env=prod
kubectl annotate <resource> <name> k=vAdd an annotationkubectl annotate pod anihpj-abc note="testing"
kubectl patch <resource> <name> -p '...'Partial update via JSON patchkubectl patch deploy anihpj -p '{"spec":{"replicas":3}}'

6.3d ❌ Deleting

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl delete <resource> <name>Delete specific resourcekubectl delete pod anihpj-abc
kubectl delete -f <file>Delete everything defined in filekubectl delete -f deployment.yaml
kubectl delete pods --allDelete all pods in namespacekubectl delete pods --all -n anihpj-app
kubectl delete ns <namespace>Delete namespace (and everything in it!)kubectl delete ns anihpj-app
🚨 Danger Zone

kubectl delete ns <name> deletes the namespace and every resource inside it — pods, services, deployments, secrets, everything. There is no undo. Always double-check with kubectl config current-context first.

6.3e 🔧 Debugging

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bashShell into a running containerkubectl exec -it anihpj-abc -- /bin/bash
kubectl exec <pod> -- <cmd>Run a single commandkubectl exec anihpj-abc -- python manage.py check
kubectl port-forward <pod> local:containerForward port to localhostkubectl port-forward anihpj-abc 8000:8000
kubectl cp <pod>:/path ./localCopy files from podkubectl cp anihpj-abc:/app/db.sqlite3 ./backup.db
kubectl attach <pod>Attach to a running processkubectl attach anihpj-abc
kubectl debug <pod>Create debug containerkubectl debug anihpj-abc -it --image=busybox

Debugging Decision Tree

  Pod not working?
       │
       ├── Not starting? ──▶ kubectl describe pod <name>
       │                      Look at "Events" section
       │
       ├── Crashing? ──────▶ kubectl logs <pod> --previous
       │                      See crash output
       │
       ├── Running but       kubectl logs <pod> -f
       │   wrong output? ──▶ Stream live logs
       │
       ├── Need to inspect   kubectl exec -it <pod> -- /bin/bash
       │   inside? ────────▶ Explore filesystem, test commands
       │
       └── Network issue? ──▶ kubectl port-forward <pod> 8000:8000
                              Test directly, bypass Service

6.3f 📊 Cluster Admin

CommandWhat It DoesExample
kubectl cluster-infoAPI server & CoreDNS endpointskubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodesList all cluster nodeskubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get eventsRecent cluster eventskubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl api-resourcesAll available resource typeskubectl api-resources --namespaced=true
kubectl api-versionsAll API versions availablekubectl api-versions
kubectl explain <resource>Built-in docs for any resourcekubectl explain pod.spec.containers
💡 kubectl explain Is Your Best Friend

kubectl explain is a built-in reference. Use it to discover every field of any resource without leaving the terminal:
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources

6.4 kubectl Output Formats

By default, kubectl shows a simple table. But you can extract much more detail:

FlagOutputWhen to Use
(default)Simple tableQuick overview
-o wideTable + extra columns (node, IP)See which node a pod runs on
-o yamlFull YAML definitionExport or inspect full resource
-o jsonFull JSON definitionPipe to jq for parsing
-o nameJust resource namesScripting & piping
-o jsonpath='{...}'Custom field extractionExtract specific values
-o custom-columns=...Custom table columnsBuild your own table view
Same Pod — Different Formats 
# Default — simple table
$ kubectl get pods
NAME           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
anihpj-abc     1/1     Running   0          5m

# Wide — extra columns
$ kubectl get pods -o wide
NAME          READY  STATUS   RESTARTS  AGE  IP          NODE      
anihpj-abc    1/1    Running  0         5m   172.17.0.4  minikube  

# YAML — full definition (great for exporting)
$ kubectl get pod anihpj-abc -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-abc
  namespace: default
...

# JSONPath — extract specific fields
$ kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
anihpj-abc anihpj-def anihpj-ghi

# Custom columns
$ kubectl get pods -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,IP:.status.podIP
NAME          STATUS    IP
anihpj-abc    Running   172.17.0.4
JSONPath PatternWhat It ExtractsUse Case
{.metadata.name}Resource nameScripting
{.items[*].metadata.name}All resource names in a listBatch operations
{.status.podIP}Pod's internal IPDebugging networking
{.spec.containers[0].image}First container's imageVersion checks
{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}One name per lineClean output for piping

6.5 Shortcuts and Resource Abbreviations

Kubernetes lets you use short names for all resource types. These will save you thousands of keystrokes:

Full NameShortExample
podspokubectl get po
servicessvckubectl get svc
deploymentsdeploykubectl get deploy
replicasetsrskubectl get rs
namespacesnskubectl get ns
configmapscmkubectl get cm
persistentvolumeclaimspvckubectl get pvc
nodesnokubectl get no
statefulsetsstskubectl get sts
daemonsetsdskubectl get ds
ingressesingkubectl get ing
serviceaccountssakubectl get sa
persistentvolumespvkubectl get pv
cronjobscjkubectl get cj
💡 Pro Tip: Aliases

Power users go further with shell aliases. Add these to your ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc:

alias k='kubectl'
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgd='kubectl get deploy'
alias kaf='kubectl apply -f'
alias kdp='kubectl describe pod'
🏷️ Labels

Used to identify and select resources.

  • Queryable with -l selectors
  • Used by Services, Deployments, ReplicaSets
  • Max 63 characters
  • Example: app: anihpj, env: prod
📝 Annotations

Used to attach metadata for tools/humans.

  • NOT queryable via selectors
  • Used by Ingress controllers, monitoring, CI/CD
  • Can store large values (256 KB)
  • Example: description: "Job posting API"

6.6 Filtering and Querying

In a cluster with hundreds of resources, filtering is essential:

Filtering Techniques 
# ── By Label ──
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj                   # exact match
kubectl get pods -l 'app in (anihpj,frontend)'   # set-based
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,env=prod          # multiple labels (AND)
kubectl get pods -l '!canary'                    # doesn't have label

# ── By Field ──
kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase=Running
kubectl get pods --field-selector spec.nodeName=minikube

# ── By Namespace ──
kubectl get pods -n kube-system                  # specific namespace
kubectl get pods -A                              # ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces                # same as -A

# ── Watch Mode (live updates) ──
kubectl get pods -w                              # streams changes live

# ── Sorting ──
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl get pods --sort-by=.status.phase

# ── Combining ──
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -n production -o wide --sort-by=.status.startTime

Filtering Flow

  All Resources in Cluster
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  pod/a  pod/b  pod/c  pod/d  pod/e   │
  │  svc/x  svc/y  deploy/z  cm/config   │
  └────────────────┬──────────────────────┘
                   │
     ┌─────────────┼─────────────┐
     ▼             ▼             ▼
  -n namespace  -l label    --field-selector
  (which room)  (which tag)  (which state)
     │             │             │
     └─────────────┼─────────────┘
                   ▼
          Filtered Results ──▶ -o format ──▶ Display

6.7 Practice Exercises with anihpj

Try these exercises to solidify your kubectl skills. Each one uses a real command you'll need when deploying anihpj:

Exercise 1

List all running pods and sort by age

Show Solution kubectl get pods --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
Exercise 2

Get the complete YAML of a running pod

Show Solution kubectl get pod anihpj-abc -o yaml
Exercise 3

Execute a Django shell inside the anihpj pod

Show Solution kubectl exec -it anihpj-abc -- python manage.py shell
Exercise 4

View the last 50 lines of logs from anihpj

Show Solution kubectl logs anihpj-abc --tail=50
Exercise 5

Forward port 8000 and access the jobpost API

Show Solution kubectl port-forward anihpj-abc 8000:8000
Then visit: http://localhost:8000/jobs/
Exercise 6

Find all pods with label app=anihpj across all namespaces

Show Solution kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj -A
Exercise 7

List all available resource types in your cluster

Show Solution kubectl api-resources
Exercise 8

Get only the names of all pods in JSON path

Show Solution kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
Exercise 9

Scale the anihpj deployment to 5 replicas

Show Solution kubectl scale deploy anihpj --replicas=5
Exercise 10

Copy the SQLite database from a pod to your local machine

Show Solution kubectl cp anihpj-abc:/app/db.sqlite3 ./backup.db

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: kubectl Speed Mastery

⏱️ Target: 5 minutes for all 8 tasks

Complete these tasks without looking at documentation. Every second counts on exam day:

  1. Create a pod named web with image nginx:1.25 in namespace ckad-drill — do NOT apply it, save to web.yaml
  2. Create a deployment named api with image python:3.11 and 3 replicas
  3. Expose the api deployment as a ClusterIP service on port 8080
  4. Get all pods in ALL namespaces, sorted by creation time
  5. Use jsonpath to extract just the IP addresses of all pods in default namespace
  6. Use kubectl explain to find the field path for container resource limits
  7. Get all resources with the label app=api across all resource types
  8. Force-delete a stuck pod named zombie in namespace ckad-drill
💡 Hint: --dry-run=client -o yaml, --sort-by, -o jsonpath, --grace-period=0 --force
View Solution 
# 1. Generate pod YAML without applying
kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 -n ckad-drill \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web.yaml

# 2. Create deployment imperatively
kubectl create deployment api --image=python:3.11 --replicas=3

# 3. Expose deployment
kubectl expose deployment api --port=8080 --target-port=8080

# 4. All pods sorted by creation
kubectl get pods -A --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

# 5. Extract pod IPs with jsonpath
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.podIP}'

# 6. Explore field paths
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits

# 7. Get all resources by label
kubectl get all -l app=api

# 8. Force-delete stuck pod
kubectl delete pod zombie -n ckad-drill \
  --grace-period=0 --force
The kubectl explain Trick — Your In-Exam Documentation

Can't remember a YAML field? kubectl explain is faster than searching kubernetes.io:

# Show ALL fields for a resource
kubectl explain pod.spec --recursive

# Drill into specific fields
kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy.rollingUpdate

# Find field types and descriptions
kubectl explain service.spec.type

# List all API resources (find resource names)
kubectl api-resources | grep -i "network"

# Check API versions for a resource
kubectl api-versions | grep apps

Chapter 6 — Practice Questions

Q1
Write a single command to create a pod called debug with image busybox, executing sleep 3600, without actually creating it — just output the YAML to a file called debug.yaml.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl run debug --image=busybox --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  --command -- sleep 3600 > debug.yaml
Explanation

The --dry-run=client -o yaml pattern is the most important CKAD technique. It generates valid YAML without creating the resource. Use --command -- before the command args when you need a custom entrypoint. Redirect with > to save. You'll use this pattern dozens of times during the exam.

Q2
What's the difference between kubectl get pods, kubectl describe pod <name>, and kubectl get pod <name> -o yaml? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
CommandOutputUse When
kubectl get podsBrief list: name, status, restarts, ageQuick overview of all pods
kubectl describe pod XDetailed events, conditions, volumes, envDebugging why a pod isn't working
kubectl get pod X -o yamlFull YAML spec including defaultsGetting the complete resource definition
Explanation

On the CKAD: use get to find status quickly, describe to read events and debug failures (look at the Events section at the bottom), and -o yaml when you need to copy or edit a resource's specification. Pro tip: kubectl get pod X -o yaml > fix.yaml lets you edit and re-apply.

Q3
A pod named webapp is running. Write a command to get only its IP address using jsonpath.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl get pod webapp -o jsonpath='{.status.podIP}'
Explanation

JSONPath lets you extract specific fields from kubectl output. Common patterns for the CKAD:

# Get all pod names
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'

# Get node names and IPs
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.addresses[0].address}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get specific container image
kubectl get pod X -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}'
Q4
You need to label 15 pods with tier=frontend, but they all start with the prefix web-. What's the fastest single command?
Show Answer
Answer
# Label all pods at once using a selector or name pattern
kubectl label pods -l app=web tier=frontend

# Or if they share no common label, use --all with a grep pipe:
kubectl get pods --no-headers | grep ^web- | awk '{print $1}' | \
  xargs kubectl label pod tier=frontend
Explanation

Labeling with -l selector is the fastest approach on the CKAD. If pods already share a label, use it as the selector. The xargs approach works when you have no common label. You can also overwrite existing labels with --overwrite. Labels are key to Services, selectors, and NetworkPolicies.

Q5
Explain the difference between kubectl apply -f and kubectl create -f. What happens if you run each on a resource that already exists?
Show Answer
Answer
CommandResource Exists?Result
kubectl create -fNo✅ Creates it
kubectl create -fYes❌ Error: already exists
kubectl apply -fNo✅ Creates it
kubectl apply -fYes✅ Updates it (patches)
Explanation

apply is declarative (idempotent — safe to run repeatedly). create is imperative (fails if resource exists). On the CKAD, use create --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate YAML, then apply -f to create/update it. This two-step pattern handles most exam tasks efficiently.

Q6
How do you execute a command inside a running pod? Connect to the webapp pod and run curl localhost:8080 to test the application.
Show Answer
Answer
# Execute a single command
kubectl exec webapp -- curl -s localhost:8080

# Interactive shell (for exploration)
kubectl exec -it webapp -- /bin/sh

# Multi-container pod — specify container
kubectl exec webapp -c sidecar -- curl -s localhost:8080
Explanation

kubectl exec is your primary debugging tool on the CKAD. The -- separator is required to separate kubectl flags from the command. Use -it for interactive sessions (TTY + stdin). For multi-container pods, -c container-name is mandatory — kubectl picks the first container by default.

Q7
Write a command to get all pods in namespace production that have the label app=api AND are in Running state, showing only their names.
Show Answer
Answer
kubectl get pods -n production -l app=api \
  --field-selector=status.phase=Running \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
Explanation

This combines three filtering techniques: -n for namespace, -l for label selector, and --field-selector for runtime fields. JSONPath extracts just the names. On the CKAD, combining these filters is faster than piping through grep. Field selectors are limited — they only support a few fields like status.phase, metadata.name, and metadata.namespace.

Q8
What does kubectl explain do? Use it to find the exact YAML path for setting a container's memory limit.
Show Answer
Answer
# Start from the top
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers

# Drill down to resources
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.resources.limits

# The path is:
# spec.containers[].resources.limits.memory

The YAML would be:

spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    resources:
      limits:
        memory: "256Mi"
Explanation

kubectl explain is your built-in documentation during the exam. It's faster than searching kubernetes.io. Add --recursive to see all fields at once. This is especially useful for resources you don't use daily, like PodDisruptionBudgets or NetworkPolicies.

Q9
You accidentally deployed a pod with the wrong image. Without deleting and recreating it, fix the image of container app in pod webapp to nginx:1.25.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl set image (fastest)
kubectl set image pod/webapp app=nginx:1.25

# Method 2: kubectl edit (interactive)
kubectl edit pod webapp
# Change the image field, save and exit

# Method 3: kubectl patch
kubectl patch pod webapp -p \
  '{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"app","image":"nginx:1.25"}]}}'
Explanation

Important caveat: Most pod fields are immutable after creation. Changing the image on a bare pod will work, but the pod needs to be restarted. For Deployments, kubectl set image triggers a rolling update automatically. On the CKAD, prefer editing Deployments over bare pods since they handle updates gracefully.

Q10
Sort all pods across ALL namespaces by their restart count (descending) and display the output as a custom table with columns: NAMESPACE, POD, RESTARTS.
Show Answer
Answer
# Using custom-columns + sort
kubectl get pods -A \
  -o custom-columns=\
NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,\
POD:.metadata.name,\
RESTARTS:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount \
  --sort-by='.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount'
Explanation

custom-columns creates readable, exam-friendly output. --sort-by uses JSONPath to sort. Note: --sort-by sorts in ascending order only — there's no built-in descending sort. For descending, pipe to sort -rn. These output formatting skills are essential for CKAD questions that require specific output formats.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Kubernetes Audit Logging & Kubeconfig Security

MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Kubernetes Audit Policy

# /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
  # Don't log read-only requests to certain resources
  - level: None
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["endpoints", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  # Log secret access at Metadata level (don't log body!)
  - level: Metadata
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["secrets"]

  # Log everything else at RequestResponse
  - level: RequestResponse
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["pods", "deployments", "services"]

  # Catch-all: log at Metadata level
  - level: Metadata

Audit Levels: None → Metadata → Request → RequestResponse (most detailed)

Kubeconfig Security Checklist

RiskMitigation
kubeconfig with cluster-admin creds left on shared serverRestrict file permissions: chmod 600 ~/.kube/config
Embedded client certificates in kubeconfigUse OIDC or short-lived tokens instead
Default namespace pointing to kube-systemSet default namespace per context
kubeconfig pointing to wrong clusterAlways verify: kubectl config current-context

Chapter 6 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create an audit policy that logs all Secret access at Metadata level, pod changes at RequestResponse level, and ignores read-only endpoint requests.
Show Answer
Answer
# /etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
  # Rule 1: Ignore read-only endpoint/service requests
  - level: None
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["endpoints", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

  # Rule 2: Log Secret access (metadata only — never log secret body)
  - level: Metadata
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["secrets"]

  # Rule 3: Log pod changes at RequestResponse
  - level: RequestResponse
    resources:
    - group: ""
      resources: ["pods"]
    verbs: ["create", "update", "patch", "delete"]

  # Rule 4: Catch-all
  - level: Metadata
# Enable in API server:
# /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
--audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
--audit-log-maxage=30
--audit-log-maxbackup=10
--audit-log-maxsize=100

# Mount volumes for policy and log directory
Explanation

Audit policy rules are evaluated in order — first match wins. Always put None rules first to reduce noise. NEVER log Secret bodies (RequestResponse on secrets) — use Metadata only. On the CKS exam, you'll likely create or modify an audit policy file and enable it on the API server.

CKS-2
How do you check who accessed a specific Secret using audit logs?
Show Answer
Answer
# Search audit logs for Secret access
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.resource == "secrets" and
  .objectRef.name == "db-credentials"
)' | jq '{
  timestamp: .requestReceivedTimestamp,
  user: .user.username,
  verb: .verb,
  secret: .objectRef.name,
  namespace: .objectRef.namespace,
  sourceIP: .sourceIPs[0]
}'

# Output example:
# {
#   "timestamp": "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z",
#   "user": "system:serviceaccount:default:my-sa",
#   "verb": "get",
#   "secret": "db-credentials",
#   "namespace": "default",
#   "sourceIP": "10.0.1.5"
