# }
Explanation

Audit logs record every API server request with user identity, verb, resource, and source IP. Use jq to filter audit log JSON. On CKS, you may need to investigate suspicious access patterns. Key fields: user.username, verb, objectRef, sourceIPs.

CKS-3
A user has been given a kubeconfig with cluster-admin access. How do you restrict their access to only namespace "dev"?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Remove their ClusterRoleBinding to cluster-admin
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding developer-admin

# 2. Create namespace-scoped Role and RoleBinding
kubectl create role dev-admin \
  --verb='*' --resource='*' -n dev

kubectl create rolebinding dev-admin-binding \
  --role=dev-admin \
  --user=developer@example.com -n dev

# 3. Update their kubeconfig context with namespace
kubectl config set-context dev-context \
  --cluster=production \
  --user=developer \
  --namespace=dev

# 4. Verify they cannot access other namespaces
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n kube-system --as=developer@example.com
# no
Explanation

cluster-admin is the most privileged ClusterRole — it grants full access to everything. On CKS, always follow least privilege: use namespace-scoped Roles instead of ClusterRoles. Use kubectl auth can-i to verify permissions. Setting the namespace in the kubeconfig context prevents accidental cross-namespace access.

CKS-4
What are the four audit levels and when should you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
LevelWhat's LoggedUse For
NoneNothingHealth checks, noisy endpoints
MetadataWho, what, when (no body)Secrets, ConfigMaps (avoid logging body)
RequestMetadata + request bodyCreate/update operations where you need the input
RequestResponseMetadata + request + response bodySecurity-critical resources (RBAC changes)
 
# CKS tip: NEVER use RequestResponse for Secrets
# It would log the Secret values in the audit log!

# Rule order matters — first match wins
# Put specific rules BEFORE the catch-all
Explanation

CKS exam expects you to choose the right audit level. Key principle: log enough to detect issues, but never log sensitive data. RequestResponse on Secrets would put plaintext secrets in the audit log. Rule order matters — Kubernetes uses the first matching rule.

CKS-5
How do you prevent anonymous access to the Kubernetes API server?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Disable anonymous authentication on API server
# Edit /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml
--anonymous-auth=false

# 2. Verify anonymous access is blocked
kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:anonymous
# Should show no permissions (or very limited)

# 3. Check from outside the cluster
curl -k https://APISERVER:6443/api
# Should return 401 Unauthorized (not 200 or 403)

# 4. Also disable anonymous auth on kubelet
# /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
authentication:
  anonymous:
    enabled: false
  webhook:
    enabled: true
authorization:
  mode: Webhook

# 5. Restart kubelet after config change
systemctl restart kubelet
Explanation

Anonymous auth allows unauthenticated API requests as system:anonymous. Some RBAC configs (like the default discovery ClusterRoleBinding) grant anonymous users access to discovery endpoints. On CKS, disable anonymous auth on both API server and kubelet. Note: some health check endpoints still need to work.

Chapter 7: YAML Configuration Files — The Language of Kubernetes

Every Kubernetes resource is defined in YAML. This chapter teaches you the syntax, the four required fields, and how to write complete manifests for every component of the anihpj project — pods, deployments, services, configmaps, and secrets.

🟢 Beginner ⏱️ ~2 hours 📄 Configuration 📝 Declarative

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why Kubernetes chose YAML over JSON and XML
  • ✅ YAML syntax fundamentals — keys, lists, indentation, multi-line strings
  • ✅ The 4 required fields in every Kubernetes manifest
  • ✅ Common apiVersion values for each resource type
  • ✅ Write complete YAML files for anihpj (Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret)
  • ✅ Multi-document YAML and validation best practices
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Application Design & Build 20% Application Deployment 20%
YAML on the CKAD — The 80/20 Rule

You'll write YAML on every single question. But you should almost never write it from scratch:

  • Generate first — kubectl run/create --dry-run=client -o yaml gives you 80% of the manifest
  • Edit the 20% — Add only what's missing (probes, volumes, env vars, resource limits)
  • The 4 required fields — apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec — know these cold
  • apiVersion cheat sheet: v1 (Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, PV, PVC, Namespace) | apps/v1 (Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, ReplicaSet) | batch/v1 (Job, CronJob) | networking.k8s.io/v1 (NetworkPolicy, Ingress)
  • When stuck on a field — kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts --recursive

Complete apiVersion Reference Table

Wrong apiVersion = instant error. Memorize this table — it covers every resource you'll see on the CKAD.

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# apiVersion       │ Resources
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# v1               │ Pod, Service, ConfigMap, Secret,
#                  │ PersistentVolume, PersistentVolumeClaim,
#                  │ Namespace, ServiceAccount, Endpoints,
#                  │ ResourceQuota, LimitRange
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# apps/v1          │ Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet,
#                  │ DaemonSet
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# batch/v1         │ Job, CronJob
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# networking.k8s.io/v1 │ NetworkPolicy, Ingress, IngressClass
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 │ Role, ClusterRole,
#                               │ RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# policy/v1        │ PodDisruptionBudget
# ─────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
# autoscaling/v2   │ HorizontalPodAutoscaler
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# QUICK LOOKUP during exam:
kubectl api-resources | grep -i deployment
# Output: deployments  deploy  apps/v1  true  Deployment

YAML Indentation Traps

  • ❌ Using TABS → YAML parsers reject tabs. K8s returns cryptic "mapping values not allowed" error
  • ✅ Always use SPACES — configure your editor: set expandtab shiftwidth=2 in vim
  • containers is a list — each item starts with -. Forgetting the dash = parse error
  • Multiline strings: Use | for literal blocks (preserves newlines), > for folded (joins lines)
  • Exam vim setup — Add this to ~/.vimrc: set ts=2 sw=2 et ai paste (tabs=2, spaces, autoindent, paste mode)
  • Validate before applying: kubectl apply -f pod.yaml --dry-run=server catches both YAML and API errors
  • Quick syntax check: kubectl apply -f pod.yaml --dry-run=client catches YAML parsing errors only

🔬 Anatomy of Every Kubernetes YAML Manifest

📋 apiVersion
Which API group this resource belongs to. Examples: v1, apps/v1, batch/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1
🏷️ kind
The resource type — Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, etc.
🔖 metadata
Identity of the object — name (required), namespace, labels, annotations
⚙️ spec
The desired state — container images, ports, volumes, replicas, selectors, and everything else
📝
Write YAML Define 4 required fields
→
✅
Validate --dry-run=client
→
📤
Apply kubectl apply -f
→
🔍
Verify kubectl get / describe
→
🔄
Iterate Edit and re-apply
😰 Top 5 YAML Errors That Waste Exam Time
  • 🔴 Using tabs instead of spaces — "mapping values are not allowed here"
  • 🔴 Wrong apiVersion — Deployment needs apps/v1, not v1
  • 🔴 Mismatched labels — selector.matchLabels must match template.metadata.labels
  • 🔴 Forgetting the dash (-) for list items — containers is a YAML list
  • 🔴 Wrong indentation level — containers must be under spec, not metadata
🎉 Fastest YAML Workflow for the Exam
  • ✅ Generate with kubectl run nginx --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml
  • ✅ Edit only what's needed — add probes, volumes, env vars
  • ✅ Validate with kubectl apply -f pod.yaml --dry-run=server
  • ✅ Apply with kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
  • ✅ Verify with kubectl get pod nginx -o wide

7.1 Why YAML? (And Not JSON or XML?)

Kubernetes accepts both YAML and JSON, but the community overwhelmingly uses YAML. Here's why:

✅ YAML (5 lines)
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
image: anihpj:v1
port: 8000
debug: false

Clean & readable

🟡 JSON (10 lines)
{
  "name": "anihpj",
  "replicas": 3,
  "image": "anihpj:v1",
  "port": 8000,
  "debug": false
}

More brackets & quotes

❌ XML (12 lines)
<config>
  <name>anihpj</name>
  <replicas>3</replicas>
  <image>anihpj:v1</image>
  <port>8000</port>
  <debug>false</debug>
</config>

Very verbose

⭐ YAML Wins — 60% Fewer Characters, 100% More Readable

📄
Human-writable
💬
Supports comments
📏
Less syntax noise
🔀
Git-diff friendly

7.2 YAML Syntax Crash Course

Everything you need to know about YAML syntax in one section. Master these rules and you'll never have a YAML error again:

YAML Fundamentals 
# ── 1. Key-Value Pairs ──
name: anihpj
replicas: 3
debug: false

# ── 2. Nested Objects (indent 2 spaces) ──
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    env: production

# ── 3. Lists / Arrays (- prefix) ──
ports:
  - containerPort: 8000
  - containerPort: 8080
  
containers:
  - name: web
    image: anihpj:v1
  - name: sidecar
    image: nginx

# ── 4. Multi-line Strings ──
description: |          # literal block — preserves newlines
  This is line one.
  This is line two.
  Each line stays separate.

summary: >             # folded block — joins lines
  This is a long sentence
  that spans multiple lines
  but becomes a single paragraph.

# ── 5. Comments ──
# This is a comment — ignored by K8s

# ── 6. Data Types ──
enabled: true           # boolean
emptyValue: null        # null
replicas: 3             # integer
ratio: 0.75             # float
name: "anihpj"          # string (quotes optional unless ambiguous)
port: "8080"            # string (forced with quotes)

📋 YAML Quick Reference Card

  ┌─────────────────┬──────────────────┬────────────────────┐
  │  Key: Value      │  Lists            │  Indentation        │
  │  name: bob      │  - apple         │  2 spaces ONLY     │
  │  age: 30        │  - banana        │  (never tabs!)     │
  │                 │  - cherry        │                    │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │  Boolean         │  Null             │  Comments           │
  │  enabled: true  │  value: null     │  # this is ignored │
  │  debug: false   │  value: ~        │                    │
  ├─────────────────┼──────────────────┼────────────────────┤
  │  Multi-line      │  Multi-doc        │  AVOID              │
  │  text: |        │  --- (separator) │  ❌ tabs           │
  │    preserves    │  ... (end)       │  ❌ : in values    │
  │    newlines     │                  │  ❌ unquoted yes   │
  └─────────────────┴──────────────────┴────────────────────┘
🚨 The #1 YAML Mistake

Using tabs instead of spaces. YAML strictly requires spaces for indentation. Configure your editor to use 2 spaces for YAML files. In VS Code: add "[yaml]": { "editor.tabSize": 2, "editor.insertSpaces": true } to your settings.

❌ Common YAML Errors
# Wrong: colon missing space
name:anihpj

# Wrong: inconsistent indentation
metadata:
  name: pod
   labels:         # 3 spaces instead of 2!
    app: anihpj

# Wrong: tab character (invisible!)
spec:
→ containers:      # tab instead of spaces
✅ Correct YAML
# Right: space after colon
name: anihpj

# Right: consistent 2-space indent
metadata:
  name: pod
  labels:          # 2 spaces consistently
    app: anihpj

# Right: spaces only
spec:
  containers:      # 2 spaces, always

7.3 The Four Required Fields

Every Kubernetes YAML manifest has exactly four top-level fields. Know these, and you can write any K8s resource:

The 4 Required Fields — Annotated

  apiVersion: v1              ◄── ① Which API group & version?
                                    (v1, apps/v1, batch/v1, etc.)

  kind: Pod                   ◄── ② What type of object?
                                    (Pod, Deployment, Service, etc.)

  metadata:                   ◄── ③ Who is it? (identity)
    name: anihpj-pod              name, namespace, labels, annotations
    labels:
      app: anihpj

  spec:                       ◄── ④ What do you want? (desired state)
    containers:                   The actual configuration — differs
      - name: anihpj               per resource type
        image: anihpj:v1
        ports:
          - containerPort: 8000
① apiVersion

Tells K8s which API endpoint to use. Different resources live in different API groups.

② kind

The type of object you're creating. Must match the apiVersion.

③ metadata

Identity: name, namespace, labels, annotations. How K8s finds this resource.

④ spec

Your desired state. This is where the real config goes — differs for each kind.

7.4 Common apiVersion Values

Each resource type belongs to a specific API group. Here's the mapping you'll use most often:

Kind apiVersion API Group Notes
Podv1coreCore API — no group prefix
Servicev1coreCore API
ConfigMapv1coreCore API
Secretv1coreCore API
Namespacev1coreCore API
PersistentVolumev1coreCore API
PersistentVolumeClaimv1coreCore API
Deploymentapps/v1appsMost common workload
ReplicaSetapps/v1appsUsually managed by Deployment
StatefulSetapps/v1appsFor stateful apps (databases)
DaemonSetapps/v1appsOne per node
Jobbatch/v1batchRun-to-completion
CronJobbatch/v1batchScheduled jobs
Ingressnetworking.k8s.io/v1networkingHTTP/HTTPS routing
NetworkPolicynetworking.k8s.io/v1networkingFirewall rules
💡 How to Find the Right apiVersion

Can't remember? Use: kubectl api-resources | grep Deployment — it shows the API group. Or use kubectl explain deployment — the first line shows the version.

7.5 Creating YAML for anihpj — Complete Examples

Let's write real, production-ready YAML files for every component of our Django application:

7.5a Pod YAML — anihpj-pod.yaml

anihpj-pod.yaml 
apiVersion: v1                    # Core API
kind: Pod                          # Resource type
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod                 # Pod name (must be unique in namespace)
  labels:
    app: anihpj                    # Label for selection
    component: web                 # What this pod does
    version: v1                    # Track version
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj                 # Container name
      image: anihpj:v1             # Docker image
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000      # Django default port
          protocol: TCP
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE
          value: "anihpj.settings"
        - name: DEBUG
          value: "False"
      resources:                   # Resource limits
        requests:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "250m"              # 0.25 CPU core
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"              # 0.5 CPU core
      readinessProbe:              # Is the app ready for traffic?
        httpGet:
          path: /jobs/
          port: 8000
        initialDelaySeconds: 10
        periodSeconds: 5
⚠️ Pods Alone Are Not Enough

A bare Pod has no self-healing — if it crashes, it stays dead. That's why we wrap pods in a Deployment which ensures the desired number of replicas are always running. Use bare pods only for quick tests.

Pod vs Deployment vs ReplicaSet Hierarchy

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Deployment  (you write this)                        │
  │  ├── manages rolling updates                        │
  │  ├── stores revision history                        │
  │  └── creates ↓                                      │
  │     ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │     │  ReplicaSet  (auto-created — don't edit!)    │   │
  │     │  ├── ensures N pods are always running     │   │
  │     │  └── creates ↓                             │   │
  │     │     ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐│   │
  │     │     │ Pod #1   │ │ Pod #2   │ │ Pod #3   ││   │
  │     │     │ anihpj:v1│ │ anihpj:v1│ │ anihpj:v1││   │
  │     │     └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘│   │
  │     └───────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

7.5b Deployment YAML — anihpj-deployment.yaml

anihpj-deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1               # apps API group
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                      # Run 3 instances
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                  # Must match template labels
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1            # At most 1 pod down during update
      maxSurge: 1                  # At most 1 extra pod during update
  template:                        # Pod template (creates the pods)
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj                # Must match selector above
        component: web
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config   # Load all ConfigMap keys as env vars
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret   # Load all Secret keys as env vars
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 15

7.5c Service YAML — anihpj-service.yaml

anihpj-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: NodePort                   # Accessible from outside the cluster
  selector:
    app: anihpj                    # Targets pods with this label
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                     # Service port (what clients use)
      targetPort: 8000             # Container port (Django)
      nodePort: 30080              # External port on node (30000-32767)

Traffic Flow Through This Service

  Browser → :30080 (nodePort) → :80 (service port) → :8000 (container)

  ┌──────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
  │  User    │────▶│  Service       │────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
  │  :30080  │     │  anihpj-svc    │     │  container :8000 │
  └──────────┘     │  selector:     │     └──────────────────┘
                   │    app=anihpj  │────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
                   └────────────────┘     │  container :8000 │
                                          └──────────────────┘
                                    ────▶│  Pod (anihpj)    │
                                          │  container :8000 │
                                          └──────────────────┘

7.5d ConfigMap YAML — anihpj-config.yaml

anihpj-config.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  labels:
    app: anihpj
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DEBUG: "False"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.sqlite3"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
📝 ConfigMap Values Are Always Strings

Even though Django expects DEBUG=False as a boolean, in a ConfigMap all values are strings. Your Django code should handle the conversion: DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'False') == 'True'

7.5e Secret YAML — anihpj-secret.yaml

anihpj-secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  labels:
    app: anihpj
type: Opaque                       # Generic secret type
stringData:                        # Use stringData for plain text (K8s auto-encodes)
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-change-this-in-production-abc123"
  DATABASE_URL: "sqlite:///db.sqlite3"

# Alternative: use 'data' with base64-encoded values
# data:
#   SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLWNoYW5nZS10aGlzLWluLXByb2R1Y3Rpb24=
🚨 Secrets Are Not Encrypted by Default

Kubernetes Secrets are only base64-encoded, not encrypted. Anyone with API access can decode them. For production, use a proper secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault) or enable K8s encryption at rest. Never commit Secret YAML files to Git.

7.6 Multi-Document YAML Files

You can combine multiple Kubernetes resources in a single file using the --- separator:

anihpj-all-in-one.yaml — Combined Deployment + Service 
# ── Deployment ──
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
---
# ── Service ──
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - port: 80
      targetPort: 8000
      nodePort: 30080
✅ One File Per Resource
  • Easier to manage individually
  • Cleaner Git diffs
  • Can apply selectively
  • Best for: production
📄 All-in-One File
  • Quick to share
  • Single kubectl apply
  • Harder to find things
  • Best for: demos / learning

7.7 Validating YAML Before Applying

Always validate your YAML before sending it to the cluster. Catch mistakes early:

Validation Commands 
# Dry-run — simulates without creating anything
$ kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment created (dry run)

# Diff — shows what would change vs. current state
$ kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml
# Shows a unified diff of new vs. existing resource

# Server-side dry-run — validates against the API server
$ kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server

# Explain — check valid fields for a resource
$ kubectl explain deployment.spec.strategy
🔧 VS Code Setup for YAML
  • Install the YAML extension (Red Hat)
  • Install the Kubernetes extension (Microsoft)
  • Auto-complete for all K8s fields
  • Inline validation & error highlighting
  • Hover docs for every field
🌐 Online Validators
  • kubeval — CLI schema validator
  • kubeconform — faster alternative
  • yamllint — pure YAML syntax check
  • kube-score — best practice checks
  • Datree — policy enforcement

7.8 YAML Best Practices

Follow these rules to write clean, maintainable Kubernetes YAML:

✅ DO
  • ✅ Use 2-space indentation consistently
  • ✅ Always specify resource requests and limits
  • ✅ Use labels on every resource
  • ✅ Add comments for non-obvious config
  • ✅ Quote strings that look like numbers/booleans
  • ✅ Use stringData for secrets (auto-encodes)
  • ✅ Include readinessProbe and livenessProbe
  • ✅ Validate with --dry-run=client before applying
❌ DON'T
  • ❌ Never use tabs (spaces only!)
  • ❌ Don't hardcode secrets in YAML
  • ❌ Don't use :latest tag in production
  • ❌ Don't skip resource limits
  • ❌ Don't commit Secret files to Git
  • ❌ Don't copy-paste without understanding
  • ❌ Don't mix tabs and spaces
  • ❌ Don't forget the selector field in Deployments

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: YAML Generation Speed Run

⏱️ Target: 6 minutes

Generate and customize YAML manifests without writing from scratch:

  1. Generate a Pod YAML for image nginx:1.25 named web with labels app=web, tier=frontend — save to web.yaml
  2. Edit web.yaml to add a readiness probe (HTTP GET on port 80, path /)
  3. Generate a Deployment YAML for anihpj:v2 with 3 replicas — save to deploy.yaml
  4. Edit deploy.yaml to add resource requests (cpu: 100m, memory: 128Mi) and limits (cpu: 500m, memory: 256Mi)
  5. Generate a ConfigMap YAML from literal values DB_HOST=postgres and DB_PORT=5432
  6. Create a multi-document YAML file combining a Deployment and a Service (use --- separator)
💡 Hint: --dry-run=client -o yaml, kubectl explain for field paths
View Solution 
# 1. Generate pod YAML with labels
kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 \
  -l app=web,tier=frontend \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web.yaml

# 2. Add readiness probe (add to containers section):
# readinessProbe:
#   httpGet:
#     path: /
#     port: 80
#   initialDelaySeconds: 5
#   periodSeconds: 10

# 3. Generate deployment YAML
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v2 \
  --replicas=3 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml

# 4. Add resources (add under containers):
# resources:
#   requests:
#     cpu: "100m"
#     memory: "128Mi"
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "256Mi"

# 5. ConfigMap from literals
kubectl create configmap db-config \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml

# 6. Multi-document YAML
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=anihpj:v2 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > combo.yaml
echo "---" >> combo.yaml
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=8000 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml >> combo.yaml
YAML Debugging — Common Exam Pitfalls

When kubectl apply -f file.yaml fails, check these in order:

# Validate YAML syntax before applying
kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=client

# Check indentation errors (most common!)
# Tabs are NEVER allowed — use 2 spaces only

# selector.matchLabels MUST match template.metadata.labels
# This is the #1 Deployment error on the exam

# Check apiVersion is correct
kubectl api-resources | grep Deployment
# Shows: deployments  deploy  apps/v1  true  Deployment

# Quick field path reference
kubectl explain deploy.spec.selector --recursive

Chapter 7 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is wrong with this YAML? Find and fix all errors: 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-pod
  labels:
    app: web
spec:
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25
      ports:
      - containerPort: "80"
      resources:
        requests:
        memory: 64Mi
Show Answer
Answer

Two errors:

  1. containerPort: "80" → should be containerPort: 80 (integer, not string)
  2. memory: 64Mi is not indented under requests: — it should be a child of requests
spec:
  containers:
    - name: web
      image: nginx:1.25
      ports:
      - containerPort: 80          # integer, no quotes
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: 64Mi             # indented under requests
Explanation

YAML indentation and type errors are the most common CKAD mistakes. Remember: containerPort is an integer, but protocol is a string. Resource values like "64Mi" can be either string or bare — both work. Always validate with kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f file.yaml before submitting.

Q2
Write a complete Pod YAML manifest that runs an nginx:1.25 container with labels app: web and tier: frontend, exposes port 80, sets an environment variable ENV=production, and requests 128Mi memory.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web
  labels:
    app: web
    tier: frontend
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx:1.25
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    env:
    - name: ENV
      value: "production"
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "128Mi"
Explanation

On the CKAD, you'd generate this faster with: kubectl run web --image=nginx:1.25 --port=80 --labels="app=web,tier=frontend" --env="ENV=production" --dry-run=client -o yaml > pod.yaml, then edit to add resource requests. The imperative + edit approach is always faster than writing YAML from scratch.

Q3
What are the four required top-level keys in every Kubernetes manifest? What happens if you omit metadata.name?
Show Answer
Answer

The four required keys: apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec

Omitting metadata.name causes an API error: "metadata.name: Required value". Every Kubernetes resource must have a unique name within its namespace.

Explanation

status is NOT a required field — it's managed by Kubernetes. Some resources use metadata.generateName instead of name (e.g., pods created by ReplicaSets), but when writing manifests manually, you always provide name. Use kubectl explain pod to see which fields are required.

Q4
Convert this imperative command to a declarative YAML manifest:
kubectl create deployment api --image=python:3.11 --replicas=3 --port=8000
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: api
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: api
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: python
        image: python:3.11
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
Explanation

Key points: (1) selector.matchLabels MUST match template.metadata.labels — this is how the Deployment finds its pods. (2) The apiVersion for Deployments is apps/v1, not v1. (3) On the CKAD, use kubectl create deployment api --image=python:3.11 --replicas=3 --port=8000 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml to generate this automatically.

Q5
What is a multi-document YAML file? How do you separate multiple resources in a single file, and why would you do this?
Show Answer
Answer

Use --- (three dashes) to separate multiple resources in a single file:

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  key: value
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: app-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
# ...

Apply all at once: kubectl apply -f all-resources.yaml

Explanation

Multi-document YAML keeps related resources together. On the CKAD, this saves time — create ConfigMap + Deployment + Service in one file, one kubectl apply. Order matters: put dependencies first (ConfigMap before Deployment that uses it). You can also apply an entire directory: kubectl apply -f ./manifests/

Q6
What's the difference between labels and annotations in Kubernetes metadata? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureLabelsAnnotations
PurposeIdentify and select resourcesAttach non-identifying metadata
Used by selectors✅ Yes❌ No
Size limit63 chars per value256KB total
Example useapp: webdescription: "Main web app"
Explanation

Labels are for K8s operations (selectors, grouping). Annotations are for humans and external tools (build info, descriptions, Ingress controller configs). The CKAD tests labels heavily — Services, Deployments, and NetworkPolicies all use label selectors. Annotations appear in Ingress configuration (e.g., nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target).

Q7
You have a YAML file with a typo. Instead of deleting and recreating, what command validates the YAML without applying it? What about server-side validation?
Show Answer
Answer
# Client-side validation (no API call)
kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f pod.yaml

# Server-side validation (validates against API schema)
kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f pod.yaml

# Diff — see what would change
kubectl diff -f pod.yaml
Explanation

--dry-run=client only validates YAML syntax locally. --dry-run=server sends the request to the API server for full validation (catches invalid field names, wrong types, missing required fields). On the CKAD, always dry-run before applying. kubectl diff shows exactly what would change — useful when modifying existing resources.

Q8
What is the selector.matchLabels field in a Deployment, and why must it match template.metadata.labels? What happens if they don't match?
Show Answer
Answer

selector.matchLabels tells the Deployment which pods it owns. template.metadata.labels defines the labels placed on pods created by this Deployment.

If they don't match: the API server rejects the manifest with error: "selector does not match template labels".

# selector MUST match template labels
selector:
  matchLabels:
    app: web      # ← Must match ↓
template:
  metadata:
    labels:
      app: web    # ← Must match ↑
Explanation

This is a safety mechanism. If selectors didn't have to match, a Deployment could accidentally adopt (or lose) pods from other Deployments. The selector is immutable after creation — you cannot change it later. If you need different selectors, delete and recreate the Deployment.

Q9
Write the YAML for a Service of type ClusterIP named web-svc that routes traffic on port 80 to pods labeled app: web on their container port 8080.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web-svc
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80            # Service listens on port 80
    targetPort: 8080     # Forwards to container port 8080
    protocol: TCP
Explanation

The key relationship: port = what clients connect to, targetPort = what the container listens on. If omitted, targetPort defaults to port. Faster CKAD approach: kubectl expose deployment web --name=web-svc --port=80 --target-port=8080 --dry-run=client -o yaml. Verify with kubectl get endpoints web-svc to confirm pods are found.

Q10
What are the common YAML mistakes that fail silently in Kubernetes (no error, but resource doesn't work as expected)?
Show Answer
Answer
Silent MistakeSymptomHow to Catch
Label mismatch (Service ↔ Pod)Service has 0 endpointskubectl get endpoints
Wrong containerPortConnection refusedkubectl exec -- curl localhost:PORT
Missing namespace flagResource created in defaultkubectl get X -A
Typo in env var nameApp uses wrong/empty configkubectl exec -- env
Extra whitespace in valuesString comparison failskubectl get cm -o yaml
Explanation

These are the trickiest CKAD debugging scenarios. Kubernetes won't error on a label mismatch — it just has no matching pods. Always verify: (1) kubectl get endpoints to check Service→Pod binding, (2) kubectl describe for events, (3) kubectl logs for application errors. Build a verification habit after every resource creation.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Static Analysis & Manifest Scanning

SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY 20%

YAML Security Scanning Tools

ToolPurposeCKS Relevance
kubesecRisk-scores Kubernetes manifests🔴 Exam tested
conftest / OPAPolicy-as-code for YAML🔴 Exam tested
TrivyVulnerability + misconfig scanner🔴 Exam tested
kube-linterLint K8s manifests for best practices🟡 Awareness

kubesec — Manifest Risk Scoring

# Scan a YAML file
kubesec scan pod.yaml

# Output shows score and critical issues:
# [{"object":"Pod/test","valid":true,"score":-30,
#   "scoring":{"critical":[
#     {"id":"CapSysAdmin","reason":"SYS_ADMIN capability"},
#     {"id":"AllowPrivilegeEscalation","reason":"..."}
#   ]}}]

# Online scan:
curl -sSX POST --data-binary @pod.yaml https://v2.kubesec.io/scan

Chapter 7 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Use kubesec to scan a pod manifest and interpret the results. What score indicates a critical issue?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scan with kubesec
kubesec scan pod.yaml

# Results interpretation:
# score > 0   → Good security practices detected
# score = 0   → Neutral (no good or bad practices)
# score < 0   → CRITICAL security issues found

# Example output:
# {
#   "score": -30,
#   "scoring": {
#     "critical": [
#       {"id": "CapSysAdmin", "selector": "containers[].securityContext.capabilities.add"},
#       {"id": "AllowPrivilegeEscalation"}
#     ],
#     "advise": [
#       {"id": "ApparmorAny", "reason": "Well defined AppArmor policies"},
#       {"id": "ServiceAccountName", "reason": "Use non-default SA"}
#     ]
#   }
# }

# Fix based on critical findings, then re-scan
Explanation

kubesec scores manifests based on security best practices. Negative scores mean critical issues (SYS_ADMIN, privileged, etc.). The "advise" section suggests improvements. On CKS, you may be asked to scan a manifest, identify issues, and fix them.

CKS-2
Write an OPA/conftest Rego policy that denies pods running as root.
Show Answer
Answer
# policy/deny-root.rego
package main

deny[msg] {
  input.kind == "Pod"
  not input.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot
  msg := "Pod must set securityContext.runAsNonRoot to true"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.kind == "Pod"
  container := input.spec.containers[_]
  container.securityContext.runAsUser == 0
  msg := sprintf("Container '%s' must not run as root (UID 0)", [container.name])
}

deny[msg] {
  input.kind == "Pod"
  container := input.spec.containers[_]
  container.securityContext.privileged
  msg := sprintf("Container '%s' must not be privileged", [container.name])
}
# Run conftest
conftest test pod.yaml -p policy/

# Output:
# FAIL - pod.yaml - Pod must set securityContext.runAsNonRoot to true
# 1 test, 1 passed, 1 warning, 1 failure
Explanation

OPA (Open Policy Agent) with conftest allows policy-as-code for Kubernetes manifests. Rego is OPA's policy language. The deny rule returns a message when a policy is violated. On CKS, you may need to write or modify Rego policies. This is part of Supply Chain Security.

CKS-3
Use Trivy to scan a Kubernetes manifest for misconfigurations.
Show Answer
Answer
# Scan manifest for misconfigurations
trivy config pod.yaml

# Scan for vulnerabilities in container images referenced in manifest
trivy image nginx:1.25

# Scan entire directory of manifests
trivy config ./manifests/

# Filter by severity
trivy config --severity HIGH,CRITICAL pod.yaml

# Output example:
# pod.yaml (kubernetes)
# Tests: 41 (SUCCESSES: 35, FAILURES: 6)
# CRITICAL: Container runs as root
# HIGH: capabilities not dropped
# HIGH: allowPrivilegeEscalation not set to false

# Scan image for CVEs
trivy image --severity CRITICAL nginx:latest
# Shows CVE IDs, package name, installed/fixed versions
Explanation

Trivy is a comprehensive scanner for vulnerabilities and misconfigurations. trivy config checks Kubernetes manifests against best practices. trivy image scans container images for CVEs. On CKS, Trivy is commonly used for both manifest scanning and image vulnerability scanning. Know both modes.

CKS-4
What security fields should ALWAYS be in a Kubernetes YAML manifest for CKS compliance?
Show Answer
Answer
# CKS-compliant pod template:
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false   # Don't auto-mount SA token
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true                  # Pod level
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault               # Syscall filtering
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/app:v1.2.3@sha256:abc...  # Pinned digest!
    imagePullPolicy: Always
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false   # Block SUID attacks
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]                   # Remove all capabilities
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true      # Prevent file writes
    resources:                          # Prevent resource abuse
      limits:
        memory: "256Mi"
        cpu: "500m"
      requests:
        memory: "128Mi"
        cpu: "250m"
Explanation

Every CKS-compliant manifest should have: image pinned by digest (not just tag), automountServiceAccountToken: false, non-root user, seccomp profile, dropped capabilities, no privilege escalation, read-only root filesystem, and resource limits. This is the baseline for all CKS exam questions.

CKS-5
How do you integrate manifest scanning into a CI/CD pipeline as a security gate?
Show Answer
Answer
# CI/CD pipeline security scanning steps:

# Step 1: Lint manifests
kube-linter lint manifests/ --config .kube-linter.yaml

# Step 2: Scan manifests with kubesec
kubesec scan deployment.yaml | jq '.[0].score'
# Fail pipeline if score < 0

# Step 3: Scan images for vulnerabilities
trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL myapp:latest
# --exit-code 1 = fail pipeline on CRITICAL vulns

# Step 4: Policy check with conftest
conftest test manifests/ -p policies/ --fail-on-warn
# Fail pipeline on policy violations

# Step 5: Admission control (runtime gate)
# ValidatingAdmissionWebhook with OPA Gatekeeper
# Rejects non-compliant resources even if CI/CD is bypassed
Explanation

Defense in depth: scan in CI/CD AND enforce at admission time. CI/CD catches issues early, admission webhooks prevent bypass. On CKS, understand the full supply chain: source → build → scan → deploy → runtime. Use --exit-code 1 on Trivy to fail the pipeline on findings.

Chapter 8: Deploying anihpj to Kubernetes — Full Walkthrough

This is the big one. We'll take our anihpj Django project from a local codebase to a fully running Kubernetes deployment — step by step. By the end, you'll have a real app served by Kubernetes pods.

🟡 Intermediate ⏱️ ~2.5 hours 🚀 Full Deployment 🛠️ Hands-On

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Pre-deployment checklist — everything you need ready
  • ✅ Structure your project for Kubernetes
  • ✅ Build and make your Docker image available to Minikube
  • ✅ Create namespace, ConfigMap, Secret, Deployment, and Service
  • ✅ Verify everything works and access your app
  • ✅ Run Django migrations inside a pod
  • ✅ Troubleshoot common deployment issues
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Application Deployment 20% Application Design & Build 20%
End-to-End Deployment — The CKAD Pattern

Many CKAD questions ask you to deploy a complete application. The fastest workflow:

  • Step 1 — Create namespace: kubectl create ns my-app
  • Step 2 — Generate deployment: kubectl create deploy my-app --image=nginx -n my-app --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
  • Step 3 — Edit YAML to add probes, volumes, env vars, resources
  • Step 4 — Apply: kubectl apply -f deploy.yaml
  • Step 5 — Expose: kubectl expose deploy my-app --port=80 -n my-app
  • Step 6 — Verify: kubectl get all -n my-app → check pods are Running

⚡ Don't write YAML from scratch — generate → edit → apply

Deployment YAML Skeleton — Memorize This

Deployments are the most common resource on the exam. Start from kubectl create deploy --dry-run=client -o yaml, then add these fields:

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-web
  namespace: production          # ← Always specify!
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                # ← MUST match template labels
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0          # ← Zero-downtime
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj              # ← MUST match selector
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
      containers:
      - name: anihpj
        image: anihpj/web:1.0
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config  # ← All keys as env vars
        - secretRef:
            name: anihpj-secrets
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "100m"
          limits:
            memory: "256Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
        readinessProbe:            # ← Traffic only when ready
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 5
          periodSeconds: 10
        livenessProbe:             # ← Restart if dead
          httpGet:
            path: /health
            port: 8000
          initialDelaySeconds: 15
          periodSeconds: 20
        securityContext:
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false

Deployment Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Create deploymentkubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --replicas=3
Generate YAMLkubectl create deploy web --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml > deploy.yaml
Scalekubectl scale deploy web --replicas=5
Update imagekubectl set image deploy/web web=nginx:1.26
Check rolloutkubectl rollout status deploy/web
Rollout historykubectl rollout history deploy/web
Undo rolloutkubectl rollout undo deploy/web
Undo to specific revkubectl rollout undo deploy/web --to-revision=2
Pause rolloutkubectl rollout pause deploy/web
Resume rolloutkubectl rollout resume deploy/web
Autoscalekubectl autoscale deploy web --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=80

Deployment Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get deploy web -n myns → READY shows 3/3
  • kubectl get rs -n myns → ReplicaSet has DESIRED=CURRENT=READY
  • kubectl get pods -n myns -l app=web → All pods Running
  • kubectl rollout status deploy/web -n myns → "successfully rolled out"
  • kubectl describe deploy web -n myns → Check Events, Strategy, Selector
  • kubectl get deploy web -n myns -o yaml | grep -A3 strategy → Verify strategy type
  • kubectl get endpoints web-svc -n myns → Service has pod IPs listed

🚀 Django-to-Kubernetes Deployment Pipeline

1
Dockerize

Create Dockerfile + requirements.txt

2
Build Image

docker build -t anihpj:v1 .

3
Push Registry

Push to Docker Hub or private registry

4
Write Manifests

Deployment + Service + ConfigMap YAML

5
Apply

kubectl apply -f k8s/

6
Verify

kubectl get all & test endpoints

🏗️ anihpj Deployment Architecture Stack

🌐 Service (NodePort / LoadBalancer) Exposes Django app externally — routes traffic to pods
🔄 Deployment (replicas: 3) Manages ReplicaSet → ensures 3 Django pods are always running
📦 Pod (anihpj container) Runs Django with gunicorn, mounts ConfigMaps for settings
💾 ConfigMap + Secret Database credentials, Django settings, environment variables

8.1 Preparation Checklist

Before deploying, make sure you have everything in place:

Pre-Flight Checklist

  ✅  Docker installed and running            docker --version
  ✅  Minikube installed                       minikube version
  ✅  kubectl installed                        kubectl version --client
  ✅  anihpj Dockerfile created and tested     docker build -t anihpj:v1 .
  ✅  Kubernetes cluster running               minikube status
  ✅  YAML manifests prepared                  ls k8s/
  ✅  requirements.txt up to date              pip freeze

8.2 Project Structure for K8s Deployment

Here's how our anihpj project looks when ready for Kubernetes:

📁 Directory Layout

  anihpj/
  ├── manage.py                   ← Django entry point
  ├── anihpj/
  │   ├── settings.py              ← Django settings
  │   ├── urls.py                  ← Root URL config
  │   └── wsgi.py                  ← WSGI app
  ├── jobpost/
  │   ├── models.py                ← Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
  │   ├── views.py                 ← jobpost_list, jobpost_detail, skills_list
  │   └── urls.py                  ← App URLs
  ├── Dockerfile                   ← How to containerize
  ├── .dockerignore                ← Files to exclude from image
  ├── requirements.txt             ← Python dependencies
  └── k8s/                        ← Kubernetes manifests
      ├── namespace.yaml           ← Isolated namespace
      ├── configmap.yaml           ← Non-sensitive config
      ├── secret.yaml              ← Sensitive config (not in Git!)
      ├── deployment.yaml          ← Pod template + replicas
      ├── service.yaml             ← Networking / routing
      └── ingress.yaml             ← HTTP routing (optional)
💡 The k8s/ Directory Convention

Keeping all Kubernetes manifests in a k8s/ folder lets you deploy everything at once with kubectl apply -f k8s/. This is a common convention used across the industry.

Dockerfile — The Blueprint for Our Image 
# Use official Python 3.11 slim image for smaller size
FROM python:3.11-slim

# Set environment variables
ENV PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 \
    PYTHONUNBUFFERED=1

# Set the working directory
WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies first (Docker layer caching)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy the entire project
COPY . .

# Expose Django's port
EXPOSE 8000

# Run the Django development server
CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
.dockerignore — Keep the Image Clean 
__pycache__
*.pyc
*.pyo
db.sqlite3
.git
.gitignore
env/
venv/
k8s/
*.md

8.3 Step 1: Build and Push the Docker Image

Minikube runs its own Docker daemon. To avoid pushing to a remote registry, we build directly inside Minikube's Docker environment:

Building for Minikube 
# Option A: Use Minikube's Docker daemon (recommended for local dev)
# This makes images built here visible to the Minikube cluster
$ eval $(minikube docker-env)        # macOS/Linux
$ minikube docker-env | Invoke-Expression  # Windows PowerShell

# Build the image
$ docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/
Sending build context to Docker daemon  46.08kB
Step 1/7 : FROM python:3.11-slim
Step 2/7 : WORKDIR /app
Step 3/7 : COPY requirements.txt .
Step 4/7 : RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
Step 5/7 : COPY . .
Step 6/7 : EXPOSE 8000
Step 7/7 : CMD ["python", "manage.py", "runserver", "0.0.0.0:8000"]
Successfully built abc123def
Successfully tagged anihpj:v1

# Verify the image exists in Minikube's Docker
$ docker images | grep anihpj
anihpj   v1   abc123def   2 minutes ago   198MB
Alternative: Push to a Registry (for cloud deployments) 
# Option B: Push to Docker Hub (for remote/cloud clusters)
$ docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/
$ docker tag anihpj:v1 yourusername/anihpj:v1
$ docker login
$ docker push yourusername/anihpj:v1
📝 imagePullPolicy: Never

When using Minikube's Docker daemon, add imagePullPolicy: Never to your deployment YAML. This tells Kubernetes to use the local image instead of trying to pull from a registry.

🔄 How minikube docker-env Works

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     YOUR HOST MACHINE                          │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────┐    eval $(minikube     ┌─────────────────┐   │
  │  │  Terminal /   │    docker-env)         │   Minikube VM   │   │
  │  │  Shell        │ ─────────────────────▶ │                 │   │
  │  │              │    Points DOCKER_HOST   │  ┌───────────┐  │   │
  │  │  $ docker    │    to Minikube's        │  │  Docker   │  │   │
  │  │    build ... │    Docker daemon        │  │  Daemon   │  │   │
  │  └──────┬───────┘                         │  └─────┬─────┘  │   │
  │         │                                 │        │        │   │
  │         │    docker build -t app:v1 .     │        ▼        │   │
  │         └─────────────────────────────────┼▶ ┌──────────┐   │   │
  │                                           │  │  Images  │   │   │
  │    WITHOUT docker-env:                    │  │ app:v1   │   │   │
  │    Image built on HOST → not in Minikube  │  │ nginx:.. │   │   │
  │                                           │  └──────────┘   │   │
  │    WITH docker-env:                       │        │        │   │
  │    Image built INSIDE Minikube → visible  │        ▼        │   │
  │    to kubelet → no registry needed! ✅     │  ┌──────────┐   │   │
  │                                           │  │  Pods    │   │   │
  │                                           │  │ can pull │   │   │
  │                                           │  │ locally  │   │   │
  │                                           │  └──────────┘   │   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────┴─────────────────┘

8.4 Step 2: Create the Namespace

Isolate our app in its own namespace to keep things organized:

k8s/namespace.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-app
  labels:
    project: anihpj
    environment: development
Apply it 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yaml
namespace/anihpj-app created

$ kubectl get ns
NAME              STATUS   AGE
default           Active   30m
kube-system       Active   30m
kube-public       Active   30m
anihpj-app        Active   5s    ← our new namespace!

8.5 Step 3: Create ConfigMap and Secrets

Separate your configuration from your code. ConfigMaps for non-sensitive settings, Secrets for passwords and keys:

k8s/configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
data:
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DEBUG: "False"
k8s/secret.yaml (DO NOT commit to Git!) 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
type: Opaque
stringData:
  SECRET_KEY: "your-super-secret-django-key-here"
  DATABASE_URL: "sqlite:///db.sqlite3"
Apply both 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
configmap/anihpj-config created

$ kubectl apply -f k8s/secret.yaml
secret/anihpj-secret created

# Verify
$ kubectl get cm,secret -n anihpj-app
NAME                         DATA   AGE
configmap/anihpj-config      3      10s

NAME                         TYPE     DATA   AGE
secret/anihpj-secret         Opaque   2      5s

8.6 Step 4: Create the Deployment

The Deployment controls how many pod instances run and how updates are rolled out:

k8s/deployment.yaml — Fully Annotated 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-deployment
  namespace: anihpj-app              # Deploy into our namespace
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3                        # Run 3 pod instances
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                    # How Deployment finds its pods
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1              # Keep at least 2 running during updates
      maxSurge: 1                    # Allow 1 extra pod during rollout
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: v1
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          imagePullPolicy: Never     # Use local image (Minikube)
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:                   # Load ALL keys from ConfigMap
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:             # Load ALL keys from Secret
                name: anihpj-secret
          resources:
            requests:                # Minimum guaranteed resources
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:                  # Maximum allowed resources
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          readinessProbe:            # Is the app ready for traffic?
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 10
          livenessProbe:             # Is the app still alive?
            httpGet:
              path: /jobs/
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 15
Apply and watch 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment created

# Watch pods come up
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w
NAME                                 READY   STATUS              RESTARTS   AGE
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    1/1     Running             0          8s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    1/1     Running             0          9s
anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    1/1     Running             0          10s

🔄 Rolling Update — How Pods Transition

With maxUnavailable: 1 and maxSurge: 1, Kubernetes ensures minimal downtime during deployments:

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ROLLING UPDATE: v1 → v2  (replicas: 3, maxSurge: 1)      │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 1: Initial State (3 pods running v1)                  │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 2: Create 1 new pod (surge), terminate 1 old          │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐       │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v1 ❌│  │ Pod v2 🔄│       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘       │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 3: v2 pod ready, continue replacing                   │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v1 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 🔄│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  Step 4: Complete — all pods running v2                      │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                     │
  │  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│  │ Pod v2 ✅│                     │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                     │
  │                                                             │
  │  ✅ = Running    🔄 = Starting    ❌ = Terminating           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Trigger a Rolling Update 
# Option 1: Update image version
$ kubectl set image deployment/anihpj-deployment anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Option 2: Edit the YAML and re-apply
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml

# Watch the rollout progress
$ kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
Waiting for deployment "anihpj-deployment" rollout to finish: 1 out of 3 new replicas updated...
Waiting for deployment "anihpj-deployment" rollout to finish: 2 out of 3 new replicas updated...
deployment "anihpj-deployment" successfully rolled out

# Rollback if something goes wrong!
$ kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment rolled back

8.7 Step 5: Create the Service

Expose the deployment so traffic can reach our Django app:

k8s/service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-service
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj                    # Matches pods with label app=anihpj
  ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 80                     # Service port
      targetPort: 8000             # Django port inside container
      nodePort: 30080              # External port (30000-32767)
Apply 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/service.yaml
service/anihpj-service created

$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
NAME             TYPE       CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
anihpj-service   NodePort   10.96.142.178   <none>        80:30080/TCP   5s

📊 Kubernetes Service Types Comparison

Type Access Scope Use Case External IP?
ClusterIP Internal only (within cluster) Microservice-to-microservice communication ❌ No
NodePort External via node IP:port Development, testing, simple exposure ⚠️ Via node IP + port (30000-32767)
LoadBalancer External via cloud LB Production on cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) ✅ Yes (cloud-provisioned)
ExternalName DNS alias to external service Map cluster DNS to external domains N/A (CNAME record)

🌐 Service Routing — How Traffic Flows

    External Traffic
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐     NodePort opens port 30080
  │    Node IP   │     on EVERY node in the cluster
  │  :30080      │
  └──────┬───────┘
         │
         ▼
  ┌──────────────┐     Service uses selector: app=anihpj
  │   Service    │     to find matching pods
  │  ClusterIP   │
  │ 10.96.142.x  │
  └──────┬───────┘
         │ load balances (round-robin)
    ┌────┼────┐
    ▼    ▼    ▼
  ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐  
  │Pod1││Pod2││Pod3│  Each pod receives
  │:8K ││:8K ││:8K │  traffic on port 8000
  └────┘└────┘└────┘

8.8 Step 6: Verify Everything

Time to check that all our resources are healthy:

Verification Commands 
# See ALL resources in our namespace
$ kubectl get all -n anihpj-app
NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12    1/1     Running   0          2m
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-def34    1/1     Running   0          2m
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-ghi56    1/1     Running   0          2m

NAME                     TYPE       CLUSTER-IP      EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)        AGE
service/anihpj-service   NodePort   10.96.142.178   <none>        80:30080/TCP   1m

NAME                                READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   AGE
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment   3/3     3            3           2m

NAME                                           DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
replicaset.apps/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4    3         3         3       2m

# View detailed deployment info
$ kubectl describe deployment anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app

# Stream application logs
$ kubectl logs -f deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app

# Check ConfigMap values
$ kubectl get cm anihpj-config -n anihpj-app -o yaml

What You Should See

  ┌─ anihpj-app namespace ──────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                         │
  │  ConfigMap: anihpj-config ✅                             │
  │  Secret: anihpj-secret ✅                                │
  │                                                         │
  │  Deployment: anihpj-deployment (3/3 ready) ✅            │
  │  ├── Pod: anihpj-...-abc12  (Running) ✅                │
  │  ├── Pod: anihpj-...-def34  (Running) ✅                │
  │  └── Pod: anihpj-...-ghi56  (Running) ✅                │
  │                                                         │
  │  Service: anihpj-service (NodePort :30080) ✅            │
  │                                                         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

✅ Deployment Health Checklist

🟢 Healthy Indicators
  • All pods → STATUS: Running
  • READY → Shows 1/1 (all containers ready)
  • RESTARTS → 0 (no crash loops)
  • Deployment → AVAILABLE matches replicas
  • Service endpoints → Lists pod IPs (not empty)
  • Logs → No errors, HTTP 200 responses
🔴 Trouble Signs
  • STATUS → Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, Error
  • READY → Shows 0/1 (container not ready)
  • RESTARTS → High number (app keeps crashing)
  • Events → FailedScheduling, FailedMount
  • Endpoints → Empty (selector doesn't match)
  • Describe → Warning events at bottom

8.9 Step 7: Access Your Application

The moment of truth — let's access our Django app through Kubernetes:

Accessing the app 
# Option 1: minikube service (opens browser automatically)
$ minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| NAMESPACE |      NAME      | TARGET PORT |            URL            |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
| anihpj-app| anihpj-service |          80 | http://192.168.49.2:30080 |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|---------------------------|
🎉 Opening service anihpj-app/anihpj-service in default browser...

# Option 2: Get the URL manually
$ minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app --url
http://192.168.49.2:30080

# Option 3: Port-forward directly to a pod
$ kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-service 8000:80 -n anihpj-app
# Visit http://localhost:8000/jobs/

🎉 Your App is Live on Kubernetes!

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                              │
  │   anihpj is running on Kubernetes!           │
  │                                              │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/         │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/1/       │
  │   📍 http://192.168.49.2:30080/skills/       │
  │                                              │
  │   3 replicas  •  auto-healing  •  scalable   │
  │                                              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

8.10 Step 8: Run Django Migrations

Django needs database migrations to set up tables. Run them inside one of the pods:

Running migrations & creating superuser 
# Get one of the pod names
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o name | head -1
pod/anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12

# Run migrations
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py migrate
Operations to perform:
  Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
Running migrations:
  Applying contenttypes.0001_initial... OK
  Applying auth.0001_initial... OK
  Applying jobpost.0001_initial... OK
  ...

# Create superuser
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py createsuperuser
Username: admin
Email: admin@anihpj.com
Password: ********

# Load sample data (if you have a fixture)
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-deployment-7d6b8c9f4-abc12 -n anihpj-app \
    -- python manage.py loaddata sample_data.json
⚠️ SQLite in Kubernetes

With SQLite, data is stored inside the container. If the pod restarts, the data is lost. This is fine for learning, but for production you'd use PostgreSQL or MySQL with a PersistentVolume (covered in Part 3). Migrations also only run on one pod — each pod has its own SQLite file.

8.11 Troubleshooting First Deployment

Things don't always go right the first time. Here are the most common issues and how to fix them:

Symptom Possible Cause Diagnosis Command Fix
ImagePullBackOff Image not found kubectl describe pod Check image name, add imagePullPolicy: Never for local
CrashLoopBackOff App crashes on start kubectl logs <pod> Fix code error, check env vars and dependencies
Pending Not enough resources kubectl describe pod Lower resource requests or add nodes
CreateContainerConfigError Missing ConfigMap/Secret kubectl describe pod Create the ConfigMap/Secret first
Connection refused Service misconfigured kubectl get svc + kubectl get endpoints Verify selector matches pod labels and ports match
ErrImageNeverPull Image not in local Docker docker images Run eval $(minikube docker-env) then rebuild
Debugging Workflow 
# 1. Check pod status
$ kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app
# Look for non-Running status

# 2. Get detailed events
$ kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n anihpj-app
# Scroll to "Events" section at the bottom

# 3. Check logs
$ kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj-app
# Add --previous to see logs from a crashed container

# 4. Shell in and inspect
$ kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n anihpj-app -- /bin/bash
# Check files, env vars, network connectivity

# 5. Check endpoints (Service → Pod binding)
$ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
# Should list pod IPs — empty means selector mismatch

🔍 Debugging Decision Flowchart

  Pod not working? Start here:
  ─────────────────────────────
                │
                ▼
      ┌───────────────────┐
      │ kubectl get pods  │
      │ What is STATUS?   │
      └────────┬──────────┘
               │
     ┌─────────┼──────────┬───────────────┐
     ▼         ▼          ▼               ▼
  Pending   ImagePull   CrashLoop     Running but
            BackOff     BackOff       not working
     │         │          │               │
     ▼         ▼          ▼               ▼
  describe   describe   kubectl        kubectl
  pod →      pod →      logs →         get svc →
  Events     Events     App error?     Endpoints
     │         │          │            empty?
     ▼         ▼          ▼               │
  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐    ┌──┴──┐
  │Resource││ │Image   ││ │Fix the││    │YES  │ NO
  │limits? ││ │name    ││ │code or││    │     │ │
  │Lower   ││ │wrong?  ││ │env var││    ▼     ▼
  │requests││ │Policy? ││ │error  ││  Fix    Port
  │or add  ││ │docker- ││ │       ││  label  mismatch?
  │nodes   ││ │env?    ││ │       ││  selector Check
  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘  └─────────┘

8.12 The Complete Deployment Workflow

Here's the entire end-to-end flow you just completed, in one visual summary:

End-to-End Deployment Pipeline

  ① PREPARE              ② BUILD              ③ DEPLOY              ④ VERIFY
  ┌────────────┐       ┌────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
  │ Dockerfile │──────▶│ docker     │──────▶│ kubectl apply  │───▶│ kubectl get │
  │ YAML files │       │ build -t   │       │ -f k8s/        │    │ all -n app  │
  │ .env       │       │ anihpj:v1  │       │   namespace    │    │ logs -f     │
  │ requirements│      └────────────┘       │   configmap    │    │ describe    │
  └────────────┘                            │   secret       │    └─────────────┘
                                            │   deployment   │          │
                                            │   service      │          ▼
                                            └────────────────┘    ⑤ ACCESS
                                                                  ┌─────────────┐
                                                                  │ minikube    │
                                                                  │ service     │
                                                                  │ anihpj-svc  │
                                                                  │ -n app      │
                                                                  │             │
                                                                  │ 🎉 LIVE!    │
                                                                  │ /jobs/      │
                                                                  │ /skills/    │
                                                                  └─────────────┘
Quick Reference — All Commands in Order 
# The complete deployment in 8 commands:

# 1. Point Docker to Minikube
eval $(minikube docker-env)

# 2. Build the image
docker build -t anihpj:v1 ./anihpj/

# 3. Deploy everything
kubectl apply -f k8s/namespace.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/secret.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f k8s/service.yaml

# 4. Or deploy all at once:
kubectl apply -f k8s/

# 5. Check status
kubectl get all -n anihpj-app

# 6. Run migrations
kubectl exec -it $(kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o name | head -1) \
    -n anihpj-app -- python manage.py migrate

# 7. Access the app
minikube service anihpj-service -n anihpj-app
💡 Part 2 Complete!

You've gone from zero to a fully deployed Django application on Kubernetes. You can now set up clusters, use kubectl fluently, write YAML manifests, and deploy real applications. In Part 3, we'll learn about ConfigMaps in depth, Secrets management, and Persistent Storage — the key to stateful applications.

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Full Application Deployment

⏱️ Target: 10 minutes

Deploy a complete application stack from scratch using only imperative commands and minimal YAML editing:

  1. Create a namespace called exam-app
  2. Create a ConfigMap named app-config with APP_ENV=production and LOG_LEVEL=info
  3. Create a Secret named app-secret with DB_PASSWORD=exam123
  4. Create a Deployment named web-app with image nginx:1.25, 3 replicas, mounting the ConfigMap as environment variables
  5. Expose the deployment as a ClusterIP service on port 80
  6. Verify all resources are running: pods, services, configmaps, secrets
  7. Check the logs of one pod to confirm it's working
  8. Exec into a pod and verify the environment variables are set
💡 Hint: Use --dry-run=client -o yaml for step 4 to generate the deployment, then edit to add envFrom
View Solution 
# 1. Create namespace
kubectl create ns exam-app

# 2. Create ConfigMap
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info \
  -n exam-app

# 3. Create Secret
kubectl create secret generic app-secret \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=exam123 \
  -n exam-app

# 4. Generate deployment YAML
kubectl create deployment web-app --image=nginx:1.25 \
  --replicas=3 -n exam-app \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web-app.yaml

# Edit web-app.yaml to add envFrom under containers:
#   envFrom:
#   - configMapRef:
#       name: app-config
#   - secretRef:
#       name: app-secret

kubectl apply -f web-app.yaml

# 5. Expose as service
kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80 -n exam-app

# 6. Verify all resources
kubectl get all,cm,secret -n exam-app

# 7. Check logs
kubectl logs -l app=web-app -n exam-app --tail=5

# 8. Verify env vars
kubectl exec -it deploy/web-app -n exam-app -- env | grep -E "APP_ENV|LOG_LEVEL|DB_PASSWORD"

Chapter 8 — Practice Questions

Q1
You're deploying a Django application (anihpj) to Kubernetes. What are the minimum Kubernetes resources you need, and in what order should you create them?
Show Answer
Answer

Minimum resources and creation order:

  1. Namespace — isolate the application
  2. ConfigMap — non-sensitive config (DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE, ALLOWED_HOSTS)
  3. Secret — sensitive data (SECRET_KEY, DB password)
  4. Deployment — runs the app containers (references ConfigMap + Secret)
  5. Service — exposes the Deployment internally
# Create in dependency order:
kubectl create namespace anihpj
kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml -n anihpj
kubectl apply -f secret.yaml -n anihpj
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml -n anihpj
kubectl apply -f service.yaml -n anihpj
Explanation

Order matters because the Deployment references ConfigMap and Secret. If they don't exist when the pod starts, it will fail with CreateContainerConfigError. On the CKAD, always create dependencies first. Using a multi-document YAML with --- separators lets you kubectl apply -f everything in one shot — K8s handles ordering.

Q2
Your Django app container starts but immediately crashes (CrashLoopBackOff). How do you debug this? List the commands in order.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check pod status and events
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n anihpj

# Step 2: Check application logs
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj --previous  # previous crash

# Step 3: Check if env vars are injected
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n anihpj -- env

# Step 4: If pod won't stay up, run a debug container
kubectl run debug --image=python:3.11 -n anihpj \
  --rm -it -- /bin/bash
Explanation

The --previous flag is critical — it shows logs from the last crashed container. Common Django crash causes: missing SECRET_KEY, database connection failure, syntax error in settings. On the CKAD, the debug sequence is always: describe → logs → exec. Master this pattern — it's tested in almost every troubleshooting question.

Q3
Write a Dockerfile for a Django application that: uses Python 3.11, installs dependencies from requirements.txt, copies the source, and runs with gunicorn on port 8000.
Show Answer
Answer
FROM python:3.11-slim

WORKDIR /app

# Install dependencies first (cache layer)
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Collect static files
RUN python manage.py collectstatic --noinput

EXPOSE 8000

CMD ["gunicorn", "anihpj.wsgi:application", \
     "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "--workers", "3"]
Explanation

Key best practices: (1) Use -slim base image to reduce size, (2) Copy requirements.txt separately for Docker layer caching, (3) --no-cache-dir reduces image size, (4) EXPOSE documents the port but doesn't publish it. The CKAD doesn't test Dockerfile writing, but understanding how images are built helps with container debugging.

Q4
How do you inject a ConfigMap as environment variables into a Deployment? Write the relevant YAML section using both envFrom and individual env with valueFrom.
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0

    # Method 1: ALL keys from ConfigMap as env vars
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config

    # Method 2: Individual keys (rename possible)
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_HOST        # env var name in pod
      valueFrom:
        configMapKeyRef:
          name: app-config       # ConfigMap name
          key: db_host           # key in ConfigMap
Explanation

envFrom imports ALL keys — faster but you can't rename them. valueFrom.configMapKeyRef imports individual keys with custom names. On the CKAD, envFrom is quicker to write. If a ConfigMap doesn't exist, the pod stays in CreateContainerConfigError. Add optional: true to allow the pod to start without it.

Q5
Your Django app needs the SECRET_KEY stored securely. Create a Kubernetes Secret and mount it as an environment variable. Include the full command and YAML.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create the secret imperatively
kubectl create secret generic django-secret \
  --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='my-super-secret-key-123' \
  -n anihpj
# Reference in Deployment:
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    env:
    - name: SECRET_KEY
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: django-secret
          key: SECRET_KEY
Explanation

Secrets are base64-encoded (NOT encrypted) by default. On the CKAD, use kubectl create secret generic for speed. For multiple keys: --from-literal=KEY1=val1 --from-literal=KEY2=val2. The pattern is identical to ConfigMap but uses secretKeyRef instead of configMapKeyRef.

Q6
You've deployed the anihpj app but the Service shows Endpoints: <none>. What's the most likely cause and how do you diagnose it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check 1: Service selector
kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj
# Look at: Selector: app=anihpj

# Check 2: Pod labels
kubectl get pods -n anihpj --show-labels
# Do they have app=anihpj?

# Check 3: Are pods Running?
kubectl get pods -n anihpj
# Endpoints only include Running pods

# Fix: Correct the label mismatch
kubectl label pod <pod-name> app=anihpj -n anihpj
Explanation

Endpoints: <none> means the Service's label selector doesn't match any running pods. The three causes: (1) labels don't match — most common, (2) pods aren't in Running state, (3) pods are in a different namespace. This is a top-3 CKAD debugging scenario. Always verify with kubectl get endpoints.

Q7
How do you run Django database migrations as part of a Kubernetes deployment? Should you put python manage.py migrate in the container command, an init container, or a Job?
Show Answer
Answer

Best practice: use an Init Container or a Job.

# Init Container approach (runs before main container):
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: migrate
    image: myapp:1.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config
    - secretRef:
        name: app-secret
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
Explanation

Don't put migrations in the container CMD — with multiple replicas, all pods would try to migrate simultaneously, causing race conditions. Init containers run sequentially before the main container. A Job is better for one-time migrations since it runs only once regardless of replicas. The CKAD tests init container configuration.

Q8
Write a kubectl port-forward command to access the anihpj app from your local browser. What's the difference between forwarding to a pod vs. a service?
Show Answer
Answer
# Forward to a Service (load-balanced to any pod)
kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-svc 8080:80 -n anihpj

# Forward to a specific Pod (single pod only)
kubectl port-forward pod/anihpj-abc123 8080:8000 -n anihpj

# Forward to a Deployment (picks one pod)
kubectl port-forward deploy/anihpj 8080:8000 -n anihpj

# Access in browser: http://localhost:8080
Explanation

Service forwarding respects the Service's port mapping (port → targetPort). Pod forwarding uses the container port directly. On the CKAD, use port-forward to verify your application is working correctly. Note: the forwarding only lasts while the command runs — it's not a permanent exposure mechanism.

Q9
Your anihpj deployment uses a SQLite database file. Why is this problematic in Kubernetes, and what's the correct solution?
Show Answer
Answer

Problem: SQLite stores data in a local file. In Kubernetes:

  • Each pod gets its own ephemeral filesystem — data lost on pod restart
  • Multiple replicas = multiple separate databases (no shared state)
  • Pod rescheduling to a different node loses all data

Solutions:
