  1. Use a PersistentVolumeClaim to store the SQLite file (single replica only)
  2. Switch to PostgreSQL/MySQL as a separate Deployment with a PVC (supports multiple replicas)
Explanation

This is a real-world architecture question the CKAD touches on. SQLite with a PVC works for development but not production (single-writer limitation). For the exam, understand that stateful data requires PersistentVolumes and that stateful databases should use StatefulSets with individual PVCs per replica.

Q10
After deploying all resources, write a single verification script that checks everything is working correctly.
Show Answer
Answer
#!/bin/bash
NS=anihpj
echo "=== Verification Checklist ==="

# 1. All pods running?
echo "--- Pods ---"
kubectl get pods -n $NS

# 2. Service has endpoints?
echo "--- Endpoints ---"
kubectl get endpoints -n $NS

# 3. ConfigMap and Secret exist?
echo "--- Config ---"
kubectl get cm,secret -n $NS

# 4. Env vars injected correctly?
echo "--- Env Vars ---"
kubectl exec deploy/anihpj -n $NS -- env | grep -E "DJANGO|SECRET"

# 5. App responding?
echo "--- Health Check ---"
kubectl exec deploy/anihpj -n $NS -- \
  curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" localhost:8000

# 6. Recent events (errors?)
echo "--- Events ---"
kubectl get events -n $NS --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -5
Explanation

This verification pattern is your CKAD safety net. After completing any exam task, always verify: pods Running, endpoints populated, env vars correct, app responding. The kubectl get events command catches issues you might miss. Build this habit — verification catches the majority of misconfigurations before they cost you points.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Secure Image Supply Chain

SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY 20%

Image Security Best Practices

PracticeInsecureSecure
Image tagnginx:latestnginx:1.25.3@sha256:abc...
Base imageFROM ubuntuFROM gcr.io/distroless/static
RegistryDocker Hub (public)Private registry with auth
Pull policyIfNotPresentAlways (with digest)
Image signingNo verificationcosign/Notary verification

Image Digest Pinning

# Always use digest — tags can be overwritten
containers:
- name: app
  image: registry.example.com/myapp@sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f6...
  # NOT: myapp:v1.0 (tag can point to different image)

Private Registry Authentication

# Create imagePullSecret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass

# Use in pod spec:
# spec.imagePullSecrets: [{name: regcred}]

Chapter 8 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A Deployment uses image: nginx:latest. Why is this a security risk and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Risk: "latest" tag can be overwritten with a malicious image
# An attacker who compromises the registry can push a new "latest"

# Fix 1: Use specific version tag
image: nginx:1.25.3

# Fix 2 (best): Use image digest
# Get the digest:
docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' nginx:1.25.3
# or: crane digest nginx:1.25.3

# Use in deployment:
image: nginx@sha256:6db391d1c0cfb30588ba0bf72ea999404f2764e...

# Fix 3: Enforce with admission controller
# OPA Gatekeeper policy to deny :latest tag
# or use ImagePolicyWebhook
Explanation

The :latest tag is mutable — anyone with registry push access can replace it. Image digests (SHA256) are immutable. On CKS, always use digests for production. An admission webhook can enforce this policy cluster-wide. This is core Supply Chain Security.

CKS-2
How do you restrict a cluster to only pull images from approved registries?
Show Answer
Answer
# OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate + Constraint:

# 1. ConstraintTemplate — defines the policy logic
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8sallowedrepos
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sAllowedRepos
      validation:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            repos:
              type: array
              items:
                type: string
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8sallowedrepos
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        not startswith(container.image, input.parameters.repos[_])
        msg := sprintf("Image '%s' not from allowed repo", [container.image])
      }

# 2. Constraint — applies the policy
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sAllowedRepos
metadata:
  name: only-trusted-registries
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      kinds: ["Pod"]
  parameters:
    repos:
    - "registry.example.com/"
    - "gcr.io/distroless/"
Explanation

OPA Gatekeeper acts as a ValidatingAdmissionWebhook. ConstraintTemplates define reusable policies in Rego. Constraints apply them with parameters. This setup rejects any pod using images not from approved registries. On CKS, know how to create and apply Gatekeeper policies.

CKS-3
Scan a container image for vulnerabilities using Trivy. How do you identify and fix CRITICAL CVEs?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scan image for vulnerabilities
trivy image nginx:1.25

# Filter critical only
trivy image --severity CRITICAL nginx:1.25

# Output shows:
# Library    Installed  Fixed      CVE             Severity
# libssl3    3.0.11     3.0.13     CVE-2024-xxxx   CRITICAL
# libcurl    7.88.1     7.88.2     CVE-2024-yyyy   HIGH

# Fix options:
# 1. Update to patched version
image: nginx:1.25.4   # Check if fixed version available

# 2. Use minimal base image (fewer packages = fewer CVEs)
image: nginx:1.25-alpine   # Alpine has fewer packages

# 3. Use distroless images (no shell, no package manager)
image: gcr.io/distroless/static:latest

# Scan output as JSON for CI/CD:
trivy image -f json -o results.json nginx:1.25
# Exit code 1 on critical findings:
trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL nginx:1.25
Explanation

Trivy scans container images for known CVEs in OS packages and language libraries. On CKS, you'll use Trivy to find vulnerabilities and determine which images to update. Use --severity CRITICAL to focus on the most dangerous issues. Distroless and Alpine images have significantly smaller attack surfaces.

CKS-4
What is ImagePolicyWebhook and how does it differ from OPA Gatekeeper for image validation?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureImagePolicyWebhookOPA Gatekeeper
TypeBuilt-in admission pluginExternal admission controller
FocusImage validation onlyAny resource validation
Policy languageExternal webhook serviceRego (OPA)
ConfigurationAdmissionConfiguration fileCRDs (ConstraintTemplate)
 
# ImagePolicyWebhook config:
# /etc/kubernetes/admission-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: AdmissionConfiguration
plugins:
- name: ImagePolicyWebhook
  configuration:
    imagePolicy:
      kubeConfigFile: /etc/kubernetes/admission-kubeconfig.yaml
      allowTTL: 50
      denyTTL: 50
      retryBackoff: 500
      defaultAllow: false  # Deny if webhook unreachable
Explanation

ImagePolicyWebhook is a built-in admission plugin specifically for image validation (e.g., checking signatures, scanning results). OPA Gatekeeper is more general-purpose. On CKS, know both approaches. Set defaultAllow: false to deny images when the webhook is unreachable (fail-closed).

CKS-5
How do you build a secure container image using a multi-stage Dockerfile?
Show Answer
Answer
# Secure multi-stage Dockerfile
# Stage 1: Build
FROM golang:1.22 AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o /app/server

# Stage 2: Runtime (minimal image)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server
USER 65534:65534            # Run as non-root (nobody)
EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]

# Security benefits:
# ✅ No shell (can't exec into container)
# ✅ No package manager (can't install tools)
# ✅ No build tools in final image
# ✅ Runs as non-root user
# ✅ Minimal CVE surface area
Explanation

Multi-stage builds separate build dependencies from runtime, dramatically reducing image size and attack surface. Distroless images contain only the application — no shell, no package manager. On CKS, understand why smaller images are more secure: fewer packages = fewer CVEs, no exec capability for attackers.

⚙️ Part 3: Configuration & Storage Chapters 9-11

Chapter 9: ConfigMaps — Managing Configuration

Learn how to externalize application configuration from your container images. ConfigMaps let you change settings without rebuilding images — the Kubernetes way of managing environment-specific configuration.

⚙️ Configuration 🔑 Key-Value Pairs 📦 Environment Injection 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What ConfigMaps are and why they exist
  • ✅ 4 ways to create ConfigMaps (literal, file, env-file, YAML)
  • ✅ 3 ways to consume ConfigMaps in Pods (envFrom, selective, volume)
  • ✅ Complete ConfigMap setup for the anihpj Django project
  • ✅ Updating strategies and immutable ConfigMaps
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Environment, Configuration & Security 25%
ConfigMaps on the CKAD — High-Frequency Topic

ConfigMaps appear in ~30% of CKAD questions. Master these patterns:

  • Create imperatively — kubectl create cm my-config --from-literal=key=value
  • From files — kubectl create cm my-config --from-file=config.txt (key = filename, value = file content)
  • Inject as env vars — Use envFrom.configMapRef for ALL keys or env.valueFrom.configMapKeyRef for specific keys
  • Mount as volume — Each key becomes a file in the mount path
  • Exam trick — When asked to "use a ConfigMap as a volume", the directory path is volumeMounts.mountPath, each key becomes a filename

ConfigMap Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
From literalskubectl create cm app-config --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432
From filekubectl create cm app-config --from-file=config.txt
From env filekubectl create cm app-config --from-env-file=app.env
From directorykubectl create cm app-config --from-file=./configs/
View datakubectl get cm app-config -o yaml
Describekubectl describe cm app-config
Edit livekubectl edit cm app-config
Deletekubectl delete cm app-config

ConfigMap as Environment Variables vs Volume Mount

The exam will ask for one of these two patterns. Know both cold.

# PATTERN 1: All ConfigMap keys as env vars
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    envFrom:                      # ← ALL keys become env vars
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config

# PATTERN 2: Specific key as env var
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_HOST         # ← env var name in container
      valueFrom:
        configMapKeyRef:
          name: app-config
          key: DB_HOST            # ← key from ConfigMap

# PATTERN 3: Mount as volume (all keys become files)
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/config      # ← ConfigMap keys are files here
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config

# PATTERN 4: Mount specific key as specific filename
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config
      items:
      - key: nginx.conf           # ← ConfigMap key
        path: default.conf        # ← filename in mount directory

ConfigMap Traps

  • ❌ ConfigMap must exist before the Pod — if the CM doesn't exist, pod stays in CreateContainerConfigError
  • ✅ Create ConfigMap first, then the Pod (or use optional: true to allow missing CMs)
  • Volume-mounted ConfigMaps auto-update (~60s delay) — but envFrom does NOT auto-update (requires pod restart)
  • Immutable ConfigMaps — set immutable: true to prevent changes. Cannot be reversed (must delete and recreate)
  • Subpath mount — subPath: file.conf mounts a single file without hiding existing directory contents. Exam loves this

🗺️ ConfigMap Injection Methods

ConfigMap
🔑 Single Env Var valueFrom → configMapKeyRef
📋 All Env Vars envFrom → configMapRef
📁 Volume Mount volumes → configMap (auto-updates)
📄 SubPath Mount Single file without hiding directory
❌ ConfigMap Anti-Patterns
  • Storing sensitive data (passwords, tokens) in ConfigMap
  • Creating Pod before the ConfigMap exists
  • Using envFrom and expecting auto-updates
  • Exceeding 1 MiB ConfigMap size limit
  • Forgetting --from-file includes the filename as key
✅ ConfigMap Best Practices
  • Use Secrets for sensitive data, ConfigMaps for config
  • Create ConfigMap FIRST, then the Pod
  • Use volume mounts when you need auto-updates
  • Set immutable: true for production configs
  • Use --from-literal for explicit key=value control
4 Creation Methods
3 Injection Patterns
1 MiB Max Size Limit
~60s Volume Update Delay

9.1 What are ConfigMaps?

A ConfigMap is a Kubernetes API object that stores non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs. It separates your configuration from your container image, so you can change settings without rebuilding or redeploying your application.

🧠 The Core Idea: Separate Config from Code

  ❌ WITHOUT ConfigMaps (hardcoded):
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Docker Image                                │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  settings.py                           │  │
  │  │  DEBUG = False          ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS = '*'    ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  │  DB_HOST = 'postgres'   ← hardcoded!   │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │  Change any setting? → Rebuild entire image! │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ✅ WITH ConfigMaps (externalized):
  ┌──────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────┐
  │ Docker Image │      │  ConfigMap (K8s)     │
  │ ┌──────────┐ │      │                     │
  │ │ settings │◀├──────┤  DEBUG: "False"      │
  │ │ reads    │ │ env  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"  │
  │ │ from env │ │ vars │  DB_HOST: "postgres" │
  │ └──────────┘ │      └─────────────────────┘
  │ Same image   │      Change config? Just update
  │ everywhere!  │      the ConfigMap! No rebuild!
  └──────────────┘
🔄 Analogy: Django's settings.py

Think of a ConfigMap like externalizing your settings.py. Instead of hardcoding DEBUG = False inside the image, you store it in a ConfigMap and inject it as an environment variable. Same image runs in dev (DEBUG=True) and prod (DEBUG=False).

📏 ConfigMap Quick Facts
  • Max size: 1 MB per ConfigMap
  • Data type: Non-sensitive only
  • Namespace-scoped: Lives in a specific namespace
  • NOT encrypted: Use Secrets for sensitive data
  • Can store: Simple values, entire config files, JSON/YAML
⚠️ ConfigMaps Are NOT for Sensitive Data

Never store passwords, API keys, tokens, or certificates in ConfigMaps. They are stored in plain text and visible to anyone with read access. Use Secrets (Chapter 10) for sensitive data.

9.2 Creating ConfigMaps — 4 Methods

Kubernetes gives you four ways to create ConfigMaps. Each method fits different workflows:

📋 4 Methods at a Glance

  Method           Source               Best For
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  --from-literal   Command-line args    Quick one-off values
  --from-file      Any file             Config files (nginx.conf, etc.)
  --from-env-file  .env formatted       Migrating from Docker .env
  YAML manifest    Full YAML file       GitOps, version control ⭐

9.2a From Literal Values (--from-literal)

The quickest way — pass key=value pairs directly on the command line:

Creating from literal values 
# Create a ConfigMap with multiple key-value pairs
$ kubectl create configmap anihpj-config \
    --from-literal=DEBUG=False \
    --from-literal=ALLOWED_HOSTS=* \
    --from-literal=DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql

configmap/anihpj-config created

# Verify what was created
$ kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: default
data:
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: django.db.backends.postgresql
  DEBUG: "False"

9.2b From a File (--from-file)

Store an entire file as a single key in the ConfigMap. The filename becomes the key, the file content becomes the value:

Creating from a file 
# Create a config file first
$ cat > nginx.conf << 'EOF'
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name anihpj.example.com;
    location / {
        proxy_pass http://localhost:8000;
    }
}
EOF

# Create ConfigMap from the file
$ kubectl create configmap nginx-config --from-file=nginx.conf
configmap/nginx-config created

# The key = filename, value = file contents
$ kubectl describe configmap nginx-config
Name:         nginx-config
Data
====
nginx.conf:     ← key is the filename
----
server {        ← value is the full file content
    listen 80;
    ...
}

9.2c From an Env File (--from-env-file)

If you already have a .env file from Docker Compose, you can import it directly:

Creating from .env file 
# Your existing .env file
$ cat .env
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=*
DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql
DATABASE_NAME=anihpj_db

# Import directly — each line becomes a separate key
$ kubectl create configmap anihpj-config --from-env-file=.env
configmap/anihpj-config created

# Each line is now a separate key-value pair
$ kubectl get configmap anihpj-config -o jsonpath='{.data}' | python -m json.tool
{
    "ALLOWED_HOSTS": "*",
    "DATABASE_ENGINE": "django.db.backends.postgresql",
    "DATABASE_NAME": "anihpj_db",
    "DEBUG": "False"
}
💡 Migrating from Docker Compose?

If you currently use env_file: .env in your docker-compose.yml, the --from-env-file method is a direct migration path. Same file format, same key=value syntax.

9.2d From YAML Manifest (Recommended ⭐)

The best method for production — version-controlled, declarative, and reproducible:

k8s/configmap.yaml — The GitOps Way 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: config
data:
  # Simple key-value pairs
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"

  # You can also embed entire config files!
  settings_override.py: |
    # This file can be volume-mounted into the container
    CACHES = {
        'default': {
            'BACKEND': 'django.core.cache.backends.redis.RedisCache',
            'LOCATION': 'redis://redis-service:6379',
        }
    }
Apply it 
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
configmap/anihpj-config created

$ kubectl get cm -n anihpj-app
NAME             DATA   AGE
anihpj-config    8      5s

9.3 Using ConfigMaps in Pods — 3 Ways

Once a ConfigMap exists, you need to inject its data into your pods. Kubernetes offers three methods, each suited for different scenarios:

📊 3 Methods — Visual Data Flow

  Method A: envFrom             Method B: Selective Key        Method C: Volume Mount
  (ALL keys → env vars)         (Pick specific keys)           (Keys → files)

  ┌──────────────┐            ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │  ConfigMap   │            │  ConfigMap   │             │  ConfigMap   │
  │  DEBUG=False │            │  DEBUG=False │             │  DEBUG=False │
  │  HOST=*      │            │  HOST=*      │             │  HOST=*      │
  │  DB=postgres │            │  DB=postgres │             │  DB=postgres │
  └──────┬───────┘            └──────┬───────┘             └──────┬───────┘
         │ ALL keys                  │ pick one                   │ as files
         ▼                           ▼                            ▼
  ┌──────────────┐            ┌──────────────┐             ┌──────────────┐
  │  Pod env:    │            │  Pod env:    │             │  Pod files:  │
  │  $DEBUG=False│            │  $HOST=*     │             │/etc/config/  │
  │  $HOST=*     │            │  (only this  │             │  DEBUG → 0   │
  │  $DB=postgres│            │   one key)   │             │  HOST  → *   │
  └──────────────┘            └──────────────┘             │  DB → pg     │
                                                           └──────────────┘
  Best for:                   Best for:                    Best for:
  All config in pod           Cherry-picking from          Config files,
                              multiple ConfigMaps          hot-reload ⭐

9.3a As Environment Variables — envFrom (All Keys)

The simplest approach — inject every key from the ConfigMap as an environment variable:

Pod with envFrom — all ConfigMap keys become env vars 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      envFrom:                        # ← inject ALL keys
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config       # ← from this ConfigMap
Verify inside the pod 
# Check that env vars are set
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- env | grep -E 'DEBUG|ALLOWED|DATABASE'
DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS=*
DATABASE_ENGINE=django.db.backends.postgresql
DATABASE_NAME=anihpj_db
DATABASE_HOST=postgres-service

9.3b As Individual Env Vars — Selective Keys

When you need specific keys from one or multiple ConfigMaps, or want to rename the environment variable:

Pod with selective keys — cherry-pick what you need 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      env:
        # Pick specific keys from a ConfigMap
        - name: DJANGO_DEBUG              # ← env var name in the pod
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-config         # ← which ConfigMap
              key: DEBUG                  # ← which key

        # You can pick from DIFFERENT ConfigMaps
        - name: DB_ENGINE
          valueFrom:
            configMapKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-config
              key: DATABASE_ENGINE

        # Mix with direct values too
        - name: APP_VERSION
          value: "2.1.0"                  # ← hardcoded value

9.3c As Volume Mounts — Files in a Directory

Mount the ConfigMap as files inside the container. Each key becomes a filename, each value becomes the file content. This is the only method that supports hot-reload — Kubernetes updates the files automatically when the ConfigMap changes.

Pod with ConfigMap volume mount 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: config-volume
          mountPath: /etc/config          # ← mount point inside container
          readOnly: true
  volumes:
    - name: config-volume
      configMap:
        name: anihpj-config              # ← which ConfigMap to use
What it looks like inside the pod 
# Each key becomes a file
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- ls /etc/config/
ALLOWED_HOSTS
DATABASE_ENGINE
DATABASE_HOST
DATABASE_NAME
DATABASE_PORT
DEBUG
STATIC_URL

# File content = key value
$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/config/DEBUG
False

$ kubectl exec anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/config/DATABASE_HOST
postgres-service
💡 Volume Mounts Support Hot-Reload!

When a ConfigMap is updated, volume-mounted files are automatically refreshed (within ~60 seconds). This means your app can detect and apply configuration changes without restarting. Environment variables do NOT auto-update — pods must be restarted.

📊 Which Method Should You Use?

Feature envFrom (All) Selective Key Volume Mount
Ease of use ⭐⭐⭐ Simplest ⭐⭐ Moderate ⭐ Most setup
Granularity All or nothing Pick specific keys All or specific
Hot-reload ❌ Needs restart ❌ Needs restart ✅ Auto-updates files
Key renaming ❌ Uses original keys ✅ Custom env var names ✅ Custom filenames
Best for Simple apps, all config Multi-source, renaming Config files, dynamic

9.4 ConfigMap for Django Settings in anihpj

Here's a complete, production-ready ConfigMap for our anihpj Django project. This is what your deployment YAML references:

k8s/configmap.yaml — Complete anihpj Config 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: config
    environment: production
data:
  # Django core settings
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com,www.anihpj.example.com"

  # Database settings (non-sensitive parts only!)
  DATABASE_ENGINE: "django.db.backends.postgresql"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"         # K8s Service name
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"

  # Static and media files
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
  MEDIA_URL: "/media/"
  STATIC_ROOT: "/app/staticfiles/"
  MEDIA_ROOT: "/app/media/"

  # Application settings
  LANGUAGE_CODE: "en-us"
  TIME_ZONE: "UTC"
  PAGINATION_SIZE: "20"

🔗 How ConfigMap Connects to the Deployment

  ┌──────────────────┐     envFrom:           ┌──────────────────────┐
  │   ConfigMap      │     configMapRef:      │   Deployment         │
  │   anihpj-config  │────────────────────▶  │   anihpj-deployment  │
  │                  │                        │                      │
  │  DEBUG=False     │     Pod reads these    │   Container env:     │
  │  DB_HOST=pg-svc  │     as env vars        │   $DEBUG → False     │
  │  DB_PORT=5432    │     at startup         │   $DB_HOST → pg-svc  │
  │  STATIC_URL=/s/  │                        │   $DB_PORT → 5432    │
  └──────────────────┘                        └──────────────────────┘
                                                       │
  ┌──────────────────┐     secretRef:                  │
  │   Secret         │────────────────────────────────│
  │   anihpj-secret  │     (passwords, keys)          │
  │  SECRET_KEY=xxx  │                                ▼
  │  DB_PASS=yyy     │                          ┌──────────┐
  └──────────────────┘                          │ Django    │
                                                │ settings  │
                                                │ reads env │
                                                │ os.environ│
                                                └──────────┘
settings.py — Reading from environment 
import os

# These values come from ConfigMap (env vars)
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DEBUG', 'True') == 'True'
ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_HOSTS', '*').split(',')

# Database — ConfigMap for host/port, Secret for password
DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': os.environ.get('DATABASE_ENGINE', 'django.db.backends.sqlite3'),
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DATABASE_NAME', BASE_DIR / 'db.sqlite3'),
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DATABASE_HOST', 'localhost'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PORT', '5432'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DATABASE_USER', 'anihpj'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PASSWORD', ''),  # From Secret!
    }
}

STATIC_URL = os.environ.get('STATIC_URL', '/static/')
MEDIA_URL = os.environ.get('MEDIA_URL', '/media/')

9.5 Updating ConfigMaps

What happens when you need to change a ConfigMap that's already in use? The behavior depends on how the ConfigMap is consumed:

📂 Volume Mounts

✅ Auto-updates (~60 seconds)

Files inside the mounted directory are automatically refreshed by the kubelet. Your app can detect and reload the new values — no restart needed.

🔤 Environment Variables

❌ NOT auto-updated

Environment variables are set at pod startup and never change. You must restart (delete) the pod for new values to take effect.

Updating and restarting 
# Method 1: Edit directly
$ kubectl edit configmap anihpj-config -n anihpj-app
# Opens in editor → change values → save → done

# Method 2: Replace with updated YAML
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml

# For env-var based pods — you MUST restart:
$ kubectl rollout restart deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
deployment.apps/anihpj-deployment restarted

# Watch the rolling restart
$ kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj-deployment -n anihpj-app
⚠️ ConfigMap Updates ≠ Automatic Pod Restarts

Kubernetes will never automatically restart pods when a ConfigMap changes. If your app consumes config via environment variables, you must trigger a kubectl rollout restart manually. Only volume-mounted ConfigMaps auto-refresh the files (but your app still needs to re-read them).

9.6 Immutable ConfigMaps

For configuration that should never change once deployed, Kubernetes supports immutable ConfigMaps:

Immutable ConfigMap 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-config-v2
  namespace: anihpj-app
immutable: true                    # ← Cannot be changed after creation!
data:
  DEBUG: "False"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"
🔒 Protection

Prevents accidental changes. Any kubectl edit or apply with modified data will be rejected.

⚡ Performance

The kubelet skips periodic re-checks on immutable ConfigMaps, reducing API server load at scale.

📋 Versioning

To update, create a new ConfigMap (e.g., config-v3) and update your Deployment to reference it.

🛠️ ConfigMap kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all ConfigMaps kubectl get configmaps / kubectl get cm
View ConfigMap data kubectl describe cm anihpj-config
View as YAML kubectl get cm anihpj-config -o yaml
Get a specific key kubectl get cm anihpj-config -o jsonpath='{.data.DEBUG}'
Edit interactively kubectl edit cm anihpj-config
Delete ConfigMap kubectl delete cm anihpj-config
Create from YAML kubectl apply -f k8s/configmap.yaml
Dry-run + export kubectl create cm my-cm --from-literal=k=v --dry-run=client -o yaml

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: ConfigMap Mastery

⏱️ Target: 7 minutes
  1. Create a ConfigMap named app-settings with keys APP_COLOR=blue and APP_MODE=production
  2. Create a Pod named webapp (image: nginx) that loads ALL ConfigMap keys as environment variables using envFrom
  3. Create another ConfigMap named nginx-conf from a file: create /tmp/nginx.conf with content server { listen 8080; }, then create the ConfigMap from it
  4. Create a Pod named web-custom (image: nginx) that mounts nginx-conf ConfigMap as a volume at /etc/nginx/conf.d
  5. Verify the file exists inside the pod at the correct path
💡 Hint: envFrom vs env, volumes + volumeMounts
View Solution 
# 1. Create ConfigMap from literals
kubectl create configmap app-settings \
  --from-literal=APP_COLOR=blue \
  --from-literal=APP_MODE=production

# 2. Pod with envFrom (generate then edit)
kubectl run webapp --image=nginx \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > webapp.yaml
# Add under spec.containers[0]:
#   envFrom:
#   - configMapRef:
#       name: app-settings
kubectl apply -f webapp.yaml

# 3. Create ConfigMap from file
echo 'server { listen 8080; }' > /tmp/nginx.conf
kubectl create configmap nginx-conf --from-file=/tmp/nginx.conf

# 4. Pod with volume mount
kubectl run web-custom --image=nginx \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > custom.yaml
# Add to spec:
#   volumes:
#   - name: nginx-vol
#     configMap:
#       name: nginx-conf
# Add to containers:
#   volumeMounts:
#   - name: nginx-vol
#     mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
kubectl apply -f custom.yaml

# 5. Verify
kubectl exec web-custom -- ls /etc/nginx/conf.d/
kubectl exec web-custom -- cat /etc/nginx/conf.d/nginx.conf

Chapter 9 — Practice Questions

Q1
Create a ConfigMap named app-config with two keys: APP_ENV=production and LOG_LEVEL=info. Use the imperative command, then verify the contents.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create ConfigMap
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info

# Verify contents
kubectl get configmap app-config -o yaml
kubectl describe configmap app-config
Explanation

--from-literal creates key-value pairs directly. For multiple keys from a file: --from-file=config.properties (each line becomes a key). For an entire file as a single key: --from-file=key-name=file.conf. On the CKAD, imperative creation is faster than writing the YAML manually.

Q2
What are the three ways to consume a ConfigMap in a pod? Demonstrate each with YAML.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: envFrom (all keys as env vars)
envFrom:
- configMapRef:
    name: app-config

# Method 2: Single key as env var
env:
- name: MY_ENV
  valueFrom:
    configMapKeyRef:
      name: app-config
      key: APP_ENV

# Method 3: Mount as volume (files)
volumes:
- name: config-vol
  configMap:
    name: app-config
containers:
- volumeMounts:
  - name: config-vol
    mountPath: /etc/config
Explanation

Volume mounts create files (one per key) at the mount path. The key becomes the filename, the value becomes the content. Volume-mounted ConfigMaps update automatically (within ~60 seconds), while environment variables do NOT update until the pod restarts. The CKAD may test both approaches.

Q3
Create a ConfigMap from a file. You have nginx.conf on disk. Mount it into a pod at /etc/nginx/nginx.conf without overwriting the entire directory.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create ConfigMap from file
kubectl create configmap nginx-config --from-file=nginx.conf
# Mount a SINGLE file without overwriting directory
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: nginx-conf
    configMap:
      name: nginx-config
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: nginx-conf
      mountPath: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
      subPath: nginx.conf          # ← KEY: prevents directory overwrite
Explanation

subPath is the critical field here. Without it, mounting to /etc/nginx/ would replace the entire directory with only the ConfigMap files — deleting other nginx configs. With subPath, only the specific file is mounted. Downside: subPath mounts do NOT auto-update when the ConfigMap changes.

Q4
A pod references a ConfigMap that doesn't exist. What happens? How can you make the pod start anyway?
Show Answer
Answer

Default behavior: Pod stays in CreateContainerConfigError and never starts.

# Make it optional:
# For envFrom:
envFrom:
- configMapRef:
    name: maybe-missing
    optional: true        # ← Pod starts even if CM missing

# For a volume mount:
volumes:
- name: config-vol
  configMap:
    name: maybe-missing
    optional: true
Explanation

This is a common CKAD troubleshooting scenario. If a pod is stuck in CreateContainerConfigError, check kubectl describe pod for events like "configmap not found". The fix is either (1) create the missing ConfigMap, or (2) add optional: true if the config isn't required for startup.

Q5
You need to update a ConfigMap value from LOG_LEVEL=info to LOG_LEVEL=debug. How do you update it, and how do you ensure running pods pick up the change?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Edit in-place
kubectl edit configmap app-config

# Method 2: Replace entirely
kubectl create configmap app-config \
  --from-literal=APP_ENV=production \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=debug \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl replace -f -

# Force pods to reload:
# Volume mounts: auto-update in ~60 seconds
# Env vars: must restart pods
kubectl rollout restart deployment/my-app
Explanation

The key distinction: volume-mounted ConfigMaps auto-update, env var ConfigMaps do NOT. For env vars, you must restart the pod. kubectl rollout restart does a graceful rolling restart. On the CKAD, this behavior difference is frequently tested — know when a restart is needed.

Q6
Can you make a ConfigMap immutable? Why would you want to?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
data:
  APP_ENV: production
immutable: true          # ← Cannot be modified after creation
Explanation

Immutable ConfigMaps: (1) prevent accidental changes in production, (2) improve cluster performance — the API server doesn't need to watch for changes, significantly reducing load when you have thousands of ConfigMaps. To update an immutable ConfigMap, you must delete and recreate it. This is a GA feature since K8s 1.21.

Q7
Create a ConfigMap from a multi-line configuration file content directly in YAML (not from a file). Show how nginx.conf content would be embedded.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: nginx-config
data:
  nginx.conf: |
    server {
        listen 80;
        server_name localhost;
        location / {
            proxy_pass http://backend:8000;
            proxy_set_header Host $host;
        }
    }

The | (pipe) preserves newlines. Use |- to strip the trailing newline.

Explanation

YAML multi-line strings: | (literal — preserves newlines), > (folded — joins lines with spaces). On the CKAD, you'll often embed entire config files this way. The key (e.g., nginx.conf) becomes the filename when volume-mounted. Make sure the indentation under | is consistent.

Q8
What's the maximum size of a ConfigMap? What happens if your configuration data exceeds this limit?
Show Answer
Answer

Maximum size: 1 MiB (1,048,576 bytes) for the entire ConfigMap.

Exceeding this limit causes the API server to reject the creation with an error. If you need more:

  • Split across multiple ConfigMaps
  • Use a PersistentVolume for large config files
  • Mount from an external config service (Vault, etc.)
Explanation

ConfigMaps are stored in etcd, which has a per-object size limit. The same 1 MiB limit applies to Secrets. In practice, ConfigMaps should contain configuration, not large datasets. On the CKAD, you're unlikely to hit this limit, but understanding it shows architectural awareness.

Q9
You want to mount only specific keys from a ConfigMap as files (not all keys). How do you do this?
Show Answer
Answer
volumes:
- name: config-vol
  configMap:
    name: app-config
    items:                   # ← Select specific keys
    - key: nginx.conf        # key in ConfigMap
      path: nginx.conf       # filename in mount
    - key: mime.types
      path: mime.types
containers:
- volumeMounts:
  - name: config-vol
    mountPath: /etc/nginx/
Explanation

Using items gives you selective mounting — only the keys you list become files. You can also rename files: set path to a different filename than key. Without items, ALL keys in the ConfigMap become files in the mount directory. This is useful when a ConfigMap has many keys but you only need a few mounted as files.

Q10
You have a running pod using envFrom to load a ConfigMap. You update the ConfigMap. Does the pod see the new values immediately? Explain the behavior for each injection method.
Show Answer
Answer
Injection MethodAuto-Updates?DelayAction Needed
envFrom❌ No—Restart pod / rollout restart
env.valueFrom❌ No—Restart pod / rollout restart
Volume mount✅ Yes~60 secondsNone (automatic)
Volume + subPath❌ No—Restart pod
Explanation

This is a frequently tested CKAD concept. Environment variables are set at pod creation and frozen. Volume mounts use symlinks that kubelet updates periodically. The subPath exception is critical — it bypasses the symlink mechanism. If the exam asks about "live config updates," volume mounts (without subPath) are the answer.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — ConfigMap Security Anti-Patterns

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

ConfigMap Security Risks

Anti-PatternRiskCorrect Approach
Storing passwords in ConfigMapVisible to anyone with CM read access, not encryptedUse Secrets with encryption at rest
Database connection strings with credentialsCredentials exposed in env vars, logsSeparate credentials into Secret, reference via env
API keys in ConfigMapNo access auditing, plaintext in etcdExternal secret store (Vault, cloud KMS)
Mounting all ConfigMap dataOver-exposure of configurationMount only needed keys with items

Chapter 9 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A ConfigMap contains a database password. Identify the security issue and migrate the sensitive data to a Secret.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Find the insecure ConfigMap
kubectl get cm app-config -o yaml
# data:
#   DB_HOST: mysql.default.svc
#   DB_PASSWORD: SuperSecret123    ← INSECURE!

# 2. Create a Secret for sensitive data
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=SuperSecret123

# 3. Update the ConfigMap (remove password)
kubectl edit cm app-config
# Remove DB_PASSWORD from data

# 4. Update Deployment to use both
env:
  - name: DB_HOST
    valueFrom:
      configMapKeyRef:
        name: app-config
        key: DB_HOST
  - name: DB_PASSWORD
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:          # From Secret, not ConfigMap
        name: db-creds
        key: DB_PASSWORD
Explanation

ConfigMaps are not designed for sensitive data — they are stored plaintext in etcd with no special access controls. Secrets (with encryption at rest) are the minimum for sensitive data. On CKS, always check ConfigMaps for accidentally stored credentials and migrate them to Secrets.

CKS-2
How do you limit which pods can access a specific ConfigMap using RBAC?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create a ServiceAccount for the pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: app-sa
  namespace: production
---
# 2. Role that allows reading only specific ConfigMaps
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: cm-reader
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["configmaps"]
  resourceNames: ["app-config"]     # Only THIS ConfigMap
  verbs: ["get"]
---
# 3. Bind Role to ServiceAccount
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: cm-reader-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: app-sa
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: cm-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
Explanation

Use resourceNames in RBAC to limit access to specific ConfigMaps/Secrets. Assign a dedicated ServiceAccount to each application. On CKS, this is part of the least-privilege principle — pods should only access the specific resources they need.

CKS-3
How can ConfigMap data leak through environment variables? How do you prevent it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Leak vector 1: Application crash dumps include env vars
# Many frameworks log ALL env vars on startup or crash

# Leak vector 2: kubectl describe shows env values
kubectl describe pod myapp
# Environment:
#   DB_HOST: mysql.default.svc
#   API_KEY: sk-secret123    ← Visible in describe output!

# Leak vector 3: Process listing shows env
kubectl exec myapp -- env
# All environment variables visible

# Prevention:
# 1. Mount as files instead of env vars
volumes:
- name: config
  configMap:
    name: app-config
    items:              # Mount only specific keys
    - key: config.yaml
      path: config.yaml
      mode: 0400        # Read-only, owner only

# 2. Never use envFrom (exposes everything)
# BAD:  envFrom: [{configMapRef: {name: app-config}}]
# GOOD: Use individual valueFrom with specific keys
Explanation

Environment variables are visible via kubectl describe, env command, and crash logs. Mounting as files with restricted permissions is more secure. On CKS, prefer volume mounts over env vars for any data that shouldn't be easily visible. Use items to mount only needed keys.

CKS-4
How do you ensure immutable ConfigMaps to prevent accidental or malicious changes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Make ConfigMap immutable
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: app-config
  namespace: production
data:
  config.yaml: |
    database:
      host: mysql.default.svc
      port: 3306
immutable: true          # Cannot be modified after creation
# Once immutable, any edit attempt fails:
kubectl edit cm app-config
# Error: ConfigMap is immutable

# To modify, you must delete and recreate:
# 1. Create new versioned ConfigMap
kubectl create cm app-config-v2 --from-file=config.yaml
# 2. Update Deployment to reference new ConfigMap
# 3. Delete old ConfigMap

# Security benefit: prevents runtime tampering
# Performance benefit: kubelet doesn't watch for changes
Explanation

Immutable ConfigMaps cannot be modified after creation — protecting against accidental or malicious changes. This is a CKS security best practice. The trade-off is you must create new ConfigMaps for updates. Immutable ConfigMaps also improve cluster performance as the kubelet doesn't need to watch them.

CKS-5
What is the difference between storing sensitive data in ConfigMaps vs Secrets from a security perspective?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureConfigMapSecret
Data encodingPlaintextBase64 (NOT encryption!)
Encryption at rest❌ Never✅ With EncryptionConfiguration
RBAC separationSame as other resourcesCan restrict Secret access separately
etcd storagePlaintextEncrypted (if configured)
Audit loggingStandardCan log Secret access specifically
tmpfs mounting❌ Regular filesystem✅ tmpfs (memory-only, not on disk)
 
# Key CKS point: base64 is NOT encryption!
echo "password123" | base64   # cGFzc3dvcmQxMjMK
echo "cGFzc3dvcmQxMjMK" | base64 -d  # password123
# Anyone can decode base64!
Explanation

Secrets are slightly more secure than ConfigMaps: they're mounted as tmpfs, can be encrypted at rest, and have separate RBAC controls. But base64 encoding is NOT encryption. On CKS, always enable encryption at rest for Secrets via EncryptionConfiguration. For truly sensitive data, use external secret stores.

Chapter 10: Secrets — Managing Sensitive Data

Secrets are like ConfigMaps, but designed for sensitive data — passwords, API keys, TLS certificates. Learn how to create, consume, and secure them properly. Spoiler: base64 is NOT encryption.

🔐 Sensitive Data 🔑 Passwords & Keys 🛡️ RBAC Protected 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What Secrets are and how they differ from ConfigMaps
  • ✅ The 6 types of Kubernetes Secrets
  • ✅ 4 ways to create Secrets (literal, file, YAML, stringData)
  • ✅ 3 ways to consume Secrets in Pods
  • ✅ Complete Secret setup for the anihpj Django project
  • ✅ Security best practices for production
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Environment, Configuration & Security 25%
Secrets on the CKAD — Know the Differences

Secrets and ConfigMaps are nearly identical in usage. Key exam distinctions:

  • Create — kubectl create secret generic my-secret --from-literal=password=mysecretpass
  • Types matter — generic (Opaque), docker-registry, tls — know when to use each
  • stringData vs data — Use stringData in YAML (plain text, auto-encodes). Use data with base64-encoded values
  • Mounted volumes — Secret files are mounted with 0644 permissions by default; use defaultMode: 0400 for security
  • Exam gotcha — kubectl get secret my-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' returns base64. Pipe through | base64 -d to decode

Secret Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Generic (Opaque)kubectl create secret generic db-creds --from-literal=user=admin --from-literal=pass=s3cret
From filekubectl create secret generic tls-cert --from-file=cert.pem --from-file=key.pem
Docker registrykubectl create secret docker-registry regcred --docker-server=ghcr.io --docker-username=user --docker-password=token
TLSkubectl create secret tls my-tls --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key
View (encoded)kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data}'
Decode valuekubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.pass}' | base64 -d

Secret YAML — stringData vs data

Use stringData (plain text) when writing YAML by hand. Use data (base64-encoded) when copying from existing secrets.

# OPTION A: stringData (plain text — easiest for exam)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-creds
type: Opaque
stringData:                    # ← K8s auto-encodes to base64
  username: admin
  password: supersecret123
  connection-string: "postgresql://admin:supersecret123@postgres:5432/anihpj"

# OPTION B: data (base64-encoded)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-creds
type: Opaque
data:                          # ← Must be base64-encoded
  username: YWRtaW4=           # echo -n 'admin' | base64
  password: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXQxMjM=

# CONSUMING IN A POD:
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    envFrom:
    - secretRef:               # ← All keys as env vars
        name: db-creds
    volumeMounts:
    - name: creds-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true           # ← Always readOnly for secrets
  volumes:
  - name: creds-vol
    secret:
      secretName: db-creds
      defaultMode: 0400        # ← Restrict file permissions

Secret Traps on the Exam

  • ❌ Secrets are NOT encrypted by default — they're only base64-encoded (trivially reversible)
  • base64 encoding: echo -n 'mypass' | base64 (the -n removes trailing newline — forgetting it corrupts the value!)
  • ❌ echo 'mypass' | base64 → includes a \n in the encoded value = broken credentials
  • ✅ echo -n 'mypass' | base64 → correct encoding without newline
  • Secret type must match usage: docker-registry type for imagePullSecrets, tls type for Ingress TLS
  • If question says "create a Secret" without specifying type → use generic (Opaque)

📊 Kubernetes Secret Types at a Glance

🔐 Opaque Generic key-value (default)
🐳 docker-registry Image pull credentials
🔒 tls TLS cert + private key
🔑 basic-auth Username + password
🗝️ ssh-auth SSH private key
🎟️ service-account-token Auto-mounted SA token

⏳ Secret Lifecycle in Kubernetes

Step 1 Create Secret

kubectl create secret generic / from YAML (data: base64 or stringData: plain text)

Step 2 Store in etcd

Stored base64-encoded (NOT encrypted unless EncryptionConfiguration is enabled)

Step 3 Mount into Pod

As env vars (envFrom/valueFrom) or volume mounts (tmpfs, never written to disk on node)

Step 4 Application Reads

App reads plain text from env var or file — base64 decoding is automatic

⚠️ Risk Exposure Vectors

Pod logs, env dumps, etcd access, RBAC over-permissions — always restrict with RBAC

10.1 What are Secrets?

A Secret is a Kubernetes object that stores sensitive data such as passwords, OAuth tokens, SSH keys, and TLS certificates. Like ConfigMaps, they decouple configuration from containers — but with extra access controls.

🚨 CRITICAL: Base64 Is NOT Encryption!

Secrets are base64 encoded, not encrypted. Anyone who can read the Secret can decode the values instantly:

This is NOT security 
# "Encoded" secret value
$ echo 'c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==' | base64 -d
supersecretpassword    ← 😱 Anyone can decode this!

# Base64 is just packaging, like putting a letter in an envelope
# vs actual encryption, which is like putting it in a locked safe

🔐 Base64 Encoding vs Actual Encryption

  BASE64 ENCODING (what Secrets do by default):
  ┌──────────────┐   encode    ┌──────────────────────────┐   decode
  │ "mypassword" │───────────▶│ "bXlwYXNzd29yZA=="       │──────────▶ "mypassword"
  └──────────────┘             └──────────────────────────┘
  Anyone can reverse this! It's just a different representation.

  ACTUAL ENCRYPTION (what you should enable):
  ┌──────────────┐   encrypt   ┌──────────────────────────┐   need key
  │ "mypassword" │───────────▶│ "a8#$kL!m@9xZ..."        │──────────▶ 🔑 required!
  └──────────────┘    🔑 key   └──────────────────────────┘
  Only someone with the encryption key can reverse this.

📊 Secret vs ConfigMap Comparison

Feature ConfigMap Secret
Data type Non-sensitive configuration Passwords, keys, certificates
Encoding Plain text Base64 encoded
Size limit 1 MB 1 MB
Mount as Env vars / Volumes Env vars / Volumes
RBAC Recommended ⚠️ Required
Memory storage Normal storage tmpfs (RAM) when mounted
Git-safe? ✅ Yes ❌ Never commit to Git!

10.2 Types of Secrets

Kubernetes has 6 built-in Secret types, each designed for a specific use case:

Type Use Case Required Keys
Opaque (default) General-purpose key-value pairs Any
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Docker registry credentials .dockerconfigjson
kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificate + private key tls.crt, tls.key
kubernetes.io/basic-auth Username + password username, password
kubernetes.io/ssh-auth SSH private key ssh-privatekey
kubernetes.io/service-account-token Auto-created for ServiceAccounts Auto-managed
📝 Which Type Should You Use?

For most applications like anihpj, Opaque is what you need — it stores any key-value data (Django SECRET_KEY, database passwords, API tokens). The specialized types add validation and are used by specific Kubernetes components.

10.3 Creating Secrets — Methods

Similar to ConfigMaps, Secrets can be created in multiple ways:

📋 4 Secret Creation Methods at a Glance

  Method               Source                Best For
  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  --from-literal       Command-line args     Quick one-off secrets
  --from-file          TLS certs, SSH keys   File-based credentials
  YAML (data:)         Base64-encoded YAML   When you manually encode
  YAML (stringData:)   Plain text YAML       GitOps workflow ⭐ (don't commit!)

10.3a From Literal Values

Creating from the command line 
# Create secret with key-value pairs
$ kubectl create secret generic anihpj-secret \
    --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123' \
    --from-literal=DATABASE_PASSWORD='supersecretpassword' \
    --from-literal=EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD='smtp-pass-456'

secret/anihpj-secret created

# View the secret (values are base64 encoded)
$ kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -o yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: c210cC1wYXNzLTQ1Ng==
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==

10.3b From a File

Creating from files (TLS certs, SSH keys, etc.) 
# TLS certificates
$ kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
    --cert=tls.crt \
    --key=tls.key

# Generic secret from file
$ kubectl create secret generic ssh-key \
    --from-file=ssh-privatekey=~/.ssh/id_rsa

10.3c From YAML — Base64 Encoded

When using the data field, values must be base64-encoded:

Step 1: Encode your values 
# Encode values to base64 (use -n to avoid trailing newline!)
$ echo -n 'supersecretpassword' | base64
c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==

$ echo -n 'django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123' | base64
ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==
Step 2: Use in YAML 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
type: Opaque
data:                                           # ← base64-encoded values
  SECRET_KEY: ZGphbmdvLWluc2VjdXJlLXg3ayFtI2FiYzEyMw==
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: c3VwZXJzZWNyZXRwYXNzd29yZA==

10.3d Using stringData — Auto-Encoded (Recommended ⭐)

The stringData field lets you write plain text — Kubernetes encodes it for you:

k8s/secret.yaml — Much easier to read! 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:                                    # ← plain text! Auto-encoded on apply
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "supersecretpassword"
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: "smtp-pass-456"
💡 stringData vs data

Use stringData when writing YAML — no manual base64 encoding needed. Kubernetes automatically converts it to base64 data when the Secret is stored. However, never commit this YAML to Git — the plain-text values are visible in the file!

10.4 Using Secrets in Pods

Secrets are consumed in pods the same 3 ways as ConfigMaps — but with secretRef instead of configMapRef:

Method YAML Key App Reads Via Auto-Update? Best For
Env Vars secretRef / secretKeyRef os.environ["KEY"] ❌ Pod restart needed DB passwords, API keys
Volume Mounts volumes.secret Read file from path ✅ ~60s kubelet sync TLS certs, config files
imagePullSecrets imagePullSecrets Kubelet uses internally N/A Private registries only

10.4a As Environment Variables

Pod with Secret as env vars 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: anihpj-config          # Non-sensitive config
        - secretRef:
            name: anihpj-secret          # Sensitive data
      # Or pick individual keys:
      env:
        - name: DJANGO_SECRET_KEY
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secret
              key: SECRET_KEY

10.4b As Volume Mounts

Pod with Secret as mounted files 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: secret-volume
          mountPath: /etc/secrets         # ← files appear here
          readOnly: true                  # ← always set readOnly!
  volumes:
    - name: secret-volume
      secret:
        secretName: anihpj-secret
        defaultMode: 0400                 # ← restrictive permissions
📝 Secrets Are Stored in RAM

When mounted as volumes, Secrets are stored in tmpfs (RAM-based filesystem), not on disk. This means they're never written to the node's persistent storage — more secure against disk-based attacks.

10.4c As imagePullSecrets

If your container images are in a private registry (Docker Hub private, AWS ECR, GCR), you need credentials to pull them:

Private registry authentication 
# Create Docker registry secret
$ kubectl create secret docker-registry my-registry-cred \
    --docker-server=registry.example.com \
    --docker-username=myuser \
    --docker-password=mypassword \
    --docker-email=dev@example.com
Reference in Pod/Deployment 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pod
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:                           # ← at pod level
    - name: my-registry-cred
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: registry.example.com/anihpj:v1   # private image

10.5 Secrets for anihpj — Complete Setup

Here's a complete, production-ready Secret setup for our Django project:

k8s/secret.yaml — Complete anihpj Secrets 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-secret
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: secrets
type: Opaque
stringData:
  # Django core
  SECRET_KEY: "django-insecure-x7k!m#abc123def456ghi789"

  # Database credentials
  DATABASE_USER: "anihpj_admin"
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "P@ssw0rd!Str0ng2024"

  # Email SMTP
  EMAIL_HOST_USER: "noreply@anihpj.com"
  EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD: "smtp-app-password-here"

  # Third-party API keys
  STRIPE_SECRET_KEY: "sk_live_abc123..."
  SENTRY_DSN: "https://key@sentry.io/12345"

🔐 Complete Config + Secrets Architecture for anihpj

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    anihpj Deployment                           │
  │                                                                │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐    envFrom:         ┌────────────────┐   │
  │  │   ConfigMap      │    configMapRef  ──▶│   Pod          │   │
  │  │   anihpj-config  │                     │                │   │
  │  │                  │                     │  DEBUG=False   │   │
  │  │  DEBUG           │    envFrom:         │  DB_HOST=pg    │   │
  │  │  DB_HOST         │    secretRef    ──▶│  SECRET_KEY=.. │   │
  │  │  DB_PORT         │                     │  DB_PASS=..    │   │
  │  │  ALLOWED_HOSTS   │                     │                │   │
  │  └──────────────────┘                     │  settings.py   │   │
  │                                           │  reads via     │   │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐                     │  os.environ    │   │
  │  │   Secret         │                     └────────────────┘   │
  │  │   anihpj-secret  │                                         │
  │  │                  │    ⚠️ Never in Git!                      │
  │  │  SECRET_KEY      │    ⚠️ RBAC protected                    │
  │  │  DB_PASSWORD     │    ⚠️ Base64 in etcd                    │
  │  │  STRIPE_KEY      │                                         │
  │  └──────────────────┘                                         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

10.6 Secret Security Best Practices

Kubernetes Secrets provide a basic level of security. For production, follow these practices:

✅ DO
  • Enable encryption at rest for etcd
  • Use RBAC to limit who can read Secrets
  • Keep Secret YAML out of Git (.gitignore)
  • Rotate secrets regularly
  • Use stringData over manual base64
  • Set readOnly: true on volume mounts
  • Use external secret managers (Vault, AWS SM)
  • Set restrictive file permissions (0400)
❌ DON'T
  • Never commit Secret YAML to Git
  • Never log secret values in apps
  • Never echo secrets in Dockerfiles
  • Never share Secrets across namespaces
  • Never assume base64 = encrypted
  • Never grant get secrets to all users
  • Never hardcode secrets in container images
  • Never use kubectl get secret -o yaml in logs

🛡️ Secret Security Layers

  Layer 1: RBAC                    Who can read Secrets?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ ClusterRole: only admins get "get secrets"         │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 2: Encryption at Rest      How are they stored?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ etcd stores encrypted values (AES-CBC, AES-GCM)   │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 3: Network Policies        Who can access pods?
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ Only authorized pods can reach secret-consuming pods│
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
           │
  Layer 4: External Managers       Best-in-class
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │ HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure KV     │
  │ Automatic rotation, audit logs, fine-grained ACL   │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🛠️ Secret kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Secrets kubectl get secrets
View Secret (keys only) kubectl describe secret anihpj-secret
Decode a value kubectl get secret anihpj-secret -o jsonpath='{.data.SECRET_KEY}' | base64 -d
Create from literal kubectl create secret generic my-secret --from-literal=key=value
Create TLS Secret kubectl create secret tls my-tls --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key
Create registry cred kubectl create secret docker-registry my-reg --docker-server=... --docker-username=...
Delete Secret kubectl delete secret anihpj-secret
Dry-run + export kubectl create secret generic my-s --from-literal=k=v --dry-run=client -o yaml

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Secrets Management

⏱️ Target: 8 minutes
  1. Create a Secret named db-creds with username=admin and password=S3cureP@ss!
  2. Create a Pod named db-app (image: mysql:8) that uses the Secret as environment variables: MYSQL_USER from key username, MYSQL_PASSWORD from key password
  3. Create a TLS Secret named tls-cert (you can use dummy cert/key files)
  4. Create a Pod named secure-app (image: nginx) that mounts tls-cert to /etc/tls as read-only
  5. Verify you can read the decoded password from within the db-app pod
💡 Hint: env.valueFrom.secretKeyRef for selective keys, volumes[].secret.secretName for mounts
View Solution 
# 1. Create Secret
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password='S3cureP@ss!'

# 2. Pod with selective Secret env vars
kubectl run db-app --image=mysql:8 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > db-app.yaml
# Edit db-app.yaml — add under containers[0]:
#   env:
#   - name: MYSQL_USER
#     valueFrom:
#       secretKeyRef:
#         name: db-creds
#         key: username
#   - name: MYSQL_PASSWORD
#     valueFrom:
#       secretKeyRef:
#         name: db-creds
#         key: password
kubectl apply -f db-app.yaml

# 3. Generate dummy TLS cert (for practice)
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
  -keyout tls.key -out tls.crt -subj "/CN=example.com"
kubectl create secret tls tls-cert --cert=tls.crt --key=tls.key

# 4. Mount TLS Secret as volume
kubectl run secure-app --image=nginx \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > secure-app.yaml
# Edit — add volumes and volumeMounts:
#   volumes:
#   - name: tls-vol
#     secret:
#       secretName: tls-cert
#   containers:
#     volumeMounts:
#     - name: tls-vol
#       mountPath: /etc/tls
#       readOnly: true
kubectl apply -f secure-app.yaml

# 5. Verify
kubectl exec db-app -- env | grep MYSQL
kubectl exec secure-app -- ls /etc/tls/

Chapter 10 — Practice Questions

Q1
Create a Secret named db-creds with keys username=admin and password=S3cur3P@ss! using the imperative command. Then decode the stored values.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create the secret
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password='S3cur3P@ss!'

# View (values are base64 encoded)
kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml

# Decode a specific value
kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

# Or decode inline
echo "UzNjdXIzUEBzcyE=" | base64 -d
Explanation

Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default. Anyone with API access can decode them. The CKAD uses kubectl create secret generic frequently. Remember to quote values with special characters (!, $, @) to prevent shell interpretation. Use single quotes to be safe.

Q2
What are the different types of Kubernetes Secrets? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
TypeUsageCreated By
OpaqueGeneral purpose key-value datakubectl create secret generic
kubernetes.io/tlsTLS certificate + private keykubectl create secret tls
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjsonDocker registry credentialskubectl create secret docker-registry
kubernetes.io/service-account-tokenServiceAccount tokensAuto-created by K8s
Explanation

On the CKAD, you'll mostly use Opaque (generic) and tls Secrets. To create a TLS Secret: kubectl create secret tls my-tls --cert=cert.pem --key=key.pem. For pulling images from private registries, use docker-registry type and reference it with imagePullSecrets in the pod spec.

Q3
Mount a Secret named app-secret as a volume at /etc/secrets with file permissions 0400 (read-only by owner). Write the YAML.
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: app-secret
      defaultMode: 0400        # Octal permissions
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true           # Extra protection
Explanation

defaultMode sets UNIX file permissions for all files in the mounted Secret. 0400 = owner read-only. Default is 0644. Add readOnly: true on the mount as defense-in-depth. Inside the pod, each Secret key becomes a file — the filename is the key, the content is the decoded value (NOT base64).

Q4
Write the YAML to create a Secret declaratively. Why must you base64-encode the values in the data field, and how do you avoid this?
Show Answer
Answer
# With base64-encoded data:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  username: YWRtaW4=          # echo -n "admin" | base64
  password: UzNjdXIzUEBzcyE=

# WITHOUT encoding — use stringData:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
type: Opaque
stringData:                    # ← Plain text, auto-encoded
  username: admin
  password: S3cur3P@ss!
Explanation

stringData is the CKAD shortcut — it auto-encodes to base64 on creation. You can mix data and stringData in the same Secret. stringData values override data values for the same key. On the CKAD, use stringData to avoid base64 encoding mistakes. Note: kubectl get secret -o yaml always shows data (base64).

Q5
A pod is stuck in CreateContainerConfigError. The events show "secret 'db-creds' not found". How do you fix this without modifying the pod's YAML?
Show Answer
Answer
# Option 1: Create the missing secret
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password=changeme

# The pod will automatically retry and start
# No need to restart or delete the pod

# Verify
kubectl get pod <pod-name>  # Should transition to Running
Explanation

Kubernetes continuously retries container creation when dependencies are missing. Once the Secret exists, the pod automatically starts — no manual restart needed. This is the reconciliation loop in action. On the CKAD, this is a quick-fix scenario — create the missing resource and the pod heals itself.

Q6
How do you create a Secret for pulling images from a private Docker registry? Show the command and how to reference it in a pod.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create docker-registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry my-registry \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass \
  --docker-email=user@example.com
# Reference in pod spec:
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
  - name: my-registry
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/myapp:1.0
Explanation

imagePullSecrets is a pod-level field (not container-level). All containers in the pod can use it. You can also attach it to a ServiceAccount so every pod using that SA automatically gets the registry credentials. The CKAD may test this when the question specifies a private image registry.

Q7
What security concern exists with Secrets by default in Kubernetes? List three ways to make Secrets more secure.
Show Answer
Answer

Concern: Secrets are stored as base64 in etcd — not encrypted at rest by default.

  1. Enable encryption at rest — configure EncryptionConfiguration on the API server
  2. RBAC — restrict who can get/list Secrets with Role/ClusterRole
  3. External secret managers — HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager with CSI driver

Additional: avoid kubectl get secret -o yaml in shared terminals, don't commit Secrets to Git.

Explanation

The CKAD tests awareness that base64 ≠ encryption. Anyone with kubectl get secrets permission can read any Secret in their namespace. In production: enable etcd encryption, use RBAC to limit access, and consider external vault solutions. The exam won't ask you to configure encryption, but may ask about the security implications.

Q8
Compare ConfigMaps and Secrets. When should you use which?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureConfigMapSecret
Data typeNon-sensitive configSensitive credentials
StoragePlain text in etcdBase64 in etcd
Size limit1 MiB1 MiB
RBACStandardCan be restricted separately
tmpfs mountNoYes (in-memory, not written to disk)
Use forURLs, feature flags, configsPasswords, tokens, TLS certs
Explanation

Secrets are mounted as tmpfs (RAM-backed) — they're never written to the node's disk. ConfigMaps use regular disk storage. The YAML syntax is nearly identical: configMapRef vs secretRef, configMapKeyRef vs secretKeyRef. On the CKAD, always use Secrets for anything credential-related.

Q9
Create a pod that uses both a ConfigMap and a Secret: ConfigMap app-config mounted as env vars, and Secret app-secret mounted as files at /etc/secrets.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: app-secret
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true
Explanation

Mixing ConfigMaps (env) and Secrets (volumes) is a common pattern. Config goes in env vars for easy access (os.environ), while secrets go in files for security (apps read file contents). On the CKAD, you'll frequently combine both. Remember: envFrom is for ALL keys, env.valueFrom is for specific keys.

Q10
You need to change a Secret value. What happens to pods currently using that Secret (mounted as env var vs volume)?
Show Answer
Answer
Injection MethodAuto-Updates?Pod Restart Needed?
Env var (secretKeyRef)❌ No✅ Yes
Volume mount✅ Yes (~60s delay)❌ No
Volume + subPath❌ No✅ Yes
 
# Update the secret
kubectl edit secret db-creds

# Restart pods for env var changes
kubectl rollout restart deployment/my-app
Explanation

Identical behavior to ConfigMaps. Secrets mounted as volumes auto-update (kubelet re-syncs), but env vars and subPath mounts are frozen at pod creation. This is tested on the CKAD — know which method requires a restart. rollout restart is the graceful way to restart all pods in a Deployment.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Secret Encryption & External Secret Management

CLUSTER SETUP 15% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Secret Encryption Providers

ProviderStrengthKey ManagementCKS Recommendation
identityNone (plaintext)N/A❌ Default — insecure
aescbcAES-256-CBCLocal key in config file✅ Standard CKS choice
aesgcmAES-256-GCMMust rotate keys frequently🟡 Requires rotation
secretboxXSalsa20+Poly1305Local key✅ Strong alternative
kmsEnvelope encryptionExternal KMS (AWS/GCP/Azure)✅ Best for production

Key Rotation Procedure

# 1. Generate new key
NEW_KEY=$(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)

# 2. Update EncryptionConfiguration — new key FIRST
# providers:
# - aescbc:
#     keys:
#     - name: key2          ← New key first (used for encryption)
#       secret: $NEW_KEY
#     - name: key1          ← Old key second (used for decryption)
#       secret: $OLD_KEY

# 3. Restart API server

# 4. Re-encrypt all secrets with new key
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# 5. Remove old key from config, restart API server

Chapter 10 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Verify that Secrets are encrypted at rest in etcd. Show how to check if encryption is enabled and working.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check if API server has encryption config
ps aux | grep kube-apiserver | grep encryption-provider-config
# Should show: --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml

# 2. Read the encryption config
cat /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
# Verify aescbc/aesgcm/secretbox provider is FIRST (before identity)

# 3. Create a test secret
kubectl create secret generic test-enc --from-literal=key=testvalue

# 4. Read directly from etcd
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  get /registry/secrets/default/test-enc

# If encrypted: binary/garbled output with "k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:key1" prefix
# If NOT encrypted: plaintext "testvalue" visible

# 5. Clean up
kubectl delete secret test-enc
Explanation

This is one of the most common CKS tasks. You must verify encryption works by reading directly from etcd. Encrypted data starts with a provider prefix like k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:key1. If you see plaintext values, encryption is NOT configured. Remember the etcdctl flags for TLS client certs.

CKS-2
How do you rotate the encryption key for Secrets? What happens to existing Secrets during rotation?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Add new key FIRST in providers list
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
- resources:
  - secrets
  providers:
  - aescbc:
      keys:
      - name: key2                          # NEW key (encrypts new data)
        secret: NEW_BASE64_KEY_HERE
      - name: key1                          # OLD key (decrypts old data)
        secret: OLD_BASE64_KEY_HERE
  - identity: {}
# Step 2: Restart API server
# (Static pod auto-restarts when manifest changes)

# Step 3: Re-encrypt ALL existing secrets with new key
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces -o json | kubectl replace -f -
# This reads each secret (decrypted with old key) and writes it back
# (re-encrypted with new key)

# Step 4: Remove old key, keep only new key
# Step 5: Restart API server again

# IMPORTANT: Never remove old key before re-encrypting!
Explanation

Key rotation keeps old key for decryption while encrypting new data with the new key. The first key in the list is used for encryption, all keys are tried for decryption. You MUST re-encrypt existing secrets before removing the old key. On CKS, know this full rotation procedure.

CKS-3
How do you prevent ServiceAccount tokens from being automatically mounted in pods?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: In Pod spec
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false   # No auto-mount
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25

# Method 2: On the ServiceAccount itself
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: no-token-sa
automountServiceAccountToken: false     # Applies to all pods using this SA
# Why this matters:
# Default SA token at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
# Can be used to call the Kubernetes API from inside the pod
# If pod is compromised, attacker gets API access

# Verify no token is mounted:
kubectl exec secure-pod -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# Should fail: No such file or directory
Explanation

By default, every pod gets a ServiceAccount token mounted. If the pod doesn't need API access (most don't), disable auto-mounting. On CKS, this is a key hardening step — a compromised pod with a SA token can enumerate the cluster, read Secrets, etc.

CKS-4
What is the External Secrets Operator and how does it integrate with HashiCorp Vault?
Show Answer
Answer
# ExternalSecret syncs secrets from Vault/AWS/GCP → K8s Secrets

# 1. SecretStore — defines connection to Vault
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: SecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: "https://vault.example.com"
      path: "secret"
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: "kubernetes"
          role: "my-role"

# 2. ExternalSecret — defines what to sync
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h            # Auto-rotate
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend
  target:
    name: db-secret              # Creates this K8s Secret
  data:
  - secretKey: password
    remoteRef:
      key: database/credentials
      property: password
Explanation

External Secrets Operator syncs secrets from external stores (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager) into Kubernetes Secrets. Benefits: centralized management, automatic rotation, audit logging. On CKS, know the pattern — SecretStore defines the backend, ExternalSecret defines what to sync.

CKS-5
How do you find all Secrets that are not encrypted at rest in a cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. List all secrets
kubectl get secrets -A --no-headers | wc -l

# 2. Check encryption config
cat /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
# If identity{} is first provider → ALL secrets are unencrypted!

# 3. Spot-check specific secrets in etcd
for ns in $(kubectl get ns -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
  for secret in $(kubectl get secrets -n $ns -o name); do
    name=$(echo $secret | cut -d/ -f2)
    echo "Checking $ns/$name..."
    ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/$ns/$name \
      --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
      --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key \
      --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
      --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 | head -c 100
    echo ""
  done
done

# Encrypted secrets start with: k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:keyname
# Unencrypted secrets show plaintext

# 4. Re-encrypt all after enabling encryption:
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -
Explanation

After enabling encryption, existing Secrets remain unencrypted until re-encrypted. Check the EncryptionConfiguration to see if identity (plaintext) is the first provider. Use etcdctl to verify data is encrypted. Run kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f - to re-encrypt everything.

Chapter 11: Volumes and Persistent Storage

Containers are ephemeral — when a pod dies, so does its data. Volumes solve this by providing storage that outlives individual containers and even pods. Master PVs, PVCs, and StorageClasses to keep your data safe.

💾 Persistent Data 📁 Volume Mounts 🏗️ StorageClasses 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why container data is ephemeral and how volumes solve it
  • ✅ Volume types: emptyDir, hostPath, PV, PVC, and more
  • ✅ PersistentVolumes — the cluster-wide storage pool
  • ✅ PersistentVolumeClaims — requesting storage from pods
  • ✅ StorageClasses — dynamic provisioning magic
  • ✅ Adding persistent storage to anihpj (DB, media, static)
  • ✅ Volume lifecycle and reclaim policies
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Application Design & Build 20% Environment, Configuration & Security 25%
