Volumes on the CKAD — Critical Knowledge

Volume questions are common and tricky. Key patterns to memorize:

  • emptyDir — Shared storage between containers in the SAME pod. Dies when pod dies. Perfect for sidecar patterns
  • PV + PVC — PV is the actual storage (admin creates). PVC is the claim (developer requests). They bind by accessModes and storageClassName
  • Access Modes — ReadWriteOnce (RWO), ReadOnlyMany (ROX), ReadWriteMany (RWX) — know the abbreviations
  • Two-step mount — (1) Define volumes in pod spec, (2) Add volumeMounts in container spec. Forget either → fail
  • Exam shortcut — Use kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes to see all volume types during the exam

Volume Patterns — The 4 You MUST Know

Every volume question uses one of these 4 patterns. Memorize the two-step: volumes in spec + volumeMounts in container.

# PATTERN 1: emptyDir (shared between containers in same pod)
spec:
  containers:
  - name: writer
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo hello > /data/greeting; sleep 3600']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-data
      mountPath: /data
  - name: reader
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'cat /data/greeting; sleep 3600']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-data
      mountPath: /data
  volumes:
  - name: shared-data
    emptyDir: {}               # ← Created when pod starts, deleted when pod dies

# PATTERN 2: PersistentVolumeClaim
spec:
  containers:
  - name: db
    image: postgres:15
    volumeMounts:
    - name: db-storage
      mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumes:
  - name: db-storage
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: postgres-pvc  # ← Must exist before pod

# PATTERN 3: ConfigMap as volume
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    configMap:
      name: app-config

# PATTERN 4: Secret as volume
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: db-creds
      defaultMode: 0400

PersistentVolume + PersistentVolumeClaim Pair

PV is cluster-scoped (admin creates). PVC is namespace-scoped (dev requests). They bind by accessModes + storageClassName + capacity.

# PersistentVolume (admin creates this)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: pv-data
  labels:
    type: local
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 5Gi
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce            # RWO = one node read-write
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain   # Retain | Delete | Recycle
  storageClassName: manual
  hostPath:
    path: /mnt/data
---
# PersistentVolumeClaim (developer creates this)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: production
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce            # ← Must match PV
  storageClassName: manual     # ← Must match PV
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 3Gi             # ← Must be ≤ PV capacity

kubectl explain — Volume Fields Quick Lookup

kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes                        # All volume types
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.emptyDir                # emptyDir options
kubectl explain pod.spec.volumes.persistentVolumeClaim   # PVC reference
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.volumeMounts         # Mount options
kubectl explain pv.spec                                  # PV spec fields
kubectl explain pvc.spec                                 # PVC spec fields
kubectl explain pv.spec.accessModes                      # Access modes
kubectl explain pv.spec.persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy    # Reclaim policies

Volume Traps on the Exam

  • ❌ Forgetting the volumes definition — volumeMounts without matching volumes = pod won't start
  • ❌ Volume name mismatch — volumeMounts.name must exactly match volumes.name
  • PVC must be in same namespace as Pod — PVs are cluster-scoped but PVCs are namespace-scoped
  • Access modes: ReadWriteOnce (RWO) = one node, ReadOnlyMany (ROX) = many nodes read-only, ReadWriteMany (RWX) = many nodes read-write
  • PVC pending? → Check: storageClassName matches PV, requested size ≤ PV capacity, accessModes match
  • subPath — mount a specific file without hiding directory contents: subPath: nginx.conf

Volume Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get pv → STATUS should be Bound (not Available or Released)
  • kubectl get pvc -n myns → STATUS should be Bound (not Pending)
  • kubectl describe pvc postgres-pvc -n myns → Shows which PV it's bound to
  • kubectl exec mypod -- ls /mount/path → Verify files are accessible
  • kubectl exec mypod -- df -h /mount/path → Verify correct storage size
  • kubectl get pod mypod -o yaml | grep -A10 volumes → Confirm volume definitions

🏗️ Kubernetes Storage Stack

📦 Pod — volumeMounts Container sees a directory path (e.g. /data) — reads/writes like a local filesystem
📋 PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) Request for storage — specifies size, access mode, storageClass. Binds to a PV
💾 PersistentVolume (PV) Cluster-level storage resource — provisioned statically or dynamically via StorageClass
🖥️ Physical / Cloud Storage AWS EBS, GCE PD, Azure Disk, NFS, local disk, Ceph, iSCSI — the actual backend

🔄 PersistentVolume Lifecycle

1
Available

PV exists, not yet claimed by any PVC

2
Bound

PVC matched & bound to this PV

3
In Use

Pod mounts the PVC, reads/writes data

4
Released

PVC deleted, PV retains data (Retain policy)

5
Reclaimed

Admin cleans up → PV available again or deleted

📦 Volume Types You Must Know

💨
emptyDir — Temporary storage, dies with the pod. Great for scratch space & sidecar sharing
🖥️
hostPath — Maps node directory into pod. Dangerous in multi-node — data tied to one node
💾
persistentVolumeClaim — Binds to PV via PVC. The standard way to use durable storage
⚙️
configMap / secret — Mounts ConfigMap or Secret data as files in the container
📡
nfs — Network filesystem mount. Multiple pods can read/write simultaneously (RWX)

11.1 The Problem: Container Data is Ephemeral

Every container gets a writable filesystem layer. But this layer is ephemeral — it's destroyed when the container stops. This is a huge problem for stateful applications.

💥 Data Loss on Pod Restart

  BEFORE RESTART:                        AFTER RESTART:
  ┌──────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj Pod              │          │  anihpj Pod (NEW)        │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  💾 db.sqlite3           │   🔄→   │  💾 db.sqlite3           │
  │     5 JobPosts ✅         │          │     0 JobPosts ❌         │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  📷 media/uploads/       │          │  📷 media/uploads/       │
  │     12 resumes ✅         │          │     0 resumes ❌          │
  │                          │          │                          │
  │  📄 logs/django.log      │          │  📄 logs/django.log      │
  │     500 entries ✅        │          │     0 entries ❌          │
  └──────────────────────────┘          └──────────────────────────┘
  😀 Everything is here!                😱 Everything is GONE!
❌ Without Volumes
  • Database resets on every restart
  • Uploaded files disappear
  • Logs gone — can't debug
  • Session data lost — users logged out
✅ With Volumes
  • Database persists across restarts
  • Uploaded files survive pod recreation
  • Logs preserved for monitoring
  • Data on external storage, not container

11.2 Volume Types Overview

Kubernetes supports many volume types, organized into four categories:

Category Type Description Survives Pod?
Temporary emptyDir Scratch space, shared between containers ❌ No
hostPath Mounts from node filesystem (dev only!) ⚠️ Node-tied
Network/Cloud nfs Network File System mount ✅ Yes
awsElasticBlockStore AWS EBS disk ✅ Yes
csi Container Storage Interface (universal) ✅ Yes
Abstracted PersistentVolume Cluster-wide storage resource ✅ Yes
PersistentVolumeClaim Request for storage by a pod ✅ Yes
Config configMap Mounts ConfigMap as files ✅ (data in CM)
secret Mounts Secret as files (tmpfs) ✅ (data in Secret)
projected Combines multiple sources ✅ (varies)

🗂️ Volume Decision Tree

  Need storage?
  │
  ├── Temporary scratch space between containers?
  │   └── ✅ emptyDir
  │
  ├── Config files or secrets?
  │   └── ✅ configMap / secret volume
  │
  ├── Persistent data (survives pod restart)?
  │   │
  │   ├── Dynamic provisioning (cloud)?
  │   │   └── ✅ PVC + StorageClass (recommended)
  │   │
  │   └── Pre-provisioned by admin?
  │       └── ✅ PVC + manually created PV
  │
  └── Development on Minikube only?
      └── ⚠️ hostPath (never in production!)

11.3 emptyDir — Temporary Shared Storage

An emptyDir volume is created when a pod is assigned to a node, and exists as long as that pod runs. When the pod is removed, the data is permanently deleted.

📝 When to Use emptyDir

Use emptyDir for temporary data that containers in the same pod need to share — like a sidecar processing temp files, or caching data between an init container and the main app.

Pod with emptyDir — shared between 2 containers 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-cache
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-cache
          mountPath: /app/cache              # Django writes temp files here

    - name: cache-cleaner                     # Sidecar: cleans old cache
      image: busybox
      command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do find /cache -mmin +60 -delete; sleep 300; done"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: shared-cache
          mountPath: /cache                   # Same volume, different path

  volumes:
    - name: shared-cache
      emptyDir:
        sizeLimit: 500Mi                     # Optional: limit size

📦 emptyDir Lifecycle

  Pod Created    Pod Running              Pod Deleted
  ────┬──────────────┬───────────────────────┬────
      │              │                       │
      ▼              ▼                       ▼
  ┌────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   ┌────────────┐
  │emptyDir│   │  Container A     │   │  emptyDir  │
  │created │   │  writes /cache   │   │  DELETED   │
  │(empty) │   │  Container B     │   │  💀 gone!  │
  └────────┘   │  reads  /cache   │   └────────────┘
               └──────────────────┘
               Both share the same data
⚡ RAM-Backed emptyDir with medium: Memory

By default, emptyDir uses the node's disk. Set medium: "Memory" to use tmpfs (RAM-based) for blazing-fast I/O — ideal for caches, temp computation, or session data. Be aware: it counts against your container's memory limit!

RAM-backed emptyDir — ultra-fast I/O 
volumes:
  - name: fast-cache
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory           # ← stored in RAM (tmpfs), not disk
      sizeLimit: 128Mi         # ← counts against container memory limit!
Feature emptyDir: {} (Disk) emptyDir: {medium: Memory}
Backing store Node disk (ext4/xfs) RAM (tmpfs)
Speed Standard disk I/O ⚡ Near-instant
Counts against Node ephemeral storage Container memory limit
Use case Temp files, build artifacts Caches, session stores, IPC

11.4 hostPath — Node Filesystem (Development Only!)

A hostPath volume mounts a file or directory from the host node's filesystem into a pod. Data persists even when the pod is deleted — but only on that specific node.

🚨 WARNING: Never Use hostPath in Production!
  • Data is tied to ONE node — if pod moves nodes, data is gone
  • Security risk — pod can access the host filesystem
  • No portability — breaks in multi-node clusters
  • Only use in Minikube/kind for local development
hostPath — Minikube development only 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: dev-data
          mountPath: /app/data
  volumes:
    - name: dev-data
      hostPath:
        path: /tmp/anihpj-data              # Path on the NODE
        type: DirectoryOrCreate              # Create if not exists

📋 hostPath type Values

Type Behavior
"" (empty) No validation — whatever path you give, it uses (default)
DirectoryOrCreate Creates directory if missing (permissions 0755, same owner as kubelet)
Directory Directory must already exist — fails otherwise
FileOrCreate Creates an empty file if missing (permissions 0644)
File File must already exist — fails otherwise
Socket UNIX socket must already exist at given path
CharDevice Character device must already exist
BlockDevice Block device must already exist

11.5 PersistentVolumes (PV) — The Storage Pool

A PersistentVolume (PV) is a piece of storage provisioned by an administrator (or dynamically via StorageClass). It exists independently of any pod — like a "parking spot" for data in the cluster.

🏗️ PV as a Storage Pool

  ┌────────────────── Kubernetes Cluster ──────────────────────┐
  │                                                            │
  │   PV Pool (provisioned by admin):                          │
  │   ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐    │
  │   │ PV-1    │  │ PV-2    │  │ PV-3    │  │ PV-4    │    │
  │   │ 10Gi    │  │ 50Gi    │  │ 5Gi     │  │ 100Gi   │    │
  │   │ RWO     │  │ RWX     │  │ RWO     │  │ RWO     │    │
  │   │ SSD     │  │ NFS     │  │ HDD     │  │ SSD     │    │
  │   │ 🟢 Free │  │ 🔵 Bound│  │ 🟢 Free │  │ 🟢 Free │    │
  │   └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘    │
  │                     │                                      │
  │                     ▼ Bound to                             │
  │                ┌──────────┐                                │
  │                │ PVC:     │                                │
  │                │ postgres │                                │
  │                │ 50Gi RWX │                                │
  │                └──────────┘                                │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Access Modes

Mode Abbreviation Description Use Case
ReadWriteOnce RWO Read-write by a single node Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL)
ReadOnlyMany ROX Read-only by many nodes Shared config, static assets
ReadWriteMany RWX Read-write by many nodes Shared uploads (NFS)
k8s/pv-postgres.yaml — PersistentVolume 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: postgres-pv
  labels:
    type: local
    app: anihpj
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 10Gi                            # Storage size
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                          # Single node read-write
  persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy: Retain      # Keep data on PVC delete
  storageClassName: manual                   # Must match PVC
  hostPath:                                  # For Minikube dev
    path: /mnt/data/postgres

11.6 PersistentVolumeClaims (PVC) — Requesting Storage

A PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC) is a pod's request for storage. Kubernetes looks through the available PVs and binds the PVC to the best matching one — based on size, access mode, and StorageClass.

🔗 PVC → PV Binding Process

  Developer creates PVC:              Kubernetes finds matching PV:
  ┌──────────────────────┐            ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  PVC: postgres-pvc   │            │  PV: postgres-pv     │
  │  Request: 10Gi       │───BIND───▶│  Capacity: 10Gi      │
  │  Access: RWO         │            │  Access: RWO         │
  │  Class: manual       │            │  Class: manual       │
  └──────────────────────┘            │  Status: 🔵 Bound    │
                                      └──────────────────────┘

  Pod uses the PVC:
  ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  Pod: anihpj-db      │
  │  volumes:            │
  │    - persistentVolume │
  │      Claim:          │
  │        claimName:    │──── references ──── PVC ── bound to ── PV
  │        postgres-pvc  │
  └──────────────────────┘
k8s/pvc-postgres.yaml — PersistentVolumeClaim 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                          # Must match PV
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                          # Must be ≤ PV capacity
  storageClassName: manual                   # Must match PV
Using PVC in a Deployment 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgres-storage
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data   # PostgreSQL data dir
      volumes:
        - name: postgres-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: postgres-pvc                 # Reference the PVC

11.7 StorageClasses — Dynamic Provisioning

Instead of manually creating PVs, StorageClasses enable dynamic provisioning — Kubernetes automatically creates a PV when a PVC is submitted. No admin intervention needed!

⚡ Dynamic Provisioning Flow

  ① Developer              ② StorageClass          ③ Cloud Provider     ④ Pod
  ┌──────────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │ Creates PVC: │     │ Provisioner    │     │ Creates real    │  │ Mounts   │
  │ "I need 10Gi │────▶│ triggers auto- │────▶│ disk (EBS, GCE  │─▶│ volume   │
  │  SSD storage"│     │ create PV      │     │ PD, Azure Disk) │  │ ✅ data!  │
  └──────────────┘     └────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘  └──────────┘
                                                     │
                        No admin setup needed!       ▼
                        StorageClass handles     10Gi SSD disk
                        everything!              auto-created
k8s/storageclass.yaml — Dynamic Provisioning 
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs         # Cloud provider plugin
parameters:
  type: gp3                                # SSD disk type
  iopsPerGB: "10"
  fsType: ext4
reclaimPolicy: Retain                      # Keep disk on PVC delete
allowVolumeExpansion: true                 # Allow resizing later
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer    # Bind when pod is scheduled
PVC using StorageClass (no PV needed!) 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
  storageClassName: fast-ssd               # ← References the StorageClass
                                            # PV will be auto-created!
🔧 Manual (PV + PVC)

Admin pre-creates PVs. PVCs bind to existing PVs. Good for on-prem or special storage.

⚡ Dynamic (SC + PVC)

StorageClass auto-creates PVs. No admin needed. Standard for cloud environments.

🌟 Default StorageClass

Most clusters have a default SC. PVCs without storageClassName use it automatically.

11.8 Adding Persistent Storage to anihpj

Our Django job posting app needs persistent storage for three things: the database, user-uploaded media files, and static files. Here's the complete setup:

11.8a PostgreSQL Database Volume

The database is the most critical piece — losing it means losing all JobPost records, user accounts, and application state.

k8s/postgres-storage.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: database
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                  # Only one postgres pod writes
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                  # Enough for job postings data
  storageClassName: standard         # Use cluster default
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 1                        # Single DB instance
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret
          volumeMounts:
            - name: postgres-data
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
              subPath: pgdata              # Avoids "lost+found" issue
      volumes:
        - name: postgres-data
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: postgres-pvc
💡 The subPath Trick

PostgreSQL fails if the data directory contains anything other than its own files. Using subPath: pgdata mounts into a subdirectory, avoiding the lost+found directory that cloud volumes create at the root.

11.8b Django Media Files Volume

User-uploaded files (resumes, profile photos) need to persist and be accessible by all Django pods if you scale horizontally.

k8s/media-storage.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: media-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: media
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany                   # Multiple pods can write uploads
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
  storageClassName: standard

11.8c Putting It All Together — anihpj Deployment

Here's the Django Deployment referencing both ConfigMap, Secret, and persistent volumes:

k8s/anihpj-deployment.yaml — Complete with storage 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config         # Non-sensitive settings
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secret         # Passwords, keys
          volumeMounts:
            - name: media-files
              mountPath: /app/media          # User uploads
            - name: static-files
              mountPath: /app/staticfiles    # Collected static files
      volumes:
        - name: media-files
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: media-pvc
        - name: static-files
          emptyDir: {}                       # Rebuilt on collectstatic

🏗️ Complete anihpj Storage Architecture

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        anihpj Namespace                           │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐       ┌────────────────────┐              │
  │  │  ConfigMap          │       │  Secret             │              │
  │  │  (anihpj-config)   │       │  (anihpj-secret)   │              │
  │  │  DEBUG, DB_HOST...  │       │  SECRET_KEY, PASS.. │              │
  │  └─────────┬──────────┘       └──────────┬─────────┘              │
  │            │ envFrom                      │ envFrom                │
  │            ▼                              ▼                        │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
  │  │                  anihpj Deployment                   │          │
  │  │     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐     │          │
  │  │     │  Pod: anihpj                             │     │          │
  │  │     │                                          │     │          │
  │  │     │  /app/media ──── PVC: media-pvc ──── 5Gi │     │          │
  │  │     │  /app/static ── emptyDir (temp)          │     │          │
  │  │     └─────────────────────────────────────────┘     │          │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐          │
  │  │                postgres Deployment                   │          │
  │  │     ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐     │          │
  │  │     │  Pod: postgres                           │     │          │
  │  │     │                                          │     │          │
  │  │     │  /var/lib/postgresql/data                │     │          │
  │  │     │    └── PVC: postgres-pvc ──── 10Gi       │     │          │
  │  │     └─────────────────────────────────────────┘     │          │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘          │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

11.9 Volume Lifecycle and Reclaim Policies

PersistentVolumes have a lifecycle independent of pods. Understanding the states and reclaim policies prevents accidental data loss.

🔄 PV State Machine

  ┌───────────────┐   PVC binds    ┌───────────────┐   PVC deleted   ┌───────────────┐
  │   Available   │───────────────▶│    Bound      │───────────────▶│   Released    │
  │   🟢 Free     │                │   🔵 In use   │                │   🟡 Waiting  │
  └───────────────┘                └───────────────┘                └───────┬───────┘
         ▲                                                                  │
         │                                                       ┌──────────┴──────────┐
         │                                                       ▼                      ▼
         │ Admin reclaims                               ┌───────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
         └──────────────────────────────────────────────│  Retain       │     │   Delete     │
                                                        │  📋 Manual    │     │   🔴 Gone    │
                                                        │  cleanup      │     │   PV removed │
                                                        └───────────────┘     └──────────────┘

📊 Reclaim Policies

Policy What Happens Use Case
Retain PV stays, data preserved. Admin manually reclaims. ⭐ Databases, critical data
Delete PV and underlying storage (cloud disk) deleted automatically Temporary workloads, CI/CD
Recycle (deprecated) PV data wiped (rm -rf), PV made available again Legacy — use dynamic provisioning instead
💡 Best Practice

Always use Retain for databases and important data. Use Delete only for ephemeral workloads. And always take regular snapshots/backups — reclaim policies are not a backup strategy!

🛠️ PV / PVC kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List PersistentVolumes kubectl get pv
List PersistentVolumeClaims kubectl get pvc -n anihpj-app
PV details (capacity, status) kubectl describe pv postgres-pv
PVC binding status kubectl describe pvc postgres-pvc -n anihpj-app
List StorageClasses kubectl get storageclass
Watch PV/PVC status live kubectl get pv,pvc -n anihpj-app -w
Delete PVC (careful!) kubectl delete pvc media-pvc -n anihpj-app
Patch PV reclaim policy kubectl patch pv my-pv -p '{"spec":{"persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy":"Retain"}}'

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Volumes & Persistent Storage

⏱️ Target: 10 minutes
  1. Create a PersistentVolume named exam-pv with 1Gi capacity, ReadWriteOnce access mode, hostPath at /data/exam, and storageClassName: manual
  2. Create a PersistentVolumeClaim named exam-pvc requesting 500Mi with the same storageClassName and access mode
  3. Create a Pod named data-pod (image: busybox, command: sleep 3600) that mounts the PVC at /mnt/data
  4. Verify the PV and PVC are bound
  5. Create a multi-container Pod named shared-pod with two containers (writer and reader) sharing data via an emptyDir volume
💡 Hint: PV/PVC bind by accessModes + storageClassName. emptyDir needs volume + volumeMount in each container
View Solution 
# 1. PersistentVolume (exam-pv.yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: exam-pv
spec:
  capacity:
    storage: 1Gi
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  hostPath:
    path: /data/exam
  storageClassName: manual
---
# 2. PersistentVolumeClaim (exam-pvc.yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: exam-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 500Mi
  storageClassName: manual
---
# 3. Pod with PVC (data-pod.yaml)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: data-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: busybox
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: data-vol
      mountPath: /mnt/data
  volumes:
  - name: data-vol
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: exam-pvc
# Apply and verify
kubectl apply -f exam-pv.yaml
kubectl apply -f exam-pvc.yaml
kubectl apply -f data-pod.yaml

# 4. Check binding
kubectl get pv,pvc
# STATUS should show "Bound"
# 5. Multi-container with emptyDir
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: shared-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: writer
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "while true; do date >> /shared/log.txt; sleep 5; done"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  - name: reader
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "tail -f /shared/log.txt"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-vol
      mountPath: /shared
  volumes:
  - name: shared-vol
    emptyDir: {}

Chapter 11 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is the difference between an emptyDir volume and a PersistentVolumeClaim? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureemptyDirPVC
LifetimeSame as the podIndependent of pod
Data survives restart?Yes (same node)Yes (any node)
Data survives pod deletion?❌ No✅ Yes
Shared between containers?✅ Yes (same pod)✅ Yes
Use caseScratch space, cache, sidecar sharingDatabases, persistent logs, uploads
Explanation

emptyDir is created when a pod is assigned to a node and deleted when the pod is removed. It's ideal for sharing data between containers in a multi-container pod (e.g., sidecar pattern). PVCs provide storage that outlives pods — required for databases. The CKAD tests both types frequently.

Q2
Create a pod with two containers that share data using an emptyDir volume. Container writer writes to /data/log.txt, container reader reads from /logs/log.txt.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: shared-data
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: shared
    emptyDir: {}
  containers:
  - name: writer
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args: ["while true; do date >> /data/log.txt; sleep 5; done"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared
      mountPath: /data
  - name: reader
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args: ["tail -f /logs/log.txt"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared
      mountPath: /logs
Explanation

Both containers mount the SAME volume (shared) at different paths. What writer writes to /data/, reader sees at /logs/ — it's the same underlying storage. This is the sidecar pattern and a classic CKAD scenario. The volume name must match between volumes[] and volumeMounts[].

Q3
Explain the PV → PVC → Pod relationship. Draw the binding flow.
Show Answer
Answer
┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐     ┌─────────────────┐
│   Admin creates  │     │   Dev creates    │     │   Dev creates    │
│   PersistentVol  │────▶│   PVC (request)  │────▶│   Pod (uses PVC) │
│                  │bind │                  │ref  │                  │
│ capacity: 10Gi   │     │ request: 5Gi     │     │ claimName: my-pvc│
│ accessModes: RWO │     │ accessModes: RWO │     │ mountPath: /data │
│ storageClass: std│     │ storageClass: std│     │                  │
└─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘     └─────────────────┘
     Cluster-wide            Namespaced              Namespaced
Explanation

PVs are cluster-scoped (no namespace). PVCs are namespaced and act as "requests" for storage. K8s matches PVCs to PVs based on: (1) capacity ≥ request, (2) matching accessModes, (3) matching storageClassName. A PV can only bind to one PVC. With dynamic provisioning (StorageClass), PVs are created automatically when a PVC is created.

Q4
Create a PersistentVolumeClaim requesting 1Gi of storage with ReadWriteOnce access mode and storage class standard. Then create a pod that mounts it at /data.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: my-pvc
spec:
  accessModes:
  - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: standard
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: storage-pod
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: data-vol
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: my-pvc
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: data-vol
      mountPath: /data
Explanation

Key CKAD points: (1) The PVC claimName in the pod must match the PVC's metadata.name. (2) ReadWriteOnce = one node can mount read-write. (3) If no PV matches, the PVC stays in Pending state and the pod won't schedule. Check with kubectl get pvc — look for Bound status.

Q5
What are the three PersistentVolume access modes? Which one allows multiple pods on different nodes to write simultaneously?
Show Answer
Answer
Access ModeShortDescription
ReadWriteOnceRWORead-write by a single node
ReadOnlyManyROXRead-only by many nodes
ReadWriteManyRWXRead-write by many nodes ✅

RWX allows multiple pods on different nodes to write simultaneously. Not all storage backends support it (NFS does, AWS EBS does not).

Explanation

RWO is the most common (works with all cloud block storage). RWX is needed for shared storage (e.g., shared uploads directory). RWO means one node, not one pod — multiple pods on the same node can still access it. The CKAD may specify "multiple pods need to write" — that's the RWX signal. Also: ReadWriteOncePod (RWOP) limits to a single pod.

Q6
What are the PersistentVolume reclaim policies? You delete a PVC — what happens to the data for each policy?
Show Answer
Answer
Reclaim PolicyAfter PVC DeletionWhen to Use
RetainPV kept, data preserved, manual cleanup neededProduction databases
DeletePV and underlying storage deleted automaticallyTemporary/disposable data
RecycleData wiped (rm -rf /vol/*), PV reusable⚠️ Deprecated — don't use
Explanation

Default for dynamic provisioning is Delete — dangerous for production! Retain keeps the data but leaves the PV in Released state (it won't auto-bind to a new PVC). You must manually clear the claimRef to reuse it. The CKAD may test scenarios where data is accidentally lost due to the wrong reclaim policy.

Q7
A PVC is stuck in Pending state. List the possible causes and how to diagnose each.
Show Answer
Answer
# Diagnose
kubectl describe pvc my-pvc
# Look at Events section for clues

Common causes:

  1. No matching PV — capacity, accessMode, or storageClass mismatch
  2. StorageClass doesn't exist — typo in storageClassName
  3. No default StorageClass — PVC has no storageClassName and no default is set
  4. All matching PVs already bound — PV:PVC is 1:1
  5. Volume plugin not installed — CSI driver missing for the storage type
Explanation

kubectl describe pvc events tell you exactly what's wrong. On the CKAD, the most common cause is a storageClass mismatch or a capacity request larger than any available PV. Fix by creating a matching PV or correcting the PVC spec. With dynamic provisioning, check the StorageClass exists: kubectl get sc.

Q8
What is a StorageClass? Create a pod that uses dynamic provisioning instead of a pre-created PV.
Show Answer
Answer
# StorageClass enables dynamic provisioning
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/gce-pd
parameters:
  type: pd-ssd
---
# PVC references the StorageClass — PV created automatically
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: fast-storage
spec:
  accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
  storageClassName: fast-ssd    # ← triggers dynamic provisioning
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
Explanation

With dynamic provisioning, you skip creating PVs manually. The StorageClass's provisioner automatically creates the backend storage (cloud disk, NFS share, etc.) and a matching PV. The CKAD exam clusters typically have a default StorageClass — if you omit storageClassName, it uses the default. Check with kubectl get sc (look for (default)).

Q9
How do you use emptyDir with medium: Memory? What is this useful for?
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  volumes:
  - name: cache
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory         # tmpfs — RAM-backed
      sizeLimit: 256Mi       # limit memory usage
  containers:
  - name: app
    volumeMounts:
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /tmp/cache
Explanation

medium: Memory creates a tmpfs mount — stored in RAM, not disk. It's extremely fast but volatile and counts against the container's memory limit. Use for: (1) scratch computation space, (2) sensitive temp files that shouldn't touch disk, (3) high-performance caching. The sizeLimit prevents the volume from consuming all node memory.

Q10
You need to resize an existing PVC from 5Gi to 10Gi. How do you do this? What prerequisites must be met?
Show Answer
Answer
# Edit the PVC to increase storage
kubectl edit pvc my-pvc
# Change spec.resources.requests.storage from 5Gi to 10Gi

# Or use patch
kubectl patch pvc my-pvc -p \
  '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"10Gi"}}}}'

Prerequisites:

  1. StorageClass must have allowVolumeExpansion: true
  2. You can only increase size, never decrease
  3. For file system volumes, pod may need to be restarted for resize
Explanation

PVC expansion is a two-step process: (1) the backend volume is resized, (2) the filesystem is expanded. Step 2 may require a pod restart (depends on the CSI driver). Check if expansion is supported: kubectl get sc <name> -o yaml | grep allowVolumeExpansion. On the CKAD, verify the StorageClass supports expansion before attempting.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Volume Security Risks

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

Dangerous Volume Types

Volume TypeRisk LevelSecurity Concern
hostPath🔴 CRITICALAccess ANY host file — container escape vector
hostPID: true🔴 CRITICALSee/signal host processes
hostNetwork: true🔴 CRITICALAccess host network stack
emptyDir🟢 SafePod-scoped, ephemeral
projected🟢 SafeBound SA tokens with expiry

hostPath — The Container Escape

# DANGEROUS — never allow in production
volumes:
- name: host-root
  hostPath:
    path: /              # Access ENTIRE host filesystem!
    
# An attacker in this container can:
# 1. Read /etc/shadow (password hashes)
# 2. Write SSH keys to /root/.ssh/authorized_keys
# 3. Read kubelet credentials
# 4. Modify host binaries

# PSS "baseline" and "restricted" both BLOCK hostPath

Chapter 11 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A pod uses hostPath to mount / (root filesystem). Explain the security risk and how to prevent it cluster-wide.
Show Answer
Answer
# Risk: Full host filesystem access enables:
# - Reading /etc/shadow, /etc/kubernetes/pki/
# - Writing to /etc/crontab (persistence)
# - Modifying kubelet config
# - Container escape → full node compromise

# Prevention:
# 1. Pod Security Admission (enforce baseline or restricted)
kubectl label namespace production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=baseline
# baseline blocks: hostPath, hostPID, hostNetwork, privileged

# 2. OPA Gatekeeper constraint
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sPSPHostFilesystem
metadata:
  name: deny-host-filesystem
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      kinds: ["Pod"]
  parameters:
    allowedHostPaths: []    # Block ALL hostPath mounts

# 3. Find existing pods with hostPath:
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] |
  select(.spec.volumes[]? | .hostPath != null) |
  {name: .metadata.name, ns: .metadata.namespace}'
Explanation

hostPath is one of the most dangerous volume types — it's effectively a container escape. Both PSA baseline and restricted profiles block hostPath. On CKS, know how to find pods using hostPath and prevent them via admission control. Some system pods (kube-proxy, CNI) legitimately use hostPath in kube-system.

CKS-2
How do you use projected volumes for secure ServiceAccount token mounting with expiry?
Show Answer
Answer
# Use projected volume for time-limited, audience-bound SA tokens
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-token-pod
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false    # Disable default mount
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    volumeMounts:
    - name: token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/tokens
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: token
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          path: api-token
          expirationSeconds: 3600        # Expires in 1 hour
          audience: my-api               # Audience-bound
# Benefits over default SA token:
# ✅ Expires (default tokens never expire!)
# ✅ Audience-bound (only accepted by specified API)
# ✅ Auto-rotated by kubelet before expiry
Explanation

Projected service account tokens are more secure: they expire, are audience-bound, and auto-rotate. Default SA tokens never expire and work for the API server. On CKS, disable auto-mounting and use projected tokens when API access is needed. This limits the damage if a token is stolen.

CKS-3
How do you enforce that PersistentVolumes use encryption?
Show Answer
Answer
# AWS EBS encrypted StorageClass
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: encrypted-gp3
provisioner: ebs.csi.aws.com
parameters:
  type: gp3
  encrypted: "true"                    # Enable encryption
  kmsKeyId: arn:aws:kms:...            # Optional: custom KMS key

# GCP PD encrypted StorageClass
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: encrypted-pd
provisioner: pd.csi.storage.gke.io
parameters:
  type: pd-ssd
  disk-encryption-kms-key: projects/.../cryptoKeys/my-key

# Make encrypted class the default
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: encrypted-gp3
  annotations:
    storageclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
Explanation

PersistentVolume encryption is configured at the StorageClass level using cloud-provider-specific parameters. Set the encrypted StorageClass as default so all PVCs automatically get encrypted volumes. On CKS, ensure PVs holding sensitive data (databases, logs) use encrypted storage.

CKS-4
What is the security risk of emptyDir with medium: Memory? When is it appropriate?
Show Answer
Answer
# emptyDir with tmpfs (RAM-backed)
volumes:
- name: sensitive-data
  emptyDir:
    medium: Memory          # Stored in RAM, not disk
    sizeLimit: 64Mi         # ALWAYS set a limit!

# Security properties:
# ✅ Data never written to disk (no disk forensics risk)
# ✅ Automatically cleared when pod is deleted
# ✅ Good for temporary sensitive data (tokens, session keys)

# Risk:
# ⚠️ Without sizeLimit, a container can consume all node RAM → OOM
# ⚠️ Other containers in same pod can read the data
# ⚠️ Still visible via /proc filesystem if not properly restricted

# Best practice: combine with resource limits
containers:
- name: app
  resources:
    limits:
      memory: "128Mi"       # Includes tmpfs usage!
Explanation

medium: Memory (tmpfs) is appropriate for sensitive temporary data that shouldn't touch disk. But always set sizeLimit to prevent memory exhaustion DoS. The memory used by tmpfs counts against the container's memory limit. On CKS, know when to use tmpfs vs. regular emptyDir.

CKS-5
Find all pods in the cluster that use hostPath volumes and determine which are legitimate vs. risky.
Show Answer
Answer
# Find all pods with hostPath volumes
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.spec.volumes[]? | .hostPath != null) |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name): " +
  ([.spec.volumes[] | select(.hostPath != null) |
    .hostPath.path] | join(", "))
'

# Typical output:
# kube-system/kube-proxy-xxxxx: /lib/modules, /run/xtables.lock
# kube-system/calico-node-xxxxx: /var/run/calico, /var/lib/calico
# production/malicious-pod: /                    ← SUSPICIOUS!

# Legitimate hostPath uses (kube-system only):
# ✅ CNI plugins: /opt/cni/bin, /etc/cni/net.d
# ✅ kube-proxy: /lib/modules
# ✅ Logging agents: /var/log
# ✅ Device plugins: /dev

# RISKY hostPath:
# ❌ / (root filesystem)
# ❌ /etc (system configuration)
# ❌ /var/run/docker.sock (container escape!)
# ❌ Any path in non-kube-system namespace
Explanation

On CKS, you need to audit hostPath usage. System components in kube-system legitimately use hostPath. Any hostPath in user namespaces is suspicious. Mounting the Docker socket (/var/run/docker.sock) allows creating containers on the host — a common container escape. Use PSA to block hostPath in non-system namespaces.

🌐 Part 4: Networking & Exposure Chapters 12-14

Chapter 12: Kubernetes Networking Fundamentals

Understand how pods, services, and the outside world communicate. Kubernetes networking follows a flat network model where every pod gets its own IP — no NAT, no port mapping. Master these fundamentals before diving into Services and Ingress.

🌐 Networking 🔗 Pod-to-Pod 📡 DNS & Routing 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ The 4 fundamental rules of the Kubernetes network model
  • ✅ How containers, pods, and services communicate
  • ✅ How DNS resolution works inside a cluster (CoreDNS)
  • ✅ Network Policies — firewall rules for pods
  • ✅ CNI plugins and how to choose one
  • ✅ Troubleshooting common network issues
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Networking on the CKAD — NetworkPolicies Are Key

While you won't configure CNI plugins, you MUST master NetworkPolicies:

  • Default behavior — Without any NetworkPolicy, all pods can talk to all pods. Adding a policy to a namespace starts DENYING by default
  • Deny-all, then allow — Start with a default deny, then add allow rules for specific pods/namespaces
  • DNS resolution — Know the pattern: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
  • Port 53 — If you create a deny-all policy, remember to allow DNS (port 53) or nothing works!

NetworkPolicy YAML Patterns

NetworkPolicy is tested on EVERY CKAD exam. Memorize deny-all + allow pattern.

# STEP 1: Default Deny All Ingress in a namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}              # ← Empty = applies to ALL pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress                    # ← Blocks all incoming traffic

# STEP 2: Allow specific traffic
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:                 # ← Target pods (who gets this rule)
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj-api
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:             # ← Allow from pods with this label
        matchLabels:
          app: anihpj-frontend
    - namespaceSelector:       # ← Allow from pods in this namespace  
        matchLabels:
          name: monitoring
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8000

# DENY ALL EGRESS (block outgoing)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-egress
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:                        # ← Allow DNS only!
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53

NetworkPolicy Traps

  • ❌ AND vs OR confusion — Multiple items in from[] array = OR. Multiple selectors in same from item = AND
  • Example OR: from: [{podSelector: {app: web}}, {namespaceSelector: {name: test}}] → web pods OR anything from test ns
  • Example AND: from: [{podSelector: {app: web}, namespaceSelector: {name: test}}] → web pods only in test ns
  • ❌ Forgetting DNS in egress deny-all → pods can't resolve service names = everything breaks
  • podSelector: {} means ALL pods. Don't confuse with missing podSelector (also means all)
  • NetworkPolicy is namespaced — it only affects pods in its own namespace

kubectl explain — NetworkPolicy Fields

kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.podSelector
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.ingress
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.ingress.from
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.ingress.ports
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.egress
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.egress.to
kubectl explain networkpolicy.spec.policyTypes

📊 The 4 Kubernetes Networking Rules

🔢 Unique Pod IPs Every pod gets its own cluster-wide IP
🔗 Pod-to-Pod Any pod can reach any other pod directly
🚫 No NAT Pods see each other's real IPs, no translation
📡 Node-to-Pod Nodes can communicate with all pods directly
🌐
Client External request
→
🚪
Ingress L7 routing rules
→
🔀
Service ClusterIP / NodePort / LB
→
📋
Endpoints Pod IP list via selector
→
📦
Pod Container handles request
🔴 Service Type: ClusterIP
  • Internal only — no external access
  • Default type if not specified
  • DNS: svc-name.ns.svc.cluster.local
  • Use for: inter-service communication
🟢 Service Type: NodePort / LoadBalancer
  • NodePort opens port 30000-32767 on every node
  • LoadBalancer provisions cloud LB + NodePort
  • External traffic reaches pods via node IP:port
  • Use for: exposing apps externally

12.1 The Kubernetes Network Model — 4 Rules

Every Kubernetes cluster must implement these 4 networking rules. They are non-negotiable — every CNI plugin, cloud provider, and cluster setup must guarantee them:

🔢 Rule 1: Unique Pod IPs

Every pod gets its own unique IP address. No two pods share the same IP within the cluster.

🔗 Rule 2: Same-Node Communication

Pods on the same node can communicate directly using their pod IPs — no extra configuration needed.

🌉 Rule 3: Cross-Node, No NAT

Pods on different nodes can communicate without NAT (Network Address Translation). The source IP is preserved.

🤖 Rule 4: Agent-to-Pod

System agents on a node (kubelet, kube-proxy) can communicate with all pods on that node.

🗺️ Kubernetes Network Model — Visualized

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        KUBERNETES CLUSTER                              │
  │                                                                         │
  │   NODE 1  (10.0.1.x)                    NODE 2  (10.0.2.x)            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────┐          ┌──────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                          │          │                          │    │
  │  │  📦 Pod A (anihpj)       │  Rule 3  │  📦 Pod C (redis)        │    │
  │  │  IP: 10.0.1.5           │◄────────►│  IP: 10.0.2.8           │    │
  │  │         │                │ No NAT!  │                          │    │
  │  │  Rule 2 │ same node     │          │  📦 Pod D (postgres)     │    │
  │  │         ▼                │          │  IP: 10.0.2.9           │    │
  │  │  📦 Pod B (nginx)        │          │                          │    │
  │  │  IP: 10.0.1.6           │          │                          │    │
  │  │                          │          │                          │    │
  │  │  🤖 kubelet (Rule 4)    │          │  🤖 kubelet (Rule 4)    │    │
  │  │     can reach all pods  │          │     can reach all pods  │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────┘          └──────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                         │
  │  Rule 1: ✅ Every pod has a unique IP (10.0.1.5, 10.0.1.6, etc.)       │
  │  Rule 2: ✅ Pod A ↔ Pod B communicate directly (same node)             │
  │  Rule 3: ✅ Pod A ↔ Pod C communicate without NAT (cross-node)         │
  │  Rule 4: ✅ kubelet on each node can reach every pod on its node        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 Why This Matters for anihpj

Because of these rules, your anihpj Django pod at 10.0.1.5 can directly reach the PostgreSQL pod at 10.0.2.9 on a different node — no special configuration. Kubernetes networking is flat by default: every pod can talk to every other pod. (We'll restrict this with Network Policies in section 12.6.)

💡 Real-World Analogy: The Office Building

Think of the Kubernetes network like an office building with an intercom system:

  • Rule 1 — Every office has its own unique extension number (pod IP)
  • Rule 2 — Offices on the same floor can shout down the hall (same-node bridge)
  • Rule 3 — Offices on different floors use the intercom — no operator needed (cross-node, no NAT)
  • Rule 4 — Building security can reach any office on each floor (agents/kubelet)

12.2 Container-to-Container Communication (Same Pod)

Containers within the same pod share the same network namespace. This means they share the same IP address and can communicate via localhost — just like two processes on the same machine.

📦 Shared Network Namespace Inside a Pod

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    POD  (IP: 10.0.1.5)                   │
  │                 Shared Network Namespace                  │
  │                                                          │
  │   ┌─────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────┐       │
  │   │  Container 1    │         │  Container 2    │       │
  │   │  🐍 Django       │  local  │  🌐 Nginx        │       │
  │   │  (anihpj)       │◄─────►│  (reverse proxy) │       │
  │   │  :8000          │  host  │  :80             │       │
  │   └─────────────────┘         └─────────────────┘       │
  │            │                          │                   │
  │            └──────────┬───────────────┘                   │
  │                       │                                   │
  │              localhost (127.0.0.1)                        │
  │              Shared port space                            │
  │              Same IP for both                             │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  
  Nginx config:  proxy_pass http://localhost:8000;
  Django:        ALLOWED_HOSTS = ["localhost", "10.0.1.5"]
Pod with Django + Nginx sidecar sharing localhost 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-nginx
spec:
  containers:
    - name: anihpj
      image: anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8000             # Django listens here

    - name: nginx-sidecar
      image: nginx:alpine
      ports:
        - containerPort: 80               # Nginx listens here
      volumeMounts:
        - name: nginx-conf
          mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
  volumes:
    - name: nginx-conf
      configMap:
        name: nginx-config                # proxy_pass http://localhost:8000
⚠️ Port Conflicts!

Since containers in the same pod share the port space, two containers cannot listen on the same port. If both try to use port 8000, one will fail to start. Always assign unique ports to each container.

12.3 Pod-to-Pod Communication

Pods communicate directly using their IP addresses. The mechanism differs depending on whether the pods are on the same node or different nodes.

🔀 Same Node vs Cross-Node Communication

  SAME NODE (via virtual bridge):          CROSS-NODE (via CNI overlay):
  ─────────────────────────────            ───────────────────────────────

  ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐                ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
  │ Pod A    │ │ Pod B    │                │ Pod A    │    │ Pod C    │
  │ 10.0.1.5 │ │ 10.0.1.6 │                │ 10.0.1.5 │    │ 10.0.2.8 │
  └────┬─────┘ └────┬─────┘                └────┬─────┘    └────┬─────┘
       │             │                          │               │
  ┌────┴─────────────┴─────┐          ┌────────┴───┐    ┌─────┴────────┐
  │   veth pairs           │          │  Node 1    │    │  Node 2      │
  │      │                 │          │  eth0      │    │  eth0        │
  │   cbr0 (bridge)        │          └────┬───────┘    └─────┬────────┘
  │   Virtual L2 bridge    │               │                  │
  └────────────────────────┘               │   CNI overlay    │
                                           │   (VXLAN/BGP)    │
  ⚡ Fast: direct bridge                   └────────┬─────────┘
     No routing needed                              │
                                           Physical network / cloud VPC
Aspect Same Node Cross-Node
Mechanism Linux virtual bridge (cbr0) CNI plugin overlay network
Speed ⚡ Very fast (no network hop) 🔄 Slight overhead (encapsulation)
NAT required? No No (Rule 3)
Example tech veth pairs + bridge Calico (BGP), Flannel (VXLAN), Cilium (eBPF)

12.4 Pod-to-Service Communication

Direct pod-to-pod communication has a problem: pod IPs change when pods restart. Services solve this by providing a stable virtual IP (ClusterIP) that routes traffic to healthy pods. We'll deep-dive into Services in Chapter 13.

🔄 How kube-proxy Routes Service Traffic

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       SERVICE ROUTING FLOW                         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  1. Pod sends request to Service ClusterIP                        │
  │                                                                    │
  │     📦 anihpj pod                                                  │
  │     curl http://postgres-svc:5432                                 │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  2. CoreDNS resolves "postgres-svc" → 10.96.45.120 (ClusterIP)   │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  3. kube-proxy intercepts traffic to 10.96.45.120                 │
  │     (via iptables rules or IPVS)                                  │
  │         │                                                          │
  │         ▼                                                          │
  │  4. Routes to a healthy backend pod                                │
  │                                                                    │
  │     ┌────────────────┐                                             │
  │     │  postgres-svc  │ ClusterIP: 10.96.45.120                    │
  │     │  (Service)     │                                             │
  │     └───┬─────┬──────┘                                             │
  │         │     │      load-balanced                                 │
  │         ▼     ▼                                                    │
  │     ┌──────┐ ┌──────┐                                              │
  │     │Pod 1 │ │Pod 2 │ ← Endpoints (healthy pods matching labels)  │
  │     │pg-abc│ │pg-xyz│                                              │
  │     └──────┘ └──────┘                                              │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🔧 iptables Mode (Default)

kube-proxy writes iptables rules on each node. Simple and reliable but O(n) — performance degrades with thousands of Services. Random backend selection.

⚡ IPVS Mode (Performance)

Uses Linux IPVS (IP Virtual Server) kernel module. O(1) lookups — handles 10,000+ Services efficiently. Supports round-robin, least-connections, and weighted algorithms.

💡 Why Not Just Use Pod IPs?

When a Deployment rolls out an update, old pods are terminated and new ones are created with new IPs. If your anihpj app is hardcoded to 10.0.2.9 for the database, it breaks instantly. Services give you a stable DNS name and IP that always routes to the current healthy pods.

🔢 Service Port vs TargetPort vs ContainerPort

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  These three port fields confuse everyone. Here's how they chain:  │
  │                                                                     │
  │   Client (another pod)                                             │
  │   curl http://anihpj-svc:80                                       │
  │                    │                                                │
  │                    │  ① Service "port" = 80                        │
  │                    │     (what clients connect to)                  │
  │                    ▼                                                │
  │            ┌──────────────┐                                        │
  │            │  Service     │                                        │
  │            │  port: 80    │──────────────┐                         │
  │            │             ─┤              │                         │
  │            │  targetPort: │  ② "targetPort" = 8000                │
  │            │    8000      │     (forwards to pod port)             │
  │            └──────────────┘              │                         │
  │                                          ▼                         │
  │                               ┌────────────────┐                   │
  │                               │  Pod           │                   │
  │                               │  containerPort:│  ③ Container     │
  │                               │    8000        │     listens here  │
  │                               │  (Django)      │                   │
  │                               └────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                                     │
  │  Summary:  client:80 ──▶ Service ──▶ pod:8000 ──▶ container:8000  │
  │                 ①              ②              ③                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

12.5 DNS in Kubernetes

Every Kubernetes cluster runs CoreDNS — a built-in DNS server that automatically creates DNS records for every Service and Pod. This is why you can use names like postgres-svc instead of IP addresses.

🌐 CoreDNS Resolution Flow

  📦 anihpj pod
  │
  │  import psycopg2
  │  conn = psycopg2.connect(host="postgres-svc", port=5432)
  │                                  │
  │                                  ▼
  │                          ┌──────────────┐
  │                          │   CoreDNS    │
  │                          │  (kube-dns)  │
  │                          └──────┬───────┘
  │                                 │ resolves
  │                                 ▼
  │         "postgres-svc"                           Full FQDN:
  │         ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  │         Same namespace:  postgres-svc              (short)
  │         Cross namespace: postgres-svc.database     (.namespace)
  │         Full FQDN:       postgres-svc.database.svc.cluster.local
  │                                 │
  │                                 ▼
  │                          ClusterIP: 10.96.45.120
  │                                 │
  │                                 ▼
  │                          ┌──────────────┐
  │                          │ postgres pod │
  │                          │ 10.0.2.9     │
  │                          └──────────────┘

📋 DNS Name Formats

Resource DNS Format Example (anihpj)
Service (same ns) <service-name> anihpj-svc
Service (cross ns) <svc>.<namespace> postgres-svc.database
Service (full FQDN) <svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local anihpj-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local
Pod <pod-ip-dashed>.<ns>.pod.cluster.local 10-0-1-5.anihpj-app.pod.cluster.local
Headless Service <pod-name>.<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local postgres-0.postgres-headless.database.svc.cluster.local
Testing DNS from inside a pod 
# Verify DNS is working
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- nslookup kubernetes
Server:    10.96.0.10
Address:   10.96.0.10#53
Name:      kubernetes.default.svc.cluster.local
Address:   10.96.0.1

# Resolve a service name
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- nslookup postgres-svc.anihpj-app
Name:      postgres-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local
Address:   10.96.45.120

# Check the pod's DNS config (/etc/resolv.conf)
$ kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
nameserver 10.96.0.10
search anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
ndots:5
💡 Why Short Names Work

The search line in /etc/resolv.conf tells the pod to automatically append .anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local when resolving short names. That's why postgres-svc works — it becomes postgres-svc.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local automatically.

🔍 Understanding ndots:5 — Why External DNS Is Slow

  ndots:5 means: if the name has fewer than 5 dots, try all search
  domains FIRST before querying it as-is.

  Looking up "google.com" (1 dot, < 5):
  ──────────────────────────────────────
  Try 1: google.com.anihpj-app.svc.cluster.local  → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 2: google.com.svc.cluster.local              → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 3: google.com.cluster.local                  → NXDOMAIN ❌
  Try 4: google.com                                → 142.250.x.x ✅

  😱 That's 3 wasted DNS queries for every external name!

  ✅ Fix: Use FQDN with trailing dot to bypass search:
     "google.com."  → resolves immediately (0 wasted queries)
  
  ✅ Or set ndots:1 in pod's dnsConfig for external-heavy apps:
     spec:
       dnsConfig:
         options:
           - name: ndots
             value: "1"

12.6 Network Policies — Firewall Rules for Pods

By default, Kubernetes has no network isolation — every pod can talk to every other pod. Network Policies let you restrict traffic flow, acting as firewall rules at the pod level.

🔥 Before vs After Network Policy

  ❌ WITHOUT NetworkPolicy              ✅ WITH NetworkPolicy
  (default — wide open):               (locked down):

  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐        ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐  ┌──────┐
  │ Pod A │←→│ Pod B │←→│ Pod C │        │ Pod A │  │ Pod B │  │ Pod C │
  │ front │  │ back  │  │  db   │        │ front │──│ back  │──│  db   │
  └──┬────┘  └──┬────┘  └──┬────┘        └──────┘  └──────┘  └──────┘
     │          │          │                 │         │          │
     ├──────────┼──────────┤              ┌──┘    ┌────┘     ┌───┘
     │          │          │              │       │          │
  ALL talk to ALL! 😱                  front→back only  back→db only ✅
                                       front✗→db  BLOCKED! 🛡️
k8s/network-policy.yaml — Only allow frontend → backend 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                        # Apply to anihpj (backend) pods
      role: backend
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress                             # Control incoming traffic
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
              role: frontend              # Only from pods with this label
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 8000                      # Only on Django's port
k8s/db-network-policy.yaml — Database only accepts backend 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: database-restrict
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres                       # Apply to postgres pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
              role: backend               # Only backend can reach DB
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432
  egress:
    - to: []                              # DB doesn't need outbound access
    - ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53                        # Except DNS resolution
⚠️ CNI Must Support NetworkPolicy

Not all CNI plugins enforce Network Policies! Flannel does NOT support them. You need Calico, Cilium, or Weave Net. In Minikube, enable the Calico addon: minikube start --cni=calico.

Default Deny All — The Zero Trust Starting Point 
# Block ALL traffic to all pods in a namespace.
# Then whitelist only what's needed (like the policies above).
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  podSelector: {}           # {} = all pods in the namespace
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress                # Blocks both incoming AND outgoing
💡 Best Practice: Default Deny + Whitelist

In production, apply a Default Deny All policy first, then add specific allow policies. This follows the principle of least privilege — pods can only communicate with what you explicitly permit.

12.7 CNI Plugins — The Network Plumbing

The Container Network Interface (CNI) is the standard that defines how networking is configured for containers. Your CNI plugin is responsible for assigning IPs to pods, creating virtual networks, and routing traffic between nodes.

🔌 Where CNI Fits in the Stack

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Your Application (anihpj)                       │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Kubernetes API (Services, Pods, NetworkPolicy)  │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  CNI Plugin (Calico / Flannel / Cilium)          │  ← You choose this!
  │  • Assigns pod IPs from CIDR range               │
  │  • Creates virtual network interfaces (veth)     │
  │  • Routes traffic between nodes                  │
  │  • Enforces NetworkPolicy (if supported)         │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Linux Kernel (iptables, IPVS, eBPF, bridges)   │
  ├──────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  Physical / Cloud Network                        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 CNI Plugin Comparison

Plugin Overlay NetworkPolicy Performance Best For
Calico BGP (native) or VXLAN ✅ Full support ⚡ Excellent Production, security-focused
Flannel VXLAN ❌ None 🔄 Good Simple clusters, learning
Cilium eBPF (kernel-level) ✅ Advanced (L7) ⚡⚡ Best High-performance, observability
Weave Net VXLAN mesh ✅ Basic 🔄 Good Multi-cloud, ease of use
Canal Flannel networking ✅ Calico policies 🔄 Good Flannel users who need policies
💡 Recommendation for anihpj

For learning, Flannel is simplest (Minikube default). For production, use Calico (battle-tested, NetworkPolicy support) or Cilium (next-gen eBPF performance). Most managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS) come with a pre-configured CNI.

12.8 Troubleshooting Network Issues

Network problems are the most common issues in Kubernetes. Use this systematic checklist to diagnose and resolve them:

🔍 Network Debugging Flowchart

  Pod can't reach a Service?
  │
  ├── 1. Can the pod resolve DNS?
  │   $ kubectl exec pod -- nslookup kubernetes
  │   ├── ❌ DNS fails → check CoreDNS pods (kube-system namespace)
  │   └── ✅ DNS works → continue
  │
  ├── 2. Can the pod reach the Service IP?
  │   $ kubectl exec pod -- curl -s http://svc-name:port
  │   ├── ❌ Connection refused → Service port YAML wrong?
  │   └── ✅ Response OK → not a network issue
  │
  ├── 3. Does the Service have endpoints?
  │   $ kubectl get endpoints svc-name
  │   ├── ❌ No endpoints → labels don't match! Check:
  │   │   $ kubectl describe svc svc-name     (Selector)
  │   │   $ kubectl get pods --show-labels     (Pod labels)
  │   └── ✅ Endpoints listed → continue
  │
  ├── 4. Are the backend pods running?
  │   $ kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj
  │   ├── ❌ CrashLoopBackOff → app issue, check logs
  │   └── ✅ Running → continue
  │
  └── 5. Is a NetworkPolicy blocking traffic?
      $ kubectl get networkpolicy -n namespace
      ├── Policy exists → check ingress/egress rules
      └── No policies → check CNI plugin health

🛠️ Network Debugging Commands

Task Command
Test DNS resolution kubectl exec -it pod -- nslookup svc-name
Curl a service from a pod kubectl exec -it pod -- curl -s http://svc:port
Check Service endpoints kubectl get endpoints svc-name
View pod's IP and node kubectl get pod -o wide
Check CoreDNS status kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
List network policies kubectl get networkpolicy -A
Debug with a temp pod kubectl run debug --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh
Check kube-proxy logs kubectl logs -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-proxy
📝 The #1 Mistake: Selector Mismatch

Over 50% of "Service not working" issues come from label selector mismatch. The Service's spec.selector labels must exactly match the pod's metadata.labels. Use kubectl describe svc and kubectl get pods --show-labels to compare.

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: NetworkPolicy

⏱️ Target: 8 minutes
  1. Create a namespace secure-ns and deploy two pods: web (nginx) and db (mysql:8) with labels role=web and role=db
  2. Create a default-deny-all ingress NetworkPolicy for secure-ns
  3. Create a NetworkPolicy that allows role=web pods to reach role=db pods on port 3306
  4. Verify: web → db should work on 3306, but NOT on any other port
💡 Hint: spec.podSelector selects WHICH pods the policy applies TO. ingress.from specifies WHO can connect
View Solution 
# 1. Setup
# kubectl create ns secure-ns
# kubectl run web --image=nginx -n secure-ns -l role=web
# kubectl run db --image=mysql:8 -n secure-ns -l role=db

# 2. Default deny all ingress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: secure-ns
spec:
  podSelector: {}    # applies to ALL pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress          # deny all incoming
---
# 3. Allow web → db on 3306
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-web-to-db
  namespace: secure-ns
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      role: db       # applies TO db pods
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          role: web   # allow FROM web pods
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 3306      # only on this port

Chapter 12 — Practice Questions

Q1
Explain the three levels of Kubernetes networking. How does a pod communicate with: (a) another container in the same pod, (b) a pod on the same node, (c) a pod on a different node?
Show Answer
Answer
CommunicationMechanismAddress
Same pod, different containerlocalhostlocalhost:PORT
Different pod, same nodeVirtual bridge (cbr0)Pod IP directly
Different pod, different nodeCNI plugin (overlay/routing)Pod IP directly
Explanation

Kubernetes guarantees: (1) every pod gets a unique IP, (2) all pods can communicate without NAT, (3) containers in the same pod share a network namespace (same IP, same ports). The CNI plugin (Calico, Flannel, Cilium) handles cross-node communication. On the CKAD, focus on pod-to-pod and pod-to-Service communication — you won't configure CNI plugins.

Q2
What is Kubernetes DNS? How do you resolve a Service named api-svc in namespace backend from a pod in namespace frontend?
Show Answer
Answer
# Full DNS name format:
# <service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

# From frontend namespace, resolve backend's service:
curl http://api-svc.backend.svc.cluster.local:8080

# Short form (works since DNS search domains):
curl http://api-svc.backend:8080
Explanation

CoreDNS runs as a Deployment in kube-system. Within the SAME namespace, just use the service name: curl api-svc. Cross-namespace requires service.namespace at minimum. The CKAD often tests cross-namespace communication — always use the full svc.namespace format to be safe.

Q3
Two pods are in the same namespace. Pod A can reach Pod B by IP but NOT by Service name. What's wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check 1: Does the Service exist?
kubectl get svc

# Check 2: Does DNS resolve?
kubectl exec pod-a -- nslookup pod-b-svc

# Check 3: Is CoreDNS running?
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Check 4: Pod's DNS config
kubectl exec pod-a -- cat /etc/resolv.conf

Most likely causes: (1) Service doesn't exist, (2) CoreDNS pods are crashlooping, (3) dnsPolicy is set to None or Default instead of ClusterFirst.

Explanation

Default dnsPolicy is ClusterFirst, which directs DNS queries to CoreDNS. If someone sets dnsPolicy: Default, pods use the node's DNS — which can't resolve K8s service names. This is a sneaky CKAD troubleshooting scenario. Always check /etc/resolv.conf inside the pod and kubectl get endpoints.

Q4
What is a NetworkPolicy? Write a NetworkPolicy that allows only pods with label role: api to access pods with label app: db on port 5432.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-allow-api
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: db              # Apply to db pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          role: api         # Allow only api pods
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
Explanation

podSelector = which pods this policy applies TO. ingress.from.podSelector = which pods are allowed to connect. Once ANY NetworkPolicy selects a pod, all non-matching traffic is denied by default. Without any NetworkPolicy, all traffic is allowed. The CKAD tests NetworkPolicy creation frequently — practice the YAML structure.

Q5
Write a NetworkPolicy that blocks ALL ingress traffic to pods with label app: secure (a "deny all" policy).
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all-ingress
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secure
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  # No ingress rules = deny all incoming traffic
Explanation

The absence of ingress: rules combined with policyTypes: ["Ingress"] means "apply ingress policy but allow nothing." For deny-all egress: add Egress to policyTypes with no egress rules. For deny-all BOTH directions, include both in policyTypes with no rules. This is a must-know CKAD pattern.

Q6
What's the difference between a pod IP and a Service ClusterIP? Why shouldn't you use pod IPs directly?
Show Answer
Answer
FeaturePod IPService ClusterIP
Stable?❌ Changes on restarts✅ Permanent (until deleted)
Load balanced?❌ Single pod✅ Across all matching pods
DNS name?Only with headless svc✅ Always resolvable
Used byDebugging onlyAll inter-service communication
Explanation

Pod IPs are ephemeral — when a pod restarts, it gets a new IP. Services provide a stable virtual IP and DNS name that persists regardless of pod lifecycle. This abstraction layer is fundamental to Kubernetes. On the CKAD, always create a Service to expose pods — never hardcode pod IPs in application config.

Q7
How do you test network connectivity between two pods? Pod A needs to reach Pod B's service on port 8080.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: From Pod A, curl the service
kubectl exec pod-a -- curl -s pod-b-svc:8080

# Method 2: Run a temporary debug pod
kubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- pod-b-svc:8080

# Method 3: Use netcat to test port
kubectl exec pod-a -- nc -zv pod-b-svc 8080

# Method 4: DNS resolution test
kubectl exec pod-a -- nslookup pod-b-svc
Explanation

The CKAD debugging flow: (1) Test DNS with nslookup, (2) Test connectivity with curl or wget, (3) Check endpoints with kubectl get endpoints. The temporary pod (--rm -it) is invaluable when existing pods don't have networking tools installed. Use busybox or nicolaka/netshoot for debugging.

Q8
What is a headless Service? When would you use one instead of a regular ClusterIP Service?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: db-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None          # ← This makes it headless
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432

DNS lookup returns individual pod IPs instead of a single ClusterIP. Used for StatefulSets where each pod needs a unique, stable DNS name.

Explanation

Regular Service: nslookup db-svc → returns one ClusterIP. Headless Service: nslookup db-headless → returns ALL pod IPs. Each pod gets pod-name.headless-svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local. Used by databases (Postgres, MySQL) in StatefulSets. The CKAD tests this pattern with StatefulSet questions.

Q9
Write a NetworkPolicy that allows egress from app: web pods ONLY to DNS (port 53) and to app: api pods on port 8080.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: web-egress
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:                        # Rule 1: DNS
