    - namespaceSelector: {}    # any namespace (kube-system)
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53
  - to:                        # Rule 2: API pods
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: api
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
Explanation

Critical trap: If you restrict egress but forget to allow DNS (port 53), pods can't resolve service names — everything breaks silently. Always include DNS in egress policies. The namespaceSelector: {} matches ALL namespaces (needed because CoreDNS is in kube-system). This is a frequent CKAD gotcha.

Q10
A pod's container listens on port 3000 but your Service targets port 8080. No error appears but the Service doesn't work. Why?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check what the pod actually listens on
kubectl exec my-pod -- netstat -tlnp
# Shows: 3000 (not 8080)

# Check Service target port
kubectl describe svc my-svc
# Shows: TargetPort: 8080  ← WRONG

# Fix: Update the service targetPort to 3000
kubectl patch svc my-svc -p '{"spec":{"ports":[{"port":80,"targetPort":3000}]}}'
Explanation

targetPort must match what the container actually listens on. The port is what clients use. Kubernetes doesn't validate that the targetPort matches a real listening process — it simply forwards traffic that then gets "connection refused." On the CKAD: always verify with kubectl get endpoints (should show IP:PORT) and kubectl exec -- curl localhost:PORT.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Network Security & CNI

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% CLUSTER SETUP 15%

CNI Security Features Comparison

FeatureCalicoCiliumFlannel
NetworkPolicy support✅ Full✅ Full + L7❌ None
WireGuard encryption✅✅❌
eBPF-basedOptional✅ Native❌
DNS-based policies✅✅❌

Pod-to-Pod Traffic Encryption

# Calico WireGuard encryption
kubectl patch felixconfiguration default --type='merge' \
  -p '{"spec":{"wireguardEnabled":true}}'

# Cilium WireGuard
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium \
  --set encryption.enabled=true \
  --set encryption.type=wireguard

# Without encryption: pod-to-pod traffic is plaintext
# Any node in the network can sniff inter-pod communication

Chapter 12 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create a NetworkPolicy that allows pods with label app=frontend to connect ONLY to pods with label app=backend on port 8080, and block all other egress.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: frontend-to-backend-only
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: backend
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
  - to:                          # Allow DNS resolution
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53
# Verify
kubectl exec frontend-pod -- wget -qO- http://backend-svc:8080 --timeout=2
# ✅ Works

kubectl exec frontend-pod -- wget -qO- http://other-svc:80 --timeout=2
# ❌ Blocked by NetworkPolicy
Explanation

On CKS, NetworkPolicy is heavily tested. Key pattern: always allow DNS (UDP/TCP 53) when restricting egress, otherwise pods can't resolve service names. Add DNS as a separate egress rule. Without a CNI that supports NetworkPolicy (e.g., Flannel doesn't), policies are ignored.

CKS-2
How do you create a default-deny-all policy and then selectively allow traffic?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Default deny all ingress AND egress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}          # All pods
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress

---
# Step 2: Allow specific communication
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-ingress
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: frontend
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          name: ingress-ns    # Only from ingress namespace
    ports:
    - port: 80

---
# Step 3: Allow DNS for all pods
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector: {}
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
Explanation

CKS best practice: default-deny everything, then whitelist. NetworkPolicies are additive — multiple policies are OR'd together. The default-deny policy blocks everything, then specific policies open holes. Always create a DNS egress policy or nothing can resolve service names.

CKS-3
How do you block pods from accessing the cloud metadata service (169.254.169.254)?
Show Answer
Answer
# NetworkPolicy to block metadata endpoint
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-metadata
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
        except:
        - 169.254.169.254/32    # Block metadata service
# Why this matters:
# Cloud metadata (AWS/GCP/Azure) at 169.254.169.254 exposes:
# - IAM role credentials (AWS)
# - Service account tokens (GCP)
# - VM identity tokens (Azure)

# An attacker in a pod can:
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
# → Get cloud IAM credentials → lateral movement

# Verify the policy works:
kubectl exec -n production test-pod -- \
  wget -qO- http://169.254.169.254 --timeout=2
# wget: download timed out
Explanation

The cloud metadata service (169.254.169.254) is a top attack target. SSRF vulnerabilities can expose cloud credentials. On CKS, know how to block metadata access via NetworkPolicy. AWS also supports IMDSv2 (requiring tokens) as an additional defense layer. This is a critical CKS exam topic.

CKS-4
A namespace needs to allow ingress only from pods in namespace "monitoring" (labeled purpose=monitoring). Show the NetworkPolicy.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-monitoring
  namespace: app-namespace
spec:
  podSelector: {}               # All pods in app-namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          purpose: monitoring   # Only from monitoring namespace
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 9090               # Metrics port only
# Ensure the monitoring namespace has the label
kubectl label namespace monitoring purpose=monitoring

# Note: Both namespaceSelector AND podSelector can be combined
# - from:
#   - namespaceSelector:
#       matchLabels: {purpose: monitoring}
#     podSelector:
#       matchLabels: {app: prometheus}
# ↑ This is AND (same list item)
# vs:
# - from:
#   - namespaceSelector: ...
#   - podSelector: ...
# ↑ This is OR (separate list items)
Explanation

Cross-namespace NetworkPolicy uses namespaceSelector. Critical CKS distinction: when namespaceSelector and podSelector are in the SAME from item, it's AND logic. When they are separate items, it's OR logic. This is a very common exam trap. Always label target namespaces first.

CKS-5
How do you encrypt all pod-to-pod traffic in a cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Option 1: WireGuard with Calico
kubectl patch felixconfiguration default --type='merge' \
  -p '{"spec":{"wireguardEnabled":true}}'

# Verify WireGuard is active:
kubectl get nodes -o yaml | grep -A5 wireguard

# Option 2: WireGuard with Cilium
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium \
  --set encryption.enabled=true \
  --set encryption.type=wireguard

# Option 3: IPsec with Cilium
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium \
  --set encryption.enabled=true \
  --set encryption.type=ipsec

# Option 4: Service Mesh (Istio/Linkerd) — mTLS
# All pod-to-pod traffic is TLS-encrypted via sidecars

# Why it matters:
# Without encryption, any process on a node can sniff pod traffic
# tcpdump -i any -n src 10.244.0.5 → captures plaintext
Explanation

By default, pod-to-pod traffic is unencrypted. A compromised node can sniff all traffic. WireGuard is the modern approach (fast, kernel-level). Service mesh mTLS is application-transparent. On CKS, know at least one method to encrypt pod traffic. WireGuard is simplest — one command with Calico or Cilium.

Chapter 13: Services Deep Dive

Services are the glue of Kubernetes networking. They provide stable endpoints for mortal pods, load-balance traffic, and expose your application internally or to the outside world. This chapter covers all 4 Service types, when to use each, and complete examples for anihpj.

🔗 Services ⚖️ Load Balancing 🌐 Exposure 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why Services exist — the pod IP instability problem
  • ✅ 4 Service types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName
  • ✅ Headless Services for StatefulSets
  • ✅ Multi-port Services and session affinity
  • ✅ Decision guide for choosing the right Service type
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Services on the CKAD — Imperative Creation Is King

Creating Services imperatively is 5x faster than writing YAML:

  • ClusterIP — kubectl expose deploy my-app --port=80 --target-port=8080
  • NodePort — kubectl expose deploy my-app --port=80 --type=NodePort
  • Specific port — kubectl create svc nodeport my-svc --tcp=80:8080 --node-port=30080
  • Port vs TargetPort — port = Service port (what clients connect to). targetPort = container port (where app listens)
  • DNS format — my-svc.my-namespace.svc.cluster.local — within same namespace, just use my-svc

Service Imperative Commands

Service TypeCommand
ClusterIP (default)kubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --target-port=8000
ClusterIP namedkubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --target-port=8000 --name=web-svc
NodePortkubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --type=NodePort
NodePort specifickubectl create svc nodeport web-np --tcp=80:8000 --node-port=30080
LoadBalancerkubectl expose deploy web --port=80 --type=LoadBalancer
Expose a podkubectl expose pod nginx --port=80 --name=nginx-svc
Check endpointskubectl get endpoints web-svc
Test from insidekubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- web-svc:80
DNS resolution testkubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup web-svc.default.svc.cluster.local

Service Type Comparison — Decision Guide

Pick the right type based on the question's requirements.

# ClusterIP (Internal only — default)
# → Pod-to-pod communication within cluster
# → "Expose service internally" or "other pods need to reach it"
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj               # ← MUST match pod labels
  ports:
  - port: 80                  # ← Service port (clients use this)
    targetPort: 8000           # ← Container port (app listens here)

# NodePort (External via node IP + high port)
# → "Access from outside the cluster" or "expose on port 30xxx"
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
    nodePort: 30080            # ← Optional: 30000-32767 range

# Headless (No load balancing, returns pod IPs)
# → "StatefulSet" or "direct pod addressing"
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  clusterIP: None              # ← This makes it headless
  selector:
    app: postgres

Service Traps

  • ❌ Selector doesn't match pod labels → kubectl get endpoints svc-name shows 0 endpoints = no traffic!
  • ✅ Always verify: kubectl get ep svc-name should list pod IPs
  • port vs targetPort: Mixing these up is the #1 Service mistake. port = Service, targetPort = container
  • Named ports — you can reference container port by name: targetPort: http (matches containerPort named http)
  • Multi-port Services — each port needs a unique name field

🗺️ How Services Connect Everything

Service
🏷️ Selector Matches pods by labels
📋 Endpoints Auto-populated pod IP list
🌐 DNS svc.ns.svc.cluster.local
🔀 kube-proxy iptables/IPVS load balancing
4 Service Types
ClusterIP Default Type
30000-32767 NodePort Range
#1 Bug Selector Mismatch

🔬 Service Port Mapping — Most Confused Concept

🚪 port
The port the Service listens on — what other pods use to reach this service
🎯 targetPort
The port on the container — where the app is actually running (e.g. 8000 for Django)
🌍 nodePort
External port on every node — only used with NodePort/LoadBalancer types (30000-32767)

13.1 Why Services Exist — The Problem

Pods are mortal. They get created, crash, are replaced by Deployments, and each time they get a new IP address. You can't point your frontend to a pod IP — it will break on the next restart.

💀 The Problem: Pod IPs Are Unstable

  ❌ WITHOUT Services:                       ✅ WITH Services:

  ┌───────┐  hardcoded IP   ┌───────┐       ┌───────┐  stable name   ┌──────────┐
  │ front │──10.0.2.9──────▶│ db    │       │ front │──"db-svc"────▶│ Service  │
  │  end  │                 │Pod v1 │       │  end  │               │ db-svc   │
  └───────┘                 └───┬───┘       └───────┘               └───┬──┬───┘
                                │ 💥 crash!                              │  │
                                ▼                               routes to│  │
  ┌───────┐  10.0.2.9?     ┌───────┐        any healthy pod     │  │
  │ front │──❌ BROKEN!────▶│ db    │                      ┌─────┘  └─────┐
  │  end  │  OLD IP!       │Pod v2 │                      ▼              ▼
  └───────┘                │10.0.3.2│                 ┌───────┐     ┌───────┐
                           └───────┘                  │ db    │     │ db    │
                                                      │Pod v1 │     │Pod v2 │
                                                      └───────┘     └───────┘
❌ Without Services
  • Pod IPs change on every restart
  • No load balancing between replicas
  • No health checking of backends
  • Apps hardcode IPs → instant breakage
✅ With Services
  • Stable DNS name + virtual IP
  • Automatic load balancing across pods
  • Only routes to healthy (ready) pods
  • Apps use names → survive any restart
📝 How Services Find Pods

Services use label selectors to discover pods. Any pod whose labels match the Service's spec.selector becomes a backend endpoint. When pods are created or destroyed, the endpoint list updates automatically.

🔄 Service Label Selector — How Matching Works

  Service YAML:                         Pod YAML:
  ───────────                           ────────
  spec:                                 metadata:
    selector:                             labels:
      app: anihpj  ◄──── MUST MATCH ────► app: anihpj   ✅ Endpoint!
      role: backend ◄─── MUST MATCH ────► role: backend  ✅

  If ALL selector labels match → pod becomes an endpoint
  If ANY label is missing/different → pod is EXCLUDED

  ❌ Common mistake:
     Service selector:  app: anihpj         (lowercase)
     Pod label:         app: Anihpj         (capital A)
     Result: 0 endpoints → Service returns nothing!

13.2 ClusterIP Service (Default)

A ClusterIP Service gets a stable virtual IP accessible only from inside the cluster. This is the default type and the most common — use it for internal communication between microservices.

🔒 ClusterIP — Internal Only

  🔒 INTERNAL CLUSTER ONLY — not accessible from outside

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                   │
  │                                                       │
  │    📦 frontend pod                                    │
  │    curl http://anihpj-svc:8000/api/jobs/             │
  │         │                                             │
  │         ▼                                             │
  │    ┌──────────────────────┐                           │
  │    │  anihpj-svc          │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50  │
  │    │  (ClusterIP Service) │  Port: 8000              │
  │    └────┬────────────┬────┘                           │
  │         │            │     label selector:            │
  │         ▼            ▼     app: anihpj               │
  │    ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                           │
  │    │ anihpj  │  │ anihpj  │  ← endpoint pods         │
  │    │ pod-abc │  │ pod-xyz │                           │
  │    │ :8000   │  │ :8000   │                           │
  │    └─────────┘  └─────────┘                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
    ❌ Cannot reach 10.96.78.50 from your laptop browser
k8s/clusterip-service.yaml — anihpj internal Service 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  type: ClusterIP                         # Default — can omit this line
  selector:
    app: anihpj                           # Must match pod labels!
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000                          # Service listens on this port
      targetPort: 8000                    # Forward to container port
Verify the Service is working 
# Create and check the Service
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/clusterip-service.yaml
$ kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
NAME          TYPE        CLUSTER-IP     EXTERNAL-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
anihpj-svc    ClusterIP   10.96.78.50    <none>         8000/TCP   5s

# Verify endpoints (must show pod IPs!)
$ kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
NAME          ENDPOINTS                       AGE
anihpj-svc    10.0.1.5:8000,10.0.1.6:8000    5s

# Test from inside the cluster
$ kubectl exec -it debug-pod -- curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/api/jobs/
{"jobs": [...]}
💡 Using Different port and targetPort

The Service port and targetPort don't have to match. A common pattern is to expose port 80 on the Service while the container listens on 8000:

  ports:
    - port: 80          # Clients use: curl anihpj-svc:80
      targetPort: 8000  # Forwards to container port 8000

This lets internal consumers use the standard port 80 while Django runs on its default 8000.

13.3 NodePort Service

A NodePort Service opens a specific port (30000-32767) on every node in the cluster. External traffic hitting any node's IP on that port gets routed to the Service, then to a healthy pod.

🚪 NodePort — External Access via Node IP

  YOUR BROWSER (external)
  http://192.168.49.2:30080/jobs/
         │
         ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                     │
  │                                                         │
  │   Node 1 (192.168.49.2)        Node 2 (192.168.49.3)  │
  │   Port 30080 OPEN ✅            Port 30080 OPEN ✅      │
  │         │                              │                │
  │         └──────────┬───────────────────┘                │
  │                    ▼                                     │
  │           ┌──────────────────┐                          │
  │           │  anihpj-svc      │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50 │
  │           │  NodePort: 30080 │                          │
  │           └───┬─────────┬────┘                          │
  │               │         │                               │
  │               ▼         ▼                               │
  │          ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                          │
  │          │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│                          │
  │          │ :8000  │ │ :8000  │                          │
  │          └────────┘ └────────┘                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/nodeport-service.yaml — Access anihpj from outside 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-nodeport
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000                          # Service port (internal)
      targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
      nodePort: 30080                     # External port (30000-32767)
Access anihpj from Minikube 
# Get the Minikube node IP
$ minikube ip
192.168.49.2

# Access anihpj in your browser
$ curl http://192.168.49.2:30080/api/jobs/

# Or use Minikube's built-in shortcut
$ minikube service anihpj-nodeport -n anihpj-app --url
http://192.168.49.2:30080
⚠️ NodePort Limitations

NodePort is great for development but not ideal for production: limited port range (30000-32767), no HTTPS, no domain names, and users must know the node IP. Use LoadBalancer or Ingress for production.

🔢 NodePort Three-Port Mapping

  External user → NodeIP:nodePort → Service:port → Pod:targetPort

  curl http://192.168.49.2:30080
                      │
          ┌───────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┐
          │                                                  │
          │   nodePort: 30080    port: 8000    targetPort: 8000
          │   ─────────────    ───────────    ───────────────
          │   Opens on EVERY   Service's     Container's
          │   cluster node     internal port  listening port
          │                                                  │
          │   Accessible from  Used by pods   Where app code │
          │   OUTSIDE cluster  inside cluster actually runs  │
          └──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  💡 These don't all have to be the same number!
     nodePort: 30080 → port: 80 → targetPort: 8000

13.4 LoadBalancer Service

A LoadBalancer Service provisions an external load balancer from your cloud provider (AWS ELB, GCP LB, Azure LB). It gets a public IP that routes traffic to your pods — the production way to expose services.

☁️ LoadBalancer — Cloud-Provisioned Public IP

  🌍 INTERNET
  Users browse https://anihpj.example.com
         │
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────┐
  │  Cloud Load        │ Public IP: 52.14.200.130
  │  Balancer          │ (AWS ELB / GCP LB / Azure LB)
  │  (auto-provisioned)│
  └────────┬───────────┘
           │
  ┌────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   Kubernetes Cluster                  │
  │                                                      │
  │    ┌───────────────────────┐                         │
  │    │  anihpj-lb            │  ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50│
  │    │  (LoadBalancer)       │  External: 52.14.200.130│
  │    └───┬──────────────┬────┘                         │
  │        │              │                              │
  │        ▼              ▼                              │
  │   ┌────────┐    ┌────────┐    ┌────────┐            │
  │   │anihpj-1│    │anihpj-2│    │anihpj-3│            │
  │   │ :8000  │    │ :8000  │    │ :8000  │            │
  │   └────────┘    └────────┘    └────────┘            │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/loadbalancer-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-lb
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http
      protocol: TCP
      port: 80                            # Public-facing port
      targetPort: 8000                    # Container port
Check external IP assignment 
# Wait for external IP (may take 1-2 minutes on cloud)
$ kubectl get svc anihpj-lb -n anihpj-app -w
NAME        TYPE           CLUSTER-IP    EXTERNAL-IP      PORT(S)        AGE
anihpj-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.78.50   <pending>          80:31234/TCP   10s
anihpj-lb   LoadBalancer   10.96.78.50   52.14.200.130    80:31234/TCP   90s

# On Minikube (no cloud LB), use tunnel:
$ minikube tunnel
# Now EXTERNAL-IP will be assigned
📝 Cost Warning

Each LoadBalancer Service provisions a separate cloud load balancer (~$18-25/month each on AWS/GCP/Azure). If you have 5 services, that's 5 LBs = $100+/month. Use Ingress (Chapter 14) to share one LB across multiple services.

13.5 ExternalName Service

An ExternalName Service creates a DNS CNAME alias for an external service. No proxying, no port mapping — it simply resolves the service name to an external DNS name. Use it to abstract external dependencies.

🔗 ExternalName — DNS Alias to External Service

  📦 anihpj pod
  │
  │  conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  # Uses K8s service name
  │         │
  │         ▼
  │  ┌───────────────────────┐
  │  │  Service: database    │   type: ExternalName
  │  │  (ExternalName)       │   externalName: mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
  │  └───────────┬───────────┘
  │              │ CNAME redirect (DNS level)
  │              ▼
  │  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐
  │  │  mydb.rds.amazonaws.com          │  ← External managed database
  │  │  (AWS RDS / Azure DB / Cloud SQL) │     outside the cluster
  │  └───────────────────────────────────┘
  
  ✅ If you migrate to in-cluster DB later, just change the Service type
     to ClusterIP — your app code doesn't change at all!
k8s/externalname-service.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: database
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.us-east-1.rds.amazonaws.com   # No selector needed!

♻️ ExternalName Migration Scenario

  Phase 1: Using managed cloud DB
  ════════════════════════════════
  Service "database" (ExternalName) → mydb.rds.amazonaws.com
  App code: conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  ← doesn't change!

  Phase 2: Migrate to in-cluster PostgreSQL
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
  Change Service "database" from ExternalName → ClusterIP
  Add selector: app: postgres
  App code: conn = psycopg2.connect(host="database")  ← STILL the same!

  ✅ Zero code changes during database migration!

13.6 Headless Services (clusterIP: None)

A Headless Service has no ClusterIP. Instead of load-balancing, DNS returns the individual pod IPs directly. Essential for StatefulSets where each pod needs to be addressed individually (e.g., database replicas).

🎯 Regular vs Headless DNS Resolution

  REGULAR Service (ClusterIP):           HEADLESS Service (clusterIP: None):

  nslookup postgres-svc                   nslookup postgres-headless
  → 10.96.45.120  (1 ClusterIP)          → 10.0.1.10  (pod-0 IP)
                                          → 10.0.2.11  (pod-1 IP)
                                          → 10.0.2.12  (pod-2 IP)

  ┌────────────────┐                      ┌────────────────┐
  │  postgres-svc  │ 10.96.45.120        │ postgres-      │ No ClusterIP!
  │  (ClusterIP)   │                      │ headless       │
  └───┬──────┬─────┘                      └───┬──────┬─────┘
      │      │  random                        │      │  direct to each
      ▼      ▼                                ▼      ▼
  ┌──────┐┌──────┐                       ┌──────┐┌──────┐
  │pg-0  ││pg-1  │                       │pg-0  ││pg-1  │
  └──────┘└──────┘                       └──────┘└──────┘
                                         postgres-0.postgres-headless
                                         postgres-1.postgres-headless
                                         ↑ Each pod addressable by name!
k8s/headless-service.yaml — For StatefulSet pods 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  clusterIP: None                         # ← This makes it headless!
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
💡 When to Use Headless

Use Headless Services with StatefulSets — where each pod has a unique identity (postgres-0, postgres-1). This enables direct pod-to-pod communication for replication: a replica can find the primary at postgres-0.postgres-headless.

13.7 Multi-Port Services

A single Service can expose multiple ports. Useful when your application listens on more than one port — for example, HTTP + metrics, or HTTP + gRPC.

k8s/multi-port-service.yaml — HTTP + Metrics 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-svc
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  ports:
    - name: http                          # Name required for multi-port!
      protocol: TCP
      port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
    - name: metrics                       # Prometheus scrape endpoint
      protocol: TCP
      port: 9090
      targetPort: 9090
⚠️ Port Names Are Required

When a Service has multiple ports, each port must have a name field. This is required so Kubernetes (and Ingress) can distinguish between them. Single-port Services don't need names but it's good practice to always add one.

13.8 Session Affinity

By default, Services use random/round-robin load balancing. With session affinity, requests from the same client IP always go to the same pod — useful for sticky sessions.

Setting Behavior Use Case
None (default) Random pod per request (round-robin) Stateless apps (REST APIs, microservices)
ClientIP Same client IP → same pod (10,800s timeout) Legacy apps with server-side sessions
Service with session affinity 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sticky
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP               # Sticky sessions
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 3600                # 1 hour sticky window
  ports:
    - port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
💡 Django Best Practice

For anihpj, prefer stateless design (session affinity: None). Store sessions in Redis or the database instead of in-memory. This way any pod can handle any request and you can scale freely without sticky sessions.

13.9 Service Type Decision Guide

Choosing the right Service type is critical. Use this decision tree and comparison table to make the right choice for each workload:

🗺️ Service Type Decision Tree

  ┌───────────────────────────────┐
  │  Where does traffic           │
  │  come from?                   │
  └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                 │
       ┌─────────┴──────────┐
       ▼                    ▼
   INTERNAL              EXTERNAL
   (cluster only)        (outside world)
       │                    │
  ┌────┴────────┐    ┌──────┴───────────────┐
  │ Need direct │    │                      │
  │ pod IPs?    │    │ Is it for            │
  │             │    │ development or       │
  │ Yes → HEADLESS   │ production?          │
  │ No  → ClusterIP  │                      │
  └─────────────┘    ┌──────┴────────┐
                     ▼               ▼
                Development      Production
                     │               │
               ┌─────┴─────┐   ┌────┴──────────┐
               │  NodePort  │   │ Need HTTP     │
               │  :30000+   │   │ routing?      │
               └────────────┘   │               │
                           ┌───┴───┐     ┌─────┴──────┐
                           │  No   │     │   Yes      │
                           │       │     │            │
                       LoadBalancer │     │  Ingress   │
                       (1 per svc) │     │  (Ch 14)   │
                       └───────────┘     └────────────┘

  Aliasing an external DNS?  →  ExternalName

📊 Complete Service Type Comparison

Feature ClusterIP NodePort LoadBalancer ExternalName Headless
Accessible from Inside cluster Node IP:Port Public IP DNS CNAME Inside cluster
Load balancing ✅ Yes ✅ Yes ✅ Cloud LB ❌ No ❌ No (direct)
Stable IP ✅ ClusterIP ✅ ClusterIP + NodePort ✅ External IP ❌ No IP ❌ No IP
Cloud cost Free Free 💰 ~$18/mo each Free Free
Use case Internal comms Dev/testing Production External alias StatefulSets

🛠️ Service kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Services kubectl get svc -n anihpj-app
Service details kubectl describe svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
Check endpoints kubectl get endpoints anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app
Expose a Deployment kubectl expose deploy anihpj --port=8000 --type=ClusterIP
Port-forward to local kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj-svc 8000:8000
Delete a Service kubectl delete svc anihpj-svc -n anihpj-app

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Services Speed Run

⏱️ Target: 5 minutes
  1. Create a Deployment named backend with image nginx and 2 replicas
  2. Expose it as a ClusterIP service named backend-svc on port 80
  3. Expose it again as a NodePort service named backend-np on port 80, nodePort 30080
  4. Verify Service endpoints match running pod IPs
  5. From a temporary pod, test connectivity: curl backend-svc:80
💡 Hint: kubectl expose, kubectl create svc nodeport, kubectl get endpoints
View Solution 
# 1. Create deployment
kubectl create deployment backend --image=nginx --replicas=2

# 2. ClusterIP service
kubectl expose deployment backend --name=backend-svc \
  --port=80 --target-port=80

# 3. NodePort service
kubectl create svc nodeport backend-np --tcp=80:80 \
  --node-port=30080
# Note: you may need to edit selector to match deployment labels

# 4. Verify endpoints
kubectl get endpoints backend-svc
kubectl get pods -o wide  # compare IPs

# 5. Test from temp pod
kubectl run test --image=busybox --rm -it --restart=Never \
  -- wget -qO- backend-svc:80

Chapter 13 — Practice Questions

Q1
List the four types of Kubernetes Services. When would you choose each type?
Show Answer
Answer
TypeScopeUse Case
ClusterIPInternal onlyInter-service communication (default)
NodePortExternal via node IP:portDevelopment, testing, demos
LoadBalancerExternal via cloud LBProduction cloud deployments
ExternalNameDNS aliasExternal service integration (CNAME)
Explanation

Each type builds on the previous: LoadBalancer creates a NodePort which creates a ClusterIP. ClusterIP is the default — if you don't specify type:, you get ClusterIP. ExternalName has no selector and no proxy — it just returns a CNAME record. On the CKAD, ClusterIP and NodePort are most commonly tested.

Q2
Create a ClusterIP Service imperatively for a deployment named web-app running on port 8080.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: expose deployment
kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80 --target-port=8080 --name=web-svc

# Method 2: create service directly
kubectl create service clusterip web-svc --tcp=80:8080

# Verify
kubectl get svc web-svc
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
Explanation

kubectl expose is the CKAD-preferred way — it automatically copies the selector from the deployment. kubectl create service sets the selector to app: web-svc (matching the service name, NOT the deployment). If your deployment has a different label, create service won't match and endpoints will be empty. Always verify with kubectl get endpoints.

Q3
What's the difference between port, targetPort, and nodePort in a Service definition? Write a NodePort service YAML demonstrating all three.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: my-nodeport-svc
spec:
  type: NodePort
  selector:
    app: web
  ports:
  - port: 80           # Service listens on (ClusterIP:80)
    targetPort: 3000    # Container listens on (Pod IP:3000)
    nodePort: 30080     # External access (NodeIP:30080)
  External          Node              Service           Pod
  Client   ──→  NodeIP:30080  ──→  ClusterIP:80  ──→  PodIP:3000
             (nodePort)          (port)           (targetPort)
Explanation

port = what other pods in the cluster use. targetPort = what the container actually listens on (defaults to port if omitted). nodePort = external access port on every node (range: 30000-32767, auto-assigned if omitted). This three-port distinction is a classic CKAD question and a common source of confusion.

Q4
You create a Service but kubectl get endpoints my-svc shows <none>. What's wrong and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check Service selector
kubectl describe svc my-svc | grep Selector
# Output: app=web

# Step 2: Check pod labels
kubectl get pods --show-labels
# Output: app=webapp  ← MISMATCH!

# Step 3: Fix — either relabel pods or update service selector
kubectl label pod my-pod app=web --overwrite

# OR edit the service
kubectl edit svc my-svc
# Change selector to match actual pod labels
Explanation

Empty endpoints = the Service selector doesn't match any running pod labels. Other causes: (1) pods aren't in Running state, (2) pods are in a different namespace, (3) selector uses matchLabels incorrectly. The debugging sequence: describe svc → check selector → get pods --show-labels → compare. This is the #1 Service troubleshooting step on the CKAD.

Q5
Create a Service that exposes multiple ports — HTTP on 80→8080 and HTTPS on 443→8443 for pods with label app: api-gateway.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: api-gateway-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: api-gateway
  ports:
  - name: http              # name is REQUIRED for multi-port
    port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
    protocol: TCP
  - name: https             # name is REQUIRED for multi-port
    port: 443
    targetPort: 8443
    protocol: TCP
Explanation

Key rule: When a Service exposes multiple ports, each port MUST have a name field. Without names, the API server rejects it. Port names must be unique within the Service and follow DNS label format (lowercase, alphanumeric, hyphens). This is tested on the CKAD in combination with Ingress questions.

Q6
What is an ExternalName Service? Give a practical example.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-db
  namespace: production
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: mydb.example.com   # External DNS name
# Pods can now access the external database via:
curl http://external-db:5432

# DNS resolves: external-db → CNAME → mydb.example.com
Explanation

ExternalName has NO selector, NO ClusterIP, NO proxying. It simply creates a CNAME DNS record mapping the service name to an external hostname. Use cases: (1) pointing to managed cloud databases, (2) gradual migration from external to in-cluster services, (3) abstracting external endpoints. If you later migrate the DB into the cluster, just change the Service type to ClusterIP — no code changes needed.

Q7
Write a Service with sessionAffinity: ClientIP. What does this do and when would you use it?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: sticky-svc
spec:
  selector:
    app: web
  sessionAffinity: ClientIP
  sessionAffinityConfig:
    clientIP:
      timeoutSeconds: 10800    # 3 hours (default)
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8080
Explanation

sessionAffinity: ClientIP routes all requests from the same client IP to the same backend pod — "sticky sessions." Default is None (round-robin). Use when applications store session state in memory (not recommended but common with legacy apps). The CKAD might ask about this but it's less common than selector-based questions.

Q8
How do you expose a pod on a specific NodePort (30100) using kubectl imperatively?
Show Answer
Answer
# Can't set nodePort imperatively — use dry-run + edit
kubectl expose deployment web-app \
  --type=NodePort \
  --port=80 \
  --target-port=8080 \
  --name=web-np \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > svc.yaml

# Edit svc.yaml to add: nodePort: 30100
# Then apply:
kubectl apply -f svc.yaml
Explanation

CKAD trick: kubectl expose doesn't support --node-port flag. You must generate YAML with --dry-run=client -o yaml, add the nodePort field manually, then kubectl apply. This is a common exam pattern: generate → edit → apply. Time-saver: kubectl create service nodeport accepts --node-port but selector matching is limited.

Q9
What is the Endpoints object? How does Kubernetes automatically manage it for a Service?
Show Answer
Answer
# View endpoints for a service
kubectl get endpoints web-svc
# NAME      ENDPOINTS                     AGE
# web-svc   10.244.0.5:8080,10.244.1.3:8080  5m

# Detailed view
kubectl describe endpoints web-svc
  Service (web-svc)          Endpoints Controller
  selector: app=web    ──→   Watches pods with app=web
                              │
                              ├─ Pod running → add IP:port to Endpoints
                              ├─ Pod deleted → remove from Endpoints
                              └─ Pod not Ready → remove from Endpoints
Explanation

The Endpoints controller automatically creates and updates an Endpoints object with the same name as the Service. It watches pods matching the Service's selector and adds/removes their IPs. When a pod fails its readiness probe, it's removed from Endpoints (traffic is redirected). For CKAD troubleshooting, kubectl get endpoints is your key debugging command.

Q10
Create a Service without a selector that points to an external IP 203.0.113.50 on port 3306, and create the matching Endpoints object manually.
Show Answer
Answer
# Service (no selector)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: external-mysql
spec:
  ports:
  - port: 3306
    targetPort: 3306
---
# Manual Endpoints (must match service name!)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Endpoints
metadata:
  name: external-mysql    # ← Same name as Service
subsets:
- addresses:
  - ip: 203.0.113.50
  ports:
  - port: 3306
Explanation

When a Service has no selector, Kubernetes does NOT auto-create Endpoints. You must create the Endpoints object manually with the exact same name as the Service. This lets you point to external resources (databases, APIs outside the cluster) while using standard Kubernetes DNS within the cluster. Less common on CKAD than ExternalName but tests deep understanding.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Service Exposure Risks

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Service Types — Security Risk Levels

Service TypeExposureRiskCKS Recommendation
ClusterIPInternal only🟢 Low✅ Default — use for internal services
NodePortEvery node IP:port🟡 Medium⚠️ Avoid — opens port on all nodes
LoadBalancerExternal IP🔴 High⚠️ Use with caution, restrict source IPs
ExternalNameDNS CNAME🟡 Medium⚠️ Can be used for SSRF

Restrict LoadBalancer Source IPs

# Limit who can access a LoadBalancer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: secure-lb
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  loadBalancerSourceRanges:
  - "10.0.0.0/8"           # Only internal IPs
  - "203.0.113.0/24"       # Specific office IP range

Chapter 13 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Why should NodePort services be avoided in production? What alternative is more secure?
Show Answer
Answer
# NodePort risks:
# 1. Opens a port (30000-32767) on EVERY node
# 2. No TLS termination
# 3. Cannot restrict source IPs (without external firewall)
# 4. Bypasses Ingress security controls
# 5. Port scanning reveals available services

# Secure alternatives:
# 1. ClusterIP + Ingress Controller (with TLS)
# 2. LoadBalancer with loadBalancerSourceRanges
# 3. ClusterIP + port-forward (for debugging)

# Block NodePorts via ResourceQuota:
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: no-nodeports
  namespace: production
spec:
  hard:
    services.nodeports: "0"    # Block all NodePorts
Explanation

NodePort exposes services on every node's IP, creating a wide attack surface. On CKS, use ClusterIP + Ingress for external access (centralizes TLS, authentication). Block NodePorts with ResourceQuota. If NodePort is required, use firewall rules to restrict access to specific IPs.

CKS-2
How do you protect the Kubernetes Dashboard service from unauthorized access?
Show Answer
Answer
# Security measures for K8s Dashboard:

# 1. NEVER expose via NodePort or LoadBalancer
# Use ClusterIP + kubectl proxy instead:
kubectl proxy --port=8001
# Access: http://localhost:8001/api/v1/namespaces/kubernetes-dashboard/services/https:kubernetes-dashboard:/proxy/

# 2. Use token-based authentication (not skip-login!)
# Create a read-only ServiceAccount for dashboard access:
kubectl create sa dashboard-viewer -n kubernetes-dashboard
kubectl create clusterrolebinding dashboard-viewer \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --serviceaccount=kubernetes-dashboard:dashboard-viewer

# 3. NetworkPolicy — only allow from specific pods
# 4. Never give dashboard cluster-admin access!

# Find if dashboard is insecurely exposed:
kubectl get svc -n kubernetes-dashboard
# If type=NodePort or LoadBalancer → FIX immediately
Explanation

The Kubernetes Dashboard is a common attack target. CKS best practices: use ClusterIP only, access via kubectl proxy, use token auth (never --skip-login), grant minimal RBAC permissions. On CKS, you may be asked to secure an insecurely deployed dashboard.

CKS-3
How do you prevent services from exposing the Kubernetes API server metadata?
Show Answer
Answer
# Risk: ExternalName services can point to internal endpoints
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: exploit-svc
spec:
  type: ExternalName
  externalName: kubernetes.default.svc  # Points to API server!
# An attacker can use this for SSRF attacks

# Prevention:
# 1. Restrict who can create ExternalName services via RBAC
# 2. Use NetworkPolicy to block egress to API server
# 3. Disable automountServiceAccountToken
# 4. OPA policy to deny ExternalName services:

# Rego policy:
# deny[msg] {
#   input.kind == "Service"
#   input.spec.type == "ExternalName"
#   msg := "ExternalName services are not allowed"
# }
Explanation

ExternalName services create CNAME DNS records that can point to internal services, enabling SSRF attacks. An attacker could redirect traffic to the API server or cloud metadata. On CKS, know this attack vector and how to restrict ExternalName services via RBAC or admission policies.

CKS-4
How do you find all externally-exposed services in a cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find all NodePort services
kubectl get svc -A --field-selector spec.type=NodePort

# Find all LoadBalancer services
kubectl get svc -A --field-selector spec.type=LoadBalancer

# Find all with external IPs
kubectl get svc -A -o wide | grep -v "ClusterIP\|"

# Comprehensive audit:
kubectl get svc -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.spec.type != "ClusterIP") |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name): \(.spec.type) " +
  if .spec.type == "NodePort" then
    (.spec.ports[]? | "port:\(.nodePort)") // ""
  elif .spec.type == "LoadBalancer" then
    (.status.loadBalancer.ingress[]?.ip // "pending")
  else "" end
'

# Check if any have unrestricted access:
kubectl get svc -A -o json | jq '.items[] |
  select(.spec.type == "LoadBalancer" and
         (.spec.loadBalancerSourceRanges == null)) |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name) has NO source IP restriction"'
Explanation

On CKS, you need to audit the cluster for unnecessary external exposure. Any NodePort or LoadBalancer without source IP restrictions is a risk. Use --field-selector spec.type=NodePort for quick filtering. LoadBalancers without loadBalancerSourceRanges are accessible from anywhere.

CKS-5
What is the risk of service.spec.externalIPs and how do you prevent its misuse?
Show Answer
Answer
# externalIPs allows a service to claim ANY IP address
# An attacker with service create permission can:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mitm-service
spec:
  externalIPs:
  - 10.0.0.1              # Hijack traffic to this IP!
  ports:
  - port: 443

# This redirects all traffic to 10.0.0.1:443 → attacker's pod!
# Known as CVE-2020-8554 (Man-in-the-Middle via externalIPs)

# Prevention:
# 1. Use admission controller to deny externalIPs
# 2. OPA policy:
# deny[msg] {
#   input.kind == "Service"
#   input.spec.externalIPs
#   msg := "externalIPs not allowed"
# }

# 3. Enable DenyServiceExternalIPs admission plugin:
--enable-admission-plugins=...,DenyServiceExternalIPs
Explanation

externalIPs is dangerous: it lets a Service claim external IP addresses, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks (CVE-2020-8554). On CKS, enable the DenyServiceExternalIPs admission plugin or use OPA to block. Most clusters don't need externalIPs.

Chapter 14: Ingress — HTTP/HTTPS Smart Routing

Ingress is a Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) load balancer that provides path-based routing, host-based routing, and TLS termination through a single entry point. Instead of provisioning one LoadBalancer per service, Ingress lets you share one load balancer across all your services.

🚀 Ingress 🔀 Routing 🔒 TLS/SSL 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What Ingress is and why it saves money over LoadBalancers
  • ✅ Ingress Controllers — the required first step
  • ✅ Path-based, host-based, and combined routing rules
  • ✅ TLS/SSL termination and automatic certificates
  • ✅ Common Ingress annotations for fine-tuning
  • ✅ Complete Ingress setup for the anihpj project
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Services & Networking 20%
Ingress on the CKAD — Know the YAML Structure

Ingress can't be created imperatively — you MUST know the YAML. Memorize this skeleton:

  • apiVersion — networking.k8s.io/v1
  • pathType — Prefix (most common on exam) or Exact
  • IngressClassName — spec.ingressClassName: nginx (replaces old annotation)
  • backend structure — service.name + service.port.number (NOT the old serviceName/servicePort)
  • Quick reference — kubectl explain ingress.spec.rules.http.paths

Ingress YAML — Complete Exam Template

Ingress cannot be created imperatively. You MUST type this YAML from memory.

# Single host, path-based routing
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx         # ← Required (replaces old annotation)
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com      # ← Optional: omit for IP-based access
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix           # ← Prefix or Exact
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-svc       # ← Backend Service name
            port:
              number: 80           # ← Service port (NOT container port)
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080

# Multi-host routing
spec:
  rules:
  - host: app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service: {name: frontend-svc, port: {number: 80}}
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service: {name: api-svc, port: {number: 8080}}

# TLS termination
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - anihpj.example.com
    secretName: anihpj-tls         # ← Secret of type kubernetes.io/tls
  rules:
  - host: anihpj.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service: {name: anihpj-svc, port: {number: 80}}

Ingress Traps

  • ❌ Using old API format: serviceName: x / servicePort: 80 → deprecated! Use service.name + service.port.number
  • ❌ Missing ingressClassName → Ingress won't be picked up by any controller
  • pathType is required since networking.k8s.io/v1 — choose Prefix or Exact
  • port.number is the Service port, NOT the container port
  • TLS secret must be type kubernetes.io/tls with keys tls.crt and tls.key
  • Default backend — add spec.defaultBackend for unmatched paths (optional but exam may ask)

Ingress Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get ingress -n production → Check HOST, ADDRESS, and PORTS columns
  • kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n production → Check Rules and Backend
  • kubectl get svc -n production → Verify backend Services exist
  • kubectl get endpoints [svc-name] → Ensure Services have pod endpoints
  • curl http://anihpj.example.com/ → Test from outside (or use port-forward)
  • kubectl get ingressclass → Verify IngressClass exists
🌐
Browser app.example.com/api
→
🔒
TLS Termination HTTPS → HTTP
→
🚦
Ingress Rules Host + path matching
→
🔀
Backend Service ClusterIP Service
→
📦
Pod Handles request
🔴 Without Ingress
  • Each service needs its own LoadBalancer ($$$)
  • No host-based or path-based routing
  • No TLS termination at cluster edge
  • No URL rewriting or redirects
  • 10 services = 10 Load Balancers = 10× cost
🟢 With Ingress
  • Single LoadBalancer for all services
  • Route by host: api.example.com → api-svc
  • Route by path: /api → api-svc, /web → web-svc
  • TLS termination with cert-manager
  • 10 services = 1 Load Balancer = 1× cost

📊 Ingress Controller Options

🟢 NGINX Most popular, exam default
🟣 Traefik Auto-discovery, Let's Encrypt
🔵 HAProxy High performance L7
🟡 Istio Gateway Service mesh integration

14.1 What is Ingress?

An Ingress is a Kubernetes API object that manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to services. It acts as a smart reverse proxy — routing requests based on URL paths and hostnames to the correct backend service.

💰 Without Ingress vs With Ingress

  ❌ WITHOUT Ingress — 1 LoadBalancer per Service ($$$):

  ┌───────┐   ┌───────┐   ┌───────┐
  │ LB 1  │   │ LB 2  │   │ LB 3  │    3 × $18/mo = $54/mo 😱
  │$18/mo │   │$18/mo │   │$18/mo │
  └───┬───┘   └───┬───┘   └───┬───┘
      │           │           │
  ┌───┴──┐   ┌───┴──┐   ┌───┴──┐
  │ API  │   │Admin │   │ Docs │
  └──────┘   └──────┘   └──────┘


  ✅ WITH Ingress — 1 shared entry point ($):

  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         1 Ingress Controller       │   1 × $18/mo = $18/mo 🎉
  │                                    │
  │  /api/*    → anihpj-svc:8000      │
  │  /admin/*  → admin-svc:8000       │
  │  /docs/*   → docs-svc:3000        │
  └──┬──────────────┬─────────────┬────┘
     │              │             │
  ┌──┴──┐      ┌───┴──┐     ┌───┴──┐
  │ API │      │Admin │     │ Docs │
  └─────┘      └──────┘     └──────┘

  SAVINGS: $36/month (66% cheaper!) 💪
🔧 What Ingress Provides
  • Path-based routing (/api, /admin)
  • Host-based routing (virtual hosts)
  • TLS/SSL termination (HTTPS)
  • Load balancing across pods
  • URL rewriting and redirects
⚡ Two Parts Required
  • Ingress Resource — YAML rules (routing config)
  • Ingress Controller — the actual proxy (NGINX, Traefik)
  • Without a controller, Ingress resources do nothing!
  • Controller runs as pods in the cluster

14.2 Ingress vs LoadBalancer Service

Both expose services externally, but they operate at different layers and serve different purposes:

Feature LoadBalancer Service Ingress
OSI Layer L4 (TCP/UDP) L7 (HTTP/HTTPS)
Cost 💰 1 cloud LB per service 💵 1 LB shared across all services
Path routing ❌ No (/api, /admin) ✅ Yes
Host routing ❌ No (virtual hosts) ✅ Yes
TLS termination ❌ No (pass-through) ✅ Yes (HTTPS at the edge)
Non-HTTP protocols ✅ TCP, UDP, gRPC ⚠️ HTTP/HTTPS only (usually)
URL rewriting ❌ No ✅ Yes (annotations)
Best for Single service, non-HTTP Multiple HTTP services, web apps
💡 Rule of Thumb

For web applications like anihpj, always use Ingress. Use LoadBalancer only for non-HTTP protocols (databases, message queues, gRPC services) that Ingress can't handle.

14.3 Ingress Controllers — Required First Step

An Ingress resource alone does nothing — you need an Ingress Controller running in the cluster to process the rules. Think of Ingress resources as config files and the controller as the web server that reads them.

⚙️ How the Ingress Controller Works

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   INGRESS CONTROLLER FLOW                     │
  │                                                               │
  │  1. You create Ingress YAML                                  │
  │     (rules: /api → svc-a, /admin → svc-b)                   │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  2. K8s API stores Ingress resource                          │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  3. Ingress Controller watches for changes                   │
  │     (nginx-ingress-controller pod)                           │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  4. Controller generates NGINX config from rules             │
  │     /api  → proxy_pass http://svc-a-upstream;               │
  │     /admin → proxy_pass http://svc-b-upstream;              │
  │                    │                                          │
  │                    ▼                                          │
  │  5. NGINX reloads config, starts routing traffic             │
  │                                                               │
  │  External request → Controller pod → matched Service → Pod   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

14.3a NGINX Ingress Controller

The most popular Ingress controller. Battle-tested, widely documented, and works everywhere.

Install NGINX Ingress on Minikube 
# Option 1: Minikube addon (easiest)
$ minikube addons enable ingress

# Verify the controller is running
$ kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
NAME                                        READY   STATUS
ingress-nginx-controller-5d88495688-abc12   1/1     Running

# Option 2: Helm install (any cluster)
$ helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx
$ helm install ingress-nginx ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx

14.3b Traefik

Auto-discovery, built-in Let's Encrypt support, and a web dashboard. Default in k3s. Great for automatic HTTPS.

Install Traefik via Helm 
$ helm repo add traefik https://traefik.github.io/charts
$ helm install traefik traefik/traefik

# Traefik auto-discovers Ingress resources
# and can obtain Let's Encrypt certs automatically

14.3c HAProxy Ingress

High-performance option used by enterprises needing advanced TCP/HTTP features, rate limiting, and WAF capabilities.

Install HAProxy Ingress via Helm 
$ helm repo add haproxy-ingress https://haproxy-ingress.github.io/charts
$ helm install haproxy haproxy-ingress/haproxy-ingress

# Handles high concurrency better than NGINX
# Supports TCP passthrough for databases

14.3d AWS ALB Ingress Controller

Provisions AWS Application Load Balancers natively. Best choice for EKS clusters — integrates with AWS WAF, Shield, and ACM certificates.

Install AWS Load Balancer Controller on EKS 
# Requires IAM OIDC provider + IAM policy
$ helm repo add eks https://aws.github.io/eks-charts
$ helm install aws-load-balancer-controller eks/aws-load-balancer-controller \
    -n kube-system \
    --set clusterName=my-cluster

# Uses annotation: alb.ingress.kubernetes.io/scheme: internet-facing

14.3e Istio Ingress Gateway

Part of the Istio service mesh. Provides advanced traffic management, mTLS, and observability. Overkill for simple apps but powerful for microservice architectures.

Install Istio + Gateway 
# Install Istio
$ istioctl install --set profile=demo
$ kubectl label namespace anihpj-app istio-injection=enabled

# Uses Gateway + VirtualService CRDs instead of Ingress
# Provides automatic mTLS between all services

📊 Ingress Controller Comparison

Controller Auto-HTTPS Complexity Best For
NGINX Via cert-manager 🟢 Low General purpose, learning
Traefik ✅ Built-in Let's Encrypt 🟢 Low Auto HTTPS, k3s, small teams
HAProxy Via cert-manager 🟡 Medium Enterprise, high performance
AWS ALB ✅ Via ACM 🟡 Medium AWS EKS only
Istio ✅ mTLS built-in 🔴 High Service mesh, microservices

14.4 Ingress Rules — Routing

Ingress rules define how external HTTP requests are routed to internal Services. You can route by URL path, hostname, or both.

14.4a Path-Based Routing

Route different URL paths to different backend services. All paths share the same domain name.

🔀 Path-Based Routing Flow

  anihpj.local
       │
       ├── /api/jobs/     ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000    (Django API)
       │
       ├── /admin/        ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000    (Django Admin)
       │
       ├── /static/       ──▶  static-svc:80      (Nginx static files)
       │
       └── /              ──▶  frontend-svc:3000   (React/Vue SPA)
k8s/ingress-path.yaml — Path-based routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /admin
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: frontend-svc
                port:
                  number: 3000
pathType Behavior Example: path: /api
Prefix Matches URL prefix (with path segments) Matches /api, /api/jobs, /api/jobs/1
Exact Matches exact URL only Matches /api only, NOT /api/jobs
ImplementationSpecific Up to the Ingress controller Controller-dependent behavior

14.4b Host-Based Routing

Route different domain names to different services — like virtual hosting. Each domain can point to a different backend.

🌐 Host-Based Routing Flow

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              Ingress Controller                    │
  │                                                    │
  │  api.anihpj.com    ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000           │
  │  admin.anihpj.com  ──▶  admin-svc:8000            │
  │  docs.anihpj.com   ──▶  docs-svc:3000             │
  │  *.anihpj.com      ──▶  default-svc:80 (wildcard) │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/ingress-host.yaml — Host-based routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-host-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: api.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
    - host: admin.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: admin-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

14.4c Combined (Path + Host)

For complex setups, combine host-based and path-based routing in a single Ingress resource:

k8s/ingress-combined.yaml — Host + Path routing 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-combined-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: api.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v1-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
          - path: /v2
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v2-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
    - host: www.anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: frontend-svc
                port:
                  number: 3000

14.5 TLS/SSL with Ingress

Ingress handles HTTPS termination at the edge — clients connect via HTTPS, and the Ingress controller forwards plain HTTP to your pods internally. This means your application doesn't need to handle TLS certificates.

🔒 TLS Termination at the Ingress

  🌍 Browser (HTTPS)
  https://anihpj.com/api/jobs/
         │
         │  TLS encrypted 🔒
         ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────┐
  │  Ingress Controller      │
  │  (has TLS cert + key)    │  ← HTTPS terminates here
  │                          │
  │  Decrypts → plain HTTP   │
  └──────────┬───────────────┘
             │
             │  plain HTTP (inside cluster — safe)
             ▼
  ┌──────────────────┐
  │  anihpj-svc:8000 │
  │  (ClusterIP)     │
  └────────┬─────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌──────────────┐
  │ anihpj pod   │  ← App serves plain HTTP
  │ 🐍 Django     │     No TLS config needed!
  └──────────────┘
Step 1: Create TLS Secret from certificate files 
# Create a TLS secret from your certificate + private key
$ kubectl create secret tls anihpj-tls \
    --cert=tls.crt \
    --key=tls.key \
    -n anihpj-app

# Verify
$ kubectl get secret anihpj-tls -n anihpj-app
NAME          TYPE                DATA   AGE
anihpj-tls    kubernetes.io/tls   2      5s
Step 2: Ingress with TLS configuration 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-tls-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"    # Force HTTPS
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:                                     # ← TLS section
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.com
        - www.anihpj.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls              # TLS Secret created above
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
💡 Automatic Certificates with cert-manager

For production, install cert-manager to automatically obtain and renew free TLS certificates from Let's Encrypt. Just add an annotation and cert-manager handles everything:

Install cert-manager + Create a ClusterIssuer 
# Step 1: Install cert-manager with CRDs
$ kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.14.4/cert-manager.yaml

# Step 2: Verify it's running
$ kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
NAME                                       READY   STATUS
cert-manager-7dd5f5b4d7-abc12             1/1     Running
cert-manager-cainjector-6c9f8b5d8-xyz34   1/1     Running
cert-manager-webhook-5c4f9b6d7-def56      1/1     Running
k8s/cluster-issuer.yaml — Let's Encrypt production issuer 
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: admin@anihpj.com
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-key
    solvers:
      - http01:
          ingress:
            class: nginx
Automatic HTTPS with cert-manager + Let's Encrypt 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-auto-tls
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "letsencrypt-prod"  # ← auto-cert!
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - anihpj.com
      secretName: anihpj-tls-auto         # cert-manager creates this!
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

14.6 Ingress Annotations — Controller-Specific Config

Annotations let you fine-tune controller behavior — rewrites, redirects, rate limiting, CORS headers, and more. These are controller-specific (NGINX annotations won't work on Traefik).

📋 Common NGINX Ingress Annotations

Annotation Purpose Example Value
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target Rewrite URL path before forwarding /
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect Force HTTP → HTTPS redirect "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size Max upload size (Django file uploads) "10m"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout Backend read timeout (seconds) "60"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-cors Enable CORS headers "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps Rate limit (requests per second) "10"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex Enable regex in path matching "true"
nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/affinity Session affinity (sticky sessions) "cookie"
Ingress with Django-specific annotations 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"      # Resume uploads
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-read-timeout: "60"    # Long requests
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-cors: "true"         # API CORS
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps: "10"             # Rate limiting
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
    - hosts: ["anihpj.com"]
      secretName: anihpj-tls
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000

🔀 Understanding rewrite-target — The Tricky One

  WITHOUT rewrite-target:
  ═══════════════════════
  Request: /api/jobs/
  Backend receives: /api/jobs/        ← Django sees the full path ✅
  (Usually what you want for Django!)

  WITH rewrite-target: /
  ═══════════════════════
  Request: /api/jobs/
  Backend receives: /jobs/            ← /api stripped! Django gets /jobs/
  (Useful when backend doesn't expect the path prefix)

  WITH rewrite-target using regex capture:
  ═══════════════════════════════════════════
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /$2
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/use-regex: "true"
  path: /api(/|$)(.*)

  Request: /api/jobs/1    → Backend receives: /jobs/1
  Request: /api/admin/    → Backend receives: /admin/
  Request: /api           → Backend receives: /

  ⚠️  For Django apps: usually DON'T use rewrite-target.
      Django's URL routing expects the full path as configured.

14.7 Setting Up Ingress for anihpj — Complete Walkthrough

Let's set up a working Ingress for anihpj on Minikube — from enabling the addon to browsing anihpj.local in your browser.

🏗️ Complete anihpj Ingress Architecture

  🌍 http://anihpj.local
         │
         │  (browser / curl)
         ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                Minikube Ingress (NGINX)                    │
  │                                                            │
  │   anihpj.local/api/*    ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/admin/*  ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/static/* ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  │   anihpj.local/jobs/*   ──▶  anihpj-svc:8000 (Django)    │
  └──────────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
                ┌──────────────────┐
                │  anihpj-svc      │ ClusterIP: 10.96.78.50
                │  (ClusterIP)     │ Port: 8000
                └────┬────────┬────┘
                     │        │
                     ▼        ▼
               ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐
               │anihpj-1│ │anihpj-2│
               │ :8000  │ │ :8000  │
               └────────┘ └────────┘
Step 1: Enable Ingress addon in Minikube 
# Enable the NGINX Ingress Controller
$ minikube addons enable ingress

# Wait for the controller pod to be ready
$ kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx -w
NAME                                        READY   STATUS    AGE
ingress-nginx-controller-5d88495688-abc12   1/1     Running   30s
Step 2: Create the anihpj Ingress resource 
# k8s/ingress.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ingress
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/proxy-body-size: "10m"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.local
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 8000
Step 3: Apply and get the Ingress IP 
# Apply the Ingress
$ kubectl apply -f k8s/ingress.yaml

# Check the Ingress and get the ADDRESS
$ kubectl get ingress -n anihpj-app
NAME             CLASS   HOSTS          ADDRESS         PORTS   AGE
anihpj-ingress   nginx   anihpj.local   192.168.49.2    80      10s

# The ADDRESS is the Minikube IP
Step 4: Update hosts file (map domain to IP) 
# Windows — Run as Administrator:
# Edit C:\Windows\System32\drivers\etc\hosts
# Add this line:
192.168.49.2    anihpj.local

# macOS / Linux:
$ sudo sh -c 'echo "192.168.49.2 anihpj.local" >> /etc/hosts'
Step 5: Access anihpj in your browser! 
# Test with curl
$ curl http://anihpj.local/api/jobs/
{"jobs": [{"id": 1, "title": "Django Developer"}, ...]}

# Or open in browser:
# → http://anihpj.local/admin/
# → http://anihpj.local/api/jobs/

# Verify traffic is hitting the right pods
$ kubectl logs -f -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app
[INFO] GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK
📝 Ingress Debugging Tips
  • kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app — check rules and backend status
  • kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx -l app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx — controller logs
  • If ADDRESS stays empty → run minikube tunnel or check the controller pod
  • 404 errors → path/host mismatch or backend Service not found
  • 503 errors → backend pods not ready or endpoints empty

🛠️ Ingress kubectl Quick Reference

Task Command
List all Ingresses kubectl get ingress -n anihpj-app
Ingress details & rules kubectl describe ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app
Controller logs kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx -l app.kubernetes.io/name=ingress-nginx
Check controller pods kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx
Delete Ingress kubectl delete ingress anihpj-ingress -n anihpj-app
Enable Minikube Ingress minikube addons enable ingress

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Ingress Routing

⏱️ Target: 8 minutes
  1. Create two deployments: app-v1 (image: nginx) and app-v2 (image: httpd), each with 2 replicas
  2. Create ClusterIP services for both: app-v1-svc on port 80, app-v2-svc on port 80
  3. Create an Ingress named app-ingress with path-based routing: /v1 → app-v1-svc, /v2 → app-v2-svc
  4. Add a host-based rule: api.example.com → app-v1-svc
💡 Hint: kubectl explain ingress.spec.rules for the structure
View Solution 
# 1. Create deployments
kubectl create deployment app-v1 --image=nginx --replicas=2
kubectl create deployment app-v2 --image=httpd --replicas=2

# 2. Create services
kubectl expose deployment app-v1 --name=app-v1-svc --port=80
kubectl expose deployment app-v2 --name=app-v2-svc --port=80
# 3 & 4. Ingress with path-based and host-based routing
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: app-ingress
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /v1
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: app-v1-svc
            port:
              number: 80
      - path: /v2
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: app-v2-svc
            port:
              number: 80
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: app-v1-svc
            port:
              number: 80

Chapter 14 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is an Ingress resource and how does it differ from a Service of type LoadBalancer? Why is Ingress preferred?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureIngressLoadBalancer Service
Load Balancers1 shared LB for all services1 LB per service
Path routing✅ /api → svc-a, /web → svc-b❌ Not supported
Host routing✅ a.com → svc-a, b.com → svc-b❌ Not supported
TLS termination✅ Built-in⚠️ Cloud-specific annotations
Cost💰 Single LB💰💰💰 Per-service LB
Explanation

Ingress provides Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) routing, while LoadBalancer operates at Layer 4 (TCP/UDP). In production, 20 microservices with LoadBalancer type = 20 cloud load balancers = expensive. With Ingress, one load balancer routes to all services based on host/path rules. The CKAD focuses heavily on Ingress creation and path-based routing.

Q2
Write an Ingress resource that routes /api to service api-svc:8080 and / to service web-svc:80.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: app-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

CKAD critical: The Ingress YAML changed significantly in networking.k8s.io/v1 — the old serviceName/servicePort format is gone. You must use the nested service.name + service.port.number structure. The rewrite-target annotation strips the path prefix before forwarding (e.g., /api/users → /users to the backend). Order matters: more specific paths should come first.

Q3
What's the difference between pathType: Prefix, pathType: Exact, and pathType: ImplementationSpecific?
Show Answer
Answer
pathTypeURL: /api/v1/usersBehavior
Exact with /api❌ No matchMatches ONLY /api exactly
Prefix with /api✅ MatchesMatches /api and anything starting with /api/
ImplementationSpecific⚠️ DependsBehavior defined by the IngressClass
Explanation

pathType is REQUIRED in networking.k8s.io/v1. Prefix matches URL path prefixes split by /: path /api matches /api, /api/, /api/v1 but NOT /apitest. Exact requires an exact match. On the CKAD, always use Prefix unless the question specifically asks for exact matching.

Q4
Write an Ingress with host-based routing: api.example.com → api-svc:8080 and web.example.com → web-svc:80.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: multi-host-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
  - host: web.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

Each rules entry can have a host field. The Ingress controller inspects the HTTP Host header to route. Without a host field, the rule applies to ALL incoming traffic. Host-based routing is commonly combined with TLS — each host can have its own certificate. CKAD often tests host + path routing combined.

Q5
Add TLS termination to an Ingress for secure.example.com using a Secret named tls-secret. Show both the Ingress and how to create the Secret.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create TLS secret from cert files
kubectl create secret tls tls-secret \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: secure-ingress
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - secure.example.com
    secretName: tls-secret    # References the TLS secret
  rules:
  - host: secure.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: secure-svc
            port:
              number: 443
Explanation

The tls section references a Secret of type kubernetes.io/tls containing tls.crt and tls.key. The hosts in tls must match the host in rules. The Ingress controller terminates TLS and forwards decrypted traffic to the backend. The kubectl create secret tls command is the fastest way — memorize it for the CKAD.

Q6
What is an IngressClass? Why was it introduced and how do you reference it from an Ingress?
Show Answer
Answer
# IngressClass definition
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IngressClass
metadata:
  name: nginx
  annotations:
    ingressclass.kubernetes.io/is-default-class: "true"
spec:
  controller: k8s.io/ingress-nginx
---
# Referencing in Ingress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: my-ingress
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx     # ← References IngressClass
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: web-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

IngressClass replaced the deprecated kubernetes.io/ingress.class annotation. A cluster can have multiple Ingress controllers (nginx, traefik, haproxy) — IngressClass tells Kubernetes which controller should handle which Ingress.
