The is-default-class annotation makes it the default for Ingresses without ingressClassName. On the CKAD, use spec.ingressClassName — the annotation is deprecated.

Q7
Create an Ingress imperatively using kubectl create ingress that routes /app to app-svc:8080.
Show Answer
Answer
# Basic imperative ingress creation
kubectl create ingress app-ingress \
  --rule="/app=app-svc:8080"

# With host-based routing
kubectl create ingress app-ingress \
  --rule="myapp.com/app=app-svc:8080"

# With TLS
kubectl create ingress app-ingress \
  --rule="myapp.com/app=app-svc:8080,tls=my-tls-secret"

# Generate YAML to inspect
kubectl create ingress app-ingress \
  --rule="/app=app-svc:8080" \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml
Explanation

kubectl create ingress is a massive CKAD time-saver. The --rule format is host/path=service:port[,tls=secretName]. Without a host, omit it: /path=svc:port. Multiple --rule flags create multiple rules. Always generate with --dry-run=client -o yaml first to verify before applying — especially for complex multi-rule setups.

Q8
What is a default backend in Ingress? How do you configure it to catch all unmatched traffic?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: catch-all-ingress
spec:
  defaultBackend:
    service:
      name: default-svc
      port:
        number: 80
  rules:
  - host: app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
Explanation

The defaultBackend handles requests that don't match any rule (wrong host, unmatched path). Without it, the Ingress controller returns its own 404 page. Common use: custom error pages, catch-all redirects. On the CKAD, questions about "what happens when no path matches" test this concept. Having a defaultBackend is a best practice.

Q9
You deployed an Ingress but requests return 502 Bad Gateway. The Ingress controller is running fine. What do you check?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check Service exists and has endpoints
kubectl get svc api-svc
kubectl get endpoints api-svc

# 2. Check if pods are healthy
kubectl get pods -l app=api
kubectl logs <pod-name>

# 3. Port mismatch check
kubectl describe ingress app-ingress
# Backend port must match Service port (not targetPort)

# 4. Test service directly (bypass ingress)
kubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- api-svc:8080

# 5. Check ingress controller logs
kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx <controller-pod>
Explanation

502 = the Ingress controller reached the backend Service but got no valid response. Common causes: (1) Service exists but endpoints are empty (selector mismatch), (2) pods are crashing (check logs), (3) Ingress backend port doesn't match Service port (you must reference the Service port, not the container targetPort), (4) readiness probes failing. Debugging flow: Ingress → Service → Endpoints → Pod.

Q10
Write an Ingress that combines host-based and path-based routing: shop.example.com/products → product-svc:8080, shop.example.com/orders → order-svc:8081, admin.example.com/ → admin-svc:9090.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: shop-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  rules:
  - host: shop.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /products
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: product-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
      - path: /orders
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: order-svc
            port:
              number: 8081
  - host: admin.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: admin-svc
            port:
              number: 9090
Explanation

This pattern is a classic CKAD question: multiple hosts each with their own path rules. Each rules entry handles one host. Each host can have multiple paths. The Ingress controller evaluates: (1) match the host header, (2) find the longest matching path prefix, (3) route to the backend. All of this uses a single external load balancer — demonstrating the power of Ingress over multiple LoadBalancer Services.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Ingress TLS & WAF Hardening

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Ingress Security Checklist

ControlImplementationRisk if Missing
TLS terminationspec.tls with SecretPlaintext traffic, credential theft
Force HTTPS redirectAnnotation: ssl-redirect=trueUsers access over HTTP accidentally
Rate limitingNginx annotationsDDoS, brute force attacks
WAF rulesModSecurity integrationSQL injection, XSS
Client cert authmTLS on ingressUnauthorized API access

Chapter 14 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create an Ingress with TLS termination using a certificate stored in a Secret. Force HTTPS redirect.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create TLS Secret
kubectl create secret tls app-tls \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key \
  -n production
# 2. Ingress with TLS
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: secure-ingress
  namespace: production
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"      # Force HTTPS
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/force-ssl-redirect: "true"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - app.example.com
    secretName: app-tls          # TLS cert Secret
  rules:
  - host: app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: app-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

TLS termination at the Ingress is essential — it prevents plaintext traffic between clients and the cluster. The ssl-redirect annotation ensures HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS. On CKS, you'll create TLS Secrets and configure Ingress with them. Use cert-manager for automatic certificate management.

CKS-2
How do you enable ModSecurity WAF on an Nginx Ingress Controller?
Show Answer
Answer
# Enable ModSecurity globally in Ingress ConfigMap
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: ingress-nginx-controller
  namespace: ingress-nginx
data:
  enable-modsecurity: "true"
  enable-owasp-modsecurity-crs: "true"   # OWASP Core Rule Set

# Or per-Ingress via annotations:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-modsecurity: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/enable-owasp-core-rules: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/modsecurity-snippet: |
      SecRuleEngine On
      SecRule ARGS "@contains <script>" "id:1,phase:2,deny,status:403"
Explanation

ModSecurity is a WAF that protects against OWASP Top 10 attacks (SQL injection, XSS, etc.). The Nginx Ingress Controller has built-in ModSecurity support. On CKS, know how to enable it via ConfigMap or annotations. The OWASP Core Rule Set provides baseline protection.

CKS-3
How do you implement rate limiting on an Ingress to prevent brute force attacks?
Show Answer
Answer
# Nginx Ingress rate limiting annotations
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: api-ingress
  annotations:
    # Rate limiting
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rps: "10"          # 10 req/sec per IP
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-rpm: "300"         # 300 req/min per IP
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-connections: "5"   # 5 concurrent connections
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-burst-multiplier: "3"
    
    # Custom error page for rate limiting
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/custom-http-errors: "429"
    
    # Whitelist IPs that bypass rate limit
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/limit-whitelist: "10.0.0.0/8"
spec:
  rules:
  - host: api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /login        # Protect login endpoint
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: auth-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

Rate limiting prevents brute force, credential stuffing, and basic DDoS attacks. Apply stricter limits on authentication endpoints (/login, /api/token). On CKS, know the Nginx Ingress rate limiting annotations. The burst multiplier allows short spikes above the limit.

CKS-4
How do you configure mTLS (mutual TLS) on an Ingress to verify client certificates?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create CA cert Secret for client verification
kubectl create secret generic ca-secret \
  --from-file=ca.crt=ca.pem \
  -n production
# 2. Configure Ingress with client cert verification
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: mtls-ingress
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-tls-verify-client: "on"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-tls-secret: "production/ca-secret"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-tls-verify-depth: "1"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/auth-tls-error-page: "https://auth.example.com/error"
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - secure-api.example.com
    secretName: server-tls
  rules:
  - host: secure-api.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 443
Explanation

mTLS ensures both the server and client verify each other's identity via certificates. This is stronger than password auth — only clients with valid certificates (signed by the trusted CA) can access the service. On CKS, know how to configure client cert verification on Nginx Ingress.

CKS-5
How do you automatically manage TLS certificates for Ingress using cert-manager?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create ClusterIssuer for Let's Encrypt
apiVersion: cert-manager.io/v1
kind: ClusterIssuer
metadata:
  name: letsencrypt-prod
spec:
  acme:
    server: https://acme-v02.api.letsencrypt.org/directory
    email: admin@example.com
    privateKeySecretRef:
      name: letsencrypt-prod-key
    solvers:
    - http01:
        ingress:
          class: nginx

# 2. Ingress with cert-manager annotation
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: auto-tls-ingress
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-prod  # Auto-issue cert
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - app.example.com
    secretName: app-tls-auto     # cert-manager creates this Secret
  rules:
  - host: app.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: app-svc
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

cert-manager automates TLS certificate issuance and renewal. It integrates with Let's Encrypt, Vault, or custom CAs. On CKS, know how cert-manager works: ClusterIssuer defines the CA, the Ingress annotation triggers automatic certificate issuance, the Secret is auto-created and renewed before expiry.

📈 Part 5: Scaling & Reliability Chapters 15-17

Chapter 15: Scaling Applications

Learn how Kubernetes automatically adjusts your application's capacity to match demand. From manual replica scaling to fully automated HPA, VPA, and Cluster Autoscaler — this chapter gives you the tools to handle traffic spikes without breaking a sweat.

📈 Scaling 🤖 Autoscaling ⚖️ HPA / VPA 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why scaling matters and what happens when traffic spikes
  • ✅ Horizontal vs vertical scaling — and why K8s prefers horizontal
  • ✅ Manual scaling with kubectl scale
  • ✅ Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) — CPU, memory, and custom metrics
  • ✅ Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA) — right-sizing containers
  • ✅ Cluster Autoscaler — scaling the nodes themselves
  • ✅ Pod Disruption Budgets (PDB) — safe voluntary disruptions
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Application Deployment 20% Observability & Maintenance 15%
Scaling on the CKAD — Manual + HPA

The exam tests both manual and autoscaling. Key commands to memorize:

  • Manual scale — kubectl scale deploy my-app --replicas=5
  • Create HPA — kubectl autoscale deploy my-app --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=80
  • Check HPA status — kubectl get hpa (look at TARGETS column for current vs threshold)
  • HPA needs metrics-server — Won't work without it (pre-installed in exam environment)
  • HPA needs resource requests — Containers MUST have resources.requests.cpu set or HPA can't calculate percentage

Scaling Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Scale deploymentkubectl scale deploy web --replicas=5
Scale ReplicaSetkubectl scale rs web-rs --replicas=3
Scale StatefulSetkubectl scale sts postgres --replicas=3
Scale to zerokubectl scale deploy web --replicas=0
Create HPAkubectl autoscale deploy web --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=80
Check HPAkubectl get hpa
Describe HPAkubectl describe hpa web
Delete HPAkubectl delete hpa web
Top pods (CPU)kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu
Top pods (memory)kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory
Top nodeskubectl top nodes

HPA YAML — Autoscale Template

Use kubectl autoscale imperatively, but know the YAML for multi-metric HPA.

# HPA with CPU target
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: web-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web                      # ← Target deployment
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80     # ← Scale when CPU > 80%
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 75     # ← Scale when Memory > 75%

# Pod Disruption Budget (PDB)
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 1                  # ← OR use maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web

Scaling Traps

  • ❌ HPA shows <unknown>/80% → Means resources.requests.cpu is NOT set on containers
  • ❌ HPA shows <unknown> for all metrics → metrics-server is not running
  • kubectl top pods requires metrics-server (pre-installed in CKAD exam)
  • PDB minAvailable vs maxUnavailable — use ONE, not both. minAvailable: 1 = always keep 1 running
  • autoscaling/v2 for multi-metric HPA, autoscaling/v1 for CPU-only (simpler)

📈 Scaling Decision Flow

1
Monitor

metrics-server collects CPU/memory

2
Evaluate

HPA checks vs target utilization

3
Scale Out

CPU > 80%? Add pods

4
Scale In

CPU < 30%? Remove pods (cooldown)

5
PDB Guard

Never go below minAvailable

📉 Manual Scaling
  • kubectl scale deploy/web --replicas=5
  • Fixed replica count — doesn't react to load
  • Over-provision = wasted resources
  • Under-provision = user-facing errors
  • Requires human intervention at 3 AM
📈 HPA Auto-Scaling
  • CPU/memory-based automatic scaling
  • Custom metrics (requests/sec, queue depth)
  • Respects min/max replica boundaries
  • Cooldown prevents oscillation
  • Requires resources.requests on containers

15.1 Why Scaling Matters

Imagine your anihpj job board gets featured on Hacker News. Traffic spikes 10× in minutes. Without scaling, your users see 503 errors, slow responses, and crashes. With Kubernetes scaling, pods are added automatically, traffic is absorbed, and pods scale back down when the rush is over.

📊 Traffic Spike Without vs With Scaling

  ❌ WITHOUT SCALING                        ✅ WITH KUBERNETES SCALING
  ─────────────────────                     ─────────────────────────
  Traffic: ████████████████ 10x             Traffic: ████████████████ 10x
  Pods:    📦📦📦 (fixed 3)                 Pods:    📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦📦 (auto 10)
  Result:  🔴 503 errors, crashes           Result:  🟢 All requests served
                                            
  Response                                  Response
  Time (ms)                                 Time (ms)
  3000│     ╱╲                              200│ ──────────────────
  2000│   ╱    ╲                            150│
  1000│ ╱        ╲           (crash!)       100│
   200│╱           ╲──── dead                50│
      └──────────────────▶ time                └──────────────────▶ time
       normal  spike  down                      normal  spike  normal

  💡 Scaling = automatically matching capacity to demand
📝 Real-World Impact

Companies like Spotify and Airbnb handle millions of requests by auto-scaling pods from 10 to 1000+ during peak hours, then scaling back to save costs. Kubernetes makes this native — no custom scripts needed.

15.2 Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

There are two fundamental approaches to scaling. Horizontal means adding more pod replicas — Kubernetes' preferred method. Vertical means giving individual pods more CPU/memory. Each has trade-offs.

↔️ Horizontal vs ↕️ Vertical — Visual Comparison

  HORIZONTAL SCALING (Scale Out)             VERTICAL SCALING (Scale Up)
  Add more small pods                        Make one pod bigger

  Before:  ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐                   Before:  ┌─────┐
           │📦│ │📦│ │📦│                            │ 📦  │
           └──┘ └──┘ └──┘                            └─────┘
            3 pods × 256Mi each                       1 pod × 256Mi

  After:   ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐ ┌──┐         After:   ┌──────────┐
           │📦│ │📦│ │📦│ │📦│ │📦│                  │  📦📦📦  │
           └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘ └──┘                  └──────────┘
            5 pods × 256Mi each (+2)                   1 pod × 1Gi (+3×)

  ✅ No single point of failure              ⚠️ Single point of failure
  ✅ No downtime to add pods                 ⚠️ May need pod restart
  ✅ Practically unlimited                   ❌ Capped by node resources
Aspect Horizontal ✅ Vertical ⚠️
Mechanism Add more pod replicas Give pod more CPU/RAM
Downtime None — add pods live May need pod restart
Max Limit Practically unlimited Node resource ceiling
Failure Impact Lose 1 of 5 pods (20%) Lose 1 of 1 pod (100%)
K8s Native HPA (built-in) VPA (add-on)
Best For Stateless apps (web servers) Stateful apps (databases)
💡 Rule of Thumb

Always prefer horizontal scaling for stateless workloads like your anihpj Django app. Reserve vertical scaling for stateful workloads (databases) where you can't simply add replicas. Kubernetes was designed ground-up for horizontal scaling.

15.3 Manual Scaling

The simplest way to scale — manually set the number of replicas. Useful for predictable workloads or one-time adjustments before a known traffic event (like a product launch).

Manual Scaling Commands 
# Scale anihpj from current replicas to 5
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5 -n anihpj-app

# Watch pods spin up in real-time
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w

# Output:
# NAME                      READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-abc12   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-def34   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-ghi56   1/1     Running   0          2d
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-jkl78   0/1     Pending   0          3s   ◀── new
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-mno90   0/1     Pending   0          3s   ◀── new

# Scale back down
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=3 -n anihpj-app

# Or edit the YAML directly
kubectl edit deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Change spec.replicas: 5
k8s/deployment-scaled.yaml — Declarative Scaling 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 5                    # ← change this number to scale
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
📝 Imperative vs Declarative

kubectl scale is imperative (quick but not tracked in Git). For production, always update spec.replicas in your YAML and apply — this way your scaling decisions are version-controlled and reproducible.

15.4 Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA)

The HPA automatically adjusts the number of pod replicas based on observed metrics (CPU, memory, or custom). It's the workhorse of Kubernetes autoscaling — check metrics every 15 seconds, calculate the desired replica count, and scale.

How HPA Works

The HPA runs a continuous control loop every 15 seconds. It fetches metrics from the Metrics Server, compares them against your target, and calculates the ideal replica count using a simple formula.

🔄 HPA Control Loop — Continuous Feedback Cycle

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     HPA CONTROL LOOP                            │
  │                                                                  │
  │      ┌──────────────┐  every 15s   ┌───────────────────┐        │
  │ ┌───▶│ Metrics      │─────────────▶│ HPA Controller    │        │
  │ │    │ Server       │              │                   │        │
  │ │    │              │              │ target: 50% CPU   │        │
  │ │    │ CPU: 85%     │              │ current: 85% CPU  │        │
  │ │    │ Mem: 60%     │              │ replicas: 3       │        │
  │ │    └──────────────┘              │ → SCALE UP!       │        │
  │ │                                   └────────┬──────────┘        │
  │ │                                            │                   │
  │ │    ┌──────────────────────┐                │                   │
  │ │    │   anihpj pods        │◀───────────────┘                   │
  │ │    │                      │   adjust replicas                  │
  │ │    │ 📦📦📦  →  📦📦📦📦📦  │   3 → 5 pods                      │
  │ │    └──────────────────────┘                                    │
  │ │              │                                                  │
  │ └──────────────┘  metrics feed back into the loop                │
  │                                                                  │
  │  📐 Formula:                                                     │
  │  desiredReplicas = ⌈currentReplicas × (currentMetric / target)⌉ │
  │  Example: ⌈3 × (85% / 50%)⌉ = ⌈5.1⌉ = 6 pods                  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📝 Prerequisite: Metrics Server

HPA requires the Metrics Server to be installed in your cluster. On Minikube: minikube addons enable metrics-server. On cloud providers it's usually pre-installed. Without it, HPA cannot read CPU/memory usage.

⬆️ Scale-Up Behavior

Happens immediately when metrics exceed target. New pods are created within seconds. Stabilization window: 0 seconds (default) — fast reaction to traffic spikes.

⬇️ Scale-Down Behavior

Delayed by a 5-minute stabilization window (default). Prevents flapping — won't scale down immediately after a brief dip. Configurable via behavior.scaleDown.

CPU-Based Autoscaling

The most common autoscaling metric. Target a specific average CPU utilization across all pods — HPA adds pods when average CPU goes above the target and removes pods when it drops below.

Quick: Create HPA via kubectl 
# Create HPA: target 50% CPU, min 2 pods, max 10 pods
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj \
  --cpu-percent=50 \
  --min=2 \
  --max=10 \
  -n anihpj-app

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa -n anihpj-app
# NAME     REFERENCE           TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS   AGE
# anihpj  Deployment/anihpj   23%/50%   2         10        3          5m
k8s/hpa-cpu.yaml — HPA (CPU-Based) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj                       # Target deployment
  minReplicas: 2                       # Never go below 2 pods
  maxReplicas: 10                      # Never exceed 10 pods
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 50       # Target: 50% average CPU
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # Wait 5 min before scaling down
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 50                    # Scale down max 50% at a time
          periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0    # Scale up immediately
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 100                   # Can double pods instantly
          periodSeconds: 60
💡 Why 50% CPU Target?

Setting the target to 50% gives you headroom for bursts. If you set it to 90%, pods would already be near full capacity when a spike hits — scaling wouldn't happen fast enough. 50% means pods typically run at half capacity, so they can absorb a sudden 2× spike while HPA provisions new pods.

Memory-Based Autoscaling

Scale based on memory utilization instead of (or in addition to) CPU. Useful for applications with memory-heavy workloads like caching, data processing, or Django apps with large querysets.

k8s/hpa-memory.yaml — HPA (Memory-Based) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa-memory
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70       # Scale when avg memory > 70%
⚠️ Memory Scaling Caveat

Memory-based scaling doesn't work as cleanly as CPU. Memory often doesn't decrease after load drops — applications hold onto allocated memory. This means HPA may scale up but never scale back down. For this reason, CPU-based autoscaling is generally preferred. Use memory scaling as a secondary metric.

Custom Metrics Autoscaling

Scale on business-relevant metrics like HTTP requests per second, queue depth, or active connections. This requires a metrics adapter (typically the Prometheus Adapter) to expose custom metrics to the HPA.

k8s/hpa-custom.yaml — HPA (Custom Metrics) 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-hpa-custom
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 20
  metrics:
    # Scale on HTTP requests per second per pod
    - type: Pods
      pods:
        metric:
          name: http_requests_per_second   # From Prometheus adapter
        target:
          type: AverageValue
          averageValue: 100                # Target: 100 req/s per pod
    # Also keep CPU in check
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 60
📡 HTTP Requests/sec

Scale when each pod handles more than N requests per second. Best for web apps like anihpj.

📬 Queue Depth

Scale when a message queue (Redis, RabbitMQ) exceeds N pending jobs. Best for worker pods.

🔗 Active Connections

Scale on WebSocket or DB connection count. Best for real-time or connection-heavy apps.

Testing HPA

The best way to validate your HPA is to generate artificial load and watch it react in real time. Here's a full walkthrough using anihpj:

Load Test: HPA in Action 
# ① Terminal 1: Watch HPA status
kubectl get hpa anihpj-hpa -n anihpj-app -w
# NAME          REFERENCE           TARGETS   MINPODS   MAXPODS   REPLICAS
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   12%/50%   2         10        2

# ② Terminal 2: Generate load — hammer the /jobs/ endpoint
kubectl run load-generator --image=busybox --restart=Never -- \
  /bin/sh -c "while true; do wget -q -O- http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/; done"

# ③ Watch HPA react (Terminal 1 output over time):
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   12%/50%   2   10   2     ← idle
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   67%/50%   2   10   2     ← load detected!
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   67%/50%   2   10   4     ← scaling up!
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   52%/50%   2   10   5     ← still adjusting
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   38%/50%   2   10   5     ← stabilized ✅

# ④ Stop the load
kubectl delete pod load-generator

# ⑤ After ~5 min stabilization, HPA scales back down
# anihpj-hpa   Deployment/anihpj   8%/50%    2   10   2     ← scaled down ✅

📈 HPA Reaction Timeline

  Replicas
  10│
   8│
   6│                    ┌────────┐
   5│               ┌────┘        └──────────┐
   4│          ┌────┘                         │
   3│     ┌────┘                              │
   2│─────┘                                   └────────────── 2
   1│
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶ time
     idle    load     scaling    peak     cooldown   idle
             starts   up                  (5 min)

  ■ Scale-up: immediate (0s stabilization)
  ■ Scale-down: delayed (300s stabilization window)

15.5 Vertical Pod Autoscaler (VPA)

VPA adjusts CPU and memory requests and limits of individual containers, rather than changing the number of replicas. It watches actual usage, learns patterns, and recommends (or applies) optimal resource values.

👁️ Off Mode

VPA calculates recommendations but doesn't apply them. Use kubectl describe vpa to read suggestions. Safest mode to start with.

🚀 Initial Mode

VPA sets resources only at pod creation time. Existing pods are not restarted. Good for new deployments.

⚡ Auto Mode

VPA actively adjusts resources and restarts pods to apply new values. Most aggressive — use carefully in production.

k8s/vpa-anihpj.yaml — Vertical Pod Autoscaler 
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-vpa
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Off"                   # Start with recommendations only
  resourcePolicy:
    containerPolicies:
      - containerName: anihpj
        minAllowed:
          cpu: 100m
          memory: 128Mi
        maxAllowed:
          cpu: 2
          memory: 2Gi
Reading VPA Recommendations 
# Check what VPA recommends
kubectl describe vpa anihpj-vpa -n anihpj-app

# Output:
# Recommendation:
#   Container Recommendations:
#     Container Name:  anihpj
#     Lower Bound:     Cpu: 100m, Memory: 192Mi
#     Target:          Cpu: 250m, Memory: 384Mi     ◀── use these!
#     Upper Bound:     Cpu: 1,    Memory: 1Gi
#     Uncapped Target: Cpu: 250m, Memory: 384Mi
⚠️ VPA + HPA Conflict

Do NOT use VPA and HPA on the same metric (CPU or memory) simultaneously. They'll fight each other — VPA changes resource requests, which changes HPA's percentage calculations. Use them together only when HPA scales on custom metrics and VPA handles CPU/memory.

15.6 Cluster Autoscaler

While HPA and VPA scale pods, the Cluster Autoscaler scales nodes. When pods can't be scheduled because no node has enough resources, the Cluster Autoscaler asks your cloud provider to add more machines. When nodes are underutilized, it removes them to save cost.

🔗 The Three-Level Autoscaling Pyramid

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            🏔️ KUBERNETES AUTOSCALING PYRAMID              │
  │                                                            │
  │  Level 3:   ┌──────────────────────────┐                   │
  │  CLUSTER    │   Cluster Autoscaler     │  Nodes ±          │
  │             │   "Need more machines"   │                   │
  │             └────────────┬─────────────┘                   │
  │                          │                                  │
  │  Level 2:   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐                   │
  │  POD (H)    │   HPA                    │  Pods ±           │
  │             │   "Need more replicas"   │                   │
  │             └────────────┬─────────────┘                   │
  │                          │                                  │
  │  Level 1:   ┌────────────▼─────────────┐                   │
  │  POD (V)    │   VPA                    │  CPU/Mem ±        │
  │             │   "Need bigger pods"     │                   │
  │             └──────────────────────────┘                   │
  │                                                            │
  │  🔄 Trigger chain:                                         │
  │  Traffic ↑ → Pods need more CPU → HPA adds pods            │
  │  → Node full → Cluster Autoscaler adds node                │
  │  → Traffic ↓ → HPA removes pods → Node empty → Node removed│
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔄 Cluster Autoscaler Flow

  SCALE UP:
  ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │ HPA adds │────▶│ Pod stuck │────▶│ Cluster        │────▶│ Cloud    │
  │ pods     │     │ Pending   │     │ Autoscaler     │     │ Provider │
  └──────────┘     │ (no room) │     │ detects gap    │     │ adds node│
                   └───────────┘     └────────────────┘     └──────────┘

  SCALE DOWN:
  ┌──────────┐     ┌───────────┐     ┌────────────────┐     ┌──────────┐
  │ HPA      │────▶│ Pods      │────▶│ Cluster        │────▶│ Cloud    │
  │ removes  │     │ moved to  │     │ Autoscaler     │     │ Provider │
  │ pods     │     │ other     │     │ detects empty  │     │ removes  │
  └──────────┘     │ nodes     │     │ node (10 min)  │     │ node     │
                   └───────────┘     └────────────────┘     └──────────┘
Cloud Provider Cluster Autoscaler Setup Node Group Config
EKS (AWS) Cluster Autoscaler Helm chart + IAM role for service account Managed Node Groups with min/max sizes
GKE (Google) Built-in — enable via --enable-autoscaling Node pool autoscaling (min/max nodes)
AKS (Azure) Built-in — enable via --enable-cluster-autoscaler Node pools with --min-count / --max-count
Minikube Not applicable (single-node development cluster) Use HPA/VPA only for local testing

15.7 Pod Disruption Budgets (PDB)

A PDB limits how many pods can be voluntarily disrupted at the same time during operations like node drains, cluster upgrades, or autoscaler scale-downs. It ensures your application stays available during planned maintenance.

🔢 minAvailable

Always keep at least N pods (or N%) running. Example: minAvailable: 2 — even during a node drain, at least 2 anihpj pods must stay up.

🚫 maxUnavailable

At most N pods (or N%) can be down simultaneously. Example: maxUnavailable: 1 — only 1 anihpj pod can be disrupted at a time.

k8s/pdb-anihpj.yaml — Pod Disruption Budget 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  minAvailable: 2                        # Always keep ≥2 pods running
  # OR use: maxUnavailable: 1            # At most 1 pod down at a time
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj                        # Targets anihpj pods

🛡️ PDB in Action: Node Drain Scenario

  PDB: minAvailable: 2 (always keep ≥ 2 anihpj pods running)
  anihpj has 3 replicas spread across 2 nodes

  Node 1 (being drained)        Node 2
  ┌────────────────────┐        ┌────────────────────┐
  │ 📦 anihpj-abc      │        │ 📦 anihpj-def      │
  │ 📦 anihpj-ghi      │        │                    │
  └────────────────────┘        └────────────────────┘

  Admin runs: kubectl drain node-1

  Step 1: K8s checks PDB — need ≥ 2 pods running
  Step 2: Evict anihpj-abc (still 2 running: ghi + def) ✅ 
  Step 3: Wait for anihpj-abc to be rescheduled on Node 2
  Step 4: New pod ready on Node 2 (now 3 running)
  Step 5: Evict anihpj-ghi (still 2 running: def + new) ✅

  Result: At no point were fewer than 2 pods running!

  WITHOUT PDB: both pods on Node 1 evicted at once → only 1 pod left → outage risk!
💡 PDB Best Practices
  • Always create PDBs for production deployments with ≥ 2 replicas
  • Use minAvailable when you know the minimum safe count
  • Use maxUnavailable when the replica count varies (with HPA)
  • PDBs only protect against voluntary disruptions (drains, upgrades). They do NOT protect against crashes or node failures
  • Don't set minAvailable equal to your total replicas — it would block all drains!
📝 Chapter 15 Quick Reference
Command / Resource Purpose
kubectl scale deployment anihpj --replicas=5 Manual scaling
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj --cpu-percent=50 --min=2 --max=10 Create HPA
kubectl get hpa -w Watch HPA status
kubectl describe vpa anihpj-vpa Read VPA recommendations
kubectl get pdb List Pod Disruption Budgets
kubectl top pods Check resource usage (needs metrics-server)

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Scaling & Autoscaling

⏱️ Target: 6 minutes
  1. Create a Deployment named load-app with image nginx, 1 replica, and resource requests of cpu: 50m
  2. Scale it manually to 5 replicas
  3. Create an HPA for load-app: min 2, max 10, target CPU 60%
  4. Check the HPA status and verify current metrics
  5. Scale back the deployment to 2 replicas manually (overrides HPA temporarily)
View Solution 
# 1. Create deployment with resource requests
kubectl create deployment load-app --image=nginx --replicas=1 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > load-app.yaml
# Edit to add resources.requests.cpu: "50m"
kubectl apply -f load-app.yaml

# 2. Manual scale
kubectl scale deploy load-app --replicas=5

# 3. Create HPA
kubectl autoscale deployment load-app \
  --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=60

# 4. Check HPA
kubectl get hpa load-app
kubectl describe hpa load-app

# 5. Override (temporary)
kubectl scale deploy load-app --replicas=2

Chapter 15 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling in Kubernetes? Which Kubernetes resource handles each?
Show Answer
Answer
Scaling TypeWhat ChangesK8s ResourceCKAD Scope
Horizontal (HPA)Number of pod replicasHorizontalPodAutoscaler✅ In scope
Vertical (VPA)CPU/memory per podVerticalPodAutoscaler⚠️ Rarely tested
ManualReplicas via kubectlkubectl scale✅ In scope
Explanation

HPA adds/removes pods; VPA resizes existing pods. They solve different problems: HPA for stateless workloads that handle load via parallelism, VPA for single-instance apps that need more resources. On the CKAD, HPA and manual kubectl scale are the focus. VPA is not a built-in resource — it requires an add-on.

Q2
Scale a deployment named web-app to 5 replicas manually. Then verify the scaling.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl scale
kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=5

# Method 2: kubectl patch
kubectl patch deployment web-app -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# Method 3: kubectl edit
kubectl edit deployment web-app
# Change spec.replicas to 5

# Verify
kubectl get deployment web-app
kubectl get pods -l app=web-app
kubectl rollout status deployment web-app
Explanation

kubectl scale is the fastest for the CKAD. rollout status blocks until all replicas are ready — useful to confirm scaling completed. If scaling seems stuck, check events with kubectl describe deployment — common issues: insufficient node resources, image pull errors, or PVC binding for stateful pods.

Q3
Create an HPA that scales deployment api-server between 2-10 replicas at 70% CPU target. Do this imperatively.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative HPA creation
kubectl autoscale deployment api-server \
  --min=2 \
  --max=10 \
  --cpu-percent=70

# Verify
kubectl get hpa api-server
kubectl describe hpa api-server

# Watch HPA in real-time
kubectl get hpa -w
Explanation

CKAD requirement: The deployment MUST have resources.requests.cpu set, otherwise HPA can't calculate percentage. Without CPU requests, HPA shows <unknown>/70% and won't scale. Common exam mistake: creating HPA without setting resource requests first. The HPA checks metrics every 15 seconds by default.

Q4
Write the YAML for an HPA targeting both CPU (70%) and memory (80%) utilization.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: multi-metric-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: api-server
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: memory
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 80
Explanation

Multi-metric HPA uses autoscaling/v2 (not v1). The HPA scales based on the metric that requires the MOST replicas — it takes the maximum. So if CPU says 3 replicas and memory says 5, HPA scales to 5. kubectl autoscale can only set CPU — for memory or custom metrics, you need YAML. The CKAD may test autoscaling/v2 format.

Q5
Your HPA shows <unknown>/70% under TARGETS. What's wrong and how do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check if metrics-server is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server

# Check if deployment has resource requests
kubectl get deployment api-server -o yaml | grep -A5 resources

# Fix: Add resource requests to the deployment
kubectl set resources deployment api-server \
  --requests=cpu=100m,memory=128Mi

# Or patch:
kubectl patch deployment api-server -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"api","resources":{"requests":{"cpu":"100m"}}}]}}}}'
Explanation

<unknown> has two causes: (1) No metrics-server — install it (kubectl apply -f metrics-server.yaml), or (2) No resource requests — HPA can't compute percentage without a baseline. On the CKAD, the metrics-server is pre-installed, so issue #2 is more likely. Always set resources.requests before creating HPA.

Q6
How does Kubernetes decide how many replicas the HPA should maintain? Explain the formula.
Show Answer
Answer
  desiredReplicas = ceil[ currentReplicas × (currentMetric / targetMetric) ]

  Example:
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ Current replicas: 3                              │
  │ Current CPU:      90%                            │
  │ Target CPU:       70%                            │
  │                                                  │
  │ desiredReplicas = ceil(3 × 90/70) = ceil(3.86)  │
  │                 = 4 replicas                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Explanation

The HPA controller computes this every 15 seconds. It averages the metric across all pods, then applies the formula. There's a tolerance band (default ±10%) to prevent flapping: if the ratio is within 0.9-1.1, it doesn't scale. Scale-up has a 3-minute stabilization window; scale-down has a 5-minute window to prevent thrashing.

Q7
What happens if you have BOTH a manual replicas: 3 in the Deployment and an HPA targeting the same deployment?
Show Answer
Answer

The HPA overrides the manual replica count.

# This creates a conflict:
kubectl scale deployment web --replicas=3
kubectl autoscale deployment web --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70

# The HPA will immediately recalculate and may scale up or down
# Your manual "3" gets overridden

# Best practice: don't set replicas in Deployment when using HPA
# Or set it to minReplicas
Explanation

HPA is the authoritative controller for replica count. If you kubectl scale while HPA is active, the HPA will recalculate and potentially override your change within 15 seconds. Best practice: remove spec.replicas from your Deployment manifest when using HPA, or set it to minReplicas. This prevents GitOps sync conflicts too.

Q8
Scale a deployment to zero replicas. When is this useful? Can HPA scale to zero?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scale to zero (manual)
kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=0

# Verify — no pods running
kubectl get pods -l app=web-app
# No resources found

# HPA cannot scale to zero by default!
# minReplicas must be ≥ 1
kubectl autoscale deployment web-app --min=1 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70
Explanation

Scaling to zero is useful for: (1) cost savings on non-production environments, (2) maintenance windows, (3) blue/green deployments (scale old to 0). Standard HPA requires minReplicas ≥ 1. Scale-to-zero requires KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler) or Knative. On the CKAD, manual kubectl scale --replicas=0 is valid and tested.

Q9
What is a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) and how does it relate to scaling? Write a PDB that ensures at least 2 pods of app: web are always available.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2          # At least 2 pods must stay running
  # OR: maxUnavailable: 1  # At most 1 pod can be down
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
# Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb web-pdb
# NAME      MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS   AGE
# web-pdb   2               N/A               1                     5m
Explanation

PDB protects against VOLUNTARY disruptions (node drains, cluster upgrades) — not crashes. It ensures Kubernetes won't evict pods below the budget during maintenance. minAvailable and maxUnavailable can be absolute numbers or percentages (e.g., "50%"). PDB doesn't affect HPA scaling or pod crashes. On the CKAD, know the difference between voluntary and involuntary disruptions.

Q10
You have a deployment with 3 replicas. You need to scale it during a load test: set max to 20 replicas with HPA targeting 50% CPU. After the test, restore to 3 fixed replicas. Show the commands.
Show Answer
Answer
# Before load test: Create HPA
kubectl autoscale deployment web-app \
  --min=3 --max=20 --cpu-percent=50

# Monitor during test
kubectl get hpa -w
kubectl top pods -l app=web-app

# After load test: Remove HPA and fix replicas
kubectl delete hpa web-app
kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=3

# Verify
kubectl get deployment web-app
kubectl get hpa  # Should show no HPA
Explanation

Key sequence: create HPA before load → monitor with kubectl get hpa -w → delete HPA after → manually set replicas. If you just delete HPA without scaling down, the deployment keeps whatever replica count the HPA set. kubectl top pods requires metrics-server and shows real-time CPU/memory — useful for validating HPA behavior during tests.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Autoscaler Abuse & Resource-Based DoS

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Autoscaling introduces security risks: attackers can trigger resource exhaustion by generating artificial load, and HPA/VPA misconfigurations can create denial-of-service conditions.

Autoscaler RiskAttack VectorMitigation
HPA runaway scalingFlood requests → spike CPU → create hundreds of podsSet maxReplicas caps, rate limit at Ingress
Resource exhaustionScale to cluster capacity → legit workloads starvedResourceQuotas per namespace, PriorityClasses
Cluster Autoscaler abuseTrigger node provisioning for crypto miningNode pool limits, budget alerts, admission policies
VPA privilege escalationVPA increases memory → OOM kills other podsLimitRanges, namespace quotas
 
# Secure HPA with bounded scaling
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: secure-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: web-app
  minReplicas: 2        # Minimum for HA
  maxReplicas: 10       # CAP — prevents runaway scaling
  behavior:
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 120   # Wait before scaling up
      policies:
      - type: Pods
        value: 2                        # Max 2 pods at a time
        periodSeconds: 60
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300   # Slow scale-down
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70

Chapter 15 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
An HPA in the production namespace has no maxReplicas cap and is scaling uncontrollably. Fix it to allow maximum 15 replicas with a scale-up stabilization window of 120 seconds.
Show Answer
Answer
# Patch the HPA
kubectl patch hpa web-hpa -n production --type=merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "maxReplicas": 15,
    "behavior": {
      "scaleUp": {
        "stabilizationWindowSeconds": 120,
        "policies": [{"type": "Pods", "value": 3, "periodSeconds": 60}]
      }
    }
  }
}'

# Verify
kubectl get hpa web-hpa -n production -o yaml | grep -A5 behavior
Explanation

Without maxReplicas, an HPA can scale indefinitely, consuming all cluster resources. The stabilization window prevents rapid scale-up from short traffic spikes. On CKS, always set maxReplicas and combine with ResourceQuotas at the namespace level for defense in depth.

CKS-2
Create a ResourceQuota that prevents any single namespace from consuming more than 20 CPU cores and 40Gi memory to protect against autoscaler abuse.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: scaling-limit
  namespace: production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "20"
    requests.memory: 40Gi
    limits.cpu: "30"
    limits.memory: 60Gi
    pods: "50"             # Max 50 pods — prevents HPA runaway
kubectl apply -f quota.yaml
kubectl describe quota scaling-limit -n production
Explanation

ResourceQuotas are the safety net against autoscaler abuse. Even if an HPA tries to create more pods, the quota prevents exceeding hard limits. The pods quota directly caps replica count. On CKS, combine ResourceQuotas + LimitRanges + HPA maxReplicas for comprehensive protection.

CKS-3
How do you use PriorityClasses to ensure critical system workloads survive during resource pressure caused by autoscaling?
Show Answer
Answer
# High-priority class for critical workloads
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: critical-security
value: 1000000
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "For security monitoring and critical services"
---
# Low-priority class for auto-scaled workloads
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: autoscaled-workload
value: 100
globalDefault: false
description: "For HPA-managed workloads — preemptable"
Explanation

PriorityClasses ensure that under resource pressure, low-priority auto-scaled pods get preempted before critical security workloads (like Falco, log collectors). On CKS, assign high priority to security-monitoring DaemonSets and lower priority to user-facing auto-scaled Deployments.

CKS-4
How do you prevent HPA from using custom metrics that could be manipulated by an attacker?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Restrict who can create HPAs with custom metrics via RBAC
kubectl create clusterrole hpa-restricted \
  --verb=create,update \
  --resource=horizontalpodautoscalers

# 2. Use only built-in CPU/memory metrics (harder to spoof)
# 3. Audit HPA configurations
kubectl get hpa -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}/{.metadata.name}: {.spec.metrics[*].type}{"\n"}{end}'

# 4. Use ValidatingAdmissionPolicy to deny HPAs with external metrics
# in untrusted namespaces
Explanation

Custom and external metrics can be spoofed if the metrics pipeline is compromised. An attacker who controls the metrics server can trigger arbitrary scaling. On CKS, restrict HPA creation via RBAC, prefer built-in resource metrics, and audit existing HPAs for suspicious custom metric configurations.

CKS-5
A Cluster Autoscaler is provisioning nodes in an uncontrolled manner. How do you set safe boundaries?
Show Answer
Answer
# Cluster Autoscaler flags for safe boundaries
--max-nodes-total=50              # Global node cap
--max-node-provision-time=15m     # Timeout for node readiness
--scale-down-delay-after-add=10m  # Wait before scale-down after scale-up
--scale-down-unneeded-time=10m    # How long before removing idle nodes
--expendable-pods-priority-cutoff=-10  # Don't scale up for very low priority

# Per node group limits (cloud-specific)
# GKE: --min-nodes=1 --max-nodes=20
# EKS: ASG min/max settings
# AKS: --min-count=1 --max-count=20

# Verify current state
kubectl -n kube-system get configmap cluster-autoscaler-status -o yaml
Explanation

Without node caps, an attacker can trigger cluster autoscaler to provision expensive nodes (e.g., GPU instances). Set --max-nodes-total globally and per-node-group limits. On CKS, combine with budget alerts, ResourceQuotas, and pod priority to prevent autoscaler-based resource abuse.

Chapter 16: Health Checks and Self-Healing

Kubernetes can automatically detect unhealthy containers and take corrective action — restarting crashed processes, removing broken pods from load balancers, and waiting for slow apps to start. All powered by three types of probes you configure in your pod spec.

❤️ Health Checks 🔄 Self-Healing 🔍 Probes 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why health checks prevent silent failures in production
  • ✅ The three probe types: Liveness, Readiness, and Startup
  • ✅ HTTP GET, TCP Socket, and Command Exec probe mechanisms
  • ✅ Readiness probes for zero-downtime deployments
  • ✅ Startup probes for slow-starting applications
  • ✅ Probe configuration parameters and timing
  • ✅ Complete probe implementation for anihpj
  • ✅ Common probe mistakes to avoid
🎓 CKAD Relevance
Observability & Maintenance 15% Application Design & Build 20%
Probes on the CKAD — Almost Every Question Needs Them

Probes are tested directly and indirectly. You'll often be asked to "ensure zero-downtime" or "restart if unhealthy":

  • Liveness — "restart container if unhealthy" → livenessProbe
  • Readiness — "don't send traffic until ready" → readinessProbe
  • Startup — "give slow apps extra time" → startupProbe
  • Default HTTP probe — httpGet: {path: /, port: 8080} with initialDelaySeconds: 5
  • Exec probe — exec: {command: ["cat", "/tmp/healthy"]} — returns 0 = healthy
  • Key fields — initialDelaySeconds, periodSeconds, failureThreshold, successThreshold

Probe YAML — All 3 Types

Memorize all probe types. You WILL be asked to add probes to a deployment.

spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080

    # LIVENESS: "Is the container alive?" → Restart if failing
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz              # ← Health endpoint
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 15       # ← Wait before first check
      periodSeconds: 10             # ← Check every 10s
      failureThreshold: 3           # ← Restart after 3 failures
      timeoutSeconds: 5             # ← Max wait for response

    # READINESS: "Can it serve traffic?" → Remove from Service
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /ready
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5
      successThreshold: 1           # ← 1 success = back in rotation

    # STARTUP: "Has it started yet?" → Protect slow starters
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      failureThreshold: 30          # ← 30 × 10s = 300s to start
      periodSeconds: 10

# TCP Socket Probe (for non-HTTP services like databases)
    livenessProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 5432
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 10

# Exec Command Probe (run command inside container)
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
        - cat
        - /tmp/healthy               # ← Exit 0 = healthy
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5

kubectl explain — Probe Fields

kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.readinessProbe
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.startupProbe
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe.httpGet
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe.tcpSocket
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe.exec
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers.livenessProbe.initialDelaySeconds

Probe Traps

  • ❌ Wrong probe type: Don't use livenessProbe when they ask "don't send traffic until ready" → that's readinessProbe
  • ❌ No initialDelaySeconds → Container gets killed before it starts = CrashLoopBackOff
  • Liveness kills, Readiness removes — Liveness failure = container restart. Readiness failure = removed from Service endpoints
  • startupProbe disables other probes until it succeeds — use for apps that take >30s to start
  • Probe port must match — Use container port, NOT Service port
  • exec probe — return code 0 = success. Any non-zero = failure

Probe Verification Checklist

  • kubectl describe pod [name] → Look for "Liveness", "Readiness" sections
  • kubectl get pod [name] -o yaml | grep -A10 Probe → Verify probe config
  • kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp → Check for "Unhealthy" events
  • kubectl get endpoints [svc] → Readiness failure = missing from endpoints
  • kubectl logs [pod] --previous → Check logs of restarted container

📊 The Three Probe Types

💓 Liveness Is the container alive? Fail → restart
✅ Readiness Can it serve traffic? Fail → remove from endpoints
🚀 Startup Has it started? Protects slow-starting apps

🔬 Probe Configuration Parameters

⏰ initialDelaySeconds
Wait before first probe — give app time to start (default: 0)
🔁 periodSeconds
How often to probe — every N seconds (default: 10)
⏱️ timeoutSeconds
Max wait for response — probe fails if exceeded (default: 1)
❌ failureThreshold
How many consecutive failures before action is taken (default: 3)
✅ successThreshold
How many consecutive successes to be considered healthy (default: 1)
😰 What Happens If You Skip Probes?
  • 🔴 No liveness probe → deadlocked containers run forever, silently broken
  • 🔴 No readiness probe → traffic hits containers during startup = 500 errors
  • 🔴 No startup probe → slow Java apps get killed by liveness before they boot
  • 🔴 Wrong initialDelaySeconds → pod enters CrashLoopBackOff on every deployment

16.1 Why Health Checks Matter

Without health checks, Kubernetes only knows if your container process is running — not if it's actually working correctly. A Python process can be alive but stuck in a deadlock, connected to the wrong database, or returning 500 errors on every request.

❌ Without Probes

Django app connects to wrong DB hostname → app runs but returns 500 errors on every request → Kubernetes thinks pod is healthy (process is running) → traffic keeps routing → all users see errors for hours

✅ With Probes

Readiness probe checks /readyz → DB connection test fails → K8s removes pod from Service endpoints → no traffic routed to broken pod → liveness probe triggers restart → new pod connects correctly → zero user impact

🔄 Self-Healing Flow — How Probes Protect Users

  User Request → Service Load Balancer → Pod Endpoint
                                          │
                                    ┌─────▼──────┐
                                    │ Pod anihpj  │
                                    │             │
                                    │  Readiness  │──── FAIL ──▶ Remove from
                                    │  Probe ❌   │              Service endpoints
                                    │             │              (no traffic)
                                    │  Liveness   │
                                    │  Probe ❌   │──── FAIL ──▶ Restart container
                                    │             │              (kill → recreate)
                                    └─────────────┘
                                          │
                                    After restart:
                                    ┌─────────────┐
                                    │ Pod anihpj  │
                                    │             │
                                    │  Readiness ✅│──── PASS ──▶ Add back to
                                    │  Liveness ✅ │              Service endpoints
                                    │             │              (traffic resumes)
                                    └─────────────┘

16.2 The Three Probe Types

Kubernetes offers three distinct probes, each answering a different question about your container's health. They work together to cover the full application lifecycle — starting up, staying alive, and being ready for traffic.

Probe Question It Answers On Failure When It Runs
❤️ Liveness "Is the process alive and not deadlocked?" Container is restarted Continuously, after startup completes
🟢 Readiness "Can it handle user traffic right now?" Removed from Service endpoints Continuously, after startup completes
🚀 Startup "Has it finished starting up?" Liveness & Readiness wait Only during initial startup

⏱️ Probe Timeline — When Each Probe Runs

  Time →  0s         10s        20s        30s        40s  ...
          │           │           │           │           │
  Startup ████████████████████████ ✅ done!
          │  (checking if app started)       │           │
          │                                  │           │
  Liveness                                   ████████████████ ... (forever)
          │                          (starts after startup succeeds)
          │                                  │           │
  Readiness                                  ████████████████ ... (forever)
          │                          (starts after startup succeeds)
          │                                  │           │

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  🔑 Key Insight:                                             │
  │  Startup probe BLOCKS liveness and readiness until it passes│
  │  This prevents liveness from killing slow-starting apps!    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
❤️ Liveness

"Still alive?"
Fail → 🔄 RESTART pod
Use: GET /healthz
Check: internal process health

🟢 Readiness

"Ready for traffic?"
Fail → 🚫 REMOVE from Service
Use: GET /readyz
Check: DB, cache, dependencies

🚀 Startup

"Done booting?"
Fail → ⏳ WAIT (block probes)
Use: GET /startupz
Check: migrations, warmup

16.3 Liveness Probes — "Are You Still Alive?"

Liveness probes detect when a container is running but broken — deadlocks, infinite loops, corrupted state. Kubernetes restarts the container to recover. There are three probe mechanisms:

HTTP GET Probe

The most common type. Kubernetes sends an HTTP GET request to a specified endpoint. A response code between 200-399 means healthy, anything else means unhealthy → restart.

anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Health Endpoint 
# Simple liveness endpoint — only checks if Django process is alive
from django.http import JsonResponse

def healthz(request):
    """Liveness probe: is the process alive and responding?"""
    return JsonResponse({"status": "alive"}, status=200)
k8s/liveness-http.yaml — HTTP GET Liveness Probe 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz              # Endpoint to check
        port: 8000                  # Container port
        httpHeaders:                # Optional custom headers
          - name: Host
            value: anihpj.local
      initialDelaySeconds: 10       # Wait 10s after start
      periodSeconds: 15             # Check every 15 seconds
      timeoutSeconds: 3             # Timeout per check
      failureThreshold: 3           # 3 failures → restart
      successThreshold: 1           # 1 success → healthy

TCP Socket Probe

Kubernetes attempts a TCP connection to the specified port. If the connection succeeds, the container is alive. Useful for non-HTTP services like databases, Redis, or custom TCP servers.

k8s/liveness-tcp.yaml — TCP Socket Liveness 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    ports:
      - containerPort: 8000
    livenessProbe:
      tcpSocket:
        port: 8000                  # Try to connect to this port
      initialDelaySeconds: 10
      periodSeconds: 15
      failureThreshold: 3
📝 When to Use TCP vs HTTP

Use TCP probes for services that don't speak HTTP (PostgreSQL on port 5432, Redis on port 6379). For web apps like anihpj that serve HTTP, prefer HTTP GET probes because they validate the application layer, not just that the port is open.

Command Execution Probe

Kubernetes runs a command inside the container. Exit code 0 = healthy, any other exit code = unhealthy. Useful for complex checks that can't be expressed as HTTP or TCP.

k8s/liveness-exec.yaml — Command Execution Liveness 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command:
          - python
          - manage.py
          - check                   # Django system check
          - --deploy                # Include deployment checks
      initialDelaySeconds: 15
      periodSeconds: 30             # Less frequent — exec is heavier
      timeoutSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
Probe Mechanism Check Method Best For Overhead
HTTP GET HTTP request → check status code Web apps (Django, Flask, Express) Low
TCP Socket TCP connection → check port open Databases, Redis, gRPC Very Low
Exec Command Run command → check exit code Complex custom checks High (spawns process)

16.4 Readiness Probes — "Can You Handle Requests?"

Readiness probes determine whether a pod should receive traffic. Unlike liveness, a failed readiness probe doesn't restart the pod — it removes the pod from Service endpoints. Once the probe passes again, the pod is re-added. This is crucial for zero-downtime deployments and dependency failures.

🚦 Readiness Probe — Traffic Flow Impact

  READINESS = PASS ✅                    READINESS = FAIL ❌
  ─────────────────                      ─────────────────
  Service Endpoints:                     Service Endpoints:
  ┌──────────────────────┐               ┌──────────────────────┐
  │ • anihpj-abc (10.0.1.5) │            │ • anihpj-abc (10.0.1.5) │
  │ • anihpj-def (10.0.1.6) │            │ (anihpj-def removed!) │
  │ • anihpj-ghi (10.0.1.7) │            │ • anihpj-ghi (10.0.1.7) │
  └──────────────────────┘               └──────────────────────┘
  Traffic → all 3 pods                   Traffic → only 2 pods
                                         anihpj-def still runs but
                                         gets NO traffic until ready
anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Readiness Endpoint 
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection

def readyz(request):
    """Readiness probe: can we serve user requests?"""
    try:
        # Check database connectivity
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
        
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "ready",
            "database": "connected"
        }, status=200)
    except Exception as e:
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "not_ready",
            "error": str(e)
        }, status=503)                       # 503 → not ready
k8s/readiness-probe.yaml 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5          # Start checking after 5s
      periodSeconds: 10               # Check every 10 seconds
      timeoutSeconds: 3
      failureThreshold: 3             # 3 failures → remove from LB
      successThreshold: 2             # Need 2 consecutive passes to
                                      # be added back (prevents flapping)
💡 Use Cases for Readiness Probes
  • During deployment: New pods aren't added to Service until they're fully initialized
  • Database failover: If DB goes down, pods are removed from traffic until DB reconnects
  • Cache warmup: Pod needs to pre-load data before serving requests
  • Dependency checks: Verify Redis, external APIs, or message queues are reachable

16.5 Startup Probes — "Have You Finished Starting?"

Startup probes solve a critical problem: slow-starting applications. If your Django app runs collectstatic and database migrations on boot (taking 30+ seconds), the liveness probe might kill it before it even finishes starting. The startup probe blocks all other probes until it succeeds.

k8s/startup-probe.yaml 
containers:
  - name: anihpj
    image: anihpj:latest
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8000
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 5                # Check every 5 seconds
      failureThreshold: 30            # 30 failures × 5s = 150s max
                                      # App has up to 2.5 min to start
      successThreshold: 1             # 1 success → startup complete
    # These probes don't run until startup succeeds:
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8000
      periodSeconds: 15
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /readyz
        port: 8000
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3

🚀 Startup Probe — Protection Against Premature Kills

  WITHOUT startup probe:                 WITH startup probe:
  ─────────────────────                  ────────────────────
  0s  Container starts                   0s  Container starts
  3s  Liveness check → FAIL (starting)  5s  Startup check → FAIL (starting)
  6s  Liveness check → FAIL             10s Startup check → FAIL
  9s  Liveness check → FAIL             15s Startup check → FAIL
  ❌  3 failures → RESTART!              20s Startup check → FAIL
                                         25s Startup check → PASS ✅
  Then restarts in a loop forever...         └▶ Start liveness + readiness
  (CrashLoopBackOff)                         App works normally! 🎉

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Max startup time = failureThreshold × periodSeconds        │
  │  Example: 30 × 5s = 150 seconds (2.5 minutes)              │
  │  If app doesn't start in that time → container is killed    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

16.6 Probe Configuration Parameters

All three probe types share the same timing parameters. Getting these right is critical — too aggressive and you'll get unnecessary restarts, too lenient and failures go undetected.

Parameter Default Description Recommendation
initialDelaySeconds 0 Wait N seconds before first probe check Set to your app's typical startup time
periodSeconds 10 How often to check (seconds between probes) 10-30s for liveness, 5-10s for readiness
timeoutSeconds 1 Max time to wait for a response 3-5s (higher for DB-dependent checks)
successThreshold 1 Consecutive successes needed to pass 1 for liveness, 1-2 for readiness
failureThreshold 3 Consecutive failures before action taken 3 for liveness, 3 for readiness

⏱️ Timing Parameters — Visual Example

  initialDelay=10s    periodSeconds=15s           failureThreshold=3
  │                   │                           │
  │    ┌──────────────▼───┬───────────┬───────────┬───────────┐
  ▼    │                  │           │           │           │
  0s   10s               25s         40s         55s         70s
  ─────┤──────────────────┤───────────┤───────────┤───────────┤──▶
       │                  │           │           │           │
  wait │   1st check      2nd check   3rd check   action!
       │   (timeout: 3s)  ✅ pass     ❌ fail     ❌ fail     ❌ fail
       │                                                      │
       │                                               ┌──────▼──────┐
       │                                               │  RESTART    │
       │                                               │  (liveness) │
       │                                               │  or REMOVE  │
       │                                               │  (readiness)│
       │                                               └─────────────┘

16.7 Implementing All Probes for anihpj

Here's the complete implementation — Django health check views, URL routes, and a Deployment with all three probes configured:

anihpj/jobpost/views.py — Health Check Views 
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection

def healthz(request):
    """
    LIVENESS probe endpoint.
    Only checks if the Django process is alive and responding.
    Do NOT check external dependencies here!
    """
    return JsonResponse({"status": "alive"}, status=200)


def readyz(request):
    """
    READINESS probe endpoint.
    Checks if the app can serve user requests:
    - Database connection is working
    - (Add more checks as needed: Redis, external APIs)
    """
    checks = {}
    
    # Check database
    try:
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
        checks["database"] = "connected"
    except Exception as e:
        checks["database"] = f"failed: {e}"
        return JsonResponse({
            "status": "not_ready",
            "checks": checks
        }, status=503)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        "status": "ready",
        "checks": checks
    }, status=200)
anihpj/jobpost/urls.py — Health Routes 
from django.urls import path
from . import views

urlpatterns = [
    # ... existing job routes ...
    path('healthz', views.healthz, name='liveness'),
    path('readyz', views.readyz, name='readiness'),
]
k8s/deployment-with-probes.yaml — Complete Probes 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:latest
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 250m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
              
          # ── STARTUP PROBE ──
          # Gives app up to 150s to start (30 × 5s)
          startupProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 30
            
          # ── LIVENESS PROBE ──
          # Checks every 15s if process is alive
          # 3 failures → restart container
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 15
            timeoutSeconds: 3
            failureThreshold: 3
            
          # ── READINESS PROBE ──
          # Checks every 10s if app can serve traffic
          # 3 failures → remove from Service endpoints
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /readyz
              port: 8000
            periodSeconds: 10
            timeoutSeconds: 5
            failureThreshold: 3
            successThreshold: 2

16.8 Common Probe Mistakes

Probes are powerful but misconfiguring them can cause more problems than having no probes at all. Here are the most common mistakes and how to avoid them:

❌ Liveness Depends on External Service

Liveness probe checks database connection → DB goes down briefly → ALL pods restart → app is completely down → DB comes back but now all pods are restarting → cascading failure!

✅ Fix: Liveness should ONLY check internal process health. Put DB checks in readiness probe.

❌ Timeout Too Low for Readiness

timeoutSeconds: 1 but DB query in readiness check takes 2s under load → probe "fails" even though app is working → pod removed from traffic → fewer pods → more load → more timeouts → cascade!

✅ Fix: Set timeout to at least 2-3× your normal response time. For DB checks, use 5s.

❌ Same Endpoint for Both Probes

Using /healthz for both liveness AND readiness → if the endpoint is designed to check dependencies, liveness will restart pods when dependencies fail (wrong!) → or if it's too simple, readiness won't catch real issues.

✅ Fix: Use /healthz (simple) for liveness, /readyz (thorough) for readiness.

❌ No Startup Probe for Slow Apps

App takes 45s to run migrations on start → liveness probe starts checking at 10s → 3 failures by 40s → pod killed before it finishes starting → CrashLoopBackOff → app never starts!

✅ Fix: Add startup probe with high failureThreshold to give slow apps time to start.

💡 The Golden Rules of Probes
  • Liveness = internal only — is the process alive? (never check external dependencies)
  • Readiness = external dependencies — can I serve traffic? (check DB, cache, APIs)
  • Startup = slow starters — block other probes until the app is initialized
  • Keep probes lightweight — they run every few seconds, don't add expensive logic
  • Different endpoints — /healthz for liveness, /readyz for readiness
📝 Chapter 16 Quick Reference
Command Purpose
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> See probe status and recent events
kubectl get events --field-selector reason=Unhealthy Find failed probe events
kubectl logs <pod-name> --previous Logs from the previous container (before restart)
kubectl get endpoints <service-name> Check which pods are in the Service (readiness)

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Health Probes

⏱️ Target: 7 minutes
  1. Create a Pod named healthy-app (image: nginx) with:
    • Liveness probe: HTTP GET on / port 80, check every 10s, 3 failure threshold
    • Readiness probe: HTTP GET on / port 80, initial delay 5s
  2. Create a Pod named slow-app (image: nginx) with a startup probe that allows up to 60s for startup (failureThreshold: 12, periodSeconds: 5)
  3. Create a Pod named exec-app (image: busybox, command: sleep 3600) with an exec liveness probe that checks if /tmp/healthy exists
  4. Verify probe statuses using kubectl describe pod
View Solution 
# 1. healthy-app with liveness + readiness
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: healthy-app
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 80
      periodSeconds: 10
      failureThreshold: 3
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 80
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
---
# 2. slow-app with startup probe
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: slow-app
spec:
  containers:
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx
    startupProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 80
      failureThreshold: 12
      periodSeconds: 5
---
# 3. exec-app with exec liveness
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: exec-app
spec:
  containers:
  - name: busybox
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    livenessProbe:
      exec:
        command: ["cat", "/tmp/healthy"]
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
      periodSeconds: 10
# 4. Verify
kubectl describe pod healthy-app | grep -A5 "Liveness\|Readiness"
kubectl describe pod slow-app | grep -A5 "Startup"
kubectl describe pod exec-app | grep -A5 "Liveness"

Chapter 16 — Practice Questions

Q1
What are the three types of probes in Kubernetes? When does each probe run and what happens when each fails?
Show Answer
Answer
ProbeWhen It RunsOn FailureUse For
livenessProbeAfter startup, continuouslyContainer is RESTARTEDDetecting deadlocks/hangs
readinessProbeContinuouslyRemoved from Service endpointsWarming up, dependency checks
startupProbeDuring startup onlyContainer is RESTARTEDSlow-starting applications
Explanation

Liveness = "is the process stuck?" (restart it). Readiness = "can it handle traffic right now?" (remove from load balancer). Startup = "has it finished initializing?" (delay liveness/readiness until ready). When startupProbe is configured, liveness and readiness probes are disabled until startup succeeds. This is CKAD core knowledge — expect at least one probe question.

Q2
Write a pod spec with an HTTP liveness probe that checks /healthz on port 8080 every 10 seconds, starting after 15 seconds.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-healthy
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
    livenessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 15    # Wait before first check
      periodSeconds: 10          # Check every 10s
      timeoutSeconds: 3          # Timeout per check
      failureThreshold: 3        # Restart after 3 failures
      successThreshold: 1        # 1 success = healthy
Explanation

HTTP probes check for status codes 200-399 = success, anything else = failure. initialDelaySeconds prevents false restarts during startup.
