  │  └─────────┬──────────────────┬───────────┘                        │
  │            │                  │                                    │
  │       ┌────▼─────┐       ┌────▼─────┐                             │
  │       │ 🔵 v1    │       │ 🟢 v2    │                             │
  │       │ Control  │       │ Experiment│                             │
  │       │ Group A  │       │ Group B   │                             │
  │       └──────────┘       └──────────┘                             │
  │                                                                    │
  │  📊 Compare metrics between groups:                                │
  │     • Conversion rate                                              │
  │     • Time on page                                                 │
  │     • Error rate                                                   │
  │     • Click-through rate                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/ab-test/ingress.yaml — A/B with NGINX Ingress 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-ab-test
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary: "true"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header: "X-Beta-User"
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header-value: "true"
spec:
  rules:
    - host: anihpj.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: anihpj-v2       # ← Beta users go here
                port:
                  number: 80
🎫 Header-Based

X-Beta-User: true
Best for: Internal testers, beta program users

🍪 Cookie-Based

ab_test=variant_b
Best for: Persistent user assignment across sessions

🌍 Geo-Based

Route by country/region
Best for: GDPR compliance, localized features

📝 A/B vs Canary

Canary: Random X% of traffic to new version. Goal: test stability.
A/B Testing: Specific users to specific version. Goal: compare user behavior. Both can coexist — use canary for deployment safety, A/B for feature experiments.

18.6 Updating anihpj Safely — Complete Walkthrough

Let's walk through a real update scenario: adding pagination to the /jobs/ endpoint in anihpj, from code change to production deployment.

🚀 Update Pipeline — Code to Production

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SAFE UPDATE PIPELINE                                              │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ① CODE         ② BUILD         ③ TEST          ④ DEPLOY         │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────     │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐        │
  │  │ views.py │   │ docker   │   │ kubectl  │   │ kubectl  │        │
  │  │ edit     │──▶│ build    │──▶│ test     │──▶│ rollout  │        │
  │  │          │   │ -t :v2   │   │          │   │          │        │
  │  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘        │
  │       │              │              │              │               │
  │       ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼               │
  │  Add pagination   Tag image     Run locally    Watch pods         │
  │  to jobpost_list  anihpj:v2     or staging     transition         │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ⑤ VERIFY        ⑥ MONITOR                                        │
  │  ─────────────────────────────                                     │
  │                                                                    │
  │  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐                                      │
  │  │ curl     │   │ kubectl  │                                      │
  │  │ test     │──▶│ logs     │   🎉 Done!                           │
  │  │ endpoint │   │ -f       │                                      │
  │  └──────────┘   └──────────┘                                      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Step 1: Make Code Change — jobpost/views.py 
# jobpost/views.py — add pagination
from django.core.paginator import Paginator
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

def jobpost_list(request):
    """API endpoint for job listings with pagination"""
    jobs = JobPost.objects.all().order_by('-created_at')
    
    # NEW: Add pagination (10 jobs per page)
    paginator = Paginator(jobs, 10)
    page = request.GET.get('page', 1)
    jobs_page = paginator.get_page(page)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        'jobs': [
            {
                'id': job.id,
                'title': job.title,
                'location': str(job.location),
                'author': job.author.name,
            }
            for job in jobs_page
        ],
        'pagination': {
            'current_page': jobs_page.number,
            'total_pages': paginator.num_pages,
            'has_next': jobs_page.has_next(),
            'has_previous': jobs_page.has_previous(),
        }
    })
Steps 2-6: Build, Deploy, Verify 
# ② Build new image with version tag
docker build -t anihpj:v2 .

# ③ (Optional) Test locally first
docker run -p 8000:8000 anihpj:v2
curl http://localhost:8000/jobs/?page=1

# ④ Deploy to Kubernetes
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj \
  anihpj=anihpj:v2 \
  -n anihpj-app

# Record why this deployment was made
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Watch the rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish:
#   2 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
#   3 out of 3 new replicas have been updated...
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# ⑤ Verify the new feature
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=1"

# ⑥ Monitor logs for errors
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=50 -f

# Check deployment history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
💡 Pro Tip: Always Annotate Changes

Use kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation to document what each deployment revision contains. When you need to rollback weeks later, you'll know exactly what each revision does — invaluable for debugging production issues.

18.7 Deployment Strategy Selection Guide

Choosing the right deployment strategy depends on your requirements: downtime tolerance, rollback speed, risk level, and resource budget.

Strategy Downtime Risk Cost Rollback Best For
🔄 Rolling None Low Minimal Slow Most deployments
🛑 Recreate Yes Medium Same N/A Schema changes
🔵🟢 Blue-Green None Very Low 2× Instant Critical apps
🐦 Canary None Lowest +10-20% Fast High-risk updates
🎯 A/B Test None Low Variable Fast Feature experiments

🧭 Strategy Decision Flowchart

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  WHICH DEPLOYMENT STRATEGY SHOULD YOU USE?                          │
  │                                                                      │
  │                  ┌────────────────────────┐                         │
  │                  │ Can old + new versions │                         │
  │                  │ coexist?                │                         │
  │                  └───────────┬────────────┘                         │
  │                         NO   │   YES                                 │
  │                        ┌─────┴─────┐                                 │
  │                        │           │                                 │
  │                        ▼           ▼                                 │
  │               ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────────────┐                │
  │               │ 🛑 RECREATE │  │ Is this a high-risk│                │
  │               │ (downtime) │  │ change?            │                │
  │               └────────────┘  └─────────┬──────────┘                │
  │                                    NO   │   YES                      │
  │                                   ┌─────┴─────┐                      │
  │                                   │           │                      │
  │                                   ▼           ▼                      │
  │                          ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐          │
  │                          │ 🔄 ROLLING │  │ Need instant  │          │
  │                          │ (default)  │  │ rollback?     │          │
  │                          └────────────┘  └──────┬────────┘          │
  │                                            NO   │   YES              │
  │                                           ┌─────┴─────┐              │
  │                                           │           │              │
  │                                           ▼           ▼              │
  │                                   ┌────────────┐  ┌───────────┐     │
  │                                   │ 🐦 CANARY  │  │ 🔵🟢 BLUE │     │
  │                                   │ (10% test) │  │    GREEN  │     │
  │                                   └────────────┘  └───────────┘     │
  │                                                                      │
  │  💡 Default recommendation: Rolling Update (simple, zero-downtime)  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ For anihpj Job Board

Use Rolling Update for most updates. It's simple, zero-downtime, and sufficient for a typical web app. Add readiness probes for safety.

🔄 For anihpj DB Migrations

Use a Job for migrations before the Deployment update. Separate the migration from the app update to avoid coexistence issues.

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Deployment Strategies

⏱️ Target Time: 7 minutes
Scenario: You manage a Deployment called webapp in namespace production. Complete the following tasks:
  1. Create a Deployment webapp with image nginx:1.21, 4 replicas, in namespace production
  2. Configure the Deployment with maxSurge: 2 and maxUnavailable: 1
  3. Update the image to nginx:1.22 and watch the rollout
  4. Change the strategy to Recreate and update to nginx:1.23
  5. Verify: During the Recreate update, confirm ALL old pods terminate before new ones start
💡 Hint: Use kubectl set image for updates and kubectl patch to change strategy type. Watch with kubectl rollout status.
📖 View Solution
Solution — Deployment Strategies Drill 
# 1. Create namespace and deployment
kubectl create namespace production
kubectl create deployment webapp --image=nginx:1.21 --replicas=4 -n production

# 2. Patch rolling update parameters
kubectl patch deployment webapp -n production -p '{
  "spec": {
    "strategy": {
      "type": "RollingUpdate",
      "rollingUpdate": {
        "maxSurge": 2,
        "maxUnavailable": 1
      }
    }
  }
}'

# Verify strategy
kubectl get deployment webapp -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.strategy}' | jq .

# 3. Update image and watch rollout
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.22 -n production
kubectl rollout status deployment/webapp -n production

# 4. Change to Recreate strategy
kubectl patch deployment webapp -n production -p '{
  "spec": {
    "strategy": {
      "type": "Recreate"
    }
  }
}'

# Update image
kubectl set image deployment/webapp nginx=nginx:1.23 -n production

# 5. Watch — all old pods terminate first, then new ones start
kubectl get pods -n production -w
# You'll see: old pods → Terminating → 0 running → new pods → Running

Chapter 18 — Practice Questions

Q1
What are the two built-in deployment strategies in Kubernetes? Compare them.
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureRollingUpdate (default)Recreate
DowntimeZero downtimeBrief downtime
ProcessGradually replace podsKill all old → start all new
Two versions running?Yes, temporarilyNo
Use whenMost workloadsCan't run 2 versions simultaneously
Explanation

RollingUpdate is the default — it gradually replaces old pods with new ones. Recreate kills all existing pods first, then creates new ones (causes downtime). Use Recreate when: (1) your app can't handle two versions simultaneously, (2) schema changes require all pods on the same version, (3) volume is RWO and can't be shared. The CKAD tests both strategies.

Q2
Configure a deployment with RollingUpdate strategy: maximum 1 pod unavailable, maximum 2 extra pods during update.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 4
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1     # At most 1 pod down during update
      maxSurge: 2           # At most 2 extra pods during update
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: nginx:1.24
Explanation

maxUnavailable = how many pods can be down during update (absolute number or percentage). maxSurge = how many extra pods can be created above desired count. With 4 replicas, maxUnavailable: 1 means at least 3 pods serve traffic during update. maxSurge: 2 means up to 6 pods temporarily exist. Defaults: maxUnavailable: 25%, maxSurge: 25%. CKAD frequently tests these parameters.

Q3
Update the image of a deployment named web-app from nginx:1.23 to nginx:1.24 while recording the change. Show multiple methods.
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl set image (fastest for CKAD)
kubectl set image deployment/web-app web=nginx:1.24

# Method 2: kubectl edit (interactive)
kubectl edit deployment web-app
# Change image in the editor

# Method 3: kubectl patch
kubectl patch deployment web-app -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"web","image":"nginx:1.24"}]}}}}'

# Record the change reason (deprecated but still tested)
kubectl annotate deployment web-app \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Update to nginx 1.24"

# Monitor rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app
Explanation

kubectl set image is the CKAD speed champion. The --record flag is deprecated since K8s 1.24 but the concept is tested — use annotations instead. Any change to spec.template triggers a new rollout. Changes to spec.replicas or metadata do NOT trigger rollouts. rollout status blocks until complete — useful for scripts.

Q4
What is a blue/green deployment? How would you implement it in Kubernetes without third-party tools?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: "Blue" deployment is running (current)
kubectl get deployment web-blue  # version: v1

# Step 2: Create "Green" deployment (new version)
kubectl create deployment web-green --image=myapp:v2 --replicas=3

# Step 3: Test green independently
kubectl expose deployment web-green --port=80 --name=web-green-test

# Step 4: Switch the main Service selector to green
kubectl patch svc web-main -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"web-green"}}}'

# Step 5: Rollback if needed — switch back to blue
kubectl patch svc web-main -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"app":"web-blue"}}}'

# Step 6: Clean up old blue deployment after validation
kubectl delete deployment web-blue
  ┌──────────┐         ┌──────────┐
  │  Blue v1 │◄──┐     │ Green v2 │
  │ (current)│   │     │  (new)   │
  └──────────┘   │     └────┬─────┘
                 │          │
           ┌─────┴────┐    │    After switch:
           │  Service  ├────┘    Service → Green
           └──────────┘
Explanation

Blue/green is NOT a native Kubernetes strategy — it's implemented by creating two Deployments and switching the Service selector. Advantages: instant rollback (switch selector back), full testing before traffic switch. Disadvantage: temporarily runs 2x the pods (cost). On the CKAD, this tests understanding of how Services decouple routing from deployments.

Q5
How do you pause and resume a rolling update? When is this useful?
Show Answer
Answer
# Start an update
kubectl set image deployment/web-app web=nginx:1.24

# Pause mid-rollout (canary-like testing)
kubectl rollout pause deployment/web-app
# Some pods are v1, some are v2 — both serve traffic

# Test the new version pods
kubectl get pods -o wide  # Check which pods have new image
curl <new-pod-ip>:8080    # Verify functionality

# Resume if everything looks good
kubectl rollout resume deployment/web-app

# Or rollback if issues found
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app
Explanation

Pause + resume = poor man's canary deployment. When paused, the rollout stops creating/deleting pods, keeping a mix of old and new versions. This lets you test the new version with production traffic at reduced capacity. Multiple kubectl set commands while paused are batched into a single rollout on resume. The CKAD may test pause/resume as part of a multi-step deployment scenario.

Q6
Configure a Deployment to use the Recreate strategy. When exactly would you choose this over RollingUpdate?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: legacy-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  strategy:
    type: Recreate    # No rollingUpdate section needed
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: legacy
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: legacy
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: legacy-app:v2

Use Recreate when:

  • App can't have 2 versions running (database schema conflicts)
  • Volume is RWO (ReadWriteOnce) — only one pod can mount it
  • Application has exclusive locks or singleton behavior
  • Legacy apps that don't support graceful handoff
Explanation

With Recreate, do NOT include a rollingUpdate section — it's only for RollingUpdate type. The sequence is: scale to 0 → wait for all pods to terminate → scale to desired replicas. Downtime = termination time + startup time. On the CKAD, questions about RWO volumes often lead to Recreate strategy as the answer.

Q7
What is minReadySeconds and how does it affect deployment rollouts?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: careful-app
spec:
  minReadySeconds: 30    # Pod must be ready for 30s before considered "available"
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0
      maxSurge: 1
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: webapp:v2
Explanation

minReadySeconds adds a delay: a new pod must pass readiness probes for this many seconds before Kubernetes considers it "available" and continues the rollout. This catches pods that start successfully but crash after a few seconds. Default is 0 (pod is available as soon as ready). Combined with maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0, this creates a very conservative rollout — ideal for production.

Q8
What is progressDeadlineSeconds? What happens when a deployment exceeds it?
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 600    # 10 minutes (default)
  # If no progress for 600s, deployment is marked "Failed"
# Check deployment condition
kubectl get deployment web-app -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
# Type: Progressing, Status: False, Reason: ProgressDeadlineExceeded

# This does NOT rollback automatically!
# You must manually:
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app
Explanation

progressDeadlineSeconds is a watchdog timer. If the rollout makes no progress (no pods becoming ready) for this duration, the deployment status is set to False with reason ProgressDeadlineExceeded. Important: it does NOT automatically rollback — it just reports failure. You must check status and rollback manually. On the CKAD, this tests understanding of deployment failure detection.

Q9
How do you implement a canary deployment in Kubernetes? Show the steps.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Main deployment (stable, 4 replicas)
kubectl create deployment web-stable --image=myapp:v1 --replicas=4
kubectl label deployment web-stable track=stable

# Step 2: Create canary (new version, 1 replica)
kubectl create deployment web-canary --image=myapp:v2 --replicas=1
kubectl label deployment web-canary track=canary

# Step 3: Both deployments must share the SAME label for the Service
# Both need: app=web
kubectl label pods -l app=web-stable app=web --overwrite
kubectl label pods -l app=web-canary app=web --overwrite

# Step 4: Service selects by shared label (routes to both)
kubectl expose deployment web-stable --name=web-svc \
  --port=80 --selector=app=web

# Result: ~80% traffic → stable (4 pods), ~20% → canary (1 pod)

# Step 5: If canary is healthy, scale up canary, scale down stable
kubectl scale deployment web-canary --replicas=5
kubectl delete deployment web-stable
Explanation

Native canary is achieved via two Deployments sharing a Service selector. Traffic split is proportional to pod count: 4 stable + 1 canary ≈ 80/20 split. For precise traffic splitting (e.g., 5% canary), you need a service mesh (Istio). On the CKAD, the concept matters more than exact implementation — understand the shared-label-Service pattern.

Q10
You update a deployment image but the new pods keep crashing. The rollout is stuck. What do you do?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app
# Waiting for deployment "web-app" rollout to finish...

# 2. Check why new pods are failing
kubectl get pods  # Shows CrashLoopBackOff
kubectl logs web-app-NEW-POD --previous
kubectl describe pod web-app-NEW-POD

# 3. Immediate rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app

# 4. Verify rollback completed
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app
# deployment "web-app" successfully rolled out

# 5. Check revision history to confirm
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app
Explanation

During a stuck rollout with maxUnavailable: 0, old pods keep serving traffic (zero downtime). The new pods crash and restart but old pods are untouched. kubectl rollout undo reverts to the previous working revision. This is the CKAD's primary rollback scenario. Always check logs before rolling back to understand the failure — it could be a config issue, not an image issue.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Secure Deployment Strategies

SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY 20% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Deployment strategies impact security — rolling updates can mix old/new versions with different security postures, and canary deployments should include security validation gates.

StrategySecurity ConsiderationBest Practice
Rolling UpdateMixed versions during rollout may have different CVEsUse image digest (sha256), not tags
RecreateBrief downtime but ensures clean cutoverUse for security-critical updates
CanaryNew version exposed to limited trafficValidate security posture before full rollout
Blue-GreenOld version persists — potential stale secretsClean up old deployment resources promptly
 
# Secure deployment — image digest pinning
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0      # Zero downtime
      maxSurge: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        # Use SHA256 digest — NOT mutable tags
        image: registry.example.com/app@sha256:abc123def456...
        imagePullPolicy: Always
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: registry-cred   # Authenticated registry pull

Chapter 18 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A deployment uses image: nginx:latest. Fix it to use a pinned image digest for supply chain security.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Get the digest for the desired version
docker inspect --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}' nginx:1.25
# OR
crane digest nginx:1.25
# Output: sha256:6a5dff115...

# 2. Update the deployment
kubectl set image deployment/web \
  nginx=nginx@sha256:6a5dff115... \
  -n production

# 3. Verify
kubectl get deploy web -n production -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
Explanation

Tags like :latest are mutable — an attacker who compromises the registry can replace the image. Digests (@sha256:) are immutable, guaranteeing you run exactly the verified image. On CKS, always pin images by digest and use imagePullPolicy: Always with private registries.

CKS-2
How do you verify that a new deployment version passes security checks before completing the rollout?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Pause the rollout after initial canary
kubectl rollout pause deployment/web -n production

# 2. Run security checks on the new pods
NEW_POD=$(kubectl get pods -n production -l app=web \
  --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp -o jsonpath='{.items[-1].metadata.name}')

# Check for CVEs in running image
kubectl exec $NEW_POD -- cat /etc/os-release
trivy image $(kubectl get pod $NEW_POD -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}')

# 3. If secure, resume rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/web -n production

# If insecure, rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web -n production
Explanation

Using kubectl rollout pause lets you inspect the new version before it fully rolls out. Scan the new pods for vulnerabilities, verify security contexts, and check network policies. If anything is wrong, rollout undo reverts. On CKS, this pattern is used for security-gated deployments.

CKS-3
How do you ensure only signed images can be deployed using an admission controller?
Show Answer
Answer
# ImagePolicyWebhook configuration
# /etc/kubernetes/admission-control/admission-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: AdmissionConfiguration
plugins:
- name: ImagePolicyWebhook
  configuration:
    imagePolicy:
      kubeConfigFile: /etc/kubernetes/admission-control/webhook-kubeconfig
      allowTTL: 50
      denyTTL: 50
      retryBackoff: 500
      defaultAllow: false   # DENY by default if webhook unreachable
# Enable in API server
# --enable-admission-plugins=...,ImagePolicyWebhook
# --admission-control-config-file=/etc/kubernetes/admission-control/admission-config.yaml
Explanation

ImagePolicyWebhook calls an external webhook to verify image signatures before allowing pod creation. Setting defaultAllow: false is critical — it denies all images if the webhook is unreachable (fail-closed). On CKS, know how to configure ImagePolicyWebhook and its fail-open vs fail-closed behavior.

CKS-4
During a rolling update, how do you ensure the new pods maintain the same security context as the old pods?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Before update — capture current security context
kubectl get deploy web -n production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.securityContext}' | jq .
kubectl get deploy web -n production \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].securityContext}' | jq .

# 2. After update — compare
kubectl diff -f updated-deployment.yaml | grep -A10 securityContext

# 3. Validate with Pod Security Admission
# Ensure namespace has PSA labels:
kubectl label ns production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  --overwrite
Explanation

Security context regression during updates is a common vulnerability. A developer might remove runAsNonRoot or add capabilities in a new version. On CKS, use PSA labels on namespaces to enforce security standards — any pod violating the policy (e.g., running as root) is rejected at admission.

CKS-5
How do you configure a deployment to automatically rollback if the new version fails health checks?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: auto-rollback-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  progressDeadlineSeconds: 120   # Rollout must complete in 2 min
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 0          # Keep old pods running
      maxSurge: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: app@sha256:secure-digest...
        readinessProbe:          # Gate for traffic
          httpGet:
            path: /ready
            port: 8080
          periodSeconds: 5
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:           # Restart unhealthy
          httpGet:
            path: /healthz
            port: 8080
# If rollout exceeds progressDeadlineSeconds:
kubectl rollout status deploy/auto-rollback-app
# "error: deployment exceeded its progress deadline"
kubectl rollout undo deploy/auto-rollback-app
Explanation

progressDeadlineSeconds sets a timeout for the rollout — if new pods don't become ready in time, the deployment is marked "failed". Combined with maxUnavailable: 0, old pods keep serving traffic. On CKS, this prevents a compromised image from fully replacing healthy pods.

Chapter 19: Rollbacks and Version Management

Deployments don't always go as planned. Learn how Kubernetes tracks deployment history, how to roll back to previous versions instantly, and best practices for image tagging and change tracking.

🔙 Rollbacks 📜 Revision History 🏷️ Image Tagging 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ How Kubernetes tracks deployment revision history
  • ✅ Rolling back to previous and specific versions
  • ✅ Pausing and resuming rollouts for inspection
  • ✅ Image tagging strategies for production
  • ✅ Annotating deployments with change causes
  • ✅ Hands-on: Update and rollback anihpj
🚀 CKAD Domain 2: Application Deployment 20% of Exam

Rollbacks are a guaranteed CKAD exam topic. You WILL be asked to roll back a broken deployment. Master kubectl rollout undo — it's your emergency brake.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: Rollback Commands — Know These Cold

These commands appear on almost every CKAD exam. Practice until they're muscle memory:

  • kubectl rollout history deployment/NAME — view all revisions
  • kubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME — rollback to previous revision
  • kubectl rollout undo deployment/NAME --to-revision=N — rollback to specific revision
  • kubectl rollout status deployment/NAME — watch rollout progress
  • kubectl rollout pause/resume deployment/NAME — pause mid-rollout
  • Always use --record flag (or kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation) to track WHY

⚡ SPEED TIP: Instant Rollback Pattern

# Step 1: See what's deployed
kubectl rollout history deploy/myapp

# Step 2: Something broke? Undo immediately
kubectl rollout undo deploy/myapp

# Step 3: Need a specific version? Target it
kubectl rollout undo deploy/myapp --to-revision=2

# Step 4: Verify it worked
kubectl rollout status deploy/myapp

Rollback & Versioning Commands

ActionCommand
View revision historykubectl rollout history deploy/web
View specific revisionkubectl rollout history deploy/web --revision=2
Rollback to previouskubectl rollout undo deploy/web
Rollback to specifickubectl rollout undo deploy/web --to-revision=2
Annotate change causekubectl annotate deploy/web kubernetes.io/change-cause="upgraded to v2"
Pause rolloutkubectl rollout pause deploy/web
Resume rolloutkubectl rollout resume deploy/web
Check rollout statuskubectl rollout status deploy/web
Set revision limitkubectl patch deploy/web -p '{"spec":{"revisionHistoryLimit":5}}'

Rollback Traps

  • ❌ --record is deprecated in v1.24+ — Use kubectl annotate deploy/web kubernetes.io/change-cause="reason" instead
  • Rollback IS a rollout — it creates a NEW revision pointing to old ReplicaSet config
  • revisionHistoryLimit defaults to 10. Set to 0 = can't rollback at all!
  • ❌ Editing a paused Deployment: Changes accumulate but DON'T apply until kubectl rollout resume
  • kubectl rollout undo only works on Deployments/StatefulSets/DaemonSets, NOT on standalone pods

⏳ Rollback Decision Timeline

Deploy kubectl set image deploy/web nginx=nginx:1.25

New rollout starts. Revision 3 created. Old RS scales down, new RS scales up.

Detect kubectl rollout status deploy/web — stuck!

Pods crashing. CrashLoopBackOff or ImagePullBackOff. Rollout is stalled.

Decide Check history: kubectl rollout history deploy/web

Identify which revision was last working. Review change-cause annotations.

Rollback kubectl rollout undo deploy/web --to-revision=2

Kubernetes creates Revision 4 using Revision 2's template. Zero-downtime recovery.

10 Default revisionHistoryLimit
undo Instant Rollback Command
pause Batch Changes Together
0 History=0 = No Rollback!

19.1 Deployment Revision History

Every time you update a Deployment, Kubernetes creates a new revision and stores the previous one. This history allows you to roll back to any previous state. By default, Kubernetes keeps the last 10 revisions.

📜 Revision History Timeline

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT REVISION HISTORY (anihpj)                                │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Rev 1         Rev 2            Rev 3          Rev 4 (current)       │
  │    ●─────────────●────────────────●───────────────●                  │
  │    │             │                │               │                   │
  │  v1.0.0        v1.1.0          v1.2.0          v1.2.1                │
  │  Initial       Added           Added           Bug fix               │
  │  deploy        pagination      filtering       for /jobs/            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  $ kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj                 │    │
  │  │                                                               │    │
  │  │  REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE                                       │    │
  │  │  1         Initial deployment                                 │    │
  │  │  2         v1.1.0: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint        │    │
  │  │  3         v1.2.0: Added filtering by skills                  │    │
  │  │  4         v1.2.1: Fixed null location bug                    │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Rollback creates a NEW revision (history is append-only)         │
  │     Rolling back to Rev 2 would create Rev 5 (copy of Rev 2)         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Viewing Deployment History 
# View all revisions
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app

# View details of a specific revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj --revision=2 -n anihpj-app
# Output shows the pod template for that revision:
# Pod Template:
#   Labels:       app=anihpj
#   Containers:
#    anihpj:
#     Image:      anihpj:v1.1.0
#     Port:       8000/TCP
#     ...

# Check current revision number
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app | grep "revision"
# Annotations:  deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: 4
Configure Revision History Limit 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10        # ← Keep last 10 revisions (default)
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    # ...
💡 Storage Consideration

Each revision stores a complete ReplicaSet object. For clusters with many deployments, consider lowering revisionHistoryLimit to 3-5 to reduce etcd storage usage. For critical applications, keep it at 10+ for more rollback options.

19.2 Rolling Back

When a deployment goes wrong — bugs, crashes, performance issues — you can instantly roll back to a known-good state. Kubernetes makes this as simple as one command.

🔙 Rollback Flow — Revert to Previous State

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  ROLLBACK SCENARIO                                                    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Current: Rev 4 (v1.2.1) — 🐞 BROKEN! 500 errors everywhere          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────┐     kubectl rollout undo     ┌──────────┐              │
  │  │ Rev 4    │ ─────────────────────────▶  │ Rev 5    │              │
  │  │ v1.2.1   │       (creates new rev)     │ (=Rev 3) │              │
  │  │ 🐞 broken │                             │ v1.2.0   │              │
  │  └──────────┘                              │ ✅ works  │              │
  │                                            └──────────┘              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  What happens internally:                                            │
  │  1. K8s looks up Rev 3's ReplicaSet template                         │
  │  2. Creates new pods using that template                             │
  │  3. Terminates Rev 4 pods (rolling update style)                     │
  │  4. Records this as Rev 5 (history is append-only)                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Rev 5 is a COPY of Rev 3's config, not a pointer                 │
  │     If you rolled back again, you'd get Rev 6 (copy of Rev 4)        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

To Previous Version (One Step Back)

The most common rollback — go back to the immediately previous revision.

Rollback to Previous Revision 
# Emergency! Current version is broken. Roll back NOW!
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app

# Output:
# deployment.apps/anihpj rolled back

# Watch the rollback in action
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -w
# anihpj-6c9a2e8d3-xxx   0/1   Terminating    ← broken v4 pods dying
# anihpj-7d4b8c6f5-yyy   1/1   Running        ← good v3 pods starting

To a Specific Revision

Sometimes you need to go back multiple versions — maybe the last 2 versions were both broken.

Rollback to Specific Revision 
# First, check what revisions are available
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment  
# 2         v1.1.0: Added pagination ◀── this one was stable!
# 3         v1.2.0: Added filtering (broke search)
# 4         v1.2.1: Fix attempt (still broken)

# Roll back to revision 2 (the last known-good state)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2 -n anihpj-app

# Verify which image is now running
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1.1.0 ✅

Verifying the Rollback

Always verify that the rollback was successful and the application is healthy.

Verify Rollback Success 
# 1. Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# 2. Verify all pods are running the correct version
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -o wide
kubectl describe deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app | grep Image
# Image: anihpj:v1.1.0

# 3. Test the application
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/

# 4. Check logs for errors
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=20

# 5. Verify revision history (rollback created new revision)
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 3         v1.2.0: Added filtering
# 4         v1.2.1: Fix attempt
# 5         Rollback to revision 2   ◀── NEW
⚠️ Rollback Doesn't Fix the Code

Rollback is an emergency measure, not a solution. After stabilizing with a rollback, you still need to fix the actual bug, test properly, and deploy a corrected version. The broken code is still in your repository!

🚨 Emergency Rollback Checklist

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SHOULD I ROLL BACK?                                                  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  🚨 IMMEDIATE ROLLBACK (don't wait):                                  │
  │  ├── 500 errors spiking on monitoring                                │
  │  ├── Users reporting unable to load pages                            │
  │  ├── Error rate > 1% (or your SLO threshold)                         │
  │  ├── Pods in CrashLoopBackOff                                        │
  │  └── Memory/CPU 100% on new pods                                      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ⏳ INVESTIGATE FIRST (can wait):                                      │
  │  ├── Slightly slower response times (< 2x)                           │
  │  ├── Warning logs (but no errors)                                    │
  │  ├── One pod restarted once                                          │
  │  └── User reports but you can't reproduce                            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ✅ DON'T ROLL BACK (expected behavior):                              │
  │  ├── Deployment in progress (some pods terminating)                  │
  │  ├── Brief spike during transition                                   │
  │  └── Old revision pods gracefully shutting down                      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │  ROLLBACK COMMAND:                                          │     │
  │  │  kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app       │     │
  │  │                                                              │     │
  │  │  TO SPECIFIC VERSION:                                        │     │
  │  │  kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=N     │     │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

19.3 Pausing and Resuming Rollouts

You can pause a deployment mid-rollout to inspect its state before continuing. This is useful for canary-like testing within a rolling update, or to batch multiple changes together.

⏸️ Pause/Resume — Mid-Rollout Control

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PAUSED ROLLOUT — Partial Deployment                                 │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Before pause (mid-rollout):                                         │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   2 old, 2 new        │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ pending│                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  $ kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj                           │
  │  ⏸️ Rollout paused!                                                   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  State is FROZEN:                                                    │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v1  🔵 │ │ v1  🔵 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   ← stays like this   │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  You can now:                                                        │
  │  • Test v2 pods: Are they working correctly?                         │
  │  • Check logs: Any errors in the new version?                        │
  │  • Monitor metrics: CPU/memory normal?                               │
  │  • Make MORE changes (they batch up until resume)                    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  $ kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj                          │
  │  ▶️ Rollout continues...                                              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  After resume (complete):                                            │
  │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐                        │
  │  │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │ │ v2  🟢 │   all new ✅          │
  │  │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │ │ ready  │                        │
  │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Pause and Resume Commands 
# Start an update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Pause mid-rollout
kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj paused

# Check status (shows "paused")
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# Waiting for deployment "anihpj" rollout to finish: 
#   2 out of 4 new replicas have been updated (paused)

# While paused, you can make additional changes (they batch up):
kubectl set env deployment/anihpj DEBUG=false -n anihpj-app
kubectl set resources deployment/anihpj -c anihpj \
  --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi -n anihpj-app

# Resume the rollout (all changes apply together)
kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj resumed
✅ Good Use Cases
  • Inspect partial rollout before continuing
  • Batch multiple changes into one rollout
  • Wait for approval during deployment
❌ Not Recommended
  • Leaving deployments paused for long periods
  • Using pause as a "canary" replacement
  • Pausing with no plan to resume

19.4 Image Tagging and Version Strategy

How you tag your container images directly impacts your ability to track, reproduce, and roll back deployments. Never use :latest in production — it's impossible to know which actual build is running.

🏷️ Image Tagging Strategies

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  IMAGE TAGGING STRATEGIES                                           │
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐│
  │  │ ❌ NEVER USE      │  │ ✅ SEMANTIC       │  │ ✅ GIT SHA        ││
  │  │                   │  │    VERSIONING     │  │                   ││
  │  │  anihpj:latest    │  │  anihpj:1.2.3     │  │  anihpj:abc123f   ││
  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   ││
  │  │ • Changes without │  │ • Major.Minor.    │  │ • Exact git commit││
  │  │   warning         │  │   Patch           │  │ • Perfect for CI  ││
  │  │ • No traceability │  │ • Clear intent    │  │ • Full traceability│
  │  │ • Impossible to   │  │ • Easy to compare │  │ • Not human-      ││
  │  │   reproduce       │  │ • Team standard   │  │   readable        ││
  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   ││
  │  │ What's running?   │  │ What's running?   │  │ What's running?   ││
  │  │ 🤷 No idea        │  │ v1.2.3 ✅         │  │ commit abc123f ✅ ││
  │  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘│
  │                                                                      │
  │  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐                       │
  │  │ ✅ DATE-BASED     │  │ ✅ COMBINED       │                       │
  │  │                   │  │                   │                       │
  │  │  anihpj:2026-03-28│  │  anihpj:1.2.3-    │                       │
  │  │                   │  │  abc123f          │                       │
  │  │ • Daily builds    │  │                   │                       │
  │  │ • Easy to age out │  │ • Best of both!   │                       │
  │  │ • No version info │  │ • SemVer + SHA    │                       │
  │  │                   │  │ • Recommended     │                       │
  │  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘                       │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Strategy Example Pros Cons Best For
❌ latest anihpj:latest Simple No traceability Never in prod
SemVer anihpj:1.2.3 Clear versioning Manual tagging Release versions
Git SHA anihpj:abc123f Exact commit Not human-readable CI/CD pipelines
Date anihpj:2026-03-28 Easy to age out No version info Daily builds
Combined anihpj:1.2.3-abc123f Best of both Longer tags Production ✅
Build with Proper Tags 
# Get version from git tag or package.json
VERSION=$(git describe --tags --always)
# e.g., v1.2.3 or abc123f (if no tag)

# Get short git SHA
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)

# Build with combined tag (recommended)
docker build -t anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA} .
# Result: anihpj:v1.2.3-abc123f

# Also tag as "latest" for local dev (but never deploy this!)
docker tag anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA} anihpj:latest

# Push to registry
docker push myregistry.com/anihpj:${VERSION}-${GIT_SHA}
🚨 Why :latest is Dangerous

:latest is a moving target. When you deploy anihpj:latest, Kubernetes might pull a different image on each node depending on cache. After a few days, you won't know what version is running. Rollback becomes impossible. Always use explicit version tags!

19.5 Change Cause Annotations

The kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation records why a deployment was updated. This annotation appears in rollout history, making it invaluable when you need to roll back weeks later.

Recording Change Causes 
# Method 1: Add annotation after deployment update
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0 -n anihpj-app
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Method 2: Use --record flag (deprecated but still works)
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0 \
  --record -n anihpj-app
# Records: kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v1.2.0

# View history with change causes
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         v1.1.0: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
# 3         v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills  ◀── descriptive!
YAML with Change Cause Annotation 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj-app
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/change-cause: "v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
✅ Good Change Causes
  • v1.2.0: Added job filtering by skills
  • Hotfix: Fixed null location crash
  • Security: Updated Django to 4.2.1
  • JIRA-1234: Pagination for /jobs/
❌ Bad Change Causes
  • update (too vague)
  • kubectl set image... (default record)
  • fixed stuff (not descriptive)
  • No annotation at all

19.6 Practice: Update and Rollback anihpj

Let's walk through a complete update-and-rollback scenario. We'll deploy a new version, discover a bug, roll back, fix the bug, and deploy again properly.

🎯 Practice Scenario Timeline

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SCENARIO: The Pagination Bug                                        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Step 1      Step 2        Step 3        Step 4       Step 5        │
  │    ●───────────●─────────────●─────────────●─────────────●          │
  │    │           │             │             │             │           │
  │  Deploy v1   Deploy v2     BUG!         Rollback      Deploy v3   │
  │  (original)  (pagination)  500 errors   to v1         (fixed)      │
  │              added         on page 2!   ✅ stable     ✅ verified   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  The Bug: pagination.get_page(page) fails when page > total │    │
  │  │  Fix: Add bounds checking before get_page()                  │    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Step 1: Verify Current Deployment (v1) 
# Check current state
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1

# Test the endpoint
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "count": 50}  ← Works! But no pagination
Step 2: Deploy v2 (with pagination) 
# Deploy the new version
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v2 -n anihpj-app

# Record what changed
kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Wait for rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out ✅
Step 3: Discover the Bug! 🐞 
# Test page 1 - works!
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=1"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "pagination": {"current_page": 1, ...}}

# Test page 2 - BUG!
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=999"
# Returns: 500 Internal Server Error! 💥

# Check logs
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj -n anihpj-app --tail=10
# InvalidPage: That page contains no results
Step 4: Emergency Rollback! 🚨 
# ROLLBACK NOW! Users are seeing 500 errors
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment.apps/anihpj rolled back

# Verify rollback completed
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# deployment "anihpj" successfully rolled out

# Verify we're back on v1
kubectl get deployment anihpj -n anihpj-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# anihpj:v1 ✅

# Test - should work now (no pagination, but no crash)
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...], "count": 50}  ← Stable again!
Step 5: Fix the Bug and Deploy v3 
# jobpost/views.py — FIXED version
from django.core.paginator import Paginator, EmptyPage
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

def jobpost_list(request):
    jobs = JobPost.objects.all().order_by('-created_at')
    paginator = Paginator(jobs, 10)
    
    try:
        page = int(request.GET.get('page', 1))
        # FIX: Bounds checking!
        page = max(1, min(page, paginator.num_pages))
        jobs_page = paginator.get_page(page)
    except (ValueError, EmptyPage):
        jobs_page = paginator.get_page(1)
    
    return JsonResponse({
        'jobs': [{'id': j.id, 'title': j.title} for j in jobs_page],
        'pagination': {
            'current_page': jobs_page.number,
            'total_pages': paginator.num_pages,
        }
    })
Deploy the Fix (v3) 
# Build fixed version
docker build -t anihpj:v3 .

# Deploy
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj anihpj=anihpj:v3 -n anihpj-app

kubectl annotate deployment/anihpj \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="v3: Fixed pagination crash on invalid page" \
  -n anihpj-app

# Verify
kubectl run curl-test --image=curlimages/curl --rm -it -- \
  curl "http://anihpj-svc.anihpj-app:8000/jobs/?page=999"
# Returns: {"jobs": [...of last page...], ...}  ← Fixed! ✅

# Final revision history
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj -n anihpj-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 3         v2: Added pagination to /jobs/ endpoint
# 4         Rollback to revision 1
# 5         v3: Fixed pagination crash on invalid page  ← Latest
💡 Lessons Learned
  • Always test edge cases before deploying (page 0, page 999, negative pages)
  • Rollback is your emergency brake — use it quickly when things break
  • Change causes help you understand revision history weeks later
  • Version tags (v1, v2, v3) let you track and rollback precisely

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Rollbacks & Version Management

⏱️ Target Time: 6 minutes
Scenario: A deployment api-server in namespace staging was updated with a bad image that crashes. Fix it using rollback commands.
  1. Create deployment api-server with image nginx:1.20, 3 replicas, in namespace staging
  2. Update to nginx:1.21 with change-cause annotation "upgrade to 1.21"
  3. Update to a broken image nginx:broken-tag with change-cause "broken update"
  4. Verify the rollout is stuck (pods in ImagePullBackOff)
  5. View the rollout history and roll back to the last working version
  6. Verify all pods are running again with the correct image
💡 Hint: Use kubectl annotate with kubernetes.io/change-cause to track changes. Use kubectl rollout undo for rollback.
📖 View Solution
Solution — Rollbacks Drill 
# 1. Create namespace and deployment
kubectl create namespace staging
kubectl create deployment api-server --image=nginx:1.20 --replicas=3 -n staging
kubectl rollout status deployment/api-server -n staging

# 2. Update to 1.21 with annotation
kubectl set image deployment/api-server nginx=nginx:1.21 -n staging
kubectl annotate deployment/api-server -n staging \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="upgrade to 1.21" --overwrite
kubectl rollout status deployment/api-server -n staging

# 3. Update to broken image
kubectl set image deployment/api-server nginx=nginx:broken-tag -n staging
kubectl annotate deployment/api-server -n staging \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="broken update" --overwrite

# 4. Verify rollout is stuck
kubectl rollout status deployment/api-server -n staging
# Waiting... (Ctrl+C to exit)
kubectl get pods -n staging
# Shows: ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull

# 5. View history and rollback
kubectl rollout history deployment/api-server -n staging
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         
# 2         upgrade to 1.21
# 3         broken update

kubectl rollout undo deployment/api-server -n staging
# deployment.apps/api-server rolled back

# 6. Verify recovery
kubectl rollout status deployment/api-server -n staging
kubectl get pods -n staging       # All Running ✅
kubectl get deployment api-server -n staging \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# nginx:1.21 ✅

Chapter 19 — Practice Questions

Q1
How do you view the rollout history of a deployment? What information does each revision contain?
Show Answer
Answer
# View all revisions
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         Updated to nginx:1.24
# 3         Added readiness probe

# View specific revision details
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app --revision=2
# Shows full pod template spec for that revision
Explanation

Each revision stores a complete snapshot of the ReplicaSet's pod template. --revision=N shows the full container spec, image, env vars, probes, etc. for that revision. The change-cause column shows annotations (previously set with --record, now use kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation). On the CKAD, rollout history is used to identify which revision to rollback to.

Q2
Roll back a deployment to the previous version. Then roll back to a specific revision (revision 2).
Show Answer
Answer
# Rollback to previous version (revision N-1)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app

# Rollback to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app --to-revision=2

# Verify the rollback
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app
kubectl describe deployment web-app | grep Image

# Check: rollback creates a NEW revision
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app
# REVISION 4 now contains the same spec as REVISION 2
Explanation

rollout undo without --to-revision goes back one step. With --to-revision=N it restores that exact spec. Important: a rollback creates a NEW revision number (it doesn't "go back" — it copies forward). So if you had revisions 1,2,3 and undo to 2, you get revision 4 with the same spec as 2. The CKAD tests both forms of rollback.

Q3
What is revisionHistoryLimit? How do you configure it and what's the impact?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
spec:
  revisionHistoryLimit: 5    # Keep only 5 old ReplicaSets (default: 10)
  template:
    # ...
# See old ReplicaSets (rollback targets)
kubectl get rs
# web-app-abc123   0     (old revision — kept for rollback)
# web-app-def456   0     (old revision — kept for rollback)
# web-app-ghi789   3     (current active)

# Setting to 0: no rollback possible!
# Setting too high: many idle ReplicaSets waste etcd storage
Explanation

Each rollout creates a new ReplicaSet. revisionHistoryLimit controls how many OLD ReplicaSets are kept (scaled to 0 replicas). These are rollback targets — if you set it to 0, you can't rollback. Default is 10. On the CKAD, know that old ReplicaSets are visible with kubectl get rs — the ones with 0 desired replicas are rollback candidates.

Q4
What is the relationship between a Deployment, ReplicaSet, and Pod during a rollout? Draw the hierarchy.
Show Answer
Answer
  Deployment (web-app)
  ├── ReplicaSet (web-app-v1)  ← Old: 0 replicas (rollback target)
  │   └── (no pods)
  ├── ReplicaSet (web-app-v2)  ← Old: 0 replicas (rollback target)
  │   └── (no pods)
  └── ReplicaSet (web-app-v3)  ← Current: 3 replicas (active)
      ├── Pod web-app-v3-abc
      ├── Pod web-app-v3-def
      └── Pod web-app-v3-ghi

  During rolling update (v3 → v4):
  ├── ReplicaSet (web-app-v3)  ← Scaling DOWN: 2→1→0
  │   └── Pod web-app-v3-abc (Terminating)
  └── ReplicaSet (web-app-v4)  ← Scaling UP: 1→2→3
      └── Pod web-app-v4-xyz (Running)
Explanation

Deployment → manages ReplicaSets → manage Pods. Each image/spec change creates a new ReplicaSet. Old ReplicaSets are kept at 0 replicas for rollback. During a rolling update, two ReplicaSets are active: old scaling down, new scaling up. This is why kubectl get rs shows multiple ReplicaSets. On the CKAD, understanding this hierarchy helps debug deployment issues.

Q5
You updated a deployment image to nginx:broken (doesn't exist). Pods show ImagePullBackOff. Fix this without rollback — correct the image directly.
Show Answer
Answer
# Current state: bad image
kubectl get pods
# NAME                   READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
# web-app-xyz            0/1     ImagePullBackOff   0

# Fix: Update to correct image
kubectl set image deployment/web-app web=nginx:1.25

# Alternative: rollback to previous working version
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app

# Monitor fix
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app
kubectl get pods  # Should show Running
Explanation

You can fix a broken rollout by either: (1) correcting the image with kubectl set image (creates a new revision), or (2) kubectl rollout undo (reverts to last known good). Method 1 creates revision N+1 with the fix. Method 2 creates revision N+1 copying revision N-1. On the CKAD, ImagePullBackOff often means a typo in the image name/tag — check with kubectl describe pod.

Q6
Why should you never use :latest tag for container images in production? What should you use instead?
Show Answer
Answer
TagProblemRecommendation
:latestMutable — points to different images over time❌ Never in production
:v1.24Better but still mutable (can be re-pushed)⚠️ Acceptable
@sha256:abc123...Immutable — exact image guaranteed✅ Best for production
 
# Get image digest
docker inspect nginx:1.24 --format='{{index .RepoDigests 0}}'
# nginx@sha256:abc123def456...

# Use in deployment
kubectl set image deployment/web web=nginx@sha256:abc123def456
Explanation

:latest causes several problems: (1) no rollback — rollout undo sees the same tag, (2) imagePullPolicy: Always is default for :latest (slower starts), (3) no reproducibility. With explicit tags: every deployment change is visible in rollout history, and rollbacks work correctly because each revision references a different tag. On the CKAD, always use explicit tags.

Q7
How does imagePullPolicy affect deployments? What are the three options?
Show Answer
Answer
PolicyBehaviorDefault When
AlwaysPull image every time a pod startsTag is :latest or omitted
IfNotPresentPull only if not cached on nodeAny specific tag (e.g., :1.24)
NeverNever pull — image must exist locallyMust be explicitly set
Explanation

Always adds latency and registry dependency to pod startup. IfNotPresent is faster but won't detect updated images behind the same tag. Never is for preloaded or locally-built images. On the CKAD, this explains why pods with :latest tag may get different images on different nodes (if pulled at different times). Use specific tags to avoid this.

Q8
How do you annotate a deployment with a change-cause that appears in rollout history?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Annotate after update
kubectl set image deployment/web-app web=nginx:1.25
kubectl annotate deployment/web-app \
  kubernetes.io/change-cause="Upgrade nginx to 1.25 for CVE fix"

# Method 2: Include annotation in YAML
kubectl patch deployment web-app -p \
  '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"kubernetes.io/change-cause":"Security patch v1.25"}}}'

# View in history
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app
# REVISION  CHANGE-CAUSE
# 1         Initial deployment
# 2         Upgrade nginx to 1.25 for CVE fix
Explanation

The kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation replaces the deprecated --record flag. It's the only way to add human-readable notes to rollout history. Without it, CHANGE-CAUSE shows <none>. On the CKAD, annotating change-cause is a best practice but not always required. It becomes critical when you need to identify which revision to rollback to.

Q9
You have a deployment at revision 5. You need to check what image was used in revision 3 WITHOUT rolling back. How?
Show Answer
Answer
# View specific revision details
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app --revision=3

# Output shows full pod template:
# Pod Template:
#   Containers:
#    web:
#     Image:    nginx:1.23     ← This is what revision 3 used

# Alternative: Check the ReplicaSet directly
kubectl get rs -o wide
# Shows IMAGE column for each ReplicaSet

# Most detailed view:
kubectl get rs web-app-ABC123 -o yaml
# Full spec including all containers, volumes, env vars
Explanation

--revision=N is a read-only inspection — it doesn't modify anything. Each revision corresponds to a ReplicaSet. kubectl get rs -o wide shows all ReplicaSets with their images in a table. This is the safe way to compare revisions before deciding which one to rollback to. On the CKAD, always inspect before rolling back.

Q10
A rollback completes but the application still shows the old behavior. What could be wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check if rollback actually changed the image
kubectl describe deployment web-app | grep Image
# Still showing old image? Rollback may have failed

# 2. Check if ConfigMap/Secret changed (not tracked by rollout)
kubectl describe configmap app-config
# ConfigMaps are NOT versioned by Deployment rollouts!

# 3. Check if it's a DNS/Service cache issue
kubectl rollout restart deployment/web-app

# 4. Browser cache or CDN cache
# Test with curl directly to a pod IP

# 5. Database schema changed (can't rollback data)
kubectl logs web-app-POD  # Check for migration errors
Explanation

The #1 trap: Deployment rollback only reverts the pod template (image, env vars, probes). It does NOT rollback ConfigMaps, Secrets, PVCs, or database changes. If you changed a ConfigMap AND the image, rolling back the deployment restores the old image but keeps the new ConfigMap. Solution: version your ConfigMaps (e.g., app-config-v2) and reference them by name. This is an advanced CKAD concept.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Rollback Audit Trails & Revision Security

MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Rollbacks can silently revert secure configurations. Without audit trails, attackers can use rollbacks to re-deploy vulnerable versions undetected.

RiskAttack ScenarioMitigation
Rollback to vulnerable imageAttacker reverts to known CVE versionAudit logging + ImagePolicyWebhook
Security context regressionRollback removes runAsNonRootPSA enforce + admission policies
Unlimited revision historyOld revisions with leaked secretsSet revisionHistoryLimit
Unaudited rollbacksNo record of who rolled backKubernetes audit logging at RequestResponse level
 
# Audit policy to track rollbacks
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: "apps"
    resources: ["deployments", "deployments/rollback"]
  verbs: ["update", "patch"]
  # Captures WHO rolled back WHAT and WHEN

Chapter 19 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
How do you audit who performed a rollback on a deployment? Write an audit policy rule.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
# Track all deployment modifications including rollbacks
- level: RequestResponse
  users: ["*"]
  resources:
  - group: "apps"
    resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
  verbs: ["update", "patch", "delete"]
  namespaces: ["production", "staging"]
# Search audit logs for rollback events
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .verb == "patch" and
  .objectRef.resource == "deployments" and
  .objectRef.namespace == "production"
) | {user: .user.username, time: .requestReceivedTimestamp, 
     deploy: .objectRef.name}'
Explanation

Kubernetes audit logs record all API requests including rollbacks. Use RequestResponse level to capture the full request body — this shows what revision was rolled back to. On CKS, configuring audit policies for deployment changes is essential for incident investigation.

CKS-2
Set revisionHistoryLimit to 3 on a deployment and explain why unlimited revision history is a security risk.
Show Answer
Answer
# Patch deployment
kubectl patch deploy web -n production -p '{"spec":{"revisionHistoryLimit": 3}}'

# Verify old ReplicaSets are cleaned up
kubectl get rs -n production --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Check current revision history
kubectl rollout history deploy/web -n production
Explanation

Each revision keeps an old ReplicaSet with the pod template — including environment variables that might reference Secrets. Unlimited history means old, vulnerable configurations persist indefinitely. On CKS, set revisionHistoryLimit to a small number (2-5) to limit the rollback attack surface.

CKS-3
How do you restrict who can perform rollbacks using RBAC?
Show Answer
Answer
# Role that allows viewing but NOT rolling back
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deploy-viewer
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  # NO "update" or "patch" — prevents rollback

---
# Role for senior devs who CAN rollback
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deploy-manager
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments/rollback"]
  verbs: ["create"]
Explanation

Rollbacks require update or patch verbs on deployments. By separating viewer and manager roles, you ensure only authorized users can rollback. On CKS, use RBAC to implement least privilege — most users should only view deployments, not modify them.

CKS-4
A deployment was rolled back and is now running a container image with known CVEs. How do you detect and prevent this?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Detect — scan current running images
kubectl get pods -n production -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}' | sort -u | while read img; do
  echo "=== Scanning: $img ==="
  trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL "$img"
done

# 2. Prevent — use OPA/Gatekeeper to deny images with known CVEs
# Or use ImagePolicyWebhook with a signing verification backend

# 3. Continuous monitoring — deploy Trivy Operator
# It scans all running workloads automatically
kubectl get vulnerabilityreports -A
Explanation

Rollbacks can reintroduce vulnerable images that were replaced for security reasons. On CKS, combine admission controllers (block vulnerable images at deploy time) with continuous scanning (Trivy Operator) that catches post-rollback regressions. Never rely only on CI/CD scanning.

CKS-5
How do you use kubectl rollout history to investigate a suspicious deployment change?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. View all revisions
kubectl rollout history deploy/web -n production

# 2. Inspect a specific revision's details
kubectl rollout history deploy/web -n production --revision=3

# 3. Compare revisions — look for security changes
kubectl rollout history deploy/web --revision=3 -o yaml > rev3.yaml
kubectl rollout history deploy/web --revision=4 -o yaml > rev4.yaml
diff rev3.yaml rev4.yaml | grep -E "image|security|runAs|capability|privi"

# 4. Check annotations for change reason
kubectl get deploy web -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq .
# Look for kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation
Explanation

Revision history is your forensic tool — compare revisions to see what changed (image, security context, env vars). Use --revision=N to inspect specific versions. On CKS, always set kubernetes.io/change-cause annotation so rollout history shows who changed what and why.

Chapter 20: Jobs and CronJobs

Not all workloads run forever. Jobs handle one-time tasks like database migrations or batch processing. CronJobs schedule recurring tasks like nightly backups or weekly reports. Master these for complete workflow automation.

⚡ Jobs 🕐 CronJobs 🔄 Batch Processing 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Jobs for one-time tasks (migrations, data seeding)
  • ✅ Job configuration: parallelism, retries, timeouts
  • ✅ Three job patterns: single, parallel, work queue
  • ✅ CronJobs for scheduled tasks (backups, reports)
  • ✅ Cron syntax and concurrency policies
  • ✅ Real-world CronJobs for anihpj
  • ✅ Monitoring and debugging Jobs
🏗️ CKAD Domain 1: Application Design & Build 20% of Exam

Jobs and CronJobs are high-frequency CKAD topics. You'll be asked to create Jobs with specific completions/parallelism and CronJobs with precise schedules. Imperative creation is fastest.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: Jobs & CronJobs — Critical Fields

The exam tests your ability to create and configure Jobs quickly. Know these fields:

  • completions — how many times the Job must succeed (default: 1)
  • parallelism — how many pods run simultaneously (default: 1)
  • backoffLimit — retry count before marking failed (default: 6)
  • activeDeadlineSeconds — timeout for the entire Job
  • restartPolicy — MUST be Never or OnFailure (never Always)
  • CronJob schedule uses standard cron: "*/5 * * * *" = every 5 min
  • concurrencyPolicy: Allow | Forbid | Replace
⚡ Exam Speed Commands — Jobs & CronJobs 
# Create a Job imperatively
kubectl create job my-job --image=busybox -- echo "done"

# Create a CronJob (every 5 minutes)
kubectl create cronjob my-cron --image=busybox --schedule="*/5 * * * *" -- echo "tick"

# Generate Job YAML for editing
kubectl create job my-job --image=busybox --dry-run=client -o yaml -- sh -c "echo done"

# Check Job completion
kubectl get jobs
kubectl logs job/my-job

Job & CronJob YAML — Complete Templates

Jobs are heavily tested. Know completions, parallelism, and backoffLimit cold.

# Job — Run once (or N times)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-migration
spec:
  completions: 5                   # ← Total successful runs needed
  parallelism: 2                   # ← Run 2 pods at a time
  backoffLimit: 4                  # ← Max retries before failure
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 300       # ← Timeout (5 minutes)
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never         # ← MUST be Never or OnFailure!
      containers:
      - name: migrate
        image: myapp:1.0
        command: ["python", "migrate.py"]

# CronJob — Scheduled recurring Job
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: nightly-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"           # ← Every day at 2:00 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid        # ← Don't overlap runs
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3    # ← Keep last 3 successful Jobs
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1        # ← Keep last 1 failed Job
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 200     # ← If missed by 200s, skip it
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: backup-tool:1.0
            command: ["sh", "-c", "pg_dump -h postgres > /backup/db.sql"]

# Cron Schedule Quick Reference:
# ┌────────────── minute (0-59)
# │ ┌──────────── hour (0-23)
# │ │ ┌────────── day of month (1-31)
# │ │ │ ┌──────── month (1-12)
# │ │ │ │ ┌────── day of week (0-6, Sun=0)
# * * * * *
# */5 * * * *    = every 5 minutes
# 0 */2 * * *    = every 2 hours
# 0 0 * * 0      = every Sunday midnight
# 30 9 1 * *     = 9:30 AM on 1st of each month

Job & CronJob Traps

  • ❌ restartPolicy: Always → INVALID for Jobs! Must use Never or OnFailure
  • ❌ CronJob creates Jobs, Jobs create Pods — CronJob → Job → Pod (3 levels of nesting!)
  • CronJob YAML indentation: spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec — 4 levels of spec!
  • completions vs parallelism: completions: 5, parallelism: 2 = runs 2 at a time until 5 total succeed
  • backoffLimit: 0 = no retries. Pod failure = Job fails immediately
  • concurrencyPolicy: Forbid = skip new run if previous still running. Replace = kill old, start new
  • Completed pods aren't deleted by default — use ttlSecondsAfterFinished to auto-clean

Job Verification Checklist

  • kubectl get jobs → Check COMPLETIONS column (e.g., "3/5" = 3 of 5 done)
  • kubectl describe job data-migration → Check Events for failures
  • kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=data-migration → See all pods from Job
  • kubectl logs job/data-migration → View Job output
  • kubectl get cronjobs → Check LAST SCHEDULE and ACTIVE columns
  • kubectl get jobs --watch → Watch CronJob trigger new Jobs

📊 Jobs vs CronJobs vs Deployments

🔄 Deployment Runs forever, restarts on failure
✅ Job Run once to completion, then stop
⏰ CronJob Scheduled recurring Jobs

🔬 Job Configuration Parameters

🎯 completions
How many pods must complete successfully (default: 1)
⚡ parallelism
How many pods run simultaneously (default: 1)
🔁 backoffLimit
Max retries before marking Job as failed (default: 6)
⏱️ activeDeadlineSeconds
Max total runtime — kills Job if exceeded (no default)
🧹 ttlSecondsAfterFinished
Auto-delete completed Job after N seconds
⏰ CronJob Schedule Cheat Sheet
  • */5 * * * * → Every 5 minutes
  • 0 * * * * → Every hour on the hour
  • 0 2 * * * → Daily at 2:00 AM
  • 0 0 * * 0 → Weekly on Sunday midnight
  • 0 0 1 * * → Monthly on the 1st at midnight

20.1 Jobs — One-Time Tasks

A Job creates pods that run to completion and then stop. Unlike Deployments (which keep pods running forever), Jobs are for tasks that should execute once and exit — like database migrations, data processing, or sending batch emails.

⚡ Job vs Deployment — Different Lifecycles

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT (runs forever)           JOB (runs to completion)        │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────┐       ┌──────────────────────────┐     │
  │  │ Pod: anihpj-web          │       │ Pod: anihpj-migrate      │     │
  │  │                          │       │                          │     │
  │  │ Start ●─────────────────▶│       │ Start ●────────● Done    │     │
  │  │       (runs indefinitely)│       │         ▲      │         │     │
