  │  │                          │       │         │ runs │         │     │
  │  │ If pod dies → restart    │       │         │      ▼         │     │
  │  │ Desired state: Running   │       │      migrate tables      │     │
  │  │                          │       │                          │     │
  │  │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ ┌────┐    │       │      Status: Completed   │     │
  │  │ │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │    │       │      Pod: Succeeded ✅   │     │
  │  │ └────┘ └────┘ └────┘    │       └──────────────────────────┘     │
  │  │  Always 3 replicas       │                                       │
  │  └──────────────────────────┘                                       │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Use for:                           Use for:                         │
  │  • Web servers                      • Database migrations            │
  │  • API servers                      • Data imports/exports           │
  │  • Background workers               • Batch email sending            │
  │  • Any "always-on" service          • Report generation              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/job-migrate.yaml — Django Database Migration 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-migrate
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate", "--no-input"]
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-db-secrets     # DATABASE_URL, etc.
      restartPolicy: Never                  # ← Required for Jobs!
  backoffLimit: 3                           # Retry up to 3 times on failure
k8s/job-seed-data.yaml — Seed Demo Data 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-seed-data
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: seed
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: 
            - python
            - manage.py
            - shell
            - -c
            - |
              from jobpost.models import Skill, Author, Location, JobPost
              # Create sample skills
              skills = ['Python', 'Django', 'Kubernetes', 'Docker', 'AWS']
              for name in skills:
                  Skill.objects.get_or_create(name=name)
              # Create sample author
              author, _ = Author.objects.get_or_create(
                  name='Demo Company',
                  defaults={'email': 'demo@example.com'}
              )
              print(f'Created {len(skills)} skills and 1 author')
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-db-secrets
      restartPolicy: Never
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 300              # Auto-delete after 5 minutes
Running and Monitoring Jobs 
# Run the migration job
kubectl apply -f job-migrate.yaml

# Watch job status
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -w
# NAME              COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# anihpj-migrate   0/1           3s         3s
# anihpj-migrate   1/1           8s         8s   ◀── Completed!

# View job's pod
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-migrate
# NAME                     READY   STATUS      RESTARTS   AGE
# anihpj-migrate-abc12    0/1     Completed   0          15s

# Check migration output
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app
# Operations to perform:
#   Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
# Running migrations:
#   Applying jobpost.0001_initial... OK
#   Applying jobpost.0002_jobpost_skills... OK

# Delete completed job
kubectl delete job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app
⚠️ restartPolicy Must Be Never or OnFailure

Jobs cannot use restartPolicy: Always (the default for Deployments). Use Never (preferred) or OnFailure. With Never, a new pod is created on failure. With OnFailure, the same pod is restarted.

20.2 Job Configuration Parameters

Jobs have several parameters that control how they execute, retry on failure, and clean up after completion.

Parameter Default Description
completions 1 Number of times the job must complete successfully
parallelism 1 Number of pods running simultaneously
backoffLimit 6 How many retries before marking job as failed
activeDeadlineSeconds none Max time the job can run before being terminated
ttlSecondsAfterFinished none Auto-delete job N seconds after completion
restartPolicy — Must be Never or OnFailure
k8s/job-with-all-params.yaml — Full Configuration 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-batch-process
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  completions: 5                    # Run 5 times total
  parallelism: 2                    # 2 pods at a time
  backoffLimit: 3                   # Give up after 3 failures
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 600        # Timeout: 10 minutes max
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600     # Delete job 1 hour after completion
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: processor
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "process_batch"]
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 100m
              memory: 128Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 500m
              memory: 512Mi
      restartPolicy: Never
🔄 backoffLimit

Exponential backoff between retries: 10s, 20s, 40s... up to 6 minutes. After backoffLimit failures, job status becomes Failed.

🧹 ttlSecondsAfterFinished

Auto-cleanup! Without this, completed jobs stick around forever, cluttering your cluster. Set to 3600 (1 hour) or 86400 (1 day).

20.3 Job Patterns

There are three common patterns for running Jobs, depending on your workload: single execution, parallel with fixed completions, or work queue processing.

📊 Three Job Patterns

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  JOB PATTERNS                                                          │
  │                                                                        │
  │  A) SINGLE JOB              B) FIXED COMPLETIONS    C) WORK QUEUE      │
  │  completions: 1             completions: 5          completions: unset │
  │  parallelism: 1             parallelism: 2          parallelism: 3     │
  │                                                                        │
  │  ┌─────────────┐            Round 1:                ┌───────────────┐  │
  │  │ Pod 1       │            ┌────┐ ┌────┐           │   Queue       │  │
  │  │             │            │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │           │ [A][B][C][D] │  │
  │  │ migrate     │            └────┘ └────┘           │ [E][F][G]    │  │
  │  │             │            (2 at a time)            └───────┬───────┘  │
  │  │ ✅ done     │                                             │         │
  │  └─────────────┘            Round 2:                         ▼         │
  │                             ┌────┐ ┌────┐           ┌────┐┌────┐┌────┐ │
  │  total: 1 pod               │ 📦 │ │ 📦 │           │ 📦 ││ 📦 ││ 📦 │ │
  │                             └────┘ └────┘           └────┘└────┘└────┘ │
  │  Use:                       (2 more)                 pull from queue   │
  │  • DB migration                                      until empty       │
  │  • Single report            Round 3:                                   │
  │  • Data cleanup             ┌────┐                  Use:               │
  │                             │ 📦 │ (last 1)          • Process uploads │
  │                             └────┘                   • Handle emails   │
  │                                                      • Image processing│
  │                             total: 5                                   │
  │                                                                        │
  │                             Use:                                       │
  │                             • Process N items                          │
  │                             • Send N emails                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Single Job

The simplest pattern — one pod, one execution. Perfect for migrations or one-time operations.

Single Job Pattern 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-migrate
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  # Default: completions=1, parallelism=1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: migrate
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
      restartPolicy: Never

Parallel Jobs with Fixed Completions

Run N tasks in parallel, continuing until all completions are done. Great for processing a known number of items.

Fixed Completions Pattern — Send 100 Emails 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-send-digest-emails
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  completions: 100              # Send 100 digest emails total
  parallelism: 10               # 10 pods running at once
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: mailer
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command: 
            - python
            - manage.py
            - send_single_digest
            - --offset
            - "$(JOB_COMPLETION_INDEX)"    # Each pod processes different user
          env:
            - name: JOB_COMPLETION_INDEX
              valueFrom:
                fieldRef:
                  fieldPath: metadata.annotations['batch.kubernetes.io/job-completion-index']
      restartPolicy: Never

Work Queue Pattern

Pods pull work from a shared queue (Redis, RabbitMQ) and exit when the queue is empty. The number of completions is unknown upfront.

Work Queue Pattern — Process Resume Uploads 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-process-resumes
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  # No completions set = run until pods exit successfully
  parallelism: 5                # 5 workers pulling from queue
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: resume-processor
          image: anihpj:v1.2.0
          command:
            - python
            - manage.py
            - process_resume_queue
            - --exit-when-empty         # Exit when no more work
          env:
            - name: REDIS_URL
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: anihpj-redis
                  key: url
      restartPolicy: Never
📝 Work Queue Behavior

When completions is not set, the job is complete when any pod exits successfully (exit code 0). All workers should exit when the queue is empty. This requires your code to detect an empty queue and exit cleanly.

20.4 CronJobs — Scheduled Tasks

A CronJob creates Jobs on a time-based schedule, just like Unix cron. Each scheduled run creates a new Job, which creates a new Pod. Perfect for recurring maintenance tasks.

🕐 CronJob — Creates Jobs on Schedule

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  CRONJOB: anihpj-daily-cleanup                                      │
  │  Schedule: 0 2 * * * (daily at 2:00 AM)                              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Time ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────▶    │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Day 1           Day 2           Day 3           Day 4               │
  │   2:00 AM         2:00 AM         2:00 AM         2:00 AM            │
  │    │               │               │               │                 │
  │    ▼               ▼               ▼               ▼                 │
  │  ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐           │
  │  │ Job 1   │    │ Job 2   │    │ Job 3   │    │ Job 4   │           │
  │  │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │    │ cleanup │           │
  │  │ ✅ done │    │ ✅ done │    │ ✅ done │    │ running │           │
  │  └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘           │
  │   (deleted)      (deleted)      (kept: 3)     (current)              │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │  CronJob keeps last N jobs (successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3)  │    │
  │  │  Older jobs are automatically deleted                        │    │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📅 Cron Syntax — Five-Field Schedule

  ┌───────────────── minute (0 - 59)
  │ ┌───────────────── hour (0 - 23)
  │ │ ┌───────────────── day of month (1 - 31)
  │ │ │ ┌───────────────── month (1 - 12)  
  │ │ │ │ ┌───────────────── day of week (0 - 6, Sunday = 0)
  │ │ │ │ │
  * * * * *
  
  Special characters:
  *    = every value (every minute, every hour, etc.)
  */5  = every 5 units (*/5 in minute = every 5 minutes)
  1,15 = specific values (1st and 15th)
  1-5  = range (Monday through Friday if in day-of-week)
Schedule Meaning Use Case
*/5 * * * * Every 5 minutes Health checks, quick syncs
0 * * * * Every hour (at :00) Hourly reports
0 2 * * * Daily at 2:00 AM Nightly cleanup/backups
0 9 * * 1 Monday at 9:00 AM Weekly email digest
0 0 1 * * First of month at midnight Monthly reports
0 */6 * * * Every 6 hours Database backups

20.5 CronJob Configuration

CronJobs have additional configuration options for handling missed schedules, concurrent runs, and job history.

CronJob with All Configuration Options 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-daily-cleanup
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"                     # Daily at 2 AM
  timeZone: "America/New_York"              # Timezone (K8s 1.27+)
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid                 # Don't run if previous still running
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3             # Keep last 3 successful jobs
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1                 # Keep last 1 failed job
  startingDeadlineSeconds: 200              # If missed by >200s, skip this run
  suspend: false                            # Set true to pause the schedule
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: cleanup
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "clearsessions"]
          restartPolicy: Never
      backoffLimit: 2
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 300            # Kill job if still running after 5 min
✅ Allow

concurrencyPolicy: Allow
Multiple jobs can run simultaneously. Use for independent tasks that don't conflict.

⏸️ Forbid

concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
Skip new job if previous is still running. Use for tasks that shouldn't overlap.

🔄 Replace

concurrencyPolicy: Replace
Cancel running job, start new one. Use when latest run is more important.

📝 startingDeadlineSeconds

If the CronJob controller misses a scheduled time (e.g., controller was down), it will still start the job if less than startingDeadlineSeconds has passed. If more time has passed, that run is skipped. Set this to prevent a flood of catch-up runs after downtime.

20.6 CronJobs for anihpj — Real Examples

Here are practical CronJob examples for the anihpj job board application, covering common maintenance and automation tasks.

Clean Expired Job Postings (Daily)

Remove job postings that have passed their expiration date — runs every night at midnight.

k8s/cronjob-cleanup.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-cleanup-expired
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 0 * * *"                     # Daily at midnight
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: cleanup
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command:
                - python
                - manage.py
                - shell
                - -c
                - |
                  from django.utils import timezone
                  from jobpost.models import JobPost
                  expired = JobPost.objects.filter(
                      expires_at__lt=timezone.now(),
                      is_active=True
                  )
                  count = expired.update(is_active=False)
                  print(f'Deactivated {count} expired job postings')
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 86400        # Delete after 24 hours

Email Weekly Summary (Monday 9 AM)

Send weekly digest emails to all Authors with their job posting statistics.

k8s/cronjob-weekly-digest.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-weekly-digest
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 9 * * 1"                     # Monday at 9:00 AM
  timeZone: "America/New_York"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: mailer
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "send_weekly_digest"]
              env:
                - name: EMAIL_HOST
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-email
                      key: host
                - name: EMAIL_HOST_PASSWORD
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-email
                      key: password
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      backoffLimit: 2                       # Retry twice on failure

Generate Salary Reports (Monthly)

Generate CSV reports of salary statistics across all job postings — saved to cloud storage.

k8s/cronjob-monthly-report.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-monthly-report
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 0 1 * *"                     # First of month at midnight
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: reporter
              image: anihpj:v1.2.0
              command: ["python", "manage.py", "generate_salary_report"]
              args:
                - "--output-bucket"
                - "s3://anihpj-reports/salary/"
                - "--format"
                - "csv"
              env:
                - name: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: access_key
                - name: AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: secret_key
              envFrom:
                - secretRef:
                    name: anihpj-db-secrets
          restartPolicy: Never
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 1800           # 30 min timeout

Database Backup (Every 6 Hours)

Create PostgreSQL backups and upload to S3 — runs 4 times per day.

k8s/cronjob-db-backup.yaml 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-backup
  namespace: anihpj-app
spec:
  schedule: "0 */6 * * *"                   # Every 6 hours
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 4             # Keep last 4 backups' jobs
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 2
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: postgres:15-alpine     # Use official postgres image
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  TIMESTAMP=$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)
                  BACKUP_FILE="/tmp/anihpj-${TIMESTAMP}.sql.gz"
                  
                  echo "Starting backup: ${BACKUP_FILE}"
                  pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip > ${BACKUP_FILE}
                  
                  echo "Uploading to S3..."
                  aws s3 cp ${BACKUP_FILE} s3://anihpj-backups/db/
                  
                  echo "Backup complete: ${BACKUP_FILE}"
              env:
                - name: DATABASE_URL
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-db-secrets
                      key: DATABASE_URL
                - name: AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: access_key
                - name: AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: anihpj-aws
                      key: secret_key
          restartPolicy: Never
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 600            # 10 min timeout
      backoffLimit: 2

20.7 Monitoring Jobs and CronJobs

Keeping track of your Jobs and CronJobs is essential for ensuring scheduled tasks run correctly and debugging failures.

Job and CronJob Monitoring Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# VIEWING JOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# List all jobs
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app
# NAME                          COMPLETIONS   DURATION   AGE
# anihpj-migrate                1/1           8s         2h
# anihpj-db-backup-28456789     1/1           45s        6h
# anihpj-cleanup-expired-284..  1/1           12s        18h

# Get job details
kubectl describe job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

# View job's pod logs
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

# Watch job progress
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -w

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# VIEWING CRONJOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# List all cronjobs
kubectl get cronjobs -n anihpj-app
# NAME                    SCHEDULE      SUSPEND   ACTIVE   LAST SCHEDULE
# anihpj-cleanup-expired  0 0 * * *     False     0        18h
# anihpj-weekly-digest    0 9 * * 1     False     0        3d
# anihpj-db-backup        0 */6 * * *   False     0        2h

# Get cronjob details (shows next scheduled run)
kubectl describe cronjob anihpj-db-backup -n anihpj-app

# View jobs created by a cronjob
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-db-backup

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# DEBUGGING FAILED JOBS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# Find failed jobs
kubectl get jobs -n anihpj-app --field-selector=status.successful=0

# Get failed job's pod
kubectl get pods -n anihpj-app -l job-name=anihpj-migrate --field-selector=status.phase=Failed

# View failure logs
kubectl logs job/anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app --previous

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════
# MANUAL OPERATIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════

# Manually trigger a cronjob NOW
kubectl create job --from=cronjob/anihpj-db-backup manual-backup -n anihpj-app

# Suspend a cronjob (pause scheduling)
kubectl patch cronjob anihpj-weekly-digest -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}' -n anihpj-app

# Resume a cronjob
kubectl patch cronjob anihpj-weekly-digest -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}' -n anihpj-app

# Delete a job manually
kubectl delete job anihpj-migrate -n anihpj-app

📊 Job/CronJob Status Reference

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  JOB STATUS                                                          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  COMPLETIONS column:                                                  │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ 0/1  = Job running, 0 completions so far (out of 1 needed)     │  │
  │  │ 1/1  = Job completed successfully ✅                            │  │
  │  │ 0/5  = Parallel job, 0 of 5 completions done                   │  │
  │  │ 3/5  = Parallel job, 3 of 5 completions done                   │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CRONJOB STATUS                                                       │
  │                                                                       │
  │  ACTIVE column:                                                       │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ 0 = No jobs currently running                                  │  │
  │  │ 1 = One job instance currently running                         │  │
  │  │ 2 = Two jobs running (concurrencyPolicy: Allow)                │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                       │
  │  SUSPEND column:                                                      │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │ False = CronJob is active, will create jobs on schedule        │  │
  │  │ True  = CronJob is paused, no new jobs will be created         │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✅ Quick Reference: Jobs
  • kubectl get jobs — list all
  • kubectl logs job/NAME — view output
  • kubectl delete job NAME — cleanup
🕐 Quick Reference: CronJobs
  • kubectl get cronjobs — list all
  • kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME — manual run
  • kubectl patch cronjob NAME -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}' — pause
💡 Best Practices for Jobs and CronJobs
  • Always set ttlSecondsAfterFinished to auto-cleanup completed jobs
  • Use activeDeadlineSeconds to prevent runaway jobs
  • Set concurrencyPolicy: Forbid for jobs that shouldn't overlap
  • Keep backoffLimit low (2-3) to fail fast on broken jobs
  • Monitor CronJob history regularly — missed runs indicate problems
  • Use kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME to test manually before scheduling

🔧 Job Troubleshooting Guide

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  COMMON JOB ISSUES AND FIXES                                         │
  │                                                                       │
  │  SYMPTOM                   CAUSE                   FIX                │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────       │
  │  Job stuck at 0/1         Pod can't schedule      Check node         │
  │                           (no resources)           capacity/events   │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Pod: ImagePullBackOff    Wrong image name        Fix image tag      │
  │                           or missing auth          or add imagePull  │
  │                                                    Secret            │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Pod: CrashLoopBackOff    Command fails           Check logs:        │
  │                           immediately              kubectl logs      │
  │                                                    job/NAME          │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Job keeps retrying       restartPolicy: Always   Change to Never    │
  │  forever                  (invalid for Jobs)       or OnFailure      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Job completed but pods   Normal! Jobs don't      Set ttlSeconds     │
  │  still visible            auto-delete pods        AfterFinished      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CronJob never runs       Suspended or wrong      kubectl describe   │
  │                           schedule syntax          cronjob NAME      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  CronJob runs but fails   startingDeadline        Increase deadline  │
  │  silently                 too short               or check logs      │
  │                                                                       │
  │  Two jobs running at      concurrencyPolicy       Set to Forbid      │
  │  same time                is Allow (default)      or Replace         │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Check Command What to Look For
Job events kubectl describe job NAME FailedCreate, DeadlineExceeded
Pod logs kubectl logs job/NAME Application errors, stack traces
Pod status kubectl get pods -l job-name=NAME Running, Failed, Completed
CronJob schedule kubectl describe cronjob NAME Last Schedule, Active
Failed jobs kubectl get jobs --field-selector=status.successful=0 Any failed completions

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Jobs & CronJobs

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario: You need to set up batch processing tasks for the anihpj application. Create Jobs and CronJobs with specific configurations.
  1. Create a Job db-migrate that runs python manage.py migrate using image anihpj:v2 — it should retry 3 times on failure
  2. Create a Job data-export that must complete 5 times with 2 pods running in parallel
  3. Create a CronJob nightly-backup that runs every day at 2:00 AM using image busybox
  4. Configure nightly-backup with concurrencyPolicy: Forbid and keep only 3 successful history entries
  5. Verify: Check that the Job completed and view its logs
💡 Hint: Use kubectl create job and kubectl create cronjob imperatively, then edit YAML for advanced fields like completions and parallelism.
📖 View Solution
Solution — Jobs & CronJobs Drill 
# 1. Create migration Job with retry limit
kubectl create job db-migrate --image=anihpj:v2 --dry-run=client -o yaml \
  -- python manage.py migrate > migrate-job.yaml
# Edit to add: spec.backoffLimit: 3
kubectl apply -f migrate-job.yaml

# Or one-liner with patch approach:
kubectl create job db-migrate --image=anihpj:v2 -- python manage.py migrate
kubectl patch job db-migrate -p '{"spec":{"backoffLimit":3}}'

# 2. Parallel Job with completions
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: data-export
spec:
  completions: 5       # Must complete 5 times
  parallelism: 2       # Run 2 pods at once
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: export
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "echo 'Exporting batch...' && sleep 5"]
      restartPolicy: Never
EOF

# 3. Create nightly CronJob
kubectl create cronjob nightly-backup \
  --image=busybox \
  --schedule="0 2 * * *" \
  -- sh -c "echo 'Backing up database...'"

# 4. Configure concurrency and history
kubectl patch cronjob nightly-backup -p '{
  "spec": {
    "concurrencyPolicy": "Forbid",
    "successfulJobsHistoryLimit": 3
  }
}'

# 5. Verify completion and logs
kubectl get jobs                    # Check COMPLETIONS column
kubectl logs job/db-migrate         # View job output
kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=data-export  # See all pods
kubectl describe cronjob nightly-backup           # Verify schedule

Chapter 20 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is the difference between a Job and a Deployment? When would you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureDeploymentJob
LifecycleRuns foreverRuns to completion
Restart behaviorAlways restartsRestarts on failure (configurable)
Success state"Running" = healthy"Completed" = healthy
Use casesWeb servers, APIs, daemonsMigrations, batch processing, backups
restartPolicyAlways (required)OnFailure or Never
Explanation

Jobs are for finite tasks. A Job creates pods that run until successful completion (exit code 0). Once complete, pods stay in Completed state (not deleted immediately). Deployments are for long-running services. On the CKAD, Jobs are tested frequently — especially CronJobs and parallel Jobs.

Q2
Create a Job imperatively that runs echo "Hello CKAD" using the busybox image.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative Job creation  
kubectl create job hello-job --image=busybox -- echo "Hello CKAD"

# Verify
kubectl get jobs
kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=hello-job
kubectl logs job/hello-job

# Generate YAML for review
kubectl create job hello-job --image=busybox \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml -- echo "Hello CKAD"
Explanation

kubectl create job is the CKAD fast path. The -- separator before the command is critical. Without it, the command arguments are interpreted as kubectl flags. kubectl logs job/hello-job shows output from the job's pod directly. Job pods remain in Completed state for log inspection until cleaned up.

Q3
Write a Job YAML that runs 5 completions with 2 pods running in parallel. Set a 30-second timeout and allow 3 retries on failure.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: parallel-job
spec:
  completions: 5            # Total successful runs needed
  parallelism: 2            # Max pods running at once
  backoffLimit: 3           # Retries before marking failed
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 30 # Timeout (kills job if exceeded)
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never  # Required: Never or OnFailure
      containers:
      - name: worker
        image: busybox
        command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo Processing item; sleep 5']
Explanation

completions = how many pods must succeed total. parallelism = how many run simultaneously. backoffLimit = max retries (default: 6). activeDeadlineSeconds = total time limit for the entire Job (not per pod). With completions=5, parallelism=2: runs 2 pods at a time until 5 succeed. On the CKAD, know all four parameters and their defaults.

Q4
Create a CronJob that runs every day at 2:30 AM, executing a database backup command.
Show Answer
Answer
# Imperative CronJob
kubectl create cronjob db-backup \
  --image=postgres:15 \
  --schedule="30 2 * * *" \
  -- pg_dump -h db-svc -U admin mydb > /backup/dump.sql
# YAML version
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: db-backup
spec:
  schedule: "30 2 * * *"     # minute hour day month weekday
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: postgres:15
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'pg_dump -h db-svc mydb']
Explanation

Cron syntax: MIN HOUR DAY MONTH WEEKDAY. Common patterns: */5 * * * * (every 5 min), 0 * * * * (every hour), 0 0 * * 0 (weekly Sunday midnight). CronJob creates a Job at each scheduled time, which creates a Pod. Notice the nesting: CronJob → jobTemplate → template (pod spec). On the CKAD, memorize the cron field order — it's a guaranteed question.

Q5
What is concurrencyPolicy in CronJobs? Explain all three options.
Show Answer
Answer
PolicyBehaviorUse Case
Allow (default)Multiple jobs can run simultaneouslyIndependent tasks
ForbidSkip new job if previous still runningCan't overlap (DB locks)
ReplaceKill running job, start new oneOnly latest run matters
 
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid  # Skip if previous still running
Explanation

Forbid is safest for database backups (can't have two backups running). Replace is for cache warming or reports where only the latest matters. Allow is fine when jobs are independent and short-lived. On the CKAD, concurrencyPolicy is often tested alongside startingDeadlineSeconds and successfulJobsHistoryLimit.

Q6
What is successfulJobsHistoryLimit and failedJobsHistoryLimit? Why do completed Job pods accumulate?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: cleanup-cron
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3   # Keep last 3 successful (default)
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1       # Keep last 1 failed (default)
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 3600  # Auto-delete job after 1 hour
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
          - name: cleaner
            image: busybox
            command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo Cleaning...']
Explanation

CronJobs create new Jobs on each schedule. Without limits, completed Jobs and their pods accumulate forever. successfulJobsHistoryLimit keeps N most recent successful jobs (default: 3). failedJobsHistoryLimit keeps N failed jobs (default: 1). ttlSecondsAfterFinished on the Job spec auto-deletes finished Jobs after a duration. On the CKAD, set history limits low to prevent clutter.

Q7
What is restartPolicy in Jobs? Why can't Jobs use Always?
Show Answer
Answer
restartPolicyOn Container ExitNew Pod Created?
NeverPod enters Failed/SucceededYes (backoffLimit allows new pod)
OnFailureContainer restarts in same podNo (same pod, container restarts)
AlwaysN/A — INVALID for JobsJob would never complete!
Explanation

If restartPolicy: Always were allowed, the container would restart forever on success (exit 0), preventing the Job from ever completing. Never creates a new pod on failure (useful for debugging — you can inspect the failed pod). OnFailure restarts the container in place (less resource waste, but logs from failed attempts are lost). On the CKAD, OnFailure is the common choice; use Never when you need to inspect failures.

Q8
Create a CronJob from an existing Job. Show how to manually trigger a CronJob immediately.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create CronJob from scratch
kubectl create cronjob report-gen \
  --image=python:3.11 \
  --schedule="0 9 * * 1" \
  -- python /scripts/generate_report.py

# Manually trigger a CronJob (create a Job from CronJob template)
kubectl create job manual-report --from=cronjob/report-gen

# Verify the manual run
kubectl get jobs
kubectl logs job/manual-report

# Suspend a CronJob (prevent scheduled runs)
kubectl patch cronjob report-gen -p '{"spec":{"suspend":true}}'

# Resume
kubectl patch cronjob report-gen -p '{"spec":{"suspend":false}}'
Explanation

kubectl create job --from=cronjob/NAME is the CKAD power move — it creates an immediate Job using the CronJob's template. No need to wait for the schedule. spec.suspend: true pauses the CronJob without deleting it. These are commonly tested together: "trigger this CronJob now for testing, then disable it."

Q9
A Job pod fails with exit code 1. The Job creates new pods but they all fail. After how many retries does the Job give up? How do you check?
Show Answer
Answer
# Default backoffLimit is 6 — Jobs retries 6 times then fails
kubectl describe job failing-job
# Events:
#   Warning  BackoffLimitExceeded  Job has reached the specified backoff limit

# Check job status
kubectl get job failing-job -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions}'
# type: Failed, reason: BackoffLimitExceeded

# Check how many pods were created
kubectl get pods --selector=job-name=failing-job
# Shows 6+ pods in Error state

# View failure logs
kubectl logs job/failing-job  # Shows latest pod's logs
kubectl logs failing-job-POD  # Specific failed pod
Explanation

Default backoffLimit: 6. Each retry uses exponential backoff: 10s, 20s, 40s, up to 6 minutes max. After exceeding the limit, the Job is marked Failed. With restartPolicy: Never, each retry creates a new pod. With restartPolicy: OnFailure, the same pod's container restarts. On the CKAD, always check kubectl describe job for BackoffLimitExceeded events.

Q10
Write the cron schedule expressions for: (a) every 15 minutes, (b) weekdays at 6 PM, (c) first day of each month at midnight, (d) every Sunday at 3:30 AM.
Show Answer
Answer
# Cron format: MIN HOUR DAY MONTH WEEKDAY

# (a) Every 15 minutes
schedule: "*/15 * * * *"

# (b) Weekdays (Mon-Fri) at 6 PM
schedule: "0 18 * * 1-5"

# (c) First day of each month at midnight
schedule: "0 0 1 * *"

# (d) Every Sunday at 3:30 AM
schedule: "30 3 * * 0"
  ┌───────── minute (0-59)
  │ ┌─────── hour (0-23)
  │ │ ┌───── day of month (1-31)
  │ │ │ ┌─── month (1-12)
  │ │ │ │ ┌─ day of week (0-6, 0=Sunday)
  │ │ │ │ │
  * * * * *
Explanation

Cron syntax: */N = every N units, 1-5 = range, 1,15 = specific values. Day of week: 0 or 7 = Sunday. On the CKAD, cron schedule is almost always tested. Memorize the field order: MIN HOUR DAY MONTH DOW. Common mistake: confusing hour and minute positions. Tip: "minute first, then get bigger" (min → hour → day → month → weekday).

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Job & CronJob Security Contexts

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

Jobs and CronJobs often run with elevated privileges for batch tasks. They're frequently overlooked in security audits but create persistent attack vectors — especially CronJobs that run indefinitely.

RiskDescriptionMitigation
Privileged CronJobsBatch jobs with root access run periodicallyApply same SecurityContext as services
SA token auto-mountCronJob pods get API access via SA tokenautomountServiceAccountToken: false
CronJob persistenceAttacker creates CronJob for persistent accessRBAC: restrict CronJob creation
Failed job accumulationOld failed job pods leak env vars / secretsSet failedJobsHistoryLimit
 
# Secure CronJob template
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: secure-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1       # Clean up failed pods quickly
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid       # Prevent overlapping runs
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      activeDeadlineSeconds: 600  # Kill if running too long
      template:
        spec:
          automountServiceAccountToken: false
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 1000
            fsGroup: 1000
          containers:
          - name: backup
            image: backup-tool@sha256:abc123...
            securityContext:
              allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
              capabilities:
                drop: ["ALL"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

Chapter 20 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Harden a CronJob to prevent privilege escalation: set non-root, read-only filesystem, drop all capabilities, and disable SA token auto-mount.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: hardened-job
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          automountServiceAccountToken: false   # No API access
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534        # nobody user
            seccompProfile:
              type: RuntimeDefault
          containers:
          - name: task
            image: task@sha256:verified...
            securityContext:
              allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
              readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
              capabilities:
                drop: ["ALL"]
            resources:
              limits:
                cpu: 200m
                memory: 128Mi
          restartPolicy: Never
Explanation

CronJobs are often security-hardening blind spots — they run periodically and may outlive the team that created them. On CKS, apply the same security contexts to Jobs/CronJobs as your Deployments: non-root, read-only filesystem, dropped capabilities, no SA token, and seccomp profile.

CKS-2
An attacker has created a CronJob for persistent cluster access. How do you detect and prevent this?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Detect — audit all CronJobs across namespaces
kubectl get cronjobs -A -o custom-columns=\
  NS:.metadata.namespace,\
  NAME:.metadata.name,\
  SCHEDULE:.spec.schedule,\
  IMAGE:.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image,\
  SA:.spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.serviceAccountName

# 2. Look for suspicious patterns
kubectl get cronjobs -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image | 
  test("latest|curl|wget|alpine$")
)'

# 3. Prevent — restrict CronJob creation via RBAC
# Only CI/CD service accounts should create CronJobs
Explanation

CronJobs are a favorite persistence mechanism for attackers — they automatically restart, survive pod restarts, and often go unnoticed. On CKS, regularly audit CronJobs for: suspicious images, overly permissive service accounts, and CronJobs created by unexpected users (check audit logs).

CKS-3
How do you set activeDeadlineSeconds and ttlSecondsAfterFinished to prevent long-running and stale jobs?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: bounded-job
spec:
  activeDeadlineSeconds: 300      # Kill after 5 minutes max
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 120    # Auto-delete 2 min after completion
  backoffLimit: 2                 # Max 2 retries
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: task
        image: task@sha256:abc...
      restartPolicy: Never
# Verify cleanup
kubectl get jobs -n production --field-selector status.successful=1
# Should auto-delete after TTL
Explanation

activeDeadlineSeconds prevents a compromised job from running indefinitely (e.g., crypto mining). ttlSecondsAfterFinished auto-cleans completed/failed pods that might leak sensitive data in logs or env vars. On CKS, always set both for defense in depth.

CKS-4
How do you use NetworkPolicy to restrict a Job's network access to only the required database?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: job-network-restriction
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      job-name: data-migration    # Matches Job-created pods
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  - Ingress
  ingress: []                     # No incoming traffic
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: postgresql         # Only DB access
    ports:
    - port: 5432
  - to:                           # DNS resolution
    - namespaceSelector: {}
      podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          k8s-app: kube-dns
    ports:
    - port: 53
      protocol: UDP
Explanation

Jobs often need access to specific databases or APIs — restrict their network to only what's needed. On CKS, apply least-privilege networking: deny all, then allow specific egress to the database. Note that Job pods use labels like job-name that can be targeted by NetworkPolicies.

CKS-5
How do you audit all Jobs and CronJobs that use privileged security contexts?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find CronJobs running as root
kubectl get cronjobs -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true or
  .spec.jobTemplate.spec.template.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged == true
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

# Find Jobs with SA token mounted
kubectl get jobs -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.automountServiceAccountToken != false
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

# Find completed Job pods still hanging around
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase==Succeeded
kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase==Failed
Explanation

Regular security audits of Jobs/CronJobs are essential. On CKS, check for: root execution, SA token auto-mount, missing resource limits, privileged mode, and stale completed pods. Use PSA enforcement on namespaces to automatically reject insecure Jobs at admission.

📦 Part 7: Workload Types Chapters 21-22b

Chapter 21: StatefulSets — Stateful Applications

Not all applications are stateless. Databases, message queues, and caches need stable identities, persistent storage, and ordered operations. StatefulSets provide these guarantees — this chapter shows you how to run stateful workloads like PostgreSQL for your anihpj application.

🗄️ Databases 🔢 Ordered Pods 💾 Persistent Storage 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Stateless vs Stateful — fundamental differences
  • ✅ StatefulSet features — what makes them special
  • ✅ When to use StatefulSets (and when NOT to)
  • ✅ Running PostgreSQL for anihpj — complete example
  • ✅ Headless Services — stable network identity
  • ✅ Update strategies — RollingUpdate, OnDelete, partition
  • ✅ Backup and restore strategies
🏗️ CKAD Domain 1: Application Design & Build 20% of Exam

StatefulSets appear in CKAD as a conceptual differentiator. Know when to use StatefulSet vs Deployment. You won't be asked to build a full database cluster, but you need to understand stable network identity, ordered startup, and persistent storage.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: StatefulSet Key Differences

Know these three guarantees that StatefulSets provide (Deployments don't):

  • Stable network identity — pods get predictable names: name-0, name-1, name-2
  • Ordered operations — pods start 0→1→2 and stop 2→1→0 (sequential)
  • Persistent storage — each pod gets its own PVC that survives pod restarts
  • Requires a Headless Service (clusterIP: None) for DNS
  • DNS format: pod-name.service-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local

StatefulSet YAML — Complete Template

StatefulSet requires a Headless Service. Know the 3 guarantees structure.

# Step 1: Headless Service (REQUIRED for StatefulSet)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None                  # ← HEADLESS = no load balancing
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
    targetPort: 5432
---
# Step 2: StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless   # ← MUST match Headless Service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        env:
        - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: pg-secret
              key: password
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:            # ← Each pod gets its OWN PVC
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi

# Pod DNS: postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Pod DNS: postgres-1.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
# Pod DNS: postgres-2.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local

StatefulSet Commands

ActionCommand
Scale StatefulSetkubectl scale sts postgres --replicas=5
Check ordered podskubectl get pods -l app=postgres -w
View PVCs per podkubectl get pvc
Delete without PVCskubectl delete sts postgres (PVCs persist!)
Delete with PVCskubectl delete sts postgres && kubectl delete pvc -l app=postgres
Rollout updatekubectl rollout status sts/postgres

StatefulSet Traps

  • ❌ Missing serviceName → StatefulSet won't create! Must reference a Headless Service
  • ❌ Forgetting Headless Service → Create the clusterIP: None Service BEFORE the StatefulSet
  • PVCs are NOT deleted when StatefulSet is deleted — deliberate data safety. Delete manually if needed
  • Scaling down goes in reverse order: pod-2 deleted first, then pod-1 (data preserved in PVC)
  • volumeClaimTemplates can't be updated — must delete and recreate StatefulSet
  • Deployment vs StatefulSet: If the question says "stable network identity" or "ordered startup" → StatefulSet. Otherwise → Deployment
📦 Deployment (Stateless)
  • Pods are interchangeable — random names
  • Any pod can handle any request
  • Scaled up/down in any order
  • Share the same PVC (if any)
  • Use for: web servers, APIs, workers
🗄️ StatefulSet (Stateful)
  • Pods have stable names: db-0, db-1, db-2
  • Ordered startup (0→1→2) and shutdown (2→1→0)
  • Each pod gets its own PVC (volumeClaimTemplates)
  • Stable DNS: db-0.db-svc.ns.svc.cluster.local
  • Use for: databases, Kafka, ZooKeeper, Redis

🏗️ StatefulSet Architecture

🌐 Headless Service (clusterIP: None) Provides stable DNS records — db-0.svc, db-1.svc, db-2.svc — no load balancing
📋 StatefulSet Controller Manages ordered creation, scaling, updates. Requires serviceName field
📦 Pods: db-0, db-1, db-2 Predictable names, stable identity, survive rescheduling
💾 PVCs: data-db-0, data-db-1, data-db-2 One PVC per pod via volumeClaimTemplates — NOT deleted when pod/StatefulSet is deleted

21.1 Stateless vs Stateful Applications

Before diving into StatefulSets, you must understand the fundamental difference between stateless and stateful applications. This determines which workload controller you should use.

🔄 Stateless vs Stateful — Visual Comparison

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  STATELESS APPLICATION (anihpj Django app)                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │  anihpj-abc │  │  anihpj-xyz │  │  anihpj-123 │   ◀── Random names     │
  │  │  🔵 Pod     │  │  🔵 Pod     │  │  🔵 Pod     │                        │
  │  │  Any can    │  │  Any can    │  │  Any can    │   ◀── Interchangeable │
  │  │  handle     │  │  handle     │  │  handle     │                        │
  │  │  request    │  │  request    │  │  request    │                        │
  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │         └────────────────┴────────────────┘                               │
  │                          │                                                │
  │                    No local state                                         │
  │                    Data lives in DB                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Use: Deployment + HPA                                                  │
  ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  STATEFUL APPLICATION (PostgreSQL database)                                │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │  postgres-0 │  │  postgres-1 │  │  postgres-2 │   ◀── Ordered names   │
  │  │  🟢 PRIMARY │  │  🟡 REPLICA │  │  🟡 REPLICA │                        │
  │  │  Writes     │  │  Reads      │  │  Reads      │   ◀── Unique roles    │
  │  │  Only this  │  │  Syncs from │  │  Syncs from │                        │
  │  │  gets data  │  │  primary    │  │  primary    │                        │
  │  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │    ┌────▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐      ┌────▼────┐                          │
  │    │ PVC-0   │      │ PVC-1   │      │ PVC-2   │   ◀── Own storage       │
  │    │ 💾 10Gi │      │ 💾 10Gi │      │ 💾 10Gi │                          │
  │    └─────────┘      └─────────┘      └─────────┘                          │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Use: StatefulSet                                                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Complete Comparison Table

Aspect Stateless (Deployment) Stateful (StatefulSet)
Pod Identity Random names: anihpj-7f8b9c-abc Ordered names: postgres-0, -1, -2
Storage Shared or none (ephemeral) Each pod gets own PVC (persistent)
Network Identity Random IP, accessed via Service Stable DNS per pod: pod-0.svc.ns
Scaling Up Any order (parallel OK) Sequential: 0 → 1 → 2 (ordered)
Scaling Down Any order (terminate any) Reverse: 2 → 1 → 0 (ordered)
Pod Restart New random name, new IP Same name, reconnects to same PVC
Example Apps Web apps, APIs, workers Databases, queues, caches

⚠️ Key Misconception

"My app stores data, so it must be stateful" — Wrong! If your Django app stores data in a database, the app itself is still stateless. The database is stateful. Your Django Deployment connects to a PostgreSQL StatefulSet.

  anihpj pods (stateless)          PostgreSQL pods (stateful)
  ┌──────────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
  │  ⚡ Deployment        │  ────▶ │  🗄️ StatefulSet       │
  │  3 interchangeable   │   SQL   │  1 primary + 2 replica│
  │  pods, no local data │         │  each has own storage │
  └──────────────────────┘         └──────────────────────┘
🌐

Stateless Examples

  • Django/Flask/React apps
  • REST APIs
  • Worker processes
  • Load balancers
  • Cron job containers

Use Deployment

🗄️

Stateful Examples

  • PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB
  • Kafka, RabbitMQ
  • Redis Cluster
  • Elasticsearch
  • Zookeeper, etcd

Use StatefulSet

21.2 StatefulSet Features

StatefulSets provide four key guarantees that Deployments don't. These are essential for running databases and other stateful applications.

🏗️ StatefulSet Architecture

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  StatefulSet: postgres   (requires Headless Service)                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐         │
  │  │   postgres-0     │  │   postgres-1     │  │   postgres-2     │         │
  │  │   ───────────    │  │   ───────────    │  │   ───────────    │         │
  │  │   🗄️ PRIMARY     │  │   🗄️ REPLICA     │  │   🗄️ REPLICA     │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  │  Stable DNS:     │  │  Stable DNS:     │  │  Stable DNS:     │         │
  │  │  postgres-0.     │  │  postgres-1.     │  │  postgres-2.     │         │
  │  │  postgres-svc.   │  │  postgres-svc.   │  │  postgres-svc.   │         │
  │  │  anihpj.svc.     │  │  anihpj.svc.     │  │  anihpj.svc.     │         │
  │  │  cluster.local   │  │  cluster.local   │  │  cluster.local   │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘  └────────┬─────────┘         │
  │           │                     │                     │                   │
  │  ┌────────▼─────────┐  ┌────────▼─────────┐  ┌────────▼─────────┐         │
  │  │     PVC-0        │  │     PVC-1        │  │     PVC-2        │         │
  │  │     💾 10Gi      │  │     💾 10Gi      │  │     💾 10Gi      │         │
  │  │  data-postgres-0 │  │  data-postgres-1 │  │  data-postgres-2 │         │
  │  │                  │  │                  │  │                  │         │
  │  │    PERSISTS!     │  │    PERSISTS!     │  │    PERSISTS!     │         │
  │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │  │  Pod dies? PVC   │         │
  │  │  survives! ✅    │  │  survives! ✅    │  │  survives! ✅    │         │
  │  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘  └──────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  CREATE ORDER: 0 ─────▶ 1 ─────▶ 2                                        │
  │                (wait ready) (wait ready)                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  DELETE ORDER: 2 ◀───── 1 ◀───── 0                                        │
  │                (terminate) (terminate)                                     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔑 Four Key StatefulSet Guarantees

🏷️
1. Stable Pod Names

Pods are named with a predictable pattern: <statefulset>-0, -1, -2...

postgres-0
postgres-1
postgres-2

Names survive restarts!

🌐
2. Stable Network ID

Each pod gets a stable DNS record via the Headless Service

<pod>.<svc>.<ns>.svc.cluster.local

Clients can connect to specific pods

💾
3. Stable Storage

Each pod gets its own PVC via volumeClaimTemplates

data-postgres-0
data-postgres-1

PVC persists even if pod deleted!

📋
4. Ordered Operations

Pods are created/deleted in order, one at a time

Create: 0→1→2
Delete: 2→1→0

Essential for primary/replica setup

💡 Why Ordered Operations Matter

For databases like PostgreSQL with primary/replica architecture:

  • postgres-0 must be running and ready before postgres-1 starts (replica needs to connect to primary)
  • Scaling down removes postgres-2 first (replica), never the primary
  • This ensures data consistency and proper replication

📜 Headless Service — Required for StatefulSets

A headless service (clusterIP: None) gives each pod its own DNS record instead of a single cluster IP.

postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
# Headless Service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# This creates DNS entries for each pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-svc
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
spec:
  clusterIP: None  # ◀── This makes it a headless service
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
      name: postgres

# Result DNS records:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
# postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# postgres-2.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────

21.3 When to Use StatefulSets

StatefulSets add complexity. Use them only when you need their guarantees.

✅ Use StatefulSet When

  • Databases: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Cassandra
  • Message Queues: Kafka, RabbitMQ, NATS
  • Distributed Caches: Redis Cluster, Memcached
  • Search Engines: Elasticsearch, Solr
  • Coordination Systems: Zookeeper, etcd, Consul
  • Any app that needs:
    • Stable network identity
    • Persistent per-pod storage
    • Ordered scaling/updates

❌ Don't Use StatefulSet When

  • Web Applications: Django, Flask, React, Node.js
  • REST APIs: Any stateless HTTP service
  • Background Workers: Celery, Sidekiq
  • Cron Jobs: Scheduled tasks
  • Any app where:
    • Pods are interchangeable
    • State lives in external database
    • Order doesn't matter
  • Use Deployment + HPA instead!

🚨 Common Mistakes

Mistake Why It's Wrong Correct Approach
"Django stores sessions, so StatefulSet" Sessions go in Redis/DB, not pods Use Deployment for Django
"I need persistent logs" Logs should go to central logging Use Deployment + Fluentd
"I want stable pod names" Name stability alone isn't enough reason Use Deployment, identify by labels
"Running PostgreSQL? Always StatefulSet" For dev/test, simple Deployment is OK StatefulSet for production DBs

🤔 Decision Flowchart: Deployment vs StatefulSet

                          ┌─────────────────────────┐
                          │   Your Application      │
                          └───────────┬─────────────┘
                                      │
                          ┌───────────▼─────────────┐
                          │ Does each pod need its  │
                          │ OWN persistent storage? │
                          └───────────┬─────────────┘
                                      │
                         ┌────────────┴────────────┐
                         │                         │
                        YES                        NO
                         │                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐       ┌────────▼────────┐
                │ Does pod need   │       │                 │
                │ stable network  │       │   Use           │
                │ identity (DNS)? │       │   DEPLOYMENT    │
                └────────┬────────┘       │                 │
                         │                └─────────────────┘
                        YES
                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐
                │ Does order of   │
                │ create/delete   │
                │ matter?         │
                └────────┬────────┘
                         │
                        YES
                         │
                ┌────────▼────────┐
                │                 │
                │   Use           │
                │   STATEFULSET   │
                │                 │
                └─────────────────┘

  Examples:
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  PostgreSQL, Kafka, Elasticsearch ───▶ StatefulSet ✅
  Django, React, API servers       ───▶ Deployment  ✅
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

21.4 Running PostgreSQL for anihpj

Let's put theory into practice. We'll set up a production-ready PostgreSQL StatefulSet for our anihpj Django application with headless service, persistent volumes, and proper configuration.

🏗️ anihpj + PostgreSQL Architecture

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj Production Architecture                                              │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  EXTERNAL TRAFFIC                                                       │ │
  │  │                       ▼                                                 │ │
  │  │              ┌───────────────┐                                         │ │
  │  │              │   Ingress     │                                         │ │
  │  │              │  (anihpj.com) │                                         │ │
  │  │              └───────┬───────┘                                         │ │
  │  │                      │                                                 │ │
  │  │              ┌───────▼───────┐                                         │ │
  │  │              │   Service     │  ClusterIP (normal)                     │ │
  │  │              │ anihpj-svc    │                                         │ │
  │  │              └───────┬───────┘                                         │ │
  │  └───────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                          │                                                   │
  │  ┌───────────────────────▼─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  DEPLOYMENT: anihpj (stateless Django)                                  │ │
  │  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                  │ │
  │  │  │ anihpj-abc   │  │ anihpj-xyz   │  │ anihpj-123   │                  │ │
  │  │  │ 🐍 Django    │  │ 🐍 Django    │  │ 🐍 Django    │                  │ │
  │  │  │              │  │              │  │              │                  │ │
  │  │  │ Env:         │  │ Env:         │  │ Env:         │                  │ │
  │  │  │ DATABASE_URL │  │ DATABASE_URL │  │ DATABASE_URL │                  │ │
  │  │  │ =postgres-0. │  │ =postgres-0. │  │ =postgres-0. │                  │ │
  │  │  │  postgres-svc│  │  postgres-svc│  │  postgres-svc│                  │ │
  │  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                  │ │
  │  │              │              │              │                           │ │
  │  └──────────────┴──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                │                                             │
  │                        SQL connection                                        │
  │                                │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  HEADLESS SERVICE: postgres-svc (clusterIP: None)                       │ │
  │  │                                                                          │ │
  │  │  DNS: postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local ◀── connect here│ │
  │  │  DNS: postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local   (replica)      │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  │                                │                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────▼───────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
  │  │  STATEFULSET: postgres                                                   │ │
  │  │  ┌──────────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────────┐                     │ │
  │  │  │     postgres-0       │  │     postgres-1       │                     │ │
  │  │  │  🗄️ PRIMARY (writes) │  │  🗄️ REPLICA (reads)  │                     │ │
  │  │  │                      │  │  syncs from -0       │                     │ │
  │  │  └──────────┬───────────┘  └──────────┬───────────┘                     │ │
  │  │             │                         │                                  │ │
  │  │        ┌────▼────┐               ┌────▼────┐                            │ │
  │  │        │ PVC-0   │               │ PVC-1   │                            │ │
  │  │        │ 💾 10Gi │               │ 💾 10Gi │                            │ │
  │  │        └─────────┘               └─────────┘                            │ │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

21.4a Headless Service for PostgreSQL

First, create the headless service that will provide stable DNS for each PostgreSQL pod.

k8s/postgres-headless-svc.yaml 
# Headless Service for PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# Creates DNS entries: postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-svc
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
    component: database
spec:
  clusterIP: None    # ◀── HEADLESS: no single IP, individual pod DNS
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
    - name: postgres
      port: 5432
      targetPort: 5432
  # Important: publishNotReadyAddresses allows DNS even for not-ready pods
  publishNotReadyAddresses: true
🖥️ Apply the Headless Service
# Create namespace if not exists
kubectl create namespace anihpj --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Apply the headless service
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-headless-svc.yaml

# Verify it's headless (clusterIP should be "None")
kubectl get svc postgres-svc -n anihpj
# NAME          TYPE       CLUSTER-IP   PORT(S)    AGE
# postgres-svc  ClusterIP  None         5432/TCP   5s

21.4b StatefulSet for PostgreSQL

Now create the StatefulSet with persistent storage for each pod.

k8s/postgres-statefulset.yaml 
# PostgreSQL StatefulSet for anihpj
# Production-ready with persistent storage
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-svc    # ◀── Must match headless service name
  replicas: 2                  # Primary + 1 replica
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: postgres
          image: postgres:15-alpine
          ports:
            - containerPort: 5432
              name: postgres
          
          # Environment variables from Secret
          env:
            - name: POSTGRES_DB
              value: anihpj_db
            - name: POSTGRES_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: username
            - name: POSTGRES_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: password
            - name: PGDATA
              value: /var/lib/postgresql/data/pgdata
          
          # Resource limits (adjust for your cluster)
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "250m"
            limits:
              memory: "512Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          
          # Mount the persistent volume
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
          
          # Health checks
          readinessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - pg_isready
                - -U
                - $(POSTGRES_USER)
                - -d
                - $(POSTGRES_DB)
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          
          livenessProbe:
            exec:
              command:
                - pg_isready
                - -U
                - $(POSTGRES_USER)
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10

  # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  # │  volumeClaimTemplates — each pod gets its own PVC               │
  # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data    # Creates: data-postgres-0, data-postgres-1, etc.
      spec:
        accessModes:
          - ReadWriteOnce
        storageClassName: standard  # Use your cluster's storage class
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: 10Gi

💡 volumeClaimTemplates Explained

Unlike Deployments that share volumes, StatefulSets use volumeClaimTemplates to create a unique PVC for each pod:

PodPVC CreatedData Location
postgres-0data-postgres-0Independent 10Gi volume
postgres-1data-postgres-1Independent 10Gi volume
postgres-2data-postgres-2Independent 10Gi volume

Critical: If you delete the pod, the PVC survives! When the pod restarts, it reconnects to the same PVC.

🔐 Create the Secret First
k8s/postgres-secret.yaml 
# PostgreSQL credentials (use sealed secrets in production!)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: postgres-secret
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:    # Using stringData for plain text (auto base64 encoded)
  username: anihpj_user
  password: "super-secret-password-change-me"  # ◀── Change this!
  
# ⚠️ WARNING: Don't commit real passwords to git!
# Use: kubectl create secret generic postgres-secret \
#        --from-literal=username=anihpj_user \
#        --from-literal=password=$(openssl rand -base64 32)
🖥️ Deploy PostgreSQL StatefulSet
# Create the secret (use proper secrets management in prod!)
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-secret.yaml

# Deploy the StatefulSet
kubectl apply -f k8s/postgres-statefulset.yaml

# Watch pods being created IN ORDER
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -w
# NAME         READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
# postgres-0   0/1     Pending   0          0s
# postgres-0   0/1     ContainerCreating   0          2s
# postgres-0   1/1     Running             0          10s   ◀── 0 ready first
# postgres-1   0/1     Pending             0          0s    ◀── THEN 1 starts
# postgres-1   1/1     Running             0          12s

# Check PVCs created
kubectl get pvc -n anihpj
# NAME              STATUS   VOLUME    CAPACITY   ACCESS MODES
# data-postgres-0   Bound    pv-xxx    10Gi       RWO
# data-postgres-1   Bound    pv-yyy    10Gi       RWO

21.4c Connecting anihpj to PostgreSQL

Configure your Django application to connect to the PostgreSQL StatefulSet using its stable DNS name.

k8s/anihpj-deployment.yaml (updated) 
# anihpj Django Deployment connecting to PostgreSQL StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: yourregistry/anihpj:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          
          env:
            # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
            # │  DATABASE_URL points to StatefulSet via headless DNS    │
            # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
            - name: DATABASE_HOST
              # Connect to primary (postgres-0) for writes
              value: "postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local"
            
            - name: DATABASE_PORT
              value: "5432"
            
            - name: DATABASE_NAME
              value: "anihpj_db"
            
            - name: DATABASE_USER
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: username
            
            - name: DATABASE_PASSWORD
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: postgres-secret
                  key: password
            
            # Or use a single DATABASE_URL environment variable
            # DATABASE_URL: postgres://user:pass@postgres-0.postgres-svc/db

💡 DNS Connection Patterns

Use CaseDNS to UseExample
Write to primary Specific pod DNS postgres-0.postgres-svc
Read from any replica Service DNS (round-robin) postgres-svc
Read from specific replica Specific pod DNS postgres-1.postgres-svc
🐍 Django settings.py Configuration
anihpj/settings.py 
import os

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DATABASE_NAME', 'anihpj_db'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DATABASE_USER', 'anihpj_user'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PASSWORD', ''),
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DATABASE_HOST', 'localhost'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DATABASE_PORT', '5432'),
        'OPTIONS': {
            'connect_timeout': 10,
        },
    }
}

# For read replicas (optional):
# 'read_replica': {
#     'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
#     'HOST': 'postgres-1.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local',
#     ...
# }

21.4d Initializing the Database

After deploying PostgreSQL, you need to run Django migrations to set up the database schema.

🖥️ Database Initialization Commands
# 1. Verify PostgreSQL is running
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -l app=postgres
# NAME         READY   STATUS    AGE
# postgres-0   1/1     Running   5m
# postgres-1   1/1     Running   4m

# 2. Connect to PostgreSQL to verify
kubectl exec -it postgres-0 -n anihpj -- psql -U anihpj_user -d anihpj_db
# psql (15.2)
# anihpj_db=# \dt
# No relations found.  (empty database, ready for migrations!)
# anihpj_db=# \q

# 3. Run Django migrations (one-time Job or exec)
kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj -n anihpj -- python manage.py migrate
# Operations to perform:
#   Apply all migrations: admin, auth, contenttypes, jobpost, sessions
# Running migrations:
#   Applying contenttypes.0001_initial... OK
#   Applying auth.0001_initial... OK
#   ...

# 4. Create Django superuser (optional)
kubectl exec -it deploy/anihpj -n anihpj -- python manage.py createsuperuser

# 5. Verify tables created
kubectl exec -it postgres-0 -n anihpj -- psql -U anihpj_user -d anihpj_db -c "\dt"
#               List of relations
#  Schema |            Name             | Type  |   Owner
# --------+-----------------------------+-------+-----------
#  public | auth_group                  | table | anihpj_user
#  public | auth_user                   | table | anihpj_user
#  public | jobpost_job                 | table | anihpj_user
#  ...

⚠️ Migration Best Practices

  • Use a Job: For production, use a Kubernetes Job to run migrations during deployment
  • Rolling migrations: Ensure migrations are backward compatible so old pods work during rollout
  • Never run concurrent migrations: Only one migration job should run at a time

21.5 StatefulSet Update Strategies

StatefulSets support two update strategies. Unlike Deployments, updates happen in reverse ordinal order (highest to lowest) to protect the primary.

📊 Update Strategies Comparison

Strategy Behavior Use When
RollingUpdate Updates pods automatically
Order: 2 → 1 → 0 (reverse)
One pod at a time 		Default choice for most apps
OnDelete Manual control
Pod updates only when deleted
You control the pace 		Database version upgrades
Critical systems
partition: N Canary updates
Only pods ≥ N get updated
Gradual rollout 		Testing new version on replicas
before updating primary

🔄 RollingUpdate (Default) — Reverse Order

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  StatefulSet RollingUpdate: postgres v14 → v15                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 1: Initial state (all v14)                                          │
