  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │                     │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 2: Update highest ordinal FIRST (postgres-2)                        │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  ◀── Updated      │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │  ◀── first!        │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 3: Continue with postgres-1                                         │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v14 🔵   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │                     │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Step 4: Finally update primary (postgres-0) — LAST!                      │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │  │  postgres-0  │  │  postgres-1  │  │  postgres-2  │                     │
  │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  │  🗄️ v15 🟢   │  ✅ Complete       │
  │  │  PRIMARY     │  │  REPLICA     │  │  REPLICA     │                     │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  💡 Primary updated LAST = safer! Replicas verified first.                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📜 Partition Strategy — Canary for StatefulSets

postgres-statefulset.yaml (partition strategy) 
# Using partition for canary updates
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  replicas: 3
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      partition: 2    # ◀── Only pods with ordinal ≥ 2 get updated
  # ... rest of spec

# How partition works:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# partition: 2 → Only postgres-2 gets the new spec
# partition: 1 → postgres-1 and postgres-2 get new spec
# partition: 0 → All pods get new spec (default behavior)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# Workflow:
# 1. Set partition: 2, update image to v15
# 2. Only postgres-2 updates — verify it works
# 3. Set partition: 1 — postgres-1 updates
# 4. Set partition: 0 — postgres-0 (primary) finally updates
🖥️ Update Strategy Commands
# Check current update strategy
kubectl get sts postgres -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.updateStrategy}'

# Trigger a rolling update (change image version)
kubectl set image sts/postgres postgres=postgres:15.1-alpine -n anihpj

# Watch the rollout (note the reverse order!)
kubectl rollout status sts/postgres -n anihpj

# Use partition for canary update
kubectl patch sts postgres -n anihpj -p '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":2}}}}'

# After verifying postgres-2, reduce partition
kubectl patch sts postgres -n anihpj -p '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":0}}}}'

21.6 Backup and Restore Strategies

Data durability is critical for databases. Here are production-ready backup strategies for your PostgreSQL StatefulSet.

📋

pg_dump CronJob

Traditional logical backup using CronJob

  • SQL dump to cloud storage
  • Easy to restore anywhere
  • Good for small/medium DBs
📸

Volume Snapshots

CSI snapshot of the entire volume

  • Fast point-in-time recovery
  • Instant clone for dev/test
  • Requires CSI snapshot support
🏠

Velero

Full cluster backup solution

  • Backs up resources + volumes
  • Disaster recovery
  • Cluster migration

📜 pg_dump CronJob for Automated Backups

k8s/postgres-backup-cronjob.yaml 
# Automated PostgreSQL backup CronJob
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: postgres-backup
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"    # ◀── Every day at 2 AM
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: postgres:15-alpine
              env:
                - name: PGPASSWORD
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: postgres-secret
                      key: password
                - name: PGUSER
                  valueFrom:
                    secretKeyRef:
                      name: postgres-secret
                      key: username
              
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  # Generate backup filename with timestamp
                  BACKUP_FILE="anihpj-$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S).sql.gz"
                  
                  # Run pg_dump and compress
                  pg_dump -h postgres-0.postgres-svc.anihpj.svc.cluster.local \
                          -U $PGUSER \
                          -d anihpj_db \
                          --format=plain | gzip > /backup/$BACKUP_FILE
                  
                  # Upload to S3 (requires aws-cli, configure with your bucket)
                  # aws s3 cp /backup/$BACKUP_FILE s3://your-bucket/postgres-backups/
                  
                  echo "Backup completed: $BACKUP_FILE"
              
              volumeMounts:
                - name: backup-storage
                  mountPath: /backup
          
          volumes:
            - name: backup-storage
              emptyDir: {}
              # For persistent backups, use a PVC:
              # persistentVolumeClaim:
              #   claimName: backup-pvc

📸 Volume Snapshot Architecture

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  CSI Volume Snapshots for PostgreSQL                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐                                                  │
  │  │  postgres-0         │                                                  │
  │  │  🗄️ PRIMARY         │                                                  │
  │  └──────────┬──────────┘                                                  │
  │             │                                                             │
  │        ┌────▼────┐        VolumeSnapshot                                  │
  │        │ PVC-0   │  ──────────────────────┐                              │
  │        │ 💾 10Gi │                        │                              │
  │        └─────────┘                        ▼                              │
  │                              ┌─────────────────────────┐                  │
  │                              │  📸 Snapshot             │                  │
  │                              │  postgres-0-snap-001     │                  │
  │                              │  2024-01-15 02:00:00     │                  │
  │                              │                          │                  │
  │                              │  Can restore to:         │                  │
  │                              │  • Same PVC (rollback)   │                  │
  │                              │  • New PVC (clone)       │                  │
  │                              │  • Different namespace   │                  │
  │                              └─────────────────────────┘                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Commands:                                                                 │
  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────               │
  │  # Create snapshot                                                         │
  │  kubectl apply -f volume-snapshot.yaml                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  # List snapshots                                                          │
  │  kubectl get volumesnapshots -n anihpj                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  # Restore: create PVC from snapshot                                       │
  │  kubectl apply -f pvc-from-snapshot.yaml                                   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💡 Backup Best Practices

PracticeWhy
Backup from replica, not primary Reduces load on primary
Test restores regularly Untested backups aren't backups
Store backups off-cluster Survives cluster failure
Use 3-2-1 rule 3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite
Encrypt backups Protect sensitive data

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: StatefulSets

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario: Deploy a StatefulSet for a simple key-value store that requires stable network identity and persistent storage.
  1. Create a namespace stateful-test
  2. Create a Headless Service kvstore-svc (clusterIP: None) on port 6379
  3. Create a StatefulSet kvstore with 3 replicas using image redis:7
  4. Include a volumeClaimTemplate requesting 1Gi of storage
  5. Verify pods are named kvstore-0, kvstore-1, kvstore-2
  6. Verify DNS: kvstore-0.kvstore-svc.stateful-test.svc.cluster.local resolves
💡 Hint: StatefulSets require a serviceName field matching the Headless Service. Pods get DNS entries automatically.
📖 View Solution
Solution — StatefulSet Drill 
# 1. Create namespace
kubectl create namespace stateful-test

# 2. Create Headless Service
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: kvstore-svc
  namespace: stateful-test
spec:
  clusterIP: None          # ← Headless!
  selector:
    app: kvstore
  ports:
  - port: 6379
    targetPort: 6379
EOF

# 3 & 4. Create StatefulSet with volumeClaimTemplate
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: kvstore
  namespace: stateful-test
spec:
  serviceName: kvstore-svc    # ← Must match service name
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: kvstore
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: kvstore
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: redis
        image: redis:7
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 1Gi
EOF

# 5. Verify pod names (ordered: 0, 1, 2)
kubectl get pods -n stateful-test
# kvstore-0   Running
# kvstore-1   Running
# kvstore-2   Running

# 6. Verify DNS resolution
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox --rm -it -n stateful-test \
  -- nslookup kvstore-0.kvstore-svc.stateful-test.svc.cluster.local

Chapter 21 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet? When should you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureDeploymentStatefulSet
Pod namesRandom (web-abc123)Ordinal (db-0, db-1, db-2)
StorageShared or ephemeralDedicated PVC per pod
Startup orderParallel (any order)Sequential (0→1→2)
DNSService only (no per-pod DNS)Stable per-pod DNS name
ScalingAny pod can be removedLast ordinal removed first
Use forStateless (web, API)Stateful (DB, Kafka, Redis)
Explanation

StatefulSets provide three guarantees that Deployments don't: (1) stable, unique pod names (ordinal indices), (2) stable persistent storage (PVC per pod survives rescheduling), (3) ordered, graceful deployment and scaling. On the CKAD, know when to choose StatefulSet over Deployment — the answer is always "when you need stable identity or dedicated storage."

Q2
Write a StatefulSet with 3 replicas for PostgreSQL, each pod getting its own 10Gi volume. Include the headless Service.
Show Answer
Answer
# Headless Service (required for StatefulSet)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: postgres-headless
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: postgres
  ports:
  - port: 5432
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: postgres
spec:
  serviceName: postgres-headless  # Must match headless Service
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: postgres
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: postgres
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: postgres
        image: postgres:15
        ports:
        - containerPort: 5432
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
Explanation

serviceName MUST reference a headless Service (clusterIP: None). volumeClaimTemplates creates a unique PVC for each pod: data-postgres-0, data-postgres-1, data-postgres-2. Pods start in order: postgres-0 first, then postgres-1 after 0 is Ready. On the CKAD, this full pattern (headless Service + StatefulSet + volumeClaimTemplates) is a high-value question.

Q3
How do you address specific pods in a StatefulSet by DNS? What is the DNS format?
Show Answer
Answer
# DNS format for StatefulSet pods:
# <pod-name>.<headless-service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local

# Examples (StatefulSet: postgres, headless svc: postgres-headless):
postgres-0.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
postgres-1.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local
postgres-2.postgres-headless.default.svc.cluster.local

# Test from another pod:
kubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- \
  nslookup postgres-0.postgres-headless
  StatefulSet: postgres (replicas: 3)
  Headless Service: postgres-headless

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │ postgres-0.postgres-headless  → PVC: data-postgres-0 │
  │ postgres-1.postgres-headless  → PVC: data-postgres-1 │
  │ postgres-2.postgres-headless  → PVC: data-postgres-2 │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Explanation

Each StatefulSet pod gets a stable DNS name: pod-name.headless-service. This DNS name persists even if the pod is rescheduled to a different node. This is critical for database replication: the primary can always be found at postgres-0.postgres-headless. On the CKAD, the DNS format for StatefulSet pods is a must-know pattern.

Q4
What happens to PVCs when you delete a StatefulSet or scale it down?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scale down from 3 to 1
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=1
# postgres-2 deleted, postgres-1 deleted
# BUT: PVCs data-postgres-1 and data-postgres-2 REMAIN!

kubectl get pvc
# data-postgres-0   Bound
# data-postgres-1   Bound   ← Still exists!
# data-postgres-2   Bound   ← Still exists!

# Scale back up — pods reattach to existing PVCs
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=3
# postgres-1 gets data-postgres-1 back (data preserved!)

# Manual PVC cleanup required
kubectl delete pvc data-postgres-1 data-postgres-2
Explanation

PVCs are NEVER automatically deleted when scaling down or deleting a StatefulSet — this is a safety feature to prevent data loss. When scaling back up, pods automatically reattach to their original PVCs. You must manually delete PVCs to reclaim storage. In K8s 1.27+, persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy can automate cleanup. On the CKAD, know that PVCs persist — it's a common exam trap.

Q5
What is podManagementPolicy? What's the difference between OrderedReady and Parallel?
Show Answer
Answer
PolicyStartup BehaviorUse Case
OrderedReady (default)Pods created one at a time (0→1→2). Each must be Ready before next starts.Databases with replication
ParallelAll pods start simultaneouslyStateful apps that don't need ordered boot
 
spec:
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel  # All pods start at once
Explanation

OrderedReady ensures pod-0 is Running and Ready before creating pod-1. This matters for databases where the primary (pod-0) must be up before replicas join. Parallel is faster for apps that just need stable identity/storage but don't depend on boot order. On the CKAD, default OrderedReady is assumed unless stated otherwise.

Q6
Why does a StatefulSet require a headless Service? What happens without it?
Show Answer
Answer

A headless Service (clusterIP: None) is required because it enables per-pod DNS names. Without it:

  • The serviceName field in StatefulSet references this Service
  • Without a matching headless Service, pods don't get stable DNS names
  • The StatefulSet will still create pods, but DNS-based discovery won't work
  • Applications can't address specific pods (e.g., "always connect to the primary")
# Regular Service: nslookup returns ONE ClusterIP
nslookup my-svc  → 10.96.0.5

# Headless Service: nslookup returns ALL pod IPs
nslookup my-headless → 10.244.0.5, 10.244.1.3, 10.244.2.7
Explanation

The headless Service provides the DNS subdomain for the StatefulSet pods. Format: pod-name.headless-svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local. Without a headless Service, you'd need to use pod IPs (which are ephemeral). The serviceName in the StatefulSet spec MUST match the headless Service name. On the CKAD, if asked to create a StatefulSet, always create the headless Service alongside it.

Q7
What are updateStrategy options for StatefulSets? How is it different from Deployment update strategies?
Show Answer
Answer
# RollingUpdate (default) — reverse order: 2→1→0
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      partition: 2    # Only update pods with ordinal ≥ 2

# OnDelete — manual control
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: OnDelete    # Pods only update when manually deleted
Explanation

StatefulSet rolling updates go in REVERSE order (highest ordinal first: 2→1→0) — opposite of Deployments. The partition parameter enables canary updates: setting partition: 2 updates only pods with ordinal ≥ 2, leaving 0 and 1 on the old version. OnDelete requires you to manually delete pods to trigger updates — maximum control for sensitive databases. The CKAD may test the partition concept.

Q8
Scale a StatefulSet from 3 to 5 replicas. What is the order of pod creation?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scale up
kubectl scale statefulset postgres --replicas=5

# Order with OrderedReady:
# postgres-3 created → wait until Ready
# postgres-4 created → wait until Ready

# Verify
kubectl get pods -w  # Watch in real-time
# postgres-3   0/1   Pending → ContainerCreating → Running
# postgres-4   0/1   Pending → ContainerCreating → Running

# New PVCs created automatically:
kubectl get pvc
# data-postgres-3   Bound
# data-postgres-4   Bound
Explanation

Scale up: pods created in ascending order (3, then 4). Scale down: pods deleted in descending order (4, then 3). With OrderedReady, each new pod must be Ready before the next is created. New PVCs are created from volumeClaimTemplates. Existing pods (0-2) are unaffected. On the CKAD, the ordering guarantee is what makes StatefulSets special.

Q9
A StatefulSet pod postgres-1 is stuck in Pending state. Pods 0 and 2 are running. Why might pod-1 be stuck?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check events
kubectl describe pod postgres-1
# Check for: "no persistent volumes available for this claim"

# Check PVC status
kubectl get pvc data-postgres-1
# STATUS: Pending ← PVC can't bind!

# Common causes:
# 1. No StorageClass available or default not set
kubectl get sc

# 2. Node affinity prevents scheduling
kubectl get nodes -o wide

# 3. Insufficient resources on available nodes
kubectl describe node <node> | grep -A5 Allocated
Explanation

Most common reason: the PVC can't bind — no StorageClass provisioner, or no available PV matching the request. StatefulSets won't create pod N+1 until pod N is Ready (with OrderedReady). If pod-0 ran fine but pod-1 can't schedule, it's likely a node capacity issue (only one node has the right storage, and it's full). On the CKAD, always check PVC status and describe the pod for events.

Q10
What is volumeClaimTemplates? How is it different from regular volumes in a pod spec?
Show Answer
Answer
FeaturevolumeClaimTemplates (StatefulSet)volumes + PVC (Deployment)
PVC creationAutomatic per pod (data-app-0, data-app-1)Manual, shared by all pods
Storage isolationEach pod has its own PVCAll pods share one PVC
PVC lifecycleSurvives pod deletionManaged separately
Namingtemplate-name-sts-name-ordinalUser-defined name
Explanation

volumeClaimTemplates is a StatefulSet-only feature that creates a unique PVC for each pod replica using a template. The PVC naming convention is {volumeClaimTemplate.name}-{statefulset.name}-{ordinal}. This ensures data isolation: postgres-0's data never mixes with postgres-1's. Regular volumes reference a single pre-existing PVC shared by all pods. This is a fundamental CKAD concept for stateful workloads.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — StatefulSet Data Encryption & Pod Identity

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

StatefulSets manage stateful workloads with persistent data — making them high-value targets. Compromising a StatefulSet pod can expose databases, message queues, and other sensitive data stores.

RiskImpactMitigation
Unencrypted PVsData at rest exposed if disk stolenUse encrypted StorageClass, LUKS
Inter-replica plaintextReplication traffic sniffableTLS between StatefulSet pods
Pod identity spoofingRogue pod claims stable hostnamemTLS with per-pod certificates
PVC data persistenceDeleted pods leave data on PVsReclaim policy: Delete, encryption
 
# Encrypted StorageClass for StatefulSets
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: encrypted-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs   # Or pd.csi.storage.gke.io
parameters:
  type: gp3
  encrypted: "true"                  # Enable encryption at rest
  kmsKeyId: "arn:aws:kms:REGION:ACCOUNT:key/KEY-ID"
reclaimPolicy: Delete               # Remove data when PVC deleted
allowVolumeExpansion: true

Chapter 21 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create a StatefulSet with encrypted persistent volumes and a hardened security context.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: secure-db
  namespace: production
spec:
  serviceName: secure-db
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secure-db
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: secure-db
    spec:
      automountServiceAccountToken: false
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 999
        fsGroup: 999
        seccompProfile:
          type: RuntimeDefault
      containers:
      - name: db
        image: postgres@sha256:verified...
        securityContext:
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: false  # DB needs writes
          capabilities:
            drop: ["ALL"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /var/lib/postgresql/data
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      storageClassName: encrypted-ssd   # Encrypted storage
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
Explanation

StatefulSets with persistent data must use encrypted StorageClasses. On CKS, combine data-at-rest encryption (StorageClass) with hardened pod security (non-root, dropped capabilities, seccomp). Note readOnlyRootFilesystem is often false for databases — use encrypted volumes instead.

CKS-2
How do you configure TLS encryption between StatefulSet replicas (e.g., MongoDB replica set)?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create TLS certificates for each pod using stable DNS names
# StatefulSet pods get: pod-0.svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
for i in 0 1 2; do
  openssl req -new -x509 -days 365 -nodes \
    -out "mongo-${i}.crt" \
    -keyout "mongo-${i}.key" \
    -subj "/CN=mongo-${i}.mongo-svc.production.svc.cluster.local"
done

# 2. Create Secret with TLS certs
kubectl create secret generic mongo-tls \
  --from-file=mongo-0.crt --from-file=mongo-0.key \
  --from-file=mongo-1.crt --from-file=mongo-1.key \
  --from-file=mongo-2.crt --from-file=mongo-2.key \
  --from-file=ca.crt \
  -n production
Explanation

StatefulSet pods get stable DNS names (e.g., mongo-0.mongo-svc) — use these as certificate CNs for per-pod TLS. On CKS, inter-pod encryption prevents eavesdropping on replication traffic. Use cert-manager for automatic certificate lifecycle management.

CKS-3
How do you ensure PersistentVolumes are properly cleaned up when a StatefulSet is deleted?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Check reclaim policy of StorageClass
kubectl get sc encrypted-ssd -o jsonpath='{.reclaimPolicy}'
# Should be "Delete" to prevent data leaks

# 2. After deleting StatefulSet, manually clean PVCs
kubectl delete statefulset secure-db -n production
# PVCs are NOT auto-deleted!
kubectl get pvc -n production -l app=secure-db
kubectl delete pvc -n production -l app=secure-db

# 3. Verify PVs are released/deleted
kubectl get pv | grep production
Explanation

StatefulSet PVCs survive pod AND StatefulSet deletion — this is by design but creates a security risk. Old PVs may contain sensitive data. On CKS, use reclaimPolicy: Delete and manually clean PVCs after StatefulSet deletion. For compliance, verify PVs are actually deleted, not just released.

CKS-4
How do you use NetworkPolicy to restrict a StatefulSet so pods can only communicate with their own replicas and a specific client?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-network-policy
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secure-db
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: secure-db         # Allow inter-replica traffic
    ports:
    - port: 27017                # DB replication port
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: web-backend       # Only this client
    ports:
    - port: 27017
  egress:
  - to:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: secure-db         # Replicas can talk to each other
    ports:
    - port: 27017
  - to:                          # DNS
    - namespaceSelector: {}
      podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          k8s-app: kube-dns
    ports:
    - port: 53
      protocol: UDP
Explanation

Databases in StatefulSets should have tight network restrictions — only allow inter-replica replication and authorized client access. On CKS, use NetworkPolicy to enforce least-privilege networking for stateful workloads. Block all other ingress and egress traffic.

CKS-5
How do you audit StatefulSets for security compliance across all namespaces?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check for StatefulSets running as root
kubectl get sts -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

# Check for unencrypted storage
kubectl get sts -A -o json | jq '.items[] | 
  .spec.volumeClaimTemplates[] | 
  {sc: .spec.storageClassName}' | sort -u

# Verify each StorageClass is encrypted
kubectl get sc -o json | jq '.items[] | 
  {name: .metadata.name, encrypted: .parameters.encrypted}'

# Check for SA token auto-mount
kubectl get sts -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.automountServiceAccountToken != false
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'
Explanation

Regular auditing of StatefulSets is critical because they manage persistent, high-value data. On CKS, check: root execution, unencrypted storage, SA token mounting, and missing NetworkPolicies. StatefulSets are high-priority audit targets due to their data persistence.

Chapter 22: DaemonSets — One Pod Per Node

Some workloads need to run on every node in your cluster — log collectors, monitoring agents, network plugins. DaemonSets ensure exactly one pod per node, automatically adjusting as nodes join or leave. This chapter shows you when and how to use them.

📝 Log Collection 📊 Monitoring 🌐 Network Plugins 🟢 Beginner-Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What DaemonSets are and how they work
  • ✅ Common use cases — logging, monitoring, networking
  • ✅ Writing DaemonSet YAML from scratch
  • ✅ Node selection — targeting specific nodes
  • ✅ DaemonSet vs Deployment — when to use which
🏗️ CKAD Domain 1: Application Design & Build 20% of Exam

DaemonSets are a lower-frequency CKAD topic but can appear. Know the concept (one pod per node) and common use cases (logging, monitoring). You may need to identify when a DaemonSet is the right choice.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: DaemonSet Essentials

Quick facts for the exam:

  • DaemonSets have no replicas field — automatically one per node
  • Use nodeSelector or nodeAffinity to target specific nodes
  • Common use cases: Fluentd (logging), Prometheus Node Exporter (monitoring), kube-proxy (networking)
  • When a new node joins → DaemonSet automatically schedules a pod on it

DaemonSet YAML — Template

DaemonSets have no replicas field. One pod per matching node, always.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: log-collector
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentd
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: fluentd
    spec:
      # Target specific nodes (optional)
      nodeSelector:
        node-role: worker          # ← Only on worker nodes
      tolerations:                 # ← Run on tainted nodes too
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd:v1.16
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "200Mi"
          limits:
            memory: "500Mi"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: varlog
          mountPath: /var/log
      volumes:
      - name: varlog
        hostPath:                  # ← Access node's /var/log
          path: /var/log

DaemonSet Traps

  • No replicas field — if asked "run on every node", DaemonSet is the answer, not replicas: nodeCount
  • Tolerations needed for control-plane/master nodes (they have taints by default)
  • DaemonSet update — default strategy is RollingUpdate. Can also use OnDelete (only update when pod manually deleted)
  • Cannot create DaemonSet imperatively — must write YAML (or kubectl create deploy --dry-run then change kind)

🖥️ Common DaemonSet Use Cases

📊
Monitoring Agent — Prometheus node-exporter, Datadog agent on every node
📝
Log Collector — Fluentd, Filebeat collecting logs from all nodes
🔌
CNI Plugin — Calico, Cilium, Weave network plugins on every node
💾
Storage Driver — CSI drivers, GlusterFS on storage-labeled nodes
🔒
Security Agent — Falco, Sysdig runtime security on every node
1 per node Guaranteed Placement
Auto Scales with Nodes
RollingUpdate Default Strategy
No replicas Field Not Available

22.1 What are DaemonSets?

A DaemonSet ensures that exactly one copy of a pod runs on every node (or a selected subset of nodes) in your cluster. It's like having one dedicated worker on every machine.

🔄 DaemonSet — One Pod Per Node

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DaemonSet: fluentd-log-collector                                          │
  │  "Exactly 1 pod per node — ALWAYS"                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐               │
  │  │    Node 1      │  │    Node 2      │  │    Node 3      │               │
  │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │               │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │               │
  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │               │
  │  │  │ 📦 app-1 │  │  │  │ 📦 app-x │  │  │  │ 📦 app-y │  │               │
  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │  │ 📦 app-z │  │  │  └──────────┘  │               │
  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │  ┌──────────┐  │               │
  │  │  │ 📦 app-2 │  │  │                │  │  │ 📦 app-q │  │               │
  │  │  └──────────┘  │  │                │  │  └──────────┘  │               │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │               │
  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │               │
  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │               │
  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │  │  ║ DAEMON  ║  │               │
  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │  │  ║(fluentd)║  │               │
  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │               │
  │  │   1 per node   │  │   1 per node   │  │   1 per node   │               │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✨ NEW NODE JOINS THE CLUSTER                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────────┐ │
  │  │    Node 1      │  │    Node 2      │  │    Node 3      │  │  Node 4  │ │
  │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │    (worker)    │  │  (NEW!)  │ │
  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │  ╔══════════╗  │  │          │ │
  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │  ║ 📝 LOG  ║  │  │╔════════╗│ │
  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │  ╚══════════╝  │  │║📝 LOG ║│ │
  │  │                │  │                │  │                │  │║AUTO!  ║│ │
  │  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  └────────────────┘  │╚════════╝│ │
  │                                                              └──────────┘ │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Node joins  → DaemonSet pod auto-created                              │
  │  🗑️ Node removed → DaemonSet pod auto-deleted                             │
  │  💡 No manual scaling needed — it's automatic!                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
✨
Auto Node Tracking

New node joins? Pod gets scheduled automatically.

🗑️
Auto Cleanup

Node removed? Pod gets garbage collected.

1️⃣
Exactly One

Guaranteed single pod per node — never more, never less.

22.2 Common DaemonSet Use Cases

DaemonSets are essential for infrastructure-level workloads that need to run everywhere in your cluster.

📝

a) Log Collection

Collect and ship logs from every node to a central location.

Popular Tools:
  • Fluentd / Fluent Bit — CNCF logging solution
  • Filebeat — Elastic Stack log shipper
  • Promtail — Loki/Grafana log agent
  • Vector — High-performance log pipeline
Node 1       Node 2       Node 3
┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐
│📝 FD│     │📝 FD│     │📝 FD│   FD = Fluentd
│  │  │     │  │  │     │  │  │
│  ▼  │     │  ▼  │     │  ▼  │
└──┼──┘     └──┼──┘     └──┼──┘
   │           │           │
   └─────┬─────┴─────┬─────┘
         │           │
         ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────────────────┐
    │ 📊 Elasticsearch    │
    │    or Loki          │
    └─────────────────────┘
📊

b) Node Monitoring

Collect CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics from each node.

Popular Tools:
  • Prometheus Node Exporter — Exposes node metrics
  • Datadog Agent — Full-stack observability
  • New Relic Agent — APM + infrastructure
  • cAdvisor — Container resource metrics
Node 1       Node 2       Node 3
┌─────┐     ┌─────┐     ┌─────┐
│📊 NE│     │📊 NE│     │📊 NE│   NE = Node Exporter
│:9100│     │:9100│     │:9100│   (metrics port)
└──▲──┘     └──▲──┘     └──▲──┘
   │           │           │
   └─────┬─────┴─────┬─────┘
         │
    ┌────▼─────────────┐
    │ 📈 Prometheus    │
    │    (scrapes all) │
    └──────────────────┘
🌐

c) Network Plugins (CNI)

Container Network Interface agents that handle pod networking.

Popular CNIs:
  • Calico — Network policy + BGP
  • Cilium — eBPF-based networking
  • Weave Net — Simple mesh networking
  • Flannel — Basic overlay network

📍 These are usually pre-installed by your cluster provider.

💾

d) Storage Plugins (CSI)

Container Storage Interface node components for volume management.

Examples:
  • AWS EBS CSI — EBS volume driver
  • GCP PD CSI — Persistent Disk driver
  • Azure Disk CSI — Azure Disk driver
  • Longhorn — Distributed storage

📍 The node plugin runs as DaemonSet; controller is Deployment.

🔒

e) Security Agents

Runtime security monitoring and intrusion detection on every node.

Popular Tools:
  • Falco — Runtime threat detection
  • Sysdig — Security + monitoring
  • Aqua Security — Container security
  • Twistlock — Vulnerability scanning

22.3 DaemonSet YAML Example

Let's create two practical DaemonSet examples: a log collector and a node monitoring agent.

📜 Example 1: Fluentd Log Collector

k8s/fluentd-daemonset.yaml 
# Fluentd DaemonSet — collects logs from every node
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: fluentd
  namespace: kube-system
  labels:
    app: fluentd
    component: logging
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentd
  
  # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  # │  updateStrategy — how to update DaemonSet pods                  │
  # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1    # ◀── Only 1 pod unavailable during update
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: fluentd
    spec:
      # Tolerate control plane taint (run on master nodes too)
      tolerations:
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
          effect: NoSchedule
        - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master
          effect: NoSchedule
      
      containers:
        - name: fluentd
          image: fluent/fluentd-kubernetes-daemonset:v1.16-debian-elasticsearch8
          
          env:
            - name: FLUENT_ELASTICSEARCH_HOST
              value: "elasticsearch.logging.svc.cluster.local"
            - name: FLUENT_ELASTICSEARCH_PORT
              value: "9200"
          
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "200Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "500Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          
          # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
          # │  Mount host paths to collect logs from nodes           │
          # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          volumeMounts:
            # Container logs (Docker/containerd)
            - name: varlog
              mountPath: /var/log
              readOnly: true
            
            # Container data directory
            - name: containers
              mountPath: /var/lib/docker/containers
              readOnly: true
            
            # Fluentd config
            - name: config
              mountPath: /fluentd/etc/
      
      # Terminate quickly during node drain
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      
      volumes:
        - name: varlog
          hostPath:
            path: /var/log
        - name: containers
          hostPath:
            path: /var/lib/docker/containers
        - name: config
          configMap:
            name: fluentd-config

📜 Example 2: Prometheus Node Exporter

k8s/node-exporter-daemonset.yaml 
# Prometheus Node Exporter DaemonSet
# Exposes hardware and OS metrics from each node
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-exporter
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    app: node-exporter
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-exporter
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-exporter
      annotations:
        prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
        prometheus.io/port: "9100"
    spec:
      # Run on ALL nodes including control plane
      tolerations:
        - operator: Exists
      
      # Use host network for accurate network metrics
      hostNetwork: true
      hostPID: true
      
      containers:
        - name: node-exporter
          image: prom/node-exporter:v1.7.0
          
          args:
            - "--path.procfs=/host/proc"
            - "--path.sysfs=/host/sys"
            - "--path.rootfs=/host/root"
            - "--collector.filesystem.ignored-mount-points=^/(sys|proc|dev|host|etc)($$|/)"
          
          ports:
            - containerPort: 9100
              name: metrics
              hostPort: 9100    # ◀── Expose on node IP
          
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "30Mi"
              cpu: "50m"
            limits:
              memory: "100Mi"
              cpu: "200m"
          
          # Security: read-only, non-root
          securityContext:
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            runAsNonRoot: true
            runAsUser: 65534   # nobody user
          
          volumeMounts:
            - name: proc
              mountPath: /host/proc
              readOnly: true
            - name: sys
              mountPath: /host/sys
              readOnly: true
            - name: root
              mountPath: /host/root
              mountPropagation: HostToContainer
              readOnly: true
      
      volumes:
        - name: proc
          hostPath:
            path: /proc
        - name: sys
          hostPath:
            path: /sys
        - name: root
          hostPath:
            path: /
🖥️ DaemonSet Management Commands
# Apply DaemonSet
kubectl apply -f k8s/fluentd-daemonset.yaml

# Check DaemonSet status (should match node count)
kubectl get ds -n kube-system
# NAME      DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   NODE SELECTOR   AGE
# fluentd   3         3         3       3            3                     1h

# List pods — should be one per node
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l app=fluentd -o wide
# NAME            READY   STATUS    NODE
# fluentd-abc12   1/1     Running   node-1
# fluentd-def34   1/1     Running   node-2
# fluentd-ghi56   1/1     Running   node-3

# View an individual pod's logs
kubectl logs -n kube-system daemonset/fluentd

# Describe DaemonSet for events
kubectl describe ds fluentd -n kube-system

22.4 Node Selection with DaemonSets

By default, DaemonSets run on every node. But you can target specific nodes using nodeSelector, nodeAffinity, or tolerations.

🎯 Node Selection Methods

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Node Selection Methods for DaemonSets                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  1. nodeSelector — Simple label matching                            │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  nodeSelector:                                                      │   │
  │  │    node-type: worker     ◀── Only nodes with this label            │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  Cluster:                                                           │   │
  │  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ✗ ┌─────────┐              │   │
  │  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │    │ control │              │   │
  │  │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │    │ ✗ SKIP │              │   │
  │  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘    └─────────┘              │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  2. tolerations — Run on tainted nodes                              │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  tolerations:                                                       │   │
  │  │    - key: gpu                                                       │   │
  │  │      operator: Exists       ◀── Tolerates GPU taint                 │   │
  │  │      effect: NoSchedule                                             │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                             │   │
  │  │  │ worker  │  │ worker  │  │ GPU 🎮  │                             │   │
  │  │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │  │ ✓ RUNS │ ◀── With toleration          │   │
  │  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  │(tainted)│                             │   │
  │  │                             └─────────┘                             │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  3. nodeAffinity — Advanced node selection                          │   │
  │  │  ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────    │   │
  │  │  affinity:                                                          │   │
  │  │    nodeAffinity:                                                    │   │
  │  │      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:                │   │
  │  │        nodeSelectorTerms:                                           │   │
  │  │          - matchExpressions:                                        │   │
  │  │              - key: kubernetes.io/os                                │   │
  │  │                operator: In                                         │   │
  │  │                values: [linux]                                      │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📜 DaemonSet with Node Selection

k8s/selective-daemonset.yaml 
# DaemonSet that runs only on worker nodes (not control plane)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: app-monitor
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: app-monitor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: app-monitor
    spec:
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  nodeSelector — only nodes with these labels                    │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      nodeSelector:
        node-type: worker              # Only worker nodes
        kubernetes.io/os: linux        # Only Linux nodes
      
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  tolerations — needed for special nodes                         │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      tolerations:
        - key: nvidia.com/gpu
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
        - key: node.kubernetes.io/not-ready
          operator: Exists
          effect: NoSchedule
      
      # ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
      # │  affinity — advanced selection                                  │
      # └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
              - matchExpressions:
                  - key: kubernetes.io/os
                    operator: In
                    values: [linux]
                  - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
                    operator: DoesNotExist
      
      containers:
        - name: monitor
          image: prom/node-exporter:latest

📊 Node Selection Quick Reference

GoalMethodExample
Workers only nodeAffinity DoesNotExist node-role.../control-plane
Linux nodes nodeSelector kubernetes.io/os: linux
GPU nodes nodeSelector + toleration gpu: "true"
Include control plane tolerations control-plane NoSchedule
🖥️ Node Label Commands
# View node labels
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# Add label to a node
kubectl label nodes node-1 node-type=worker

# Remove label
kubectl label nodes node-1 node-type-

# View taints
kubectl describe node node-1 | grep Taints

22.5 DaemonSet vs Deployment

Both create pods, but they serve different purposes. Here's how to decide.

🔄 DaemonSet vs Deployment — Visual Comparison

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  DEPLOYMENT (replicas: 3)                                                  │
  │  "I need 3 pods somewhere in the cluster"                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                       │
  │  │ Node 1  │  │ Node 2  │  │ Node 3  │  │ Node 4  │                       │
  │  │ ┌─────┐ │  │ ┌─────┐ │  │         │  │ ┌─────┐ │                       │
  │  │ │Pod 1│ │  │ │Pod 2│ │  │  empty  │  │ │Pod 3│ │                       │
  │  │ └─────┘ │  │ └─────┘ │  │         │  │ └─────┘ │                       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Scheduler decides where    ✅ Can have multiple pods/node             │
  │  ✅ Scales with HPA            ❌ No guarantee of node coverage           │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  DAEMONSET                                                                 │
  │  "I need exactly 1 pod on EVERY node"                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐                       │
  │  │ Node 1  │  │ Node 2  │  │ Node 3  │  │ Node 4  │                       │
  │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │  │ ╔═════╗ │                       │
  │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │  │ ║ DS  ║ │                       │
  │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │  │ ╚═════╝ │                       │
  │  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Exactly 1 pod per node     ✅ Auto adds when node joins               │
  │  ✅ Auto removes when gone     ❌ Cannot have multiple pods/node          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Detailed Comparison

FeatureDaemonSetDeployment
Pods per node Exactly 1 (or 0) 0 to many
Replicas field None (auto) replicas: N
Scaling Auto (node count) Manual or HPA
Use case Infrastructure Applications
Example Fluentd, Node Exporter Django, React

💡 Quick Decision Rule

Ask: "Does this pod need access to the node, or just needs to exist?"

  • Needs node access (logs, metrics) → DaemonSet
  • Just needs to run (web app) → Deployment
  • Needs storage + identity → StatefulSet
  • Runs once → Job

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: DaemonSets

⏱️ Target Time: 5 minutes
Scenario: Deploy a log collector DaemonSet that runs on every worker node in the cluster.
  1. Create a DaemonSet log-collector in namespace monitoring using image fluentd:latest
  2. Mount the host's /var/log directory as read-only into the container at /host-logs
  3. Verify exactly one pod runs on each node
  4. Identify which workload type (DaemonSet, Deployment, StatefulSet, Job) fits each scenario: log collector, web app, database, migration
💡 Hint: DaemonSets look like Deployments but with no replicas field. Use hostPath volumes for node-level access.
📖 View Solution
Solution — DaemonSet Drill 
# 1. Create namespace
kubectl create namespace monitoring

# 2. Create DaemonSet with hostPath volume
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: log-collector
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: log-collector
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: log-collector
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd:latest
        volumeMounts:
        - name: host-logs
          mountPath: /host-logs
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: host-logs
        hostPath:
          path: /var/log
          type: Directory
EOF

# 3. Verify one pod per node
kubectl get pods -n monitoring -o wide
kubectl get nodes
# Pod count should equal node count

# 4. Workload type answers:
# Log collector → DaemonSet (needs node access)
# Web app       → Deployment (scalable, stateless)
# Database      → StatefulSet (persistent storage, identity)
# Migration     → Job (run once to completion)

Chapter 22 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is a DaemonSet and why would you use one? Give three real-world examples.
Show Answer
Answer

A DaemonSet ensures exactly one pod runs on every node (or a subset of nodes). When a new node joins the cluster, the DaemonSet automatically schedules a pod there.

#Use CaseExample
1Log collectionFluentd, Filebeat on every node
2Node monitoringPrometheus Node Exporter
3Network pluginCalico, Cilium, kube-proxy
Explanation

DaemonSets run infrastructure-level concerns that need node-wide coverage. Unlike Deployments (which might schedule 3 pods on 2 nodes), DaemonSets guarantee exactly one pod per node. They're system-level workloads. On the CKAD, when you see "every node" or "all nodes," think DaemonSet.

Q2
Write a DaemonSet YAML for a Fluentd log collector that mounts /var/log from the host.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: fluentd
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentd
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: fluentd
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: fluentd
        image: fluentd:v1.16
        volumeMounts:
        - name: varlog
          mountPath: /var/log
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: varlog
        hostPath:
          path: /var/log
          type: Directory
Explanation

DaemonSet YAML is nearly identical to Deployment — swap kind: Deployment for kind: DaemonSet and remove replicas. DaemonSets have NO replicas field because they auto-scale to the number of nodes. hostPath is common in DaemonSets since they need node-level access. On the CKAD, the key structural difference from a Deployment is the missing replicas field.

Q3
How do you restrict a DaemonSet to run only on specific nodes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: nodeSelector (simple)
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        disk: ssd

# Method 2: nodeAffinity (advanced)
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: node-role
                operator: In
                values: ["worker"]

# Label nodes first!
kubectl label node worker-1 disk=ssd
Explanation

By default, DaemonSets run on ALL nodes (including control-plane in some setups). Use nodeSelector for simple label matching or nodeAffinity for complex rules. DaemonSets also respect taints — if a node has a taint, add a matching toleration. On the CKAD, nodeSelector is the fastest approach and is usually sufficient for exam questions.

Q4
Compare DaemonSet, Deployment, and StatefulSet. When do you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureDeploymentStatefulSetDaemonSet
ReplicasUser-defined (N)User-defined (N)One per node (auto)
Pod identityRandom namesOrdinal (0,1,2)Random names
StorageShared/ephemeralPVC per podUsually hostPath
SchedulingScheduler decidesScheduler decidesEvery matching node
Typical useWeb apps, APIsDatabases, queuesLogging, monitoring
Explanation

This three-way comparison is a classic CKAD scenario question. Deployments for stateless scale-out apps, StatefulSets for apps needing stable identity/storage, DaemonSets for node-level infrastructure. Know that DaemonSets bypass the scheduler — they use the DaemonSet controller to ensure per-node placement.

Q5
What update strategies are available for DaemonSets? What's the default?
Show Answer
Answer
# RollingUpdate (default)
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxUnavailable: 1      # How many nodes can be without DaemonSet pod
      maxSurge: 0             # K8s 1.22+: allows extra pods during update

# OnDelete — manual control
spec:
  updateStrategy:
    type: OnDelete
# Pods only update when you manually delete them
Explanation

DaemonSets default to RollingUpdate (same as Deployments). maxUnavailable controls how many nodes can simultaneously be without the DaemonSet pod. Set to 1 means one node at a time is updated (safe but slow). OnDelete gives manual control — useful for critical infrastructure where you want to update node by node. Note: DaemonSets don't have maxSurge in older K8s versions.

Q6
How do you check which nodes a DaemonSet is running on and verify coverage?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check DaemonSet status
kubectl get ds fluentd -n kube-system
# NAME     DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   UP-TO-DATE   AVAILABLE   NODE SELECTOR
# fluentd  3         3         3       3             3           <none>

# DESIRED = number of eligible nodes
# CURRENT = pods created
# READY = pods passing readiness checks

# See which node each pod is on
kubectl get pods -l app=fluentd -o wide -n kube-system
# NAME            READY   STATUS    NODE
# fluentd-abc12   1/1     Running   worker-1
# fluentd-def34   1/1     Running   worker-2
# fluentd-ghi56   1/1     Running   worker-3

# Compare with total nodes
kubectl get nodes
# If nodes > DESIRED, check nodeSelector/tolerations
Explanation

The kubectl get ds output shows DESIRED (nodes that should run a pod), CURRENT (pods that exist), and READY (pods that are healthy). If DESIRED ≠ number of nodes, the DaemonSet has a nodeSelector or missing tolerations. Use -o wide on the pods to see node placement. On the CKAD, verifying daemonset coverage is done with kubectl get ds.

Q7
A DaemonSet is not scheduling pods on the control-plane node. How do you fix this?
Show Answer
Answer
# Control-plane nodes have a taint:
# node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule

# Add a toleration to the DaemonSet:
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
        effect: NoSchedule
        operator: Exists

# Or tolerate ALL taints (for system-critical DaemonSets):
      tolerations:
      - operator: Exists
Explanation

Control-plane nodes are tainted with node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule to prevent workloads from running there. DaemonSets must tolerate this taint to schedule on control-plane nodes. Using operator: Exists without a key tolerates ALL taints. On the CKAD, taints and tolerations are key scheduling concepts that interact with DaemonSets.

Q8
What is the difference between replicas in a Deployment and the number of pods in a DaemonSet?
Show Answer
Answer
  Deployment (replicas: 3):
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │  Pod A   │  │  Pod B   │  │  Pod C   │
  │ (any node)│  │ (any node)│  │ (any node)│
  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
  → Could be 3 pods on 1 node, or spread across nodes

  DaemonSet (no replicas field):
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐
  │  Node 1  │  │  Node 2  │  │  Node 3  │
  │ [Pod]    │  │ [Pod]    │  │ [Pod]    │
  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘
  → Always exactly 1 pod per node
Explanation

Deployments have a fixed replica count regardless of node count. A Deployment with 3 replicas has 3 pods whether you have 1 node or 100. DaemonSets scale with the cluster — add a node, get a pod; remove a node, lose a pod. DaemonSets have NO replicas field. On the CKAD, if a question asks "ensure every node runs exactly one copy," use a DaemonSet, not a Deployment.

Q9
How do you roll back a DaemonSet to a previous version?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check rollout history
kubectl rollout history daemonset/fluentd -n kube-system

# Roll back to previous version
kubectl rollout undo daemonset/fluentd -n kube-system

# Roll back to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo daemonset/fluentd --to-revision=2 -n kube-system

# Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status daemonset/fluentd -n kube-system
Explanation

DaemonSet rollback commands are identical to Deployment rollbacks — kubectl rollout undo works the same way. The rolling update proceeds node by node, same as the update. With OnDelete strategy, even after rollback, you must manually delete pods for the old version to be replaced. On the CKAD, rollout undo works for Deployments, DaemonSets, and StatefulSets.

Q10
What happens when a new node joins a cluster that has existing DaemonSets?
Show Answer
Answer
  BEFORE: 3-node cluster with DaemonSet "monitor"
  ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐
  │Node-1 │ │Node-2 │ │Node-3 │
  │[mon]  │ │[mon]  │ │[mon]  │
  └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘
  Desired: 3  Current: 3  Ready: 3

  AFTER: Node-4 joins cluster
  ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐
  │Node-1 │ │Node-2 │ │Node-3 │ │Node-4 │
  │[mon]  │ │[mon]  │ │[mon]  │ │[mon]  │ ← Auto!
  └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘ └───────┘
  Desired: 4  Current: 4  Ready: 4
Explanation

When a new node joins the cluster, the DaemonSet controller automatically schedules a pod on it — no manual intervention needed. If the node has taints, the DaemonSet pod must have matching tolerations. If the DaemonSet has a nodeSelector, the new node must have matching labels. When a node is removed, the DaemonSet pod is garbage collected. This auto-scaling behavior is what makes DaemonSets ideal for infrastructure concerns.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — DaemonSet Security & Privileged Monitoring Agents

SYSTEM HARDENING 10% MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20%

DaemonSets run on every node — making them both a powerful security tool (deploy Falco, log collectors) and a dangerous attack vector if compromised.

Use CaseSecurity ToolRequired Privileges
Runtime securityFalcoPrivileged, hostPID, /dev mount
Log collectionFluentd/Fluent BithostPath /var/log
Network policyCalico/Cilium agenthostNetwork, privileged
Node scanningkube-benchhostPID, hostPath /etc
 
# Security monitoring DaemonSet (Falco)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: falco
  namespace: kube-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: falco
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: falco-sa
      hostPID: true            # Required for syscall monitoring
      containers:
      - name: falco
        image: falcosecurity/falco@sha256:verified...
        securityContext:
          privileged: true     # Required for kernel access
        volumeMounts:
        - name: dev
          mountPath: /host/dev
        - name: proc
          mountPath: /host/proc
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: dev
        hostPath:
          path: /dev
      - name: proc
        hostPath:
          path: /proc

Chapter 22 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Deploy Falco as a DaemonSet for runtime security monitoring. What minimum privileges does it need?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: falco
  namespace: falco-system
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: falco
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: falco
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: falco
      hostPID: true               # See all host processes
      hostNetwork: true           # Monitor network syscalls
      tolerations:
      - effect: NoSchedule        # Run on ALL nodes including tainted
        operator: Exists
      containers:
      - name: falco
        image: falcosecurity/falco:0.37.1
        securityContext:
          privileged: true        # Kernel module / eBPF access
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /host/dev
          name: dev-fs
        - mountPath: /host/proc
          name: proc-fs
          readOnly: true
        - mountPath: /etc/falco
          name: falco-config
      volumes:
      - name: dev-fs
        hostPath:
          path: /dev
      - name: proc-fs
        hostPath:
          path: /proc
      - name: falco-config
        configMap:
          name: falco-config
Explanation

Falco needs privileged access to monitor kernel syscalls via eBPF. On CKS, this is one of the few legitimate uses of privileged containers. Key requirements: hostPID (see all processes), privileged (kernel access), /dev and /proc mounts. Use tolerations to ensure it runs on every node.

CKS-2
How do you audit existing DaemonSets for unnecessary privileged access?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find all privileged DaemonSets
kubectl get ds -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged == true
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

# Find DaemonSets with hostPath mounts
kubectl get ds -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath != null
) | {
  ns: .metadata.namespace, 
  name: .metadata.name,
  paths: [.spec.template.spec.volumes[] | select(.hostPath) | .hostPath.path]
}'

# Find DaemonSets with hostNetwork/hostPID
kubectl get ds -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.hostNetwork == true or
  .spec.template.spec.hostPID == true
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'
Explanation

A compromised privileged DaemonSet gives attackers root access to EVERY node. On CKS, regularly audit DaemonSets for: privileged mode, hostPath mounts (especially /, /etc, /var/run/docker.sock), hostNetwork, and hostPID. Only security monitoring tools should have these privileges.

CKS-3
Deploy a log collector DaemonSet with minimum required privileges (no privileged mode).
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: log-collector
  namespace: logging
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: fluentbit
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: fluentbit
      containers:
      - name: fluentbit
        image: fluent/fluent-bit@sha256:verified...
        securityContext:
          privileged: false          # NOT privileged
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          runAsUser: 0               # Needs root to read /var/log
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          capabilities:
            drop: ["ALL"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: varlog
          mountPath: /var/log
          readOnly: true             # Read-only access to logs
        - name: containers
          mountPath: /var/lib/docker/containers
          readOnly: true
      volumes:
      - name: varlog
        hostPath:
          path: /var/log
      - name: containers
        hostPath:
          path: /var/lib/docker/containers
Explanation

Log collectors need hostPath access to /var/log but do NOT need privileged mode. On CKS, mount host paths as readOnly and drop all capabilities. The runAsUser: 0 is needed to read log files, but privileged: false limits the blast radius.

CKS-4
A DaemonSet mounts /var/run/docker.sock. Why is this dangerous and how do you replace it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find DaemonSets mounting docker.sock
kubectl get ds -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  .spec.template.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath.path == "/var/run/docker.sock"
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'

# Why dangerous: docker.sock gives full Docker API access
# Attacker can: create privileged containers, access host filesystem,
# escape container, mine crypto, exfiltrate secrets

# Fix: Use container runtime-agnostic alternatives
# For metrics: use cAdvisor (built into kubelet)
# For building: use kaniko (no Docker socket needed)
# For monitoring: use containerd metrics socket (read-only)
Explanation

Mounting /var/run/docker.sock gives the container equivalent of root access on the host. An attacker can create new privileged containers, access the host filesystem, and escape the container entirely. On CKS, this is a critical finding — replace with CRI-compatible alternatives.

CKS-5
How do you ensure a security DaemonSet runs on ALL nodes, including control plane nodes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Use tolerations to schedule on tainted nodes
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - operator: Exists       # Tolerate ALL taints
        # This ensures the DaemonSet runs on:
        # - Control plane nodes (node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule)
        # - Tainted worker nodes
        # - Nodes being drained
      priorityClassName: system-node-critical  # Don't evict
      nodeSelector: {}         # Empty = all nodes
# Verify running on all nodes
kubectl get pods -n falco-system -o wide
kubectl get nodes --no-headers | wc -l
kubectl get pods -n falco-system --no-headers | wc -l
# Both counts should match
Explanation

Security monitoring must cover ALL nodes including control plane. Use tolerations: [{operator: Exists}] to tolerate all taints, and priorityClassName: system-node-critical to prevent eviction. On CKS, gaps in monitoring coverage are exploited by attackers targeting unmonitored nodes.

Chapter 22b: ReplicaSets, ReplicationControllers, and Beyond

You've learned about Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, and Jobs. But there's one more workload type you should know — ReplicaSets. While you rarely create them directly, understanding them helps you debug and comprehend what Deployments do behind the scenes.

📦 ReplicaSets 🏛️ Legacy Controllers 🟢 Beginner

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ What ReplicaSets are and how they differ from Deployments
  • ✅ Why you rarely create ReplicaSets directly
  • ✅ ReplicationController — the deprecated predecessor
  • ✅ When you might encounter ReplicaSets
🏗️ CKAD Domain 1: Application Design & Build 20% of Exam

ReplicaSets rarely appear as standalone CKAD questions, but knowing their relationship to Deployments helps with debugging. If a Deployment rollout fails, you'll inspect ReplicaSets to understand why.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: ReplicaSet Debugging

Key debugging commands involving ReplicaSets:

  • kubectl get rs — see ReplicaSets created by Deployments
  • During rollout: old RS scales down, new RS scales up
  • Failed rollout? Check: kubectl describe rs NEW-RS-NAME
  • Never create ReplicaSets directly — always use Deployments

🗺️ ReplicaSet in the Controller Hierarchy

Deployment
📋 ReplicaSet (current) Owns current pods, desired=3
📦 ReplicaSet (old) Previous revision, desired=0
🔄 Rolling Update New RS scales up, old scales down
⏪ Rollback Old RS scales back up
⚠️ When to Use ReplicaSet Directly?
  • 🔴 Almost never! Deployments manage ReplicaSets for you
  • 🔴 Direct RS = no rolling updates, no rollbacks, no revision history
  • 🟡 Only direct RS use: custom update orchestration with external controllers
  • ✅ Always prefer Deployment → it creates ReplicaSets automatically

22b.1 ReplicaSets Revisited

A ReplicaSet ensures that a specified number of pod replicas are running at any given time. Sound familiar? That's because Deployments use ReplicaSets internally.

🏗️ Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pods Hierarchy

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  The Workload Object Hierarchy                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                    DEPLOYMENT                                     │    │
  │  │  name: anihpj                                                     │    │
  │  │  replicas: 3                                                      │    │
  │  │  strategy: RollingUpdate                                          │    │
  │  │                                                                   │    │
  │  │  ✅ Rolling updates    ✅ Rollback    ✅ Revision history          │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │               Deployment creates & manages                                │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │                                 ▼                                         │
  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
  │  │                    REPLICASET                                     │    │
  │  │  name: anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e                                          │    │
  │  │  replicas: 3                                                      │    │
  │  │  selector: app=anihpj                                             │    │
  │  │                                                                   │    │
  │  │  ⚠️ No rolling updates    ⚠️ No rollback    ⚠️ No revision history │    │
  │  └──────────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────┘    │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │                ReplicaSet creates & manages                               │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │           ┌─────────────────────┼─────────────────────┐                   │
  │           ▼                     ▼                     ▼                   │
  │  ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐            │
  │  │    POD       │      │    POD       │      │    POD       │            │
  │  │ anihpj-xxx-1 │      │ anihpj-xxx-2 │      │ anihpj-xxx-3 │            │
  │  │   🐍         │      │   🐍         │      │   🐍         │            │
  │  └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘      └──────────────┘            │
  │                                                                            │
  │  💡 When you do "kubectl get rs", you see the ReplicaSets                 │
  │     created by your Deployments!                                           │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Deployment vs ReplicaSet

FeatureDeploymentReplicaSet
Rolling Updates ✅ Yes — built-in ❌ No — must delete & recreate
Rollback ✅ Yes — kubectl rollout undo ❌ No — manual recreation
Revision History ✅ Yes — tracks versions ❌ No — single version
Selectors matchLabels + matchExpressions matchLabels + matchExpressions
Create directly? ✅ Yes — recommended ⚠️ Rarely — use Deployment instead

⚠️ Why You Rarely Create ReplicaSets Directly

Deployments do everything ReplicaSets do, plus more:

  • Rolling updates with zero downtime
  • Rollback to any previous revision
  • Pause/resume deployments
  • Declarative updates

Bottom line: Use Deployment for 99% of stateless workloads. Only use bare ReplicaSet if you have a specific reason (e.g., custom controller).

📜 Standalone ReplicaSet YAML (For Understanding Only)

k8s/replicaset-standalone.yaml (⚠️ use Deployment instead) 
# Standalone ReplicaSet — rarely used directly
# ⚠️ Prefer Deployment for real workloads
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet   # ◀── Not Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-rs
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
    # Advanced: matchExpressions
    matchExpressions:
      - key: environment
        operator: In
        values: [production, staging]
  
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        environment: production
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: yourregistry/anihpj:v1.0.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000

# ⚠️ If you change the image:
#    - ReplicaSet: All pods keep running old image!
#                  Must delete RS or pods manually!
#    - Deployment: Rolling update automatically! ✅
🖥️ View ReplicaSets Created by Deployments
# See all ReplicaSets
kubectl get rs -n anihpj
# NAME                  DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e     3         3         3       2h    ◀── Current
# anihpj-5a4b3c2d1f     0         0         0       1d    ◀── Old (rollback)
# anihpj-9x8y7z6w5v     0         0         0       2d    ◀── Older

# View ReplicaSet details (owned by Deployment)
kubectl describe rs anihpj-6f7b8c9d0e -n anihpj
# Controlled By:  Deployment/anihpj  ◀── Shows parent

# Orphaned pods — when you delete a RS without deleting pods
kubectl delete rs anihpj-rs --cascade=orphan

22b.2 ReplicationController (Legacy)

The ReplicationController is the original method for ensuring a specified number of pods run. It's now deprecated — use Deployments or ReplicaSets instead.

🚨 Deprecated — Don't Use in New Projects

ReplicationController is superseded by ReplicaSet. The key differences:

AspectReplicationControllerReplicaSet
API Version v1 (core) apps/v1
Selectors Equality only: app=anihpj Set-based: app in (anihpj, test)
Status ⚠️ Deprecated ✅ Current (but use Deployment)

📜 Why You Might See ReplicationController

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Evolution of Pod Replication in Kubernetes                                │
  │                                                                            │
  │  2014 │  2015  │  2016  │  2017  │  2018  │  2019  │  Now                 │
  │       │        │        │        │        │        │                       │
  │  ┌────────────────┐                                                       │
  │  │ Replication    │                                                       │
  │  │ Controller     │────────────────▶ ⚠️ DEPRECATED                        │
  │  │ (v1)           │                                                       │
  │  └────────────────┘                                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │        ┌────────────────┐                                                 │
  │        │  ReplicaSet    │────────────▶ ✅ Still works                     │
  │        │  (apps/v1)     │              (but don't use directly)           │
  │        └────────────────┘                                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │              ┌─────────────────────────┐                                  │
  │              │      Deployment         │──────▶ ✅ USE THIS!              │
  │              │      (apps/v1)          │                                  │
  │              │  [manages ReplicaSets]  │                                  │
  │              └─────────────────────────┘                                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  WHERE YOU MIGHT SEE ReplicationController:                                │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                        │
  │  • Old tutorials (pre-2016)                                               │
  │  • Legacy systems not yet migrated                                        │
