  │  • Old exam questions (CKA/CKAD)                                          │
  │  • kubectl run --generator=run/v1 (removed in 1.18)                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ACTION: If you see RC in production, migrate to Deployment! 🔄            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
📜

ReplicationController YAML

(For reference only — don't use!)

apiVersion: v1  # core API
kind: ReplicationController
metadata:
  name: anihpj-rc
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    app: anihpj  # equality only!
  template:
    # pod spec...
✅

Deployment YAML

(What you should use!)

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    # pod spec...

💡 Workload Controller Summary

Workload TypeUse ForCreate Directly?
Deployment Stateless apps (web, API) ✅ Yes
StatefulSet Databases, stateful apps ✅ Yes
DaemonSet One pod per node ✅ Yes
Job / CronJob Batch / scheduled tasks ✅ Yes
ReplicaSet Pod replication ⚠️ Rarely (use Deployment)
ReplicationController Legacy replication ❌ Never (deprecated)

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Workload Type Selection

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario: For each workload below, identify the correct Kubernetes controller and explain why:
  1. A Django web application that needs 5 replicas and zero-downtime updates
  2. A PostgreSQL database that needs stable storage and ordered startup
  3. A Prometheus node-exporter that must run on every worker node
  4. A one-time database schema migration
  5. A nightly report generator that runs at 3 AM
  6. During a failed Deployment rollout, which objects do you inspect?
📖 View Solution
  1. Deployment — stateless, scalable, supports rolling updates
  2. StatefulSet — needs stable identity (pg-0, pg-1), persistent PVCs, ordered startup
  3. DaemonSet — exactly one pod per node, no replicas field needed
  4. Job — runs to completion, then stops. restartPolicy: Never
  5. CronJob — scheduled Job with schedule: "0 3 * * *"
  6. ReplicaSets — run kubectl get rs to see old vs new RS during rollout. Check events on the new RS for failure reasons.

Chapter 22b — Practice Questions

Q1
What is the relationship between a Deployment, a ReplicaSet, and a Pod? Draw the hierarchy.
Show Answer
Answer
  Deployment (manages rollouts)
  └── ReplicaSet (maintains desired replicas)
      ├── Pod-1 (actual container)
      ├── Pod-2
      └── Pod-3

  During rolling update:
  Deployment
  ├── ReplicaSet-v1 (old) → scaling down
  │   ├── Pod-1 (terminating)
  │   └── Pod-2
  └── ReplicaSet-v2 (new) → scaling up
      ├── Pod-3
      └── Pod-4 (creating)
Explanation

A Deployment creates and manages ReplicaSets. Each time you update the Deployment spec, a NEW ReplicaSet is created. The old ReplicaSet is scaled down while the new one scales up (rolling update). Pods are owned by ReplicaSets, not by the Deployment directly. On the CKAD, understanding this three-level hierarchy explains how rollbacks work — you're just switching which ReplicaSet is active.

Q2
Should you create ReplicaSets directly? Why or why not?
Show Answer
Answer

No — almost never create ReplicaSets directly. Use Deployments instead.

FeatureBare ReplicaSetDeployment (wraps RS)
Rolling updates❌ No✅ Yes
Rollback❌ No✅ Yes
Update history❌ No✅ Yes
Pause/resume❌ No✅ Yes
Replica maintenance✅ Yes✅ Yes
Explanation

ReplicaSets only ensure a desired number of pods are running — they have no concept of updates, rollbacks, or versioning. Deployments add all those features on top of ReplicaSets. The only time you'd use a bare ReplicaSet is for a workload that never changes (rare). On the CKAD, always create Deployments, not ReplicaSets.

Q3
What is a ReplicationController? How does it differ from a ReplicaSet?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureReplicationController (deprecated)ReplicaSet
API versionv1apps/v1
Selector typeEquality-based only (=, !=)Set-based (In, NotIn, Exists)
StatusDeprecated — legacyCurrent — recommended
Used byStandalone onlyDeployments manage them
 
# ReplicaSet uses set-based selector:
selector:
  matchExpressions:
  - key: app
    operator: In
    values: [web, api]

# ReplicationController uses equality only:
selector:
  app: web
Explanation

ReplicationController is the legacy predecessor of ReplicaSet. The key improvement in ReplicaSet is set-based selectors: matchExpressions with operators like In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist. ReplicationControllers only support key=value matching. On the CKAD, ReplicationControllers won't appear in exam questions as they're fully deprecated, but understanding the evolution helps.

Q4
How do you view the ReplicaSets managed by a Deployment?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all ReplicaSets for a deployment
kubectl get rs -l app=nginx
# NAME               DESIRED   CURRENT   READY   AGE
# nginx-7848d4b86f   3         3         3       10m  ← current
# nginx-6b4ffc5c97   0         0         0       25m  ← old version

# Show which Deployment owns a ReplicaSet
kubectl get rs nginx-7848d4b86f -o yaml | grep ownerReferences -A5

# See the template diff between ReplicaSets
kubectl rollout history deployment/nginx --revision=1
kubectl rollout history deployment/nginx --revision=2
Explanation

Each Deployment update creates a new ReplicaSet. Old ReplicaSets are kept (scaled to 0) for rollback capability. The number of old ReplicaSets retained is controlled by revisionHistoryLimit (default: 10). ReplicaSet names follow the pattern deployment-name-hash. On the CKAD, kubectl get rs helps you verify rolling update progress.

Q5
What happens if you manually create a pod with labels matching a ReplicaSet's selector?
Show Answer
Answer
# ReplicaSet has selector: app=web, replicas=3
# Currently: pod-1, pod-2, pod-3 (all Running)

# Manually create a pod with matching labels
kubectl run rogue --image=nginx --labels=app=web

# ReplicaSet now sees 4 pods matching its selector
# But desired = 3, so it TERMINATES one pod!
kubectl get pods -l app=web
# The rogue pod (or one of the originals) gets deleted
Explanation

A ReplicaSet counts ALL pods matching its selector, not just pods it created. If extra pods appear with matching labels, the RS will delete excess pods to maintain the desired count. This is why labels must be carefully managed. On the CKAD, this behavior explains why orphaned pods can be "adopted" by a ReplicaSet — and conversely, removing labels from a pod "releases" it from RS management.

Q6
What is matchLabels vs matchExpressions in a ReplicaSet selector?
Show Answer
Answer
# matchLabels — simple key:value equality
selector:
  matchLabels:
    app: web
    tier: frontend

# matchExpressions — advanced set-based
selector:
  matchExpressions:
  - key: app
    operator: In
    values: [web, api]
  - key: environment
    operator: NotIn
    values: [staging]
  - key: release
    operator: Exists

# Combined — ALL conditions must be true (AND)
selector:
  matchLabels:
    tier: frontend
  matchExpressions:
  - key: version
    operator: In
    values: [v1, v2]
Explanation

matchLabels is shorthand for equality matching. matchExpressions supports four operators: In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist. When both are used, all conditions are ANDed together. On the CKAD, most questions use matchLabels for simplicity, but know that matchExpressions exists for complex selection.

Q7
How do you scale a ReplicaSet? Does scaling a Deployment's underlying ReplicaSet directly work?
Show Answer
Answer
# Scale a bare ReplicaSet
kubectl scale rs my-replicaset --replicas=5

# Scale through Deployment (recommended)
kubectl scale deployment my-deployment --replicas=5

# DON'T scale a Deployment's RS directly!
kubectl scale rs my-deployment-7848d4b86f --replicas=5
# ⚠️ This works temporarily, but the Deployment controller
# will immediately override it back to the Deployment's replicas!
Explanation

If you scale a ReplicaSet that's owned by a Deployment, the Deployment controller detects the drift and reverts the RS replica count to match the Deployment spec. Always scale via the Deployment. Scaling a bare ReplicaSet (not owned by a Deployment) works fine. On the CKAD, always use kubectl scale deployment, not kubectl scale rs.

Q8
What does kubectl get rs output tell you during a rolling update?
Show Answer
Answer
# During a rolling update:
kubectl get rs
# NAME                  DESIRED  CURRENT  READY  AGE
# nginx-6b4ffc5c97      1        1        1      30m  ← old (scaling down)
# nginx-7848d4b86f      3        3        2      2m   ← new (scaling up)

# After update completes:
# nginx-6b4ffc5c97      0        0        0      32m  ← old (retained for rollback)
# nginx-7848d4b86f      3        3        3      4m   ← new (fully ready)

# Key columns:
# DESIRED = target replicas for this RS
# CURRENT = pods that exist (may be starting)
# READY   = pods passing readiness probe
Explanation

During a rolling update, you see two ReplicaSets: old (scaling down) and new (scaling up). The transition speed is controlled by the Deployment's maxSurge and maxUnavailable settings. Old RS DESIRED reaches 0 when complete — but the RS object is kept (scaled to 0) for rollback history. On the CKAD, watching kubectl get rs -w is the best way to observe rolling update progress.

Q9
How do you delete a ReplicaSet without deleting its pods?
Show Answer
Answer
# Delete RS but keep pods running (orphan them)
kubectl delete rs my-replicaset --cascade=orphan

# Pods continue running but are now "orphaned"
# No controller watches them anymore
kubectl get pods -l app=web
# Still running, but no ownerReferences

# If you create a new RS with matching selector,
# it will "adopt" the existing pods!

# Normal delete (also deletes pods):
kubectl delete rs my-replicaset  # cascade=background (default)
Explanation

--cascade=orphan removes the controller without touching the pods it manages. The pods lose their ownerReferences and become standalone. This is useful for debugging (keeping pods alive while replacing the controller) or migrating between workload types. On the CKAD, --cascade=orphan is the key flag. Default cascade is background (delete everything).

Q10
Write a ReplicaSet YAML that ensures 4 replicas of an nginx pod with set-based selectors.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: ReplicaSet
metadata:
  name: nginx-rs
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: app
      operator: In
      values: [nginx, web-server]
    - key: env
      operator: NotIn
      values: [staging]
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
        env: production
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.25
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
Explanation

Key points: (1) template.metadata.labels must satisfy the selector — here app: nginx matches In [nginx, web-server] and env: production matches NotIn [staging]. (2) The RS ensures exactly 4 pods with these labels exist. (3) On the CKAD, you'd typically use a Deployment instead, but the selector syntax is worth knowing for understanding how controllers work.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — ReplicaSet Label-Based Attacks & Pod Adoption

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

ReplicaSets use label selectors to manage pods — an attacker who can manipulate labels can hijack or orphan pods, disrupting workloads or injecting malicious containers.

AttackTechniquePrevention
Pod adoptionCreate pod with matching labels → RS adopts itRBAC: restrict pod creation, use Deployments
Label manipulationRelabel existing pod → orphan it from RSRBAC: restrict label updates
Image substitutionAdopted pod has different (malicious) imageAdmission policies, image allowlists
Replica count manipulationScale RS to 0 → DoSRBAC: restrict scale subresource

Chapter 22b — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
An attacker creates a pod with labels matching an existing ReplicaSet. What happens and how do you prevent this?
Show Answer
Answer
# What happens: RS adopts the pod if labels match
# If RS has desired=3 and now counts 4 pods, it DELETES one
# The attacker's pod might survive while a legit pod is killed

# Prevention via RBAC — restrict pod creation
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: no-direct-pods
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]   # NO create/update
  # Force users to use Deployments instead
Explanation

ReplicaSets manage pods by label selector — any pod with matching labels is "adopted". This is a design feature that becomes a vulnerability. On CKS, prevent direct pod creation via RBAC (force Deployments), and use admission policies to validate images match the expected template.

CKS-2
How do you use RBAC to prevent unauthorized scaling of ReplicaSets?
Show Answer
Answer
# Role that allows viewing but NOT scaling
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: rs-viewer
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
# Note: "scale" subresource NOT included

---
# Separate role for operators who CAN scale
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: rs-operator
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["replicasets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["replicasets/scale"]
  verbs: ["update", "patch"]
Explanation

Scaling a ReplicaSet to 0 causes immediate DoS. On CKS, separate the replicasets/scale subresource from general ReplicaSet access. Only operators and automation (HPA) should have scale permissions. Audit who has scale access with kubectl auth can-i.

CKS-3
How do you detect orphaned pods (pods not managed by any controller)?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find pods without ownerReferences (orphans)
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  (.metadata.ownerReferences == null) or 
  (.metadata.ownerReferences | length == 0)
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, 
     image: .spec.containers[0].image}'

# These are suspicious — could be:
# 1. Attacker-created backdoor pods
# 2. Orphaned debugging pods with stale credentials
# 3. Pods created by kubectl run that were never cleaned up

# Clean up orphaned pods
kubectl delete pod suspicious-pod -n production
Explanation

Orphaned pods (no ownerReference) are not managed by any Deployment/RS/Job and persist indefinitely. Attackers create orphaned pods as backdoors. On CKS, regularly audit for orphaned pods — they should not exist in production namespaces.

CKS-4
How do label selectors in ReplicaSets relate to NetworkPolicy security?
Show Answer
Answer
# If an attacker creates a pod with matching labels:
# 1. The RS adopts it
# 2. NetworkPolicies that select by the same labels
#    will ALSO apply to the attacker's pod
# 3. The attacker's pod gets network access intended
#    for legitimate pods

# Mitigation: Use additional labels for NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-access
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
      verified: "true"     # Additional label — harder to guess
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: web
          verified: "true"
Explanation

Labels are the foundation of both ReplicaSets and NetworkPolicies. If an attacker's pod matches RS labels, it also inherits NetworkPolicy access. On CKS, use additional verification labels or admission controllers to prevent unauthorized pods from inheriting network access.

CKS-5
Why should you always use Deployments instead of bare ReplicaSets, from a security perspective?
Show Answer
Answer
# Deployments add security-relevant features over bare ReplicaSets:

# 1. Revision history — audit trail of changes
kubectl rollout history deploy/web

# 2. Rollback capability — revert compromised updates
kubectl rollout undo deploy/web

# 3. progressDeadlineSeconds — fail-safe for bad rollouts

# 4. Strategy control — maxSurge/maxUnavailable
#    Prevents total outage during compromised updates

# Find bare ReplicaSets (not owned by a Deployment)
kubectl get rs -A -o json | jq '.items[] | select(
  (.metadata.ownerReferences == null) or
  (.metadata.ownerReferences | length == 0) or
  (.metadata.ownerReferences[0].kind != "Deployment")
) | {ns: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name}'
Explanation

Bare ReplicaSets lack audit trails, rollback, and controlled updates — all security-relevant features. On CKS, always use Deployments (which create ReplicaSets internally). Bare ReplicaSets in production are a red flag — they may have been created by attackers to bypass Deployment-level controls.

📋 Part 7 Quick Reference

A comprehensive cheat sheet for StatefulSets, DaemonSets, and ReplicaSets.

🗺️ Workload Controller Decision Tree

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Which Workload Controller Should I Use?                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │                    ┌──────────────────────────┐                            │
  │                    │   What kind of workload? │                            │
  │                    └─────────────┬────────────┘                            │
  │                                  │                                         │
  │       ┌──────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────┐            │
  │       │                          │                           │            │
  │       ▼                          ▼                           ▼            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐         ┌──────────────┐          ┌────────────────┐    │
  │  │ Run once?   │         │ Run forever? │          │ Run on every   │    │
  │  │ Batch task? │         │ Long-running │          │ node?          │    │
  │  │             │         │ service?     │          │                │    │
  │  └──────┬──────┘         └──────┬───────┘          └───────┬────────┘    │
  │         │                       │                          │             │
  │         ▼                       │                          ▼             │
  │  ┌─────────────┐                │               ┌─────────────────────┐  │
  │  │    JOB      │                │               │     DAEMONSET       │  │
  │  │  or CRONJOB │                │               │  One per node       │  │
  │  └─────────────┘                │               │  (logs, monitoring) │  │
  │                                 │               └─────────────────────┘  │
  │                    ┌────────────┴────────────┐                           │
  │                    │ Needs stable identity,  │                           │
  │                    │ persistent storage,     │                           │
  │                    │ or ordered operations?  │                           │
  │                    └────────────┬────────────┘                           │
  │                                 │                                         │
  │              ┌──────────────────┴──────────────────┐                     │
  │              │                                     │                     │
  │             YES                                    NO                    │
  │              │                                     │                     │
  │              ▼                                     ▼                     │
  │    ┌─────────────────────┐             ┌─────────────────────┐          │
  │    │    STATEFULSET      │             │     DEPLOYMENT      │          │
  │    │  Databases, Kafka,  │             │  Web apps, APIs,    │          │
  │    │  Redis Cluster      │             │  workers, etc.      │          │
  │    └─────────────────────┘             └─────────────────────┘          │
  │                                                                          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Complete Workload Comparison Matrix

Feature Deployment StatefulSet DaemonSet ReplicaSet
Pod Naming Random hash Ordered (0,1,2) Random hash Random hash
Scaling Set replicas Ordered scale Auto (=nodes) Set replicas
Storage Shared/none Per-pod PVC Host path Shared/none
Network Via Service Stable DNS per pod Host network OK Via Service
Rolling Update ✅ Built-in ✅ Reverse order ✅ Built-in ❌ None
Rollback ✅ Built-in ✅ partition ❌ Manual ❌ Manual
Use Case Web, API DB, stateful Logs, metrics Rarely direct
⚡
StatefulSet Commands
# List StatefulSets
kubectl get sts -n anihpj

# Scale StatefulSet
kubectl scale sts postgres --replicas=3

# View pod status (note ordering)
kubectl get pods -l app=postgres

# Partition update (canary)
kubectl patch sts postgres -p \
  '{"spec":{"updateStrategy":{"rollingUpdate":{"partition":2}}}}'

# Check PVCs
kubectl get pvc -l app=postgres
🔧
DaemonSet Commands
# List DaemonSets
kubectl get ds --all-namespaces

# Check pod distribution
kubectl get pods -l app=fluentd -o wide

# View DaemonSet details
kubectl describe ds fluentd

# Update DaemonSet image
kubectl set image ds/fluentd \
  fluentd=fluent/fluentd:v1.17

# Check nodes vs pods
kubectl get nodes | wc -l
kubectl get pods -l app=fluentd | wc -l
📦
ReplicaSet Commands
# List ReplicaSets
kubectl get rs -n anihpj

# View owner (Deployment)
kubectl describe rs anihpj-abc123 \
  | grep "Controlled By"

# List old ReplicaSets (0 replicas)
kubectl get rs --sort-by='.metadata.creationTimestamp'

# Delete orphaned RS
kubectl delete rs old-rs --cascade=orphan

# Check revision history
kubectl rollout history deploy/anihpj

🔧 Part 7 Troubleshooting Guide

ProblemCheckSolution
StatefulSet pods stuck Pending kubectl describe pod postgres-0 Check PVC binding, StorageClass exists, capacity available
StatefulSet pod won't start kubectl logs postgres-0 Check secrets mounted, env vars correct
DaemonSet missing on node kubectl describe node NODE Check taints — add tolerations to DS
DaemonSet has multiple pods on node kubectl get pods -l app=ds -o wide Shouldn't happen — check node affinity or selector
ReplicaSet not updating kubectl get rs Use Deployment instead — RS doesn't have rolling updates
PVC persists after StatefulSet deleted kubectl get pvc Expected! Delete PVCs manually or use persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy
DNS not working for StatefulSet nslookup postgres-0.postgres-svc Ensure headless service exists (clusterIP: None)

💡 Part 7 Best Practices Summary

✅ DO
  • Use Deployment for stateless apps
  • Use StatefulSet for databases
  • Use DaemonSet for node-level tools
  • Test backup restores regularly
  • Set resource limits on DaemonSets
  • Use headless services with StatefulSets
  • Use partition for canary updates
❌ DON'T
  • Create ReplicaSets directly (use Deployment)
  • Use ReplicationController (deprecated)
  • Run databases without persistent storage
  • Forget tolerations for DaemonSets on masters
  • Assume PVCs are deleted with StatefulSet
  • Skip health checks on StatefulSets
  • Use StatefulSet for stateless apps

🔐 Part 8: Security Chapters 23-25

Chapter 23: RBAC — Role-Based Access Control

In a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster, who can do what matters. RBAC is the gatekeeper — it controls which users or services can access which resources. Without RBAC, anyone with cluster access could delete production databases. This chapter shows you how to implement the principle of least privilege in Kubernetes.

🔐 Authentication 🛡️ Authorization 👤 Roles 🟡 Intermediate

🎯 What You'll Learn

  • ✅ Why security matters in Kubernetes
  • ✅ Authentication — how Kubernetes identifies users
  • ✅ Authorization — RBAC, the standard access control
  • ✅ The four RBAC objects: Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding
  • ✅ Service Accounts — identity for pods
  • ✅ Real team scenarios for anihpj
  • ✅ Testing and debugging RBAC with kubectl auth can-i
🔐 CKAD Domain 4: Application Environment, Configuration & Security 25% of Exam — HIGHEST WEIGHT!

RBAC is heavily tested on CKAD. You MUST be able to create Roles, ClusterRoles, RoleBindings, and ClusterRoleBindings. The kubectl auth can-i command is your best friend for verification.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: RBAC — The Four Objects

Memorize this matrix — it appears on every CKAD exam:

Object Scope Purpose
RoleNamespaceDefine permissions within a namespace
ClusterRoleCluster-wideDefine permissions across all namespaces
RoleBindingNamespaceBind Role/ClusterRole to user in namespace
ClusterRoleBindingCluster-wideBind ClusterRole to user cluster-wide
⚡ RBAC Speed Commands 
# Create Role imperatively
kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n myns

# Create ClusterRole
kubectl create clusterrole secret-reader --verb=get,list --resource=secrets

# Create RoleBinding
kubectl create rolebinding dev-pods --role=pod-reader --user=dev-user -n myns

# Create ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create clusterrolebinding admin-bind --clusterrole=cluster-admin --user=admin

# Test permissions (CRITICAL for exam!)
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n myns --as=dev-user       # yes
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n myns --as=dev-user     # no
kubectl auth can-i '*' '*' --as=admin                    # yes

RBAC YAML — Complete Role + Binding

RBAC is 25% of the exam. Memorize Role/RoleBinding structure cold.

# Role — Namespace-scoped permissions
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-manager
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]                  # ← "" = core API (pods, services, etc.)
  resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]  # ← Resources to grant access to
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "delete"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]

# RoleBinding — Bind Role to user/ServiceAccount
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-pod-manager
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: User
  name: dev-user
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
- kind: ServiceAccount             # ← Can bind to ServiceAccount too
  name: app-sa
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: Role                       # ← Or ClusterRole
  name: pod-manager
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding (cluster-wide)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: node-reader
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["nodes"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ops-node-reader
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: ops-team
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: node-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

RBAC & ServiceAccount Commands

ActionCommand
Create Rolekubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n prod
Create ClusterRolekubectl create clusterrole node-viewer --verb=get,list --resource=nodes
Create RoleBindingkubectl create rolebinding dev-pods --role=pod-reader --user=dev -n prod
Bind to ServiceAccountkubectl create rolebinding app-pods --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=prod:app-sa -n prod
Create ClusterRoleBindingkubectl create clusterrolebinding admin-bind --clusterrole=cluster-admin --user=admin
Test permissionskubectl auth can-i get pods -n prod --as=dev
Test SA permissionskubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:prod:app-sa
List all permissionskubectl auth can-i --list --as=dev -n prod
Create ServiceAccountkubectl create sa app-sa -n prod
Set pod SAkubectl set sa deploy/web app-sa -n prod

RBAC Traps

  • ❌ apiGroups: [""] vs apiGroups: ["apps"] — Pods/Services/Secrets = "" (core). Deployments/ReplicaSets = "apps"
  • ❌ RoleBinding can reference ClusterRole → but ONLY grants permissions in the binding's namespace
  • roleRef is immutable — can't change what Role/ClusterRole a binding points to. Must delete and recreate
  • ServiceAccount format: --serviceaccount=NAMESPACE:SA_NAME (colon-separated!)
  • kubectl auth can-i is the fastest way to verify RBAC — always use it after creating roles
  • Sub-resources: pods/log, pods/exec, pods/portforward need separate rules

kubectl explain — RBAC Fields

kubectl explain role.rules
kubectl explain role.rules.apiGroups
kubectl explain role.rules.resources
kubectl explain role.rules.verbs
kubectl explain rolebinding.subjects
kubectl explain rolebinding.roleRef
kubectl explain clusterrole.rules
kubectl explain clusterrolebinding.subjects

🗺️ RBAC Components — How They Connect

RBAC
👤 Subject User, Group, or ServiceAccount
📜 Role / ClusterRole Defines permissions (verbs on resources)
🔗 RoleBinding / ClusterRoleBinding Binds subject to role
🎯 Resources pods, services, secrets, deployments...
🟠 Namespaced (Role + RoleBinding)
  • Permissions scoped to ONE namespace
  • "Dev can edit pods in staging namespace"
  • Most common for team isolation
  • Role + RoleBinding in same namespace
🟢 Cluster-wide (ClusterRole + ClusterRoleBinding)
  • Permissions across ALL namespaces
  • "Admin can manage everything everywhere"
  • Also for non-namespaced resources (nodes, PVs)
  • ClusterRole can be re-used with RoleBinding!

23.1 Why Security in Kubernetes?

Kubernetes clusters are powerful — and dangerous if misconfigured. A single overprivileged user can delete databases, steal secrets, or bring down production.

⚠️ Without RBAC vs ✅ With RBAC

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  WITHOUT RBAC — Everyone Can Do Everything  🚨                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │ 👨‍💼 DevOps    │  │ 👩‍💻 Intern   │  │ 🤖 CI Bot    │                        │
  │  │ ADMIN       │  │ ADMIN       │  │ ADMIN       │  ◀── Same access!     │
  │  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │         ▼                ▼                ▼                               │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │                     KUBERNETES CLUSTER                              │  │
  │  │                                                                     │  │
  │  │  ✅ Create pods      ✅ Delete secrets    ⚠️ kubectl delete ns prod │  │
  │  │  ✅ Read logs        ✅ Modify RBAC       💀 Oops, deleted database │  │
  │  │                                                                     │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Risk: Intern accidentally runs "kubectl delete all --all" 💣              │
  │                                                                            │
  ├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │  WITH RBAC — Principle of Least Privilege  ✅                              │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐                        │
  │  │ 👨‍💼 DevOps    │  │ 👩‍💻 Intern   │  │ 🤖 CI Bot    │                        │
  │  │ ADMIN       │  │ VIEW-ONLY   │  │ DEPLOY      │  ◀── Different roles  │
  │  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘  └──────┬──────┘                        │
  │         │                │                │                               │
  │         ▼                ▼                ▼                               │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │                     KUBERNETES CLUSTER                              │  │
  │  │                                                                     │  │
  │  │  DevOps:         Intern:            CI Bot:                         │  │
  │  │  ✅ Everything    ✅ View pods      ✅ Deploy in anihpj ns         │  │
  │  │  ✅ All ns        ✅ View logs      ✅ Update Deployment           │  │
  │  │                   ❌ Delete         ❌ Access secrets              │  │
  │  │                   ❌ Modify         ❌ Other namespaces            │  │
  │  │                                                                     │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Safe: Intern can only view, CI can only deploy to one namespace ✅        │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯
Multi-Tenant Clusters

Multiple teams sharing one cluster need isolation. Team A shouldn't access Team B's secrets.

🔒
Least Privilege

Users get only permissions they need. Developer doesn't need cluster-admin access.

📋
Audit & Compliance

Track who did what. Required for SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA compliance.

🚨 Real-World RBAC Disasters

IncidentWhat HappenedRBAC Would Have Prevented
Tesla 2018 Unprotected Kubernetes dashboard exposed RBAC + authentication on dashboard
Shopify Bug Bounty Service account with too many permissions Least privilege service accounts
"kubectl delete all" Intern deleted production namespace View-only role for juniors

23.2 Authentication — "Who Are You?"

Before Kubernetes checks what you can do, it must know who you are. This is authentication. Kubernetes itself doesn't manage users — it delegates to external identity providers.

🔐 API Server Security Pipeline

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  kubectl get pods → API Server Security Pipeline                           │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     │
  │  │        │     │            │     │            │     │            │     │
  │  │ 👤     │────▶│  🔐 AUTHN  │────▶│  🛡️ AUTHZ  │────▶│  ☑️ ADMIT  │     │
  │  │Request │     │            │     │            │     │            │     │
  │  │        │     │ Who are    │     │ Can you    │     │ Validate   │     │
  │  └────────┘     │ you?       │     │ do this?   │     │ & Mutate   │     │
  │                 │            │     │            │     │            │     │
  │                 │ • Cert     │     │ • RBAC     │     │ • Webhooks │     │
  │                 │ • Token    │     │ • Check    │     │ • Policies │     │
  │                 │ • OIDC     │     │   roles    │     │            │     │
  │                 │            │     │            │     │            │     │
  │                 └──────┬─────┘     └──────┬─────┘     └──────┬─────┘     │
  │                        │                  │                  │           │
  │                        ▼                  ▼                  ▼           │
  │                   ❌ 401            ❌ 403              ❌ 400           │
  │                   Unauthorized      Forbidden           Bad Request      │
  │                                                              │           │
  │                                                              ▼           │
  │                                                        ┌──────────┐     │
  │                                                        │ ✅ etcd  │     │
  │                                                        │ Execute  │     │
  │                                                        │ request  │     │
  │                                                        └──────────┘     │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💡 Key Concept: Kubernetes Doesn't Manage Users

Unlike databases that have CREATE USER, Kubernetes has no user database. Users exist in external systems (Active Directory, Google, certificates). Kubernetes just validates the identity.

However, Kubernetes does manage Service Accounts — identities for pods.

23.2a Client Certificates (X.509)

The most common authentication method. Your kubeconfig file contains a client certificate signed by the cluster's CA.

~/.kube/config (certificate authentication) 
# kubeconfig with client certificate authentication
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
clusters:
  - cluster:
      certificate-authority-data: LS0tLS1CRU...  # CA cert
      server: https://k8s-api.example.com:6443
    name: prod-cluster
users:
  - name: alice
    user:
      client-certificate-data: LS0tLS1CRU...    # Alice's cert ◀── AUTHN
      client-key-data: LS0tLS1CRU...            # Alice's private key
contexts:
  - context:
      cluster: prod-cluster
      user: alice
      namespace: anihpj
    name: prod-context
current-context: prod-context

# The certificate contains:
# - Common Name (CN): alice         ◀── Username
# - Organization (O): developers    ◀── Groups
✅

Pros

  • No external dependencies
  • Works offline
  • Built into Kubernetes
⚠️

Cons

  • Hard to revoke (no CRL by default)
  • Certificate management overhead
  • Expires — needs renewal

23.2b Bearer Tokens

Service accounts and some authentication methods use bearer tokens — a string passed in the HTTP header.

Using a bearer token 
# Service account tokens are auto-mounted in pods
cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token
# eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6Ii...

# Use token in API request
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
     https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/namespaces/anihpj/pods

# In kubeconfig
users:
  - name: ci-bot
    user:
      token: eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsImtpZCI6Ii...

23.2c OpenID Connect (OIDC)

The enterprise standard for Kubernetes authentication. Integrates with identity providers like Google, Azure AD, Okta, or Keycloak.

🔄 OIDC Authentication Flow

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  OIDC Authentication Flow                                                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌──────────┐          ┌──────────────────┐          ┌──────────────┐    │
  │  │          │    1.    │                  │          │              │    │
  │  │  👤 User │────────▶ │  Identity        │   ◀────  │  API Server  │    │
  │  │          │  Login   │  Provider        │   3.     │  validates   │    │
  │  │          │          │  (Google/Okta)   │  Verify  │  token       │    │
  │  └──────────┘          │                  │  Token   │              │    │
  │       │                └────────┬─────────┘          └──────────────┘    │
  │       │                         │                            ▲           │
  │       │   2. ID Token (JWT)     │                            │           │
  │       │◀────────────────────────┘                            │           │
  │       │                                                      │           │
  │       │                    4. kubectl with token             │           │
  │       └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘           │
  │                                                                            │
  │  Token contains claims:                                                   │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────                            │
  │  {                                                                         │
  │    "sub": "alice@company.com",          ◀── username                      │
  │    "groups": ["developers", "k8s-users"], ◀── groups for RBAC            │
  │    "exp": 1699900000                    ◀── expiration                    │
  │  }                                                                         │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💡 When to Use OIDC

  • Enterprise environments: Integrate with existing Active Directory / Okta
  • Multiple clusters: Single sign-on across all clusters
  • Easy revocation: Disable user in IdP → immediately loses access
  • Group-based RBAC: Map AD groups to Kubernetes roles

23.2d Service Accounts

Service Accounts are identities for pods, not humans. Every pod gets a service account, and Kubernetes manages them.

👤 Users vs 🤖 Service Accounts

AspectUsersService Accounts
For Humans (kubectl) Pods (in-cluster)
Managed by External (OIDC, certs) Kubernetes (built-in)
Namespaced No (cluster-wide) Yes (per namespace)
Default N/A default SA auto-created
Token location kubeconfig file /var/run/secrets/...
Creating a custom Service Account 
# Custom Service Account for anihpj pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj

---
# Use it in a Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa   # ◀── Use custom SA
      automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Default: true
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: yourregistry/anihpj:v1.0.0

23.3 Authorization — "What Can You Do?"

After authentication confirms who you are, authorization determines what you can do. Kubernetes supports multiple authorization modes.

🛡️
RBAC (Standard)

Role-Based Access Control

Permissions based on roles. The standard for production clusters.

✅ Use this!

📋
ABAC (Legacy)

Attribute-Based Access Control

JSON policy files. Hard to manage. Requires API server restart.

⚠️ Avoid (legacy)

🔌
Webhook

External Authorization

Delegate decisions to an external HTTP service.

🔧 For custom policies

🖥️
Node

Kubelet Authorization

Special mode for kubelet to access node resources.

⚙️ System use only

💡 RBAC Concepts You Must Know

RBAC answers one question: "Can [SUBJECT] perform [VERB] on [RESOURCE] in [SCOPE]?"

TermMeansExamples
Subject Who is making the request User, Group, ServiceAccount
Verb What action is being performed get, list, watch, create, update, delete
Resource What Kubernetes object pods, services, secrets, deployments
Scope Where the permission applies Namespace or Cluster-wide
🖥️ Check Your Authorization Mode
# Check API server authorization modes
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l component=kube-apiserver -o yaml | grep authorization-mode
# --authorization-mode=Node,RBAC  ◀── Both Node and RBAC enabled

# Most production clusters use: Node,RBAC

23.4 RBAC Components — The 4 Objects

RBAC uses four objects to define permissions. Two define what (Role, ClusterRole), two define who gets it (RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding).

🗺️ RBAC 4-Object Relationship Map

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  RBAC: WHO (subjects) → BINDING → WHAT (permissions)                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  NAMESPACE-SCOPED                                                    │  │
  │  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │  │
  │  │                                                                      │  │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────┐      ┌─────────────────┐  │  │
  │  │  │     WHO          │      │             │      │      WHAT        │  │  │
  │  │  │  ─────────────   │      │             │      │  ───────────── │  │  │
  │  │  │  👤 User         │      │ RoleBinding │      │  📝 Role        │  │  │
  │  │  │  👥 Group        │─────▶│             │─────▶│  (namespaced)  │  │  │
  │  │  │  🤖 ServiceAcc   │      │ (grants in  │      │                │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │ 1 namespace)│      │ verbs:        │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │             │      │ get, list,    │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │             │      │ watch, create │  │  │
  │  │  └─────────────────┘      └─────────────┘      └─────────────────┘  │  │
  │  │                                                                      │  │
  │  │  Example: alice can read pods in anihpj namespace only               │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  CLUSTER-SCOPED                                                      │  │
  │  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │  │
  │  │                                                                      │  │
  │  │  ┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────┐      ┌─────────────────┐  │  │
  │  │  │     WHO          │      │   Cluster   │      │      WHAT        │  │  │
  │  │  │  ─────────────   │      │   Role      │      │  ───────────── │  │  │
  │  │  │  👤 User         │      │   Binding   │      │  🌐 ClusterRole │  │  │
  │  │  │  👥 Group        │─────▶│             │─────▶│  (cluster-wide)│  │  │
  │  │  │  🤖 ServiceAcc   │      │ (grants     │      │                │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │  cluster-   │      │ • All NS      │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │  wide)      │      │ • Nodes, PVs  │  │  │
  │  │  │                  │      │             │      │ • CRDs        │  │  │
  │  │  └─────────────────┘      └─────────────┘      └─────────────────┘  │  │
  │  │                                                                      │  │
  │  │  Example: sre-team can view pods in ALL namespaces                   │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔑 The 4 RBAC Objects

📝
Role

Defines permissions within one namespace

  • Namespaced
  • Lists allowed verbs & resources
🌐
ClusterRole

Defines permissions cluster-wide

  • Not namespaced
  • Covers nodes, PVs, etc.
🔗
RoleBinding

Grants Role to subjects in one namespace

  • Binds Role OR ClusterRole
  • To User/Group/SA
🌍
ClusterRoleBinding

Grants ClusterRole cluster-wide

  • All namespaces
  • Powerful — use carefully!

23.4a Role (Namespaced)

A Role grants permissions within a specific namespace. It defines which verbs (actions) are allowed on which resources.

k8s/rbac/role-pod-reader.yaml 
# Role: Allow read-only access to pods in anihpj namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role        # ◀── Namespaced
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
  namespace: anihpj    # ◀── Only applies to this namespace
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]    # "" = core API group (pods, services, etc.)
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

# ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  VERBS REFERENCE                                                │
# │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
# │  get         Read a specific resource by name                  │
# │  list        List all resources of a type                      │
# │  watch       Watch for changes (real-time)                     │
# │  create      Create new resources                              │
# │  update      Modify existing resources (full replace)          │
# │  patch       Partial modification                              │
# │  delete      Delete resources                                  │
# │  deletecollection  Delete multiple resources                   │
# │                                                                 │
# │  Read-only:   get, list, watch                                 │
# │  Write:       create, update, patch, delete                    │
# │  All:         * (dangerous!)                                   │
# └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

💡 Common API Groups

apiGroupsResourcesExample
"" (core) pods, services, secrets, configmaps, namespaces v1
apps deployments, replicasets, statefulsets, daemonsets apps/v1
networking.k8s.io networkpolicies, ingresses networking.k8s.io/v1
batch jobs, cronjobs batch/v1

23.4b ClusterRole (Cluster-wide)

A ClusterRole grants permissions across all namespaces or on non-namespaced resources (nodes, PVs, CRDs).

k8s/rbac/clusterrole-pod-viewer.yaml 
# ClusterRole: Allow viewing pods in ALL namespaces
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole    # ◀── Cluster-wide (not namespaced)
metadata:
  name: pod-viewer   # No namespace field!
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  
  # Non-namespaced resources (need ClusterRole)
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes"]       # ◀── Nodes are cluster-wide
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
  
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["persistentvolumes"]  # ◀── PVs are cluster-wide
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

# When to use ClusterRole:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# ✅ View pods across ALL namespaces
# ✅ Access non-namespaced resources (nodes, PVs, clusterroles)
# ✅ Define once, reuse via RoleBinding in multiple namespaces
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

⚠️ Built-in ClusterRoles

Kubernetes comes with pre-defined ClusterRoles. Don't modify them — they get overwritten on upgrades.

ClusterRolePermissionsUse Case
cluster-admin Everything (superuser) ⚠️ Platform team only
admin Full access within a namespace Namespace owner
edit Read/write most resources Developers
view Read-only access Viewers, auditors

23.4c RoleBinding (Namespaced)

A RoleBinding grants a Role to a subject (user, group, or service account) within a specific namespace.

k8s/rbac/rolebinding-alice.yaml 
# RoleBinding: Grant pod-reader Role to user alice in anihpj namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding       # ◀── Namespaced
metadata:
  name: read-pods-alice
  namespace: anihpj     # ◀── Grant access in this namespace only

subjects:               # WHO gets the permissions
  - kind: User
    name: alice         # ◀── Username from authentication
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  
  # Can also bind to groups:
  # - kind: Group
  #   name: developers
  #   apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  
  # Or service accounts:
  # - kind: ServiceAccount
  #   name: anihpj-sa
  #   namespace: anihpj

roleRef:                # WHAT permissions to grant
  kind: Role            # Role or ClusterRole
  name: pod-reader      # ◀── The Role we created earlier
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Result:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# alice can now run:
#   kubectl get pods -n anihpj         ✅
#   kubectl get pods -n production     ❌ (different namespace)
#   kubectl delete pod app -n anihpj   ❌ (delete not in Role)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🔗 RoleBinding Connects WHO to WHAT

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  RoleBinding: read-pods-alice                                              │
  │  Namespace: anihpj                                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐                          ┌─────────────────┐         │
  │  │   subjects:     │                          │   roleRef:      │         │
  │  │   ───────────   │                          │   ────────────  │         │
  │  │                 │                          │                 │         │
  │  │   👤 alice      │ ─────── BINDING ──────▶  │   📝 pod-reader │         │
  │  │   (User)        │                          │   (Role)        │         │
  │  │                 │                          │                 │         │
  │  └─────────────────┘                          │   verbs:        │         │
  │                                               │   get, list,    │         │
  │                                               │   watch         │         │
  │                                               │                 │         │
  │                                               │   resources:    │         │
  │                                               │   pods, services│         │
  │                                               └─────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  📍 Scope: Only anihpj namespace                                          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

23.4d ClusterRoleBinding (Cluster-wide)

A ClusterRoleBinding grants a ClusterRole to subjects across the entire cluster.

k8s/rbac/clusterrolebinding-sre.yaml 
# ClusterRoleBinding: Grant pod-viewer ClusterRole to sre-team
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding    # ◀── Cluster-wide (no namespace)
metadata:
  name: sre-pod-viewers     # No namespace field!

subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: sre-team          # ◀── All users in sre-team group
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole         # Must be ClusterRole for ClusterRoleBinding
  name: pod-viewer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Result:
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# All members of sre-team can:
#   kubectl get pods --all-namespaces    ✅ All namespaces!
#   kubectl get nodes                    ✅ Cluster resources!
#   kubectl delete pod app -n anihpj    ❌ (delete not in role)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

🚨 Warning: ClusterRoleBinding + cluster-admin = God Mode

Never bind cluster-admin ClusterRole via ClusterRoleBinding unless absolutely necessary!

# ⚠️ DANGEROUS — Gives full cluster control
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: danger-admin
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob           # Bob can now delete ANYTHING
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: cluster-admin   # ◀── SUPERUSER — be careful!
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

📊 Role vs ClusterRole × Binding Summary

RoleClusterRole
RoleBinding ✅ Grants Role in 1 namespace ✅ Grants ClusterRole in 1 namespace
ClusterRoleBinding ❌ Not possible ✅ Grants ClusterRole cluster-wide

23.5 Service Accounts Deep Dive

Service Accounts provide identity for applications running inside pods. Unlike user accounts for humans, ServiceAccounts are Kubernetes objects managed by the cluster.

🤖 ServiceAccount Lifecycle

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Pod → ServiceAccount → Token → RBAC Authorization                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐                                                    │
  │  │  1. SA Created      │                                                   │
  │  │  ────────────────   │                                                   │
  │  │  namespace: anihpj │                                                   │
  │  │  name: backend-sa  │                                                   │
  │  └─────────┬──────────┘                                                    │
  │            │                                                               │
  │            ▼                                                               │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐                       │
  │  │  2. Pod Uses SA     │      │  3. Token Mounted   │                       │
  │  │  ────────────────   │─────▶│  ─────────────────  │                       │
  │  │  spec:             │      │  /var/run/secrets/ │                       │
  │  │   serviceAccountName│     │   kubernetes.io/   │                       │
  │  │    : backend-sa    │      │   serviceaccount/  │                       │
  │  └────────────────────┘      │   ├── token        │                       │
  │                              │   ├── ca.crt       │                       │
  │                              │   └── namespace    │                       │
  │                              └─────────┬──────────┘                       │
  │                                        │                                   │
  │                                        ▼                                   │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐                       │
  │  │  4. API Request     │      │  5. RBAC Check     │                       │
  │  │  ────────────────   │─────▶│  ─────────────────  │                       │
  │  │  Authorization:    │      │  Is backend-sa     │                       │
  │  │  Bearer     │      │  allowed to GET    │                       │
  │  │                    │      │  secrets?          │                       │
  │  │  GET /api/v1/...   │      │                    │──▶ ✅ or ❌           │
  │  └────────────────────┘      └────────────────────┘                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/rbac/serviceaccount-example.yaml 
# ServiceAccount for anihpj backend application
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: backend-sa
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    app: anihpj-backend

# Optional: Secrets to pull images from private registry
imagePullSecrets:
  - name: docker-registry-key

# Kubernetes 1.24+: No automatic Secret creation
# Use TokenRequest API for short-lived tokens (more secure)

---
# Using the ServiceAccount in a Deployment
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: backend
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: backend
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: backend-sa    # ◀── Use our SA
      
      # Security best practices
      automountServiceAccountToken: true  # Mount token (default)
      
      containers:
        - name: app
          image: anihpj/backend:v1
          
          # Access API from inside the pod:
          # TOKEN=$(cat /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token)
          # curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
          #      --cacert /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ca.crt \
          #      https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/namespaces/anihpj/pods

🔐 ServiceAccount Best Practices

✅
DO
  • Create dedicated SA per app
  • Use minimal RBAC permissions
  • Use short-lived tokens (TokenRequest API)
  • Disable automount if not needed
❌
DON'T
  • Use default SA for apps
  • Grant cluster-admin to SA
  • Share SA across unrelated apps
  • Hard-code tokens in images
🖥️ ServiceAccount Commands
# Create ServiceAccount
kubectl create serviceaccount backend-sa -n anihpj

# List ServiceAccounts
kubectl get serviceaccounts -n anihpj

# Describe ServiceAccount (shows secrets, tokens)
kubectl describe serviceaccount backend-sa -n anihpj

# Create short-lived token (Kubernetes 1.24+)
kubectl create token backend-sa -n anihpj --duration=1h

# Check what permissions a ServiceAccount has
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:backend-sa \
  -n anihpj
# yes / no

23.6 RBAC for anihpj Team — Real Scenarios

Let's implement RBAC for the anihpj team with different roles: developers, SREs, CI/CD pipelines, and read-only viewers.

🏢 anihpj Team RBAC Structure

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj Team Access Matrix                                                 │
  │                                                                            │
  │                        anihpj        production      monitoring           │
  │                        namespace     namespace       namespace            │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  👨‍💻 Developers           ✅ Full        ❌ None        👁️ View            │
  │     (dev-team group)     edit                         only                │
  │                                                                            │
  │  👷 SRE Team              ✅ Full        ✅ Full        ✅ Full             │
  │     (sre-team group)     admin         admin          admin               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  🤖 CI/CD Pipeline        ✅ Deploy      ✅ Deploy      ❌ None             │
  │     (cicd-sa SA)         only          only                               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  👁️ Viewers               👁️ View       👁️ View        👁️ View            │
  │     (viewer group)       only          only           only                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

23.6a Developer Access (dev-team)

Developers need full access to anihpj namespace for development, but only read access to monitoring for debugging.

k8s/rbac/developer-access.yaml 
# Role: Full access to anihpj namespace for developers
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: developer-full
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  # Workloads
  - apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
    resources: ["pods", "deployments", "replicasets", "jobs", "cronjobs"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  
  # Networking
  - apiGroups: ["", "networking.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["services", "ingresses", "networkpolicies"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  
  # Config
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  
  # Debugging
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods/log", "pods/exec", "pods/portforward"]
    verbs: ["get", "create"]
  
  # Events (read-only for debugging)
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["events"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]

---
# RoleBinding: Grant to dev-team group
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: developer-access
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: dev-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: developer-full
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# ClusterRole (reusable): Read-only for monitoring namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: monitoring-viewer
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "services", "endpoints"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "daemonsets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

---
# RoleBinding: Developers can VIEW monitoring namespace
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-monitoring-view
  namespace: monitoring
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: dev-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole       # ◀── Using ClusterRole via RoleBinding!
  name: monitoring-viewer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

23.6b SRE Team Access (sre-team)

SREs need admin access across multiple namespaces plus access to cluster-wide resources like nodes and PVs.

k8s/rbac/sre-access.yaml 
# ClusterRole: Namespace admin for SRE (all resources in namespace)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: namespace-admin
rules:
  # Full namespace control
  - apiGroups: ["*"]
    resources: ["*"]
    verbs: ["*"]
  
  # Excluding cluster-level destructive operations
  # (will use separate ClusterRole for that)

---
# Grant namespace-admin in anihpj
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: sre-anihpj-admin
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: sre-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# Grant namespace-admin in production
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: sre-production-admin
  namespace: production
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: sre-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: namespace-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# Cluster resources access for SRE
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: sre-cluster-view
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["nodes", "persistentvolumes", "namespaces"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  - apiGroups: ["storage.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["storageclasses"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: sre-cluster-view
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: sre-team
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: sre-cluster-view
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

23.6c CI/CD Pipeline Access

The CI/CD pipeline needs deployment permissions only — no access to secrets directly, only to deploy pre-built images.

k8s/rbac/cicd-access.yaml 
# ServiceAccount for CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc.)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: cicd-deployer
  namespace: anihpj
  labels:
    purpose: ci-cd

---
# Role: Minimal deployment permissions
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: deployer
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  # Deploy workloads
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments", "replicasets"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch"]
  
  # Manage services and ingress
  - apiGroups: ["", "networking.k8s.io"]
    resources: ["services", "ingresses"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "patch"]
  
  # ConfigMaps for deployment config (not secrets!)
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update"]
  
  # Check pod status after deploy
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
  
  # NO delete permission — let humans handle that
  # NO secrets access — secrets managed separately

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: cicd-deployer-binding
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: cicd-deployer
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: deployer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

---
# For production namespace, same pattern
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: cicd-deployer-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: cicd-deployer
    namespace: anihpj     # ◀── SA from anihpj, binding in production
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: deployer          # ◀── Same role defined in production too
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

💡 CI/CD Token for External Systems

# Generate token for GitHub Actions / GitLab CI
kubectl create token cicd-deployer -n anihpj --duration=24h

# Store as CI/CD secret:
# - GitHub: Settings → Secrets → KUBE_TOKEN
# - GitLab: Settings → CI/CD → Variables → KUBE_TOKEN

# In CI pipeline:
kubectl config set-credentials cicd --token="$KUBE_TOKEN"
kubectl apply -f k8s/deployment.yaml

23.6d Read-Only Viewer Access

For stakeholders, auditors, or dashboards that need read-only access across namespaces.

k8s/rbac/viewer-access.yaml 
# Use built-in 'view' ClusterRole with ClusterRoleBinding
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: viewers-all-namespaces
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: viewers           # All users in 'viewers' group
    apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view                # ◀── Built-in read-only role
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Result: viewers can kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
# but cannot kubectl exec, delete, or create anything

📊 anihpj RBAC Summary

RoleTypeBindingScope
dev-team Group RoleBinding → Role anihpj: full, monitoring: view
sre-team Group RoleBinding + ClusterRoleBinding Multi-namespace admin + cluster view
cicd-deployer ServiceAccount RoleBinding → Role anihpj + production: deploy only
viewers Group ClusterRoleBinding → view All namespaces: read-only

23.7 Testing & Debugging RBAC

RBAC issues can be frustrating. Here are the essential tools and techniques for testing and troubleshooting.

🔍 RBAC Debugging Flowchart

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  "Forbidden" Error Debugging                                               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────┐                                                   │
  │  │  User gets 403       │                                                   │
  │  │  "Forbidden"        │                                                   │
  │  └──────────┬──────────┘                                                   │
  │             │                                                              │
  │             ▼                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  Step 1: Check current identity                                      │  │
  │  │  kubectl auth whoami                                                │  │
  │  │  → Shows user/group from authentication                             │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │             │                                                              │
  │             ▼                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  Step 2: Test specific permission                                   │  │
  │  │  kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj                             │  │
  │  │  → yes / no                                                         │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │             │                                                              │
  │             ▼                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  Step 3: Test as another user/SA                                    │  │
  │  │  kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=alice -n anihpj                  │  │
  │  │  kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:sa  │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │             │                                                              │
  │             ▼                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  Step 4: Find relevant bindings                                     │  │
  │  │  kubectl get rolebindings,clusterrolebindings -A                   │  │
  │  │  grep for username/group/serviceaccount                            │  │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Essential RBAC Debug Commands
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  WHO AM I?
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl auth whoami
# Username: alice@example.com
# Groups:   [system:authenticated dev-team]

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  CAN I DO X?
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl auth can-i get pods -n anihpj
# yes

kubectl auth can-i delete deployments -n production
# no

kubectl auth can-i create pods --all-namespaces
# no  (unless you have ClusterRoleBinding)

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  TEST AS ANOTHER USER (impersonation)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# As user
kubectl auth can-i get secrets -n anihpj --as=alice

# As ServiceAccount
kubectl auth can-i list pods -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:backend-sa

# As group member
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n production --as=bob --as-group=sre-team

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  LIST ALL MY PERMISSIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl auth can-i --list -n anihpj
# Resources                          Non-Resource URLs   Verbs
# pods                               []                  [get list watch create update delete]
# deployments.apps                   []                  [get list watch create update delete]
# secrets                            []                  [get list]

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  FIND BINDINGS FOR A USER
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# All RoleBindings in a namespace
kubectl get rolebindings -n anihpj -o yaml | grep -A5 "alice"

# All ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o yaml | grep -A5 "sre-team"

# Describe specific binding
kubectl describe rolebinding developer-access -n anihpj

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  VIEW ROLE PERMISSIONS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe role developer-full -n anihpj
kubectl describe clusterrole view

⚠️ Common RBAC Mistakes & Fixes

❌
Wrong Namespace

Problem: RoleBinding in wrong namespace

Fix: Check binding's metadata.namespace matches target

❌
Missing apiGroups

Problem: Role has "" but needs apps

Fix: Deployments need apiGroups: ["apps"]

❌
Case Sensitivity

Problem: name: Alice vs alice

Fix: Usernames are case-sensitive!

❌
SA Namespace Missing

Problem: ServiceAccount subject missing namespace

Fix: Add namespace: anihpj in subjects

🔧 Quick RBAC Validation Script

#!/bin/bash
# validate-rbac.sh - Test RBAC setup for anihpj team

echo "=== Testing Developer Access ==="
kubectl auth can-i create pods -n anihpj --as-group=dev-team
kubectl auth can-i delete secrets -n production --as-group=dev-team  # Should be 'no'

echo "=== Testing SRE Access ==="
kubectl auth can-i "*" "*" -n anihpj --as-group=sre-team
kubectl auth can-i get nodes --as-group=sre-team

echo "=== Testing CI/CD Access ==="
kubectl auth can-i create deployments -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:cicd-deployer
kubectl auth can-i delete secrets -n anihpj \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:anihpj:cicd-deployer  # Should be 'no'

echo "=== Testing Viewer Access ==="
kubectl auth can-i get pods --all-namespaces --as-group=viewers
kubectl auth can-i delete pods -n anihpj --as-group=viewers  # Should be 'no'

🗺️ RBAC Decision Tree

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  RBAC Decision Tree: What Do You Need?                                     │
  │                                                                            │
  │                    ┌─────────────────────┐                                 │
  │                    │  Need to grant      │                                 │
  │                    │  permissions?       │                                 │
  │                    └──────────┬──────────┘                                 │
  │                               │                                            │
  │              ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐                           │
  │              ▼                                 ▼                            │
  │      ┌──────────────┐                 ┌──────────────┐                     │
  │      │ Single       │                 │ Multiple /   │                     │
  │      │ namespace?   │                 │ All NS?      │                     │
  │      └──────┬───────┘                 └──────┬───────┘                     │
  │             │                                │                             │
  │    ┌────────┴────────┐              ┌────────┴────────┐                   │
  │    ▼                 ▼              ▼                 ▼                    │
  │ ┌────────┐      ┌────────┐      ┌────────┐      ┌────────┐               │
  │ │ Custom │      │ Reuse  │      │ Custom │      │ Use    │               │
  │ │ perms? │      │ same   │      │ perms? │      │ built-in│              │
  │ │        │      │ perms  │      │        │      │ cluster │              │
  │ │        │      │ in many│      │        │      │ roles?  │              │
  │ │        │      │ NS?    │      │        │      │         │              │
  │ └───┬────┘      └───┬────┘      └───┬────┘      └────┬────┘               │
  │     │               │               │                │                     │
  │     ▼               ▼               ▼                ▼                     │
  │ ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────┐    ┌─────────────┐             │
  │ │  Role   │    │Cluster  │    │Cluster  │    │ view/edit/  │             │
  │ │    +    │    │Role     │    │Role     │    │ admin/      │             │
  │ │  Role   │    │   +     │    │    +    │    │ cluster-    │             │
  │ │ Binding │    │ Role    │    │Cluster  │    │ admin       │             │
  │ │         │    │ Binding │    │RoleBinding│  │             │             │
  │ │(in NS)  │    │(per NS) │    │(cluster) │   │ +           │             │
  │ └─────────┘    └─────────┘    └─────────┘    │ClusterRole  │             │
  │                                              │  Binding    │             │
  │                                              └─────────────┘             │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🎴 RBAC Quick Reference Cards

📝
Role
kind: Role
namespace: anihpj
rules: [verbs, resources]

📍 Single namespace

🌐
ClusterRole
kind: ClusterRole
(no namespace)
rules: [verbs, resources]

🌍 Cluster-wide or reusable

🔗
RoleBinding
kind: RoleBinding
namespace: anihpj
subjects + roleRef

🔗 Grants in 1 NS

🌍
ClusterRoleBinding
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
(no namespace)
subjects + roleRef

🌍 Grants cluster-wide

📊 Common RBAC Verbs

ActionVerbExample Command
Read single resource get kubectl get pod nginx
List all resources list kubectl get pods
Watch for changes watch kubectl get pods -w
Create new create kubectl create deploy...
Full update update kubectl replace -f pod.yaml
Partial update patch kubectl patch deploy...
Delete delete kubectl delete pod nginx
Exec into pod create (pods/exec) kubectl exec -it pod -- sh
View logs get (pods/log) kubectl logs pod
Port forward create (pods/portforward) kubectl port-forward pod 8080:80

📋 Chapter 23 Summary: RBAC

  • ✅ Authentication — WHO are you? (certificates, tokens, OIDC)
  • ✅ Authorization — WHAT can you do? (RBAC most common)
  • ✅ 4 RBAC Objects — Role, ClusterRole, RoleBinding, ClusterRoleBinding
  • ✅ Service Accounts — Identity for pods/applications
  • ✅ Principle of Least Privilege — Grant minimal necessary permissions
  • ✅ Testing — Use kubectl auth can-i extensively

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: RBAC

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario: Set up RBAC for a development team. A developer named dev-user should only be able to view and manage pods in the development namespace, but not delete secrets or access other namespaces.
  1. Create a namespace development
  2. Create a ServiceAccount dev-sa in that namespace
  3. Create a Role pod-manager that allows get, list, watch, create, update, delete on pods
  4. Create a RoleBinding dev-binding binding the Role to dev-user
  5. Verify: dev-user can list pods but cannot list secrets
  6. Verify: dev-user cannot list pods in default namespace
