  │  $ echo "cGFzc3dvcmQxMjM=" | base64 -d                                    │
  │  password123    ◀── Anyone can decode this!                               │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════    │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ WHAT SECRETS PROVIDE:                                                 │
  │  • Separate sensitive data from pod specs                                 │
  │  • RBAC-controlled access (who can read secrets)                          │
  │  • Encryption at rest (optional, must configure)                          │
  │  • Mounted as files or env vars (not in image)                            │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
k8s/secrets/secret-basic.yaml 
# Secret with base64-encoded values (encoded, NOT encrypted!)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque          # Generic secret (most common)
data:
  # base64-encoded values
  username: YW5paHBqX3VzZXI=      # echo -n "anihpj_user" | base64
  password: c3VwZXJTZWNyZXQxMjM=  # echo -n "superSecret123" | base64

---
# Alternative: stringData for plain text (K8s encodes for you)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials-v2
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:           # ◀── Plain text, K8s converts to base64
  username: anihpj_user
  password: superSecret123
  
# Both methods produce identical secrets!
# stringData is easier but shows password in YAML (gitignore!)

---
# Connection string as a single secret value
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: database-url
  namespace: anihpj
type: Opaque
stringData:
  DATABASE_URL: postgresql://anihpj_user:superSecret123@db:5432/anihpj

📊 Secret Types

TypeUse CaseRequired Keys
Opaque Generic (passwords, API keys) Any
kubernetes.io/tls TLS certificates tls.crt, tls.key
kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson Docker registry auth .dockerconfigjson
kubernetes.io/basic-auth Basic authentication username, password
kubernetes.io/ssh-auth SSH private keys ssh-privatekey

25.2 Using Secrets in Pods

Secrets can be consumed as environment variables or mounted files.

k8s/secrets/pod-with-secrets.yaml 
# Method 1: Environment Variables (convenient but visible in logs)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backend-env
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: anihpj/backend:v1
      env:
        # Single secret key
        - name: DB_USERNAME
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: username
        
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: db-credentials
              key: password
              optional: false    # Pod fails if secret missing
      
      # All keys from secret as env vars
      envFrom:
        - secretRef:
            name: db-credentials
            # Creates DB_USERNAME and DB_PASSWORD env vars

---
# Method 2: Volume Mount (more secure, file-based)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: backend-mount
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: app
      image: anihpj/backend:v1
      volumeMounts:
        - name: db-secrets
          mountPath: /etc/secrets/db
          readOnly: true        # ◀── Secrets should be read-only!
      
      # Application reads: /etc/secrets/db/username
      #                    /etc/secrets/db/password
  
  volumes:
    - name: db-secrets
      secret:
        secretName: db-credentials
        defaultMode: 0400       # ◀── Restrictive permissions
        items:                  # Optional: select specific keys
          - key: username
            path: db_username   # Mounts as /etc/secrets/db/db_username
          - key: password
            path: db_password

# ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  BEST PRACTICE: Volume mounts over env vars                       │
# │  ✅ Secrets auto-update when secret changes                      │
# │  ✅ Not visible in `kubectl describe pod`                        │
# │  ✅ Not in container environment (env vars can leak to logs)     │
# └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📊 Env Vars vs Volume Mounts

⚠️
Environment Variables
  • Requires pod restart to update
  • Visible in kubectl describe
  • May leak to logs, crash dumps
  • Simple to use
✅
Volume Mounts
  • Auto-updates (kubelet syncs)
  • Not in pod describe output
  • File permissions control
  • App must read from file

25.3 Securing Secrets

Native K8s Secrets need additional hardening. Here's the complete security checklist.

🔐 Secret Security Maturity Levels

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Secrets Security Maturity Model                                          │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 1: Basic K8s Secrets (DEFAULT — INSECURE)                          │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────                        │
  │  ❌ base64-encoded (readable)                                             │
  │  ❌ Stored unencrypted in etcd                                            │
  │  ❌ Secrets in Git (if not careful)                                       │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + RBAC                                                              │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 2: RBAC-Protected Secrets                                          │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────────                                    │
  │  ✅ Only specific ServiceAccounts can read secrets                       │
  │  ❌ Still unencrypted in etcd                                            │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + Encryption at Rest                                                │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 3: Encrypted at Rest                                               │
  │  ─────────────────────────────────                                        │
  │  ✅ RBAC + etcd encryption enabled                                       │
  │  ⚠️ Still managed in-cluster                                             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
  │  │  + External Secret Manager                                           │  │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  LEVEL 4: External Secret Management (RECOMMENDED)                        │
  │  ────────────────────────────────────────────────────                     │
  │  ✅ Secrets in HashiCorp Vault / AWS Secrets Manager / Azure Key Vault   │
  │  ✅ Automatic rotation                                                    │
  │  ✅ Audit logging                                                         │
  │  ✅ Not stored in K8s (injected at runtime)                               │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

25.3a RBAC for Secrets

Restrict who can read secrets. Most pods should NOT have access to all secrets.

k8s/secrets/rbac-secrets.yaml 
# Role: Only allow reading specific secret
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: backend-secrets-reader
  namespace: anihpj
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["secrets"]
    resourceNames: ["db-credentials"]  # ◀── Only this secret!
    verbs: ["get"]

---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: backend-read-db-secret
  namespace: anihpj
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: backend-sa
    namespace: anihpj
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: backend-secrets-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Result: backend pods can only read db-credentials
# Cannot read other secrets in the namespace

25.3b Encryption at Rest

Configure the API server to encrypt Secrets in etcd.

/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml 
# Encryption configuration for kube-apiserver
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
  - resources:
      - secrets      # ◀── Encrypt Secrets
    providers:
      # Most secure: Use external KMS
      - kms:
          name: aws-kms
          endpoint: unix:///var/run/kmsplugin/socket.sock
      
      # Alternative: Local AES encryption
      - aescbc:
          keys:
            - name: key1
              secret: 
      
      # Fallback: identity means no encryption (for reading old secrets)
      - identity: {}

# ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
# │  API SERVER FLAG:                                                   │
# │  --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/encryption-config.yaml│
# │                                                                     │
# │  After enabling, re-encrypt existing secrets:                      │
# │  kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -             │
# └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Verify Encryption
# Check if secrets are encrypted (on control plane node)
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/anihpj/db-credentials \
  --endpoints=https://127.0.0.1:2379 \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key

# If encrypted: Shows binary/encrypted data
# If NOT encrypted: Shows readable base64 YAML

25.3c Sealed Secrets (GitOps-Safe)

Sealed Secrets allow encrypted secrets in Git. Only the cluster can decrypt them.

🔐 Sealed Secrets Workflow

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Sealed Secrets (Bitnami)                                                  │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐             │
  │  │ Developer   │     │   kubeseal    │     │    Git Repo   │             │
  │  │ creates:    │────▶│  (CLI tool)   │────▶│               │             │
  │  │ Secret.yaml │     │  encrypts     │     │ SealedSecret  │             │
  │  │ (plain)     │     │  with cluster │     │ .yaml         │             │
  │  └─────────────┘     │  public key   │     │ (encrypted)   │             │
  │                      └───────────────┘     └───────┬───────┘             │
  │                                                     │                     │
  │                                                     │ GitOps sync        │
  │                                                     ▼                     │
  │  ┌─────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐     ┌───────────────┐             │
  │  │   Pod uses  │◀────│    Secret     │◀────│ SealedSecret  │             │
  │  │   Secret    │     │  (decrypted)  │     │ Controller    │             │
  │  │             │     │               │     │ (in-cluster)  │             │
  │  └─────────────┘     └───────────────┘     └───────────────┘             │
  │                                                                            │
  │  ✅ Secrets safe in Git (only cluster can decrypt)                        │
  │  ✅ GitOps compatible (ArgoCD, Flux)                                       │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🖥️ Sealed Secrets Setup
# Install Sealed Secrets controller
kubectl apply -f https://github.com/bitnami-labs/sealed-secrets/releases/download/v0.24.0/controller.yaml

# Install kubeseal CLI
brew install kubeseal  # macOS
# or download from GitHub releases

# Seal a secret (encrypt)
kubectl create secret generic db-credentials \
  --from-literal=password=secret123 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubeseal --format=yaml > sealed-secret.yaml

# Apply sealed secret (controller decrypts → creates Secret)
kubectl apply -f sealed-secret.yaml

25.3d External Secret Managers

Use dedicated secret management tools for production workloads.

🔑 External Secret Options

🔐
HashiCorp Vault

Industry standard, self-hosted or cloud

Dynamic secrets, leases, audit

☁️
AWS Secrets Manager

Native AWS integration

Auto-rotation, IAM-based

🔷
Azure Key Vault

Native Azure integration

HSM-backed, RBAC

🔴
GCP Secret Manager

Native GCP integration

Versioning, IAM

k8s/secrets/external-secret.yaml 
# External Secrets Operator example (syncs from Vault/AWS/Azure)
# Install: helm install external-secrets external-secrets/external-secrets

apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: ExternalSecret
metadata:
  name: db-credentials
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  refreshInterval: 1h          # ◀── Sync every hour
  
  secretStoreRef:
    name: vault-backend        # Reference to SecretStore
    kind: SecretStore
  
  target:
    name: db-credentials       # K8s Secret name to create
    creationPolicy: Owner
  
  data:
    - secretKey: username
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/anihpj/database
        property: username
    
    - secretKey: password
      remoteRef:
        key: secret/anihpj/database
        property: password

---
# SecretStore: How to connect to Vault
apiVersion: external-secrets.io/v1beta1
kind: SecretStore
metadata:
  name: vault-backend
  namespace: anihpj
spec:
  provider:
    vault:
      server: "https://vault.example.com"
      path: "secret"
      auth:
        kubernetes:
          mountPath: "kubernetes"
          role: "anihpj-role"
          serviceAccountRef:
            name: external-secrets-sa

25.3e Secret Best Practices Summary

✅
DO
  • Use volume mounts over env vars
  • Enable encryption at rest
  • RBAC restrict secret access
  • Use external secret managers
  • Rotate secrets regularly
  • Use Sealed Secrets for GitOps
❌
DON'T
  • Commit plain secrets to Git
  • Log secrets in applications
  • Give pods access to all secrets
  • Trust base64 as "encryption"
  • Use default ServiceAccount for apps
  • Share secrets across namespaces

25.4 Secret Rotation

Secrets should be rotated periodically to limit exposure from potential leaks.

🔄 Secret Rotation Strategies

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Secret Rotation Methods                                                   │
  │                                                                            │
  │  1. MANUAL ROTATION                                                        │
  │  ───────────────────                                                       │
  │  Human: kubectl → update Secret → restart pods                            │
  │  ⚠️ Prone to errors, not scalable                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  2. AUTOMATED + POD RESTART                                                │
  │  ────────────────────────────                                              │
  │  External tool: rotates secret → triggers pod rollout                     │
  │  ⚠️ Brief downtime during rollout                                         │
  │                                                                            │
  │  3. VOLUME MOUNT + HOT RELOAD                                              │
  │  ──────────────────────────────                                            │
  │  Secret updated → kubelet syncs file → app detects change                 │
  │  ✅ Zero downtime (if app supports)                                        │
  │                                                                            │
  │  4. DYNAMIC SECRETS (VAULT)                                                │
  │  ────────────────────────────                                              │
  │  Vault: generates short-lived credentials on-demand                       │
  │  ✅ Secrets auto-expire, never stored permanently                         │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
scripts/rotate-secret.sh 
#!/bin/bash
# Secret rotation script for anihpj database credentials

NAMESPACE="anihpj"
SECRET_NAME="db-credentials"

# Generate new password
NEW_PASSWORD=$(openssl rand -base64 24)

# Update secret
kubectl create secret generic $SECRET_NAME \
  --from-literal=username=anihpj_user \
  --from-literal=password="$NEW_PASSWORD" \
  --namespace=$NAMESPACE \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

# Trigger rolling restart (if using env vars)
kubectl rollout restart deployment/backend -n $NAMESPACE

# For volume mounts: kubelet auto-syncs, app should detect
echo "Secret rotated. New password set."

# Also update the database password!
# psql -h db -U postgres -c "ALTER USER anihpj_user PASSWORD '$NEW_PASSWORD';"

25.5 Chapter Summary

🔑 Kubernetes Secret Types

📦
Opaque (default)
type: Opaque
data:
  key: base64value

Generic key-value pairs, most common

🐳
Docker Registry
type: docker-registry
--docker-server
--docker-username

Pull images from private registries

🔐
TLS Certificate
type: tls
tls.crt: <cert>
tls.key: <key>

TLS/SSL for Ingress and apps

🎯 Secrets Security Maturity Levels

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    SECRETS SECURITY MATURITY MODEL                      │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─ LEVEL 1 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🔴 ]┐
  │  CONFIG FILES / HARDCODED                                               │
  │  • Secrets in code, .env files committed to Git                        │
  │  • ❌ Never do this! Exposed in version history forever                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 2 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟠 ]┐
  │  KUBERNETES SECRETS (Base64)                                            │
  │  • kubectl create secret, base64-encoded                               │
  │  • ⚠️ Better, but not encrypted in etcd by default                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 3 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟡 ]┐
  │  ENCRYPTED AT REST + RBAC                                               │
  │  • EncryptionConfiguration on API server                               │
  │  • ✅ Secrets encrypted in etcd, RBAC controls access                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 4 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🟢 ]┐
  │  SEALED SECRETS / GITOPS-SAFE                                           │
  │  • Bitnami Sealed Secrets, Mozilla SOPS                                │
  │  • ✅ Encrypted secrets safe to commit to Git                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                    ⬇️
  ┌─ LEVEL 5 ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────[ 🏆 ]┐
  │  EXTERNAL SECRETS MANAGER                                               │
  │  • HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault               │
  │  • ✅ Centralized, audited, dynamic secrets, auto-rotation            │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🔄 External Secrets Managers Comparison

ManagerBest ForK8s IntegrationKey Feature
HashiCorp Vault Multi-cloud, on-prem CSI Driver, Agent Sidecar Dynamic secrets, leases
AWS Secrets Manager AWS-native workloads External Secrets Operator RDS integration, rotation
Azure Key Vault Azure/AKS workloads CSI Driver (native) Managed identity auth
GCP Secret Manager GKE workloads External Secrets Operator IAM-based access
Sealed Secrets GitOps, simple setup Native controller Git-safe encryption

📋 Chapter 25 Summary: Secrets Management

  • ✅ K8s Secrets — Base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default
  • ✅ Volume mounts — Preferred over environment variables
  • ✅ RBAC — Restrict secret access per ServiceAccount
  • ✅ Encryption at rest — Configure API server for etcd encryption
  • ✅ Sealed Secrets — Encrypt secrets for Git (GitOps-safe)
  • ✅ External managers — Vault, AWS, Azure for production
  • ✅ Rotation — Regular rotation with zero-downtime strategies
🖥️ Quick Secret Commands Cheat Sheet
# Create secret from literal
kubectl create secret generic my-secret \
  --from-literal=key1=value1 \
  --from-literal=key2=value2 -n anihpj

# Create secret from file
kubectl create secret generic tls-secret \
  --from-file=tls.crt \
  --from-file=tls.key -n anihpj

# Create docker registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=docker.io \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass -n anihpj

# View secret (base64 decoded)
kubectl get secret db-credentials -n anihpj -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

# Edit secret
kubectl edit secret db-credentials -n anihpj

# Delete secret
kubectl delete secret db-credentials -n anihpj

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Secrets Management

⏱️ Target Time: 7 minutes
Scenario: Set up secrets for the anihpj application that needs database credentials, an API key, and a TLS certificate.
  1. Create a Secret db-creds with username=anihpj_admin and password=Str0ngP@ss!
  2. Create a Secret api-key from a file containing the API key
  3. Create a Pod app-pod that uses db-creds as environment variables
  4. Mount api-key as a volume at /etc/api in the same pod
  5. Verify: Exec into the pod and confirm the env vars and mounted file exist
  6. Decode the password from the Secret using kubectl
💡 Hint: Use envFrom.secretRef for env vars and volumes[].secret for volume mount. Use base64 -d to decode.
📖 View Solution
Solution — Secrets Management Drill 
# 1. Create db-creds Secret
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=anihpj_admin \
  --from-literal=password='Str0ngP@ss!'

# 2. Create api-key Secret from file
echo -n "sk-abc123xyz789" > /tmp/api-key.txt
kubectl create secret generic api-key --from-file=key=/tmp/api-key.txt

# 3 & 4. Create Pod with env vars + volume mount
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ["sleep", "3600"]
    envFrom:
    - secretRef:
        name: db-creds      # All keys become env vars
    volumeMounts:
    - name: api-vol
      mountPath: /etc/api
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: api-vol
    secret:
      secretName: api-key
EOF

# 5. Verify
kubectl exec app-pod -- env | grep -E 'username|password'
# username=anihpj_admin
# password=Str0ngP@ss!

kubectl exec app-pod -- cat /etc/api/key
# sk-abc123xyz789

# 6. Decode password from Secret
kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
# Str0ngP@ss!

Chapter 25 — Practice Questions

Q1
What are the three types of Kubernetes Secrets and when do you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
TypeValueUse Case
OpaqueOpaqueGeneric key-value data (passwords, tokens)
Docker registrykubernetes.io/dockerconfigjsonPrivate container registry credentials
TLSkubernetes.io/tlsTLS certificates (cert + key pairs)
SA tokenkubernetes.io/service-account-tokenAuto-created for ServiceAccounts
Basic authkubernetes.io/basic-authUsername + password
Explanation

The three you'll use on the CKAD are Opaque (generic), kubernetes.io/tls (for Ingress TLS), and kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson (for imagePullSecrets). Opaque is the default type when using kubectl create secret generic.

Q2
Create a Secret from literal values and mount it as environment variables in a pod. Then do the same with a volume mount.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create the secret
kubectl create secret generic db-creds \
  --from-literal=username=admin \
  --from-literal=password=s3cure!P@ss
# Method 1: As environment variables
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-env
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: my-app:v1
    envFrom:
    - secretRef:
        name: db-creds
    # Or individual keys:
    env:
    - name: DB_USER
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: db-creds
          key: username

---
# Method 2: As volume mount (files)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-vol
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: my-app:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: db-creds
# Creates:
# /etc/secrets/username  (content: admin)
# /etc/secrets/password  (content: s3cure!P@ss)
Explanation

Two ways to consume Secrets: environment variables (envFrom or env.valueFrom) and volume mounts. Volume mounts create one file per key. Key advantage of volume mounts: they auto-update when the Secret changes (env vars don't). On the CKAD, know both methods — the question will specify which to use.

Q3
Are Kubernetes Secrets encrypted at rest by default? What should you do for production?
Show Answer
Answer

No — Secrets are only base64-encoded by default, NOT encrypted. base64 is an encoding, not encryption.

# Anyone with etcd access can read secrets:
echo "cGFzc3dvcmQ=" | base64 -d
# password  ← trivially decoded!

Production hardening:

  1. Enable encryption at rest (EncryptionConfiguration)
  2. Use external secret stores (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
  3. Enable RBAC to restrict Secret access
  4. Use Sealed Secrets for GitOps workflows
  5. Rotate secrets regularly
Explanation

This is a critical security concept. Base64 ≠ encryption. By default, Secrets are stored as plaintext in etcd. Enable EncryptionConfiguration on the API server for encryption at rest. For production, integrate with external KMS providers. On the CKAD, know that Secrets are base64-encoded (not encrypted) and understand the security implications.

Q4
How do you create a Secret from a file? How does the key name differ from --from-literal?
Show Answer
Answer
# From a file — key = filename
echo -n 'my-password' > password.txt
kubectl create secret generic file-secret \
  --from-file=password.txt
# Key: "password.txt", Value: "my-password"

# Custom key name
kubectl create secret generic file-secret \
  --from-file=db-pass=password.txt
# Key: "db-pass", Value: "my-password"

# From literal — you specify the key
kubectl create secret generic lit-secret \
  --from-literal=db-pass=my-password
# Key: "db-pass", Value: "my-password"

# From .env file (key=value format)
kubectl create secret generic env-secret \
  --from-env-file=app.env
Explanation

With --from-file, the default key name is the filename itself. Use --from-file=custom-key=filename to override. With --from-literal, you always specify the key. --from-env-file reads all key=value pairs from a file. On the CKAD, watch for trailing newlines in files — use echo -n to avoid them.

Q5
How do you create a TLS Secret for an Ingress?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create TLS secret from cert and key files
kubectl create secret tls my-tls-secret \
  --cert=tls.crt \
  --key=tls.key

# Use in Ingress
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: secure-ingress
spec:
  tls:
  - hosts:
    - myapp.example.com
    secretName: my-tls-secret    # ← references TLS secret
  rules:
  - host: myapp.example.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: my-service
            port:
              number: 80
Explanation

kubectl create secret tls creates a Secret of type kubernetes.io/tls with keys tls.crt and tls.key. The Ingress references this Secret by name in spec.tls[].secretName. The TLS Secret must be in the same namespace as the Ingress. On the CKAD, TLS Secret + Ingress is a combination question.

Q6
What is imagePullSecrets? How do you pull images from a private registry?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create docker-registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=user \
  --docker-password=pass \
  --docker-email=user@example.com
# Reference in pod spec
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: private-app
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
  - name: regcred              # ← pod-level
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/my-app:v1

# Or attach to ServiceAccount (applies to all pods using it)
# kubectl patch sa default -p '{"imagePullSecrets":[{"name":"regcred"}]}'
Explanation

imagePullSecrets is a pod-level field that tells kubelet to use credentials when pulling images. You can also attach it to a ServiceAccount so all pods using that SA automatically get the credentials. On the CKAD, if you see "private registry" or "ImagePullBackOff," think imagePullSecrets.

Q7
How do you decode a Secret value? How do you edit a Secret?
Show Answer
Answer
# View secret (base64 encoded)
kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml
# data:
#   password: czNjdXJlIVBAc3M=

# Decode
kubectl get secret db-creds -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
# s3cure!P@ss

# One-liner for all keys
kubectl get secret db-creds -o go-template='{{range $k,$v := .data}}{{$k}}: {{$v | base64decode}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}'

# Edit secret (opens editor with base64 values)
kubectl edit secret db-creds

# Replace a value
kubectl patch secret db-creds -p '{"data":{"password":"'$(echo -n newpass | base64)'"}}'
Explanation

Secrets are stored as base64. Use jsonpath + base64 -d to decode specific keys. When editing with kubectl edit, you must provide base64-encoded values. On the CKAD, the jsonpath + base64 -d combo is the fastest decode method. Also know stringData in YAML for plaintext values (auto-encoded on creation).

Q8
What is the stringData field in a Secret YAML? How is it different from data?
Show Answer
Answer
# Using data (must be base64-encoded)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
data:
  username: YWRtaW4=           # base64 of "admin"
  password: czNjdXJlIVBAc3M=   # base64 of "s3cure!P@ss"

---
# Using stringData (plaintext — auto-encoded)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: my-secret
stringData:
  username: admin              # Plaintext!
  password: s3cure!P@ss        # Plaintext!

# stringData is write-only: when you GET the secret,
# it always shows base64-encoded "data" field
Explanation

stringData accepts plaintext values and auto-encodes to base64 on creation. It's write-only — after creation, kubectl get secret -o yaml shows the data field with base64 values. If both data and stringData have the same key, stringData wins. On the CKAD, stringData saves time when writing Secret YAML manually — no need to manually base64-encode.

Q9
How do you mount only specific keys from a Secret as files?
Show Answer
Answer
volumes:
- name: secret-vol
  secret:
    secretName: db-creds
    items:
    - key: password          # Secret key
      path: db-password.txt  # File name in mount
    - key: username
      path: db-user.txt

# Mount result:
# /etc/secrets/db-password.txt  (contains: s3cure!P@ss)
# /etc/secrets/db-user.txt      (contains: admin)
# Other keys are NOT mounted
Explanation

The items field lets you cherry-pick which Secret keys to mount and rename the files. Without items, all keys are mounted as files named after the key. With items, only listed keys are mounted. The path field controls the filename. On the CKAD, this is useful when you need a specific file name for an application config.

Q10
What is immutable: true on a Secret? Why would you use it?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: api-key
immutable: true          # ← Cannot be modified after creation
data:
  key: bXktYXBpLWtleQ==
# Trying to update an immutable secret:
kubectl edit secret api-key
# Error: "immutable field cannot be updated"

# Must delete and recreate to change:
kubectl delete secret api-key
kubectl create secret generic api-key --from-literal=key=new-value
Explanation

Benefits of immutable Secrets: (1) protection from accidental or malicious updates, (2) significant performance improvement — Kubernetes skips watch operations on immutable Secrets, reducing API server load. (3) Works for ConfigMaps too. Downside: must delete and recreate to change. On the CKAD, know that immutable: true works for both Secrets and ConfigMaps.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Secrets Encryption at Rest & Rotation

CLUSTER SETUP 15% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Kubernetes Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default. Without encryption at rest, anyone with etcd access can read all secrets. This is one of the most tested CKS topics.

Encryption ProviderKey ManagementCKS Recommendation
identity (default)None — plaintextNever use in production
aescbcStatic key in config fileMinimum acceptable
aesgcmStatic key, fasterGood for performance
kms v2External KMS (Vault, AWS KMS)Best — keys never on disk
secretboxStatic key, modern cipherGood alternative to aescbc
 
# EncryptionConfiguration — encrypt Secrets at rest
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
- resources:
  - secrets
  providers:
  - aescbc:
      keys:
      - name: key1
        secret: BASE64_ENCODED_32_BYTE_KEY
  - identity: {}    # Fallback for reading old unencrypted secrets

Chapter 25 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Configure encryption at rest for Secrets using aescbc and verify it's working.
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Generate encryption key
ENCRYPTION_KEY=$(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)

# 2. Create EncryptionConfiguration
cat > /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml << EOF
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
- resources:
  - secrets
  providers:
  - aescbc:
      keys:
      - name: key1
        secret: ${ENCRYPTION_KEY}
  - identity: {}
EOF

# 3. Add to API server manifest
# --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
# Mount the directory in volumeMounts

# 4. Restart API server (kubeadm: move manifest out and back)

# 5. Re-encrypt existing secrets
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# 6. Verify encryption
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/test-secret \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key
# Output should be encrypted (k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:key1:...)
Explanation

Secret encryption at rest is a top CKS exam topic. The process: create EncryptionConfiguration → configure API server → re-encrypt existing secrets → verify via etcdctl. The identity provider at the end allows reading old unencrypted secrets. After re-encryption, all secrets show k8s:enc:aescbc:v1 prefix.

CKS-2
How do you rotate the encryption key for Secrets at rest?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Generate new key
NEW_KEY=$(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)

# 2. Update EncryptionConfiguration — add new key FIRST
providers:
- aescbc:
    keys:
    - name: key2           # NEW key first (used for encryption)
      secret: ${NEW_KEY}
    - name: key1           # OLD key second (used for decryption)
      secret: ${OLD_KEY}
- identity: {}

# 3. Restart API server

# 4. Re-encrypt all secrets with new key
kubectl get secrets -A -o json | kubectl replace -f -

# 5. Remove old key from config
# 6. Restart API server again
Explanation

Key rotation is critical — a compromised key means all secrets are exposed. On CKS, the process is: add new key first (for encryption) → keep old key second (for decryption) → re-encrypt all secrets → remove old key. The first provider in the list is used for NEW encryptions.

CKS-3
How do you use projected volumes to mount time-limited ServiceAccount tokens instead of static ones?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false   # Disable default mount
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: app@sha256:verified...
    volumeMounts:
    - name: token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/tokens
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: token
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          path: token
          expirationSeconds: 3600    # Expires in 1 hour
          audience: api-server       # Restrict audience
Explanation

Default SA tokens never expire — a stolen token gives permanent access. Projected tokens have configurable expiration and audience. On CKS, use projected tokens with short TTLs (1h) and specific audiences. This limits the blast radius of token theft.

CKS-4
How do you verify that Secrets in etcd are actually encrypted and not stored in plaintext?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create a test secret
kubectl create secret generic test-enc -n default \
  --from-literal=password=supersecret

# 2. Read directly from etcd
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/test-enc \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key | hexdump -C | head -20

# 3. Check output:
# If encrypted: starts with "k8s:enc:aescbc:v1:key1:" (binary data)
# If plaintext: you'll see "supersecret" in clear text

# 4. Clean up
kubectl delete secret test-enc -n default
Explanation

Verification is essential — just configuring encryption doesn't mean it's working. On CKS, create a test secret and read it directly from etcd using etcdctl. Encrypted secrets show the k8s:enc:<provider> prefix. If you see plaintext, the encryption config is not applied correctly.

CKS-5
How do you restrict Secret access so only specific pods can read specific secrets?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. Create dedicated SA per workload
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: payment-sa
  namespace: production
---
# 2. Role with access to ONLY specific secrets
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: payment-secrets
  namespace: production
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["secrets"]
  resourceNames: ["payment-db-cred", "payment-api-key"]
  verbs: ["get"]
  # Only these 2 secrets, only "get" — no list/watch
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: payment-secrets-binding
  namespace: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: payment-sa
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: payment-secrets
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
Explanation

Use resourceNames in RBAC roles to restrict access to specific secrets by name. This prevents a compromised pod from reading ALL secrets in the namespace. On CKS, combine this with automountServiceAccountToken: false and projected tokens for defense in depth.

👁️ Part 9: Observability Chapters 26-28

📊
📝
🔍
📈

PART 9 Observability — Seeing What's Happening

Master the three pillars of observability: Logs, Metrics, and Traces. Learn to monitor, debug, and troubleshoot your Kubernetes applications like a pro.

📝 Logging 📊 Monitoring 🔍 Debugging ⏱️ ~3 hours

🎯 The Three Pillars of Observability

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     THE THREE PILLARS OF OBSERVABILITY                           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

       ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐   ┌──────────────────┐
       │     📝 LOGS       │   │   📊 METRICS      │   │   🔗 TRACES       │
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  "What happened" │   │ "How many/much"  │   │ "Request journey"│
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  ┌────────────┐  │   │  A ──▶ B ──▶ C  │
       │  │ ERROR: DB   │  │   │  │ CPU: 45%   │  │   │  │     │     │  │
       │  │ connection  │  │   │  │ Req/s: 200 │  │   │  10ms  5ms  3ms │
       │  │ failed at   │  │   │  │ Error: 2%  │  │   │                  │
       │  │ 14:32:05    │  │   │  │ Latency:50ms│ │   │  Spans show time │
       │  └────────────┘  │   │  └────────────┘  │   │  per service     │
       │                  │   │                  │   │                  │
       │  ✅ Debugging     │   │  ✅ Alerting      │   │  ✅ Bottlenecks   │
       │  ✅ Audit trail   │   │  ✅ Dashboards    │   │  ✅ Dependencies  │
       │  ✅ Compliance    │   │  ✅ Trends        │   │  ✅ Performance   │
       └──────────────────┘   └──────────────────┘   └──────────────────┘
               ▲                      ▲                      ▲
               │                      │                      │
               └──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┘
                                      │
                             ┌────────┴────────┐
                             │  OBSERVABILITY   │
                             │  "Understanding  │
                             │   system state   │
                             │   from outputs"  │
                             └─────────────────┘

       Chapter 26: 📝 Logging        Chapter 27: 📊 Monitoring        Chapter 28: 🔍 Debug
       └─ kubectl logs              └─ Prometheus + Grafana           └─ Troubleshooting
       └─ EFK/Loki stacks           └─ Metrics Server                 └─ kubectl debug
       └─ Django logging            └─ Alerting                       └─ Pod states

Chapter 26: Logging

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 👁️ OBSERVABILITY

Logs are your first line of defense when something goes wrong. In Kubernetes, understanding how to access, aggregate, and analyze logs is essential for maintaining healthy applications. This chapter covers everything from basic kubectl logs to centralized logging stacks.

🔍 CKAD Domain 3: Application Observability & Maintenance 15% of Exam

kubectl logs is the #1 debugging command on the CKAD exam. You WILL use it in multiple questions. Master the flags!

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: kubectl logs — Essential Flags

These flags save critical time on the exam:

  • kubectl logs POD — current container logs
  • kubectl logs POD -c CONTAINER — specific container in multi-container pod
  • kubectl logs POD --previous — logs from crashed/restarted container
  • kubectl logs POD -f — follow/stream logs in real-time
  • kubectl logs POD --tail=50 — last 50 lines only
  • kubectl logs POD --since=1h — logs from last hour
  • kubectl logs -l app=myapp — logs from all pods with label
  • kubectl logs POD --all-containers — all containers at once

Logging Commands — Complete Reference

ScenarioCommand
Current logskubectl logs my-pod
Specific containerkubectl logs my-pod -c sidecar
Previous (crashed)kubectl logs my-pod --previous
Follow/streamkubectl logs my-pod -f
Last N lineskubectl logs my-pod --tail=100
Since timekubectl logs my-pod --since=30m
Since timestampkubectl logs my-pod --since-time='2024-01-01T00:00:00Z'
All containerskubectl logs my-pod --all-containers=true
All pods by labelkubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers
Job/CronJob logskubectl logs job/my-job
Init container logskubectl logs my-pod -c init-container-name
Grep in logskubectl logs my-pod | grep ERROR

Logging Traps

  • ❌ Multi-container pod without -c → Error: "must specify container" — use -c container-name
  • --previous only works if the container restarted — shows logs from LAST container instance
  • Sidecar container logs — if exam says "extract logs from sidecar", use -c sidecar-name
  • Logs are ephemeral — when pod is deleted, logs are gone. Use --previous before deletion

📊 Kubernetes Logging Commands

📝 kubectl logs View container stdout/stderr
🔄 logs -f Stream logs in real-time
⏪ logs --previous Logs from crashed container
📦 logs -c name Specific container in multi-pod
🎯 logs -l app=web All pods matching label
⏰ logs --since=1h Time-scoped log retrieval

🏗️ Logging Architecture Stack

📊 Visualization (Kibana, Grafana Loki) Dashboards, search, alerting on log patterns
💾 Storage (Elasticsearch, S3, CloudWatch) Centralized log storage with indexing and retention
🚚 Collector (Fluentd, Filebeat, Promtail) DaemonSet agents collecting logs from every node
📦 Container stdout/stderr Apps write to stdout → kubelet stores on node → kubectl logs reads them

26.1 Why Logging Matters

📝 The Difference Logs Make

Without logs, debugging is guesswork. With logs, you have evidence.

🔍 Debugging: With vs Without Logs

❌
Without Proper Logging
  • User: "The app is down"
  • You: shrug "Let me restart it"
  • Hours of guessing what happened
  • Same issue returns next week
  • No evidence for post-mortems
✅
With Proper Logging
  • User: "The app is down"
  • You: "DB password changed at 3:42 PM"
  • Root cause found in minutes
  • Fix deployed, documented
  • Prevent recurrence

🏛️ The Three Pillars of Observability

📝
LOGS

What: Discrete events with timestamps

When: Debug specific issues

Example: "ERROR: DB connection failed at 14:32:05"

📊
METRICS

What: Numeric measurements over time

When: Dashboards, alerting, trends

Example: "CPU: 45%, Requests/sec: 200"

🔗
TRACES

What: Request path across services

When: Distributed system debugging

Example: "Request took 10ms→API, 50ms→DB"

💡 Focus for This Chapter

This chapter focuses on Logs. Chapter 27 covers Metrics (Prometheus/Grafana). Traces (Jaeger, OpenTelemetry) are typically added once you have microservices — we'll touch on them in advanced patterns.

26.2 kubectl Logs — The Basics

The kubectl logs command is your primary tool for viewing container output in Kubernetes. Every container writes to stdout/stderr, and Kubernetes captures this automatically.

📦 How Kubernetes Captures Logs

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    KUBERNETES LOG FLOW                               │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Your App (anihpj)          Container Runtime           Node Filesystem
   ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────┐
   │   Django app    │       │   containerd    │       │ /var/log/pods/  │
   │                 │       │                 │       │                 │
   │  print("...")   │──────▶│  Captures       │──────▶│  pod-xyz.log    │
   │  logger.info()  │ stdout│  stdout/stderr  │ writes│                 │
   │                 │ stderr│                 │       │  (rotated)      │
   └─────────────────┘       └─────────────────┘       └────────┬────────┘
                                                                │
                                                                │ kubectl logs
                                                                ▼
                                                       ┌─────────────────┐
                                                       │   Your Terminal │
                                                       │                 │
                                                       │   $ kubectl logs│
                                                       │   anihpj-xyz    │
                                                       └─────────────────┘

View Current Logs

🖥️ Basic Log Commands
# View logs from a specific pod
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz

# View logs from a deployment (picks one pod)
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj-deployment

# View logs from all pods with a label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj

# Shorthand: k logs (if alias set)
k logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz
📤 Example Output
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Starting Gunicorn 21.2.0
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Listening at: http://0.0.0.0:8000
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] INFO: Booting worker with pid: 1
[2024-01-15 14:32:10] INFO: GET /jobs/ 200 OK 45ms
[2024-01-15 14:32:12] INFO: GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK 23ms

Follow Live Logs

🖥️ Stream Logs in Real-Time
# Follow logs (like tail -f) — streams new logs as they arrive
kubectl logs -f anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz

# Follow logs from deployment
kubectl logs -f deployment/anihpj-deployment

# Follow logs from all pods matching label
kubectl logs -f -l app=anihpj --all-containers

# Press Ctrl+C to stop following
💡 Pro Tip

Combine -f (follow) with --tail=50 to see the last 50 lines, then stream new ones. Perfect for watching deployments in action.

View Previous Container Logs

🔴 Critical for Debugging Crashes!

When a container crashes and restarts, the logs from the crashed instance are gone — unless you use --previous.

🖥️ Retrieve Logs from Crashed Container
# View logs from the PREVIOUS container instance
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz --previous

# Short form
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz -p

# This is ESSENTIAL for CrashLoopBackOff debugging!
📤 Example: Finding the Crash Cause
[2024-01-15 14:32:05] INFO: Starting Gunicorn 21.2.0
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] ERROR: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'psycopg2'
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] ERROR: Worker failed to boot
[2024-01-15 14:32:06] CRITICAL: Application startup failed
# ← Container crashed here — this log would be lost without --previous!

View Specific Container Logs

Multi-container pods have multiple log streams. Use -c to specify which container.

🖥️ Select Container in Multi-Container Pod
# List containers in a pod first
kubectl get pod anihpj-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# Output: anihpj nginx sidecar

# View logs from specific container
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c nginx

# View logs from init container
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c init-migrations --previous

# View ALL containers at once
kubectl logs anihpj-pod --all-containers
  Multi-Container Pod: anihpj-pod
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                     │
  │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐ │
  │  │  anihpj     │  │   nginx     │  │  sidecar    │ │
  │  │  (Django)   │  │  (proxy)    │  │  (logging)  │ │
  │  │             │  │             │  │             │ │
  │  │  -c anihpj  │  │  -c nginx   │  │  -c sidecar │ │
  │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘ │
  │                                                     │
  │  kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c nginx   ← specific     │
  │  kubectl logs anihpj-pod --all-containers ← all    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

View Last N Lines

🖥️ Limit Log Output
# View last 50 lines only
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --tail=50

# View last 100 lines and follow
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --tail=100 -f

# View last 10 lines from all pods with label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --tail=10 --all-containers

View Logs Since a Time

🖥️ Time-Based Log Filtering
# Logs from the last hour
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=1h

# Logs from the last 30 minutes
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=30m

# Logs from the last 2 hours, following
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since=2h -f

# Logs since specific timestamp (RFC 3339)
kubectl logs anihpj-deployment-xyz --since-time=2024-01-15T14:00:00Z

⏱️ Time Duration Formats

FormatMeaningExample
--since=30sLast 30 secondsQuick check after deploy
--since=5mLast 5 minutesRecent errors
--since=1hLast hourCurrent session
--since=24hLast 24 hoursDaily review
--since-time=...Since exact timestampIncident investigation

View Logs by Label

🖥️ Aggregate Logs from Multiple Pods
# All pods with app=anihpj label
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj

# All containers in all matching pods
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers

# Follow with tail, all pods
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers --tail=20 -f

# Multiple labels (AND)
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj,tier=backend --all-containers

# With prefix showing pod name (great for debugging)
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --prefix
📤 Output with --prefix
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-abc/anihpj] GET /jobs/ 200 OK
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz/anihpj] GET /api/jobs/ 200 OK
[pod/anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-123/anihpj] POST /jobs/create/ 201 Created

📋 kubectl logs Quick Reference

FlagPurposeExample
-fFollow/stream logskubectl logs -f pod-name
-p / --previousPrevious (crashed) containerkubectl logs -p pod-name
-cSpecific containerkubectl logs pod -c nginx
--all-containersAll containers in podkubectl logs pod --all-containers
--tail=NLast N lineskubectl logs pod --tail=100
--since=Since durationkubectl logs pod --since=1h
--since-time=Since timestampkubectl logs pod --since-time=2024-01-15T14:00:00Z
-lBy label selectorkubectl logs -l app=anihpj
--prefixShow pod name prefixkubectl logs -l app=anihpj --prefix
--timestampsAdd timestampskubectl logs pod --timestamps
🔥 Pro Tool: Stern — Multi-Pod Log Tailing Made Easy

stern is a popular open-source tool that makes tailing logs from multiple pods effortless. Unlike kubectl logs, it auto-follows new pods and color-codes output.

🖥️ Stern: Install and Usage
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  INSTALL STERN
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# macOS
brew install stern

# Windows (with Chocolatey)
choco install stern

# Linux (download binary)
curl -Lo stern https://github.com/stern/stern/releases/download/v1.28.0/stern_1.28.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz
tar -xvf stern_1.28.0_linux_amd64.tar.gz
sudo mv stern /usr/local/bin/

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  USE STERN FOR MULTI-POD LOGGING
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Tail all pods matching "anihpj" (auto-follows new pods!)
stern anihpj

# In specific namespace
stern anihpj -n anihpj

# Only specific container
stern anihpj -c django

# With timestamps
stern anihpj -t

# Last 10 lines per pod, then follow
stern anihpj --tail 10

# Exclude certain pods
stern anihpj --exclude-pod postgres

# Output with JSON parsing (if logs are JSON)
stern anihpj -o json | jq '.message'

⚖️ kubectl logs vs Stern

Featurekubectl logsStern
Multiple podsManual with -l label✅ Auto with regex
Color coding❌ None✅ Per pod colors
Auto-follow new pods❌ No✅ Yes
Regex filtering❌ No✅ --include/exclude
Built-in✅ Yes❌ Install required

26.3 Application-Level Logging in Django

While kubectl logs captures stdout, your Django application should use Python's logging module for structured, useful output.

📊 Python Log Levels

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     LOG LEVELS (ascending severity)              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    LEVEL       │ VALUE │ USE CASE                      │ PRODUCTION?
  ──────────────┼───────┼───────────────────────────────┼───────────
    DEBUG       │  10   │ Detailed diagnostic info      │ ❌ NO
    INFO        │  20   │ Normal operations, requests   │ ✅ YES
    WARNING     │  30   │ Something unexpected          │ ✅ YES
    ERROR       │  40   │ Error occurred, handled       │ ✅ YES  
    CRITICAL    │  50   │ System failure, shutting down │ ✅ YES

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  💡 Set level to INFO in production, DEBUG only in dev         │
  │     Higher level = less noise, but might miss context          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj/settings.py 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  LOGGING CONFIGURATION FOR KUBERNETES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
import os

LOGGING = {
    'version': 1,
    'disable_existing_loggers': False,
    
    # ─── FORMATTERS ───
    'formatters': {
        # JSON format: machine-parseable, perfect for log aggregation
        'json': {
            'format': '{"time": "%(asctime)s", "level": "%(levelname)s", '
                      '"logger": "%(name)s", "message": "%(message)s", '
                      '"module": "%(module)s", "line": %(lineno)d}'
        },
        # Simple format: human-readable for development
        'simple': {
            'format': '[%(asctime)s] %(levelname)s %(name)s: %(message)s'
        },
    },
    
    # ─── HANDLERS ───
    'handlers': {
        # Console handler: outputs to stdout (captured by K8s)
        'console': {
            'class': 'logging.StreamHandler',
            'formatter': 'json' if os.getenv('K8S_ENV') else 'simple',
            'stream': 'ext://sys.stdout',  # stdout, not stderr
        },
    },
    
    # ─── LOGGERS ───
    'loggers': {
        # Django framework logs
        'django': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': os.getenv('DJANGO_LOG_LEVEL', 'INFO'),
            'propagate': False,
        },
        # Django request logs (HTTP requests)
        'django.request': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': 'INFO',
            'propagate': False,
        },
        # Your application logs
        'jobpost': {
            'handlers': ['console'],
            'level': 'DEBUG' if os.getenv('DEBUG') else 'INFO',
            'propagate': False,
        },
    },
    
    # Root logger (catch-all)
    'root': {
        'handlers': ['console'],
        'level': 'WARNING',
    },
}
jobpost/views.py 
import logging
from django.shortcuts import render
from django.http import JsonResponse
from .models import JobPost

# Get logger for this module
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)  # 'jobpost.views'

def job_list(request):
    """List all job postings."""
    logger.info(f"Job list requested by {request.META.get('REMOTE_ADDR')}")
    
    try:
        jobs = JobPost.objects.filter(is_active=True)
        logger.debug(f"Found {jobs.count()} active jobs")
        
        return render(request, 'jobpost/list.html', {'jobs': jobs})
        
    except Exception as e:
        logger.error(f"Failed to fetch jobs: {e}", exc_info=True)
        return JsonResponse({'error': 'Database error'}, status=500)


def job_create(request):
    """Create a new job posting."""
    if request.method == 'POST':
        logger.info(f"Job creation attempt by user {request.user}")
        
        # ❌ NEVER log sensitive data!
        # logger.info(f"API key: {request.headers.get('Authorization')}")  # BAD!
        
        try:
            job = JobPost.objects.create(
                title=request.POST['title'],
                description=request.POST['description'],
            )
            logger.info(f"Job created: id={job.id}, title={job.title}")
            return JsonResponse({'id': job.id}, status=201)
            
        except Exception as e:
            logger.exception("Job creation failed")  # Includes stack trace
            return JsonResponse({'error': str(e)}, status=400)

✅❌ What to Log vs What NOT to Log

✅
DO Log
  • Request method, path, status code
  • User ID (not username/email)
  • Operation start/end times
  • Error messages with context
  • Database query counts
  • External API call results
  • Authentication events (login/logout)
❌
NEVER Log
  • Passwords or credentials
  • API keys or tokens
  • Credit card numbers
  • Personal data (PII)
  • Session tokens
  • Full request bodies with sensitive data
  • Database connection strings

26.4 Centralized Logging Architecture

kubectl logs works for quick checks, but production clusters need centralized logging. Why?

🤔 Why Centralize Logs?

😰
Problems Without Centralization
  • Pods die → logs disappear forever
  • 50 pods = 50 separate log streams
  • Can't search across pods/time
  • No correlation with metrics
  • No long-term retention
🎯
Benefits of Centralization
  • Logs persist beyond pod lifecycle
  • Single pane of glass for all logs
  • Full-text search across cluster
  • Dashboards and visualizations
  • Alerting on log patterns

🏗️ Centralized Logging Architecture

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    CENTRALIZED LOGGING IN KUBERNETES                          │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Node 1                    Node 2                    Node 3
  ┌────────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐       ┌────────────────┐
  │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │       │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │       │ ┌────┐ ┌────┐ │
  │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │       │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │       │ │Pod │ │Pod │ │
  │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │       │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │       │ │📦  │ │📦  │ │
  │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │       │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │       │ └──┬─┘ └──┬─┘ │
  │    │ stdout│   │       │    │      │   │       │    │      │   │
  │    ▼      ▼   │       │    ▼      ▼   │       │    ▼      ▼   │
  │  /var/log/pods│       │  /var/log/pods│       │  /var/log/pods│
  │       │       │       │       │       │       │       │       │
  │   ┌───┴───┐   │       │   ┌───┴───┐   │       │   ┌───┴───┐   │
  │   │Fluentd│   │       │   │Fluentd│   │       │   │Fluentd│   │
  │   │(DaemonSet)│       │   │(DaemonSet)│       │   │(DaemonSet)│
  │   └───┬───┘   │       │   └───┬───┘   │       │   └───┬───┘   │
  └───────┼───────┘       └───────┼───────┘       └───────┼───────┘
          │                       │                       │
          └───────────────────────┼───────────────────────┘
                                  │
                                  ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │    Elasticsearch         │ ← Store & Index
                    │    (or Loki)             │
                    └────────────┬────────────┘
                                 │
                                 ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────────┐
                    │    Kibana / Grafana      │ ← Search & Visualize
                    │    🔍 Dashboard           │
                    └─────────────────────────┘

EFK Stack (Elasticsearch + Fluentd + Kibana)

📝
Fluentd / Fluent Bit

Role: Log collector

Runs as: DaemonSet (1 per node)

Does: Reads /var/log/pods, parses, ships to Elasticsearch

🗄️
Elasticsearch

Role: Log storage + indexing

Runs as: StatefulSet

Does: Full-text search, aggregations

📊
Kibana

Role: Visualization UI

Runs as: Deployment

Does: Search, dashboards, discover

🖥️ Deploy EFK with Helm
# Add Elastic Helm repo
helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co
helm repo update

# Install Elasticsearch
helm install elasticsearch elastic/elasticsearch \
  --namespace logging --create-namespace \
  --set replicas=1 \
  --set resources.requests.memory=1Gi

# Install Kibana
helm install kibana elastic/kibana \
  --namespace logging

# Install Fluent Bit (lightweight Fluentd)
helm repo add fluent https://fluent.github.io/helm-charts
helm install fluent-bit fluent/fluent-bit \
  --namespace logging \
  --set backend.type=es \
  --set backend.es.host=elasticsearch-master
⚠️ Resource Warning

Elasticsearch is memory-hungry (requires 2GB+ heap). For learning/small clusters, consider Loki (next section) which is much lighter.

Loki + Grafana (Lightweight Alternative)

🚀 Loki: "Prometheus, but for Logs"

Loki is designed by Grafana Labs. It doesn't index log content (unlike Elasticsearch), only labels — making it 10x cheaper to operate.

📝
Promtail

Role: Log collector (like Fluentd)

Runs as: DaemonSet

📦
Loki

Role: Log aggregator

Runs as: StatefulSet

📊
Grafana

Role: Visualization

Bonus: Also shows metrics!

🖥️ Deploy Loki Stack with Helm
# Add Grafana Helm repo
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

# Install Loki Stack (includes Loki, Promtail, Grafana)
helm install loki grafana/loki-stack \
  --namespace logging --create-namespace \
  --set grafana.enabled=true \
  --set prometheus.enabled=false

# Get Grafana password
kubectl get secret loki-grafana -n logging \
  -o jsonpath="{.data.admin-password}" | base64 -d

# Port-forward to access Grafana
kubectl port-forward svc/loki-grafana 3000:80 -n logging
# Open http://localhost:3000, login: admin / (password from above)

⚖️ EFK vs Loki: Which to Choose?

AspectEFK StackLoki + Grafana
Memory UsageHigh (2GB+ for ES)Low (256MB+)
Search SpeedFast (indexed)Slower (grep-like)
Full-text Search✅ Excellent⚠️ Label-based only
Cost at ScaleExpensive10x cheaper
Metrics IntegrationSeparateSame dashboard
Best ForLarge enterpriseMost K8s clusters
RecommendationComplex log analysis✅ Start here

26.5 Log Aggregation Best Practices

✅ Logging Best Practices Checklist

✅
DO These
  • ✅ Use structured JSON logging
  • ✅ Include request IDs for tracing
  • ✅ Set retention policies (30/90 days)
  • ✅ Alert on ERROR frequency spikes
  • ✅ Log to stdout (not files)
  • ✅ Use consistent timestamp format
  • ✅ Add K8s metadata (pod, namespace)
❌
AVOID These
  • ❌ Logging sensitive data (PII, secrets)
  • ❌ DEBUG level in production
  • ❌ Logging inside tight loops
  • ❌ Unstructured log messages
  • ❌ Logging request/response bodies
  • ❌ No rotation (disk fills up)
  • ❌ Ignoring log storage costs

📋 Chapter 26 Summary: Logging

  • ✅ kubectl logs — Basic log access, use -f, --previous, -c, --since
  • ✅ Structured logging — JSON format in Django for parsing
  • ✅ Log levels — DEBUG dev only, INFO+ in production
  • ✅ EFK Stack — Elasticsearch + Fluentd + Kibana (powerful, heavy)
  • ✅ Loki Stack — Promtail + Loki + Grafana (lightweight, recommended)
  • ✅ Best practices — Structured, retention policies, no sensitive data
🖥️ Chapter 26 Quick Reference
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  KUBECTL LOGS ESSENTIALS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj-deployment          # Basic logs
kubectl logs -f pod-name                            # Follow live
kubectl logs pod-name --previous                    # Crashed container
kubectl logs pod-name -c container-name             # Specific container
kubectl logs -l app=anihpj --all-containers         # All matching pods
kubectl logs pod-name --tail=100 --since=1h         # Last 100 lines, 1 hour

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  INSTALL LOKI STACK
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts
helm install loki grafana/loki-stack -n logging --create-namespace \
  --set grafana.enabled=true

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Logging

⏱️ Target Time: 5 minutes
Scenario: A multi-container pod is having issues. Use logging commands to diagnose the problem.
  1. Create a pod multi-log with two containers: app (nginx) and sidecar (busybox running while true; do echo "sidecar tick"; sleep 5; done)
  2. View logs from the app container only
  3. View logs from the sidecar container only
  4. Stream logs from all containers simultaneously
  5. Get the last 10 lines of logs from the app container
📖 View Solution
Solution — Logging Drill 
# 1. Create multi-container pod
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: multi-log
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
  - name: sidecar
    image: busybox
    command: ["sh", "-c", "while true; do echo 'sidecar tick'; sleep 5; done"]
EOF

# 2. Logs from app container
kubectl logs multi-log -c app

# 3. Logs from sidecar container
kubectl logs multi-log -c sidecar

# 4. Stream all containers
kubectl logs multi-log --all-containers -f

# 5. Last 10 lines from app
kubectl logs multi-log -c app --tail=10

Chapter 26 — Practice Questions

Q1
What are the three logging levels in Kubernetes? Where does each type of log come from?
Show Answer
Answer
LevelSourceHow to Access
Container logsstdout/stderr from appkubectl logs pod-name
Node logskubelet, container runtimejournalctl -u kubelet
Cluster logsAPI server, scheduler, etc./var/log/kube-apiserver.log
Explanation

K8s captures anything written to stdout/stderr as container logs. Node-level logs come from system components (kubelet, container runtime). Cluster-level logs from control-plane components. On the CKAD, you primarily deal with container logs via kubectl logs. Node/cluster logs are more CKA territory but understanding the levels is valuable.

Q2
How do you view logs from a specific container in a multi-container pod? How do you stream logs in real-time?
Show Answer
Answer
# Specific container in multi-container pod
kubectl logs my-pod -c sidecar-container

# Stream logs in real-time (follow)
kubectl logs my-pod -f

# Last N lines
kubectl logs my-pod --tail=100

# Logs since a time
kubectl logs my-pod --since=1h
kubectl logs my-pod --since-time="2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"

# Previous container (after restart/crash)
kubectl logs my-pod -c app --previous

# All containers in a pod
kubectl logs my-pod --all-containers=true

# All pods with a label
kubectl logs -l app=web --all-containers=true
Explanation

Key flags: -c for container name, -f for follow, --previous for crashed container logs, --tail=N for last N lines, --since for time-based filtering. On the CKAD, --previous is critical for debugging CrashLoopBackOff — it shows logs from the last container run before it crashed.

Q3
What is the sidecar logging pattern? Write a pod with an app container and a sidecar that ships logs.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-logging
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: my-app:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  - name: log-shipper
    image: fluentd:v1.16
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/app
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}
  ┌─────────── Pod ───────────┐
  │                           │
  │  ┌─────────┐              │
  │  │  App    │──writes──┐   │
  │  │Container│          │   │
  │  └─────────┘          ▼   │
  │               ┌─────────┐ │
  │               │ shared  │ │
  │               │ volume  │ │
  │               └────┬────┘ │
  │                    │      │
  │  ┌─────────┐      │      │
  │  │Sidecar  │──reads┘     │
  │  │(fluentd)│──ships──▶ Elasticsearch
  │  └─────────┘              │
  └───────────────────────────┘
Explanation

The sidecar pattern uses a shared emptyDir volume. The app writes logs to files, and the sidecar reads and forward them. This decouples logging from the application. On the CKAD, the shared volume pattern between containers is a foundational multi-container concept. The sidecar container is the most common multi-container pattern tested.

Q4
A pod is in CrashLoopBackOff. How do you get the logs from the crashed container?
Show Answer
Answer
# The pod keeps crashing — current container may not have logs
kubectl logs crashing-pod
# Error: the container has already stopped

# Use --previous to get logs from the LAST container instance
kubectl logs crashing-pod --previous

# Check events for crash reason
kubectl describe pod crashing-pod | tail -20

# Common CrashLoopBackOff causes in logs:
# - Missing env vars / config files
# - Database connection failures
# - Permission denied errors
# - Segfaults / OOM kills
Explanation

--previous (or -p) retrieves logs from the previous container instance — essential for CrashLoopBackOff debugging. Without it, you can't see why the container crashed because the current container has already exited. On the CKAD, this is one of the most important debugging commands. Combine with kubectl describe pod for events.

Q5
How do you view logs from ALL pods in a Deployment at once?
Show Answer
Answer
# Using label selector
kubectl logs -l app=web -f --all-containers=true

# Using deployment name (shorthand, but limited)
kubectl logs deployment/web-app

# With prefix (shows which pod each line comes from)
kubectl logs -l app=web --prefix=true

# Third-party tools for better multi-pod logging:
# stern — real-time multi-pod log tailing
# stern web-app -n production
# kubetail — tail logs from multiple pods
Explanation

-l app=web selects all pods with matching labels and aggregates their logs. --prefix=true prepends each line with the pod name — essential for distinguishing which pod generated which line. On the CKAD, kubectl logs -l is the fastest way to aggregate logs across multiple pods without any additional tools.

Q6
Where are container logs stored on the node's filesystem?
Show Answer
Answer
# Container logs on the node:
/var/log/containers/<pod-name>_<namespace>_<container-name>-<container-id>.log
# Symlinks to:
/var/log/pods/<namespace>_<pod-name>_<pod-uid>/<container-name>/0.log

# These are the same logs kubectl logs reads
# Node-level log agents (Fluentd DaemonSet) read from here

# Log rotation configured via kubelet flags:
# --container-log-max-size=10Mi  (default: 10Mi)
# --container-log-max-files=5    (default: 5)
Explanation

Container logs are stored at /var/log/containers/ on the node. DaemonSet-based log collectors (Fluentd, Filebeat) read from this path. Kubelet handles log rotation based on size and file count. On the CKAD, you'll use kubectl logs rather than reading node files directly, but understanding the storage location helps with DaemonSet log collector questions.

Q7
How do you output logs in JSON format for structured logging?
Show Answer
Answer
# Kubernetes captures whatever your app outputs to stdout
# If your app logs JSON, kubectl logs shows JSON:
kubectl logs api-pod
# {"level":"info","msg":"Server started","port":8080,"ts":"2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"}

# Filter JSON logs with jq:
kubectl logs api-pod | jq '.level == "error"'

# Kubernetes Events (not logs, but useful):
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning
Explanation

K8s doesn't enforce log format — it captures stdout/stderr as-is. Structured logging (JSON) is an application responsibility. Use jq to filter JSON logs. Events (not exactly logs) are another data source — kubectl get events shows scheduler decisions, image pulls, container crashes, etc. On the CKAD, events are often more useful than logs for infrastructure issues.

Q8
What is the difference between kubectl logs and kubectl describe pod for debugging?
Show Answer
Answer
CommandShowsBest For
kubectl logsApplication stdout/stderr outputApp errors, stack traces, business logic issues
kubectl describePod metadata, conditions, eventsScheduling failures, image pull errors, OOM kills
 
# Use BOTH for comprehensive debugging:
kubectl describe pod failing-pod | grep -A20 Events
kubectl logs failing-pod --previous
Explanation

kubectl logs shows what the application outputs. kubectl describe shows Kubernetes-level events (scheduling, pulling images, readiness checks). For a complete picture, always use both. On the CKAD, start with describe (infrastructure issues), then check logs (application issues). If the container never starts, only describe will have useful info.

Q9
How do you configure log verbosity for Kubernetes components?
Show Answer
Answer
# kubectl verbosity levels (0-9):
kubectl get pods -v=6    # Show API calls
kubectl get pods -v=8    # Show request/response bodies
kubectl get pods -v=9    # Show curl commands

# Common verbosity levels:
# -v=0  Minimal output
# -v=4  Debug-level info
# -v=6  API request URLs
# -v=8  Request/response bodies
# -v=9  Full curl commands (maximum detail)

# Example: debug why a command fails
kubectl apply -f broken.yaml -v=8
Explanation

-v=N controls kubectl's verbosity. Level 6+ shows API URLs (useful for understanding what kubectl does). Level 8+ shows full request/response bodies (useful for debugging API errors). On the CKAD, -v=6 helps you understand API calls, while -v=8 helps debug complex failures.

Q10
How do you pipe pod logs to a file in a CKAD exam scenario?
Show Answer
Answer
# Save logs to a file
kubectl logs my-pod > /tmp/pod-logs.txt

# Save with timestamps
kubectl logs my-pod --timestamps > /tmp/pod-logs.txt

# Save error logs from previous container
kubectl logs my-pod --previous 2>&1 > /tmp/crash-logs.txt

# Filter for errors and save
kubectl logs my-pod | grep -i error > /tmp/errors.txt

# Save last 50 lines from specific container
kubectl logs my-pod -c sidecar --tail=50 > /tmp/sidecar.txt
Explanation

On the CKAD, exam questions often ask you to "save logs to /opt/answers/pod-logs.txt". Use simple shell redirection: kubectl logs pod-name > file. Add --timestamps if requested. Use 2>&1 to capture stderr as well. This is a straightforward but common exam task — practice the exact command to save time.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Kubernetes Audit Logging Deep Dive

MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20% CLUSTER SETUP 15%

Audit logging records every API request — who did what, when, and to what resource. It's your forensic evidence for security investigations and a critical CKS exam topic.

Audit LevelWhat's LoggedUse Case
NoneNothingSkip noise (healthz, system:metrics)
MetadataRequest metadata (user, verb, resource)Most resources — who did what
RequestMetadata + request bodySecret creation — see what was created
RequestResponseMetadata + request + response bodyCritical resources — full audit trail
 
# CKS-ready audit policy
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
omitStages:
- RequestReceived
rules:
# Don't log healthz/readyz/livez
- level: None
  nonResourceURLs: ["/healthz*", "/readyz*", "/livez*"]

# Don't log system component noise
- level: None
  users: ["system:apiserver", "system:kube-scheduler"]
  
# Log secret operations at Request level
- level: Request
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["secrets", "configmaps"]
  
# Log RBAC changes at RequestResponse level  
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    resources: ["clusterroles", "clusterrolebindings", "roles", "rolebindings"]

# Everything else at Metadata
- level: Metadata

Chapter 26 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create an audit policy that logs Secret access at Request level and all RBAC changes at RequestResponse level.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
omitStages:
- RequestReceived
rules:
- level: None
  nonResourceURLs: ["/healthz*", "/readyz*"]
- level: None
  users: ["system:kube-proxy"]
  verbs: ["watch"]
- level: Request
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["secrets"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "create", "update", "delete"]
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    resources: ["*"]
- level: Metadata
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods", "services"]
- level: Metadata
# API server flags:
# --audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit/policy.yaml
# --audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log
# --audit-log-maxage=30
# --audit-log-maxbackup=10
# --audit-log-maxsize=100
Explanation

Audit policies are evaluated top-down — first matching rule wins. On CKS, structure rules from most specific to least: exclude noise → high-detail for sensitive resources → metadata for everything else. Always include --audit-log-path and size/rotation flags.

CKS-2
How do you investigate a security incident using audit logs?
Show Answer
Answer
# Find who accessed secrets in the last hour
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.resource == "secrets" and
  .requestReceivedTimestamp > "2024-01-15T10:00:00Z"
) | {
  user: .user.username,
  verb: .verb,
  secret: .objectRef.name,
  ns: .objectRef.namespace,
  time: .requestReceivedTimestamp,
  source: .sourceIPs[0]
}'

# Find failed authentication attempts
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .responseStatus.code >= 400
) | {user: .user.username, verb: .verb, code: .responseStatus.code}'

# Find pod exec events (shell access)
