cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.subresource == "exec"
) | {user: .user.username, pod: .objectRef.name}'
Explanation

On CKS, you're expected to parse audit logs with jq. Key fields: .user.username (who), .verb (action), .objectRef (target resource), .sourceIPs (origin), .responseStatus.code (success/failure). Focus on secret access, RBAC changes, and pod exec events.

CKS-3
Configure the API server to use audit logging with webhook backend.
Show Answer
Answer
# API server flags for webhook audit backend
--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit/policy.yaml
--audit-webhook-config-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit/webhook-config.yaml
--audit-webhook-batch-max-wait=5s
# webhook-config.yaml (kubeconfig format)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
clusters:
- name: audit-webhook
  cluster:
    server: https://audit-collector.security:8443/audit
    certificate-authority: /etc/kubernetes/audit/ca.crt
users:
- name: audit-webhook
  user:
    client-certificate: /etc/kubernetes/audit/client.crt
    client-key: /etc/kubernetes/audit/client.key
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: audit-webhook
    user: audit-webhook
  name: default
current-context: default
Explanation

The webhook backend sends audit events to an external SIEM or log aggregator in real-time. On CKS, know both file and webhook backends. Webhook is preferred for production — it enables centralized security monitoring and alerting on suspicious API activity.

CKS-4
How do you reduce audit log volume without losing security visibility?
Show Answer
Answer
# Optimized audit policy — reduce noise
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
omitStages:
- RequestReceived           # Skip duplicate stage
rules:
# Skip high-volume, low-value endpoints
- level: None
  nonResourceURLs: ["/healthz*", "/readyz*", "/livez*", "/metrics"]
- level: None
  users: ["system:apiserver"]
  verbs: ["get"]
- level: None
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["events"]   # Events are very noisy
  
# Skip read-only system operations
- level: None
  userGroups: ["system:nodes"]
  verbs: ["get", "watch"]
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["nodes", "nodes/status"]

# Keep security-relevant at high detail
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    resources: ["*"]
- level: Request
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["secrets"]
  
# Everything else at Metadata
- level: Metadata
Explanation

Audit logs can be extremely verbose. On CKS, reduce volume by: skipping health checks and metrics, excluding system component reads, and using omitStages: [RequestReceived]. Keep high detail only for security-sensitive resources (secrets, RBAC, exec). Balance visibility with storage costs.

CKS-5
How do you detect unauthorized kubectl exec usage via audit logs?
Show Answer
Answer
# Audit policy to capture exec events
# Add this rule BEFORE the catch-all:
- level: RequestResponse
  resources:
  - group: ""
    resources: ["pods/exec", "pods/attach", "pods/portforward"]
  
# Search for exec events in audit log
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.subresource == "exec" or
  .objectRef.subresource == "attach"
) | {
  user: .user.username,
  pod: .objectRef.name,
  ns: .objectRef.namespace,
  time: .requestReceivedTimestamp,
  command: .requestObject.command,
  sourceIP: .sourceIPs[0]
}'

# Alert on exec to production namespace
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.subresource == "exec" and
  .objectRef.namespace == "production"
)'
Explanation

kubectl exec gives shell access to containers — it's a high-risk operation. On CKS, audit exec events at RequestResponse level to capture the command executed. Also audit pods/attach and pods/portforward. In production, alert on any exec events.

Chapter 27: Monitoring

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 📊 METRICS

Logs tell you what happened. Metrics tell you how your system is performing right now — and how it's trending. In this chapter, you'll learn to monitor Kubernetes clusters with the Metrics Server, Prometheus, and Grafana.

🔍 CKAD Domain 3: Application Observability & Maintenance 15% of Exam

kubectl top is essential for CKAD. You'll need it to identify resource-hungry pods and troubleshoot performance issues.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: kubectl top — Quick Monitoring

These commands require metrics-server to be installed (it is in the exam environment):

  • kubectl top nodes — CPU and memory usage per node
  • kubectl top pods — CPU and memory usage per pod
  • kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu — sort by CPU usage
  • kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory — sort by memory
  • kubectl top pods -A — all namespaces
  • kubectl top pods --containers — per-container breakdown

Monitoring & Resource Usage Commands

ActionCommand
Node CPU/Memorykubectl top nodes
Pod CPU/Memorykubectl top pods -n production
Sort by CPUkubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu
Sort by memorykubectl top pods --sort-by=memory
All namespaceskubectl top pods -A
Per-containerkubectl top pods --containers
Identify highest CPU podkubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu | head -2
Check OOMKilledkubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].lastState.terminated.reason}'

Monitoring Traps

  • ❌ kubectl top shows "error: Metrics API not available" → metrics-server not installed (but IS available in CKAD exam)
  • kubectl top shows CURRENT usage, not requests/limits — current usage can exceed requests but not limits
  • OOMKilled detection: kubectl describe pod X → look for "Last State: Terminated, Reason: OOMKilled"
  • Sort by CPU vs memory — exam may ask "find the pod consuming most CPU" → --sort-by=cpu

📊 Monitoring Investigation Flow

1
kubectl top

Check current CPU/memory usage

2
Sort

--sort-by=cpu or memory

3
Compare

Usage vs requests vs limits

4
Describe

Check Events & OOMKilled status

5
Logs

kubectl logs --previous for crash info

top pods Pod-Level Metrics
top nodes Node-Level Metrics
metrics-server Required Backend
OOMKilled Memory Limit Exceeded

27.1 What to Monitor in Kubernetes

🏔️ The Three Layers of K8s Monitoring

                    ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │     LAYER 3: APPLICATION            │
                    │                                     │
                    │  📊 HTTP latency (p50, p99)        │
                    │  📈 Request rate (req/sec)         │
                    │  ❌ Error rate (4xx, 5xx %)        │
                    │  🎯 Business metrics (jobs/min)    │
                    │                                     │
                    │  "Is my Django app healthy?"       │
                    └───────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                                    │
              ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
              │        LAYER 2: KUBERNETES OBJECTS          │
              │                                              │
              │  🔄 Pod restarts (CrashLoopBackOff?)       │
              │  📦 Deployment replicas (desired vs ready) │
              │  💾 PVC usage (storage full?)              │
              │  🔌 Service endpoints (healthy?)           │
              │                                              │
              │  "Are my K8s resources in good state?"     │
              └───────────────────┬───────────────────────┘
                                  │
    ┌─────────────────────────────┴─────────────────────────────┐
    │              LAYER 1: INFRASTRUCTURE                        │
    │                                                              │
    │  🖥️ Node CPU utilization (%)      │  💾 Memory usage (%)   │
    │  💿 Disk I/O and space            │  🌐 Network bandwidth   │
    │  🔋 Node conditions (Ready?)      │  📊 kubelet health      │
    │                                                              │
    │  "Are my physical/virtual machines healthy?"                │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    ⚡ Golden Rule: Monitor ALL THREE layers for complete visibility!

📊 Key Metrics for anihpj

🎯
Application Layer
  • Response time: /jobs/ endpoint
  • Requests per second
  • Error rate (5xx responses)
  • Database query time
  • Jobs created per hour
☸️
Kubernetes Layer
  • Pod restarts count
  • Deployment replica status
  • HPA scaling events
  • PVC usage percentage
  • Service endpoint health
🖥️
Infrastructure Layer
  • Node CPU & memory
  • Disk space / I/O
  • Network throughput
  • Node conditions
  • kubelet health

27.2 Metrics Server

📊 Metrics Server: Lightweight Built-in Monitoring

Metrics Server is a cluster-wide aggregator of resource usage data. It enables kubectl top and is required for Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA).

🔄 How Metrics Server Works

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    METRICS SERVER FLOW                         │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    Node 1              Node 2              Node 3
  ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐
  │  kubelet   │     │  kubelet   │     │  kubelet   │
  │    │       │     │    │       │     │    │       │
  │ cAdvisor   │     │ cAdvisor   │     │ cAdvisor   │
  │ (container │     │ (container │     │ (container │
  │  metrics)  │     │  metrics)  │     │  metrics)  │
  └─────┬──────┘     └─────┬──────┘     └─────┬──────┘
        │                  │                  │
        └──────────────────┼──────────────────┘
                           │ scrape every 15s
                           ▼
                  ┌─────────────────┐
                  │  Metrics Server │
                  │  (aggregates)   │
                  └────────┬────────┘
                           │
          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
          ▼                ▼                ▼
    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
    │ kubectl  │    │   HPA    │    │   VPA    │
    │   top    │    │ (scaler) │    │ (scaler) │
    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
🖥️ Enable and Use Metrics Server
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  ENABLE METRICS SERVER (Minikube)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
minikube addons enable metrics-server

# Wait for it to be ready (~1-2 minutes)
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  VIEW RESOURCE USAGE
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Node-level metrics
kubectl top nodes

# Pod-level metrics
kubectl top pods

# Sorted by CPU usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu

# Sorted by memory usage
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

# Specific namespace
kubectl top pods -n anihpj

# With containers breakdown
kubectl top pods --containers
📤 Example kubectl top Output
$ kubectl top nodes
NAME       CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
minikube   245m         12%    1847Mi          47%

$ kubectl top pods -n anihpj
NAME                                  CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-abc       25m          128Mi
anihpj-deployment-7d9f8b6c4-xyz       31m          135Mi
postgres-0                            15m          256Mi
💡 CPU Units

245m = 245 millicores = 0.245 CPU cores. 1000m = 1 full CPU core.

27.3 Prometheus

Prometheus is the industry standard for Kubernetes monitoring. It's a CNCF graduated project (like Kubernetes itself).

What is Prometheus?

🔥
Prometheus Features
  • Pull-based: Scrapes /metrics endpoints
  • Time-series DB: Stores metrics with timestamps
  • PromQL: Powerful query language
  • Alerting: Built-in AlertManager
  • Service discovery: Auto-finds K8s pods
✅
Why Prometheus for K8s?
  • Native K8s integration
  • Labels match K8s metadata
  • Auto-discovers pods/services
  • Pre-built K8s metrics
  • De facto standard (all tools support it)

How it Works in Kubernetes

🔄 Prometheus Architecture in K8s

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                      PROMETHEUS IN KUBERNETES                          │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

         Application Pods             System Components
  ┌────────────────────────┐    ┌────────────────────────┐
  │  anihpj  │  postgres   │    │  kubelet │ kube-state  │
  │  :8000   │  :9187      │    │  :10255  │  -metrics   │
  │ /metrics │ /metrics    │    │ /metrics │  /metrics   │
  └─────┬────┴──────┬──────┘    └─────┬────┴──────┬──────┘
        │           │                 │           │
        └───────────┴─────────────────┴───────────┘
                              │
                        scrapes /metrics
                         every 15-30s
                              │
                              ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │     PROMETHEUS       │
                    │                     │
                    │  ┌───────────────┐  │
                    │  │ Time-series   │  │
                    │  │   Database    │  │
                    │  └───────────────┘  │
                    │                     │
                    │  ┌───────────────┐  │
                    │  │  Alert Rules  │──┼──▶ AlertManager ──▶ Slack/PagerDuty
                    │  └───────────────┘  │
                    └──────────┬──────────┘
                               │
                    PromQL queries
                               │
                               ▼
                    ┌─────────────────────┐
                    │       GRAFANA        │
                    │    📊 Dashboards     │
                    │    📈 Graphs         │
                    │    🔔 Alerts         │
                    └─────────────────────┘

Installing with Prometheus Operator

🖥️ Install kube-prometheus-stack (Recommended)
# Add Prometheus Community Helm repo
helm repo add prometheus-community \
  https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts
helm repo update

# Install the full stack (Prometheus + Grafana + AlertManager + exporters)
helm install prometheus prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \
  --namespace monitoring --create-namespace

# Check what got installed
kubectl get pods -n monitoring

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  OUTPUT: Multiple components deployed
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# prometheus-kube-prometheus-operator-xxx   (manages Prometheus)
# prometheus-prometheus-xxx                  (Prometheus server)
# prometheus-grafana-xxx                     (Grafana UI)
# prometheus-alertmanager-xxx                (AlertManager)
# prometheus-kube-state-metrics-xxx          (K8s object metrics)
# prometheus-prometheus-node-exporter-xxx    (Node metrics - DaemonSet)
🖥️ Access the Dashboards
# Port-forward Prometheus UI
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-prometheus 9090:9090 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:9090

# Port-forward Grafana
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-grafana 3000:80 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:3000
# Default login: admin / prom-operator

# Port-forward AlertManager
kubectl port-forward svc/prometheus-kube-prometheus-alertmanager 9093:9093 -n monitoring
# Open http://localhost:9093

Instrumenting anihpj

To expose Django metrics to Prometheus, use the django-prometheus library:

requirements.txt 
Django==4.2
gunicorn==21.2.0
psycopg2-binary==2.9.9
django-prometheus==2.3.1  # Add this line
anihpj/settings.py 
INSTALLED_APPS = [
    # ... other apps
    'django_prometheus',  # Add this
]

MIDDLEWARE = [
    'django_prometheus.middleware.PrometheusBeforeMiddleware',  # First!
    # ... other middleware
    'django_prometheus.middleware.PrometheusAfterMiddleware',   # Last!
]
anihpj/urls.py 
from django.urls import path, include

urlpatterns = [
    # ... your other URLs
    path('', include('django_prometheus.urls')),  # Exposes /metrics
]
📤 What /metrics Exposes
# HELP django_http_requests_total Total HTTP requests
# TYPE django_http_requests_total counter
django_http_requests_total{method="GET",path="/jobs/",status="200"} 1523

# HELP django_http_request_duration_seconds HTTP request duration
# TYPE django_http_request_duration_seconds histogram
django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.05"} 1200
django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket{le="0.1"} 1400

# HELP django_db_execute_total Total DB queries
# TYPE django_db_execute_total counter
django_db_execute_total{alias="default"} 4521

📊 Essential PromQL Queries for anihpj

What to MeasurePromQL Query
Request rate (req/sec) sum(rate(django_http_requests_total[5m]))
Error rate (%) sum(rate(django_http_responses_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])) / sum(rate(django_http_responses_total[5m])) * 100
p99 latency (seconds) histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))
Pod CPU usage sum(rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="anihpj"}[5m])) by (pod)
Pod memory (MB) sum(container_memory_usage_bytes{namespace="anihpj"}) by (pod) / 1024 / 1024
Pod restarts sum(increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="anihpj"}[1h])) by (pod)

🚦 The RED Method: 3 Key Metrics

📈
Rate

Requests per second

rate(requests_total[5m])
❌
Errors

Failed request percentage

rate(errors[5m]) / rate(total[5m])
⏱️
Duration

Response time (p50, p99)

histogram_quantile(0.99, ...)

💡 Tip: Dashboard these 3 metrics first — they cover 80% of what you need to know about service health.

27.4 Grafana

📊 Grafana: Visualization Powerhouse

Grafana turns Prometheus metrics into beautiful, actionable dashboards. It comes pre-installed with kube-prometheus-stack.

🎨 Popular Pre-built Dashboards

Dashboard IDNameShows
3119Kubernetes ClusterCluster overview, nodes, pods
6417Kubernetes PodsPer-pod CPU, memory, network
6663Kubernetes DeploymentsDeployment health, replicas
1860Node Exporter FullDetailed node metrics
9628PostgreSQLDatabase metrics (with exporter)
🖥️ Import a Dashboard
1. Go to Grafana (http://localhost:3000)
2. Click + → Import
3. Enter Dashboard ID: 3119
4. Select Prometheus data source
5. Click Import

Done! 📊 Instant Kubernetes visibility

📊 Grafana Dashboard Layout (Conceptual)

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  📊 ANIHPJ MONITORING DASHBOARD                          🔄 Last 1h  ▾     │
  ├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐      │
  │  │ 🟢 PODS      │ │ 📈 REQ/SEC   │ │ ⏱️ LATENCY   │ │ ❌ ERROR %   │      │
  │  │     3/3      │ │    156       │ │   45ms       │ │   0.2%       │      │
  │  └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘      │
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  HTTP Request Rate                                                  │   │
  │  │                                                                     │   │
  │  │  200 ┤                    ╭──────────╮                              │   │
  │  │      │               ╭───╯          ╰───╮                           │   │
  │  │  100 ┤          ╭───╯                    ╰──╮                       │   │
  │  │      │     ╭───╯                             ╰──────                │   │
  │  │    0 ┼────╯                                                         │   │
  │  │      └────────────────────────────────────────────────              │   │
  │  │       10:00   10:15   10:30   10:45   11:00   11:15                 │   │
  │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                             │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────────┐         │
  │  │ CPU Usage by Pod            │  │ Memory Usage by Pod          │         │
  │  │   ████████░░░░ anihpj-abc  │  │   ██████████░░ anihpj-abc    │         │
  │  │   ███████░░░░░ anihpj-xyz  │  │   █████████░░░ anihpj-xyz    │         │
  │  │   ███░░░░░░░░░ postgres    │  │   ████████████ postgres      │         │
  │  └─────────────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────────┘         │
  │                                                                             │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

27.5 Alerting

Metrics without alerts mean staring at dashboards 24/7. Configure alerts to notify you when thresholds are breached.

🚨 What to Alert On (anihpj)

🔴
Critical (Page immediately)
  • Pod restart count > 5 in 10m
  • All replicas down
  • Error rate > 10%
  • Response time > 5s (p99)
🟡
Warning (Investigate soon)
  • Error rate > 5%
  • PVC usage > 80%
  • CPU > 90% for 5m
  • Response time > 2s (p95)
🔵
Info (Log/ticket)
  • HPA scaled up
  • Deployment updated
  • Certificate expires < 30d
anihpj-alerts.yaml 
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: PrometheusRule
metadata:
  name: anihpj-alerts
  namespace: monitoring
  labels:
    release: prometheus  # Must match Prometheus Operator selector
spec:
  groups:
    - name: anihpj.rules
      rules:
        # ─── Critical: High error rate ───
        - alert: AnihpjHighErrorRate
          expr: |
            sum(rate(django_http_responses_total{status=~"5.."}[5m]))
            /
            sum(rate(django_http_responses_total[5m])) > 0.10
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: critical
          annotations:
            summary: "High error rate on anihpj"
            description: "Error rate is {{ $value | humanizePercentage }}"

        # ─── Warning: Pod restarts ───
        - alert: AnihpjPodRestarts
          expr: |
            sum(increase(kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total
              {namespace="anihpj"}[1h])) > 5
          for: 0m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: "Pod restarts detected"
            description: "{{ $value }} restarts in the last hour"

        # ─── Warning: High latency ───
        - alert: AnihpjHighLatency
          expr: |
            histogram_quantile(0.95,
              sum(rate(django_http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le)
            ) > 2
          for: 5m
          labels:
            severity: warning
          annotations:
            summary: "High response latency"
            description: "p95 latency is {{ $value }}s"

27.6 Kubernetes Dashboard

The Kubernetes Dashboard is a built-in web UI for cluster management. It's simpler than Grafana but useful for quick overviews.

🖥️ Access Kubernetes Dashboard
# Minikube: Built-in dashboard
minikube dashboard

# Or manually install for other clusters
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/dashboard/v2.7.0/aio/deploy/recommended.yaml

# Create admin user for login
kubectl create serviceaccount dashboard-admin -n kubernetes-dashboard
kubectl create clusterrolebinding dashboard-admin \
  --clusterrole=cluster-admin \
  --serviceaccount=kubernetes-dashboard:dashboard-admin

# Get login token
kubectl create token dashboard-admin -n kubernetes-dashboard

# Port-forward
kubectl port-forward svc/kubernetes-dashboard 8443:443 -n kubernetes-dashboard
# Open https://localhost:8443

📋 Chapter 27 Summary: Monitoring

  • ✅ 3 Layers — Monitor application, K8s objects, AND infrastructure
  • ✅ Metrics Server — Enables kubectl top, required for HPA
  • ✅ Prometheus — Pull-based metrics collection, time-series DB
  • ✅ Grafana — Visualization dashboards, import pre-built with IDs
  • ✅ Alerting — PrometheusRule for automated notifications
  • ✅ django-prometheus — Expose Django metrics at /metrics

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Monitoring

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario: The cluster is running slow. Use monitoring commands to identify resource-hungry workloads.
  1. List CPU and memory usage for all nodes
  2. Find the top 3 pods consuming the most CPU in the default namespace
  3. Find the top 3 pods consuming the most memory across ALL namespaces
  4. Check resource usage for a specific pod named web-app
  5. Verify the metrics-server is running in the cluster
📖 View Solution
Solution — Monitoring Drill 
# 1. Node resource usage
kubectl top nodes

# 2. Top 3 CPU-consuming pods (default namespace)
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu | head -4

# 3. Top 3 memory-consuming pods (all namespaces)
kubectl top pods -A --sort-by=memory | head -4

# 4. Specific pod usage
kubectl top pod web-app

# 5. Verify metrics-server
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep metrics-server
# OR
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system

Chapter 27 — Practice Questions

Q1
What Kubernetes metrics does kubectl top show? What component must be installed?
Show Answer
Answer
# Requires: Metrics Server installed in the cluster
kubectl top nodes
# NAME      CPU(cores)   CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)   MEMORY%
# worker-1  250m         12%    1024Mi          52%

kubectl top pods -n production
# NAME       CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# web-app    50m          128Mi
# db-pod     200m         512Mi

# Sort by CPU
kubectl top pods --sort-by=cpu

# Sort by memory
kubectl top pods --sort-by=memory

# Check if metrics server is running
kubectl get deployment metrics-server -n kube-system
Explanation

kubectl top requires the Metrics Server (not installed by default in all clusters). It shows real-time CPU and memory usage. CPU is in millicores (1000m = 1 core). Memory is in bytes (Mi = mebibytes). On the CKAD, kubectl top pods is how you identify resource-hungry pods and verify resource limits are appropriate.

Q2
What is the difference between Metrics Server and Prometheus? When do you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureMetrics ServerPrometheus
DataCPU/memory onlyAny metric (custom included)
HistoryCurrent snapshot onlyTime-series history
Used byHPA, kubectl topDashboards, alerts
ComplexityLightweight, built-in APIFull monitoring stack
AlertingNoYes (Alertmanager)
Explanation

Metrics Server provides the minimum for HPA and kubectl top. Prometheus is a full monitoring solution with storage, querying (PromQL), alerting, and visualization (Grafana). On the CKAD, you'll mainly use kubectl top (Metrics Server). Prometheus is more relevant for CKA/production.

Q3
How do readiness and liveness probes relate to monitoring? What metrics do they generate?
Show Answer
Answer
# Probe failures appear as Kubernetes Events:
kubectl describe pod my-app | grep -A5 Events
# Events:
#   Warning  Unhealthy  Liveness probe failed: HTTP 503
#   Normal   Killing    Container will be restarted

# Probe-related monitoring queries:
# - Container restarts (liveness failures):
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name} {.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount}{"\n"}{end}'

# - Not-ready pods (readiness failures):
kubectl get pods --field-selector status.phase!=Running
Explanation

Liveness probe failures cause container restarts (visible via restart count). Readiness probe failures remove pods from Service endpoints (pod stays Running but not Ready). Both generate Events visible in kubectl describe. On the CKAD, high restart counts indicate liveness probe issues, while "0/1 Ready" indicates readiness probe failures.

Q4
How do you check resource usage per container in a multi-container pod?
Show Answer
Answer
# Show per-container metrics
kubectl top pod my-pod --containers
# POD      NAME       CPU(cores)   MEMORY(bytes)
# my-pod   app        45m          128Mi
# my-pod   sidecar    10m          32Mi
# my-pod   init       0m           0Mi

# Compare with resource limits
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].resources}'

# Detect memory pressure (close to OOM)
# If MEMORY usage approaches the limit → container will be OOM killed
Explanation

--containers flag breaks down resource usage per container within a pod. This is essential for multi-container pods (sidecar pattern). Compare actual usage against resources.limits to identify containers at risk of OOM kill. On the CKAD, kubectl top pod --containers is the go-to command for diagnosing resource issues in multi-container pods.

Q5
What happens when a container exceeds its memory limit? What about CPU limit?
Show Answer
Answer
ResourceExceeds LimitResult
MemoryUses more than limits.memoryOOM Killed — container terminated, pod may restart
CPUTries to use more than limits.cpuThrottled — slowed down, NOT killed
 
# Detect OOM kills:
kubectl describe pod my-pod | grep -i oom
# Last State: Terminated
#   Reason: OOMKilled
#   Exit Code: 137

# Exit code 137 = killed by SIGKILL (OOM)
# Exit code 143 = killed by SIGTERM (graceful)
Explanation

Memory limits are HARD — exceeding them kills the container immediately (OOMKilled, exit code 137). CPU limits are SOFT — the container is throttled (slowed) but never killed. On the CKAD, knowing that memory → kill and CPU → throttle is essential. Exit code 137 always means OOM kill.

Q6
How do you monitor node resource usage and identify capacity issues?
Show Answer
Answer
# Node-level metrics
kubectl top nodes
# NAME      CPU(cores)  CPU%   MEMORY(bytes)  MEMORY%
# worker-1  500m        25%    2048Mi         64%

# Detailed node capacity and allocations
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A10 "Allocated resources"
# Allocated resources:
#   Resource    Requests     Limits
#   cpu         850m (42%)   2000m (100%)
#   memory      1280Mi (40%) 3072Mi (96%)  ← Near limit!

# Check node conditions
kubectl get nodes
kubectl describe node worker-1 | grep -A5 Conditions
# MemoryPressure: False
# DiskPressure: False
# PIDPressure: False
Explanation

kubectl top nodes shows actual usage. kubectl describe node shows allocated requests/limits vs capacity. "Allocated" is based on pod requests — actual usage may differ. Watch for high percentage in either. On the CKAD, node conditions (MemoryPressure, DiskPressure) trigger evictions. Check node capacity when pods are stuck in Pending.

Q7
How does Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) use metrics? What metric sources are available?
Show Answer
Answer
# HPA targets CPU utilization by default
kubectl autoscale deployment web \
  --min=2 --max=10 \
  --cpu-percent=70

# Check HPA status
kubectl get hpa
# NAME  REFERENCE    TARGETS   MINPODS  MAXPODS  REPLICAS
# web   Deployment   45%/70%   2        10       3

# Metric sources:
# 1. Resource metrics (CPU/memory) — Metrics Server
# 2. Custom metrics — Prometheus Adapter
# 3. External metrics — Cloud provider metrics
Explanation

HPA relies on Metrics Server for CPU/memory metrics. It periodically checks current usage vs target and adjusts replicas. If TARGETS shows <unknown>/70%, Metrics Server isn't working or resource requests aren't set. On the CKAD, HPA needs resources.requests set on containers to calculate percentage utilization.

Q8
How do you view Events in a namespace and filter them?
Show Answer
Answer
# All events in current namespace (sorted by time)
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Warning events only
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning

# Events for a specific resource
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=my-pod

# Watch events in real-time
kubectl get events -w

# Events across all namespaces
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Recent events (default retention: 1 hour)
kubectl get events --sort-by='.firstTimestamp'
Explanation

Events are short-lived (default TTL: 1 hour) but invaluable for debugging. They show scheduling decisions, image pulls, probe failures, OOM kills, and volume mounts. --field-selector filters by any event field. On the CKAD, kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' is often the fastest way to understand cluster issues.

Q9
What is the Prometheus Node Exporter and how is it typically deployed?
Show Answer
Answer
# Node Exporter is deployed as a DaemonSet
# (one pod per node to collect node-level metrics)

# Metrics it collects:
# - CPU usage per core
# - Memory usage/available
# - Disk I/O and space
# - Network traffic
# - System load

# Verify it's running on all nodes:
kubectl get ds node-exporter -n monitoring
# DESIRED   CURRENT   READY
# 3         3         3

# Metrics endpoint on each node:
curl http://node-ip:9100/metrics
Explanation

Node Exporter is a Prometheus component deployed as a DaemonSet (one per node) to collect hardware and OS metrics. It exposes metrics on port 9100 that Prometheus scrapes. On the CKAD, this is a classic DaemonSet use case. Prometheus scrapes → stores → Grafana visualizes. The DaemonSet ensures every node is monitored.

Q10
A pod's CPU usage is at 95%. How do you identify and resolve the issue?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Confirm CPU usage
kubectl top pod high-cpu-pod --containers

# Step 2: Check resource limits
kubectl get pod high-cpu-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# requests: 100m, limits: 200m → at 95% of 200m = 190m

# Step 3: Check if it's throttled
kubectl describe pod high-cpu-pod | grep -i throttl

# Resolution options:
# a) Increase CPU limit
kubectl patch deployment my-app -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"app","resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"500m"}}}]}}}}'

# b) Scale horizontally
kubectl scale deployment my-app --replicas=4

# c) Enable HPA
kubectl autoscale deployment my-app --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=8
Explanation

High CPU → check if it's a limit issue (throttling) or actual overload. Resolution: increase limits (vertical), add replicas (horizontal), or enable HPA (automatic). Remember: CPU exceeding limits causes throttling, not killing. On the CKAD, diagnose with kubectl top, then decide between vertical scaling (more resources) or horizontal scaling (more pods).

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Runtime Threat Detection & Behavioral Monitoring

MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

Runtime security goes beyond static analysis — it monitors container behavior at execution time, detecting anomalies like unexpected processes, file access, or network connections.

ToolPurposeDetection Method
FalcoRuntime anomaly detectionSyscall monitoring via eBPF/kernel module
SysdigContainer forensics & visibilitySystem call capture & analysis
TraceeeBPF-based security tracingEvent tracing from kernel
TetragoneBPF runtime enforcementKernel-level policy enforcement
 
# Falco rule — detect shell spawned in container
- rule: Terminal shell in container
  desc: Detect shell process started in a container
  condition: >
    spawned_process and container and
    proc.name in (bash, sh, zsh, dash) and
    not proc.pname in (cron, sshd)
  output: >
    Shell spawned in container
    (user=%user.name container=%container.name
    shell=%proc.name parent=%proc.pname
    image=%container.image.repository)
  priority: WARNING
  tags: [container, shell, mitre_execution]

# Falco rule — detect sensitive file access
- rule: Read sensitive file untrusted
  desc: Detect read of sensitive files
  condition: >
    open_read and container and
    (fd.name startswith /etc/shadow or
     fd.name startswith /etc/passwd or
     fd.name startswith /run/secrets)
  output: >
    Sensitive file read (user=%user.name
    file=%fd.name container=%container.name)
  priority: WARNING

# Falco rule — detect network tool usage
- rule: Launch suspicious network tool
  desc: Detect network scanning/recon tools
  condition: >
    spawned_process and container and
    proc.name in (nmap, ncat, nc, netcat, curl, wget)
  output: >
    Network tool launched (user=%user.name
    command=%proc.cmdline container=%container.name)
  priority: NOTICE

Chapter 27 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Write a Falco rule that detects when a process tries to modify files under /etc inside a container.
Show Answer
Answer
- rule: Write to /etc in container
  desc: Detect writes to /etc directory in running containers
  condition: >
    open_write and container and
    fd.name startswith /etc/ and
    not proc.name in (apt, dpkg, yum, rpm)
  output: >
    File under /etc modified in container
    (user=%user.name file=%fd.name
    process=%proc.name command=%proc.cmdline
    container=%container.name
    image=%container.image.repository
    namespace=%k8s.ns.name pod=%k8s.pod.name)
  priority: ERROR
  tags: [filesystem, mitre_persistence]
Explanation

Containers should be immutable — writes to /etc indicate potential config tampering. On CKS, know Falco rule syntax: condition uses Sysdig filter syntax, output uses %field placeholders, and priority sets severity (EMERGENCY → DEBUG). Exclude known package managers to reduce false positives.

CKS-2
How do you install Falco as a DaemonSet and view its alerts?
Show Answer
Answer
# Install Falco via Helm
helm repo add falcosecurity https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm install falco falcosecurity/falco \
  --namespace falco --create-namespace \
  --set driver.kind=ebpf \
  --set falcosidekick.enabled=true

# View Falco alerts from DaemonSet logs
kubectl logs -n falco -l app.kubernetes.io/name=falco -f

# Check Falco is running on all nodes
kubectl get pods -n falco -o wide

# View custom rules loaded
kubectl get configmap -n falco falco-rules -o yaml

# Test: trigger a Falco alert
kubectl exec -it test-pod -- /bin/bash
# This should generate "Terminal shell in container" alert
Explanation

Falco runs as a DaemonSet to monitor syscalls on every node. On CKS, know that Falco can use either a kernel module or eBPF driver. eBPF is preferred (no kernel module compilation). Falcosidekick forwards alerts to Slack, PagerDuty, or other backends. Check logs with kubectl logs for real-time alerts.

CKS-3
Use sysdig or crictl to investigate a suspicious container.
Show Answer
Answer
# Use crictl to inspect running containers
crictl ps | grep suspicious-pod
crictl inspect <container-id> | jq '.info.config.process'

# Check what processes are running
crictl exec <container-id> ps aux

# Use sysdig to trace container activity
sysdig -p "%evt.time %proc.name %evt.type %fd.name" \
  container.name=suspicious-container

# Filter for network connections
sysdig -p "%proc.name %fd.sip %fd.sport -> %fd.cip %fd.cport" \
  container.name=suspicious-container and evt.type=connect

# Filter for file writes
sysdig -p "%proc.name %fd.name" \
  container.name=suspicious-container and evt.type=write \
  and fd.name contains /etc
Explanation

On CKS, you may need to investigate a compromised container. Use crictl (CRI-compatible container tool) to inspect containers on the node. Use sysdig to trace system calls in real-time. Know the filter syntax: container.name, proc.name, fd.name, evt.type.

CKS-4
How do you use seccomp profiles for runtime syscall restriction?
Show Answer
Answer
# Pod with seccomp profile
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: seccomp-pod
spec:
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault    # Use container runtime's default profile
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      seccompProfile:
        type: Localhost
        localhostProfile: profiles/strict.json  # Custom profile
// /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/profiles/strict.json
{
  "defaultAction": "SCMP_ACT_ERRNO",
  "architectures": ["SCMP_ARCH_X86_64"],
  "syscalls": [
    {
      "names": ["read", "write", "open", "close",
                "stat", "fstat", "mmap", "exit_group"],
      "action": "SCMP_ACT_ALLOW"
    }
  ]
}
Explanation

Seccomp restricts which syscalls a container can make — it's a kernel-level defense. On CKS, know three profile types: Unconfined (no restriction), RuntimeDefault (container runtime's default — recommended baseline), and Localhost (custom profile at /var/lib/kubelet/seccomp/). PSA restricted profile requires seccomp.

CKS-5
How do you use AppArmor to restrict container behavior?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check AppArmor profiles loaded on node
ssh node01
aa-status

# Load a custom AppArmor profile
cat /etc/apparmor.d/k8s-deny-write
#include <tunables/global>
profile k8s-deny-write flags=(attach_disconnected) {
  #include <abstractions/base>
  file,
  deny /etc/** w,
  deny /proc/** w,
  deny /sys/** w,
}

# Load the profile
apparmor_parser -q /etc/apparmor.d/k8s-deny-write
# Pod with AppArmor profile (Kubernetes v1.30+)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: apparmor-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      appArmorProfile:
        type: Localhost
        localhostProfile: k8s-deny-write
Explanation

AppArmor provides Mandatory Access Control (MAC) restricting file/network/capability access. On CKS, profiles must be loaded on the node before use. Since Kubernetes v1.30, AppArmor uses securityContext.appArmorProfile (before that, annotations). Know how to check loaded profiles with aa-status and load new ones with apparmor_parser.

Chapter 28: Debugging and Troubleshooting

PART 9 INTERMEDIATE 🔍 DEBUGGING

Things will break. Pods will crash. Services will be unreachable. This chapter teaches you the systematic approach to debugging Kubernetes issues — from quick checks to deep investigation.

🔍 CKAD Domain 3: Application Observability & Maintenance 15% of Exam

Debugging is what the CKAD exam IS. Multiple questions will give you a broken application and ask you to fix it. Master the 5-step flow: Status → Events → Describe → Logs → Exec.

🎓 CKAD EXAM TIP: Debugging Workflow

When something is broken on the exam, follow this exact order:

  1. kubectl get pods -n NAMESPACE — what's the STATUS?
  2. kubectl describe pod POD -n NAMESPACE — check Events section (bottom)
  3. kubectl logs POD -n NAMESPACE — application errors
  4. kubectl logs POD --previous — if pod crashed and restarted
  5. kubectl exec -it POD -- sh — interactive debugging inside container
  6. kubectl get events -n NAMESPACE --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' — timeline of events

⚡ SPEED TIP: Common Error Patterns

# CrashLoopBackOff → check logs --previous
# ImagePullBackOff → check image name/tag spelling
# Pending → check resources (kubectl describe pod, look for Events)
# Running but not working → check service selectors match pod labels
# OOMKilled → increase memory limits
# CreateContainerConfigError → missing ConfigMap/Secret

Debugging Commands — Complete Toolkit

Debug ScenarioCommand
Pod status overviewkubectl get pods -o wide
Detailed pod infokubectl describe pod my-pod
Recent eventskubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
Warning events onlykubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning
Shell into podkubectl exec -it my-pod -- /bin/sh
Shell specific containerkubectl exec -it my-pod -c sidecar -- /bin/sh
Ephemeral debug containerkubectl debug my-pod -it --image=busybox -- /bin/sh
Debug nodekubectl debug node/node01 -it --image=ubuntu
Copy pod for debugkubectl debug my-pod -it --copy-to=debug-pod --container=debug -- /bin/sh
Test DNSkubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- nslookup my-svc
Test connectivitykubectl run tmp --image=busybox --rm -it -- wget -qO- my-svc:80
Port-forwardkubectl port-forward pod/my-pod 8080:80
Check YAML for errorskubectl apply -f pod.yaml --dry-run=server

Common Error → Fix Quick Reference

Memorize these patterns. They appear on every exam.

# CrashLoopBackOff
└── Check: kubectl logs pod --previous
└── Common: wrong command, missing env var, bad config
└── Fix: correct command/args, add missing ConfigMap/Secret

# ImagePullBackOff
└── Check: kubectl describe pod (look at Events)
└── Common: typo in image name, wrong tag, private registry
└── Fix: correct image, add imagePullSecrets

# Pending
└── Check: kubectl describe pod (Events: FailedScheduling)
└── Common: insufficient CPU/memory, no matching nodes, PVC pending
└── Fix: reduce requests, add nodes, fix PVC/StorageClass

# CreateContainerConfigError
└── Check: kubectl describe pod
└── Common: referenced ConfigMap/Secret doesn't exist
└── Fix: create missing ConfigMap/Secret

# Running but not accessible
└── Check: kubectl get endpoints svc-name
└── Common: selector doesn't match pod labels, wrong port
└── Fix: match Service selector to pod labels, fix port/targetPort

# OOMKilled (exit code 137)
└── Check: kubectl describe pod (Last State: OOMKilled)
└── Fix: increase memory limit, fix memory leak in app

# Readiness probe failing (pod Running but 0/1 READY)
└── Check: kubectl describe pod (Readiness probe failed)
└── Fix: correct probe path/port, increase initialDelaySeconds

Debugging Verification Flow

  • Step 1: kubectl get pods -o wide → Is the STATUS correct? Which node?
  • Step 2: kubectl describe pod X → Read Events section (bottom) for scheduling/pulling errors
  • Step 3: kubectl logs X (or --previous) → Application-level errors
  • Step 4: kubectl get ep svc-name → Service endpoints populated?
  • Step 5: kubectl exec -it X -- sh → Debug from inside (env vars, DNS, filesystem)
  • Step 6: kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' → Timeline of what happened

🔍 Systematic Debugging Workflow

1
Get Status

kubectl get pods -o wide

2
Describe

kubectl describe pod X (Events!)

3
Logs

kubectl logs X (--previous)

4
Endpoints

kubectl get ep svc-name

5
Exec In

kubectl exec -it X -- sh

6
Events

kubectl get events --sort-by

📊 Pod Status Quick Reference

⏳ Pending Scheduling or image pull issue
🟢 Running Container is executing
🔄 CrashLoopBackOff Container crashing repeatedly
❌ ImagePullBackOff Can't pull container image
🟡 CreateContainerConfigError Missing ConfigMap or Secret
🔴 OOMKilled Memory limit exceeded

28.1 The Debugging Mindset

🧘 Rule #1: Don't Panic

Kubernetes errors look scary but are usually fixable. Follow a systematic approach: Status → Events → Describe → Logs → Exec

🔍 Debugging Decision Tree

                              🚨 Something's wrong!
                                      │
                                      ▼
                        ┌─────────────────────────┐
                        │   kubectl get pods       │
                        └────────────┬────────────┘
                                     │
         ┌───────────┬───────────┬───┴───┬───────────┬───────────┐
         ▼           ▼           ▼       ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │ Pending │ │CrashLoop│ │ImagePull│ │ Running │ │  No     │ │Terminat-│
    │         │ │BackOff  │ │BackOff  │ │ but bad │ │  pods   │ │  ing    │
    └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘
         │           │           │           │           │           │
         ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │describe │ │  logs   │ │describe │ │  logs   │ │  check  │ │  wait   │
    │  pod    │ │--previous│ │  pod    │ │ + exec │ │ deploy  │ │ or force│
    │         │ │         │ │         │ │         │ │         │ │ delete  │
    └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬────┘
         │           │           │           │           │           │
         ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼           ▼
    ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐
    │ Check   │ │ Fix app │ │ Fix     │ │ Check   │ │ Check   │ │--force  │
    │ node    │ │ crash   │ │ image   │ │ service │ │ events  │ │--grace- │
    │resources│ │ issue   │ │ name    │ │selector │ │         │ │period=0 │
    └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘

🔢 The 5-Step Debugging Flow

1️⃣
STATUS
kubectl get pods
2️⃣
EVENTS
kubectl get events
3️⃣
DESCRIBE
kubectl describe
4️⃣
LOGS
kubectl logs
5️⃣
EXEC
kubectl exec

28.2 Essential Debugging Commands

🛠️ Debugging Command Reference

CommandWhen to UseWhat it Shows
kubectl get pods First check Pod status, restarts, age
kubectl get pods -o wide Node issues IP, node assignment
kubectl describe pod <name> Scheduling/startup issues Events, conditions, volumes
kubectl logs <pod> Application errors Container stdout/stderr
kubectl logs <pod> -p CrashLoopBackOff Previous container logs
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- sh Runtime investigation Shell access to container
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp Recent cluster activity Scheduling, errors, warnings
kubectl top pods Resource issues CPU/memory usage
kubectl port-forward <pod> 8000:8000 Connection testing Direct access to pod
kubectl debug <pod> -it --image=busybox Distroless containers Debug sidecar shell
🖥️ Complete Debugging Sequence
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 1: CHECK POD STATUS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -n anihpj
kubectl get pods -n anihpj -o wide  # See node, IP

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 2: CHECK RECENT EVENTS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get events -n anihpj --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -20

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 3: DESCRIBE THE PROBLEM POD
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n anihpj
# Look for: Events section, Conditions, State/Reason

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 4: CHECK LOGS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n anihpj --previous  # If crashed

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  STEP 5: EXEC INTO CONTAINER (if running)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n anihpj -- /bin/sh
# Check: env vars, file paths, network connectivity

28.3 Pod States — What They Mean

📊 Pod Status Quick Reference

StateMeaningCommon CausesFix
✅ Running All containers running — None needed
⏳ Pending Waiting to be scheduled No node capacity, nodeSelector mismatch, PVC pending Check resources, node labels, PVC
🔄 ContainerCreating Image pulling or volume mounting Large image, slow registry, volume issues Wait, or check describe events
💀 CrashLoopBackOff Container keeps crashing App error, missing config, wrong command kubectl logs --previous
❌ ImagePullBackOff Can't pull image Wrong name, no auth, registry down Check image name, imagePullSecret
⚠️ ErrImagePull Image pull failed Network issue, image doesn't exist Verify image exists, check network
🔒 Init:0/1 Init container running Init container taking long/stuck kubectl logs -c init-container
🏁 Completed Pod finished (normal for Jobs) Job completed successfully Check Job status
⏸️ Terminating Pod shutting down Being deleted, stuck on finalizers Wait, or force delete
❓ Unknown Node lost contact Node crashed, network partition Check node health

🔄 Pod Lifecycle States

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        POD LIFECYCLE                                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

        Create                                                Delete
          │                                                      │
          ▼                                                      │
     ┌─────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌─────────┐         │
     │ Pending │─────▶│ContainerCreate│─────▶│ Running │─────────┤
     └────┬────┘      └──────────────┘      └────┬────┘         │
          │                                      │               │
          │ (scheduling fails)                   │ (crash)       │
          ▼                                      ▼               ▼
    ┌───────────┐                         ┌───────────────┐ ┌───────────┐
    │ Unschedul-│                         │ CrashLoopBack │ │Terminating│
    │   able    │                         │     Off       │ └─────┬─────┘
    └───────────┘                         └───────┬───────┘       │
                                                  │               │
                                            (restart)             ▼
                                                  │          ┌─────────┐
                                                  └─────────▶│ Deleted │
                                                             └─────────┘

28.4 Scenario-Based Debugging for anihpj

Real-world debugging scenarios you'll encounter with anihpj:

🎯 Common Debugging Scenarios

💀 Scenario 1: CrashLoopBackOff

Problem: anihpj pod keeps restarting

# Investigate
kubectl get pods -n anihpj
# NAME                        READY   STATUS             RESTARTS
# anihpj-xyz                  0/1     CrashLoopBackOff   5

kubectl logs anihpj-xyz --previous
# ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'psycopg2'

# Fix: Add missing dependency to requirements.txt, rebuild image
🖼️ Scenario 2: ImagePullBackOff

Problem: Pod can't pull container image

# Investigate
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz
# Events:
#   Failed to pull image "myregistry/anihpj:latest": 
#   Error: repository not found

# Fix options:
# 1. Correct image name in deployment
# 2. Add imagePullSecret for private registry
# 3. Push image to registry
⏳ Scenario 3: Pod Stuck in Pending

Problem: Pod won't start

# Investigate
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz
# Events:
#   0/1 nodes are available: 1 Insufficient memory.

# Fix: Reduce resource.requests or add more nodes
# Quick fix for testing:
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"anihpj","resources":{"requests":{"memory":"128Mi"}}}]}}}}'
🌐 Scenario 4: Service Not Reachable

Problem: Can't connect to service

# Check if service has endpoints
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-service -n anihpj
# NAME              ENDPOINTS
# anihpj-service    <none>   # ❌ No endpoints!

# Check label selector
kubectl describe svc anihpj-service
# Selector: app=anihpjj  # Typo!

kubectl get pods --show-labels
# app=anihpj  # Correct label

# Fix: Update service selector to match pod labels
🗄️ Scenario 5: Database Connection Failed

Problem: Django can't connect to PostgreSQL

# Check logs
kubectl logs anihpj-xyz
# django.db.utils.OperationalError: could not connect to server

# Debug: Exec into pod and test connection
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xyz -- sh
# Inside pod:
nc -zv postgres-service 5432
# Connection refused? Check postgres pod

# Verify env vars
env | grep DATABASE
# DATABASE_HOST=postgres-service  ✓
# DATABASE_PASSWORD=   # ❌ Empty! Check secret
💥 Scenario 6: OOMKilled (Out of Memory)

Problem: Container keeps getting killed

# Check pod status
kubectl get pods
# NAME                        READY   STATUS      RESTARTS
# anihpj-xyz                  0/1     OOMKilled   3

# Describe to see Last State
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz
# Last State:     Terminated
#   Reason:       OOMKilled
#   Exit Code:    137

# Check current memory limits
kubectl get pod anihpj-xyz -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# {"limits":{"memory":"128Mi"},"requests":{"memory":"64Mi"}}

# Fix: Increase memory limit
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"anihpj","resources":{"limits":{"memory":"512Mi"},"requests":{"memory":"256Mi"}}}]}}}}'
💡 Exit Code 137 = OOMKilled

Exit code 137 means the container was killed by SIGKILL (128 + 9). This is almost always an OOM situation. Increase your memory limits or profile your app for leaks.

🌐 Scenario 7: DNS Resolution Failed

Problem: Can't resolve service names inside pod

# Exec into pod and test DNS
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xyz -- sh

# Inside pod:
nslookup postgres-service
# Server:    10.96.0.10
# Name:      postgres-service.anihpj.svc.cluster.local

# If DNS fails, try:
nslookup kubernetes.default
# Should work - if not, CoreDNS is broken

# Check CoreDNS pods
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
# Make sure they're running!

# Check your /etc/resolv.conf inside pod
cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search anihpj.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local

🔌 Network Debugging Quick Reference

IssueCheck ThisCommand
Can't reach service Service has endpoints? kubectl get endpoints <svc>
DNS not resolving CoreDNS running? kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
Pod can't egress NetworkPolicy blocking? kubectl get networkpolicy
Pod-to-pod fails CNI plugin healthy? kubectl get pods -n kube-system
External unreachable Ingress configured? kubectl get ingress,svc

28.5 Debug Containers (kubectl debug)

🔧 When kubectl exec Won't Work

Distroless images have no shell. Crashed pods have no running container. Use kubectl debug to attach a debug container.

🖥️ kubectl debug Commands
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  DEBUG A RUNNING POD (add sidecar)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Attach a debug container to running pod
kubectl debug anihpj-xyz -it --image=busybox -- sh

# With network tools
kubectl debug anihpj-xyz -it --image=nicolaka/netshoot -- bash

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  DEBUG A CRASHED POD (copy with modified command)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Create a copy that runs shell instead of crashing app
kubectl debug anihpj-xyz -it --copy-to=anihpj-debug \
  --container=anihpj -- sh

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  DEBUG NODE ISSUES
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Get shell on node (privileged)
kubectl debug node/minikube -it --image=busybox

🐳 Useful Debug Images

ImageTools IncludedBest For
busybox sh, wget, nc, vi Basic debugging (tiny: 1MB)
alpine sh, apk (install more) Flexible (5MB)
nicolaka/netshoot curl, dig, netstat, tcpdump, iperf Network debugging
curlimages/curl curl only HTTP testing

28.6 Events — Cluster Activity Timeline

Events are Kubernetes' journal — they record what happened and when.

🖥️ Viewing Events
# All events (noisy)
kubectl get events

# Recent events, sorted by time
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Events in specific namespace
kubectl get events -n anihpj --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

# Watch live events
kubectl get events -w

# Events for a specific pod (from describe)
kubectl describe pod anihpj-xyz | grep -A 20 "Events:"

# Filter warnings only
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning
📤 Example Events Output
LAST SEEN   TYPE      REASON              OBJECT                      MESSAGE
3m          Normal    Scheduled           pod/anihpj-xyz              Successfully assigned...
3m          Normal    Pulling             pod/anihpj-xyz              Pulling image "anihpj:v2"
2m          Normal    Pulled              pod/anihpj-xyz              Successfully pulled image
2m          Normal    Created             pod/anihpj-xyz              Created container anihpj
2m          Normal    Started             pod/anihpj-xyz              Started container anihpj
30s         Warning   BackOff             pod/anihpj-xyz              Back-off restarting failed

📋 Chapter 28 Summary: Debugging and Troubleshooting

  • ✅ 5-Step Flow — Status → Events → Describe → Logs → Exec
  • ✅ Pod States — Know what Pending, CrashLoopBackOff, ImagePullBackOff mean
  • ✅ kubectl describe — Deep dive into pod events and conditions
  • ✅ kubectl logs --previous — Essential for crashed containers
  • ✅ kubectl debug — Debug distroless/crashed containers
  • ✅ kubectl get events — Cluster activity timeline
🖥️ Part 9 Debugging Cheat Sheet
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
#  QUICK DEBUGGING COMMANDS
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -o wide                                    # Status + node
kubectl describe pod <name>                                 # Events + details
kubectl logs <pod> --previous                               # Crashed logs
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container>                            # Multi-container
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- sh                                # Shell access
kubectl debug <pod> -it --image=busybox -- sh               # Debug container
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'               # Recent events
kubectl port-forward <pod> 8000:8000                        # Test connectivity
kubectl top pods                                            # Resource usage

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Debugging

⏱️ Target Time: 8 minutes
Scenario: Four pods are broken in different ways. Diagnose each failure and apply the fix.
  1. Create a pod crash-pod that will CrashLoopBackOff (image: busybox, command: exit 1). Identify the failure using describe and logs.
  2. Create a pod bad-image with a non-existent image nginx:doesnotexist. Identify the ImagePullBackOff error and fix it by changing the image to nginx:latest.
  3. Create a pod pending-pod that requests cpu: "100" (impossible). Confirm it stays Pending and identify why from events.
  4. Create a working pod debug-target (nginx). Use kubectl debug to attach an ephemeral container and verify nginx is serving on port 80.
  5. List all events in the namespace sorted by timestamp, and filter only Warning events.
📖 View Solution
Solution — Debugging Drill 
# 1. CrashLoopBackOff diagnosis
kubectl run crash-pod --image=busybox -- sh -c "exit 1"
# Wait ~30 seconds then:
kubectl get pod crash-pod          # STATUS: CrashLoopBackOff
kubectl describe pod crash-pod     # Events show Back-off restarting
kubectl logs crash-pod --previous  # Shows nothing (exit 1 = no output)

# 2. ImagePullBackOff and fix
kubectl run bad-image --image=nginx:doesnotexist
kubectl get pod bad-image          # STATUS: ImagePullBackOff
kubectl describe pod bad-image     # Events: Failed to pull image
kubectl set image pod/bad-image bad-image=nginx:latest
kubectl get pod bad-image -w       # Watch it become Running

# 3. Pending pod (insufficient resources)
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: pending-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "100"
EOF
kubectl get pod pending-pod        # STATUS: Pending
kubectl describe pod pending-pod   # Events: FailedScheduling — Insufficient cpu

# 4. Debug with ephemeral container
kubectl run debug-target --image=nginx
kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod/debug-target --timeout=30s
kubectl debug debug-target -it --image=busybox -- wget -qO- localhost:80
# Should return nginx welcome page HTML

# 5. Events sorted + filtered
kubectl get events --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl get events --field-selector type=Warning --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'

Chapter 28 — Practice Questions

Q1
A pod is stuck in Pending state. What are the possible causes and how do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check pod events
kubectl describe pod pending-pod | grep -A20 Events
# Look for: FailedScheduling, Insufficient cpu/memory

# Step 2: Check node resources
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 "Allocated resources"

# Step 3: Check PVC status (if using volumes)
kubectl get pvc
CauseEvent MessageFix
Insufficient resourcesInsufficient cpu/memoryAdd nodes or reduce requests
No matching nodesdidn't match Pod's node selectorFix nodeSelector or label nodes
PVC not boundpod has unbound PVCCreate PV or fix StorageClass
Taint not toleratednode had taintAdd toleration or remove taint
Explanation

Pending = scheduler can't place the pod. Always start with kubectl describe pod and read the Events section at the bottom. The event message tells you exactly why. On the CKAD, Pending pods are one of the most common debugging scenarios — the answer is almost always in the Events.

Q2
A pod is in ImagePullBackOff. List the possible causes and debugging steps.
Show Answer
Answer
# Check events
kubectl describe pod backoff-pod | grep -A5 Events
# Failed to pull image: rpc error: ...

# Common causes:
# 1. Wrong image name/tag
kubectl get pod backoff-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].image}'

# 2. Private registry without imagePullSecrets
kubectl get pod backoff-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.imagePullSecrets}'

# 3. Image doesn't exist
docker pull nginx:nonexistent  # Test locally

# 4. Registry is unreachable
kubectl exec debug-pod -- nslookup registry.example.com

# Fixes:
kubectl set image deployment/app app=nginx:1.25  # Fix image
# OR add imagePullSecrets
Explanation

ImagePullBackOff means kubelet can't pull the container image. The most common CKAD causes: (1) typo in image name, (2) wrong tag, (3) missing imagePullSecrets for private registries. Always check the exact image name with -o jsonpath and read the event message for the specific error.

Q3
How do you run a temporary debug pod to troubleshoot networking?
Show Answer
Answer
# Quick debug pod with common tools
kubectl run debug --image=busybox --rm -it -- sh

# Inside the debug pod:
nslookup my-service                    # DNS resolution
wget -qO- http://my-service:8080       # HTTP test
nc -zv my-service 8080                 # TCP connectivity

# Debug pod in specific namespace
kubectl run debug --image=nicolaka/netshoot \
  --rm -it -n production -- bash

# Debug with same network as existing pod
kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=busybox \
  --target=my-container

# Ephemeral debug container (K8s 1.23+)
kubectl debug my-pod -it --image=busybox --share-processes
Explanation

kubectl run --rm -it creates a temporary pod that's auto-deleted when you exit. kubectl debug attaches to an existing pod (useful for distroless images that don't have a shell). On the CKAD, busybox is the go-to debug image (has nslookup, wget, nc). Use nicolaka/netshoot for more advanced tools.

Q4
A Service is not routing traffic to pods. How do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Check Service selector matches pod labels
kubectl get svc my-svc -o jsonpath='{.spec.selector}'
# {"app":"web"}
kubectl get pods -l app=web
# Should list matching pods

# Step 2: Check endpoints (pods backing the Service)
kubectl get endpoints my-svc
# If empty → selector doesn't match any Running pods!

# Step 3: Verify pods are Ready
kubectl get pods -l app=web
# STATUS should be Running, READY should be 1/1

# Step 4: Check port mapping
kubectl get svc my-svc -o yaml | grep -A5 ports
# port: 80        ← Service port
# targetPort: 8080 ← Container port (must match!)

# Step 5: Test connectivity
kubectl exec debug -- wget -qO- http://my-svc:80
Explanation

Debugging flow: selector → endpoints → pod readiness → ports. Empty endpoints means the Service can't find matching pods. Common issues: label mismatch, pods not Ready (readiness probe failing), or wrong targetPort. On the CKAD, kubectl get endpoints svc-name is the fastest way to check if a Service is connected to pods.

Q5
How do you use kubectl exec to troubleshoot a running container?
Show Answer
Answer
# Interactive shell
kubectl exec -it my-pod -- /bin/sh
kubectl exec -it my-pod -- /bin/bash

# Run a single command
kubectl exec my-pod -- env                    # Check env vars
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat /etc/config/app.conf  # Check mounted config
kubectl exec my-pod -- ls /var/run/secrets/   # Check SA token
kubectl exec my-pod -- printenv DB_PASSWORD   # Check specific env var

# Specific container in multi-container pod
kubectl exec -it my-pod -c sidecar -- sh

# Check network from inside the pod
kubectl exec my-pod -- wget -qO- http://other-service:8080
kubectl exec my-pod -- nslookup kubernetes.default
Explanation

kubectl exec is the Swiss Army knife for debugging running containers. Check env vars, mounted files, network connectivity, and process state. Use -c for multi-container pods. On the CKAD, common exec patterns: verify env vars from Secrets/ConfigMaps, test DNS resolution, check mounted files exist.

Q6
What is the difference between kubectl get, kubectl describe, and kubectl logs for debugging?
Show Answer
Answer
CommandShowsUse When
kubectl getResource status overviewQuick overview: Running? Ready? Restarts?
kubectl describeFull details + EventsWHY something failed (scheduling, probes, resources)
kubectl logsApplication stdout/stderrApplication-level errors, stack traces
 
# Debugging workflow:
kubectl get pods                     # 1. What's the status?
kubectl describe pod failing-pod     # 2. Why is it failing?
kubectl logs failing-pod --previous  # 3. What did the app say?
Explanation

The three-step debugging flow: get → describe → logs. get shows you WHAT (current state). describe shows you WHY (events, conditions). logs shows you WHAT THE APP SAYS. On the CKAD, this flow handles 90% of debugging scenarios. Always start with get to identify the problem pod, then drill down.

Q7
A container keeps restarting (CrashLoopBackOff). The logs show nothing. How do you debug?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check the previous container's logs
kubectl logs crashing-pod --previous

# Check exit code
kubectl describe pod crashing-pod | grep -A3 "Last State"
# Exit Code: 1   → application error
# Exit Code: 137 → OOM killed (SIGKILL)
# Exit Code: 143 → graceful shutdown (SIGTERM)

# If logs are empty, the app might crash before logging:
# 1. Check if the command/entrypoint is correct
kubectl get pod crashing-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].command}'

# 2. Override the container command to keep it alive
kubectl debug crashing-pod -it --copy-to=debug-pod \
  --container=app -- sh -c "sleep 3600"

# 3. Check resource limits
kubectl describe pod crashing-pod | grep -A3 resources
Explanation

When logs are empty, the container crashes before producing output. Check exit codes: 137=OOM, 1=app error, 126=permission denied, 127=command not found. Use kubectl debug --copy-to to create a copy of the pod with a different command (keeps it alive for investigation). On the CKAD, exit codes are critical clues.

Q8
How do you copy files to/from a running container?
Show Answer
Answer
# Copy FROM container to local machine
kubectl cp my-pod:/var/log/app.log ./app.log
kubectl cp my-pod:/etc/config/ ./local-config/ -c app

# Copy TO container from local machine
kubectl cp ./fix-script.sh my-pod:/tmp/fix-script.sh

# With specific namespace
kubectl cp production/db-pod:/backup/dump.sql ./dump.sql

# Note: kubectl cp requires tar in the container
# For containers without tar, use kubectl exec:
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat /var/log/app.log > ./app.log
Explanation

kubectl cp uses tar under the hood, so the container needs tar installed. For minimal/distroless images, use kubectl exec -- cat with shell redirection. On the CKAD, kubectl cp is useful for extracting logs or config files for analysis. Remember the format: pod:path local-path.

Q9
How do you check DNS resolution inside a Kubernetes cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Launch a debug pod
kubectl run dns-test --image=busybox --rm -it -- sh

# Inside the pod:
# Resolve a Service
nslookup my-service
# Server: 10.96.0.10 (CoreDNS)
# Name: my-service.default.svc.cluster.local

# Resolve a Service in another namespace
nslookup my-service.other-namespace

# Resolve a StatefulSet pod
nslookup pod-0.headless-svc.namespace.svc.cluster.local

# Check CoreDNS is running
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns

# Check resolv.conf inside a pod
kubectl exec my-pod -- cat /etc/resolv.conf
# nameserver 10.96.0.10
# search default.svc.cluster.local svc.cluster.local cluster.local
Explanation

CoreDNS resolves Service names to ClusterIPs. DNS lookup paths: svc-name (same namespace) → svc-name.namespace (cross-namespace) → svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local (FQDN). On the CKAD, if services can't connect, always check DNS first. The /etc/resolv.conf search domains enable short-name resolution.

Q10
A pod's readiness probe is failing but the application is actually healthy. What could be wrong?
Show Answer
Answer
# Check the probe configuration
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].readinessProbe}' | jq

# Common probe misconfigurations:
# 1. Wrong port number
readinessProbe:
  httpGet:
    path: /health
    port: 8080    # App listens on 3000!

# 2. Wrong path
    path: /healthz  # Actual endpoint is /health

# 3. Too aggressive timing
  initialDelaySeconds: 0   # App needs 10s to start
  periodSeconds: 1
  failureThreshold: 1      # One failure = not ready

# 4. Test the probe manually
kubectl exec my-pod -- wget -qO- http://localhost:8080/health

# Fix: Adjust probe parameters
  initialDelaySeconds: 15
  periodSeconds: 10
  failureThreshold: 3
Explanation

Probe failures with healthy apps: wrong port (most common), wrong path, too-short initial delay, or too-strict thresholds. Test the probe endpoint manually with kubectl exec. On the CKAD, always verify the probe port matches the container's actual listening port. containerPort in the pod spec is informational — the probe must target the actual port.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Container Forensics & Incident Response

MONITORING & RUNTIME SECURITY 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

When a container is compromised, you need to investigate without destroying evidence. CKS tests your ability to perform forensic analysis using ephemeral containers, crictl, and log analysis.

Forensic ActionCommand/ToolWhat It Reveals
Inspect running processescrictl exec <id> ps auxUnexpected processes (miners, shells)
Check network connectionscrictl exec <id> netstat -tlnpReverse shells, C2 connections
Examine filesystem changescrictl exec <id> find / -newer /proc -writableModified/dropped files
Ephemeral debug containerkubectl debug -it pod --image=busybox --target=containerProcess namespace sharing for investigation
Copy files from containerkubectl cp pod:/path ./evidence/Preserve evidence before deletion
 
# CKS Incident Response Procedure
# Step 1: Isolate the pod (deny all network)
kubectl label pod compromised-pod quarantine=true
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: quarantine-policy
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      quarantine: "true"
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
  # Empty ingress/egress = deny all
EOF

# Step 2: Collect evidence using ephemeral container
kubectl debug -it compromised-pod \
  --image=busybox --target=app -- sh
# Inside: ps aux, netstat -tlnp, cat /proc/1/environ

# Step 3: Copy suspicious files
kubectl cp compromised-pod:/tmp/suspicious ./evidence/
kubectl cp compromised-pod:/var/log/ ./evidence/logs/

# Step 4: Check audit logs for how compromise occurred
cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log | jq 'select(
  .objectRef.name == "compromised-pod"
)'

# Step 5: Delete the compromised pod
kubectl delete pod compromised-pod --grace-period=0

Chapter 28 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A pod named web-app in namespace production is suspected of being compromised. Investigate using an ephemeral debug container without restarting the pod.
Show Answer
Answer
# Attach an ephemeral debug container
kubectl debug -it web-app -n production \
  --image=busybox:1.36 \
  --target=web-app -- sh

# Inside the debug container:
# Check processes (shared PID namespace)
ps aux

# Check network connections
netstat -tlnp
# or
cat /proc/net/tcp

# Check for suspicious files
find / -name "*.sh" -newer /proc/1/exe 2>/dev/null
find /tmp -type f -ls

# Check environment variables for leaked secrets
cat /proc/1/environ | tr '\0' '\n'

# Exit and save findings
exit
Explanation

kubectl debug creates an ephemeral container that shares the target container's PID namespace. This lets you inspect processes without modifying the original container. On CKS, the --target flag is critical — it specifies which container's namespace to share. The debug container won't appear in kubectl get pods output.

CKS-2
How do you use crictl on a node to investigate a container that may be running a crypto miner?
Show Answer
Answer
# SSH to the worker node
ssh node01

# List running containers
crictl ps

# Find the suspicious container
crictl ps | grep web-app

# Inspect container config
crictl inspect <container-id> | jq '.info.config'

# Check running processes
crictl exec <container-id> ps aux
# Look for: xmrig, minerd, kdevtmpfsi

# Check CPU/memory usage
crictl stats <container-id>

# Check what binary is running as PID 1
crictl exec <container-id> cat /proc/1/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '

# Check open network connections
crictl exec <container-id> cat /proc/net/tcp
# Decode hex IPs: high CPU + connections to mining pools = miner

# Stop and remove the container
crictl stop <container-id>
crictl rm <container-id>
Explanation

crictl is the CRI-compatible CLI for container runtimes (containerd, CRI-O). On CKS, you may need to SSH to a node and use crictl directly. Key commands: crictl ps (list), crictl inspect (config details), crictl exec (run command), crictl stats (resource usage). Crypto miners show high CPU and connections to mining pool IPs.

CKS-3
Isolate a compromised pod using NetworkPolicy while preserving it for forensic analysis.
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Label the pod for quarantine
kubectl label pod compromised-pod -n production \
  quarantine=true --overwrite

# Step 2: Apply deny-all NetworkPolicy
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: quarantine-deny-all
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      quarantine: "true"
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  - Egress
EOF

# Step 3: Verify isolation
kubectl exec compromised-pod -n production -- \
  wget --timeout=3 -qO- http://other-service 2>&1
# Should fail with timeout

# Step 4: Scale down the deployment to prevent replacement
kubectl scale deployment web-app -n production --replicas=0
# But keep the pod alive for investigation
Explanation

On CKS, quarantine before investigation. The deny-all NetworkPolicy blocks all traffic while keeping the pod running. Label-based isolation is instant and doesn't destroy evidence. Scale the deployment to zero to prevent new (potentially vulnerable) pods from spawning, but keep the compromised pod for analysis.

CKS-4
How do you check if a container image has been tampered with at runtime?
Show Answer
Answer
# Compare running image digest with expected
kubectl get pod web-app -o jsonpath='{.status.containerStatuses[0].imageID}'
# Returns: docker.io/library/nginx@sha256:abc123...

# Compare with the image specified in the deployment
kubectl get deployment web-app -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
# Should match: nginx@sha256:abc123...

# On the node — verify the image
crictl images | grep nginx
crictl inspecti <image-id> | jq '.status.repoDigests'

# Check if container filesystem was modified (diff from image)
# SSH to node, find container rootfs
crictl inspect <container-id> | jq '.info.runtimeSpec.root.path'
# Compare with original image layers

# Use cosign to verify image signature
cosign verify --key cosign.pub nginx@sha256:abc123...
Explanation

Image integrity verification ensures containers run expected code. On CKS, always use image digests (@sha256:) instead of tags (tags can be overwritten). Check .status.containerStatuses[].imageID for the actual running digest. Use cosign verify for cryptographic image signature verification.

CKS-5
How do you use /proc filesystem to investigate a running container from the host?
Show Answer
Answer
# SSH to the node where the pod is running
ssh node01

# Find the container's PID on the host
crictl inspect <container-id> | jq '.info.pid'
# Returns: 12345

# Investigate via /proc
# Check what the process is running
cat /proc/12345/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '
cat /proc/12345/status | grep -E "Name|Uid|Gid"

# Check environment variables (may contain secrets!)
cat /proc/12345/environ | tr '\0' '\n'

# Check open files
ls -la /proc/12345/fd/

# Check network connections
cat /proc/12345/net/tcp
cat /proc/12345/net/tcp6

# Check mounted filesystems
cat /proc/12345/mounts | grep -v "proc\|sys\|cgroup"

# Check capabilities
cat /proc/12345/status | grep Cap
