# Decode: capsh --decode=00000000a80425fb
Explanation

Every container process is visible in the host's /proc filesystem. On CKS, know how to find a container's host PID via crictl inspect and examine /proc/<pid>/. Key files: cmdline (command), environ (env vars), fd/ (open files), net/tcp (connections), status (capabilities). Decode capabilities with capsh --decode.

🎯 Part 9 Complete: Observability Mastered!

  • 📝 Chapter 26 — Logging with kubectl, Django, EFK/Loki stacks
  • 📊 Chapter 27 — Monitoring with Metrics Server, Prometheus, Grafana
  • 🔍 Chapter 28 — Debugging pod states, events, and troubleshooting scenarios

Next: Part 10 takes you to production — cloud providers, CI/CD, and production readiness checklists!

🚀 Part 10: Production Chapters 29-31

🚀 Going to Production: The Real World

You've learned Kubernetes. Now it's time to deploy for real users. Part 10 covers the production checklist, cloud providers, and CI/CD pipelines that turn your anihpj project into a live, scalable application.

🛤️ The Production Journey

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    YOUR PRODUCTION JOURNEY                                    │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

     Local Dev          Production Ready         Cloud Deployed        Production
  ┌─────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
  │  minikube   │───▶│  ✅ Checklist       │───▶│  ☁️ EKS/GKE  │───▶│  🌐 Live!   │
  │  localhost  │    │  ✅ HA Setup        │    │  ☁️ AKS      │    │  🔄 CI/CD   │
  │             │    │  ✅ Security        │    │  ☁️ Multi-AZ │    │  📊 Monitored│
  └─────────────┘    └─────────────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └─────────────┘
       │                      │                       │                    │
       │                      │                       │                    │
       ▼                      ▼                       ▼                    ▼
    Chapter               Chapter                 Chapter              Chapter
     1-28                   29                      30                   31
   (learned)          (checklist)             (cloud K8s)          (CI/CD pipes)

Chapter 29: Production Checklist

PART 10 ADVANCED 🔴 CRITICAL

Before going live, every Kubernetes deployment needs to pass a production readiness assessment. This chapter provides a comprehensive checklist covering application, container, Kubernetes, and operational readiness.

🎯 CKAD Domain: Application Design & Build + Deployment Composite — Validates All Domains

The CKAD exam expects production-ready configurations: resource limits, probes, security contexts, and proper pod design. This checklist covers every testable aspect.

Production Config Checklist for CKAD

Every CKAD pod spec should include these production-ready elements:

production-pod-checklist.yaml 
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.2.3          # Always use specific tags, NEVER :latest
    resources:                   # ALWAYS set resources
      requests:
        cpu: "100m"
        memory: "128Mi"
      limits:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "256Mi"
    livenessProbe:               # ALWAYS set probes
      httpGet:
        path: /healthz
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 10
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /ready
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 5
    securityContext:             # ALWAYS set security
      runAsNonRoot: true
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false

📊 Production Readiness Score Card

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                     PRODUCTION READINESS SCORECARD                           │
  │                          Rate Your Deployment                                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    CATEGORY               ITEMS CHECKED    SCORE      STATUS
  ┌─────────────────────┬────────────────┬──────────┬──────────────────────────┐
  │ 🎯 Application       │  __ / 5        │  ___%   │ ○ Ready  ○ Needs Work   │
  ├─────────────────────┼────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────┤
  │ 📦 Container         │  __ / 6        │  ___%   │ ○ Ready  ○ Needs Work   │
  ├─────────────────────┼────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────┤
  │ ☸️ Kubernetes        │  __ / 10       │  ___%   │ ○ Ready  ○ Needs Work   │
  ├─────────────────────┼────────────────┼──────────┼──────────────────────────┤
  │ 🔧 Operations        │  __ / 7        │  ___%   │ ○ Ready  ○ Needs Work   │
  └─────────────────────┴────────────────┴──────────┴──────────────────────────┘

    OVERALL SCORE:  _____ / 28  =  _____%

    ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  RATING GUIDE:                                                          │
    │                                                                         │
    │  🟢 90-100%  →  PRODUCTION READY - Ship it!                            │
    │  🟡 70-89%   →  ALMOST READY - Fix critical items first               │
    │  🟠 50-69%   →  NEEDS WORK - Address major gaps                        │
    │  🔴 <50%     →  NOT READY - Significant preparation needed            │
    └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
💀 Top 10 Production Mistakes to Avoid
#MistakeConsequencePrevention
1 No resource limits OOMKilled, node starvation Always set requests AND limits
2 Using :latest tag Unpredictable deploys Use semantic versioning or SHA
3 No readiness probe Traffic to unready pods Configure /readyz endpoint
4 Running as root Container escape risk runAsNonRoot: true
5 Secrets in ConfigMaps Exposed credentials Use Secrets + external vault
6 Single replica Zero availability on failure Min 2 replicas + PDB
7 No PodDisruptionBudget Downtime during upgrades Always define PDB
8 No network policies Lateral movement attacks Default deny + allow rules
9 No backup strategy Data loss, no recovery Velero + DB backups daily
10 Manual deployments Human error, no audit GitOps (ArgoCD/FluxCD)

Production Readiness — CKAD Quick Checklist

Exam questions often implied: "make this production-ready". Apply these patterns.

# ✅ Production-Ready Deployment Checklist:
# 1. Resource requests AND limits
resources:
  requests: {cpu: "100m", memory: "128Mi"}
  limits: {cpu: "500m", memory: "256Mi"}

# 2. Readiness + Liveness probes
readinessProbe:
  httpGet: {path: /ready, port: 8080}
  initialDelaySeconds: 5
livenessProbe:
  httpGet: {path: /healthz, port: 8080}
  initialDelaySeconds: 15

# 3. Security hardening
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  capabilities: {drop: ["ALL"]}

# 4. Multiple replicas + PDB
replicas: 3
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
spec: {minAvailable: 1, selector: {matchLabels: {app: web}}}

# 5. Rolling update with safe params
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate: {maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0}

✅ Production Readiness Checklist

📦
Container — Non-root user, read-only filesystem, no :latest tag, health probes
📊
Resources — CPU/memory requests AND limits on every container. ResourceQuota per namespace
🔒
Security — RBAC, NetworkPolicies, SecurityContext, no privileged containers
📈
Scaling — HPA configured, PDB defined, replicas ≥ 2 for availability
📝
Observability — Log to stdout, metrics-server, monitoring dashboards, alerting
🔄
Deployments — Rolling updates, rollback tested, ConfigMap/Secret versioning
≥ 2 Min Replicas
3 Probes Liveness+Readiness+Startup
PDB Disruption Budget
No :latest Pin Image Tags

29.1 Pre-Production Readiness Assessment

⚠️ Don't Skip This!

90% of production incidents come from skipping basic readiness checks. Use this checklist before every production deployment.

📋 Four Pillars of Production Readiness

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   PRODUCTION READINESS PILLARS                              │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐
    │  🎯 APPLICATION  │  │  📦 CONTAINER   │  │  ☸️ KUBERNETES  │  │  🔧 OPERATIONS  │
    │                 │  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │
    │ □ Health checks │  │ □ Minimal image │  │ □ Resources set │  │ □ Logging       │
    │ □ Graceful shut │  │ □ Non-root user │  │ □ Probes config │  │ □ Monitoring    │
    │ □ 12-Factor     │  │ □ Multi-stage   │  │ □ PDB defined   │  │ □ Alerting      │
    │ □ Stateless     │  │ □ Scan passing  │  │ □ Anti-affinity │  │ □ Backups       │
    │ □ Migrations    │  │ □ No :latest    │  │ □ Security ctx  │  │ □ CI/CD ready   │
    │                 │  │                 │  │ □ RBAC / secrets│  │ □ Runbooks      │
    └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘
           ▲                    ▲                    ▲                    ▲
           │                    │                    │                    │
           └────────────────────┴────────────────────┴────────────────────┘
                                        │
                              ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                              │   ✅ ALL CHECKED   │
                              │  = PRODUCTION READY │
                              └───────────────────┘

a) Application Readiness

✅ Application Checklist for anihpj
CheckStatusDetails
Health endpoints implemented □ /healthz and /readyz endpoints
Graceful shutdown handling □ Handles SIGTERM, finishes in-flight requests
12-Factor App principles □ Config via env vars, stateless processes
Stateless design □ Session in Redis/DB, no local file state
Database migrations strategy □ Init containers or Jobs for migrations
jobpost/views.py — Health Endpoints 
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.db import connection

def healthz(request):
    """Liveness probe: Is the process alive?"""
    return JsonResponse({'status': 'healthy'})

def readyz(request):
    """Readiness probe: Can we handle traffic?"""
    # Check database connection
    try:
        with connection.cursor() as cursor:
            cursor.execute("SELECT 1")
        db_ok = True
    except Exception:
        db_ok = False

    if db_ok:
        return JsonResponse({'status': 'ready', 'db': 'connected'})
    else:
        return JsonResponse({'status': 'not ready', 'db': 'disconnected'}, 
                          status=503)

b) Container Readiness

📦 Container Checklist
CheckStatusWhy It Matters
Minimal base image □ Smaller attack surface (use python:3.12-slim)
Non-root user □ Security: USER app in Dockerfile
.dockerignore optimized □ Exclude .git, __pycache__, .env files
Multi-stage build □ Smaller final image, no build tools in prod
Vulnerability scan passing □ trivy image anihpj:v1 — no critical CVEs
Specific image tags □ Never :latest — use :v1.2.3 or git SHA
Dockerfile.prod — Production-Ready 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STAGE 1: BUILD
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder

WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --user --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STAGE 2: PRODUCTION
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
FROM python:3.12-slim

# Security: Create non-root user
RUN groupadd -r app && useradd --no-log-init -r -g app app

WORKDIR /app

# Copy only what we need
COPY --from=builder /root/.local /home/app/.local
COPY --chown=app:app . .

# Security: Run as non-root
USER app

# Ensure scripts in .local are usable
ENV PATH=/home/app/.local/bin:$PATH

EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["gunicorn", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000", "anihpj.wsgi:application"]

c) Kubernetes Readiness

☸️ Kubernetes Configuration Checklist
CheckStatusK8s Resource
Resource requests AND limits □ resources.requests and resources.limits
Liveness probe configured □ livenessProbe → restart if dead
Readiness probe configured □ readinessProbe → remove from service if not ready
Startup probe (slow apps) □ startupProbe → wait for slow initialization
Pod Disruption Budget □ PodDisruptionBudget → min availability during maintenance
Pod anti-affinity □ podAntiAffinity → spread across nodes
Security context □ runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem
Network Policies □ Isolate pods, allow only required traffic
RBAC configured □ ServiceAccount with minimal permissions
Secrets externalized □ HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager

d) Operational Readiness

🔧 Operations Checklist
CheckStatusTool/Approach
Centralized logging □ Loki/Grafana or EFK Stack
Monitoring configured □ Prometheus + Grafana dashboards
Alerting rules set □ PrometheusRule for critical conditions
Backup strategy □ Velero + database backups
CI/CD pipeline □ GitHub Actions + ArgoCD
Rollback tested □ kubectl rollout undo works
Runbook documented □ On-call procedures, incident response
📝 Production-Ready Deployment Example

Below is a complete production-ready deployment incorporating all checklist items:

anihpj-deployment-prod.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: production
spec:
  replicas: 3   # HA: multiple replicas
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0  # Zero downtime
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        version: v1.2.3
    spec:
      # ─── Security Context ───
      securityContext:
        runAsNonRoot: true
        runAsUser: 1000
        fsGroup: 1000
      
      # ─── Service Account ───
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-sa
      
      # ─── Pod Anti-Affinity: Spread across nodes ───
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - weight: 100
              podAffinityTerm:
                labelSelector:
                  matchLabels:
                    app: anihpj
                topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      
      containers:
        - name: anihpj
          image: registry.example.com/anihpj:v1.2.3  # Never :latest!
          imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
          
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
          
          # ─── Resource Limits (REQUIRED) ───
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "100m"
              memory: "256Mi"
            limits:
              cpu: "500m"
              memory: "512Mi"
          
          # ─── Probes ───
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 15
            periodSeconds: 20
          
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /readyz
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          
          # ─── Security Context (Container) ───
          securityContext:
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            capabilities:
              drop: ["ALL"]
          
          # ─── Environment from Secrets ───
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: anihpj-secrets
            - configMapRef:
                name: anihpj-config

29.2 High Availability (HA) Setup

🏗️ HA = No Single Point of Failure

High availability means your application stays up even when components fail. The key: redundancy at every layer.

🏛️ High Availability Architecture

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │            HIGH AVAILABILITY ACROSS 3 AVAILABILITY ZONES                      │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                              ┌─────────────────────┐
                              │    Load Balancer    │
                              │  (AWS ALB / GCP LB) │
                              └──────────┬──────────┘
                                         │
           ┌─────────────────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┐
           │                             │                             │
           ▼                             ▼                             ▼
  ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────┐
  │     AZ-1 (us-east-1a)│   │     AZ-2 (us-east-1b)│   │     AZ-3 (us-east-1c)│
  │                     │   │                     │   │                     │
  │  ┌────────────────┐ │   │  ┌────────────────┐ │   │  ┌────────────────┐ │
  │  │  Node (m5.large)│ │   │  │  Node (m5.large)│ │   │  │  Node (m5.large)│ │
  │  │                │ │   │  │                │ │   │  │                │ │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod1 │ │   │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod2 │ │   │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod3 │ │
  │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod4 │ │   │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod5 │ │   │  │ 📦 anihpj-pod6 │ │
  │  │                │ │   │  │                │ │   │  │                │ │
  │  └────────────────┘ │   │  └────────────────┘ │   │  └────────────────┘ │
  │                     │   │                     │   │                     │
  │  ┌────────────────┐ │   │  ┌────────────────┐ │   │  ┌────────────────┐ │
  │  │ 🗄️ postgres-0  │ │   │  │ 🗄️ postgres-1  │ │   │  │ 🗄️ postgres-2  │ │
  │  │   (PRIMARY)    │ │   │  │   (REPLICA)    │ │   │  │   (REPLICA)    │ │
  │  └────────────────┘ │   │  └────────────────┘ │   │  └────────────────┘ │
  └─────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────┘

  ✅ AZ-1 goes down? → AZ-2 and AZ-3 keep serving traffic
  ✅ Node fails?     → Pods rescheduled to healthy nodes
  ✅ Pod crashes?    → ReplicaSet creates a replacement
  ✅ DB primary fail?→ Replica promotes automatically (Patroni/CloudSQL)

📋 HA Requirements

📦
Application Layer
  • Min 3 replicas
  • Pod anti-affinity
  • Horizontal Pod Autoscaler
  • Pod Disruption Budget
☸️
Cluster Layer
  • 3+ control plane nodes
  • Nodes across 3 AZs
  • Cluster autoscaling
  • etcd HA (3+ members)
🗄️
Data Layer
  • DB primary + replicas
  • Automatic failover
  • Cross-AZ replication
  • Regular backups
pdb.yaml — Pod Disruption Budget 
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-pdb
  namespace: production
spec:
  minAvailable: 2           # Always keep at least 2 pods running
  # OR: maxUnavailable: 1   # Allow at most 1 pod to be unavailable
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj

# During node maintenance or cluster upgrade:
# - K8s will NOT evict pods if it would drop below minAvailable
# - Ensures rolling updates don't take down all pods at once
🌐 Topology Spread Constraints (Better than Anti-Affinity)

For even distribution across zones/nodes, use Topology Spread Constraints instead of just podAntiAffinity:

topology-spread.yaml — Even Pod Distribution 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 6
  template:
    spec:
      # ─── Spread across Availability Zones ───
      topologySpreadConstraints:
        - maxSkew: 1                          # Max difference between zones
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
          whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule    # Strict enforcement
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj
        
        # ─── Also spread across Nodes ───
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
          whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway   # Soft enforcement
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: anihpj

# Result with 6 pods across 3 AZs:
#   AZ-1: 2 pods (on different nodes)
#   AZ-2: 2 pods (on different nodes)  
#   AZ-3: 2 pods (on different nodes)

✅ HA Quick Checklist

  □ replicas: 3+ (minimum for HA)
  □ PodDisruptionBudget defined (minAvailable: 2)
  □ topologySpreadConstraints configured
  □ Nodes in 3+ Availability Zones
  □ HorizontalPodAutoscaler enabled
  □ Database has primary + replica(s)
  □ Load balancer health checks configured
  □ Graceful shutdown (terminationGracePeriodSeconds)
  □ preStop hook for connection draining

29.3 Disaster Recovery

When everything fails, you need a plan. Disaster recovery covers backup, restore, and failover procedures.

🔄 Disaster Recovery Flow

  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                 DISASTER RECOVERY FLOW                        │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    💥 Disaster           🔍 Detect           🔄 Failover         ✅ Recover
  ┌─────────────┐      ┌─────────────┐      ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
  │ Region down │─────▶│ Alerts fire │─────▶│ Switch to   │────▶│ Post-mortem│
  │ Data center │      │ Monitoring  │      │ DR region   │     │ Restore    │
  │ failure     │      │ detects     │      │ Traffic     │     │ primary    │
  └─────────────┘      └─────────────┘      └─────────────┘     └─────────────┘
                                                  │
                                                  ▼
                                       ┌───────────────────┐
                                       │  Key Metrics:     │
                                       │                   │
                                       │  RTO: 4 hours     │
                                       │  RPO: 1 hour      │
                                       └───────────────────┘

  📊 RTO (Recovery Time Objective): Max time to restore service
  📊 RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Max data loss (time since last backup)

🔧 Backup Tools

🦺
Velero — Cluster Backup

Backs up K8s resources AND persistent volumes

velero backup create anihpj-backup \
  --include-namespaces production

velero restore create --from-backup anihpj-backup
🗄️
DB Backups — pg_dump

Regular database backups to S3/GCS

# CronJob runs daily
pg_dump $DATABASE_URL | gzip \
  | aws s3 cp - s3://backups/$(date +%Y%m%d).sql.gz

🏢 Disaster Recovery Tiers

TierStrategyRTORPOCostUse Case
Tier 1 Active-Active Multi-Region ~0 (instant) ~0 💰💰💰💰 Banking, Healthcare
Tier 2 Warm Standby Minutes Minutes 💰💰💰 E-commerce, SaaS
Tier 3 Pilot Light Hours Minutes-Hours 💰💰 Internal apps
Tier 4 Backup & Restore Hours-Days Hours 💰 Dev/Test, Archives
velero-schedule.yaml — Automated Backups 
# Install Velero first:
# velero install --provider aws --bucket my-backups \
#   --secret-file ./credentials-velero

apiVersion: velero.io/v1
kind: Schedule
metadata:
  name: daily-production-backup
  namespace: velero
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"  # Daily at 2 AM
  template:
    includedNamespaces:
      - production
      - database
    excludedResources:
      - events
      - pods.logs
    ttl: 720h  # Keep backups for 30 days
    storageLocation: default
    volumeSnapshotLocations:
      - default
---
# Verify backups
# velero backup get
# velero backup describe daily-production-backup-20240115

29.4 Cost Optimization

💰 Cloud Bills Can Surprise You

K8s doesn't cost money; the cloud resources running it do. Optimize early to avoid sudden $10k bills.

💵 Cost Optimization Strategies

StrategySavingsHow to Implement
Right-size resources 10-30% Use VPA recommendations, analyze metrics
Spot/Preemptible nodes 60-80% For non-critical workloads (batch jobs, dev)
Scale down dev/staging 30-50% CronJob: scale to 0 at night/weekends
Remove unused resources Variable Orphaned PVCs, idle LoadBalancers
Committed use discounts 20-40% 1-3 year commitments for stable workloads
scale-down-cronjob.yaml — Save Money at Night 
# Scale down dev environment at 6 PM (save 75% compute costs!)
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: scale-down-dev
  namespace: development
spec:
  schedule: "0 18 * * 1-5"  # 6 PM Mon-Fri
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: scale-controller
          containers:
            - name: kubectl
              image: bitnami/kubectl
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  kubectl scale deployment --all --replicas=0 -n development
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
---
# Scale up at 8 AM
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: scale-up-dev
  namespace: development
spec:
  schedule: "0 8 * * 1-5"  # 8 AM Mon-Fri
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          serviceAccountName: scale-controller
          containers:
            - name: kubectl
              image: bitnami/kubectl
              command:
                - /bin/sh
                - -c
                - |
                  kubectl scale deployment --all --replicas=2 -n development
          restartPolicy: OnFailure

📋 Chapter 29 Summary: Production Checklist

  • ✅ 4 Pillars — Application, Container, Kubernetes, Operations readiness
  • ✅ Health endpoints — /healthz and /readyz for probes
  • ✅ HA Setup — 3+ replicas, 3 AZs, pod anti-affinity, PDB
  • ✅ Disaster Recovery — Velero backups, RTO/RPO objectives
  • ✅ Cost Optimization — Right-sizing, spot nodes, scale down dev

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Production-Ready Pod

⏱️ Target Time: 6 minutes
Scenario: Deploy a production-ready pod from scratch using only imperative commands + YAML patches. Every production element must be present.
  1. Create namespace production
  2. Generate a deployment YAML for web-prod (image: nginx:1.25, 3 replicas) with --dry-run=client -o yaml and save to a file
  3. Edit the file to add: CPU/memory requests+limits, liveness probe (HTTP /healthz:80), readiness probe (HTTP /:80), runAsNonRoot: true
  4. Apply the deployment to the production namespace
  5. Create a PodDisruptionBudget allowing no more than 1 pod unavailable
  6. Verify all pods are running and the PDB is active
📖 View Solution
Solution — Production-Ready Pod Drill 
# 1. Namespace
kubectl create ns production

# 2. Generate YAML
kubectl create deployment web-prod --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=3 \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml > web-prod.yaml

# 3. Edit web-prod.yaml — add under containers[0]:
#    resources:
#      requests: { cpu: "100m", memory: "128Mi" }
#      limits: { cpu: "500m", memory: "256Mi" }
#    livenessProbe:
#      httpGet: { path: /healthz, port: 80 }
#      initialDelaySeconds: 10
#    readinessProbe:
#      httpGet: { path: /, port: 80 }
#      initialDelaySeconds: 5
#    securityContext:
#      runAsNonRoot: true

# 4. Apply
kubectl apply -f web-prod.yaml -n production

# 5. PDB
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-prod-pdb
  namespace: production
spec:
  maxUnavailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-prod
EOF

# 6. Verify
kubectl get pods -n production
kubectl get pdb -n production

Chapter 29 — Practice Questions

Q1
What are the essential items on a Kubernetes production readiness checklist? Name at least 8.
Show Answer
Answer
#CategoryChecklist Item
1ResourcesSet resource requests AND limits for every container
2HealthConfigure liveness + readiness probes
3SecurityRun as non-root, read-only filesystem
4AvailabilitySet PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)
5ScalingConfigure HPA with appropriate metrics
6StorageUse PVCs (not hostPath) for persistent data
7NetworkingApply NetworkPolicies (deny-all + whitelist)
8RBACDedicated ServiceAccount with least-privilege Role
9UpdatesRolling update strategy with maxUnavailable
10LoggingStructured logging to stdout, log aggregation
Explanation

A production-ready workload addresses resources, health, security, availability, networking, RBAC, updates, and observability. On the CKAD, questions may ask you to "make this deployment production-ready" — which means adding resource limits, probes, security context, and appropriate update strategy. Every item above maps to a CKAD domain.

Q2
What is a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)? Write one for a deployment with at least 2 pods always available.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: web-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2            # At least 2 pods must be available
  # OR: maxUnavailable: 1    # At most 1 pod can be unavailable
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
# Check PDB status
kubectl get pdb
# NAME     MIN AVAILABLE   MAX UNAVAILABLE   ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS
# web-pdb  2               N/A               1

# PDB blocks voluntary disruptions:
# - kubectl drain node
# - Cluster autoscaler removing nodes
# - kubectl delete pod (when initiated by API)
# PDB does NOT block:
# - Node failures (involuntary)
# - kubectl delete pod --force
Explanation

PDBs protect against voluntary disruptions (drains, autoscaler). minAvailable sets the minimum running pods, maxUnavailable sets how many can be down. Only one of these can be set. On the CKAD, PDBs ensure high availability during node maintenance. They work with kubectl drain to prevent draining too many pods at once.

Q3
What are namespaces used for in production? How do you enforce resource limits per namespace?
Show Answer
Answer
# ResourceQuota — limits total resources in a namespace
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: team-quota
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"          # Total CPU requests
    requests.memory: 8Gi       # Total memory requests
    limits.cpu: "8"            # Total CPU limits
    limits.memory: 16Gi        # Total memory limits
    pods: "20"                 # Max pods
    services: "10"             # Max services

---
# LimitRange — default limits per container
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: default-limits
  namespace: team-a
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: 500m
      memory: 256Mi
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 128Mi
    type: Container
Explanation

ResourceQuota limits total resources per namespace. LimitRange sets defaults and min/max per pod/container. Together they prevent any team from consuming all cluster resources. On the CKAD, when ResourceQuota exists, ALL pods MUST specify resource requests/limits (otherwise creation is rejected). LimitRange provides defaults so pods without explicit limits still get reasonable values.

Q4
How do you handle graceful shutdown of an application in Kubernetes?
Show Answer
Answer
  Pod Termination Flow:
  
  1. Pod marked for deletion
  2. Removed from Service endpoints
  3. preStop hook runs (if any)
  4. SIGTERM sent to PID 1
  5. terminationGracePeriodSeconds countdown (default: 30s)
  6. SIGKILL sent (forced kill)

spec:
  terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60  # Give app 60s to shutdown
  containers:
  - name: app
    lifecycle:
      preStop:
        exec:
          command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 5"]  # Allow endpoint removal
    # App must handle SIGTERM!
Explanation

The preStop hook runs before SIGTERM is sent. A common pattern is sleep 5 to give load balancers time to remove the pod from rotation. terminationGracePeriodSeconds is the total time before SIGKILL (includes preStop duration). On the CKAD, know that applications must handle SIGTERM for graceful shutdown — otherwise they get killed after the grace period.

Q5
What is topologySpreadConstraints? How does it improve availability?
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  topologySpreadConstraints:
  - maxSkew: 1
    topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
    whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
    labelSelector:
      matchLabels:
        app: web
  # Spreads pods evenly across nodes
  # maxSkew: 1 = difference between nodes ≤ 1
  
  # Spread across availability zones:
  - maxSkew: 1
    topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
    whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway
Explanation

topologySpreadConstraints ensures pods are spread evenly across failure domains (nodes, zones, regions). maxSkew is the maximum imbalance. DoNotSchedule blocks scheduling if it would violate the constraint; ScheduleAnyway prefers spread but allows imbalance. On the CKAD, this prevents all pods from landing on one node (single point of failure).

Q6
What is the difference between requests and limits in production? What happens if you set only limits?
Show Answer
Answer
SettingPurposeScheduler UsesRuntime Enforced
requestsMinimum guaranteed resources✅ Yes (for placement)No (can use more)
limitsMaximum allowed resourcesNo✅ Yes (throttle/kill)
 
# If only limits are set (no requests):
# K8s 1.28+: requests default to limits (1:1 ratio)
# This means the pod gets Guaranteed QoS class
# But may waste resources since requests = limits
Explanation

Requests determine scheduling (which node has room). Limits determine runtime enforcement (throttle/kill). Best practice: set requests at typical usage, limits at peak usage. If only limits → requests default to limits → Guaranteed QoS (safe but potentially wasteful). On the CKAD, understand QoS classes: Guaranteed (requests=limits), Burstable (requests<limits), BestEffort (no resources set).

Q7
What are the three QoS classes in Kubernetes? How are they determined?
Show Answer
Answer
QoS ClassConditionEviction Priority
Guaranteedrequests == limits (all containers)Last to be evicted
Burstablerequests < limits (at least one set)Evicted after BestEffort
BestEffortNo requests or limits setFirst to be evicted
 
# Check pod QoS class
kubectl get pod my-pod -o jsonpath='{.status.qosClass}'
# Guaranteed
Explanation

Under memory pressure, K8s evicts BestEffort pods first, then Burstable pods exceeding their requests, then Guaranteed pods last. Production databases should be Guaranteed (requests=limits). Stateless web apps can be Burstable. Never run production workloads as BestEffort. On the CKAD, understand how QoS affects eviction order.

Q8
What Docker image best practices should you follow for Kubernetes production?
Show Answer
Answer
  1. Use specific tags — nginx:1.25.3 not nginx:latest
  2. Multi-stage builds — smaller images, fewer vulnerabilities
  3. Non-root user — USER 1000 in Dockerfile
  4. Read-only filesystem — minimize attack surface
  5. Scan for vulnerabilities — Trivy, Snyk, Clair
  6. Minimal base images — Alpine, distroless
  7. Set imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent — with specific tags
  8. Use digest pinning — nginx@sha256:abc123...
Explanation

Never use :latest in production — it's unpredictable and bypasses rollback. Always pin to specific version tags or digests. On the CKAD, using :latest with imagePullPolicy: Always causes unnecessary image pulls. Specific tags with IfNotPresent is the optimal combination.

Q9
How do you configure pod anti-affinity to prevent all replicas from landing on the same node?
Show Answer
Answer
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: web
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  # Ensures no two web pods land on the same node

  # Soft preference version (allows if no other option):
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 100
        podAffinityTerm:
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: web
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
Explanation

podAntiAffinity prevents pods with matching labels from colocating on the same topology (node, zone). required is a hard rule — pods won't schedule if violated. preferred is best-effort. On the CKAD, pod anti-affinity is used for HA: spread database replicas across nodes so one node failure doesn't lose all replicas.

Q10
What is kubectl drain and why is it important for production maintenance?
Show Answer
Answer
# Drain a node (gracefully evict all pods)
kubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

# What happens:
# 1. Node marked as unschedulable (cordoned)
# 2. All pods gracefully evicted (respects PDBs)
# 3. Pods rescheduled on other nodes
# 4. DaemonSet pods stay (--ignore-daemonsets)

# After maintenance, uncordon:
kubectl uncordon worker-1

# Just cordon (prevent new pods, don't evict existing):
kubectl cordon worker-1
Explanation

drain = cordon + evict. It respects PodDisruptionBudgets — if evicting would violate a PDB, drain blocks until it's safe. --ignore-daemonsets is typically required since DaemonSet pods can't be rescheduled elsewhere. On the CKAD, know the drain → maintain → uncordon workflow for node maintenance.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Production Security Hardening Checklist

CLUSTER SETUP 15% CLUSTER HARDENING 15% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

Production clusters require defense-in-depth: multiple security layers working together. This checklist covers the critical hardening steps tested on CKS.

Security LayerControlCKS Priority
API ServerDisable anonymous auth, enable audit logging🔴 Critical
etcdEncrypt secrets at rest, TLS client certs🔴 Critical
KubeletDisable anonymous auth, set authorization mode to Webhook🔴 Critical
NetworkDefault-deny NetworkPolicy, encrypt pod-to-pod traffic🔴 Critical
RBACNo cluster-admin for users, namespace-scoped roles🟡 High
AdmissionPSA enforce restricted, OPA/Gatekeeper policies🟡 High
ImagesSigned images, vulnerability scanning, no latest tag🟡 High
RuntimeFalco/seccomp/AppArmor, immutable containers🟡 High
 
# CKS Production Hardening Quick Reference

# 1. Kubelet hardening — /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
authentication:
  anonymous:
    enabled: false          # MUST disable
  webhook:
    enabled: true
authorization:
  mode: Webhook             # Not AlwaysAllow!
readOnlyPort: 0             # Disable read-only port (10255)
protectKernelDefaults: true

# 2. Verify kubelet security
curl -sk https://<node-ip>:10250/pods
# Should return 401 Unauthorized (not pod list!)

# 3. API server hardening flags
--anonymous-auth=false
--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,PodSecurity
--insecure-port=0           # Deprecated but verify it's 0
--profiling=false
--audit-log-path=/var/log/audit.log
--audit-policy-file=/etc/kubernetes/audit-policy.yaml
--encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc.yaml

# 4. CIS Benchmark scan
kube-bench run --targets=master,node
# Fix all FAIL items

# 5. Verify no privileged containers in production
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}/{.metadata.name}: {range .spec.containers[*]}{.securityContext.privileged}{" "}{end}{"\n"}{end}' | grep true

Chapter 29 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
The kubelet on node worker-1 is configured insecurely. Fix it to disable anonymous authentication and enable webhook authorization.
Show Answer
Answer
# SSH to the worker node
ssh worker-1

# Edit kubelet config
sudo vi /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
# Ensure these settings in config.yaml
authentication:
  anonymous:
    enabled: false
  webhook:
    enabled: true
    cacheTTL: 2m0s
  x509:
    clientCAFile: /etc/kubernetes/pki/ca.crt
authorization:
  mode: Webhook
  webhook:
    cacheAuthorizedTTL: 5m0s
    cacheUnauthorizedTTL: 30s
readOnlyPort: 0
protectKernelDefaults: true
# Restart kubelet
sudo systemctl restart kubelet
sudo systemctl status kubelet

# Verify: anonymous access should be denied
curl -sk https://localhost:10250/pods
# Expected: 401 Unauthorized
Explanation

Kubelet hardening is a CKS staple. Anonymous auth allows anyone to query the kubelet API. Webhook authorization delegates to the API server. readOnlyPort: 0 disables the unauthenticated metrics port (10255). Always restart kubelet after config changes and verify with curl.

CKS-2
Run a CIS benchmark scan using kube-bench and fix the critical findings.
Show Answer
Answer
# Run kube-bench on a master node
kube-bench run --targets=master

# Common critical findings and fixes:

# FIX 1: API server profiling enabled
# Add to /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml:
--profiling=false

# FIX 2: etcd data directory permissions
chmod 700 /var/lib/etcd
chown etcd:etcd /var/lib/etcd

# FIX 3: Kubelet config file permissions
chmod 600 /var/lib/kubelet/config.yaml
chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/kubelet.conf

# FIX 4: PKI file permissions
chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.key
chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/pki/*.crt

# Run kube-bench on worker nodes
kube-bench run --targets=node

# Re-run to verify fixes
kube-bench run --targets=master 2>&1 | grep -E "FAIL|WARN"
Explanation

kube-bench checks CIS Kubernetes Benchmark compliance. On CKS, know the --targets flag (master, node, etcd, policies). Common fixes: file permissions on PKI keys, disabling profiling, securing etcd. Focus on FAIL items first — WARN items are recommendations.

CKS-3
Ensure all namespaces in the cluster enforce the PSA restricted profile.
Show Answer
Answer
# Check current PSA labels on all namespaces
kubectl get ns --show-labels | grep -E "pod-security"

# Apply restricted enforcement to specific namespace
kubectl label namespace production \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce-version=latest \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
  pod-security.kubernetes.io/audit=restricted

# Apply to ALL user namespaces (skip system ones)
for ns in $(kubectl get ns -o name | grep -v "kube-"); do
  kubectl label $ns \
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted \
    pod-security.kubernetes.io/warn=restricted \
    --overwrite
done

# Verify: try creating a privileged pod (should be rejected)
kubectl run test --image=nginx -n production \
  --overrides='{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"test","image":"nginx","securityContext":{"privileged":true}}]}}'
# Expected: Error — violates PodSecurity "restricted"
Explanation

Pod Security Admission (PSA) replaced PodSecurityPolicy. On CKS, apply enforce=restricted to reject non-compliant pods. Use warn and audit modes for visibility before enforcement. The restricted profile requires: non-root, no privilege escalation, drop ALL capabilities, seccomp profile set.

CKS-4
Verify the API server is configured with encryption at rest for secrets and fix if not.
Show Answer
Answer
# Check if encryption-provider-config is set
cat /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml | \
  grep encryption-provider

# If missing, create encryption config
cat <<EOF > /etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: EncryptionConfiguration
resources:
- resources:
  - secrets
  providers:
  - aescbc:
      keys:
      - name: key1
        secret: $(head -c 32 /dev/urandom | base64)
  - identity: {}
EOF

# Add to kube-apiserver.yaml
# --encryption-provider-config=/etc/kubernetes/enc/enc.yaml
# Mount the config directory

# Verify: check secret is encrypted in etcd
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl get /registry/secrets/default/my-secret \
  --cacert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/ca.crt \
  --cert=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.crt \
  --key=/etc/kubernetes/pki/etcd/server.key | hexdump -C
# Should show "k8s:enc:aescbc" prefix, not plain text
Explanation

Secrets are stored Base64-encoded in etcd by default — NOT encrypted. On CKS, you must configure EncryptionConfiguration with aescbc (or aesgcm/secretbox). The identity: {} provider is the fallback for reading existing unencrypted secrets. After enabling, re-create secrets to encrypt them. Verify with etcdctl get.

CKS-5
Audit the cluster for security misconfigurations: find all pods running as root, with host networking, or with privileged containers.
Show Answer
Answer
# Find privileged containers
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.spec.containers[].securityContext.privileged == true) |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name)"'

# Find pods running as root (UID 0)
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(
    .spec.securityContext.runAsUser == 0 or
    .spec.containers[].securityContext.runAsUser == 0 or
    (.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true and
     .spec.containers[].securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true)
  ) | "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name)"'

# Find pods with host networking/PID/IPC
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(
    .spec.hostNetwork == true or
    .spec.hostPID == true or
    .spec.hostIPC == true
  ) | "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name): hostNet=\(.spec.hostNetwork) hostPID=\(.spec.hostPID)"'

# Find pods with hostPath volumes
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.spec.volumes[]?.hostPath != null) |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name): \(.spec.volumes[].hostPath.path)"'
Explanation

Security auditing is a key CKS skill. Use jq to filter pod specs for dangerous configurations. Priority: privileged containers > hostPID/hostNetwork > running as root > hostPath mounts. In production, only system DaemonSets (kube-proxy, CNI) should have elevated privileges — everything else is a finding to remediate.

Chapter 30: Cloud Kubernetes Services

PART 10 ADVANCED ☁️ CLOUD

You've mastered Kubernetes on minikube. Now let's deploy to real clouds. This chapter covers the big three managed Kubernetes services: EKS (AWS), GKE (Google), and AKS (Azure), plus DigitalOcean's DOKS.

☁️ Beyond CKAD Scope — Cloud Context Background Knowledge

Cloud provider specifics are NOT on the CKAD exam. However, understanding managed K8s services helps you know what the exam environment looks like — a pre-configured cluster you interact with via kubectl.

CKAD Exam Environment Context

The CKAD exam provides pre-configured clusters. You won't provision infrastructure, but you must know:

  • kubectl context switching — kubectl config use-context <name> (you'll switch between clusters)
  • Namespace awareness — always check which namespace the question specifies
  • No cloud CLI needed — no aws, gcloud, or az commands on the exam

🤔 Which Cloud Provider Should You Choose?

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                   CLOUD PROVIDER DECISION FLOWCHART                          │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                                 START
                                   │
                                   ▼
                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                    │ Already using a cloud        │
                    │ provider's services?         │
                    └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                           │               │
                       YES ▼               ▼ NO
              ┌────────────────────┐       │
              │ Use that provider! │       │
              │ (data locality,    │       │
              │  IAM integration)  │       │
              └────────────────────┘       │
                                           ▼
                          ┌────────────────────────────────┐
                          │ Budget is the #1 priority?     │
                          └────────────────┬───────────────┘
                                   │               │
                               YES ▼               ▼ NO
                      ┌────────────────────┐       │
                      │  🔷 DigitalOcean   │       │
                      │  (cheapest, simple)│       │
                      └────────────────────┘       │
                                                   ▼
                          ┌────────────────────────────────┐
                          │ Need best autoscaling & ML?    │
                          └────────────────┬───────────────┘
                                   │               │
                               YES ▼               ▼ NO
                      ┌────────────────────┐       │
                      │  🔵 GKE (Google)   │       │
                      │  (best K8s native) │       │
                      └────────────────────┘       │
                                                   ▼
                          ┌────────────────────────────────┐
                          │ Enterprise .NET or Azure AD?   │
                          └────────────────┬───────────────┘
                                   │               │
                               YES ▼               ▼ NO
                      ┌────────────────────┐       │
                      │  🟣 AKS (Azure)    │       │
                      │  (free control     │       │
                      │   plane, .NET best)│       │
                      └────────────────────┘       │
                                                   ▼
                                      ┌────────────────────┐
                                      │  🟠 EKS (AWS)      │
                                      │  (largest ecosystem│
                                      │   most services)   │
                                      └────────────────────┘
🛠️ Self-Managed Kubernetes
  • You manage control plane (etcd, API server)
  • You handle upgrades, patches, HA
  • Full control but maximum effort
  • kubeadm, kops, Rancher
  • Use when: strict compliance, air-gapped
☁️ Managed Kubernetes (EKS/AKS/GKE)
  • Cloud provider manages control plane
  • Automatic upgrades, built-in HA
  • Integrated IAM, networking, storage
  • Pay per cluster + node compute costs
  • Use when: speed to production matters

📊 Cloud Kubernetes Providers

🟠 AWS EKS Largest ecosystem, IAM integration
🔵 Azure AKS Best Azure/AD integration, free control plane
🟢 Google GKE Best K8s experience, Autopilot mode
🟣 DigitalOcean DOKS Simplest setup, great for small teams

30.1 Cloud K8s Overview

☁️ Why Managed Kubernetes?

Managed K8s = cloud provider handles the control plane (master nodes, etcd, API server). You just manage your workloads. No 3 AM pager calls for etcd recovery.

☁️ Managed Kubernetes Landscape

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │             MANAGED KUBERNETES SERVICES - BIG PICTURE                      │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
    │   AWS    │   │  Google  │   │Microsoft │   │ Digital  │   │   Your   │
    │          │   │  Cloud   │   │  Azure   │   │  Ocean   │   │   Own    │
    └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
         │              │              │              │              │
         ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼              ▼
    ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
    │   EKS    │   │   GKE    │   │   AKS    │   │   DOKS   │   │ kubeadm  │
    │          │   │          │   │          │   │          │   │   k3s    │
    └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
    
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  WHAT THEY MANAGE FOR YOU:                                               │
    │  ✅ Control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller-manager)     │
    │  ✅ Automatic upgrades (optional)                                        │
    │  ✅ HA control plane across AZs                                          │
    │  ✅ Security patches                                                     │
    │                                                                         │
    │  WHAT YOU MANAGE:                                                        │
    │  📦 Worker nodes (or use managed node pools)                            │
    │  📦 Your applications and deployments                                    │
    │  📦 Network policies, RBAC, secrets                                     │
    └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

30.2 Provider Comparison

📊 EKS vs GKE vs AKS vs DOKS

Feature 🟠 AWS EKS 🔵 GKE 🟣 AKS 🔷 DigitalOcean
Control Plane Cost $0.10/hr (~$74/mo) Free (Autopilot: $0.10/hr) Free Free
Min Worker Nodes 2 recommended 0 (Autopilot) 1 1
Setup Complexity 🟡 Medium 🟢 Easy 🟢 Easy 🟢 Very Easy
IAM Integration ⭐⭐⭐ (deep) ⭐⭐⭐ ⭐⭐⭐ (AAD) ⭐
Autoscaling Karpenter/CA Built-in (best) VMSS autoscaler Basic
Serverless Option Fargate Autopilot / Cloud Run Virtual Nodes (ACI) App Platform
Best For AWS shops, enterprise GCP users, ML/AI .NET, enterprise Startups, simplicity

💰 Monthly Cost Estimate (3 nodes, m5.large equivalent)

🟠
AWS EKS

Control: $74

3 nodes: ~$200

~$274/mo

🔵
GKE

Control: Free

3 nodes: ~$180

~$180/mo

🟣
AKS

Control: Free

3 nodes: ~$190

~$190/mo

🔷
DOKS

Control: Free

3 nodes: ~$72

~$72/mo

⚠️ Hidden Cost: Network Egress

Data leaving the cloud (egress) can cost more than compute! Always estimate bandwidth needs.

📤 Network Egress Costs (per GB)
ProviderFirst 1GB1-10 TB/mo10+ TB/moFree Egress
🟠 AWS Free $0.09 $0.085 100GB/mo (2024+)
🔵 GCP Free $0.12 $0.08 200GB/mo
🟣 Azure Free $0.087 $0.083 100GB/mo
🔷 DigitalOcean FREE (included in node pricing)

🔗 Cloud-Native Service Integrations

🟠
AWS EKS
  • RDS (PostgreSQL)
  • ElastiCache (Redis)
  • S3 (Storage)
  • ALB (Ingress)
  • CloudWatch
🔵
GKE
  • Cloud SQL
  • Memorystore
  • Cloud Storage
  • Cloud Load Balancer
  • Cloud Monitoring
🟣
AKS
  • Azure SQL/Cosmos
  • Azure Cache
  • Blob Storage
  • App Gateway
  • Azure Monitor
🔷
DOKS
  • Managed PG/MySQL
  • Managed Redis
  • Spaces (S3 compat)
  • DO Load Balancer
  • Basic monitoring

30.3 EKS Deployment

🟠 Amazon EKS — Elastic Kubernetes Service

Deep AWS integration: IAM roles for service accounts (IRSA), ALB Ingress, EBS/EFS storage, CloudWatch logging.

🏗️ EKS Architecture

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                        AWS EKS ARCHITECTURE                                │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │  AWS Cloud                                                               │
    │  ┌───────────────────────── VPC ─────────────────────────────────────┐  │
    │  │                                                                    │  │
    │  │         ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐               │  │
    │  │         │  EKS Control Plane (AWS Managed)        │               │  │
    │  │         │  ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐       │               │  │
    │  │         │  │API Srv │ │ etcd   │ │Scheduler│      │               │  │
    │  │         │  └────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘       │               │  │
    │  │         └────────────────────┬────────────────────┘               │  │
    │  │                              │                                     │  │
    │  │  ┌───────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────┐    │  │
    │  │  │  Managed Node Group (or Fargate)                          │    │  │
    │  │  │  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐                 │    │  │
    │  │  │  │  EC2 m5  │  │  EC2 m5  │  │  EC2 m5  │                 │    │  │
    │  │  │  │ ┌──────┐ │  │ ┌──────┐ │  │ ┌──────┐ │                 │    │  │
    │  │  │  │ │anihpj│ │  │ │anihpj│ │  │ │anihpj│ │                 │    │  │
    │  │  │  │ └──────┘ │  │ └──────┘ │  │ └──────┘ │                 │    │  │
    │  │  │  └──────────┘  └──────────┘  └──────────┘                 │    │  │
    │  │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │  │
    │  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
    │                                                                          │
    │  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐  ┌─────────────┐     │
    │  │  Route 53   │  │     ALB     │  │     RDS     │  │   S3/EFS    │     │
    │  │   (DNS)     │  │  (Ingress)  │  │  (Database) │  │  (Storage)  │     │
    │  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘  └─────────────┘     │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
EKS Cluster Creation with eksctl 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Install eksctl (if not installed)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# macOS / Linux
curl --silent --location "https://github.com/weaveworks/eksctl/releases/latest/download/eksctl_$(uname -s)_amd64.tar.gz" | tar xz -C /tmp
sudo mv /tmp/eksctl /usr/local/bin

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Create EKS Cluster (10-15 minutes)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
eksctl create cluster \
  --name anihpj-cluster \
  --version 1.29 \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --nodegroup-name workers \
  --node-type m5.large \
  --nodes 3 \
  --nodes-min 2 \
  --nodes-max 5 \
  --managed

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Verify cluster
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get nodes
# NAME                              STATUS   ROLES    AGE   VERSION
# ip-192-168-12-34.ec2.internal     Ready       5m    v1.29.0-eks
# ip-192-168-56-78.ec2.internal     Ready       5m    v1.29.0-eks
# ip-192-168-90-12.ec2.internal     Ready       5m    v1.29.0-eks

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Deploy anihpj
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment-prod.yaml
kubectl apply -f anihpj-service.yaml

30.4 GKE Deployment

🔵 Google Kubernetes Engine

The original managed K8s. Best autoscaling, Autopilot mode = fully managed nodes. Created by the same team that built Kubernetes.

GKE Cluster Creation with gcloud 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Set project
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
gcloud config set project my-project-id

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Create GKE Cluster (Standard mode)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
gcloud container clusters create anihpj-cluster \
  --zone us-central1-a \
  --num-nodes 3 \
  --machine-type e2-medium \
  --enable-autoscaling \
  --min-nodes 2 \
  --max-nodes 5 \
  --release-channel regular

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# OR: Create GKE Autopilot (fully managed, pay-per-pod)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
gcloud container clusters create-auto anihpj-autopilot \
  --region us-central1

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Get credentials (configure kubectl)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
gcloud container clusters get-credentials anihpj-cluster \
  --zone us-central1-a

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Deploy anihpj
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment-prod.yaml
kubectl apply -f anihpj-service.yaml

30.5 AKS Deployment

🟣 Azure Kubernetes Service

Deep Azure AD integration. Free control plane. Best for .NET workloads and Azure ecosystem (Cosmos DB, Azure SQL, etc.).

AKS Cluster Creation with az cli 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Create resource group
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
az group create --name anihpj-rg --location eastus

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Create AKS cluster
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
az aks create \
  --resource-group anihpj-rg \
  --name anihpj-cluster \
  --node-count 3 \
  --node-vm-size Standard_B2s \
  --enable-cluster-autoscaler \
  --min-count 2 \
  --max-count 5 \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Get credentials
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
az aks get-credentials \
  --resource-group anihpj-rg \
  --name anihpj-cluster

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Verify and deploy
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get nodes
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment-prod.yaml

30.6 DigitalOcean Deployment

🔷 DigitalOcean Kubernetes (DOKS)

Simplest setup. Best for startups and small teams. Cheapest pricing. Limited regions and enterprise features.

DOKS Cluster with doctl 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Install doctl and authenticate
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
doctl auth init   # Paste your API token

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Create DOKS cluster
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
doctl kubernetes cluster create anihpj-cluster \
  --region nyc1 \
  --node-pool "name=workers;size=s-2vcpu-4gb;count=3;auto-scale=true;min-nodes=2;max-nodes=5"

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Download kubeconfig (auto-configured)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
doctl kubernetes cluster kubeconfig save anihpj-cluster

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Deploy anihpj
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl apply -f anihpj-deployment-prod.yaml
kubectl apply -f anihpj-service.yaml

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Bonus: Create LoadBalancer (DO automatically provisions)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --type=LoadBalancer --port=80 --target-port=8000

📋 Chapter 30 Summary: Cloud Kubernetes

  • ☁️ Managed K8s — Cloud handles control plane, you manage workloads
  • 🟠 EKS — Best AWS integration, IAM roles, ALB Ingress ($74/mo control plane)
  • 🔵 GKE — Best autoscaling, Autopilot mode, free control plane
  • 🟣 AKS — Azure AD, free control plane, best for .NET
  • 🔷 DOKS — Simplest, cheapest, great for startups

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Context Switching

⏱️ Target Time: 2 minutes
Scenario: The CKAD exam requires you to switch between multiple pre-configured clusters. Practice the context commands.
  1. List all available contexts
  2. Show the current context
  3. Switch to a context named cluster2
  4. Set the default namespace to dev for the current context
  5. Verify the namespace changed
📖 View Solution
Solution — Context Switching Drill 
# 1. List contexts
kubectl config get-contexts

# 2. Current context
kubectl config current-context

# 3. Switch context
kubectl config use-context cluster2

# 4. Set default namespace
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=dev

# 5. Verify
kubectl config view --minify | grep namespace

Chapter 30 — Practice Questions

Q1
Compare EKS, AKS, and GKE. What does each managed service handle for you vs. what you must manage?
Show Answer
Answer
ComponentEKS (AWS)AKS (Azure)GKE (Google)
Control planeManagedManaged (free)Managed (free for standard)
Worker nodesYou manage (or Fargate)You manage (or virtual nodes)You manage (or Autopilot)
UpgradesManual triggerManual/autoAuto or manual
Networking CNIVPC CNI (AWS)Azure CNI / kubenetGKE CNI (Calico option)
IAM integrationIRSA / Pod IdentityWorkload IdentityWorkload Identity
Control plane cost$0.10/hrFreeFree (standard)
Explanation

All three cloud providers manage the control plane (etcd, API server, scheduler, controller-manager). You manage worker nodes, application workloads, RBAC, and networking policies. On the CKAD, you won't be tested on specific cloud provider features, but understanding what "managed Kubernetes" means helps with architecture questions.

Q2
How do you switch between multiple Kubernetes clusters using kubectl?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all contexts
kubectl config get-contexts
# CURRENT  NAME       CLUSTER     NAMESPACE
# *        prod-us    prod-us     default
#          staging    staging     dev

# Switch context
kubectl config use-context staging

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=production

# View current context
kubectl config current-context

# Rename a context
kubectl config rename-context old-name new-name

# Delete a context
kubectl config delete-context old-context
Explanation

A kubeconfig context = cluster + user + namespace. The CKAD exam has MULTIPLE clusters — you MUST switch context at the start of each question. Always verify with kubectl config current-context. Forgetting to switch is a common exam mistake that can cost all points for that question.

Q3
What is the difference between a cloud load balancer Service and an Ingress? When should you use each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureService (LoadBalancer)Ingress
LayerL4 (TCP/UDP)L7 (HTTP/HTTPS)
Cost1 LB per Service ($$$)1 LB for many routes ($)
Path routingNoYes (/api, /web)
Host routingNoYes (app.example.com)
TLS terminationAt LBAt Ingress controller
Use caseNon-HTTP (databases, gRPC)HTTP APIs, web apps
Explanation

In cloud environments, each LoadBalancer Service provisions a cloud load balancer (expensive at scale). Ingress consolidates routing behind a single load balancer. On the CKAD, prefer Ingress for HTTP workloads (path/host routing), use LoadBalancer Service for non-HTTP protocols like databases.

Q4
What is a StorageClass? How does dynamic provisioning work in cloud Kubernetes?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: storage.k8s.io/v1
kind: StorageClass
metadata:
  name: fast-ssd
provisioner: kubernetes.io/aws-ebs   # or pd.csi.storage.gke.io
parameters:
  type: gp3                          # SSD type
reclaimPolicy: Retain                # Keep volume on PVC delete
volumeBindingMode: WaitForFirstConsumer  # Bind when pod schedules

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: data-pvc
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  storageClassName: fast-ssd    # References the StorageClass
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi
# Cloud automatically creates a 10Gi SSD volume!
Explanation

StorageClass defines HOW storage is provisioned. When a PVC references a StorageClass, Kubernetes automatically creates a PV (dynamic provisioning). WaitForFirstConsumer delays binding until a pod is scheduled (important for zone-aware storage). On the CKAD, know that PVCs with storageClassName trigger dynamic provisioning, while PVCs without it use static PVs.

Q5
How do you merge multiple kubeconfig files?
Show Answer
Answer
# Option 1: KUBECONFIG env with multiple files
export KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/cluster2-config

# Option 2: Merge into single file
KUBECONFIG=~/.kube/config:~/.kube/cluster2-config \
  kubectl config view --flatten > ~/.kube/merged-config

# Option 3: Use --kubeconfig flag per command
kubectl --kubeconfig=/path/to/config get pods

# View merged result
kubectl config get-contexts
# Shows contexts from ALL merged files
Explanation

The KUBECONFIG environment variable accepts colon-separated paths (semicolons on Windows). Kubectl merges them in memory. --flatten produces a single combined file. On the CKAD, the exam environment pre-configures kubeconfig — you just need to use-context to switch clusters.

Q6
What is a cloud-native node pool (node group)? Why use multiple node pools?
Show Answer
Answer
  Cluster
  ├── System Pool (3 nodes)
  │   ├── Instance: t3.medium
  │   ├── Labels: role=system
  │   └── Runs: CoreDNS, metrics-server
  │
  ├── App Pool (5 nodes, autoscaled 2-10)
  │   ├── Instance: t3.large
  │   ├── Labels: role=app
  │   └── Runs: web servers, APIs
  │
  └── GPU Pool (2 nodes)
      ├── Instance: p3.2xlarge
      ├── Labels: role=gpu
      ├── Taint: gpu=true:NoSchedule
      └── Runs: ML training jobs
Explanation

Node pools group nodes with the same configuration (instance type, labels, taints). Use multiple pools to isolate workloads: system components on small nodes, apps on medium nodes, GPU workloads on specialized nodes. On the CKAD, you use nodeSelector and tolerations to place pods on the right pool.

Q7
What is the Cluster Autoscaler? How does it differ from HPA?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureCluster Autoscaler (CA)HPA
ScalesNodes (infrastructure)Pods (application)
TriggerPending pods (unschedulable)CPU/memory/custom metrics
Scale downUnderutilized nodesLow metric values
Works withCloud node poolsDeployments, ReplicaSets
CKAD scopeAwareness onlyHands-on (create HPA)
 
# HPA creates more pods → pods Pending →
# Cluster Autoscaler adds nodes → pods scheduled
# They work together!
Explanation

HPA scales pods horizontally. When nodes run out of capacity, pods go Pending. Cluster Autoscaler detects unschedulable pods and adds nodes. They complement each other: HPA handles application-level scaling, CA handles infrastructure-level scaling. On the CKAD, you'll configure HPA directly but should understand CA conceptually.

Q8
How does Workload Identity (or IRSA on AWS) work in cloud Kubernetes?
Show Answer
Answer
  Workload Identity Flow:
  
  1. Create cloud IAM role (e.g., S3 read access)
  2. Create K8s ServiceAccount
  3. Annotate SA with IAM role ARN/email
  4. Pod uses SA → gets temporary cloud credentials
  5. No static keys needed!
  
  Pod → ServiceAccount → [OIDC] → Cloud IAM Role → S3

apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: s3-reader
  annotations:
    # AWS EKS (IRSA)
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456:role/s3-reader
    # GKE Workload Identity
    # iam.gke.io/gcp-service-account: sa@project.iam.gserviceaccount.com
Explanation

Workload Identity eliminates static credentials (access keys, JSON keys). Pods assume cloud IAM roles via Kubernetes ServiceAccounts. The cloud provider validates the pod's identity through OIDC federation. On the CKAD, understand that ServiceAccounts bridge K8s RBAC to cloud IAM — you annotate the SA to link it to a cloud role.

Q9
What is externalTrafficPolicy and why does it matter for cloud load balancers?
Show Answer
Answer
PolicyBehaviorClient IPTraffic Distribution
Cluster (default)Traffic routed to any node, then to any podLost (SNAT)Even across pods
LocalTraffic only to pods on the receiving nodePreservedMay be uneven
 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  externalTrafficPolicy: Local  # Preserves client source IP
  ports:
  - port: 80
  selector:
    app: web
Explanation

Cluster policy has an extra network hop (SNAT) but distributes evenly. Local preserves client IP (important for logging, geo-routing) but only sends traffic to local pods. If a node has no pods, health checks fail and the LB skips that node. On the CKAD, use Local when source IP matters (e.g., IP-based rate limiting).

Q10
How do you set up kubectl access to a cloud Kubernetes cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# AWS EKS
aws eks update-kubeconfig --name my-cluster --region us-east-1

# Azure AKS
az aks get-credentials --resource-group myRG --name my-cluster

# Google GKE
gcloud container clusters get-credentials my-cluster --zone us-central1-a

# All three commands:
# 1. Download cluster CA certificate
# 2. Create kubeconfig entry with cluster endpoint
# 3. Configure authentication (token/exec plugin)
# 4. Set current context to the new cluster

# Verify access
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
Explanation

Each cloud provider has a CLI command that configures kubeconfig automatically. The commands download the cluster's CA cert, API server endpoint, and configure authentication (typically using the cloud CLI as an exec-based credential plugin). On the CKAD, the exam provides pre-configured kubeconfig — but understanding this setup is essential for real-world cluster management.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Cloud IAM & Workload Identity Security

CLUSTER HARDENING 15% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Managed Kubernetes services (EKS, GKE, AKS) add cloud IAM as an additional security layer. Misconfigurations here are the #1 source of cloud K8s breaches.

CloudWorkload IdentityMetadata AttackMitigation
AWS EKSIRSA (IAM Roles for SA)169.254.169.254 (IMDSv1)IMDSv2 + NetworkPolicy
GCP GKEWorkload Identity Federationmetadata.google.internalGKE metadata concealment
Azure AKSWorkload Identity (Azure AD)169.254.169.254 (IMDS)NetworkPolicy block
 
# Block cloud metadata service via NetworkPolicy
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: block-metadata
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Egress]
  egress:
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
        except:
        - 169.254.169.254/32    # Block AWS/Azure metadata
        - 169.254.170.2/32      # Block ECS credentials
# AWS IRSA — Proper workload identity (not node-level IAM)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: s3-reader
  annotations:
    eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/s3-reader
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: s3-app
spec:
  serviceAccountName: s3-reader  # Uses IRSA, not node role
  automountServiceAccountToken: true
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0

Chapter 30 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create a NetworkPolicy that blocks all pods in namespace production from accessing the cloud metadata service (169.254.169.254).
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-metadata-access
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}       # Apply to ALL pods
  policyTypes:
  - Egress
  egress:
  # Allow all traffic EXCEPT metadata service
  - to:
    - ipBlock:
        cidr: 0.0.0.0/0
        except:
        - 169.254.169.254/32
  # Allow DNS resolution
  - to:
    - namespaceSelector: {}
    ports:
    - protocol: UDP
      port: 53
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 53
# Verify: pod should not reach metadata
kubectl exec -n production test-pod -- \
  wget --timeout=3 -qO- http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/
# Expected: timeout/connection refused

# Verify: normal egress still works
kubectl exec -n production test-pod -- \
  wget --timeout=3 -qO- http://example.com
# Expected: success
Explanation

The cloud metadata service exposes instance credentials, tokens, and configuration. Attackers with SSRF or code execution can steal cloud IAM credentials. On CKS, blocking 169.254.169.254 via NetworkPolicy is a critical control. Remember to allow DNS (port 53) or pods won't resolve any hostnames.

CKS-2
How do you prevent pods from using the node's cloud IAM role?
Show Answer
Answer
# AWS: Use IRSA instead of node-level IAM
# 1. Create IAM role with trust policy for specific SA
# 2. Annotate the ServiceAccount
kubectl annotate sa my-app \
  eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn=arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/my-app-role

# 3. Disable automount on default SA
kubectl patch sa default -n production -p '
  {"automountServiceAccountToken": false}'

# 4. AWS: Enforce IMDSv2 (blocks simple metadata curls)
aws ec2 modify-instance-metadata-options \
  --instance-id i-123 \
  --http-tokens required \
  --http-endpoint enabled

# 5. Block metadata service entirely via NetworkPolicy
# (See CKS-1 above)

# Audit: find pods without explicit service accounts
kubectl get pods -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.spec.serviceAccountName == "default" or
         .spec.serviceAccountName == null) |
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name)"'
Explanation

Node-level IAM roles give every pod on the node the same cloud permissions — a massive blast radius. On CKS, enforce pod-level identity (IRSA/Workload Identity). Disable automountServiceAccountToken on the default SA. Audit for pods using the default SA — they should use dedicated SAs with minimal cloud permissions.

CKS-3
How do you secure the Kubernetes Dashboard in a managed cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# Option 1: DON'T deploy the Dashboard in production
# It's a high-risk attack surface

# If you must use it:

# 1. Never expose via LoadBalancer/NodePort
# Use kubectl proxy or port-forward only
kubectl port-forward -n kubernetes-dashboard \
  svc/kubernetes-dashboard 8443:443

# 2. Create a read-only ClusterRole for Dashboard
kubectl create clusterrole dashboard-viewer \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods,services,deployments,namespaces

kubectl create clusterrolebinding dashboard-viewer-binding \
  --clusterrole=dashboard-viewer \
  --serviceaccount=kubernetes-dashboard:dashboard-sa

# 3. Never grant cluster-admin to Dashboard SA
# This was the Tesla cryptojacking attack vector

# 4. Enable token-based authentication only
# Remove skip-login flag if present
Explanation

The Kubernetes Dashboard with cluster-admin access caused the famous Tesla breach. On CKS, best practice is to not deploy it. If required: use RBAC with minimal read-only permissions, never expose externally, require token authentication, and remove any --skip-login flags.

CKS-4
How do you disable automounting of ServiceAccount tokens and use projected volumes instead?
Show Answer
Answer
# Disable automount at SA level
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: production
automountServiceAccountToken: false

---
# Pod with projected token (time-bound, audience-bound)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  serviceAccountName: my-app
  automountServiceAccountToken: false  # Double-disable
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/tokens
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: token
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          path: token
          expirationSeconds: 3600       # 1 hour
          audience: my-api-server       # Bound audience
Explanation

Default SA tokens are non-expiring and can be used from anywhere. On CKS, use projected ServiceAccount tokens with expirationSeconds and audience. Disable automountServiceAccountToken at both the SA and pod level. Projected tokens rotate automatically and are audience-bound, limiting the impact of token theft.

CKS-5
Find all service accounts in the cluster with cluster-admin privileges.
Show Answer
Answer
# Find all ClusterRoleBindings to cluster-admin
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin") |
  {
    binding: .metadata.name,
    subjects: [.subjects[] |
      "\(.kind): \(.namespace // "cluster")/\(.name)"]
  }'

# Find service accounts specifically
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin") |
  .subjects[]? |
  select(.kind == "ServiceAccount") |
  "\(.namespace)/\(.name)"'

# Check if any namespace-level RoleBindings
# escalate to cluster-admin
kubectl get rolebindings -A -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin") |
  "\(.metadata.namespace): \(.subjects[].name)"'

# Remediation: replace with minimal role
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding risky-binding
kubectl create clusterrolebinding safe-binding \
  --clusterrole=view \
  --serviceaccount=default:my-sa
Explanation

Service accounts with cluster-admin are dangerous — a compromised pod gains full cluster control. On CKS, audit all ClusterRoleBindings to cluster-admin. Only system:masters group should have cluster-admin. Replace SA bindings with minimal, namespace-scoped roles following least-privilege.

Chapter 31: CI/CD Integration

PART 10 ADVANCED 🔄 AUTOMATION

Manual deployments don't scale. This chapter covers CI/CD pipelines that automatically build, test, and deploy your anihpj application whenever you push code. We'll cover GitHub Actions, ArgoCD (GitOps), FluxCD, and Helm charts.

🚀 CKAD Domain 2: Application Deployment 20% of Exam — Helm is Testable

While CI/CD pipelines themselves aren't directly tested, Helm commands are fair game on the CKAD exam. You may need to install, upgrade, or inspect Helm releases.

Helm Commands You MUST Know

helm-ckad-essentials.sh 
# Repository management
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update
helm search repo nginx

# Install / Upgrade / Rollback
helm install myrelease bitnami/nginx -n mynamespace
