helm upgrade myrelease bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=3
helm rollback myrelease 1          # Rollback to revision 1

# Inspect
helm list -A                       # All releases, all namespaces
helm status myrelease              # Release status
helm history myrelease             # Revision history
helm get values myrelease          # User-supplied values
helm show values bitnami/nginx     # Chart default values

# Uninstall
helm uninstall myrelease -n mynamespace

🎯 Deployment Strategies Comparison

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       DEPLOYMENT STRATEGIES COMPARISON                           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  1️⃣ ROLLING UPDATE (Default)           2️⃣ BLUE-GREEN
  ┌─────────────────────────┐           ┌─────────────────────────┐
  │  v1 → v1 → v1 → v1      │           │  Blue (v1) ← Traffic    │
  │       ↓                 │           │  Green (v2) [standby]   │
  │  v2 → v1 → v1 → v1      │           │       ↓ switch          │
  │       ↓                 │           │  Blue (v1) [standby]    │
  │  v2 → v2 → v1 → v1      │           │  Green (v2) ← Traffic   │
  │       ↓                 │           │                         │
  │  v2 → v2 → v2 → v2 ✓    │           │  ✓ Instant rollback     │
  │                         │           │  ✗ 2x resources needed  │
  │  ✓ No extra resources   │           └─────────────────────────┘
  │  ✗ Mixed versions temp  │           
  └─────────────────────────┘           

  3️⃣ CANARY                             4️⃣ A/B TESTING
  ┌─────────────────────────┐           ┌─────────────────────────┐
  │                         │           │                         │
  │  v1 v1 v1 v1 v1 (95%)   │           │   Header: beta=true     │
  │  v2 ← 5% traffic        │           │         ↓               │
  │       ↓ monitor         │           │   ┌─────────────┐       │
  │  v1 v1 v1 (80%)         │           │   │  v2 (beta)  │       │
  │  v2 v2 ← 20% traffic    │           │         ↓               │
  │       ↓ promote         │           │   Everyone else         │
  │  v2 v2 v2 v2 v2 (100%)  │           │         ↓               │
  │                         │           │   ┌─────────────┐       │
  │  ✓ Gradual rollout      │           │   │  v1 (stable)│       │
  │  ✓ Real user testing    │           │                         │
  │  ✗ Requires monitoring  │           │  ✓ Feature flags        │
  └─────────────────────────┘           │  ✗ Complex routing      │
                                        └─────────────────────────┘
📊 When to Use Which Strategy
StrategyDowntimeRollbackResource CostBest For
Rolling Update Zero Minutes Low (+25%) Most deployments
Blue-Green Zero Instant High (2x) Critical apps, compliance
Canary Zero Fast Medium High-traffic apps, risk mitigation
A/B Testing Zero Fast Medium Feature experiments, UX testing
🛠️ CI/CD Tools Comparison
ToolTypeStrengthsWeaknessesPricing
GitHub Actions CI/CD Native GitHub, YAML, marketplace GitHub-only 2K mins free/mo
GitLab CI CI/CD All-in-one, Auto DevOps Heavy, complex 400 mins free/mo
ArgoCD GitOps CD Beautiful UI, easy rollbacks No CI built-in Free (self-hosted)
FluxCD GitOps CD CNCF, lightweight, modular No UI (use Weave) Free (self-hosted)
Jenkins CI/CD Extensible, plugins, mature Complex, dated UX Free (self-hosted)
Tekton K8s-native CI Cloud-native, flexible Steep learning curve Free (self-hosted)

Helm Commands for CKAD

ActionCommand
Add repohelm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
Search chartshelm search repo nginx
Install releasehelm install my-release bitnami/nginx
Install with valueshelm install my-app chart/ -f values.yaml
Install with sethelm install my-app chart/ --set replicaCount=3
Install in namespacehelm install my-app chart/ -n production --create-namespace
List releaseshelm list -A
Upgrade releasehelm upgrade my-app chart/ --set image.tag=v2
Rollbackhelm rollback my-app 1
Uninstallhelm uninstall my-app
Show valueshelm show values bitnami/nginx
Template renderhelm template my-app chart/ -f values.yaml

Kustomize Commands for CKAD

ActionCommand
Preview outputkubectl kustomize ./overlays/production/
Apply kustomizationkubectl apply -k ./overlays/production/
Delete kustomizationkubectl delete -k ./overlays/production/
Diff before applykubectl diff -k ./overlays/production/

Kustomize Structure — Base + Overlay

Kustomize is built into kubectl. Know the directory structure and kustomization.yaml.

# Directory Structure:
# base/
#   ├── kustomization.yaml
#   ├── deployment.yaml
#   └── service.yaml
# overlays/
#   ├── dev/
#   │   └── kustomization.yaml
#   └── prod/
#       └── kustomization.yaml

# base/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
resources:
- deployment.yaml
- service.yaml

# overlays/prod/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.config.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Kustomization
namePrefix: prod-
namespace: production
resources:
- ../../base
patches:
- patch: |-
    - op: replace
      path: /spec/replicas
      value: 5
  target:
    kind: Deployment
    name: web
📝
Code Push git push to main
→
🧪
CI: Test Lint, unit tests, scan
→
📦
CI: Build Docker build & push
→
📋
CD: Update Update K8s manifests
→
🚀
CD: Deploy kubectl apply / ArgoCD sync
📌 Push-Based CD
  • CI pipeline runs kubectl apply
  • Pipeline needs cluster credentials
  • Tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI
  • Simpler setup but less secure
🔄 Pull-Based CD (GitOps)
  • Agent in cluster watches Git repo
  • No external access to cluster needed
  • Tools: ArgoCD, Flux
  • Auto-sync, drift detection, audit trail

31.1 CI/CD Overview

🔄 The CI/CD Pipeline

CI (Continuous Integration) = automated building and testing on every push. CD (Continuous Delivery/Deployment) = automatically deploying passing builds to your cluster.

🚀 CI/CD Pipeline Flow

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                          CI/CD PIPELINE FOR ANIHPJ                             │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

   Developer        GitHub              CI (GitHub Actions)           CD (ArgoCD)         K8s Cluster
  ┌─────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌─────────────────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐
  │         │     │          │     │                         │     │            │     │            │
  │  git    │────▶│  Push    │────▶│  ┌─────┐  ┌─────┐ ┌───┐│     │   Sync     │────▶│  Deploy    │
  │  push   │     │  Code    │     │  │Build│─▶│Test │─▶│Tag││     │   ↻        │     │  Pods      │
  │         │     │          │     │  └─────┘  └─────┘ └───┘│     │            │     │            │
  └─────────┘     └──────────┘     └────────────┬────────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘
                                                 │                        ▲
                                                 │                        │
                                                 ▼                        │
                                          ┌───────────┐                   │
                                          │  Registry │                   │
                                          │  (ECR/GCR)│                   │
                                          │           │───────────────────┘
                                          │ anihpj:v2 │
                                          └───────────┘

  Timeline:
  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  │ 0 min         │ 1-3 min        │ 4-5 min       │ 6-8 min        │ 8-10 min   │
  │ Developer     │ CI: Build &    │ Push to       │ ArgoCD detects │ New pods   │
  │ pushes code   │ Run tests      │ Registry      │ new image      │ running!   │
  ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

31.2 GitOps Principles

📝 GitOps = Git is the Single Source of Truth

All Kubernetes manifests live in Git. Changes happen through pull requests. The cluster syncs to match Git. No more kubectl apply from laptops!

🔄 GitOps Workflow

  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                       GITOPS WORKFLOW                              │
  └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │              Git Repository                   │
                    │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
                    │  │  k8s-manifests/                         │  │
                    │  │  ├── base/                              │  │
                    │  │  │   ├── deployment.yaml               │  │
                    │  │  │   └── service.yaml                   │  │
                    │  │  └── overlays/                          │  │
                    │  │      ├── dev/                           │  │
                    │  │      └── prod/                          │  │
                    │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
                    └─────────────────┬────────────────────────────┘
                                      │
                                      │  Watches
                                      ▼
            ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │           ArgoCD / FluxCD Controller                 │
            │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
            │  │  1. Detects change in Git                       │ │
            │  │  2. Compares Git → Cluster                      │ │
            │  │  3. Applies diff (sync)                         │ │
            │  └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
            └─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
                                      │
                                      │  Syncs
                                      ▼
                    ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                    │           Kubernetes Cluster                  │
                    │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
                    │  │  Actual State matches Desired State    │  │
                    │  │  (from Git)                            │  │
                    │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
                    └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  🎯 Key Benefits:
  ✅ Audit trail: Every change is a Git commit
  ✅ Rollback: git revert to undo deployments
  ✅ No kubectl access needed for developers
  ✅ Drift detection: Alerts if cluster differs from Git

📋 The 4 Principles of GitOps

📁
Declarative

System state is described declaratively in YAML

📝
Versioned

Desired state is stored in Git with history

🤖
Automated

Approved changes auto-apply to cluster

🔄
Reconciled

Agents ensure cluster matches Git

31.3 GitHub Actions Workflow

🐙 GitHub Actions — CI in Your Repo

Define workflows in .github/workflows/. Triggered by push, PR, schedule, or manual dispatch.

.github/workflows/ci-cd.yaml 
name: CI/CD Pipeline for anihpj

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

env:
  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
  IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }}/anihpj

jobs:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 1: Build and Test
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: '3.12'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install -r requirements.txt
          pip install pytest

      - name: Run tests
        run: pytest tests/ -v

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 2: Build and Push Docker Image
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.event_name == 'push' && github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    outputs:
      image_tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
    
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@v5
        with:
          images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}
          tags: |
            type=sha,prefix=
            type=raw,value=latest

      - name: Build and push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}

      - name: Scan image for vulnerabilities
        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master
        with:
          image-ref: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest
          severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # JOB 3: Update K8s Manifests (GitOps trigger)
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout manifests repo
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          repository: myorg/k8s-manifests
          token: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_TOKEN }}

      - name: Update image tag
        run: |
          cd overlays/prod
          kustomize edit set image anihpj=${{ needs.build.outputs.image_tag }}

      - name: Commit and push
        run: |
          git config user.name "GitHub Actions"
          git config user.email "actions@github.com"
          git add .
          git commit -m "Update anihpj to ${{ github.sha }}"
          git push
🔐 Secrets Management in CI/CD

Never store secrets in Git! Use these approaches instead:

🐙
GitHub Secrets

Encrypted variables in repo settings

${{ secrets.DATABASE_URL }}
${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY }}
🔐
External Secrets

Sync from Vault/AWS/Azure

kind: ExternalSecret
spec:
  secretStoreRef:
    kind: SecretStore
🏰
Sealed Secrets

Encrypted secrets in Git

kubeseal --format yaml \
  < secret.yaml \
  > sealed-secret.yaml

🔒 Secrets Flow in GitOps

  Developer                        External Secrets Operator            Kubernetes
  ┌─────────────┐                 ┌─────────────────────────┐          ┌─────────────┐
  │ Push code   │                 │  Watches ExternalSecret │          │             │
  │ (NO secrets │────────────────▶│  CRD, pulls from Vault  │─────────▶│  Creates    │
  │  in Git!)   │                 │  every 1 minute         │          │  K8s Secret │
  └─────────────┘                 └─────────────────────────┘          └─────────────┘
                                             ▲
                                             │
                                  ┌──────────┴──────────┐
                                  │  HashiCorp Vault /   │
                                  │  AWS Secrets Manager │
                                  │  Azure Key Vault     │
                                  └─────────────────────┘

31.4 ArgoCD

🐙 ArgoCD — Declarative GitOps for Kubernetes

ArgoCD watches your Git repo and automatically syncs changes to your cluster. It provides a beautiful UI and handles rollbacks.

a) Installing ArgoCD

Install ArgoCD 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Create namespace
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl create namespace argocd

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Install ArgoCD
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl apply -n argocd -f \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/stable/manifests/install.yaml

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Wait for pods to be ready
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready pods --all -n argocd --timeout=300s

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Get initial admin password
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get secret argocd-initial-admin-secret -n argocd \
  -o jsonpath="{.data.password}" | base64 -d; echo

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 5: Port-forward to access UI
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl port-forward svc/argocd-server -n argocd 8080:443

# Open https://localhost:8080
# Username: admin
# Password: (from step 4)

b) Application Definition

argocd-application.yaml 
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: anihpj
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  # ─── Project (RBAC boundary) ───
  project: default
  
  # ─── Source: Git Repository ───
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/myorg/k8s-manifests.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: overlays/prod    # Kustomize overlay
  
  # ─── Destination: K8s Cluster ───
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: production
  
  # ─── Sync Policy ───
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true         # Delete resources removed from Git
      selfHeal: true      # Revert manual changes
    syncOptions:
      - CreateNamespace=true
    retry:
      limit: 5
      backoff:
        duration: 5s
        factor: 2
        maxDuration: 3m

c) Sync Strategies

🔄 ArgoCD Sync Options
StrategyBehaviorUse Case
Manual Click "Sync" in UI Production with approval gate
Automated Auto-sync on Git change Dev/staging environments
Prune Delete resources not in Git Clean up orphaned resources
Self-Heal Revert manual kubectl changes Prevent config drift

d) Rollbacks

ArgoCD Rollback Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Install ArgoCD CLI
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
brew install argocd   # macOS

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Login
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
argocd login localhost:8080 --insecure

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# View deployment history
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
argocd app history anihpj

# ID  DATE                           REVISION
# 1   2024-01-15T10:00:00Z           abc1234 (v1.0.0)
# 2   2024-01-16T14:30:00Z           def5678 (v1.1.0)  ← current
# 3   2024-01-17T09:00:00Z           ghi9012 (v1.2.0)  ← broken!

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Rollback to previous version
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
argocd app rollback anihpj 2

# OR: Rollback via Git (preferred GitOps way)
git revert ghi9012
git push

⚔️ ArgoCD vs FluxCD: At a Glance

🐙 ArgoCD
  • ✅ Beautiful Web UI
  • ✅ Rich visualization
  • ✅ Built-in SSO (OIDC, LDAP)
  • ✅ Multi-cluster from one UI
  • ⚠️ Single server (HA available)
  • 📦 All-in-one solution
Best for: Teams wanting GUI + quick setup
🔄 FluxCD
  • ✅ CNCF Graduated project
  • ✅ Modular architecture
  • ✅ Native Helm OCI support
  • ✅ Multi-tenancy built-in
  • ✅ Lightweight per-cluster
  • 📦 Composable toolkit
Best for: CLI-first, highly customizable

31.5 FluxCD

🔄 FluxCD — CNCF GitOps Toolkit

Flux is lighter than ArgoCD, more CLI-focused. Uses Kubernetes-native APIs (GitRepository, Kustomization CRDs). Great for teams comfortable with kubectl.

FluxCD Bootstrap 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Install Flux CLI
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
brew install fluxcd/tap/flux   # macOS

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Bootstrap Flux to your cluster + GitHub repo
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
flux bootstrap github \
  --owner=myorg \
  --repository=k8s-manifests \
  --branch=main \
  --path=clusters/production \
  --personal

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Check Flux status
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
flux get all

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Reconcile immediately (don't wait for poll interval)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
flux reconcile kustomization anihpj --with-source

31.6 Helm Charts

📦 Helm — The Kubernetes Package Manager

Helm packages K8s manifests into reusable, versioned "charts". Configure deployments with values.yaml files. Think npm/pip for Kubernetes.

a) Chart Structure

📁 Helm Chart Directory
anihpj-chart/
├── Chart.yaml          # Chart metadata (name, version)
├── values.yaml         # Default configuration
├── values-prod.yaml    # Production overrides
├── templates/
│   ├── _helpers.tpl    # Template helpers
│   ├── deployment.yaml
│   ├── service.yaml
│   ├── ingress.yaml
│   ├── configmap.yaml
│   └── secret.yaml
└── charts/             # Dependencies (subcharts)

b) Chart.yaml

anihpj-chart/Chart.yaml 
apiVersion: v2
name: anihpj
description: A Helm chart for the anihpj Django job posting application
type: application
version: 0.1.0          # Chart version
appVersion: "1.2.3"     # Application version

dependencies:
  - name: postgresql
    version: "13.x.x"
    repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
    condition: postgresql.enabled

maintainers:
  - name: Your Name
    email: you@example.com

c) values.yaml

anihpj-chart/values.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Default values for anihpj
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

replicaCount: 2

image:
  repository: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj
  tag: "latest"
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

service:
  type: ClusterIP
  port: 80

ingress:
  enabled: true
  className: nginx
  hosts:
    - host: anihpj.example.com
      paths:
        - path: /
          pathType: Prefix
  tls:
    - secretName: anihpj-tls
      hosts:
        - anihpj.example.com

resources:
  requests:
    cpu: 100m
    memory: 256Mi
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi

autoscaling:
  enabled: true
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70

postgresql:
  enabled: true
  auth:
    database: anihpj
    username: anihpj

d) Helm Commands

Common Helm Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Install a chart
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm install anihpj ./anihpj-chart \
  --namespace production \
  --create-namespace \
  -f values-prod.yaml

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Upgrade an existing release
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm upgrade anihpj ./anihpj-chart \
  --namespace production \
  --set image.tag=v1.2.4

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Dry-run to see what would be deployed
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm template anihpj ./anihpj-chart -f values-prod.yaml

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# View release history
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm history anihpj -n production

# REVISION  STATUS      DESCRIPTION
# 1         deployed    Install complete
# 2         deployed    Upgrade complete
# 3         failed      Upgrade "anihpj" failed

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Rollback to previous version
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm rollback anihpj 2 -n production

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Uninstall
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
helm uninstall anihpj -n production

📋 Chapter 31 Summary: CI/CD Integration

  • 🔄 CI/CD Pipeline — Build → Test → Push → Deploy (automated)
  • 📝 GitOps — Git is single source of truth, cluster syncs to Git
  • 🐙 GitHub Actions — CI workflows in .github/workflows/
  • 🐙 ArgoCD — UI + automated sync, Application CRD, rollbacks
  • 🔄 FluxCD — CNCF native, GitRepository + Kustomization CRDs
  • 📦 Helm — Package K8s apps, values.yaml configuration, versioned releases
✅ CI/CD Best Practices Checklist
Security:
  • ☐ Never commit secrets to Git
  • ☐ Scan images for vulnerabilities
  • ☐ Sign container images (cosign)
  • ☐ Use least-privilege RBAC
Reliability:
  • ☐ Run tests before deploying
  • ☐ Use digest, not :latest
  • ☐ Implement health checks
  • ☐ Automate rollbacks
Speed:
  • ☐ Cache Docker layers
  • ☐ Use multi-stage builds
  • ☐ Parallelize test jobs
  • ☐ Deploy to staging first
Observability:
  • ☐ Monitor deployment success
  • ☐ Track deployment frequency
  • ☐ Alert on failed deploys
  • ☐ Log all pipeline steps

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Helm Operations

⏱️ Target Time: 6 minutes
Scenario: Use Helm to manage application releases — install, customize, upgrade, rollback, and inspect.
  1. Add the Bitnami Helm repo and update
  2. Search for an nginx chart in the Bitnami repo
  3. Install the nginx chart as release my-web in namespace helm-test with replicaCount=2
  4. Upgrade the release to set replicaCount=4
  5. View the revision history
  6. Rollback to revision 1
  7. Uninstall the release
📖 View Solution
Solution — Helm Operations Drill 
# 1. Add repo
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update

# 2. Search
helm search repo bitnami/nginx

# 3. Install with custom values
kubectl create ns helm-test
helm install my-web bitnami/nginx -n helm-test --set replicaCount=2

# 4. Upgrade
helm upgrade my-web bitnami/nginx -n helm-test --set replicaCount=4

# 5. History
helm history my-web -n helm-test

# 6. Rollback to revision 1
helm rollback my-web 1 -n helm-test

# 7. Uninstall
helm uninstall my-web -n helm-test

Chapter 31 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is a CI/CD pipeline for Kubernetes? Draw the stages from code commit to production deployment.
Show Answer
Answer
  CI/CD Pipeline Stages:

  ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
  │  CODE     │───▶│  BUILD   │───▶│  TEST    │───▶│  PUSH    │───▶│  DEPLOY  │
  │ git push  │    │ docker   │    │ unit +   │    │ registry │    │ kubectl  │
  │           │    │ build    │    │ e2e      │    │ push     │    │ apply    │
  └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘    └──────────┘
        │                                                               │
        │              ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐                     │
        └──────────────│  SCAN    │───▶│ APPROVE  │─────────────────────┘
                       │ Trivy/   │    │ manual   │
                       │ Snyk     │    │ gate     │
                       └──────────┘    └──────────┘
Explanation

A Kubernetes CI/CD pipeline: commit → build image → run tests → scan for vulnerabilities → push to registry → deploy to cluster. Tools: GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, ArgoCD. On the CKAD, you won't build pipelines, but understanding the workflow helps with Deployment strategies, rollbacks, and image versioning questions.

Q2
What is GitOps? How does it differ from traditional CI/CD?
Show Answer
Answer
AspectTraditional CI/CD (Push)GitOps (Pull)
ModelPipeline pushes to clusterAgent pulls from Git
Source of truthCI serverGit repository
Cluster accessCI needs kubectl credentialsAgent runs inside cluster
Drift detectionNoneAuto-reconcile to Git state
Audit trailCI logsGit history (commits)
ToolsJenkins, GitHub ActionsArgoCD, Flux
Explanation

GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth. An in-cluster agent (ArgoCD/Flux) watches a Git repo and automatically applies changes. If someone manually changes the cluster, the agent reverts it to match Git. On the CKAD, GitOps concepts help understand declarative configuration — the same principle behind kubectl apply.

Q3
How do you update a Deployment image in a CI/CD pipeline using kubectl?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: kubectl set image (imperative — fast for CI/CD)
kubectl set image deployment/web web=myrepo/web:v2.0.1 --record

# Method 2: kubectl apply (declarative — GitOps friendly)
# Update YAML first, then:
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Method 3: kubectl patch (JSON patch)
kubectl patch deployment web -p \
  '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"web","image":"myrepo/web:v2.0.1"}]}}}}'

# Method 4: kustomize (overlay approach)
kubectl apply -k overlays/production/

# Verify rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/web
kubectl get rs  # Check new ReplicaSet created
Explanation

set image is the fastest imperative approach (commonly used in CI/CD scripts). apply -f is declarative (preferred for GitOps). On the CKAD, set image is ideal for speed. Always verify with rollout status and know how to rollback with rollout undo if the deployment fails.

Q4
What is Helm? What problem does it solve?
Show Answer
Answer
  Helm = Package Manager for Kubernetes

  Chart Structure:
  mychart/
  ├── Chart.yaml          # Name, version, description
  ├── values.yaml         # Default configuration
  ├── templates/
  │   ├── deployment.yaml # Templated K8s manifests
  │   ├── service.yaml
  │   └── ingress.yaml
  └── charts/             # Sub-chart dependencies

# Install a chart
helm install my-release bitnami/nginx

# Upgrade with new values
helm upgrade my-release bitnami/nginx --set replicaCount=3

# Rollback
helm rollback my-release 1

# List releases
helm list
Explanation

Helm packages multiple K8s resources into a single installable unit (chart). Values files enable customization without editing templates. Helm manages releases with rollback support. On the CKAD, Helm is not directly tested, but understanding templated deployments and release management concepts is valuable for real-world Kubernetes work.

Q5
How do you implement a canary deployment in a CI/CD pipeline?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Stable deployment (90% traffic)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-stable
spec:
  replicas: 9
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
      track: stable
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
        track: stable
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: myapp:v1.0

---
# Step 2: Canary deployment (10% traffic)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-canary
spec:
  replicas: 1            # 1/10 = ~10% of traffic
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web
      track: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
        track: canary
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: myapp:v2.0

---
# Service routes to BOTH (same app label)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  selector:
    app: web            # Matches both stable AND canary
  ports:
  - port: 80
Explanation

Native K8s canary: create two Deployments with a shared label, pointed to by one Service. Adjust replica ratios to control traffic split (9:1 = ~10% canary). On the CKAD, this technique uses only core K8s resources (no service mesh needed). Monitor canary metrics, then scale up canary and scale down stable once validated.

Q6
What are imagePullSecrets and why are they needed in CI/CD?
Show Answer
Answer
# Create registry secret
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
  --docker-server=registry.example.com \
  --docker-username=ci-user \
  --docker-password=token123 \
  --docker-email=ci@example.com
# Reference in pod spec
spec:
  imagePullSecrets:
  - name: regcred
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.example.com/myapp:v2.0

# Or attach to ServiceAccount (applies to all pods using it)
# kubectl patch serviceaccount default \
#   -p '{"imagePullSecrets": [{"name": "regcred"}]}'
Explanation

Private registries require authentication. imagePullSecrets provides credentials for pulling images. In CI/CD, the pipeline pushes to a private registry, and K8s needs these secrets to pull. On the CKAD, you may need to create a docker-registry secret and reference it. Attaching to a ServiceAccount avoids repeating it in every pod spec.

Q7
How do you implement blue-green deployments in Kubernetes?
Show Answer
Answer
  Blue-Green Deployment:

  ┌─────────────────┐    ┌─────────────────┐
  │   Blue (v1)     │    │   Green (v2)    │
  │   Deployment    │    │   Deployment    │
  │   replicas: 3   │    │   replicas: 3   │
  │   version: blue │    │   version: green│
  └────────▲────────┘    └────────▲────────┘
           │                      │
           │    ┌──────────┐      │
           └────│ Service  │──────┘
  (switch)      │ selector:│  
                │ version: │  ← Change this label
                │  blue    │     to "green" to switch
                └──────────┘

# Switch traffic from blue to green
kubectl patch service web -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"green"}}}'

# Rollback: switch back to blue
kubectl patch service web -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"blue"}}}'
Explanation

Blue-green: run both versions simultaneously, switch traffic by updating the Service selector. Instant rollback by switching back. Downside: requires 2x resources. On the CKAD, implement this with two Deployments + one Service, using kubectl patch to swap the selector label.

Q8
What is kubectl diff and how is it useful in CI/CD pipelines?
Show Answer
Answer
# Show what would change (without applying)
kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml

# Example output:
# -  replicas: 3
# +  replicas: 5
# -  image: myapp:v1.0
# +  image: myapp:v2.0

# Use in CI pipeline:
# 1. kubectl diff -f manifests/ → show changes in PR
# 2. Review changes
# 3. kubectl apply -f manifests/ → apply after approval

# Dry-run (validate without applying)
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=server
# Validates against API server (checks RBAC, quotas, etc.)

kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml --dry-run=client
# Local validation only (syntax check)
Explanation

kubectl diff shows what would change without applying — like terraform plan. --dry-run=server validates against the live API server (catches RBAC/quota issues). --dry-run=client only validates locally. On the CKAD, use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate YAML templates quickly without creating resources.

Q9
How do you use kustomize to manage environment-specific configurations?
Show Answer
Answer
  Kustomize Directory Structure:

  k8s/
  ├── base/
  │   ├── kustomization.yaml
  │   ├── deployment.yaml
  │   └── service.yaml
  ├── overlays/
  │   ├── dev/
  │   │   ├── kustomization.yaml  ← patches for dev
  │   │   └── replica-patch.yaml
  │   └── prod/
  │       ├── kustomization.yaml  ← patches for prod
  │       └── replica-patch.yaml

# Preview merged output
kubectl kustomize overlays/prod/

# Apply directly
kubectl apply -k overlays/prod/

# Kustomize is built into kubectl!
Explanation

Kustomize overlays base manifests with environment-specific patches — no templates, pure YAML. Base contains shared resources; overlays patch replicas, images, namespaces, etc. On the CKAD, kubectl apply -k is available in the exam. Know how to use it to apply a kustomization directory.

Q10
What is a rolling restart and when would you use it in production?
Show Answer
Answer
# Rolling restart — restarts all pods one by one
kubectl rollout restart deployment/web

# Use cases:
# 1. Pick up new ConfigMap/Secret changes
# 2. Force image re-pull (same tag, updated image)
# 3. Clear application cache/memory
# 4. Apply new environment variables

# Monitor the restart
kubectl rollout status deployment/web

# What happens internally:
# - New ReplicaSet created with same spec + new annotation
# - Pods gradually replaced (respects maxSurge/maxUnavailable)
# - Zero downtime if probes are configured
Explanation

rollout restart triggers a rolling replacement of all pods without changing the pod spec. It adds a restart annotation with a timestamp, causing a new ReplicaSet. On the CKAD, this is essential when ConfigMaps or Secrets change (since pods don't auto-reload mounted configs). It's a zero-downtime operation when probes are configured properly.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Supply Chain Security & Image Signing

SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY 20% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

Supply chain attacks exploit the pipeline between code commit and deployment. CKS tests your ability to verify image integrity, scan for vulnerabilities, and enforce trusted images.

ToolPurposeCKS Relevance
TrivyImage vulnerability scanner🔴 Core exam tool
cosign (Sigstore)Image signing & verification🟡 Know the concept
SyftSBOM generation🟡 Know the concept
OPA GatekeeperEnforce image policies🔴 Core exam tool
ImagePolicyWebhookAdmission controller for images🔴 Core exam tool
 
# Trivy image scanning (CKS core skill)
trivy image nginx:1.25           # Scan for vulnerabilities
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginx:1.25   # Filter severity
trivy image --ignore-unfixed nginx:1.25           # Skip unfixed CVEs

# Scan for misconfigurations in K8s manifests
trivy config deployment.yaml
trivy config --severity HIGH ./manifests/

# Image signing with cosign
cosign sign --key cosign.key myregistry/myapp@sha256:abc123
cosign verify --key cosign.pub myregistry/myapp@sha256:abc123

# Generate SBOM
syft myregistry/myapp:1.0 -o spdx-json > sbom.json
trivy sbom sbom.json    # Scan SBOM for known vulnerabilities
# OPA Gatekeeper — Allow only trusted registries
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8sallowedrepos
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sAllowedRepos
      validation:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            repos:
              type: array
              items:
                type: string
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8sallowedrepos
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        satisfied := [good | repo = input.parameters.repos[_]; good = startswith(container.image, repo)]
        not any(satisfied)
        msg := sprintf("container <%v> has an invalid image repo <%v>, allowed repos are %v", [container.name, container.image, input.parameters.repos])
      }
---
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sAllowedRepos
metadata:
  name: prod-repo-is-internal
spec:
  match:
    kinds: [{apiGroups: [""], kinds: ["Pod"]}]
    namespaces: ["production"]
  parameters:
    repos: ["myregistry.azurecr.io/", "gcr.io/my-project/"]

Chapter 31 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Use Trivy to scan the image nginx:1.25 and identify all HIGH and CRITICAL vulnerabilities. Then create a policy to block images with CRITICAL CVEs.
Show Answer
Answer
# Scan for HIGH and CRITICAL vulnerabilities
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginx:1.25

# Output shows CVE-ID, package, severity, fixed version
# Example output:
# CVE-2023-44487  libnghttp2  CRITICAL  Fixed: 1.57.0-1
# CVE-2023-38545  curl        HIGH      Fixed: 8.4.0-2

# Scan and exit with error if CRITICAL found
trivy image --severity CRITICAL --exit-code 1 nginx:1.25
echo $?  # Non-zero = CRITICAL CVEs found

# Use in CI/CD pipeline
trivy image --severity CRITICAL --exit-code 1 \
  --format json --output results.json myapp:latest

# Scan a running container's image
trivy image --input <(docker save nginx:1.25)
Explanation

Trivy scans container images for OS and language-specific vulnerabilities. On CKS, know --severity to filter, --exit-code 1 to fail CI pipelines, and --ignore-unfixed to skip CVEs without patches. Trivy also scans filesystem (trivy fs), K8s manifests (trivy config), and SBOMs (trivy sbom).

CKS-2
Configure an ImagePolicyWebhook admission controller to deny images not from a trusted registry.
Show Answer
Answer
# /etc/kubernetes/admission/admission-config.yaml
apiVersion: apiserver.config.k8s.io/v1
kind: AdmissionConfiguration
plugins:
- name: ImagePolicyWebhook
  configuration:
    imagePolicy:
      kubeConfigFile: /etc/kubernetes/admission/webhook-kubeconfig.yaml
      allowTTL: 50
      denyTTL: 50
      retryBackoff: 500
      defaultAllow: false    # DENY if webhook unavailable
# /etc/kubernetes/admission/webhook-kubeconfig.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Config
clusters:
- name: image-checker
  cluster:
    server: https://image-policy-webhook.security:8443/check
    certificate-authority: /etc/kubernetes/admission/ca.crt
users:
- name: api-server
  user:
    client-certificate: /etc/kubernetes/admission/client.crt
    client-key: /etc/kubernetes/admission/client.key
contexts:
- context:
    cluster: image-checker
    user: api-server
  name: default
current-context: default
# Enable in kube-apiserver.yaml
--enable-admission-plugins=...,ImagePolicyWebhook
--admission-control-config-file=/etc/kubernetes/admission/admission-config.yaml
Explanation

ImagePolicyWebhook is a built-in admission controller that calls an external webhook to approve/deny image pulls. On CKS, know: defaultAllow: false is critical — it denies images if the webhook is unreachable (fail-closed). The webhook kubeconfig uses the same format as regular kubeconfig. Mount all cert files into the kube-apiserver pod.

CKS-3
Create a Dockerfile that follows security best practices for production.
Show Answer
Answer
# Multi-stage build — minimize attack surface
FROM golang:1.22-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY go.mod go.sum ./
RUN go mod download
COPY . .
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -o /app/server .

# Production image — distroless (no shell, no package manager)
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static-debian12:nonroot
COPY --from=builder /app/server /server

# Run as non-root user (UID 65534)
USER 65534:65534

EXPOSE 8080
ENTRYPOINT ["/server"]
# Security checks:
# 1. No root user
# 2. Distroless base (minimal attack surface)
# 3. Multi-stage (no build tools in production)
# 4. Static binary (no runtime dependencies)
# 5. Specific base image version (not :latest)

# Scan the Dockerfile
trivy config Dockerfile

# Scan the built image
docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
trivy image myapp:1.0
Explanation

CKS tests Dockerfile security: use multi-stage builds to exclude build tools, use distroless/scratch base images (no shell = no shell exploits), run as non-root with USER directive, pin specific image versions. Distroless images have 50-90% fewer CVEs than full OS images.

CKS-4
How do you enforce that only images with specific digests (not tags) are allowed in the cluster?
Show Answer
Answer
# OPA Gatekeeper — Require image digests
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8srequiredigest
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sRequireDigest
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8srequiredigest
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        not contains(container.image, "@sha256:")
        msg := sprintf("container <%v> uses tag instead of digest: <%v>", [container.name, container.image])
      }
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.initContainers[_]
        not contains(container.image, "@sha256:")
        msg := sprintf("init container <%v> uses tag instead of digest: <%v>", [container.name, container.image])
      }
---
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequireDigest
metadata:
  name: require-digest-all
spec:
  match:
    kinds: [{apiGroups: [""], kinds: ["Pod"]}]
    namespaces: ["production"]
Explanation

Tags are mutable — nginx:1.25 can point to different images over time. Digests (@sha256:) are immutable content hashes. On CKS, enforcing digests prevents supply chain attacks where an attacker pushes a malicious image with the same tag. The Rego policy checks both containers and initContainers.

CKS-5
A pod is running an image with a known CRITICAL vulnerability (CVE-2023-44487). How do you find and fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Find pods using the vulnerable image
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.namespace}/{.metadata.name}: {.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}' | grep nginx

# Step 2: Scan the image
trivy image --severity CRITICAL nginx:1.24
# Shows CVE-2023-44487 in libnghttp2

# Step 3: Find a patched version
trivy image --severity CRITICAL nginx:1.25
# Check if the CVE is fixed

# Step 4: Update the deployment
kubectl set image deployment/web-app \
  nginx=nginx:1.25@sha256:<digest> -n production

# Step 5: Verify rollout
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app -n production

# Step 6: Verify fix
kubectl get pods -n production -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}'
# Confirm new digest

# Step 7: Add CI gate to prevent future deployments
# with CRITICAL CVEs
trivy image --severity CRITICAL --exit-code 1 nginx:1.25
Explanation

CKS incident response for vulnerable images: identify affected pods, scan with Trivy, find a patched version, update using digest, verify rollout, and add CI prevention. Always use @sha256:digest when updating to ensure exact image. The --exit-code 1 flag fails the pipeline if CRITICAL CVEs remain.

🎉 Part 10 Complete: Production Ready!

You've learned the production checklist, cloud Kubernetes services, and CI/CD pipelines. Your anihpj application is now ready for real-world deployment with high availability, disaster recovery, and automated deployments.

⚡ Part 11: Advanced Patterns Chapters 32-35

🎯 Part 11 Overview: Expert-Level Kubernetes

You've mastered the fundamentals. Now learn the advanced patterns that separate hobby projects from enterprise-grade deployments. These concepts are essential for CKA/CKAD certification and real-world complex architectures.

🤝
Ch 32

Multi-Container Pod Patterns

📍
Ch 33

Scheduling & Placement

🕸️
Ch 34

Service Mesh

🤖
Ch 35

CRDs & Operators

Chapter 32: Multi-Container Pod Patterns

PART 11 ADVANCED 🤝 DESIGN PATTERN

So far, we've used one container per Pod. But Kubernetes supports multiple containers per Pod that share networking and storage. This chapter covers the three classic multi-container patterns — Sidecar, Ambassador, and Adapter — plus Init Containers.

🎯 CKAD Domain 1: Application Design & Build 20% of Exam — CRITICAL TOPIC

Multi-container patterns appear in nearly every CKAD exam. You will be asked to create pods with sidecar containers, init containers, or shared volumes. Know all three patterns cold.

Multi-Container Patterns — What the Exam Tests

Pattern Key Trait Exam Signal Words
SidecarRuns alongside main container"log shipper", "sync", "proxy"
Init ContainerRuns BEFORE main containers"wait for", "prepare", "initialize"
AmbassadorProxies external connections"proxy", "connection pool", "localhost"
AdapterTransforms output format"convert", "transform", "export metrics"

Init vs Sidecar Memory Trick

initContainers use a separate YAML array (spec.initContainers). Sidecars go in the regular spec.containers array. Both share the pod's volumes via volumeMounts.

🎨 The Three Multi-Container Patterns

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      MULTI-CONTAINER POD PATTERNS                                   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  🤝 SIDECAR                   🌐 AMBASSADOR                  🔄 ADAPTER
  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━               ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━                ━━━━━━━━━━━━
  Helper alongside main        Proxy to external services     Transform output format

  ┌─────── Pod ──────┐        ┌─────── Pod ──────┐           ┌─────── Pod ──────┐
  │                   │        │                   │           │                   │
  │  ┌─────────────┐ │        │  ┌─────────────┐ │           │  ┌─────────────┐ │
  │  │   Django    │ │        │  │   Django    │ │           │  │   Django    │ │
  │  │  (anihpj)   │ │        │  │  (anihpj)   │ │           │  │  (anihpj)   │ │
  │  └──────┬──────┘ │        │  └──────┬──────┘ │           │  └──────┬──────┘ │
  │         │ logs    │        │         │ tcp     │           │         │ logs    │
  │         ▼         │        │         ▼         │           │         ▼         │
  │  ┌─────────────┐ │        │  ┌─────────────┐ │           │  ┌─────────────┐ │
  │  │   Fluentd   │ │        │  │  pgbouncer  │ │           │  │  Prometheus │ │
  │  │ (log ship)  │ │        │  │   (proxy)   │ │           │  │  Exporter   │ │
  │  └──────┬──────┘ │        │  └──────┬──────┘ │           │  └──────┬──────┘ │
  └─────────│─────────┘        └─────────│─────────┘           └─────────│─────────┘
            │                            │                               │
            ▼                            ▼                               ▼
     Elasticsearch            PostgreSQL (external)             Prometheus /metrics

  ▪ Log aggregation           ▪ Connection pooling              ▪ Format conversion
  ▪ Nginx reverse proxy       ▪ Rate limiting                   ▪ Log → metrics
  ▪ Sync files to bucket      ▪ Service mesh proxy              ▪ XML → JSON
📊 When to Use Each Pattern
PatternPurposeDirectionExample Use Case
🤝 Sidecar Extend/enhance main container Main ↔ Helper Nginx + Django, log shipping
🌐 Ambassador Proxy external services Main → Proxy → External pgbouncer, Envoy sidecar
🔄 Adapter Transform output format Main → Transform → Consumer Custom metrics exporter
⏳ Init Container Run before app starts Init → Main DB migrations, wait-for-db

Multi-Container Pod YAML — All Patterns

Multi-container is heavily tested. Know sidecar + init container patterns cold.

# Sidecar Pattern (log shipper sharing volume)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-sidecar
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app                     # ← Main application
    image: myapp:1.0
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  - name: log-sidecar              # ← Sidecar container
    image: busybox
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/app/app.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/app      # ← SAME volume = shared data
  volumes:
  - name: logs
    emptyDir: {}                   # ← Shared between containers
---
# Init Container Pattern (wait for dependency)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-init
spec:
  initContainers:                  # ← SEPARATE array (not containers)
  - name: wait-for-db
    image: busybox
    command: ["sh", "-c",
      "until nslookup postgres-svc; do echo waiting; sleep 2; done"]
  - name: run-migrations
    image: myapp:1.0
    command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
---
# Ambassador Pattern (proxy via localhost)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-ambassador
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    env:
    - name: DB_HOST
      value: "localhost"           # ← Connects to ambassador on localhost
  - name: pgbouncer               # ← Ambassador: proxy to external DB
    image: pgbouncer:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5432

Multi-Container Traps

  • ❌ initContainers vs containers — They are SEPARATE arrays in spec. Don't mix them up!
  • Init containers run sequentially — init-1 must complete before init-2 starts. Regular containers start in parallel
  • Shared volumes: Both containers must mount the SAME volume name at their respective mountPaths
  • ❌ Init container never completes → Pod stuck in Init:0/2 forever. Check init container logs!
  • kubectl logs pod -c init-container-name — Use -c to get init container logs
  • Containers share localhost — container A can reach container B via localhost:PORT
  • Exam pattern: "add a sidecar that writes to a shared volume" = add second container + emptyDir volume

Multi-Container Verification

  • kubectl get pod app-with-init → Check READY column (e.g., "2/2" for 2 containers)
  • kubectl describe pod app-with-init → Check Init Containers section + Containers section
  • kubectl logs app-with-sidecar -c log-sidecar → Check sidecar logs
  • kubectl logs app-with-init -c wait-for-db → Check init container logs
  • kubectl exec app-with-sidecar -c app -- ls /var/log/app → Verify shared volume

📊 Multi-Container Pod Patterns

🤝 Sidecar Extends main container (logging, sync)
📡 Ambassador Proxy outbound connections
🔄 Adapter Transform output format (metrics)
⏳ Init Container Run once before main container starts

🔬 Multi-Container Shared Resources

📁 Shared Volumes
All containers in a pod can mount the same volume (emptyDir for ephemeral sharing)
🌐 Shared Network
Same IP, same localhost. Containers communicate via localhost:PORT
💻 Shared PID (optional)
Enable shareProcessNamespace: true to see each other's processes
⚙️ Separate Lifecycle
Each container has its own image, probes, resources, and restart policy

32.1 Sidecar Pattern

🤝 What is a Sidecar?

A sidecar is a helper container that runs alongside your main application container in the same Pod. They share the same network namespace (localhost) and can share volumes. The sidecar extends or enhances the main container without modifying it.

🔍 Sidecar Pattern Anatomy

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                              POD                                          │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
│  │                     SHARED NETWORK (localhost)                     │   │
│  │                                                                    │   │
│  │   ┌─────────────────┐              ┌─────────────────┐            │   │
│  │   │  🐍 DJANGO      │   localhost  │  📦 SIDECAR     │            │   │
│  │   │  (Main App)     │◄────────────►│  (Helper)       │            │   │
│  │   │                 │    :8000      │                 │            │   │
│  │   │  Port: 8000     │              │  Nginx: 80      │            │   │
│  │   └────────┬────────┘              └───────┬─────────┘            │   │
│  │            │                               │                       │   │
│  └────────────│───────────────────────────────│───────────────────────┘   │
│               │         SHARED VOLUME         │                           │
│               └───────────► /logs ◄───────────┘                           │
│                       (EmptyDir/PVC)                                       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

a) Nginx Reverse Proxy + Django

The most common sidecar pattern: Nginx handles static files, SSL termination, and load balancing while Django handles dynamic requests.

anihpj-nginx-sidecar.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-nginx
  labels:
    app: anihpj
spec:
  containers:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # MAIN CONTAINER: Django Application
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8000
      name: gunicorn
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files
      mountPath: /app/staticfiles
    - name: nginx-config
      mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
      readOnly: true
    command: ["gunicorn"]
    args: ["anihpj.wsgi:application", "--bind", "0.0.0.0:8000"]

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # SIDECAR: Nginx Reverse Proxy
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: nginx
    image: nginx:1.25-alpine
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: static-files
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html/static
      readOnly: true
    - name: nginx-config
      mountPath: /etc/nginx/conf.d
      readOnly: true

  volumes:
  - name: static-files
    emptyDir: {}  # Shared between Django (writes) and Nginx (reads)
  - name: nginx-config
    configMap:
      name: nginx-config
💡 Why Use Nginx Sidecar?
  • Static files: Nginx serves CSS/JS 10x faster than Django
  • SSL/TLS: Offload HTTPS termination from Django
  • Buffering: Handle slow clients without blocking Gunicorn workers
  • Compression: Gzip responses automatically

b) Log Shipper Sidecar (Fluentd)

Ship logs from Django to a central logging system without modifying your application code.

anihpj-log-shipper.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-logging
spec:
  containers:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # MAIN: Django writes logs to /var/log/app/
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/app
    env:
    - name: DJANGO_LOG_FILE
      value: "/var/log/app/django.log"

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # SIDECAR: Fluentd reads logs and ships to Elasticsearch
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: log-shipper
    image: fluent/fluentd:v1.16
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/app
      readOnly: true
    - name: fluentd-config
      mountPath: /fluentd/etc
    env:
    - name: ELASTICSEARCH_HOST
      value: "elasticsearch.logging.svc.cluster.local"

  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}  # Django writes, Fluentd reads
  - name: fluentd-config
    configMap:
      name: fluentd-config
  Django Container              Log Volume              Fluentd Sidecar
  ┌─────────────────┐          ┌───────────┐          ┌─────────────────┐
  │  logger.info()  │─────────▶│ /var/log/ │─────────▶│  tail + parse   │
  │  logger.error() │  write   │ django.log│   read   │  enrich + ship  │
  └─────────────────┘          └───────────┘          └────────┬────────┘
                                                               │
                                                               ▼
                                                      Elasticsearch / Loki

32.2 Ambassador Pattern

🌐 What is an Ambassador?

An ambassador proxies network connections from your main container to external services. The main app connects to localhost, and the ambassador handles the complexity of reaching external services (connection pooling, SSL, service discovery).

🔍 Ambassador Pattern Flow

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                 POD                                            │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │                                                                          │  │
│  │   ┌─────────────────┐    localhost:5432    ┌──────────────────┐        │  │
│  │   │   🐍 Django     │─────────────────────▶│  🌐 pgbouncer    │        │  │
│  │   │                 │                       │   (Ambassador)   │        │  │
│  │   │ DB_HOST=localhost│                      │                  │        │  │
│  │   │ DB_PORT=5432    │                       │ Connection pool  │        │  │
│  │   └─────────────────┘                       │ SSL termination  │        │  │
│  │                                              └────────┬─────────┘        │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────│─────────────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────│───────────────────┘
                                                             │
                                                             ▼
                                              ┌──────────────────────────┐
                                              │  PostgreSQL (External)    │
                                              │  prod-db.us-east-1.rds... │
                                              └──────────────────────────┘

  ✅ Django thinks DB is on localhost
  ✅ pgbouncer handles: connection pooling, SSL, failover
  ✅ Easy to swap DB without changing Django config

a) pgbouncer Connection Pooler

anihpj-pgbouncer-ambassador.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-pgbouncer
spec:
  containers:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # MAIN: Django connects to localhost:5432
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_HOST
      value: "localhost"  # ← Connects to pgbouncer sidecar!
    - name: DATABASE_PORT
      value: "5432"
    - name: DATABASE_NAME
      value: "anihpj"

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # AMBASSADOR: pgbouncer proxies to external PostgreSQL
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: pgbouncer
    image: edoburu/pgbouncer:1.21.0
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5432
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: anihpj-db-secret
          key: database-url
    - name: POOL_MODE
      value: "transaction"  # Return connection after each transaction
    - name: MAX_CLIENT_CONN
      value: "100"
    - name: DEFAULT_POOL_SIZE
      value: "20"
📝 Why Use pgbouncer Ambassador?
  • Connection pooling: 100 Django processes → 20 actual DB connections
  • Faster connections: Reuse warm connections instead of TCP handshake
  • DB migration: Change DATABASE_URL in secret, Django doesn't care
  • Failover: pgbouncer can retry connections automatically

b) Envoy Proxy (Service Mesh Sidecar)

In service meshes like Istio, Envoy is automatically injected as an ambassador sidecar to handle all traffic.

🔒
mTLS

Automatic encryption between services

🔄
Retry Logic

Automatic retries on failures

📊
Observability

Request tracing and metrics

32.3 Adapter Pattern

🔄 What is an Adapter?

An adapter transforms output from the main container into a standard format. Common use cases: converting proprietary logs to JSON, exposing custom metrics in Prometheus format, or transforming API responses.

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                               ADAPTER PATTERN                                │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────┐     Raw Output      ┌─────────────────┐    Standard    │
  │  │   Django App    │────────────────────▶│   Adapter       │───────────────▶│
  │  │                 │                      │   Container     │                │
  │  │  Custom logs:   │                      │                 │    Prometheus  │
  │  │  "Job posted    │  ┌──────────────┐   │  Transforms to: │    scrapes     │
  │  │   by user X"    │  │ Shared volume │   │  /metrics       │    /metrics    │
  │  │                 │  │ or localhost  │   │                 │                │
  │  └─────────────────┘  └──────────────┘   └─────────────────┘                │
  │                                                                              │
  │  Input (Django):                    Output (Adapter):                        │
  │  "POST /api/jobs 201 0.05s"   →    http_requests_total{method="POST"} 1     │
  │  "GET /api/jobs 200 0.02s"    →    http_request_duration_seconds{..} 0.02   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-metrics-adapter.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-with-adapter
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "9113"  # Scrape the adapter, not Django
spec:
  containers:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # MAIN: Django writes access logs to shared volume
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
    volumeMounts:
    - name: access-logs
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx

  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # ADAPTER: Convert nginx access logs → Prometheus metrics
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  - name: nginx-exporter
    image: nginx/nginx-prometheus-exporter:0.11
    ports:
    - containerPort: 9113
      name: metrics
    args:
    - "-nginx.scrape-uri=http://localhost:8000/nginx_status"
    # Exposes /metrics endpoint for Prometheus

  volumes:
  - name: access-logs
    emptyDir: {}

32.4 Init Containers

⏳ What are Init Containers?

Init containers run and complete before the main containers start. They're perfect for setup tasks: waiting for dependencies, running migrations, downloading configs, or setting permissions. Each init container must complete successfully before the next one starts.

⏱️ Init Container Execution Timeline

  POD STARTS
      │
      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  INIT CONTAINERS (run sequentially, must complete)                          │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌──────────────┐   Complete   ┌──────────────┐   Complete   ┌───────────┐ │
  │  │ init-wait-db │───────────▶│ init-migrate  │───────────▶│ init-data  │ │
  │  │              │    ✓        │               │    ✓        │            │ │
  │  │ Wait until   │             │ Django        │             │ Load       │ │
  │  │ PostgreSQL   │             │ migrate       │             │ fixtures   │ │
  │  │ is reachable │             │               │             │            │ │
  │  └──────────────┘             └───────────────┘             └────┬───────┘ │
  │       5s                           30s                          10s       │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────│──────────┘
                                                                     │
                                                                     ▼  ✓ All init complete
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  MAIN CONTAINERS (start after ALL init containers complete)                  │
  │                                                                              │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │                         🐍 Django Container                          │   │
  │  │                       (Now safe to start!)                           │   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
anihpj-with-init-containers.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      # INIT CONTAINERS — Run in order, each must succeed
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      initContainers:
      
      # 1️⃣ Wait for PostgreSQL to be ready
      - name: wait-for-db
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ['sh', '-c']
        args:
        - |
          echo "Waiting for PostgreSQL..."
          until nc -z postgres-service 5432; do
            echo "PostgreSQL not ready, retrying in 2s..."
            sleep 2
          done
          echo "PostgreSQL is ready!"

      # 2️⃣ Run Django migrations
      - name: run-migrations
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
        command: ['python', 'manage.py', 'migrate', '--noinput']
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-secret
              key: database-url

      # 3️⃣ Collect static files
      - name: collect-static
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
        command: ['python', 'manage.py', 'collectstatic', '--noinput']
        volumeMounts:
        - name: static-files
          mountPath: /app/staticfiles

      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      # MAIN CONTAINER — Starts only after ALL init containers succeed
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        volumeMounts:
        - name: static-files
          mountPath: /app/staticfiles
          readOnly: true

      volumes:
      - name: static-files
        emptyDir: {}
📋 Common Init Container Use Cases
⏳
Wait for Dependencies

Wait for DB, Redis, or external APIs before starting

🔄
Database Migrations

Run migrations once, before all replicas start

📥
Download Configs

Fetch configs from S3, Vault, or Git

🔑
Set Permissions

chmod/chown files on volumes

kubectl Cheat Sheet: Multi-Container Pods 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Working with Multi-Container Pods
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# List all containers in a Pod
kubectl get pod anihpj-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# Output: django nginx fluentd

# Get logs from a SPECIFIC container (-c flag)
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c django           # Main container logs
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c nginx            # Sidecar logs
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c fluentd          # Another sidecar

# Follow logs from all containers
kubectl logs anihpj-pod --all-containers -f

# Get logs from an INIT container
kubectl logs anihpj-pod -c init-migrate

# Exec into a specific container
kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -c django -- /bin/sh
kubectl exec -it anihpj-pod -c nginx -- nginx -s reload

# Check container statuses
kubectl get pod anihpj-pod -o jsonpath='{range .status.containerStatuses[*]}{.name}: {.ready}{"\n"}{end}'

# Debug: Why is init container stuck?
kubectl describe pod anihpj-pod | grep -A 20 "Init Containers"

# Copy file from specific container
kubectl cp anihpj-pod:/var/log/nginx/error.log ./error.log -c nginx
🔧 Troubleshooting Multi-Container Pod Issues
ProblemSymptomsSolution
Pod stuck at Init:0/3 Init container failing or waiting kubectl logs <pod> -c <init-container>
Sidecar can't reach main Connection refused on localhost Check main container is listening on correct port
Shared volume empty Files not appearing between containers Verify same volumeMount path in both containers
Pod shows 1/2 Ready One container keeps crashing kubectl logs <pod> -c <failing-container> --previous
Init order wrong Migration runs before DB ready Add wait-for-db init container FIRST in sequence

📋 Chapter 32 Summary: Multi-Container Patterns

  • 🤝 Sidecar — Helper container enhances main app (Nginx, Fluentd)
  • 🌐 Ambassador — Proxy container for external services (pgbouncer, Envoy)
  • 🔄 Adapter — Transform output to standard format (metrics exporter)
  • ⏳ Init Containers — Run before main containers (migrations, wait-for-db)
  • 🔗 Shared resources — Containers share localhost network + volumes

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Multi-Container Pods

⏱️ Target Time: 10 minutes
Scenario: Build pods using all multi-container patterns — this is one of the MOST tested CKAD topics.
  1. Create a pod sidecar-pod with:
    • Main container app (nginx) that writes to /var/log/nginx/access.log
    • Sidecar container logger (busybox) that runs tail -f /var/log/app/access.log
    • Shared emptyDir volume logs mounted at /var/log/nginx in app and /var/log/app in logger
  2. Create a pod init-pod with:
    • Init container setup (busybox) that runs: echo "initialized" > /work/status.txt
    • Main container app (nginx) that mounts the same volume at /usr/share/nginx/html
    • Shared emptyDir volume workdir
  3. Verify the init container completed before the main container started
  4. View logs from the logger sidecar container
  5. Exec into the main container of init-pod and verify the file exists
📖 View Solution
Solution — Multi-Container Pod Drill 
# 1. Sidecar pod
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sidecar-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  - name: logger
    image: busybox
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/app/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs
      mountPath: /var/log/app
  volumes:
  - name: logs
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# 2. Init container pod
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: init-pod
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: setup
    image: busybox
    command: ["sh", "-c", "echo initialized > /work/status.txt"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: workdir
      mountPath: /work
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: workdir
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumes:
  - name: workdir
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# 3. Verify init completed first
kubectl get pod init-pod  # Init:0/1 → PodInitializing → Running
kubectl describe pod init-pod | grep -A5 "Init Containers"

# 4. Sidecar logs
kubectl logs sidecar-pod -c logger

# 5. Verify init file exists
kubectl exec init-pod -c app -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/status.txt
# Output: initialized

Chapter 32 — Practice Questions

Q1
Name the three main multi-container pod patterns and explain when to use each.
Show Answer
Answer
PatternPurposeExampleLifecycle
SidecarExtend/enhance main containerLog shipper, proxy, config reloaderRuns alongside main
AmbassadorProxy outgoing connectionsDB proxy, API gatewayRuns alongside main
AdapterTransform output formatLog formatter, metrics exporterRuns alongside main
Init ContainerPre-flight setup tasksDB migration, config downloadRuns before main starts
Explanation

Multi-container pods share the same network namespace (localhost) and can share volumes. Sidecar is most common (log agents, service mesh proxies). Ambassador proxies external connections. Adapter transforms data for consumption. Init containers run sequentially before app containers start. On the CKAD, expect questions about all four patterns — especially sidecar and init containers.

Q2
Write a pod with a sidecar container that ships logs. The main app writes to a shared volume and the sidecar reads from it.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-log-sidecar
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
    - while true; do
        echo "$(date) - Processing request" >> /var/log/app.log;
        sleep 5;
      done
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log

  - name: log-shipper
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args: ["tail -f /var/log/app.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log

  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}
Explanation

The sidecar pattern: main app writes to /var/log/app.log, sidecar reads and ships it. They share an emptyDir volume. On the CKAD, this is a very common question pattern. Key points: both containers mount the same volume, emptyDir is created when the pod starts and deleted when it's removed.

Q3
Write a pod with an init container that downloads configuration before the main app starts.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-init
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: config-downloader
    image: busybox
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
    - |
      echo '{"db_host": "postgres", "port": 5432}' > /config/app.json
      echo "Config downloaded successfully"
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /config

  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-vol
      mountPath: /etc/app-config
      readOnly: true

  volumes:
  - name: config-vol
    emptyDir: {}
# Check init container status
kubectl get pod app-with-init
# NAME            READY   STATUS     RESTARTS   AGE
# app-with-init   0/1     Init:0/1   0          5s

# View init container logs
kubectl logs app-with-init -c config-downloader
Explanation

Init containers run to completion before main containers start. If an init container fails, K8s restarts the pod (respecting restartPolicy). Multiple init containers run sequentially. On the CKAD, use init containers for: downloading configs, waiting for dependencies (until nslookup service; do sleep 2; done), running DB migrations, setting file permissions.

Q4
How do containers within the same pod communicate? Explain networking and storage sharing.
Show Answer
Answer
  Same Pod = Shared Resources:

  ┌─────────────── Pod ───────────────┐
  │                                    │
  │  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐      │
  │  │ App      │   │ Sidecar  │      │
  │  │ :8080    │   │ :9090    │      │
  │  └────┬─────┘   └────┬─────┘      │
  │       │              │             │
  │  ─────┴──────────────┴─────        │
  │     Shared Network (localhost)     │
  │     Shared IP Address              │
  │                                    │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────┐       │
  │  │   Shared Volume         │       │
  │  │   (emptyDir / PVC)      │       │
  │  └─────────────────────────┘       │
  └────────────────────────────────────┘
  • Network: Containers share the pod IP. Communicate via localhost:PORT
  • Storage: Mount the same volume in both containers
  • Process: Can share process namespace with shareProcessNamespace: true
Explanation

Each pod has one IP address shared by all containers. Container A on :8080 can reach Container B on localhost:9090. No Service or DNS needed — they're on the same network namespace. On the CKAD, this is fundamental: when asked "how does the sidecar access the app?" the answer is always localhost.

Q5
Write a pod with an ambassador container that proxies database connections.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-ambassador
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:v1
    env:
    - name: DB_HOST
      value: "localhost"       # App connects to localhost
    - name: DB_PORT
      value: "5432"            # Ambassador listens here

  - name: db-proxy
    image: cloud-sql-proxy:latest   # Ambassador container
    command: ["/cloud-sql-proxy"]
    args:
    - "--port=5432"
    - "project:region:instance"
    ports:
    - containerPort: 5432
Explanation

The ambassador pattern: app connects to localhost:5432, the ambassador proxy handles the real connection (authentication, encryption, routing). Common real-world examples: Cloud SQL Proxy, Envoy sidecar, AWS RDS Proxy. On the CKAD, the ambassador abstracts external service complexity so the app only needs to know about localhost.

Q6
What happens if an init container fails? How does it affect the pod?
Show Answer
Answer
restartPolicyInit Container FailsPod Status
AlwaysInit container retried until successInit:Error → Init:CrashLoopBackOff
OnFailureInit container retried until successInit:Error → retries
NeverPod fails permanentlyInit:Error → Failed
 
# Pod with failed init container
kubectl get pod broken-init
# NAME         READY   STATUS       RESTARTS
# broken-init  0/1     Init:Error   3

# Debug init container
kubectl logs broken-init -c init-container-name
kubectl describe pod broken-init  # Check Events
Explanation

Init containers must succeed before main containers start. If an init container fails with restartPolicy: Always, K8s keeps retrying (with backoff).
