Main containers never start until ALL init containers succeed. On the CKAD, if a pod is stuck in Init:0/1, check init container logs: kubectl logs pod -c init-container-name.

Q7
Write a pod with an adapter container that converts application metrics to Prometheus format.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: app-with-adapter
  labels:
    app: legacy-api
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "9113"
spec:
  containers:
  - name: legacy-app
    image: legacy-app:v1
    # Outputs metrics in custom format to /metrics/raw
    volumeMounts:
    - name: metrics
      mountPath: /metrics

  - name: metrics-adapter          # Adapter container
    image: prom/adapter:latest
    command: ["/adapter"]
    args: ["--input=/metrics/raw", "--output-port=9113"]
    ports:
    - containerPort: 9113          # Prometheus scrapes this port
    volumeMounts:
    - name: metrics
      mountPath: /metrics
      readOnly: true

  volumes:
  - name: metrics
    emptyDir: {}
Explanation

The adapter pattern transforms data from one format to another. Here, a legacy app writes metrics in a custom format; the adapter converts to Prometheus format on port 9113. On the CKAD, the adapter pattern is less common than sidecar, but know the concept: it normalizes output for consumption by external systems.

Q8
How do you access logs from a specific container in a multi-container pod?
Show Answer
Answer
# Specify container with -c flag
kubectl logs my-pod -c app
kubectl logs my-pod -c sidecar
kubectl logs my-pod -c init-container-name

# Stream all containers simultaneously
kubectl logs my-pod --all-containers=true

# Follow logs from specific container
kubectl logs my-pod -c app -f

# Previous container logs (after restart)
kubectl logs my-pod -c app --previous

# Exec into specific container
kubectl exec my-pod -c sidecar -- cat /var/log/output.log

# Without -c flag: defaults to first container
# If multiple containers exist and no -c:
# "error: a]container name must be specified"
Explanation

The -c flag specifies the container in multi-container pods. Without it, kubectl usually errors (or defaults to the first container for some commands). On the CKAD, always use -c container-name when working with multi-container pods. Use --all-containers=true to see all logs interleaved.

Q9
Write an init container that waits for a database service to be available before the app starts.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-with-db-wait
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: wait-for-db
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
    - |
      echo "Waiting for database service..."
      until nslookup postgres-svc.default.svc.cluster.local; do
        echo "DB not ready, waiting 2 seconds..."
        sleep 2
      done
      echo "Database is available!"

  containers:
  - name: web
    image: myapp:v1
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_URL
      value: "postgres://postgres-svc:5432/mydb"
Explanation

This is the most common init container pattern on the CKAD. The init container loops with nslookup until the database Service's DNS resolves. Only then does the main app container start. The FQDN format is service.namespace.svc.cluster.local. On the CKAD, use busybox (available in exam) with nslookup or wget for health probes.

Q10
What is the difference between emptyDir, hostPath, and PersistentVolumeClaim for multi-container volume sharing?
Show Answer
Answer
Volume TypeLifetimeShared AcrossUse Case
emptyDirPod lifetimeContainers in same podTemp files, caches, inter-container data
hostPathNode lifetimePods on same nodeNode-level logs, Docker socket
PVCIndependent of podPods in same namespaceDatabases, persistent state
 
# emptyDir with memory backing (RAM disk)
volumes:
- name: cache
  emptyDir:
    medium: Memory       # Uses RAM (tmpfs)
    sizeLimit: 256Mi     # Limit memory usage
Explanation

For multi-container pods sharing data, emptyDir is the standard choice — it's created per-pod and cleaned up automatically. hostPath persists on the node but breaks portability (avoid in production). PVCs persist independently but add complexity. On the CKAD, use emptyDir for sidecar patterns (log sharing, config exchange) and PVCs for data that must survive pod restarts.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Sidecar & Multi-Container Security Risks

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% SYSTEM HARDENING 10%

Multi-container pods share network namespace, volumes, and IPC. This creates unique security risks — one compromised container can access data from all others in the pod.

Shared ResourceRiskMitigation
Network namespaceContainer can sniff localhost trafficUse TLS for inter-container communication
Shared volumesSidecar reads application secretsMount volumes read-only, use subPath
Process namespaceContainer sees other container's processesDon't enable shareProcessNamespace unless needed
IPC namespaceShared memory attack vectorsDon't enable hostIPC
 
# Security-hardened multi-container pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-multi
spec:
  # shareProcessNamespace: false  # Default — keep it!
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  initContainers:
  - name: init-setup
    image: busybox:1.36@sha256:abc123  # Pinned digest
    command: ["sh", "-c", "cp /config/* /shared/"]
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
      runAsUser: 10001
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-config
      mountPath: /shared
    - name: config-source
      mountPath: /config
      readOnly: true           # Read-only source
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0@sha256:def456
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
      runAsUser: 10001
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-config
      mountPath: /app/config
      readOnly: true           # App reads only
  - name: log-sidecar
    image: fluentd:1.16@sha256:ghi789
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
      runAsUser: 10002         # Different UID from app
    volumeMounts:
    - name: app-logs
      mountPath: /logs
      readOnly: true           # Sidecar only reads logs
  volumes:
  - name: shared-config
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: config-source
    configMap:
      name: app-config
  - name: app-logs
    emptyDir: {}

Chapter 32 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
A logging sidecar has write access to the application's data volume. Fix the security issue.
Show Answer
Answer
# BEFORE (insecure): sidecar has read-write access
containers:
- name: app
  volumeMounts:
  - name: data
    mountPath: /data
- name: log-sidecar
  volumeMounts:
  - name: data
    mountPath: /data     # Read-write — can modify app data!

# AFTER (secure): separate volumes + read-only
containers:
- name: app
  volumeMounts:
  - name: data
    mountPath: /data
  - name: logs
    mountPath: /var/log/app    # App writes logs here
- name: log-sidecar
  volumeMounts:
  - name: logs
    mountPath: /logs
    readOnly: true             # Sidecar only reads logs
  # No access to /data volume at all!
Explanation

Principle of least privilege: sidecars should only access what they need. On CKS, fix volume sharing issues by: (1) using separate volumes for different data types, (2) mounting as readOnly: true when only reading, (3) never giving sidecars access to application data volumes unless necessary.

CKS-2
Why is shareProcessNamespace: true a security risk and when is it acceptable?
Show Answer
Answer
# Risky: enables process visibility across containers
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: shared-pid
spec:
  shareProcessNamespace: true  # All containers see each other's processes
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
  - name: sidecar
    image: debug-sidecar:1.0
    # Can now: ps aux, kill processes, read /proc/*/environ
# Inside sidecar — can see app's processes and secrets
ps aux
# PID  COMMAND
# 1    /app/server
# 20   /sidecar

# Read app's environment variables (may contain secrets)
cat /proc/1/environ | tr '\0' '\n'

# Acceptable use cases:
# - Ephemeral debug containers (kubectl debug)
# - Process monitoring sidecars with proper RBAC
# - Signal forwarding (graceful shutdown)
Explanation

shareProcessNamespace breaks container process isolation. On CKS, it's risky because sidecars can read /proc/*/environ (environment secrets), kill processes, and access /proc/*/fd (file descriptors). Only enable for debugging or when containers genuinely need to see each other's PIDs. Default is false — keep it that way.

CKS-3
An init container runs as root to set up files. Harden it while keeping it functional.
Show Answer
Answer
# Hardened init container
initContainers:
- name: init-permissions
  image: busybox:1.36@sha256:abc123
  command:
  - sh
  - -c
  - |
    cp /source/* /data/
    chmod 644 /data/*
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 10001           # Non-root
    runAsGroup: 10001
    readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
    capabilities:
      drop: ["ALL"]
      # Only add CHOWN/FOWNER if absolutely needed
      # add: ["CHOWN"]
  resources:
    limits:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 64Mi
    requests:
      cpu: 50m
      memory: 32Mi
  volumeMounts:
  - name: data
    mountPath: /data
  - name: source
    mountPath: /source
    readOnly: true
Explanation

Init containers often run as root for setup tasks, but this violates the restricted PSA profile. On CKS, harden init containers: run as non-root, set readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop ALL capabilities (add back only what's needed like CHOWN), set resource limits, pin image digests. Init containers also need security contexts for PSA compliance.

CKS-4
How do you prevent a compromised sidecar from accessing the Kubernetes API?
Show Answer
Answer
# Disable SA token automount for the pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-sidecar-pod
spec:
  serviceAccountName: minimal-sa
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: myapp:1.0
    # Only app gets a projected token if needed
    volumeMounts:
    - name: app-token
      mountPath: /var/run/secrets/tokens
      readOnly: true
  - name: sidecar
    image: fluentd:1.16
    # NO token mount — cannot access K8s API
  volumes:
  - name: app-token
    projected:
      sources:
      - serviceAccountToken:
          path: token
          expirationSeconds: 3600
          audience: my-api
# Also: NetworkPolicy to block sidecar → API server
# The API server is typically at 10.96.0.1 or kubernetes.default
kubectl get svc kubernetes -o jsonpath='{.spec.clusterIP}'
Explanation

By default, all containers in a pod share the same SA token. On CKS, disable automountServiceAccountToken at the pod level, then selectively mount projected tokens only to containers that need API access. This follows least privilege — a compromised sidecar can't call the K8s API to escalate privileges.

CKS-5
How do you ensure each container in a multi-container pod runs with the minimum required capabilities?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: minimal-caps
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: web-server
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
        add: ["NET_BIND_SERVICE"]  # Only for port <1024
      runAsUser: 101               # nginx user
  - name: log-collector
    image: fluentd:1.16
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]             # No capabilities needed
      runAsUser: 999
  - name: metrics-exporter
    image: prom/node-exporter:1.7
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]             # No capabilities needed
      runAsUser: 65534            # nobody user
# Verify capabilities at runtime
kubectl exec minimal-caps -c web-server -- cat /proc/1/status | grep Cap
# CapEff should show only NET_BIND_SERVICE bit

# Decode capability bitmask
capsh --decode=0000000000000400
# = cap_net_bind_service
Explanation

Each container should run with the minimum capabilities. On CKS, always drop: ["ALL"] first, then add back only what's needed. Common additions: NET_BIND_SERVICE (bind ports <1024), CHOWN (change file ownership). Verify at runtime with /proc/1/status Cap fields. The PSA restricted profile enforces dropping ALL capabilities.

Chapter 33: Scheduling and Placement

PART 11 ADVANCED 📍 SCHEDULING

The Kubernetes scheduler decides which node runs each Pod. By default, it optimizes for resource utilization. But in production, you need finer control: run Pods on specific hardware, spread Pods across zones, or isolate workloads. This chapter covers all scheduling mechanisms.

🎯 CKAD Domain 4: Application Environment, Configuration & Security 25% of Exam — nodeSelector & Tolerations

The CKAD tests nodeSelector, tolerations, and basic nodeAffinity. You may be asked to schedule a pod on a specific node or to tolerate a taint.

Scheduling Commands for Speed

scheduling-essentials.sh 
# Label a node
kubectl label node worker-1 disktype=ssd

# Taint a node (NoSchedule = no new pods unless tolerated)
kubectl taint nodes worker-1 special=true:NoSchedule

# Remove a taint (note the minus sign)
kubectl taint nodes worker-1 special=true:NoSchedule-

# nodeSelector in pod spec (simplest approach)
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd

# Toleration in pod spec
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "special"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "true"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

🎯 Kubernetes Scheduling Decision Flow

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        KUBERNETES SCHEDULER DECISION TREE                            │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  New Pod Created
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  FILTERING PHASE: Eliminate nodes that CAN'T run this pod                        │
  │                                                                                   │
  │    ❌ Node Selector mismatch?        → Filter out                                │
  │    ❌ Required Node Affinity fails?  → Filter out                                │
  │    ❌ Taint without Toleration?      → Filter out                                │
  │    ❌ Not enough CPU/Memory?         → Filter out                                │
  │    ❌ PVC not available on node?     → Filter out                                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼  Nodes that pass all filters
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SCORING PHASE: Rank remaining nodes                                             │
  │                                                                                   │
  │    📊 Preferred Node Affinity        → +N points                                 │
  │    📊 Pod Affinity/Anti-Affinity     → +/- points                                │
  │    📊 Topology Spread                → Score by balance                           │
  │    📊 Resource efficiency            → Score by utilization                       │
  │    📊 Priority/Preemption            → Can preempt lower priority pods           │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │
       ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  HIGHEST SCORE  │ ← Pod scheduled here!
  │  NODE SELECTED  │
  └─────────────────┘
📊 Scheduling Methods at a Glance
MethodDefined OnPurposeFlexibility
Node Selector Pod Simple label matching Low (exact match only)
Node Affinity Pod Advanced label matching High (In, NotIn, Exists, etc.)
Pod Affinity Pod Co-locate with other Pods High
Taints/Tolerations Node/Pod Repel Pods unless tolerated High
Topology Spread Pod Distribute across zones Medium
Priority Pod Preempt lower priority Medium

Scheduling YAML — nodeSelector, Tolerations & Affinity

Know all 3 scheduling methods. nodeSelector is simplest, affinity is most flexible.

# 1. nodeSelector (simplest — exact match)
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd                  # ← Only nodes with this label
    zone: us-east-1a

# 2. Tolerations (allow scheduling on tainted nodes)
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "dedicated"
    operator: "Equal"              # ← Equal or Exists
    value: "gpu"
    effect: "NoSchedule"           # ← NoSchedule, PreferNoSchedule, NoExecute
  # Tolerate ALL taints (dangerous!):
  - operator: "Exists"             # ← No key = match everything

# 3. Node Affinity (advanced matching)
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:    # ← HARD rule
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
            operator: In            # ← In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt
            values: ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:   # ← SOFT preference
      - weight: 100
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: disktype
            operator: In
            values: ["ssd"]

# 4. Pod Anti-Affinity (spread pods across nodes)
spec:
  affinity:
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: app
            operator: In
            values: ["web"]
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname  # ← 1 pod per node

Scheduling Imperative Commands

ActionCommand
Label nodekubectl label node worker-1 disktype=ssd
Remove labelkubectl label node worker-1 disktype-
Show node labelskubectl get nodes --show-labels
Taint nodekubectl taint node worker-1 dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule
Remove taintkubectl taint node worker-1 dedicated=gpu:NoSchedule-
View taintskubectl describe node worker-1 | grep Taint
Cordon nodekubectl cordon worker-1 (marks unschedulable)
Uncordon nodekubectl uncordon worker-1
Drain nodekubectl drain worker-1 --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data

Scheduling Traps

  • ❌ Pod Pending forever → No node matches nodeSelector or affinity. Check: kubectl describe pod → Events
  • Taint effect types: NoSchedule (block new), PreferNoSchedule (soft), NoExecute (evict existing + block new)
  • Remove taint uses minus sign: kubectl taint node X key=val:effect- (note the trailing -)
  • required vs preferred: required = must match or pod stays Pending. preferred = best effort
  • nodeSelector is just syntactic sugar for requiredDuringScheduling node affinity with In operator
  • kubectl cordon prevents NEW pods but doesn't evict existing. kubectl drain evicts too

⏳ Scheduling Complexity Ladder

Simplest nodeSelector

Simple label matching: nodeSelector: {disktype: ssd}. Pod goes to node with that label.

Flexible Node Affinity

Rich expressions: In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt. Required vs Preferred.

Co-location Pod Affinity

Schedule near other pods: "put me on the same node/zone as pods with label app=cache"

Separation Pod Anti-Affinity

Schedule away from other pods: "don't put 2 replicas on the same node" (HA pattern)

Restriction Taints & Tolerations

Nodes repel pods unless pod has matching toleration. Used for dedicated/GPU/master nodes.

🏠 Node Affinity / nodeSelector
  • Pod chooses which NODES to run on
  • "I want to run on SSD nodes"
  • Based on node labels
  • Pod's perspective → "where do I go?"
🚫 Taints & Tolerations
  • Node repels pods it doesn't want
  • "Only GPU workloads allowed here"
  • Based on taint key=value:effect
  • Node's perspective → "who can come?"

33.1 Node Selectors

🏷️ Simplest Scheduling: Node Selectors

A nodeSelector is the simplest way to constrain Pods to nodes with specific labels. The Pod will ONLY be scheduled on nodes that have ALL the specified labels.

node-selector-example.yaml 
# First: Label your nodes
# kubectl label nodes node-1 disktype=ssd
# kubectl label nodes node-2 disktype=hdd

apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-api
spec:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # SIMPLE NODE SELECTOR: Only schedule on SSD nodes
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd      # Must have this EXACT label
    region: us-west-1  # AND this label (all must match)
  
  containers:
  - name: django
    image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
  nodeSelector: disktype=ssd
       │
       ▼
  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
  │   node-1     │  │   node-2     │  │   node-3     │
  │ disktype=ssd │  │ disktype=hdd │  │ disktype=ssd │
  │              │  │              │  │              │
  │   ✅ MATCH   │  │   ❌ SKIP    │  │   ✅ MATCH   │
  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
         │                                   │
         └─────────── Scheduler picks ───────┘
                   one of these two

33.2 Node Affinity

🧲 Node Affinity: Advanced Label Matching

Node Affinity extends nodeSelector with more expressive operators (In, NotIn, Exists, Gt, Lt) and support for soft/hard requirements. It's like nodeSelector on steroids.

🔴
requiredDuring​Scheduling​IgnoredDuring​Execution

HARD requirement: Pod WILL NOT schedule if no matching nodes

Think: "I MUST have this, or don't run me"
🟡
preferredDuring​Scheduling​IgnoredDuring​Execution

SOFT preference: Scheduler tries, but schedules anyway if no match

Think: "I'd PREFER this, but I can live without"
node-affinity.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
          # HARD REQUIREMENT: MUST match or Pod won't schedule
          # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: kubernetes.io/arch
                operator: In      # Values: In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt
                values:
                - amd64
                - arm64
              - key: node-type
                operator: NotIn
                values:
                - spot           # Exclude spot instances for reliability
          
          # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
          # SOFT PREFERENCE: TRY to match, but schedule anyway if not
          # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100         # Higher weight = stronger preference
            preference:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: disktype
                operator: In
                values:
                - ssd
          - weight: 50           # Lower priority preference
            preference:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: region
                operator: In
                values:
                - us-west-2
      
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest
🔤 Node Affinity Operators
OperatorMeaningExample
InLabel value in listarch In [amd64, arm64]
NotInLabel value not in listenv NotIn [production]
ExistsLabel key exists (any value)gpu Exists
DoesNotExistLabel key missinglegacy DoesNotExist
GtGreater thangpu-count Gt 2
LtLess thancpu-gen Lt 12

33.3 Pod Affinity and Anti-Affinity

🤝 Pod Affinity: Co-locate or Separate Pods

Pod Affinity schedules Pods based on where OTHER Pods are running. Co-locate Pods that communicate heavily (reduce latency) or spread Pods for high availability.

🤝
Pod Affinity

Schedule NEAR other matching Pods

"Run me on the same node as my Redis cache"
↔️
Pod Anti-Affinity

Schedule AWAY from other matching Pods

"Spread my replicas across different nodes"

📍 Pod Anti-Affinity: HA Deployment

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│     POD ANTI-AFFINITY: Spread anihpj replicas across nodes for HA                   │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  WITHOUT Anti-Affinity                   WITH Anti-Affinity
  (Scheduler clusters pods)               (Spread across nodes)
  
  ┌──────────────────────┐               ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐  ┌────────────┐
  │     Node-1           │               │   Node-1   │  │   Node-2   │  │   Node-3   │
  │  ┌───────┐ ┌───────┐│               │  ┌───────┐ │  │  ┌───────┐ │  │  ┌───────┐ │
  │  │anihpj │ │anihpj ││               │  │anihpj │ │  │  │anihpj │ │  │  │anihpj │ │
  │  └───────┘ └───────┘│               │  │  #1   │ │  │  │  #2   │ │  │  │  #3   │ │
  │  ┌───────┐          │               │  └───────┘ │  │  └───────┘ │  │  └───────┘ │
  │  │anihpj │          │               └────────────┘  └────────────┘  └────────────┘
  │  └───────┘          │
  └──────────────────────┘               ✅ If Node-1 fails, 2 replicas still running
  
  ❌ If Node-1 fails,
     ALL 3 replicas down!
pod-anti-affinity.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      affinity:
        # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        # POD ANTI-AFFINITY: Don't put 2 anihpj pods on same node
        # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - labelSelector:
              matchLabels:
                app: anihpj       # Don't co-locate with other anihpj pods
            topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname  # Per-node separation
        
        # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        # POD AFFINITY: Put anihpj pods NEAR redis pods
        # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
        podAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            podAffinityTerm:
              labelSelector:
                matchLabels:
                  app: redis
              topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest

33.4 Taints and Tolerations

🚫 Taints = "Keep Out" Sign on Nodes

Taints are applied to NODES and repel Pods. Tolerations are applied to PODS and allow them to be scheduled on tainted nodes. Think of it as: taints are "No Entry" signs, tolerations are "VIP passes."

🚪 Taints & Tolerations Visualized

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│               TAINTS (on Nodes) + TOLERATIONS (on Pods)                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  GPU Node (Tainted: gpu=true:NoSchedule)            Regular Node
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐           ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
  │        ⚠️ TAINTED NODE              │           │        Regular Node              │
  │        gpu=true:NoSchedule          │           │        (no taints)              │
  │                                     │           │                                  │
  │   anihpj Pod ────────▶ ❌ REJECTED  │           │   anihpj Pod ────────▶ ✅ OK    │
  │   (no toleration)                   │           │                                  │
  │                                     │           │   ml-training ────────▶ ✅ OK   │
  │   ml-training ────────▶ ✅ ALLOWED  │           │                                  │
  │   (has toleration:                  │           └─────────────────────────────────┘
  │    tolerations:                     │
  │    - key: gpu                       │
  │      operator: Equal                │
  │      value: "true"                  │
  │      effect: NoSchedule)            │
  │                                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────┘

  🏷️ Node Taint:  "Only ML workloads here"
  💳 Pod Toleration: "I'm an ML workload, let me in"
⚡ Taint Effects
EffectBehaviorUse Case
NoSchedule New Pods won't schedule (existing stay) Dedicated GPU nodes
PreferNoSchedule Avoid if possible (soft) Preferred isolation
NoExecute Evict existing Pods + ban new ones Node maintenance, draining
taints-and-tolerations.sh 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Taint a node (apply "No Entry" sign)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl taint nodes gpu-node-1 gpu=true:NoSchedule

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: View taints on a node
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl describe node gpu-node-1 | grep -A 2 Taints
# Taints:  gpu=true:NoSchedule

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Remove a taint (add minus at the end)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl taint nodes gpu-node-1 gpu=true:NoSchedule-
pod-with-toleration.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ml-training
spec:
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  # TOLERATION: "I can be scheduled on gpu=true nodes"
  # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  tolerations:
  - key: "gpu"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "true"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  
  # Also need to SELECT the GPU node (toleration alone isn't enough)
  nodeSelector:
    gpu: "true"
  
  containers:
  - name: training
    image: tensorflow/tensorflow:latest-gpu
    resources:
      limits:
        nvidia.com/gpu: 1

33.5 Topology Spread Constraints

🌍 Topology Spread: Distribute Across Zones

TopologySpreadConstraints distribute Pods evenly across failure domains (zones, regions, nodes). Essential for high availability across data centers.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│     TOPOLOGY SPREAD: Distribute 6 anihpj pods across 3 availability zones           │
│     maxSkew: 1 (difference between zones can be at most 1)                           │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────────┐
  │    Zone: us-east-1a     │  │    Zone: us-east-1b     │  │    Zone: us-east-1c     │
  │                         │  │                         │  │                         │
  │  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐   │  │  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐   │  │  ┌───────┐  ┌───────┐   │
  │  │anihpj │  │anihpj │   │  │  │anihpj │  │anihpj │   │  │  │anihpj │  │anihpj │   │
  │  │  #1   │  │  #2   │   │  │  │  #3   │  │  #4   │   │  │  │  #5   │  │  #6   │   │
  │  └───────┘  └───────┘   │  │  └───────┘  └───────┘   │  │  └───────┘  └───────┘   │
  │                         │  │                         │  │                         │
  │       (2 pods)          │  │       (2 pods)          │  │       (2 pods)          │
  └─────────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────┘  └─────────────────────────┘
         │                            │                            │
         └────────────────────────────┴────────────────────────────┘
                              Evenly distributed!
                              
  ✅ Zone failure = lose only 2/6 = 33% capacity
  ✅ maxSkew: 1 ensures balanced distribution
topology-spread.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  replicas: 6
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      # Spread across ZONES (e.g., us-east-1a, 1b, 1c)
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      - maxSkew: 1                                # Max difference between zones
        topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone  # Zone label on nodes
        whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule          # Hard requirement
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: anihpj
      
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      # Also spread across NODES within each zone
      # ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
      - maxSkew: 1
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname       # Node label
        whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway         # Soft (try best effort)
        labelSelector:
          matchLabels:
            app: anihpj
      
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest

33.6 Priority and Preemption

⚡ Priority: Critical Pods First

PriorityClasses assign importance levels to Pods. High-priority Pods can preempt (evict) lower-priority Pods when resources are scarce. Essential for running both production workloads and batch jobs on the same cluster.

  PREEMPTION IN ACTION
  ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

  Node (8 CPU capacity)
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                              │
  │  batch-job (priority=100)    batch-job (priority=100)       │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐         │
  │  │   4 CPU   🔵       │      │   4 CPU   🔵       │         │
  │  └────────────────────┘      └────────────────────┘         │
  │                                                              │
  │                 NEW: anihpj (priority=1000) wants 4 CPU      │
  │                      └─────────▶ PREEMPT! ⚡                  │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                          │
                          ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                                                              │
  │  anihpj (priority=1000) 🔴    batch-job (priority=100)      │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐      ┌────────────────────┐         │
  │  │   4 CPU            │      │   4 CPU   🔵       │         │
  │  └────────────────────┘      └────────────────────┘         │
  │                                                              │
  │  ❌ One batch job evicted to make room for critical anihpj   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
priority-classes.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Priority Classes (cluster-wide resource)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: critical-production
value: 1000000           # Higher = more important
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority  # Can evict lower priority pods
description: "Critical production workloads like anihpj API"
---
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: batch-jobs
value: 100
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: Never  # Cannot preempt others
description: "Batch jobs that can be evicted"
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Use PriorityClass in Deployment
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      priorityClassName: critical-production  # Reference the PriorityClass
      containers:
      - name: django
        image: ghcr.io/myorg/anihpj:latest

🧭 Scheduling Decision Guide: Which Method to Use?

                         WHAT DO YOU NEED?
                               │
          ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
          │                    │                    │
          ▼                    ▼                    ▼
  "Pod must run on        "Pod should/must        "Protect node from
   specific node type"    be near/far from        certain Pods"
          │               other Pods"                   │
          │                    │                        │
          ▼                    ▼                        ▼
  ┌───────────────┐   ┌────────────────┐      ┌────────────────┐
  │ Simple label? │   │ Near = Affinity│      │ Use TAINTS on  │
  │               │   │ Far = Anti-Aff │      │ nodes          │
  └───────┬───────┘   └───────┬────────┘      └───────┬────────┘
          │                   │                        │
    ┌─────┴─────┐      ┌──────┴──────┐          ┌──────┴───────┐
    │           │      │             │          │              │
    ▼           ▼      ▼             ▼          ▼              ▼
  YES          NO     MUST          PREFER   NoSchedule    NoExecute
    │           │      │             │          │              │
    ▼           ▼      ▼             ▼          │              │
┌────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌──────┐ ┌──────────┐    │    (Evicts existing
│ Node   │ │ Node   │ │required│ │preferred │    │     + blocks new)
│Selector│ │Affinity│ │During  │ │During    │    │
│        │ │        │ │Sched.. │ │Sched..   │    │
└────────┘ └────────┘ └──────┘ └──────────┘    │
                                                │
          FOR EVEN DISTRIBUTION ─────────────▶ topologySpreadConstraints
          FOR RESOURCE PRIORITY ─────────────▶ PriorityClass + preemption
kubectl Cheat Sheet: Scheduling and Placement 
# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Node Labels and Selectors
# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# View node labels
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# Add label to node
kubectl label node worker-1 disktype=ssd

# Remove label from node
kubectl label node worker-1 disktype-

# See which node a Pod was scheduled to
kubectl get pod anihpj -o wide

# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Taints and Tolerations
# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Add taint to node
kubectl taint nodes gpu-node gpu=true:NoSchedule

# Remove taint
kubectl taint nodes gpu-node gpu=true:NoSchedule-

# View taints on all nodes
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A3 "Taints:"

# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Debugging Scheduling Issues
# ═════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Why is my Pod Pending?
kubectl describe pod <pending-pod> | grep -A10 "Events:"
# Look for: "0/3 nodes are available: 1 node(s) didn't match 
#           Pod's node affinity/selector"

# Check PriorityClasses
kubectl get priorityclasses

# See Pod priority
kubectl get pod anihpj -o jsonpath='{.spec.priority}'

# Check topology spread
kubectl get pods -o wide --sort-by='.spec.nodeName'

📋 Chapter 33 Summary: Scheduling and Placement

  • 🏷️ Node Selector — Simple label matching (disktype=ssd)
  • 🧲 Node Affinity — Advanced matching with In, NotIn, Exists + soft/hard requirements
  • 🤝 Pod Affinity — Co-locate Pods that communicate (reduce latency)
  • ↔️ Pod Anti-Affinity — Spread Pods across nodes/zones (HA)
  • 🚫 Taints — "No Entry" signs on nodes, require toleration
  • 🌍 Topology Spread — Distribute Pods evenly across zones
  • ⚡ Priority — Critical Pods can preempt batch jobs

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Scheduling & Placement

⏱️ Target Time: 7 minutes
Scenario: Control where pods are placed using labels, nodeSelector, taints, and tolerations.
  1. Label a node with disktype=ssd (use kubectl get nodes to find a node name)
  2. Create a pod ssd-pod (nginx) that only runs on nodes with disktype=ssd using nodeSelector
  3. Verify the pod is scheduled on the correct node
  4. Taint a node with dedicated=special:NoSchedule
  5. Create a pod tolerant-pod (nginx) that tolerates the dedicated=special:NoSchedule taint
  6. Clean up: remove the taint and label
📖 View Solution
Solution — Scheduling Drill 
# 1. Label a node
NODE=$(kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')
kubectl label node $NODE disktype=ssd

# 2. Pod with nodeSelector
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ssd-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disktype: ssd
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
EOF

# 3. Verify placement
kubectl get pod ssd-pod -o wide   # Check NODE column

# 4. Taint a node
kubectl taint nodes $NODE dedicated=special:NoSchedule

# 5. Pod with toleration
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: tolerant-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "dedicated"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "special"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
EOF

# 6. Clean up
kubectl taint nodes $NODE dedicated=special:NoSchedule-
kubectl label node $NODE disktype-

Chapter 33 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is nodeSelector? Write a pod that runs only on nodes labeled disk=ssd.
Show Answer
Answer
# First, label the node
kubectl label node worker-1 disk=ssd
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: ssd-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    disk: ssd          # Simple key-value match
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
# Verify placement
kubectl get pod ssd-pod -o wide
# Check NODE column

# Remove label
kubectl label node worker-1 disk-
Explanation

nodeSelector is the simplest way to constrain pods to specific nodes. It matches node labels with exact equality. If no node matches, the pod stays Pending. On the CKAD, nodeSelector is faster to write than affinity rules. Use it for simple placement requirements like disk=ssd or gpu=true.

Q2
What is the difference between nodeSelector, nodeAffinity, and podAffinity?
Show Answer
Answer
FeaturenodeSelectornodeAffinitypodAffinity / podAntiAffinity
MatchesNode labels (exact)Node labels (expressive)Pod labels
Operators= onlyIn, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, LtIn, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist
Soft/HardHard onlyrequired + preferredrequired + preferred
Use caseSimple node selectionComplex node rulesCo-locate or spread pods
CKAD priorityHigh (fast)MediumMedium
Explanation

nodeSelector: simple, hard constraint on node labels. nodeAffinity: expressive (In, NotIn, Gt, Lt), supports soft preferences. podAffinity: schedule near certain pods. podAntiAffinity: schedule away from certain pods. On the CKAD, use nodeSelector when possible (fewer lines), switch to affinity for complex requirements.

Q3
Write a pod with nodeAffinity that prefers nodes in zone us-east-1a but allows other zones.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: zone-preferred
spec:
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - weight: 80
        preference:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
            operator: In
            values:
            - us-east-1a
      # Hard rule: must be a Linux node
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: kubernetes.io/os
            operator: In
            values:
            - linux
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
Explanation

preferred is a soft constraint — scheduler tries to honor it but schedules elsewhere if needed. required is hard — pod stays Pending if no matching node. Weight (1-100) determines priority among multiple preferred rules. On the CKAD, combine required (must-have) with preferred (nice-to-have) for realistic scheduling rules.

Q4
What are taints and tolerations? How do they work together?
Show Answer
Answer
# Taint a node (repels pods)
kubectl taint node worker-1 env=prod:NoSchedule

# Three taint effects:
# NoSchedule    — Don't schedule new pods (existing stay)
# PreferNoSchedule — Try to avoid, but allow if needed
# NoExecute     — Evict existing pods + block new ones
# Pod with toleration (can schedule on tainted node)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: prod-pod
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: "env"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "prod"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx

# Tolerate ANY taint (dangerous, but useful for DaemonSets):
# tolerations:
# - operator: "Exists"
# Remove taint (add minus at end)
kubectl taint node worker-1 env=prod:NoSchedule-
Explanation

Taints repel pods; tolerations allow specific pods on tainted nodes. Think: taint = "keep out" sign, toleration = "VIP pass". Control plane nodes are tainted with node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule. On the CKAD, know how to taint nodes, write tolerations, and remove taints (trailing minus). Common combination: taint GPU nodes, only GPU workloads tolerate.

Q5
How do you schedule a pod on a specific node by name?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: nodeName (bypasses scheduler entirely)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: pinned-pod
spec:
  nodeName: worker-2    # Direct assignment
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx

---
# Method 2: nodeSelector with hostname label
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: labeled-pod
spec:
  nodeSelector:
    kubernetes.io/hostname: worker-2
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
Explanation

nodeName bypasses the scheduler completely — no affinity, taints, or resource checks apply. nodeSelector with kubernetes.io/hostname goes through the scheduler (safer, validates resources). On the CKAD, prefer nodeSelector unless explicitly asked for nodeName. nodeName is faster to write but less safe.

Q6
What is PriorityClass and how does it affect pod scheduling and eviction?
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: scheduling.k8s.io/v1
kind: PriorityClass
metadata:
  name: high-priority
value: 1000000              # Higher = more important
globalDefault: false
preemptionPolicy: PreemptLowerPriority
description: "Critical production workloads"

---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: critical-pod
spec:
  priorityClassName: high-priority
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
# Built-in priority classes
kubectl get priorityclasses
# system-cluster-critical  2000000000
# system-node-critical     2000001000
Explanation

PriorityClass assigns importance to pods. Higher priority pods can preempt (evict) lower priority pods when resources are scarce. System pods use system-cluster-critical (highest). preemptionPolicy: Never allows priority-based queuing without evicting others. On the CKAD, know that priority affects scheduling order and eviction decisions under resource pressure.

Q7
How do you view node labels and find which nodes match a selector?
Show Answer
Answer
# Show all labels on all nodes
kubectl get nodes --show-labels

# Filter nodes by label
kubectl get nodes -l disk=ssd
kubectl get nodes -l 'topology.kubernetes.io/zone in (us-east-1a,us-east-1b)'

# Describe node (shows labels, taints, capacity)
kubectl describe node worker-1

# Add a label
kubectl label node worker-1 env=production

# Overwrite existing label
kubectl label node worker-1 env=staging --overwrite

# Remove a label
kubectl label node worker-1 env-

# Common built-in labels:
# kubernetes.io/hostname
# kubernetes.io/os
# kubernetes.io/arch
# topology.kubernetes.io/zone
# topology.kubernetes.io/region
# node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
Explanation

Node labels drive scheduling decisions. Use --show-labels to see all labels, -l to filter. On the CKAD, always check existing node labels before writing nodeSelector/nodeAffinity — the label may already exist (e.g., kubernetes.io/hostname). Know how to add, overwrite, and remove labels with kubectl label.

Q8
Write a DaemonSet that tolerates all taints (including control-plane nodes).
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: DaemonSet
metadata:
  name: node-monitor
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: node-monitor
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: node-monitor
    spec:
      tolerations:
      - operator: "Exists"    # Tolerates ALL taints
      containers:
      - name: monitor
        image: prom/node-exporter:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9100
# Verify it runs on ALL nodes (including control plane)
kubectl get pods -l app=node-monitor -o wide
# Should see one pod per node
Explanation

operator: "Exists" with no key tolerates every taint — the universal pass. This is standard for monitoring agents (node-exporter) and log collectors that must run everywhere. On the CKAD, remember: DaemonSets auto-deploy to new nodes. Combining with tolerations: [{operator: Exists}] ensures coverage on ALL nodes including control-plane.

Q9
What is pod preemption? When does it happen?
Show Answer
Answer
  Preemption Flow:

  1. High-priority pod can't be scheduled (no resources)
  2. Scheduler finds node with lower-priority pods
  3. Lower-priority pods are evicted (gracefully)
  4. High-priority pod takes the freed resources
  
  Node capacity: 4 CPU
  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
  │ low-pri (1 CPU) │ low-pri (1 CPU) │ med-pri (2 CPU) │
  └─────────────────────────────────┘
           ↓ High-priority pod (3 CPU) arrives
  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
  │ HIGH-PRI (3 CPU)      │ med-pri (1 CPU)   │ ← low-pri evicted
  └─────────────────────────────────┘
Explanation

Preemption evicts lower-priority pods to make room for higher-priority ones. K8s tries to find a node where the fewest pods need eviction. Preempted pods get their gracePeriod but are marked for deletion. On the CKAD, understand that PriorityClass affects both scheduling order (higher priority scheduled first) and preemption behavior (can evict lower priority).

Q10
A pod is stuck in Pending with event 0/3 nodes available: 1 node had untolerated taint, 2 insufficient cpu. How do you fix it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Step 1: Understand the message
# "1 node had untolerated taint" → 1 node is tainted
# "2 insufficient cpu" → 2 nodes don't have enough CPU

# Step 2: Check node taints
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A2 Taints

# Step 3: Check CPU capacity vs requests
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A5 "Allocated resources"

# Fix options:
# Option A: Add toleration for the tainted node
# Option B: Reduce CPU requests
kubectl set resources deployment/web --requests=cpu=100m

# Option C: Scale down other pods to free resources
kubectl scale deployment other-app --replicas=1

# Option D: Remove the taint
kubectl taint node worker-1 key:NoSchedule-

# Option E: Add more nodes (if cloud autoscaler available)
Explanation

This Pending error means the scheduler checked all 3 nodes: 1 was rejected due to taints, 2 due to insufficient CPU. Fix by either tolerating the taint (gaining access to that node) or reducing resource requests. On the CKAD, always read scheduling error messages carefully — they tell you exactly why each node was rejected. Use kubectl describe pod → Events to see the message.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Taint-Based Security Isolation & Node Security Zones

CLUSTER HARDENING 15% CLUSTER SETUP 15%

Scheduling controls determine WHERE pods run. Taints, node affinity, and topology constraints create security zones — isolating sensitive workloads on dedicated, hardened nodes.

MechanismSecurity Use CaseDirection
Taints + TolerationsRestrict nodes to specific workloadsNode repels pods
Node AffinityPin sensitive pods to hardened nodesPod selects nodes
Pod Anti-AffinityPrevent co-location of sensitive podsPod avoids other pods
RuntimeClassRun pods in sandboxed runtimes (gVisor/Kata)Pod selects runtime
NodeRestrictionPrevent nodes from modifying other nodes' podsAPI server admission
 
# Create security-tiered nodes
# Tier 1: Highly sensitive (PCI-DSS, financial)
kubectl taint nodes secure-node-1 security-tier=pci:NoSchedule
kubectl label nodes secure-node-1 security-zone=pci

# Tier 2: Standard production
kubectl label nodes prod-node-1 security-zone=standard

# Tier 3: Development/testing
kubectl label nodes dev-node-1 security-zone=dev
# Pod that MUST run on PCI-compliant nodes
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: payment-service
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: security-tier
    value: pci
    operator: Equal
    effect: NoSchedule
  affinity:
    nodeAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
        nodeSelectorTerms:
        - matchExpressions:
          - key: security-zone
            operator: In
            values: ["pci"]
    podAntiAffinity:
      requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
      - labelSelector:
          matchExpressions:
          - key: security-zone
            operator: NotIn
            values: ["pci"]
        topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
  containers:
  - name: payment
    image: payment:1.0@sha256:abc123
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      runAsNonRoot: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
# RuntimeClass for sandboxed execution (gVisor)
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: gvisor
handler: runsc
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    runtime: gvisor
---
# Pod using sandboxed runtime
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: untrusted-workload
spec:
  runtimeClassName: gvisor     # Run in gVisor sandbox
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: untrusted-app:1.0

Chapter 33 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create a taint on node secure-node so only pods with a specific toleration can be scheduled there.
Show Answer
Answer
# Add taint to the secure node
kubectl taint nodes secure-node \
  security-level=high:NoSchedule

# Label the node for affinity rules
kubectl label nodes secure-node \
  security-zone=restricted

# Verify taint
kubectl describe node secure-node | grep Taints
# Expected: security-level=high:NoSchedule
# Pod that can run on the tainted node
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  tolerations:
  - key: security-level
    value: high
    operator: Equal
    effect: NoSchedule
  nodeSelector:
    security-zone: restricted
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: secure-app:1.0
# Verify: pod without toleration gets rejected
kubectl run test --image=nginx --dry-run=client -o yaml | \
  kubectl apply -f -
# Pod stays Pending — node is tainted

# Verify: secure-app is scheduled on secure-node
kubectl get pod secure-app -o wide
# NODE column should show secure-node
Explanation

Taints with NoSchedule prevent untolerated pods from being scheduled. On CKS, use taints to create security zones — sensitive workloads on dedicated nodes that reject general pods. Combine with nodeSelector or nodeAffinity to ensure the pod targets the correct node AND has the toleration to run there.

CKS-2
Configure a RuntimeClass to run untrusted workloads in a gVisor sandbox.
Show Answer
Answer
# Create RuntimeClass for gVisor
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: gvisor
handler: runsc          # gVisor handler name
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    sandbox-runtime: gvisor   # Only nodes with gVisor installed
---
# Use the RuntimeClass in a pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sandboxed-pod
spec:
  runtimeClassName: gvisor
  containers:
  - name: untrusted
    image: untrusted-app:1.0
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      runAsNonRoot: true
# Verify the pod is using gVisor
kubectl get pod sandboxed-pod -o jsonpath='{.spec.runtimeClassName}'
# Expected: gvisor

# Inside the pod — verify kernel is gVisor (not Linux)
kubectl exec sandboxed-pod -- dmesg | head
# Shows "gVisor" kernel messages instead of Linux
Explanation

RuntimeClass lets you run pods with different container runtimes. On CKS, gVisor (runsc) provides a user-space kernel that intercepts syscalls — attackers can't exploit host kernel vulnerabilities. Kata Containers use lightweight VMs. Use RuntimeClass for untrusted multi-tenant workloads where container breakout is a concern.

CKS-3
How does the NodeRestriction admission plugin improve cluster security?
Show Answer
Answer
# Verify NodeRestriction is enabled
kubectl -n kube-system get pod kube-apiserver-master \
  -o yaml | grep enable-admission
# Should include: NodeRestriction

# What NodeRestriction prevents:
# 1. Nodes can only modify their OWN Node object
# 2. Nodes can only modify pods bound to THEIR node
# 3. Nodes cannot add labels with node-restriction.kubernetes.io/ prefix
# 4. Prevents compromised node from affecting other nodes

# If NOT enabled, add to kube-apiserver.yaml:
--enable-admission-plugins=NodeRestriction,...

# Test: try to label another node from a compromised node
# (This should fail with NodeRestriction enabled)
# On the node, using the kubelet's credentials:
curl -X PATCH https://api-server:6443/api/v1/nodes/other-node \
  --cert /var/lib/kubelet/pki/kubelet-client-current.pem
# Expected: Forbidden
Explanation

NodeRestriction limits what kubelets can do via the API. Without it, a compromised node's kubelet could modify pods on other nodes or change labels to attract sensitive workloads. On CKS, verify it's in --enable-admission-plugins. It prevents lateral movement from node compromise — critical for multi-tenant clusters.

CKS-4
Use pod anti-affinity to ensure replicas of a sensitive service don't co-locate on the same node.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: secret-manager
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secret-manager
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: secret-manager
    spec:
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - labelSelector:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: app
                operator: In
                values: ["secret-manager"]
            topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname
      containers:
      - name: secret-manager
        image: vault:1.15
        securityContext:
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
# Verify pods are on different nodes
kubectl get pods -l app=secret-manager -o wide
# Each pod should be on a different NODE
Explanation

Pod anti-affinity prevents co-location. On CKS, spreading sensitive pods across nodes limits blast radius — if one node is compromised, only one replica is affected. requiredDuringScheduling is hard anti-affinity (won't schedule if no valid node). topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname means one pod per node.

CKS-5
How do you prevent a pod from being scheduled on control plane nodes?
Show Answer
Answer
# Control plane nodes have this taint by default:
kubectl describe node master | grep Taints
# node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule

# If missing, add it:
kubectl taint nodes master \
  node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule

# Verify no user pods are on control plane
kubectl get pods -A -o wide --field-selector spec.nodeName=master

# Only system pods should be there:
# - kube-apiserver
# - kube-controller-manager
# - kube-scheduler
# - etcd
# - coredns (with toleration)
# - kube-proxy (with toleration)

# Remove a taint if needed (careful!)
kubectl taint nodes master \
  node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule-
Explanation

Control plane nodes should only run system components. The default taint node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane:NoSchedule prevents user pods from being scheduled there. On CKS, verify this taint exists. System pods (kube-proxy, CNI, CoreDNS) have built-in tolerations. User pods on control plane nodes can access etcd and API server locally — a critical security risk.

Chapter 34: Service Mesh (Istio / Linkerd)

PART 11 ADVANCED 🕸️ NETWORKING

As your architecture grows beyond a handful of services, managing service-to-service communication becomes complex. A service mesh handles mTLS encryption, traffic management, retries, and observability — without changing your application code.

☁️ Beyond CKAD Scope — Advanced Networking Background Knowledge

Service meshes (Istio/Linkerd) are NOT on the CKAD exam. However, understanding sidecar proxies reinforces the multi-container sidecar pattern (Domain 1), which is testable.

🕸️ What a Service Mesh Does

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        WITHOUT SERVICE MESH                                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌────────────┐   HTTP (unencrypted)   ┌────────────┐   HTTP   ┌────────────┐
  │  anihpj    │───────────────────────▶│  payments  │─────────▶│  postgres  │
  │  Django    │                        │  service   │          │  database  │
  └────────────┘                        └────────────┘          └────────────┘
  
  ❌ No encryption between services
  ❌ No automatic retries
  ❌ No traffic visibility/tracing
  ❌ Manual load balancing


┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        WITH SERVICE MESH                                             │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────────────────────────┐       ┌─────────────────────────────┐
  │          Pod                 │       │          Pod                 │
  │  ┌────────────┐  ┌───────┐  │       │  ┌────────────┐  ┌───────┐  │
  │  │  anihpj    │◄─│ Proxy │──│──🔒──│──│ Proxy │──▶│ payments  │  │
  │  │  Django    │  │(Envoy)│  │ mTLS  │  │(Envoy)│   │  service  │  │
  │  └────────────┘  └───────┘  │       │  └───────┘   └────────────┘  │
  └─────────────────────────────┘       └─────────────────────────────┘
                        │                               │
                        └───────────────────────────────┘
                               Control Plane (Istiod)
                               • Certificate management
                               • Traffic policies
                               • Telemetry collection

  ✅ Automatic mTLS encryption
  ✅ Automatic retries + circuit breakers
  ✅ Distributed tracing (Jaeger/Zipkin)
  ✅ Traffic splitting (canary, A/B)
🛡️ Service Mesh Features
🔒
mTLS

Automatic encryption between all services

🔄
Traffic Management

Canary, A/B, mirroring, retries

📊
Observability

Metrics, traces, service graphs

🛡️
Security Policies

AuthN/AuthZ at network level

🏗️ Service Mesh Architecture

📊 Control Plane (istiod / linkerd-control) Configuration, certificate management, policy distribution, telemetry aggregation
🔀 Data Plane (Envoy / linkerd-proxy sidecars) Proxy sidecar injected into every pod — intercepts all inbound/outbound traffic
📦 Application Pods Your services run unchanged — mesh is transparent. No code modifications needed
🌐 Network (CNI Plugin) Underlying pod-to-pod networking — Calico, Cilium, or cloud VPC

🔧 What a Service Mesh Provides

🔒
mTLS Encryption — Automatic mutual TLS between all services. Zero-trust networking
📊
Observability — Metrics, traces, and service graphs without code changes
🚦
Traffic Management — Canary deployments, traffic splitting, circuit breaking, retries
🛡️
Security Policies — Authorization rules, rate limiting at network level

34.1 What is a Service Mesh?

🕸️ Service Mesh Definition

A service mesh is a dedicated infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication. It's implemented as a network of lightweight proxies (sidecars) deployed alongside your application containers, controlled by a central control plane.

🏗️ Service Mesh Architecture

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                             SERVICE MESH ARCHITECTURE                                │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                            CONTROL PLANE                                         │
  │                                                                                   │
  │  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐        │
  │  │  Certificate │  │   Traffic    │  │   Policy     │  │  Telemetry   │        │
  │  │  Authority   │  │   Config     │  │   Engine     │  │  Collector   │        │
  │  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘        │
  │                                                                                   │
  │                         Istiod (or Linkerd Control Plane)                        │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
              │                    │                    │                    │
              │                    │     Config Push    │                    │
              ▼                    ▼                    ▼                    ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                             DATA PLANE                                           │
  │                                                                                   │
  │  ┌──────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────┐                        │
  │  │        Pod            │      │        Pod            │                        │
  │  │  ┌──────┐  ┌───────┐ │      │  ┌──────┐  ┌───────┐ │                        │
  │  │  │anihpj│◄─│ Envoy │◄│──────│─▶│ Envoy│──▶│ auth  │ │                        │
  │  │  └──────┘  │ Proxy │ │ mTLS │  │ Proxy│   │service│ │                        │
  │  │            └───────┘ │      │  └───────┘  └──────┘ │                        │
  │  └──────────────────────┘      └──────────────────────┘                        │
  │                                                                                   │
  │                    Sidecar proxies handle ALL traffic                            │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🧩 Service Mesh Components
ComponentRoleIstio Implementation
Control Plane Configure proxies, issue certs, collect telemetry Istiod
Data Plane Sidecar proxies that intercept all traffic Envoy proxies
Sidecar Injection Auto-inject proxy into every Pod MutatingWebhook
CRDs Custom resources for traffic rules VirtualService, DestinationRule

34.2 When Do You Need a Service Mesh?

⚠️ Service Mesh Adds Complexity

A service mesh is powerful but adds latency, memory overhead, and operational complexity. Don't add it just because it's trendy. Use the decision flowchart below.

🤔 Do I Need a Service Mesh?

                              START
                                │
                                ▼
                  ┌───────────────────────────┐
                  │ How many microservices?   │
                  └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                                │
              ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
              │                 │                 │
              ▼                 ▼                 ▼
         < 5 services     5-20 services      20+ services
              │                 │                 │
              ▼                 ▼                 ▼
         ❌ NO MESH       MAYBE...           ✅ YES
         Use Ingress      Answer more ──────▶ Strong case
                                │                 for mesh
                                ▼
                  ┌───────────────────────────┐
                  │ Need mTLS between all     │
                  │ services (compliance)?    │
                  └─────────────┬─────────────┘
                                │
              ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
              ▼                 ▼                 │
             YES               NO                 │
              │                 │                 │
              ▼                 ▼                 ▼
         ✅ YES            Need traffic      ❌ NO MESH
         Mesh makes        splitting/canary? Use NetworkPolicy
         mTLS easy              │              for security
                                ▼
                           YES → ✅ YES (Mesh)
                           NO  → Consider alternatives
✅
Good Use Cases
  • 20+ microservices talking to each other
  • Compliance requiring mTLS everywhere
  • Need canary/A/B deployments at network level
  • Debugging latency in distributed system
  • Zero-trust security model
❌
Skip Service Mesh If
  • Monolith or < 5 services
  • Team is new to Kubernetes
  • Don't need mTLS internally
  • Can't spare 100-200MB per Pod overhead
  • Latency-sensitive, every ms matters

34.3 Istio Overview

🌐 Istio: The Feature-Rich Service Mesh

Istio is the most popular service mesh, backed by Google, IBM, and Lyft. It uses Envoy proxies as sidecars and provides comprehensive traffic management, security, and observability features.

Installing Istio 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Download istioctl
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -L https://istio.io/downloadIstio | sh -
cd istio-1.20.0
export PATH=$PWD/bin:$PATH

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Install Istio (demo profile for learning)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
istioctl install --set profile=demo -y
# Profiles: minimal, default, demo, empty

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Enable sidecar injection for namespace
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl label namespace production istio-injection=enabled
# Now any Pod created in 'production' gets Envoy sidecar!

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Verify installation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get pods -n istio-system
# NAME                      READY   STATUS
# istiod-xxx                1/1     Running
# istio-ingressgateway-xxx  1/1     Running
istio-canary-deployment.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# VirtualService: Route traffic between versions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  hosts:
  - anihpj         # Service name
  http:
  - match:
    - headers:
        x-canary:
          exact: "true"
    route:
    - destination:
        host: anihpj
        subset: v2   # New version for canary users
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: anihpj
        subset: v1   # Stable version (90% traffic)
      weight: 90
    - destination:
        host: anihpj
        subset: v2   # Canary version (10% traffic)
      weight: 10
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DestinationRule: Define subsets (versions)
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: anihpj
spec:
  host: anihpj
  trafficPolicy:
    connectionPool:
      tcp:
        maxConnections: 100
      http:
        http1MaxPendingRequests: 100
  subsets:
  - name: v1
    labels:
      version: v1
  - name: v2
    labels:
      version: v2

🛠️ Istio Key Features

  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────┐
  │ 🚦 TRAFFIC      │  │ 🔒 SECURITY     │  │ 📊 OBSERVABILITY│  │ 🔌 EXTENSIBILITY│
  │    MANAGEMENT   │  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │
  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │  │                 │
  │ • VirtualService│  │ • Auto mTLS     │  │ • Prometheus    │  │ • Wasm plugins  │
  │ • DestinationRule│ │ • AuthPolicy   │  │ • Grafana       │  │ • EnvoyFilter   │
  │ • Canary/A-B    │  │ • PeerAuthn    │  │ • Jaeger traces │  │ • Custom auth   │
  │ • Retries       │  │ • JWT validation│ │ • Kiali graph   │  │                 │
  │ • Circuit break │  │ • RBAC          │  │ • Access logs   │  │                 │
  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘  └─────────────────┘

34.4 Linkerd (Lightweight Alternative)

🔗 Linkerd: Simpler, Lighter, CNCF Graduated

Linkerd is a CNCF graduated service mesh focused on simplicity and performance. It uses a Rust-based proxy (lighter than Envoy) and has fewer features but lower operational overhead.

⚔️ Istio vs Linkerd Comparison
FeatureIstioLinkerd
Proxy Envoy (C++) Linkerd2-proxy (Rust)
Memory per Pod ~100-200 MB ~20-50 MB
Latency Overhead ~2-5 ms ~1-2 ms
mTLS ✅ Full control ✅ Always on (simpler)
Traffic Splitting ✅ VirtualService CRD ✅ TrafficSplit CRD
Web UI Kiali (separate install) ✅ Built-in dashboard
Learning Curve Steep (many CRDs) Gentle (minimal config)
Best For Complex traffic rules, Envoy expertise Quick setup, low overhead
Installing Linkerd 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1: Install linkerd CLI
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -sL run.linkerd.io/install | sh
export PATH=$PATH:$HOME/.linkerd2/bin

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2: Pre-flight check
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
linkerd check --pre

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3: Install Linkerd CRDs and control plane
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
linkerd install --crds | kubectl apply -f -
linkerd install | kubectl apply -f -

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4: Verify installation
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
linkerd check

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 5: Inject into existing deployment
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl get deploy anihpj -o yaml | linkerd inject - | kubectl apply -f -

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 6: Open dashboard
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
linkerd viz install | kubectl apply -f -
linkerd viz dashboard
📊 Service Mesh Observability Stack
📈
Kiali

Service graph visualization, traffic flow, health status

🔄
Jaeger

Distributed tracing, request latency, span analysis

📊
Grafana

Dashboards for golden signals (latency, traffic, errors)

🔍
Prometheus

Metrics collection from Envoy sidecars

🔧 Troubleshooting Service Mesh Issues
ProblemSymptomsSolution
Sidecar not injected Pod has 1/1 containers (no proxy) Check istio-injection=enabled label on namespace
503 errors after mesh Services can't communicate Check DestinationRule, verify mTLS mode matches
High latency Requests slower after mesh Use Jaeger to identify slow spans; check sidecar resources
Traffic not split Canary not getting traffic Verify VirtualService weight and subset labels match
istioctl Debug Commands 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Istio Debugging Commands
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Check if sidecar is injected
kubectl get pod anihpj-xxx -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# Should include: istio-proxy

# Analyze Istio configuration for issues
istioctl analyze -n production
# Reports misconfigurations!

# Check Envoy configuration for a Pod
istioctl proxy-config routes anihpj-xxx -n production

# View incoming/outgoing traffic rules
istioctl proxy-config listeners anihpj-xxx

# Debug connectivity between services
istioctl proxy-status

# Access Envoy admin dashboard (debug)
kubectl exec -it anihpj-xxx -c istio-proxy -- pilot-agent request GET stats

📋 Chapter 34 Summary: Service Mesh

  • 🕸️ Service Mesh — Infrastructure layer for service-to-service communication
  • 🏗️ Architecture — Control plane (config) + Data plane (sidecar proxies)
  • 🔒 mTLS — Automatic encryption between all services
  • 🚦 Traffic Management — Canary, A/B, retries, circuit breakers
  • 🌐 Istio — Feature-rich, Envoy proxies, complex but powerful
  • 🔗 Linkerd — CNCF graduated, lightweight, easier to operate
  • ⚠️ When to Use — 20+ services, mTLS requirement, distributed tracing need

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Sidecar Pattern (Service Mesh Context)

⏱️ Target Time: 4 minutes
Scenario: Service meshes inject sidecar proxies. Although Istio is not on the exam, the sidecar pattern is. Create a pod that simulates a service mesh sidecar.
  1. Create a pod mesh-sim with:
    • Main container app (nginx, port 80)
    • Sidecar container proxy (envoyproxy/envoy:v1.28-latest, port 15001)
  2. Verify both containers are running
  3. Access the main app via the sidecar's localhost network (exec into proxy, curl localhost:80)
📖 View Solution
Solution — Sidecar Pattern Drill 
# 1. Create pod with sidecar proxy
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: mesh-sim
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx
    ports:
    - containerPort: 80
  - name: proxy
    image: envoyproxy/envoy:v1.28-latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 15001
EOF

# 2. Verify both running
kubectl get pod mesh-sim
# Should show 2/2 Running

# 3. Containers share localhost
kubectl exec mesh-sim -c proxy -- wget -qO- localhost:80
# Returns nginx welcome page — proves shared network namespace

Chapter 34 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is a service mesh? What problems does it solve?
Show Answer
Answer
  Without Service Mesh:          With Service Mesh:
  
  ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐        ┌───────┐    ┌───────┐
  │ App A │───▶│ App B │        │ App A │    │ App B │
  │       │    │       │        │ ┌───┐ │    │ ┌───┐ │
  └───────┘    └───────┘        │ │ P │─┼────┼─│ P │ │
  App handles:                  │ └───┘ │    │ └───┘ │
  - retries                     └───────┘    └───────┘
  - TLS                         P = Proxy (sidecar)
  - tracing                     Proxy handles:
  - circuit breaking            - mTLS, retries, tracing
                                - circuit breaking, load balancing
Explanation

A service mesh adds a proxy sidecar to every pod, handling networking concerns transparently. App code doesn't change. Benefits: automatic mTLS encryption, traffic management, observability (distributed tracing), circuit breaking, retries. On the CKAD, service mesh is not directly tested, but understanding sidecar injection helps with multi-container pod pattern questions.

Q2
Compare Istio and Linkerd. When would you choose each?
Show Answer
Answer
FeatureIstioLinkerd
ComplexityComplex (many CRDs)Simple (minimal config)
ProxyEnvoylinkerd2-proxy (Rust)
PerformanceHigher latency overheadUltra-low latency
FeaturesFull-featured (traffic mgmt, security, observability)Focused (mTLS, metrics, retries)
Learning curveSteepGentle
Best forLarge enterprises, complex routingSimplicity, low overhead
CNCF statusGraduatedGraduated
Explanation

Istio: feature-rich but complex, uses Envoy proxy, supports advanced traffic routing (A/B testing, canary, fault injection). Linkerd: lightweight, fast, simple to install, uses a purpose-built Rust proxy. Choose Istio for complex multi-service architectures, Linkerd for simplicity and performance. On the CKAD, focus on the sidecar proxy pattern concept rather than specific mesh implementations.

Q3
What is mutual TLS (mTLS) and how does a service mesh implement it?
Show Answer
Answer
  Regular TLS:
  Client ───────▶ Server
  Client verifies server cert only
  
  Mutual TLS (mTLS):
  Client ◀──────▶ Server
  Both verify each other's certificates
  
  Service Mesh mTLS:
  ┌────────┐  encrypted  ┌────────┐
