  │ App A  │             │ App B  │
  │┌──────┐│  ◀──mTLS──▶ │┌──────┐│
  ││Proxy ││             ││Proxy ││
  │└──────┘│             │└──────┘│
  └────────┘             └────────┘
  
  Apps talk plaintext to localhost proxy
  Proxies handle mTLS between services
  Zero code changes needed!
Explanation

mTLS = both sides present certificates. Service meshes auto-inject certificates and rotate them. Apps communicate in plaintext to their local sidecar; the sidecar encrypts traffic to other sidecars. This is zero-trust networking — every service identity is verified. On the CKAD, understand that mTLS provides encryption in transit and service-to-service authentication without changing application code.

Q4
What is sidecar injection? How does it work automatically?
Show Answer
Answer
# Enable automatic sidecar injection for a namespace
kubectl label namespace default istio-injection=enabled

# How it works:
# 1. Namespace labeled for injection
# 2. Pod is created (kubectl apply / Deployment rollout)
# 3. MutatingWebhookConfiguration intercepts API call
# 4. Webhook adds sidecar container to pod spec
# 5. Pod runs with app + proxy container

# Verify injection
kubectl get pod web-abc123 -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[*].name}'
# app  istio-proxy    ← sidecar was injected!

# Disable for a specific pod
# Add annotation: sidecar.istio.io/inject: "false"
Explanation

Sidecar injection uses MutatingAdmissionWebhooks — the API server calls the mesh's webhook before creating the pod, and the webhook mutates the pod spec to add the proxy container. This is transparent to the user. On the CKAD, understand that admission webhooks can modify resources during creation — this concept extends beyond service meshes to any mutation webhook.

Q5
What is traffic splitting in a service mesh? How does it differ from native K8s canary?
Show Answer
Answer
AspectNative K8s CanaryService Mesh Traffic Split
ControlReplica ratio (9:1)Exact percentage (90:10)
GranularityCoarse (per-pod)Fine (per-request)
Header routingNot possibleRoute by headers, cookies
ObservabilityManual metricsBuilt-in per-version metrics
ComplexitySimple (2 Deployments)VirtualService + DestinationRule
 
# Istio VirtualService example
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: web
spec:
  hosts: [web]
  http:
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: web
        subset: v1
      weight: 90
    - destination:
        host: web
        subset: v2
      weight: 10    # Exactly 10% of requests
Explanation

Service mesh traffic splitting gives precise request-level control (exactly 10%), while native K8s uses replica ratios (approximately 10%). Mesh also supports header-based routing (e.g., send internal users to v2). On the CKAD, native canary with two Deployments is sufficient. In production, service mesh provides better control.

Q6
What is a circuit breaker? How does it protect microservices?
Show Answer
Answer
  Circuit Breaker States:
  
  ┌────────┐  failures > threshold  ┌────────┐
  │ CLOSED │ ────────────────────▶ │  OPEN  │
  │(normal)│                       │(reject)│
  └────┬───┘                       └────┬───┘
       ▲                                │
       │  success        timer expires  │
       │                                ▼
       │                          ┌──────────┐
       └──────────────────────────│HALF-OPEN │
                                  │(test 1   │
                                  │ request) │
                                  └──────────┘

When a downstream service fails repeatedly, the circuit breaker "opens" and stops sending requests (returning errors immediately). After a cooldown, it sends one test request (half-open). If it succeeds, circuit closes (normal). If it fails, stays open.

Explanation

Circuit breakers prevent cascade failures. Without them, a failing service causes callers to queue up and exhaust resources (thread pool, connections). With a circuit breaker, callers fail fast instead of waiting. Service meshes implement this at the proxy level. On the CKAD, circuit breaking is a concept question — understand the pattern for microservice resilience.

Q7
What is distributed tracing? How does it work in a service mesh?
Show Answer
Answer
  Request Flow with Tracing:
  
  User → [Frontend] → [API Gateway] → [Order Service] → [Payment Service]
         span-1         span-2          span-3            span-4
         ├───────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
                          Trace (single request)
  
  Each span records:
  - Service name
  - Start/end time
  - Status code
  - Tags/labels
  
  Tools: Jaeger, Zipkin, Tempo
Explanation

A trace follows a single request across all services. Each service creates a span (timing data). The mesh proxy automatically injects trace headers (x-request-id, traceparent) — apps just need to propagate them. Traces help find which service is slow. On the CKAD, distributed tracing is conceptual — understand that observability includes metrics, logs, and traces.

Q8
What is fault injection and why would you use it?
Show Answer
Answer
# Istio fault injection example
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: payment
spec:
  hosts: [payment]
  http:
  - fault:
      delay:
        percentage:
          value: 10           # 10% of requests
        fixedDelay: 5s        # Get 5-second delay
      abort:
        percentage:
          value: 5            # 5% of requests
        httpStatus: 500       # Get HTTP 500 error
    route:
    - destination:
        host: payment
Explanation

Fault injection deliberately introduces failures to test resilience: delays simulate network latency, aborts simulate service failures. This is chaos engineering at the network level. Without fault injection, you only discover weaknesses in production outages. On the CKAD, understand that fault injection tests whether apps handle timeouts and errors gracefully (circuit breakers, retries, fallbacks).

Q9
How does a service mesh handle retries and timeouts?
Show Answer
Answer
# Istio retry and timeout config
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: api
spec:
  hosts: [api]
  http:
  - timeout: 10s                    # Total request timeout
    retries:
      attempts: 3                   # Retry up to 3 times
      perTryTimeout: 3s             # Each try max 3 seconds
      retryOn: 5xx,reset,connect-failure
# Without service mesh, you'd need:
# - App-level retry logic (exponential backoff)
# - Timeout configuration in HTTP client
# - Different for each language/framework

# With service mesh:
# - Configured once at infrastructure level
# - Consistent across all services
# - No code changes needed
Explanation

Service mesh proxies handle retries transparently — the app sees a single request. retryOn defines which failures trigger retries (5xx errors, connection resets). Be careful: retries on non-idempotent requests (POST creating records) can cause duplicates. On the CKAD, understand retry patterns conceptually. In practice, always set timeouts to prevent requests from hanging indefinitely.

Q10
What resource overhead does a service mesh add? When is it NOT worth using one?
Show Answer
Answer
OverheadIstio (Envoy)Linkerd
Latency per hop~3-5ms~1ms
Memory per sidecar~50-100MB~20-30MB
CPU per sidecar~100m~20-50m
Control plane~1-2GB RAM~500MB RAM

DON'T use a mesh when:

  • Few services (< 5) — overhead outweighs benefits
  • Ultra-low latency requirements (HFT, gaming)
  • Simple request patterns (no complex routing)
  • Team lacks operational expertise
Explanation

Every sidecar is an additional container consuming CPU and memory. For 100 pods, that's 100 extra containers. The latency overhead (proxy hop) compounds with call depth. On the CKAD, service mesh concepts help with multi-container patterns and understanding proxy-based architectures. In real projects, evaluate whether the observability and security benefits justify the resource and complexity cost.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Service Mesh mTLS & Zero-Trust Networking

MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15%

Service meshes enforce mTLS (mutual TLS) for all pod-to-pod communication, implementing zero-trust networking where every connection is authenticated and encrypted.

FeatureWithout Service MeshWith Service Mesh (Istio/Linkerd)
Pod-to-pod encryption❌ Plaintext by default✅ Automatic mTLS
Identity verification❌ IP-based only✅ Certificate-based (SPIFFE)
Authorization policiesNetworkPolicy (L3/L4)L7 policies (path, method, headers)
Traffic observabilityLimitedFull request/response metrics
Certificate rotationManualAutomatic (24h default)
 
# Istio PeerAuthentication — Enforce mTLS
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: production
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT       # Only allow mTLS connections

---
# Istio AuthorizationPolicy — L7 access control
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: payment-api-policy
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: payment-api
  action: ALLOW
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        principals: ["cluster.local/ns/production/sa/frontend"]
    to:
    - operation:
        methods: ["POST"]
        paths: ["/api/v1/payments"]
  # All other traffic is DENIED
# Verify mTLS is working
# Istio: check if traffic is encrypted
istioctl x describe pod payment-api-pod -n production
# Shows: mTLS: STRICT

# Check certificate details
istioctl proxy-config secret payment-api-pod -n production
# Shows SPIFFE ID and certificate expiry

# Attempt plaintext connection (should fail in STRICT mode)
kubectl exec non-mesh-pod -- curl http://payment-api.production:8080
# Expected: connection refused (no valid TLS cert)

Chapter 34 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Configure Istio to enforce strict mTLS for all services in the production namespace.
Show Answer
Answer
# Namespace-wide strict mTLS
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: production
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT

---
# For mesh-wide enforcement (all namespaces)
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: istio-system    # Root namespace = mesh-wide
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT
# Verify enforcement
kubectl get peerauthentication -n production
kubectl get peerauthentication -n istio-system

# Check mTLS status for a service
istioctl x describe svc payment-api -n production
# Should show: mTLS STRICT

# Attempt non-mTLS connection (should fail)
kubectl exec non-injected-pod -n default -- \
  curl -v http://payment-api.production.svc:8080
# Expected: connection closed
Explanation

PeerAuthentication controls mTLS mode per namespace or mesh-wide. STRICT = only accept mTLS connections (rejects plaintext). On CKS, know the difference: PERMISSIVE accepts both (for migration), STRICT enforces mTLS. Apply in istio-system namespace for mesh-wide enforcement.

CKS-2
Create an Istio AuthorizationPolicy that allows only the frontend service to call POST /api/orders on the order-service.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: order-service-policy
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: order-service
  action: ALLOW
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        principals:
        - "cluster.local/ns/production/sa/frontend"
    to:
    - operation:
        methods: ["POST"]
        paths: ["/api/orders"]
  - from:
    - source:
        principals:
        - "cluster.local/ns/production/sa/frontend"
    to:
    - operation:
        methods: ["GET"]
        paths: ["/api/orders/*", "/health"]

---
# Deny all other traffic (implicit when ALLOW rules exist)
# But explicit deny-all is recommended for clarity
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: production
spec:
  {}  # Empty spec = deny all without explicit ALLOW
Explanation

AuthorizationPolicy provides L7 access control — far more granular than NetworkPolicy. On CKS, know that source.principals uses SPIFFE IDs (based on ServiceAccount). An empty spec: {} creates a deny-all policy. When ALLOW policies exist, traffic not matching any rule is denied (implicit deny).

CKS-3
How do you verify that pod-to-pod traffic is actually encrypted with mTLS?
Show Answer
Answer
# Method 1: Istio proxy-config
istioctl proxy-config secret frontend-pod -n production -o json | \
  jq '.dynamicActiveSecrets[0].secret.tlsCertificate'
# Shows certificate chain and SPIFFE ID

# Method 2: Check Envoy proxy logs
kubectl logs frontend-pod -c istio-proxy -n production | \
  grep "tls_inspector"

# Method 3: tcpdump from a debug pod
kubectl debug -it frontend-pod --image=nicolaka/netshoot \
  --target=istio-proxy -- tcpdump -i eth0 -w /tmp/capture.pcap
# Open in Wireshark — should show TLS handshake

# Method 4: Istio describe
istioctl x describe pod frontend-pod -n production
# Output shows:
# - mTLS: STRICT
# - Certificate chain valid
# - SPIFFE ID: spiffe://cluster.local/ns/production/sa/frontend

# Method 5: Check Kiali dashboard
# Shows green lock icons for mTLS connections
Explanation

Verification is critical — don't assume mTLS works. On CKS, use istioctl x describe for quick checks and proxy-config secret for certificate details. The SPIFFE ID format is spiffe://cluster.local/ns/NAMESPACE/sa/SERVICE_ACCOUNT. Packet capture with tcpdump can definitively prove encryption.

CKS-4
What security risks does a service mesh sidecar introduce and how do you mitigate them?
Show Answer
Answer
# Risk 1: Sidecar has access to plaintext traffic
# Mitigation: Sidecar (Envoy proxy) is trusted infrastructure
# — secure the sidecar image supply chain

# Risk 2: Sidecar injection can be bypassed
# Mitigation: Enforce injection at namespace level
kubectl label namespace production istio-injection=enabled

# Risk 3: Sidecar runs with elevated capabilities
# Check sidecar security context
kubectl get pod frontend -n production -o json | \
  jq '.spec.containers[] | select(.name == "istio-proxy") | .securityContext'

# Risk 4: Control plane compromise affects all traffic
# Mitigation: Harden istiod
kubectl get deployment istiod -n istio-system -o yaml | \
  grep -A5 securityContext

# Risk 5: Certificate authority compromise
# Mitigation: Use external CA (Vault, cert-manager)
# Rotate root CA regularly
# Prevent sidecar injection bypass via policy
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-mtls-everywhere
  namespace: istio-system
spec:
  action: DENY
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        notPrincipals: ["*"]   # Deny non-mTLS traffic
Explanation

Service mesh sidecars terminate TLS and see plaintext — they're a privileged component. On CKS, key risks: sidecar injection bypass (pods skip the mesh), control plane compromise (istiod manages all certificates), and expanded attack surface (each pod gets an extra container). Mitigate with mandatory injection, hardened control plane RBAC, and external CA integration.

CKS-5
Compare NetworkPolicy and service mesh AuthorizationPolicy for pod-level security.
Show Answer
Answer
# NetworkPolicy: L3/L4 — IP and port based
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: order-service
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: frontend
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8080
# Can only filter by IP/port — cannot distinguish
# GET /health from POST /admin

---
# AuthorizationPolicy: L7 — HTTP method, path, headers
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1
kind: AuthorizationPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-orders
  namespace: production
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: order-service
  rules:
  - from:
    - source:
        principals: ["cluster.local/ns/production/sa/frontend"]
    to:
    - operation:
        methods: ["GET", "POST"]
        paths: ["/api/orders*"]
    when:
    - key: request.headers[x-api-version]
      values: ["v2"]
# Can filter by HTTP method, path, headers, JWT claims
Explanation

On CKS, know both mechanisms. NetworkPolicy works at L3/L4 (kernel/CNI level) — zero performance overhead, works without service mesh. AuthorizationPolicy works at L7 (application level) — can filter HTTP methods, paths, headers, JWT claims. Best practice: use NetworkPolicy as a baseline (default-deny) PLUS AuthorizationPolicy for fine-grained L7 control. Defense in depth!

Chapter 35: Custom Resources & Operators

PART 11 ADVANCED 🧩 EXTENSIBILITY

Kubernetes is not just a container orchestrator — it's an extensible platform. With Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs), you can teach Kubernetes new concepts. With Operators, you can encode operational knowledge into software that manages complex applications automatically.

🎯 CKAD Domain 4: Application Environment, Configuration & Security 25% of Exam — CRD Awareness

The CKAD may test your ability to interact with CRDs: listing custom resources, creating instances from a given CRD, and using kubectl explain on custom types. You won't need to write CRD YAML from scratch.

CRD Commands for CKAD

crd-essentials.sh 
# List all CRDs in the cluster
kubectl get crd

# Get details about a specific CRD
kubectl explain <custom-resource-name>

# List instances of a custom resource
kubectl get <custom-resource-name>

# Describe a specific custom resource instance
kubectl describe <custom-resource-name> <instance-name>

# Create a custom resource from YAML
kubectl apply -f my-custom-resource.yaml

# Delete a custom resource
kubectl delete <custom-resource-name> <instance-name>

🧩 Extending Kubernetes

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         KUBERNETES EXTENSIBILITY STACK                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         BUILT-IN RESOURCES                                       │
  │                                                                                   │
  │  Pod       Deployment       Service       ConfigMap       Secret       PV/PVC   │
  │                                                                                   │
  │    ↑ These come with Kubernetes out of the box                                   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                        +
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         CUSTOM RESOURCE DEFINITIONS (CRDs)                       │
  │                                                                                   │
  │  PostgresCluster    Certificate    VirtualService    BackupSchedule   MLModel  │
  │                                                                                   │
  │    ↑ You define these! kubectl treats them like built-in resources              │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                        +
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                              OPERATORS                                           │
  │                                                                                   │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │
  │  │  Controller that watches CRs and takes action                           │   │
  │  │                                                                          │   │
  │  │   PostgresCluster CR ────▶ Operator ────▶ Creates: Pods, Services,     │   │
  │  │                                           StatefulSet, Secrets, Backups │   │
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │
  │                                                                                   │
  │    ↑ Encodes operational knowledge (backups, failover, upgrades)                │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
🎯 What You'll Learn
📝
CRDs

Define new resource types for kubectl

🤖
Operators

Controllers that manage complex apps

🐘
PostgreSQL Operator

Real-world example for anihpj

CRD + Custom Resource YAML Template

CKAD may test understanding CRDs. Know how to define one and create instances.

# Step 1: Define the CRD
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backups.stable.example.com      # ← plural.group format
spec:
  group: stable.example.com             # ← API group
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true                         # ← Is this version served?
    storage: true                        # ← Is this the storage version?
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:                   # ← Validation schema
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            properties:
              schedule:
                type: string
              retentionDays:
                type: integer
                minimum: 1
  scope: Namespaced                      # ← Namespaced or Cluster
  names:
    plural: backups                      # ← kubectl get backups
    singular: backup                     # ← kubectl get backup
    kind: Backup                         # ← kind in YAML
    shortNames:
    - bk                                 # ← kubectl get bk
---
# Step 2: Create a Custom Resource instance
apiVersion: stable.example.com/v1       # ← group/version
kind: Backup                            # ← Matches CRD kind
metadata:
  name: daily-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  retentionDays: 30

CRD & Custom Resource Commands

ActionCommand
List all CRDskubectl get crd
Describe CRDkubectl describe crd backups.stable.example.com
List custom resourceskubectl get backups or kubectl get bk
Create CR from filekubectl apply -f daily-backup.yaml
Explain CR fieldskubectl explain backup.spec
Delete CRD (+ all CRs!)kubectl delete crd backups.stable.example.com
Check API resourceskubectl api-resources | grep stable

CRD / Operator Traps

  • ⚠️ CRD name format: Must be plural.group (e.g., backups.stable.example.com)
  • ❌ Deleting a CRD deletes ALL its custom resources — this is cascading and irreversible!
  • CRD vs CR: CRD defines the type (schema). CR is an instance of that type. Don't confuse them
  • scope: Namespaced vs Cluster — Namespaced CRs live in a namespace. Cluster-scoped CRs are global
  • Operators = CRD + Controller. The CRD defines the API, the controller implements behavior

🗺️ CRD / Operator Ecosystem

Operator
📜 CRD Defines custom API schema
📦 Custom Resource Instance of the CRD type
⚙️ Controller Reconciles desired vs actual state
🔄 Reconciliation Loop Watch → Diff → Act → Repeat
📜
Define CRD YAML schema for new type
→
💾
Apply CRD kubectl apply -f crd.yaml
→
📦
Create CR kubectl apply -f backup.yaml
→
⚙️
Controller Acts Reconciles desired state
→
✅
State Achieved Continuous reconciliation

35.1 Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)

📝 What is a CRD?

A Custom Resource Definition extends the Kubernetes API by defining new resource types. Once created, you can use kubectl commands like kubectl get myresource just as you would with built-in resources.

📐 CRD Architecture

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    CUSTOM RESOURCE DEFINITION (CRD)                             │
  │                                                                                  │
  │   kind: CustomResourceDefinition                                                │
  │   metadata:                                                                      │
  │     name: backups.database.anihpj.io                                       │
  │   spec:                                                                          │
  │     group: database.anihpj.io         ◄─── API group (like apps, v1)          │
  │     names:                                                                       │
  │       kind: Backup                      ◄─── Resource kind                       │
  │       plural: backups                  ◄─── kubectl get backups                  │
  │     scope: Namespaced                  ◄─── Namespaced or Cluster-scoped          │
  │     versions:                                                                    │
  │       - name: v1                                                                │
  │         schema:                                                                  │
  │           openAPIV3Schema: ...       ◄─── Validation schema                     │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                                        │
                                        │ Creates new API endpoint
                                        ▼
  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │              kubectl get backups ──▶ /apis/database.anihpj.io/v1/backups       │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
backup-crd.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Custom Resource Definition: Define a "Backup" resource
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backups.database.anihpj.io   # plural.group
spec:
  group: database.anihpj.io          # API group
  names:
    kind: Backup                      # Kind for manifests
    plural: backups                   # kubectl get backups
    singular: backup                  # kubectl get backup mybackup
    shortNames:
    - bkp                             # kubectl get bkp
  scope: Namespaced                   # Or "Cluster"
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true                      # Enable this version
    storage: true                     # Store in etcd
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            required:
            - database
            - schedule
            properties:
              database:
                type: string
                description: Database name to backup
              schedule:
                type: string
                pattern: '^(\*|[0-9,\-\/]+)\s+(\*|[0-9,\-\/]+)\s+(\*|[0-9,\-\/]+)\s+(\*|[0-9,\-\/]+)\s+(\*|[0-9,\-\/]+)$'
                description: Cron schedule
              retention:
                type: integer
                default: 7
                minimum: 1
          status:
            type: object
            properties:
              lastBackup:
                type: string
              backupCount:
                type: integer
    additionalPrinterColumns:        # kubectl get backups output
    - name: Database
      type: string
      jsonPath: .spec.database
    - name: Schedule
      type: string
      jsonPath: .spec.schedule
    - name: Last Backup
      type: string
      jsonPath: .status.lastBackup
anihpj-backup.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Custom Resource: Create a Backup instance
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: database.anihpj.io/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  name: anihpj-daily-backup
  namespace: production
spec:
  database: anihpj_db
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"    # Daily at 2 AM
  retention: 14             # Keep 14 days
kubectl commands for CRDs 
# Apply the CRD first
kubectl apply -f backup-crd.yaml

# Verify CRD is registered
kubectl get crd | grep anihpj
# backups.database.anihpj.io   2024-01-15T10:30:00Z

# Now create a Custom Resource
kubectl apply -f anihpj-backup.yaml

# Use it like any built-in resource!
kubectl get backups
# NAME                   DATABASE     SCHEDULE      LAST BACKUP
# anihpj-daily-backup    anihpj_db    0 2 * * *     2024-01-15T02:00:00Z

kubectl describe backup anihpj-daily-backup
kubectl delete backup anihpj-daily-backup

35.2 Operators

🤖 What is an Operator?

An Operator is a controller that watches Custom Resources and takes automated actions. It encodes the operational knowledge of a human expert into software — knowing how to deploy, scale, backup, upgrade, and recover applications.

🔄 Operator Control Loop

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         OPERATOR CONTROL LOOP                                    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

       ┌──────────────┐                              ┌──────────────────────┐
       │   User       │                              │   Kubernetes API     │
       │              │       kubectl apply          │                      │
       │ PostgresCluster ─────────────────────────▶ │   PostgresCluster    │
       │   CR (yaml)  │                              │   Custom Resource    │
       └──────────────┘                              └──────────┬───────────┘
                                                                 │
                                                                 │ Watch
                                                                 ▼
                                                    ┌──────────────────────┐
                                                    │  PostgreSQL Operator │
                                                    │                      │
                                                    │  ┌─────────────────┐ │
                                                    │  │ Reconcile Loop  │ │
                                                    │  │                 │ │
                                                    │  │ Observe ──────┐ │ │
                                                    │  │      ▲         │ │ │
                                                    │  │      │         │ │ │
                                                    │  │      │         ▼ │ │
                                                    │  │ Act ◄────── Diff │ │
                                                    │  └─────────────────┘ │
                                                    └──────────┬───────────┘
                                                                │
          ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┼─────────────────┐
          │                         Creates/Manages              │                 │
          ▼                              ▼                       ▼                 ▼
  ┌───────────────┐       ┌───────────────┐       ┌───────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐
  │  StatefulSet  │       │    Service    │       │    Secret     │    │   PVC       │
  │  (3 replicas) │       │ postgres-svc  │       │ (passwords)   │    │ (storage)   │
  └───────────────┘       └───────────────┘       └───────────────┘    └─────────────┘
🛠️ Operator Capabilities
🚀
Deploy

Provision StatefulSets, Services, Secrets automatically

📈
Scale

Add replicas, rebalance shards, update PVCs

💾
Backup

Scheduled backups to S3, restore from backup

🔄
Failover

Detect failures, promote replicas automatically

🌟 Popular Kubernetes Operators
OperatorManagesKey Features
CloudNativePG PostgreSQL HA, backups, failover, pooling
Strimzi Apache Kafka Clusters, topics, users, TLS
Redis Operator Redis Sentinel, cluster mode, persistence
Prometheus Operator Prometheus + Alertmanager ServiceMonitors, PrometheusRules
cert-manager TLS Certificates Let's Encrypt, auto-renewal
ArgoCD GitOps deployments Sync from Git, rollback, RBAC

35.3 Using Operators for anihpj (PostgreSQL)

🐘 CloudNativePG for anihpj

For our Django job posting app, we'll use CloudNativePG — a CNCF Sandbox project that provides enterprise-grade PostgreSQL on Kubernetes with automatic failover, backups, and connection pooling.

✨ Why Use an Operator for anihpj's Database?
❌
Without Operator
  • Manual StatefulSet + PVC setup
  • No automatic failover
  • Manual backups with CronJob
  • Upgrades = downtime
  • Connection pooling = separate PgBouncer
✅
With CloudNativePG
  • One CR = complete HA setup
  • Automatic failover (< 30 seconds)
  • Built-in backups to S3/Azure/GCS
  • Rolling upgrades with zero downtime
  • Built-in connection pooling
Install CloudNativePG Operator 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Install CloudNativePG Operator
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
kubectl apply -f \
  https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cloudnative-pg/cloudnative-pg/release-1.22/releases/cnpg-1.22.0.yaml

# Verify operator is running
kubectl get pods -n cnpg-system
# NAME                        READY   STATUS
# cnpg-controller-manager-xxx 1/1     Running

# Check CRDs installed
kubectl get crd | grep cnpg
# clusters.postgresql.cnpg.io
# backups.postgresql.cnpg.io
# poolers.postgresql.cnpg.io
anihpj-postgres-cluster.yaml 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CloudNativePG Cluster for anihpj Django app
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db
  namespace: production
spec:
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Cluster Size: 1 primary + 2 replicas
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  instances: 3
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # PostgreSQL Version
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  imageName: ghcr.io/cloudnative-pg/postgresql:16.1
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Database and Credentials
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  bootstrap:
    initdb:
      database: anihpj_db
      owner: anihpj_user
      secret:
        name: anihpj-db-credentials   # Create this Secret!
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Storage Configuration
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  storage:
    size: 20Gi
    storageClass: standard
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Resources
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  resources:
    requests:
      memory: "512Mi"
      cpu: "500m"
    limits:
      memory: "2Gi"
      cpu: "2"
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Backup to S3 (optional)
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  backup:
    barmanObjectStore:
      destinationPath: s3://anihpj-backups/postgres/
      s3Credentials:
        accessKeyId:
          name: aws-creds
          key: ACCESS_KEY_ID
        secretAccessKey:
          name: aws-creds
          key: SECRET_ACCESS_KEY
    retentionPolicy: "14d"
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # High Availability Settings
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  primaryUpdateStrategy: unsupervised   # Auto failover
  
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # PostgreSQL Configuration
  # ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  postgresql:
    parameters:
      max_connections: "200"
      shared_buffers: "256MB"
      log_statement: "ddl"
---
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Scheduled Backup
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: ScheduledBackup
metadata:
  name: anihpj-db-daily
  namespace: production
spec:
  cluster:
    name: anihpj-db
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"           # Daily at 2 AM
  backupOwnerReference: self
Django settings for CloudNativePG 
# settings.py - Connect Django to CloudNativePG cluster
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

import os

DATABASES = {
    'default': {
        'ENGINE': 'django.db.backends.postgresql',
        # CloudNativePG creates service: {cluster-name}-rw
        'HOST': os.environ.get('DB_HOST', 'anihpj-db-rw'),
        'PORT': os.environ.get('DB_PORT', '5432'),
        'NAME': os.environ.get('DB_NAME', 'anihpj_db'),
        'USER': os.environ.get('DB_USER', 'anihpj_user'),
        'PASSWORD': os.environ.get('DB_PASSWORD'),
        'CONN_MAX_AGE': 60,  # Connection pooling
        'OPTIONS': {
            'connect_timeout': 10,
        }
    }
}

# For read replicas (optional):
# Connect to {cluster-name}-ro for read-only queries

🏗️ anihpj with CloudNativePG Architecture

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         production namespace                                     │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  ┌──────────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     anihpj Django        │      │            CloudNativePG Cluster              │
  │                          │      │                                                │
  │  ┌────────────────────┐  │      │  ┌────────────┐ ┌────────────┐ ┌────────────┐│
  │  │  Deployment        │  │      │  │  Pod       │ │  Pod       │ │  Pod       ││
  │  │  (3 replicas)      │──│─────▶│  │  Primary   │ │  Replica1  │ │  Replica2  ││
  │  │                    │  │      │  │  (RW)      │ │  (RO)      │ │  (RO)      ││
  │  │  Django + Gunicorn │  │      │  └──────┬─────┘ └──────┬─────┘ └──────┬─────┘│
  │  └────────────────────┘  │      │         │              │              │      │
  │                          │      │         ▼              ▼              ▼      │
  └──────────────────────────┘      │  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐ │
                │                   │  │     PersistentVolumeClaims (3x 20Gi)   │ │
                │                   │  └────────────────────────────────────────┘ │
                │                   └──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                │
                │                   Services created by operator:
                │                   ┌────────────────────────────────────────────┐
                └──────────────────▶│  anihpj-db-rw  ──▶ Primary (read-write)   │
                                    │  anihpj-db-ro  ──▶ Replicas (read-only)   │
                                    │  anihpj-db-r   ──▶ Any instance (random)  │
                                    └────────────────────────────────────────────┘
kubectl Cheat Sheet: CRDs and Operators 
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Working with Custom Resource Definitions
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# List all CRDs in the cluster
kubectl get crd

# View CRD details (see schema)
kubectl describe crd clusters.postgresql.cnpg.io

# List Custom Resources of type 'Cluster' (PostgreSQL)
kubectl get clusters.postgresql.cnpg.io -n production
# OR use the short name:
kubectl get cluster -n production

# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CloudNativePG Specific Commands
# ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Check PostgreSQL cluster status
kubectl get cluster anihpj-db -n production
# NAME        INSTANCES   READY   STATUS    AGE
# anihpj-db   3           3       Healthy   2d

# Get cluster details (replica lag, backup status)
kubectl describe cluster anihpj-db -n production

# Check which pod is primary
kubectl get pods -n production -l cnpg.io/cluster=anihpj-db \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name} {.metadata.labels.role}{"\n"}{end}'

# Access PostgreSQL directly
kubectl exec -it anihpj-db-1 -n production -- psql -U anihpj_user -d anihpj_db

# Manual failover (promote replica)
kubectl cnpg promote anihpj-db anihpj-db-2 -n production

# Trigger manual backup
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: postgresql.cnpg.io/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  name: manual-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d)
  namespace: production
spec:
  cluster:
    name: anihpj-db
EOF

# List backups
kubectl get backups -n production
🔍 Where to Find Kubernetes Operators
📦
OperatorHub.io

Official catalog with 300+ operators. Install via OLM.

operatorhub.io
🎖️
CNCF Landscape

Filter by "Operators" to find CNCF graduated/incubating.

landscape.cncf.io
🛠️
Artifact Hub

Search Helm charts that install operators.

artifacthub.io

🔨 Building Your Own Operator

If you can't find an operator for your needs, build one! Popular frameworks:

  • Kubebuilder — Go-based, CNCF recommended, generates scaffolding
  • Operator SDK — Red Hat's framework, supports Go, Ansible, Helm
  • KUDO — Declarative operators with YAML (no coding)
  • Metacontroller — Write controllers in any language (Python, JS)

🏋️ CKAD Practice Drill: Custom Resources

⏱️ Target Time: 5 minutes
Scenario: Interact with Custom Resource Definitions — list, inspect, and create instances of custom resources.
  1. List all CRDs currently installed in the cluster
  2. Find any CRDs related to "certificate" (e.g., from cert-manager)
  3. If a CRD certificates.cert-manager.io exists, list all instances across all namespaces
  4. Use kubectl explain on the custom resource to explore its schema
  5. Create a simple CRD named backups.example.com with spec fields: schedule (string) and retention (integer)
📖 View Solution
Solution — Custom Resources Drill 
# 1. List all CRDs
kubectl get crd

# 2. Filter for "certificate"
kubectl get crd | grep certificate

# 3. List instances (if CRD exists)
kubectl get certificates -A
# OR for any custom resource:
kubectl get certificates.cert-manager.io -A

# 4. Explore schema
kubectl explain certificates
kubectl explain certificates.spec

# 5. Create a CRD
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backups.example.com
spec:
  group: example.com
  names:
    kind: Backup
    plural: backups
    singular: backup
    shortNames: [bk]
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true
    storage: true
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            properties:
              schedule:
                type: string
              retention:
                type: integer
EOF

# Verify
kubectl get crd backups.example.com

# Create an instance
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: example.com/v1
kind: Backup
metadata:
  name: daily-backup
spec:
  schedule: "0 2 * * *"
  retention: 7
EOF

kubectl get backups

🎉 Part 11 Complete: Advanced Patterns

Congratulations! You've mastered advanced Kubernetes patterns:

  • 📦 Multi-Container Patterns — Sidecar, Ambassador, Adapter, Init Containers
  • 📍 Scheduling — Node selectors, affinity, taints, topology spread
  • 🕸️ Service Mesh — When to use, Istio vs Linkerd
  • 🤖 Operators — CRDs, operator pattern, CloudNativePG for anihpj

Next: Refer to the Appendices for quick reference materials!

Chapter 35 — Practice Questions

Q1
What is a Custom Resource Definition (CRD)? What problem does it solve?
Show Answer
Answer
  Built-in Resources           Custom Resources (CRDs)
  ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────────┐
  │ Pod              │         │ Database             │
  │ Deployment       │         │ Certificate          │
  │ Service          │         │ BackupSchedule       │
  │ ConfigMap        │         │ KafkaTopic           │
  │ Secret           │         │ VirtualService       │
  │ Ingress          │         │ PrometheusRule       │
  └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────────┘
  Defined by Kubernetes        Defined by YOU (or operators)

CRDs extend the Kubernetes API with your own resource types. After creating a CRD, you can kubectl get databases just like kubectl get pods.

Explanation

CRDs let you treat anything as a Kubernetes-native resource. Instead of scripts or external tools, you declare desired state (e.g., "I want a PostgreSQL database with 3 replicas") and an operator reconciles it. On the CKAD, CRDs are increasingly tested — know how to list, create, and interact with custom resources using standard kubectl commands.

Q2
Write a CRD for a Backup resource with fields for database name, schedule, and retention days.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: backups.mycompany.io       # plural.group
spec:
  group: mycompany.io
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true
    storage: true
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            properties:
              databaseName:
                type: string
              schedule:
                type: string      # Cron format
              retentionDays:
                type: integer
                minimum: 1
            required: [databaseName, schedule]
  scope: Namespaced
  names:
    plural: backups
    singular: backup
    kind: Backup
    shortNames: [bk]
# After applying the CRD:
kubectl get backups        # or kubectl get bk
kubectl describe backup my-backup
Explanation

CRD metadata name must be plural.group. Schema validation uses OpenAPI v3. scope: Namespaced means resources exist within namespaces (vs. Cluster). shortNames enables aliases. On the CKAD, you may need to create a CRD or a custom resource instance. Know the structure: group, versions, names, and schema.

Q3
Create an instance of a custom resource. Given a CRD for Widget (group: example.com), create a Widget.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: example.com/v1       # group/version
kind: Widget                     # CRD kind
metadata:
  name: my-widget
  namespace: default
spec:
  color: blue
  size: large
  replicas: 3
# Apply the custom resource
kubectl apply -f widget.yaml

# Interact like any K8s resource
kubectl get widgets
kubectl get widget my-widget -o yaml
kubectl describe widget my-widget
kubectl delete widget my-widget
kubectl edit widget my-widget
Explanation

Custom resource instances follow the same YAML pattern as built-in resources: apiVersion, kind, metadata, spec. The apiVersion is group/version from the CRD. On the CKAD, you can use all standard kubectl operations on CRs: get, describe, edit, delete, patch, apply. The exam may provide a CRD and ask you to create an instance.

Q4
What is the Operator pattern? How does it differ from a CRD alone?
Show Answer
Answer
  Operator = CRD + Controller
  
  ┌───────────────┐     ┌────────────────────┐
  │ Custom        │     │ Operator            │
  │ Resource      │────▶│ (Controller)        │
  │ "I want a     │     │                     │
  │ 3-node DB"    │     │ 1. Watch for CRs    │
  └───────────────┘     │ 2. Compare desired  │
                        │    vs actual state  │
  ┌───────────────┐     │ 3. Reconcile:       │
  │ Actual State  │◀────│    - Create pods    │
  │ (3 DB pods,   │     │    - Configure      │
  │  PVCs, config)│     │    - Handle upgrades│
  └───────────────┘     └────────────────────┘
Explanation

A CRD alone just stores data in the API server — nothing acts on it. An Operator adds a controller that watches for CR changes and takes action (creating pods, configuring services, etc.). It encodes operational knowledge. Examples: PostgreSQL Operator, Prometheus Operator, Cert-Manager. On the CKAD, understand the CRD → CR → Operator → Reconcile loop concept.

Q5
How do you list all CRDs in a cluster and find their API resources?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all CRDs
kubectl get crds
# NAME                              CREATED AT
# backups.mycompany.io              2024-01-15
# certificates.cert-manager.io      2024-01-10

# Describe a CRD (see schema, versions)
kubectl describe crd backups.mycompany.io

# List API resources (includes CRDs)
kubectl api-resources | grep mycompany
# backups    bk    mycompany.io/v1    true    Backup

# Explain a custom resource (if CRD has descriptions)
kubectl explain backup
kubectl explain backup.spec

# Get all custom resources of a type
kubectl get backups -A    # All namespaces
Explanation

kubectl get crds lists all custom resource definitions. kubectl api-resources shows all resource types (built-in + custom). kubectl explain works on CRDs if the schema includes descriptions. On the CKAD, if the exam asks you to interact with an unfamiliar resource, use api-resources to find its kind, shortname, and API group.

Q6
What are common real-world Kubernetes Operators? Name 5 with their purpose.
Show Answer
Answer
OperatorPurposeCustom Resources
cert-managerTLS certificate managementCertificate, Issuer, ClusterIssuer
Prometheus OperatorMonitoring stack managementPrometheus, ServiceMonitor, AlertmanagerConfig
Strimzi (Kafka)Kafka cluster managementKafka, KafkaTopic, KafkaUser
Postgres OperatorPostgreSQL HA managementPostgresCluster, Backup
ArgoCDGitOps CD deliveryApplication, AppProject
Explanation

Operators automate Day 2 operations (upgrades, backups, scaling, failure recovery). Instead of manual runbooks, the operator encodes best practices. On the CKAD, cert-manager is most likely to appear — it manages TLS certificates for Ingress. Know that operators install CRDs and watch for custom resource changes to trigger actions.

Q7
What is the reconciliation loop in an operator?
Show Answer
Answer
  Reconciliation Loop (runs continuously):
  
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                                      │
       ▼                                      │
  ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────┐    ┌─────────┐  │
  │ OBSERVE │───▶│ COMPARE  │───▶│  ACT    │──┘
  │ Watch   │    │ desired  │    │ Create/ │
  │ events  │    │ vs actual│    │ Update/ │
  └─────────┘    └──────────┘    │ Delete  │
                                  └─────────┘
  
  Example: Database Operator
  - Desired: 3 replicas, version 14, daily backups
  - Actual: 2 replicas, version 13, no backups
  - Action: Add 1 replica, upgrade version, create CronJob
Explanation

The reconciliation loop is the core of Kubernetes itself — all controllers work this way. The operator watches for changes to its CRs, compares desired state (spec) versus actual state (status), and takes corrective action. It's level-triggered (not edge-triggered) — if it misses an event, the next reconcile catches up. On the CKAD, this pattern is why kubectl apply is declarative — you state what you want, K8s figures out how to get there.

Q8
How do you add validation to a CRD so invalid resources are rejected?
Show Answer
Answer
# CRD with validation
spec:
  versions:
  - name: v1
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            required: [name, replicas]    # Required fields
            properties:
              name:
                type: string
                minLength: 1
                maxLength: 63
                pattern: '^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$'  # Regex validation
              replicas:
                type: integer
                minimum: 1
                maximum: 10
              tier:
                type: string
                enum: [free, pro, enterprise]  # Allowed values
              tags:
                type: array
                items:
                  type: string
                maxItems: 5
# Invalid resource → rejected by API server:
# Error: spec.replicas must be ≤ 10
# Error: spec.tier must be one of: free, pro, enterprise
Explanation

OpenAPI v3 schema validation runs server-side — invalid resources are rejected immediately. Use required, minimum/maximum, enum, pattern, minLength/maxLength. On the CKAD, if asked to create a CRD, always add schema validation. Without it, any arbitrary JSON is accepted as a valid CR.

Q9
What is the difference between Namespaced and Cluster scoped CRDs?
Show Answer
Answer
ScopeWhere it livesAccessExamples
NamespacedWithin a namespacePer-namespace RBACBackup, Certificate
ClusterCluster-wideGlobal RBAC onlyClusterIssuer, StorageProfile
 
# In CRD spec:
spec:
  scope: Namespaced  # or Cluster

# Namespaced: kubectl get backups -n production
# Cluster:    kubectl get clusterissuers (no -n flag)
Explanation

Namespaced CRs exist within namespaces and support per-namespace RBAC (team A can manage their backups, not team B's). Cluster-scoped CRs are global (like Nodes or ClusterRoles). On the CKAD, most CRDs you'll encounter are Namespaced. Use kubectl api-resources — the NAMESPACED column shows true/false.

Q10
How do you add custom columns to kubectl get for a custom resource?
Show Answer
Answer
# In the CRD: additionalPrinterColumns
spec:
  versions:
  - name: v1
    additionalPrinterColumns:
    - name: Database
      type: string
      jsonPath: .spec.databaseName
    - name: Schedule
      type: string
      jsonPath: .spec.schedule
    - name: Retention
      type: integer
      jsonPath: .spec.retentionDays
    - name: Age
      type: date
      jsonPath: .metadata.creationTimestamp
# Now kubectl get shows custom columns:
kubectl get backups
# NAME       DATABASE    SCHEDULE      RETENTION   AGE
# daily-pg   postgres    0 2 * * *     30          5d
# weekly-mg  mongodb     0 0 * * 0     90          10d
Explanation

additionalPrinterColumns makes kubectl get output useful for CRs — without it, you only see NAME and AGE. Use jsonPath to extract values from the CR. On the CKAD, you may see CRDs with or without printer columns. You can always use -o yaml or -o jsonpath to extract any field regardless.

🛡️ CKS Security Perspective — Admission Webhooks, OPA Gatekeeper & Policy-as-Code

SUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY 20% CLUSTER HARDENING 15% MINIMIZE MICROSERVICE VULNS 20%

CRDs and Operators can extend the Kubernetes API with custom resources, but they also introduce new attack surfaces. Admission webhooks and policy engines like OPA Gatekeeper are the CKS-tested controls for governing custom resources.

Webhook TypePurposeCan Modify?CKS Use Case
ValidatingWebhookConfigurationAccept/reject requestsNoBlock non-compliant resources
MutatingWebhookConfigurationModify requests before persistenceYesInject security defaults (labels, security context)
OPA GatekeeperPolicy-as-code frameworkValidating onlyEnforce org-wide policies via Rego
KyvernoK8s-native policy engineBothYAML-based policies (no Rego needed)
 
# ValidatingWebhookConfiguration — block privileged pods
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: deny-privileged
webhooks:
- name: deny-privileged.security.example.com
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  sideEffects: None
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: webhook-server
      namespace: security
      path: /validate
    caBundle: LS0tLS1...       # CA cert for TLS
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods"]
  failurePolicy: Fail          # Reject if webhook unavailable
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: kubernetes.io/metadata.name
      operator: NotIn
      values: ["kube-system"]  # Skip system namespace
# OPA Gatekeeper — ConstraintTemplate for label enforcement
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8srequiredlabels
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sRequiredLabels
      validation:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            labels:
              type: array
              items:
                type: string
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8srequiredlabels
      violation[{"msg": msg, "details": {"missing_labels": missing}}] {
        provided := {label | input.review.object.metadata.labels[label]}
        required := {label | label := input.parameters.labels[_]}
        missing := required - provided
        count(missing) > 0
        msg := sprintf("Missing required labels: %v", [missing])
      }
---
# Apply the constraint
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequiredLabels
metadata:
  name: require-team-label
spec:
  match:
    kinds: [{apiGroups: [""], kinds: ["Namespace"]}]
  parameters:
    labels: ["team", "cost-center"]

Chapter 35 — CKS Practice Questions

CKS-1
Create an OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate that blocks containers running as root.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8sdenyroot
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sDenyRoot
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8sdenyroot

      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        not container.securityContext.runAsNonRoot
        msg := sprintf("container <%v> must set runAsNonRoot: true", [container.name])
      }

      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        container.securityContext.runAsUser == 0
        msg := sprintf("container <%v> must not run as root (UID 0)", [container.name])
      }

      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.initContainers[_]
        container.securityContext.runAsUser == 0
        msg := sprintf("init container <%v> must not run as root", [container.name])
      }
---
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sDenyRoot
metadata:
  name: deny-root-all-namespaces
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      kinds: ["Pod"]
    excludedNamespaces: ["kube-system"]
Explanation

OPA Gatekeeper uses Rego to define policies. On CKS, know the ConstraintTemplate structure: crd.spec defines the custom resource, targets[].rego defines the policy logic. The violation rule returns messages for non-compliant resources. Check both containers and initContainers. Exclude kube-system to avoid breaking system components.

CKS-2
Configure a ValidatingWebhookConfiguration that rejects pods without resource limits.
Show Answer
Answer
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: require-resource-limits
webhooks:
- name: limits.security.example.com
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  sideEffects: None
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: resource-limits-webhook
      namespace: security-webhooks
      path: /validate-resources
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTi...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: ["", "apps"]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods", "deployments"]
  failurePolicy: Fail
  matchPolicy: Equivalent
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: kubernetes.io/metadata.name
      operator: NotIn
      values: ["kube-system", "security-webhooks"]
  timeoutSeconds: 10
# Test: create a pod without limits
kubectl run test-no-limits --image=nginx
# Expected: Error from webhook — resource limits required

# Test: create a pod WITH limits
kubectl run test-with-limits --image=nginx \
  --limits='cpu=200m,memory=256Mi' \
  --requests='cpu=100m,memory=128Mi'
# Expected: Created successfully
Explanation

ValidatingWebhookConfiguration sends admission requests to your webhook server for accept/reject decisions. On CKS, key fields: failurePolicy: Fail (deny if webhook is down — fail-closed), caBundle (TLS verification), namespaceSelector (exclude system namespaces). The webhook service must be running before creating the configuration, otherwise all matching operations will fail.

CKS-3
How do you secure a custom admission webhook service itself?
Show Answer
Answer
# 1. TLS — webhook MUST serve HTTPS
# Generate certificates (or use cert-manager)
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 \
  -keyout webhook-key.pem -out webhook-cert.pem \
  -days 365 -nodes \
  -subj "/CN=webhook-server.security.svc"
# Add SANs for the service DNS name

# 2. Store cert as Secret
kubectl create secret tls webhook-tls \
  -n security \
  --cert=webhook-cert.pem \
  --key=webhook-key.pem

# 3. RBAC — minimal permissions for webhook SA
kubectl create role webhook-role -n security \
  --verb=get,list --resource=pods,deployments
kubectl create rolebinding webhook-binding -n security \
  --role=webhook-role \
  --serviceaccount=security:webhook-sa

# 4. NetworkPolicy — limit who can reach webhook
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: webhook-netpol
  namespace: security
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webhook-server
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
  ingress:
  - from: []   # API server needs to reach it
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8443
EOF

# 5. SecurityContext — harden the webhook pod
# Run as non-root, read-only filesystem, drop all capabilities
Explanation

Admission webhooks are security-critical infrastructure — they decide what runs in your cluster. On CKS, secure them with: TLS (mandatory — API server only calls HTTPS), minimal RBAC, NetworkPolicy to restrict access, hardened SecurityContext, and monitoring. A compromised webhook can approve any resource or inject malicious mutations.

CKS-4
What are the security risks of Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and how do you mitigate them?
Show Answer
Answer
# Risk 1: CRDs with cluster-scoped RBAC
# Audit who can create CRDs
kubectl auth can-i create customresourcedefinitions --as=system:serviceaccount:default:default
# Should be "no" for most service accounts

# Risk 2: CRDs without validation
# Always add OpenAPI validation:
# spec.versions[].schema.openAPIV3Schema

# Risk 3: Operator with excessive RBAC
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] |
  select(.subjects[]?.kind == "ServiceAccount") |
  select(.roleRef.name == "cluster-admin") |
  {binding: .metadata.name,
   sa: "\(.subjects[0].namespace)/\(.subjects[0].name)"}'

# Risk 4: CRDs storing sensitive data without encryption
# Ensure EncryptionConfiguration covers CR resources:
resources:
- resources:
  - secrets
  - certificates.cert-manager.io  # CRDs with sensitive data
  providers:
  - aescbc:
      keys:
      - name: key1
        secret: <base64-key>

# Risk 5: Conversion webhooks
# CRD version conversion webhooks have same risks
# as admission webhooks — secure with TLS + RBAC
Explanation

CRDs extend the API but don't automatically get security controls. On CKS, CRD risks include: no built-in validation (add OpenAPI schemas), operators with cluster-admin (use namespaced roles), CRs storing sensitive data (add to EncryptionConfiguration), no admission control (add Gatekeeper constraints for CRDs). Audit CRD RBAC with kubectl auth can-i.

CKS-5
How do you audit all admission webhooks in the cluster and verify they are using TLS correctly?
Show Answer
Answer
# List all validating webhooks
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] | {
    name: .metadata.name,
    webhooks: [.webhooks[] | {
      name: .name,
      failurePolicy: .failurePolicy,
      service: "\(.clientConfig.service.namespace // "")/\(.clientConfig.service.name // "")",
      hasCA: (.clientConfig.caBundle != null),
      sideEffects: .sideEffects,
      timeout: .timeoutSeconds
    }]
  }'

# List all mutating webhooks
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations -o json | jq -r '
  .items[] | {
    name: .metadata.name,
    webhooks: [.webhooks[] | {
      name: .name,
      failurePolicy: .failurePolicy,
      hasCA: (.clientConfig.caBundle != null)
    }]
  }'

# Check for dangerous configurations:
# 1. failurePolicy: Ignore (fail-open — bad!)
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations -o json | jq -r '
  .items[].webhooks[] |
  select(.failurePolicy == "Ignore") |
  .name'

# 2. Missing caBundle (insecure TLS)
# 3. sideEffects not set to "None"
# 4. Very long timeouts (DoS risk)

# Verify webhook service is running
kubectl get pods -n security -l app=webhook-server
kubectl logs -n security -l app=webhook-server --tail=20
Explanation

On CKS, audit admission webhooks for: failurePolicy: Ignore (should be Fail for security webhooks — fail-closed), missing caBundle (TLS not verified), sideEffects not None (webhook makes external changes), and long timeouts (API latency). All security-critical webhooks should fail closed to prevent bypass when the webhook is unavailable.

📋 Appendix A: kubectl Command Reference

APPENDIX 📋 REFERENCE

A comprehensive reference of kubectl commands organized by category. Bookmark this appendix for quick access during development and troubleshooting.

💡 kubectl Pro Tips

  • Tab completion: Add source <(kubectl completion bash) to your shell
  • Aliases: Use alias k=kubectl for faster typing
  • Context switching: Use kubectx and kubens tools
  • Dry run: Always test with --dry-run=client -o yaml first

📖 kubectl Command Structure

  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    kubectl  [VERB]  [RESOURCE]  [NAME]  [FLAGS]            │
  │                       │        │        │         │        │               │
  │                       ▼        ▼        ▼         ▼        ▼               │
  │                    command   action   type    instance  options            │
  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

  COMMON VERBS:
  ┌─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────┬─────────────────────┐
  │  📋 READ        │  ✏️ MODIFY       │  🗑️ DELETE       │  🐛 DEBUG           │
  ├─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────┼─────────────────────┤
  │  get            │  create         │  delete         │  logs               │
  │  describe       │  apply          │  delete -f      │  exec               │
  │  explain        │  edit           │                 │  port-forward       │
  │  logs           │  patch          │                 │  attach             │
  │  top            │  scale          │                 │  cp                 │
  │  events         │  set            │                 │  debug              │
  └─────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────┴─────────────────────┘

  COMMON RESOURCES (shortnames):
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  pods (po) │ services (svc) │ deployments (deploy) │ configmaps (cm)    │
  │  nodes (no) │ namespaces (ns) │ replicasets (rs) │ secrets │ ingress   │
  │  persistentvolumeclaims (pvc) │ serviceaccounts (sa) │ endpoints (ep)  │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

🛠️ Shell Setup for Productivity

🐧
Bash/Zsh Setup
# Add to ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc
alias k=kubectl
alias kg='kubectl get'
alias kd='kubectl describe'
alias kl='kubectl logs'
alias kx='kubectl exec -it'
alias kns='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'

# Enable completion
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k
💠
PowerShell Setup
# Add to $PROFILE
Set-Alias -Name k -Value kubectl
function kg { kubectl get $args }
function kd { kubectl describe $args }
function kl { kubectl logs $args }

# Enable completion
kubectl completion powershell | Out-String | Invoke-Expression

A.1 Cluster Commands

CommandDescription
kubectl cluster-info Display cluster endpoint info (API server, CoreDNS)
kubectl get nodes List all nodes in the cluster
kubectl get nodes -o wide List nodes with IP addresses, OS, kernel version
kubectl describe node <node-name> Full node details (capacity, conditions, pods)
kubectl get namespaces List all namespaces
kubectl api-resources List all API resource types (Pods, Services, etc.)
kubectl api-versions List supported API versions (v1, apps/v1, etc.)
kubectl top nodes Show node CPU/memory usage (requires metrics-server)
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp View cluster events sorted by time
kubectl get componentstatuses Check health of control plane components

A.2 Pod Commands

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │               POD DEBUGGING WORKFLOW                                     │
  │                                                                          │
  │  1. List       2. Describe      3. Logs         4. Exec                  │
  │  ┌─────┐       ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐     ┌─────────┐              │
  │  │ get │ ────▶ │describe │ ──▶ │  logs   │ ──▶ │  exec   │              │
  │  │pods │       │  pod    │     │ -f/prev │     │ -it sh  │              │
  │  └─────┘       └─────────┘     └─────────┘     └─────────┘              │
  │   What's       What          What's app        Look                      │
  │   running?     happened?     printing?         inside!                   │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
CommandDescription
kubectl get pods List pods in current namespace
kubectl get pods -A List pods in ALL namespaces
kubectl get pods -o wide Show pod IP addresses and nodes
kubectl get pods -w Watch pods (live updates)
kubectl describe pod <name> Full pod details (events, conditions, volumes)
kubectl logs <pod> View pod logs
kubectl logs <pod> -c <container> Logs from specific container (multi-container pods)
kubectl logs <pod> -f Stream logs (follow)
kubectl logs <pod> --previous Logs from crashed/restarted container
kubectl logs <pod> --tail=100 Last 100 lines of logs
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- bash Shell into a pod (use sh if no bash)
kubectl exec <pod> -- cat /etc/config Run a single command in pod
kubectl port-forward <pod> 8080:80 Forward local port 8080 to pod port 80
kubectl cp <pod>:/path ./local Copy files from pod to local machine
kubectl cp ./local <pod>:/path Copy files to pod
kubectl delete pod <name> Delete a pod
kubectl delete pod <name> --force Force delete stuck pod
kubectl top pods Show pod CPU/memory usage
kubectl run debug --image=busybox --rm -it -- sh Quick debug pod with shell

A.3 Deployment Commands

  create ──▶ rollout status ──▶ scale ──▶ set image ──▶ rollout undo
    │            │                 │           │              │
    ▼            ▼                 ▼           ▼              ▼
  Deploy    Watch progress     Replicas   Update version   Rollback!
CommandDescription
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=myimg Create deployment imperatively
kubectl get deployments List deployments
kubectl describe deployment <name> Full deployment details
kubectl rollout status deployment/anihpj Watch rollout progress
kubectl rollout history deployment/anihpj View rollout history (revisions)
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj Rollback to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/anihpj --to-revision=2 Rollback to specific revision
kubectl rollout pause deployment/anihpj Pause rollout
kubectl rollout resume deployment/anihpj Resume paused rollout
kubectl scale deployment/anihpj --replicas=5 Scale to 5 replicas
kubectl set image deployment/anihpj django=img:v2 Update container image
kubectl edit deployment anihpj Edit deployment in $EDITOR
kubectl get rs List ReplicaSets (managed by Deployment)

A.4 Service & Networking Commands

CommandDescription
kubectl get services / kubectl get svc List services
kubectl describe service <name> Service details (endpoints, port mapping)
kubectl expose deployment/anihpj --port=80 Create ClusterIP service for deployment
kubectl expose deployment/anihpj --type=NodePort --port=80 Create NodePort service
kubectl get endpoints List service endpoints (pod IPs)
kubectl get ingress List Ingress resources
kubectl describe ingress <name> Ingress details (host rules, paths, TLS)
kubectl get networkpolicies List NetworkPolicies
kubectl port-forward svc/anihpj 8080:80 Forward local port to service
kubectl run tmp --rm -it --image=busybox -- wget -qO- http://anihpj Test service connectivity from inside cluster

A.5 Config & Secrets Commands

CommandDescription
kubectl get configmaps / kubectl get cm List ConfigMaps
kubectl describe configmap <name> View ConfigMap contents
kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-file=config.txt Create ConfigMap from file
kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-literal=KEY=value Create ConfigMap from literal
kubectl get secrets List Secrets
kubectl describe secret <name> View Secret metadata (not values)
kubectl get secret <name> -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d Decode and view secret value
kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=password=xxx Create Secret from literal
kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-file=./key.pem Create Secret from file
kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred --docker-server=... --docker-username=... --docker-password=... Create image pull secret

A.6 Apply, Diff & Dry-Run

🎯 Best Practice: Apply vs Create

kubectl apply -f is declarative and idempotent — use it for production. kubectl create is imperative and fails if resource exists.

CommandDescription
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml Apply configuration (create or update)
kubectl apply -f ./manifests/ Apply all YAML files in directory
kubectl apply -f https://url/manifest.yaml Apply from URL
kubectl apply -k ./kustomize-dir/ Apply Kustomization
kubectl diff -f deployment.yaml Preview changes before applying
kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml Test apply without sending to cluster
kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=server Server-side validation (checks quotas, etc.)
kubectl delete -f deployment.yaml Delete resources defined in file
kubectl replace -f deployment.yaml Replace resource (must already exist)
kubectl patch deployment anihpj -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}' Patch specific field

A.7 Context & Namespace

  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                         CONTEXT                                  │
  │                                                                  │
  │  Context = Cluster + User + Namespace                            │
  │                                                                  │
  │  ~/.kube/config:                                                 │
  │  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐│
  │  │ contexts:                                                    ││
  │  │ - name: dev-context                                          ││
  │  │   context:                                                   ││
  │  │     cluster: dev-cluster    ◄── Which cluster?              ││
  │  │     user: dev-user          ◄── Which credentials?          ││
  │  │     namespace: development  ◄── Default namespace           ││
  │  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘│
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
CommandDescription
kubectl config get-contexts List all contexts (clusters you can access)
kubectl config current-context Show current context
kubectl config use-context dev-cluster Switch to different context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=production Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config view View kubeconfig (clusters, users, contexts)
kubectl get pods -n kube-system List pods in specific namespace
kubectl create namespace staging Create new namespace
kubectl delete namespace staging Delete namespace (and ALL resources in it!)
🛠️ Recommended Tools for Context Switching
🔀
kubectx

Fast context switching: kubectx dev

📁
kubens

Fast namespace switching: kubens production

🖥️
k9s

Terminal UI for Kubernetes

A.8 Output Formatting

Flag / CommandDescription
-o wide Additional columns (node, IP, etc.)
-o yaml Full YAML output
-o json Full JSON output
-o name Just resource names (pod/anihpj-xxx)
-o jsonpath='{.spec.replicas}' Extract specific field with JSONPath
-o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}' Extract from list
-o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase Custom column output
--sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp Sort results by field
-l app=anihpj Filter by label
-l 'app in (anihpj,redis)' Filter with set-based selector
--field-selector status.phase=Running Filter by field
--field-selector metadata.name!=kube-system Exclude by field
Useful JSONPath Examples 
# Get all pod names
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'

# Get pod names with newlines
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get container images used by pods
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].image}' | tr ' ' '\n' | sort -u

# Get pod name and status
kubectl get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.phase}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get all node IPs
kubectl get nodes -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.addresses[?(@.type=="InternalIP")].address}'

# Get secret value (decoded)
kubectl get secret mysecret -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d

✨ Generate YAML from Commands (Super Useful!)

Use --dry-run=client -o yaml to generate YAML without actually creating resources:

# Generate Deployment YAML
kubectl create deployment anihpj --image=myimage:v1 --dry-run=client -o yaml > deployment.yaml

# Generate Service YAML
kubectl expose deployment anihpj --port=80 --dry-run=client -o yaml > service.yaml

# Generate ConfigMap from file
kubectl create configmap myconfig --from-file=config.txt --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Generate Secret
kubectl create secret generic mysecret --from-literal=password=xxx --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Generate Job YAML
kubectl create job myjob --image=busybox -- echo "hello" --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Generate CronJob YAML
kubectl create cronjob mycron --image=busybox --schedule="*/5 * * * *" -- date --dry-run=client -o yaml

# Export existing resource as YAML (to recreate elsewhere)
kubectl get deployment anihpj -o yaml > exported-deployment.yaml

⚡ Imperative One-Liners (Quick Tasks)

CommandWhat it does
kubectl run nginx --image=nginx Create a pod quickly
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx --replicas=3 Create deployment with 3 replicas
kubectl expose pod nginx --port=80 --name=nginx-svc Create service for a pod
kubectl set image deployment/web nginx=nginx:1.25 Update container image
kubectl label pod nginx env=prod Add label to resource
kubectl annotate pod nginx description="my app" Add annotation
kubectl taint node node1 key=value:NoSchedule Add taint to node
kubectl cordon node1 Mark node as unschedulable
kubectl drain node1 --ignore-daemonsets Safely evict pods from node

📋 Appendix A Summary: kubectl Quick Reference

  • 🔍 Inspect: get, describe, logs
  • 🚀 Deploy: apply -f, create, scale
  • 🔄 Update: set image, rollout, edit
  • 🐛 Debug: exec, port-forward, logs --previous
  • 📊 Format: -o yaml/json/jsonpath, -l label

📝 Appendix B: YAML Templates

APPENDIX 📝 TEMPLATES

Copy-paste ready YAML templates for common Kubernetes resources. All templates include <CHANGE-ME> placeholders for values you need to customize.

📋 How to Use These Templates

  1. Copy the template to a .yaml file
  2. Replace all <CHANGE-ME-*> placeholders with your values
  3. Preview: kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml
  4. Apply: kubectl apply -f file.yaml

B.1 Pod (Basic)

Minimal Pod definition — rarely used directly (use Deployments instead).

pod-basic.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-POD-NAME>        # e.g., anihpj-pod
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>  # e.g., default
  labels:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP-LABEL>      # e.g., anihpj
spec:
  containers:
    - name: <CHANGE-ME-CONTAINER>   # e.g., django
      image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>      # e.g., myregistry/anihpj:v1
      ports:
        - containerPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>  # e.g., 8000

B.2 Pod (Full/Production)
