Production-ready Pod with resources, probes, security context, and environment variables.

pod-production.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-POD-NAME>
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
  labels:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
    environment: <CHANGE-ME-ENV>  # e.g., production
spec:
  # Security: Run as non-root
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    fsGroup: 1000
  
  containers:
    - name: <CHANGE-ME-CONTAINER>
      image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>
      imagePullPolicy: Always
      
      ports:
        - containerPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
          protocol: TCP
      
      # Resource limits (REQUIRED for production)
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "128Mi"
          cpu: "100m"
        limits:
          memory: "256Mi"
          cpu: "500m"
      
      # Environment from ConfigMap and Secret
      envFrom:
        - configMapRef:
            name: <CHANGE-ME-CONFIGMAP>
        - secretRef:
            name: <CHANGE-ME-SECRET>
      
      # Individual env vars
      env:
        - name: <CHANGE-ME-VAR-NAME>
          value: "<CHANGE-ME-VALUE>"
      
      # Liveness probe (is container alive?)
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /healthz
          port: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
        initialDelaySeconds: 30
        periodSeconds: 10
        failureThreshold: 3
      
      # Readiness probe (can container receive traffic?)
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /ready
          port: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
        initialDelaySeconds: 5
        periodSeconds: 5
      
      # Volume mounts
      volumeMounts:
        - name: data-volume
          mountPath: /app/data
  
  # ImagePullSecret for private registry
  imagePullSecrets:
    - name: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE-PULL-SECRET>
  
  # Volumes
  volumes:
    - name: data-volume
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: <CHANGE-ME-PVC-NAME>
  
  # Pod restart policy
  restartPolicy: Always

B.3 Deployment

Production Deployment with rolling update strategy, replicas, and pod template.

deployment.yaml 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-DEPLOYMENT-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
  labels:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
spec:
  replicas: <CHANGE-ME-REPLICAS>  # e.g., 3
  
  # How to find pods to manage
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>  # Must match pod template labels
  
  # Rolling update strategy
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1        # Extra pods during update
      maxUnavailable: 0  # Zero downtime
  
  # Pod template
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>  # Must match selector
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: <CHANGE-ME-CONTAINER>
          image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>:<CHANGE-ME-TAG>
          ports:
            - containerPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
          
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"
            limits:
              memory: "256Mi"
              cpu: "500m"
          
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: <CHANGE-ME-CONFIGMAP>
            - secretRef:
                name: <CHANGE-ME-SECRET>
          
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
            initialDelaySeconds: 30
            periodSeconds: 10
          
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 5
      
      # Spread pods across nodes
      affinity:
        podAntiAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            - weight: 100
              podAffinityTerm:
                labelSelector:
                  matchLabels:
                    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
                topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname

B.4 Service: ClusterIP

Internal-only service for pod-to-pod communication within the cluster.

🔒
ClusterIP

Internal only. Default type.

🚪
NodePort

Exposes on node IP + port

☁️
LoadBalancer

Cloud LB with external IP

service-clusterip.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-SERVICE-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-svc
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  type: ClusterIP  # Default, internal only
  
  # Which pods to route traffic to
  selector:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>  # Match pod labels
  
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80            # Service port (what clients use)
      targetPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>  # Container port (e.g., 8000)
      protocol: TCP

# Access within cluster:
# - http://<service-name>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
# - http://anihpj-svc.default.svc.cluster.local
# - http://anihpj-svc (same namespace)

B.5 Service: NodePort

Exposes service on each node's IP at a static port (30000-32767).

service-nodeport.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-SERVICE-NAME>
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  type: NodePort
  
  selector:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
  
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80              # ClusterIP port
      targetPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>  # Container port
      nodePort: <CHANGE-ME-NODEPORT>  # 30000-32767, or omit for auto
      protocol: TCP

# Access:
# - http://<any-node-ip>:<nodePort>
# - http://192.168.1.100:30080

B.6 Service: LoadBalancer

Provisions cloud load balancer with external IP (AWS ELB, GCP LB, Azure LB).

service-loadbalancer.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-SERVICE-NAME>
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
  annotations:
    # Cloud-specific annotations (examples):
    # AWS: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
    # GCP: cloud.google.com/load-balancer-type: "Internal"
    # Azure: service.beta.kubernetes.io/azure-load-balancer-internal: "true"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  
  selector:
    app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
  
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
      protocol: TCP
    - name: https
      port: 443
      targetPort: <CHANGE-ME-HTTPS-PORT>
      protocol: TCP

# After creation, check external IP:
# kubectl get svc <service-name> -w
# Access: http://<EXTERNAL-IP>

B.7 ConfigMap

Store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs or files.

configmap.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-CONFIGMAP-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-config
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
data:
  # Simple key-value pairs
  DEBUG: "False"
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "*"
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-svc"
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  
  # Multi-line file content
  nginx.conf: |
    server {
        listen 80;
        server_name <CHANGE-ME-DOMAIN>;
        location / {
            proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8000;
        }
    }

# Usage in Pod:
# envFrom:
#   - configMapRef:
#       name: anihpj-config
#
# Or mount as file:
# volumeMounts:
#   - name: config-volume
#     mountPath: /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
#     subPath: nginx.conf
# volumes:
#   - name: config-volume
#     configMap:
#       name: anihpj-config

B.8 Secret

Store sensitive data (passwords, tokens, certificates). Values must be base64-encoded.

⚠️ Security Warning

Secrets are only base64-encoded, NOT encrypted by default. For production, enable encryption at rest and use external secret managers (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.).

secret.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-SECRET-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-secrets
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
type: Opaque  # Generic secret

# Values MUST be base64-encoded
# Encode: echo -n "mypassword" | base64
data:
  SECRET_KEY: <CHANGE-ME-BASE64-VALUE>  # echo -n "your-secret" | base64
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: <CHANGE-ME-BASE64-VALUE>

# OR use stringData for plain text (auto-encoded)
stringData:
  API_KEY: <CHANGE-ME-PLAIN-VALUE>

---
# Docker Registry Secret (for private images)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: regcred
type: kubernetes.io/dockerconfigjson
data:
  .dockerconfigjson: <CHANGE-ME-DOCKER-CONFIG-BASE64>

# Create with CLI instead:
# kubectl create secret docker-registry regcred \
#   --docker-server=https://index.docker.io/v1/ \
#   --docker-username=<USER> \
#   --docker-password=<PASS>

B.9 Ingress

HTTP/HTTPS routing with host-based and path-based rules. Requires an Ingress Controller.

ingress.yaml 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-INGRESS-NAME>
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
  annotations:
    # NGINX Ingress Controller annotations
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/ssl-redirect: "true"
    # For cert-manager TLS
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: "letsencrypt-prod"
spec:
  ingressClassName: nginx  # Or: traefik, alb, etc.
  
  # TLS configuration
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - <CHANGE-ME-DOMAIN>  # e.g., anihpj.example.com
      secretName: <CHANGE-ME-TLS-SECRET>  # TLS cert secret
  
  rules:
    # Host-based routing
    - host: <CHANGE-ME-DOMAIN>
      http:
        paths:
          # Path-based routing
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: <CHANGE-ME-SERVICE>  # e.g., anihpj-svc
                port:
                  number: 80
          
          # API routes to different service
          - path: /api
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: <CHANGE-ME-API-SERVICE>
                port:
                  number: 80

B.10 PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

Request persistent storage for your pods. StorageClass handles dynamic provisioning.

pvc.yaml 
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-PVC-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-data
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce  # RWO: single node, RWX: multiple nodes
  
  storageClassName: <CHANGE-ME-STORAGECLASS>  # e.g., standard, gp2, premium-rwo
  # Omit storageClassName to use default
  
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: <CHANGE-ME-SIZE>  # e.g., 10Gi

# Usage in Pod:
# spec:
#   volumes:
#     - name: data
#       persistentVolumeClaim:
#         claimName: anihpj-data
#   containers:
#     - volumeMounts:
#         - name: data
#           mountPath: /app/data

B.11 HorizontalPodAutoscaler (HPA)

Automatically scale pods based on CPU/memory usage. Requires metrics-server.

hpa.yaml 
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-HPA-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-hpa
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: <CHANGE-ME-DEPLOYMENT>  # Deployment to scale
  
  minReplicas: <CHANGE-ME-MIN>  # e.g., 2
  maxReplicas: <CHANGE-ME-MAX>  # e.g., 10
  
  metrics:
    # Scale based on CPU
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: cpu
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 70  # Scale up when CPU > 70%
    
    # Scale based on Memory
    - type: Resource
      resource:
        name: memory
        target:
          type: Utilization
          averageUtilization: 80
  
  # Scaling behavior
  behavior:
    scaleDown:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 300  # Wait 5 min before scale down
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 10
          periodSeconds: 60
    scaleUp:
      stabilizationWindowSeconds: 0
      policies:
        - type: Percent
          value: 100
          periodSeconds: 15

# Prerequisite: pods must have resource requests defined!

B.12 NetworkPolicy

Control network traffic between pods. Requires a CNI that supports NetworkPolicies (Calico, Cilium, etc.).

networkpolicy.yaml 
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-POLICY-NAME>  # e.g., anihpj-allow-web
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  # Apply to pods with this label
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>  # e.g., anihpj
  
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
  
  # Incoming traffic rules
  ingress:
    # Allow from pods with label role=frontend
    - from:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              role: frontend
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
    
    # Allow from specific namespace
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              name: ingress-nginx
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 80
  
  # Outgoing traffic rules
  egress:
    # Allow DNS
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector: {}
          podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              k8s-app: kube-dns
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
    
    # Allow to database pods
    - to:
        - podSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: postgres
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 5432

---
# Default deny all ingress (apply first, then allow specific)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  podSelector: {}  # All pods
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress

🎁 Bonus Templates

Additional commonly-used resource templates.

job.yaml — Run-to-completion task 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-JOB-NAME>  # e.g., db-migrate
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  ttlSecondsAfterFinished: 100  # Auto-delete after 100s
  backoffLimit: 3  # Retry 3 times on failure
  template:
    spec:
      restartPolicy: Never  # or OnFailure
      containers:
        - name: <CHANGE-ME-CONTAINER>
          image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>
          command: ["python", "manage.py", "migrate"]
          envFrom:
            - secretRef:
                name: <CHANGE-ME-SECRET>
cronjob.yaml — Scheduled task 
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-CRONJOB-NAME>  # e.g., daily-backup
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  schedule: "<CHANGE-ME-SCHEDULE>"  # Cron format: "0 2 * * *" = 2am daily
  concurrencyPolicy: Forbid  # Don't overlap runs
  successfulJobsHistoryLimit: 3
  failedJobsHistoryLimit: 1
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
          containers:
            - name: backup
              image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>
              command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "backup-script.sh"]

# Cron Schedule Quick Reference:
# ┌───────────── minute (0 - 59)
# │ ┌───────────── hour (0 - 23)
# │ │ ┌───────────── day of month (1 - 31)
# │ │ │ ┌───────────── month (1 - 12)
# │ │ │ │ ┌───────────── day of week (0 - 6) (Sun=0)
# │ │ │ │ │
# * * * * *
# Examples: "*/15 * * * *" = every 15 min, "0 0 * * 0" = Sunday midnight
statefulset.yaml — Stateful apps (databases) 
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: <CHANGE-ME-STATEFULSET>  # e.g., postgres
  namespace: <CHANGE-ME-NAMESPACE>
spec:
  serviceName: "<CHANGE-ME-HEADLESS-SVC>"  # Must exist
  replicas: <CHANGE-ME-REPLICAS>
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: <CHANGE-ME-APP>
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: <CHANGE-ME-CONTAINER>
          image: <CHANGE-ME-IMAGE>
          ports:
            - containerPort: <CHANGE-ME-PORT>
          volumeMounts:
            - name: data
              mountPath: /var/lib/data
  # Each replica gets its own PVC
  volumeClaimTemplates:
    - metadata:
        name: data
      spec:
        accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
        storageClassName: <CHANGE-ME-STORAGECLASS>
        resources:
          requests:
            storage: <CHANGE-ME-SIZE>  # e.g., 10Gi

# StatefulSet gives:
# - Stable network identities: pod-0, pod-1, pod-2
# - Stable storage: each pod keeps its PVC
# - Ordered startup/shutdown

✅ YAML Validation Tips

🔍
Before Applying
kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=client
kubectl apply -f file.yaml --dry-run=server
kubectl diff -f file.yaml
📋
Validate Structure
kubectl explain deployment.spec
kubectl explain pod.spec.containers
Use yamllint or kubeval tools

📝 Appendix B Summary: YAML Templates

TemplateUse Case
B.1-B.2 PodDirect pod definition (rare, use Deployment)
B.3 DeploymentStateless apps with replicas & rolling updates
B.4 ClusterIPInternal service discovery
B.5 NodePortDevelopment/testing external access
B.6 LoadBalancerProduction external access (cloud)
B.7 ConfigMapNon-sensitive configuration
B.8 SecretPasswords, tokens, certificates
B.9 IngressHTTP/HTTPS routing with domains
B.10 PVCPersistent storage for pods
B.11 HPAAuto-scale based on metrics
B.12 NetworkPolicyPod-to-pod network security
🎁 JobOne-time run-to-completion tasks (migrations)
🎁 CronJobScheduled recurring tasks (backups)
🎁 StatefulSetStateful apps (databases) with stable IDs

🔧 Appendix C: Common Errors & Solutions

APPENDIX 🔧 TROUBLESHOOTING

A comprehensive troubleshooting guide for common Kubernetes errors. Each error includes the symptom, cause, and step-by-step solutions.

🚑 First Response Debug Commands

kubectl get pods -o wide — Check pod status and node
kubectl describe pod <name> — See events and conditions
kubectl logs <pod> --previous — Logs from crashed container
kubectl get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp — Recent cluster events

C.1 Pod Errors

Error/StatusPossible CausesSolution
ImagePullBackOff
  • Image name typo
  • Image doesn't exist
  • Private registry, no imagePullSecret
  • Network issues
  1. Verify image: docker pull <image>
  2. Check spelling in YAML
  3. Add imagePullSecrets for private registry
  4. kubectl describe pod → check Events
CrashLoopBackOff
  • Application crashes on startup
  • Missing env vars or config
  • Database connection fails
  • Port conflicts
  • Container exits immediately
  1. kubectl logs <pod> --previous
  2. Verify env vars: kubectl exec <pod> -- env
  3. Test container locally first
  4. Check liveness probe isn't killing healthy pod
Pending
  • Insufficient CPU/memory
  • No matching node (nodeSelector, affinity)
  • PVC not bound
  • Taints with no tolerations
  1. kubectl describe pod → check Events
  2. kubectl get nodes -o wide — node availability
  3. Reduce resource requests
  4. Check PVC: kubectl get pvc
Error
  • Container command failed
  • Init container failed
  • Volume mount failure
  1. kubectl logs <pod>
  2. kubectl logs <pod> -c <init-container>
  3. kubectl describe pod → Container statuses
Init:Error
  • Init container crashed
  • Init script/command failed
  1. kubectl logs <pod> -c <init-container-name>
  2. Verify init container command
OOMKilled
  • Container exceeded memory limit
  • Memory leak in application
  1. Increase memory limit
  2. Profile app memory usage
  3. kubectl top pods to monitor
Evicted
  • Node under resource pressure
  • Disk pressure, memory pressure
  1. kubectl describe node → Conditions
  2. Add more nodes or clean up disk
  3. Set proper resource limits
CreateContainerConfigError
  • ConfigMap or Secret not found
  • Incorrect key in envFrom
  1. kubectl get configmaps,secrets
  2. Verify names match YAML references
  3. kubectl describe pod → check Events

C.2 Service & Networking Errors

Error/SymptomPossible CausesSolution
Connection refused to Service
  • No pods match selector
  • Pods not Ready
  • Wrong targetPort
  1. kubectl get endpoints <svc> — should show pod IPs
  2. Verify selector labels match pod labels
  3. Check targetPort matches containerPort
No endpoints available
  • Service selector doesn't match pods
  • Pods failing readiness probe
  • No pods running
  1. Compare: kubectl get svc -o yaml selector vs kubectl get pods --show-labels
  2. kubectl get pods — check READY column
DNS resolution failed
  • CoreDNS not running
  • Wrong service name
  • Cross-namespace without FQDN
  1. kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
  2. Use FQDN: svc-name.namespace.svc.cluster.local
  3. Test: kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- nslookup svc-name
Ingress 404 Not Found
  • Backend service not found
  • Path doesn't match rules
  • IngressClass not set
  1. kubectl describe ingress — check backend
  2. Verify service exists and has endpoints
  3. Check ingressClassName matches controller
Ingress 503 Service Unavailable
  • Backend pods not ready
  • Service has no endpoints
  1. kubectl get endpoints <backend-svc>
  2. Check pod readiness probes
LoadBalancer stuck Pending
  • Not running on cloud provider
  • Cloud controller not configured
  • Quota exceeded
  1. Use NodePort for local clusters (Minikube, Kind)
  2. Install MetalLB for bare-metal LB
  3. Check cloud provider quota/limits
NetworkPolicy blocking traffic
  • Default deny policy applied
  • Ingress/egress rules too restrictive
  1. kubectl get networkpolicies
  2. Temporarily delete policy to test
  3. Verify pod labels match policy selectors

C.3 Deployment Errors

Error/SymptomPossible CausesSolution
Rollout stuck
  • New pods failing readiness
  • Insufficient resources
  • Image pull error
  1. kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>
  2. kubectl get pods — check new pod status
  3. kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>
ProgressDeadlineExceeded
  • Rollout taking longer than deadline
  • Pods not becoming Ready
  1. Increase progressDeadlineSeconds
  2. Debug why pods aren't Ready
  3. Rollback: kubectl rollout undo
Zero Available replicas
  • All pods crashing
  • Resource quota exceeded
  1. kubectl describe deployment → Events
  2. kubectl get pods — debug individual pods
  3. kubectl get resourcequota
ReplicaSet not updating
  • Pod template unchanged
  • Image tag same (e.g., :latest)
  1. Use unique image tags (not :latest)
  2. Force restart: kubectl rollout restart deployment/<name>
HPA not scaling
  • metrics-server not installed
  • No resource requests defined
  • Target already at min/max
  1. kubectl get hpa — check TARGETS column
  2. Verify pods have resources.requests
  3. kubectl top pods — metrics working?

C.4 Django-Specific Errors

🐍 Django on Kubernetes Context

These errors are common when deploying Django apps (like anihpj) to Kubernetes.

ErrorCauseSolution
DisallowedHost
  • Hostname not in ALLOWED_HOSTS
  • Accessing via IP instead of domain
  1. Add to ConfigMap: ALLOWED_HOSTS=* (dev) or specific domains
  2. Include Service name: anihpj-svc
OperationalError: could not connect to server
  • Database Service not found
  • Database pod not ready
  • Wrong DB_HOST
  1. Verify: kubectl get svc — DB service exists
  2. Use Service name: DB_HOST=postgres-svc
  3. Check DB pod: kubectl get pods -l app=postgres
Migrations not running
  • No init container for migrations
  • Init container ran but failed silently
  1. Add init container that runs python manage.py migrate
  2. Or use Job for migrations before Deployment
  3. Check: kubectl logs <pod> -c init-migrate
Static files 404
  • collectstatic not run
  • STATIC_ROOT not served
  1. Run collectstatic in init container or build
  2. Use WhiteNoise or nginx sidecar
  3. Mount static volume between containers
SECRET_KEY error
  • SECRET_KEY not set
  • Secret not mounted
  1. Create Secret: kubectl create secret generic django-secret --from-literal=SECRET_KEY=...
  2. Add secretRef in envFrom
Gunicorn worker timeout
  • Slow requests killing workers
  • Readiness probe timing out
  1. Increase: gunicorn --timeout 120
  2. Match readiness probe timeout
  3. Profile slow endpoints

C.5 Debugging Flowchart

Follow this systematic approach when troubleshooting Kubernetes deployments:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                            🔍 KUBERNETES DEBUGGING FLOWCHART                              │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

                                    ┌─────────────┐
                                    │   START     │
                                    │ App not     │
                                    │ working?    │
                                    └──────┬──────┘
                                           │
                                           ▼
                            ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                            │  kubectl get pods            │
                            │  Are pods Running & Ready?   │
                            └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                                           │
                      ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
                      │ NO                 │                YES │
                      ▼                    │                    ▼
        ┌─────────────────────┐            │     ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │ What status?        │            │     │ kubectl get svc             │
        └──────────┬──────────┘            │     │ Service exists & correct?   │
                   │                       │     └──────────────┬──────────────┘
  ┌────────┬───────┴───────┬────────┐      │                    │
  │        │               │        │      │        ┌───────────┴───────────┐
  ▼        ▼               ▼        ▼      │        │ NO                YES │
┌─────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌─────┐   │        ▼                       ▼
│Pend-│ │ImagePull│ │CrashLoop │ │Error│   │ ┌────────────────┐  ┌─────────────────────┐
│ ing │ │BackOff  │ │BackOff   │ │     │   │ │Create Service  │  │kubectl get endpoints│
└──┬──┘ └────┬────┘ └────┬─────┘ └──┬──┘   │ │Check selector  │  │Has pod IPs?         │
   │         │           │          │      │ │matches pod     │  └─────────┬───────────┘
   ▼         ▼           ▼          ▼      │ │labels          │            │
┌──────┐  ┌──────┐   ┌─────────┐ ┌──────┐  │ └────────────────┘    ┌───────┴───────┐
│Check │  │Check │   │Check    │ │Check │  │                       │ NO        YES │
│nodes │  │image │   │logs     │ │logs  │  │                       ▼               ▼
│quota │  │name  │   │describe │ │desc- │  │            ┌──────────────┐  ┌───────────────┐
│taints│  │secret│   │probes   │ │ribe  │  │            │Fix selector  │  │Test internal  │
└──────┘  └──────┘   └─────────┘ └──────┘  │            │or readiness  │  │connectivity   │
                                           │            │probe         │  └───────┬───────┘
                                           │            └──────────────┘          │
                                           │                                      ▼
                                           │                           ┌─────────────────────┐
                                           │                           │kubectl exec test-pod│
                                           │                           │-- curl svc-name     │
                                           │                           │Works?               │
                                           │                           └─────────┬───────────┘
                                           │                                     │
                                           │                         ┌───────────┴───────────┐
                                           │                         │ NO                YES │
                                           │                         ▼                       ▼
                                           │              ┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────┐
                                           │              │Check:           │      │Check Ingress    │
                                           │              │- NetworkPolicy  │      │or external      │
                                           │              │- DNS (CoreDNS)  │      │access config    │
                                           │              │- Container port │      │                 │
                                           │              └─────────────────┘      └─────────────────┘
                                           │
                                           └───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

⚡ Quick Debug Commands by Layer

🔴
Pod Layer
kubectl get pods -o wide
kubectl describe pod <name>
kubectl logs <pod> --previous
kubectl exec -it <pod> -- sh
🟠
Service Layer
kubectl get svc,endpoints
kubectl describe svc <name>
kubectl port-forward svc/<name> 8080:80
🔵
DNS Layer
kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- nslookup svc-name
kubectl get pods -n kube-system -l k8s-app=kube-dns
🟣
Ingress Layer
kubectl get ingress
kubectl describe ingress <name>
kubectl logs -n ingress-nginx <controller-pod>

📋 5-Step Debugging Checklist

  1. Check Pod Status: kubectl get pods — Is it Running? Ready?
  2. Read Events: kubectl describe pod — What events occurred?
  3. Check Logs: kubectl logs --previous — What did the app print?
  4. Verify Service: kubectl get endpoints — Does service have pod IPs?
  5. Test Connectivity: kubectl exec -- curl — Can pods reach each other?

🧰 Useful Debug Container Images

🔧
busybox

Minimal: wget, nslookup, ping, sh

kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- sh
🌐
nicolaka/netshoot

Full network tools: curl, dig, tcpdump, iperf

kubectl run net --rm -it --image=nicolaka/netshoot -- bash
🐍
python:alpine

Python + pip for testing Django-related

kubectl run py --rm -it --image=python:alpine -- sh

👀 Log Patterns to Watch For

Log PatternMeaningAction
Connection refused Target service/port unreachable Check service endpoints, port numbers
ECONNREFUSED Node.js: Same as above Verify target container is running
Name or service not known DNS resolution failed Check service name spelling, namespace
timeout Network timeout Check NetworkPolicy, firewall rules
OOM killed Out of memory Increase memory limits
permission denied File/directory access issue Check securityContext, volume permissions
exec format error Binary/architecture mismatch Check image architecture (amd64 vs arm64)

🧹 Cleanup Commands (Use Carefully!)

# Delete all pods in failed/error state
kubectl delete pods --field-selector=status.phase=Failed

# Delete evicted pods
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.status.reason=="Evicted") | .metadata.name' | xargs kubectl delete pod

# Delete all completed jobs
kubectl delete jobs --field-selector=status.successful=1

# Force delete stuck pod (stuck in Terminating)
kubectl delete pod <name> --grace-period=0 --force

# Delete all resources with a label
kubectl delete all -l app=anihpj

# Delete namespace (WARNING: deletes everything in it!)
kubectl delete namespace <name>

📊 Resource Status Quick Reference

✅
Healthy States
  • Pod: Running + Ready: 1/1
  • Deployment: Available: true
  • Service: Endpoints has pod IPs
  • PVC: Bound
  • Node: Ready: True
❌
Problem States
  • Pod: Pending, CrashLoopBackOff
  • Pod: Ready: 0/1 (not ready)
  • Deployment: Available: 0
  • Service: Endpoints empty (<none>)
  • PVC: Pending

🔧 Appendix C Summary: Troubleshooting Reference

  • 🔴 Pod Errors: ImagePullBackOff, CrashLoopBackOff, Pending, OOMKilled
  • 🌐 Network Errors: Connection refused, no endpoints, DNS failures
  • 🚀 Deployment Errors: Stuck rollouts, HPA not scaling
  • 🐍 Django Errors: DisallowedHost, DB connection, migrations
  • 📋 Debug Process: Pod → Service → DNS → Ingress (layer by layer)
📖
🔤
📚
🎯

D Kubernetes Terminology Glossary

A comprehensive A-Z reference of 70+ essential Kubernetes terms. Each entry includes a one-line definition, a beginner-friendly analogy, and cross-references to related concepts.

📖 70+ Terms 🎯 Quick Reference 💡 Analogies
🔤 A-Z Full Alphabet
📚 70+ Terms Defined
💡 100% With Analogies
🔗 Cross Referenced

Appendix D Terminology Glossary

REFERENCE ALL LEVELS

Your go-to dictionary for Kubernetes concepts — organized alphabetically with practical analogies

🔤 Quick Jump

A C D E F G H I J K L M N O P Q R S T V W

🅰️ A

Admission Controller

Definition: A plugin that intercepts API requests before objects are persisted, validating or mutating them.

💡 Think of it as: A security checkpoint at an airport — every request gets inspected before proceeding.

🔗 Related: Mutating Webhook, Validating Webhook, API Server

Affinity

Definition: Rules that control which Nodes a Pod can be scheduled on, based on labels.

💡 Think of it as: A preference card that says "I'd like to sit near the window" — the scheduler tries to honor it.

🔗 Related: Node Selector, Taint, Toleration, Anti-Affinity

Annotation

Definition: Non-identifying metadata attached to objects (key-value pairs for tools, not selectors).

💡 Think of it as: Sticky notes on a folder — helpful info for humans/tools but not for organizing.

🔗 Related: Label, Metadata

API Server (kube-apiserver)

Definition: The front-end for the Kubernetes control plane — all cluster operations go through it.

💡 Think of it as: The receptionist at a hotel — everyone talks to them first before accessing services.

🔗 Related: etcd, Controller Manager, kubectl

🅲 C

CGroup (Control Group)

Definition: A Linux kernel feature that limits and isolates CPU, memory, and I/O for processes.

💡 Think of it as: A personal budget for each app — they can only spend what they're allocated.

🔗 Related: Resource Limits, QoS Class, Container Runtime

Cluster

Definition: A set of nodes (machines) running containerized applications managed by Kubernetes.

💡 Think of it as: A team of workers — some manage (control plane) and some do the work (worker nodes).

🔗 Related: Node, Control Plane, Worker Node

ConfigMap

Definition: A Kubernetes object that stores non-confidential configuration data as key-value pairs.

💡 Think of it as: A settings file for your app — database URLs, feature flags, app config.

🔗 Related: Secret, Environment Variable, Volume

Container Runtime

Definition: The software that runs containers (e.g., containerd, CRI-O, Docker Engine).

💡 Think of it as: The engine in a car — it's what actually makes containers run.

🔗 Related: kubelet, CRI, OCI, Docker

Controller

Definition: A control loop that watches cluster state and makes changes to move toward the desired state.

💡 Think of it as: A thermostat — constantly checking temperature and adjusting to reach the target.

🔗 Related: Controller Manager, Reconciliation Loop, Operator

CRD (Custom Resource Definition)

Definition: Extends Kubernetes API by letting you define your own resource types.

💡 Think of it as: Creating a new category in a filing system — now you can store and manage it like native resources.

🔗 Related: Operator, Custom Controller, API Extension

CronJob

Definition: A workload resource that creates Jobs on a time-based schedule (like Unix cron).

💡 Think of it as: An alarm clock that triggers tasks — "run backup every night at 2 AM".

🔗 Related: Job, Schedule, Batch Processing

🅳 D

DaemonSet

Definition: Ensures a copy of a Pod runs on all (or selected) Nodes in the cluster.

💡 Think of it as: Installing antivirus on every computer — one agent per machine.

🔗 Related: Node Selector, Toleration, Logging Agent, Monitoring Agent

Deployment

Definition: A controller that manages ReplicaSets and provides declarative updates for Pods.

💡 Think of it as: A recipe card — defines what your app should look like and how many copies to run.

🔗 Related: ReplicaSet, Rolling Update, Rollback, Pod

Desired State

Definition: The state you declare in your manifests — Kubernetes continuously works to achieve it.

💡 Think of it as: A blueprint — you say "I want 3 replicas" and Kubernetes makes it happen.

🔗 Related: Current State, Reconciliation, Controller

DNS (CoreDNS)

Definition: Cluster DNS that provides service discovery — Services get DNS names automatically.

💡 Think of it as: A phone directory — look up "postgres" and get its phone number (IP address).

🔗 Related: Service, Service Discovery, Headless Service

Docker

Definition: A platform for building, shipping, and running containers (container images + runtime).

💡 Think of it as: A shipping container company — packages your app so it runs anywhere.

🔗 Related: Container, Image, OCI, containerd

Drain

Definition: Safely evicts all Pods from a Node before maintenance (marks node unschedulable).

💡 Think of it as: Evacuating a building before renovation — everyone moves out safely first.

🔗 Related: Cordon, Eviction, Node Maintenance, PodDisruptionBudget

🅴 E

Endpoint

Definition: An IP address and port of a Pod that receives traffic from a Service.

💡 Think of it as: Individual employee desks — the Service routes calls to available desks.

🔗 Related: EndpointSlice, Service, kube-proxy

EndpointSlice

Definition: A scalable replacement for Endpoints — stores network endpoints in smaller chunks.

💡 Think of it as: Breaking a giant phonebook into smaller volumes — faster lookups.

🔗 Related: Endpoint, Service, kube-proxy

etcd

Definition: A distributed key-value store that stores all cluster data (the single source of truth).

💡 Think of it as: The brain's memory — remembers everything about the cluster's state.

🔗 Related: API Server, Cluster State, Backup

Eviction

Definition: The process of terminating a Pod (due to resource pressure, node drain, or preemption).

💡 Think of it as: Being asked to leave a crowded venue — your seat is needed elsewhere.

🔗 Related: Drain, QoS Class, PodDisruptionBudget, Preemption

Exec

Definition: Run a command inside a running container (via kubectl exec).

💡 Think of it as: SSH-ing into a server — except it's a container.

🔗 Related: kubectl, Container, Debugging

🅵 F

Finalizer

Definition: A marker that prevents object deletion until cleanup tasks are complete.

💡 Think of it as: A "do not delete" sticky note — someone must remove it before deletion can happen.

🔗 Related: Garbage Collection, Owner Reference, Cascading Delete

Flannel

Definition: A simple CNI plugin that provides layer-3 networking for Kubernetes clusters.

💡 Think of it as: A simple highway system connecting all Pods — no fancy tolls, just direct routes.

🔗 Related: CNI, Calico, Network Policy, Pod Network

🅶 G

Garbage Collection

Definition: Automatic cleanup of orphaned resources (e.g., Pods after Deployment deleted).

💡 Think of it as: A janitor that cleans up after you leave — removes unused objects.

🔗 Related: Owner Reference, Finalizer, Cascading Delete

Grace Period

Definition: Time given to a Pod to shut down gracefully before being forcefully terminated.

💡 Think of it as: A "last call" at a bar — you have 30 seconds to finish up before closing.

🔗 Related: terminationGracePeriodSeconds, SIGTERM, SIGKILL, PreStop Hook

🅷 H

Helm

Definition: The package manager for Kubernetes — bundles manifests into reusable charts.

💡 Think of it as: apt/yum for Kubernetes — helm install redis sets up Redis instantly.

🔗 Related: Chart, Release, Values, Kustomize

HPA (Horizontal Pod Autoscaler)

Definition: Automatically scales the number of Pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics.

💡 Think of it as: Hiring temps during rush hour — more customers, more workers.

🔗 Related: VPA, Metrics Server, Scaling, Deployment

HostNetwork

Definition: A Pod setting that uses the Node's network namespace instead of its own.

💡 Think of it as: Using the building's phone line instead of your own room extension.

🔗 Related: Pod Network, HostPort, DaemonSet

🅸 I

Image

Definition: A read-only template containing application code, runtime, libraries, and dependencies.

💡 Think of it as: A recipe + pre-measured ingredients — everything needed to cook the dish.

🔗 Related: Container, Registry, Dockerfile, Tag

ImagePullPolicy

Definition: Determines when kubelet pulls an image (Always, IfNotPresent, Never).

💡 Think of it as: Download preference — always get latest, use cached, or never download.

🔗 Related: Image, Registry, kubelet

Ingress

Definition: Manages external HTTP/HTTPS access to Services (host/path-based routing + TLS).

💡 Think of it as: A building lobby — directs visitors to the right floor/room based on who they're visiting.

🔗 Related: Ingress Controller, Service, TLS, Host-based Routing

Init Container

Definition: Special containers that run before app containers, used for setup tasks.

💡 Think of it as: Opening act at a concert — prepares the stage before the main performer.

🔗 Related: Container, Pod, Database Migration, wait-for-db

🅹 J

Job

Definition: A workload resource that runs Pods to completion (batch/one-time tasks).

💡 Think of it as: A contractor you hire for one project — does the job and leaves.

🔗 Related: CronJob, Completion, Parallelism, backoffLimit

🅺 K

Kubeconfig

Definition: A file that stores cluster connection info (server URL, credentials, contexts).

💡 Think of it as: Your address book + house keys — tells kubectl which cluster to talk to and how.

🔗 Related: kubectl, Context, Authentication, ~/.kube/config

kubectl

Definition: The official CLI tool for interacting with Kubernetes clusters.

💡 Think of it as: A universal remote control for your cluster — manages everything.

🔗 Related: API Server, Kubeconfig, YAML

kubelet

Definition: The agent that runs on each Node, ensuring containers are running as expected.

💡 Think of it as: A site supervisor — makes sure workers (containers) show up and do their jobs.

🔗 Related: Node, Pod, Container Runtime, API Server

kube-proxy

Definition: Network proxy on each Node that implements Service networking (load balancing).

💡 Think of it as: A phone operator routing calls — forwards traffic to the right Pod.

🔗 Related: Service, Endpoint, iptables, IPVS

Kustomize

Definition: A template-free tool for customizing Kubernetes manifests using overlays.

💡 Think of it as: A photo filter app — takes base manifests and applies environment-specific tweaks.

🔗 Related: Helm, Base/Overlay, Patch, kustomization.yaml

🅻 L

Label

Definition: Key-value pairs attached to objects for identification and grouping (app: django).

💡 Think of it as: Tags on luggage — helps identify and sort items quickly.

🔗 Related: Selector, Annotation, Metadata

LimitRange

Definition: Sets default/min/max resource constraints for Pods in a Namespace.

💡 Think of it as: Departmental budget rules — "no single project can use more than $10k".

🔗 Related: ResourceQuota, Namespace, Requests, Limits

Liveness Probe

Definition: A health check that restarts a container if it fails (deadlock detection).

💡 Think of it as: A heartbeat monitor — if no heartbeat, call the crash cart (restart).

🔗 Related: Readiness Probe, Startup Probe, Health Check

LoadBalancer

Definition: A Service type that provisions an external load balancer (cloud provider).

💡 Think of it as: A traffic cop at an intersection — distributes cars evenly across lanes.

🔗 Related: Service, NodePort, ClusterIP, External Traffic

🅼 M

Manifest

Definition: A YAML/JSON file describing a Kubernetes object (Deployment, Service, etc.).

💡 Think of it as: A blueprint — describes exactly what you want to build.

🔗 Related: YAML, kubectl apply, Declarative Configuration

Metrics Server

Definition: A cluster-wide aggregator of resource metrics (CPU/memory) from kubelets.

💡 Think of it as: A fitness tracker dashboard — collects and displays resource usage stats.

🔗 Related: HPA, kubectl top, Resource Monitoring

Minikube

Definition: A tool that runs a single-node Kubernetes cluster locally for development.

💡 Think of it as: A mini Kubernetes playground on your laptop — great for learning.

🔗 Related: Kind, Docker Desktop, Local Development

Mutating Webhook

Definition: An admission controller that can modify objects before they're stored.

💡 Think of it as: An editor who fixes your document before publishing — adds defaults, patches fields.

🔗 Related: Validating Webhook, Admission Controller, Policy

🅽 N

Namespace

Definition: A virtual cluster within a cluster — provides isolation and scope for resources.

💡 Think of it as: Folders on your computer — organize projects separately (dev/, staging/, prod/).

🔗 Related: ResourceQuota, LimitRange, RBAC, Multi-tenancy

Network Policy

Definition: Rules that control traffic flow to/from Pods (firewall rules for Pods).

💡 Think of it as: A bouncer at a club — decides who's allowed in and who's not.

🔗 Related: CNI, Calico, Ingress Rules, Egress Rules

Node

Definition: A worker machine (VM or physical) in the cluster that runs Pods.

💡 Think of it as: A server rack — the physical (or virtual) hardware where containers run.

🔗 Related: kubelet, Container Runtime, Control Plane

NodePort

Definition: A Service type that exposes a port on every Node (30000-32767 range).

💡 Think of it as: Opening a specific door on every building in a complex — visitors can enter from any building.

🔗 Related: Service, ClusterIP, LoadBalancer, Port Mapping

Node Selector

Definition: A simple Pod scheduling constraint based on Node labels.

💡 Think of it as: Asking for a specific type of hotel room — "I only want rooms with a view".

🔗 Related: Affinity, Taint, Toleration, Scheduling

🅾 O

Operator

Definition: A pattern that extends Kubernetes with custom controllers + CRDs for complex apps.

💡 Think of it as: A specialized robot assistant — knows how to manage complex apps like databases.

🔗 Related: CRD, Custom Controller, Helm, OperatorHub

OCI (Open Container Initiative)

Definition: Industry standards for container image formats and runtimes.

💡 Think of it as: Universal shipping container standards — ensures containers work everywhere.

🔗 Related: Docker, Container Runtime, Image

🅿 P

PersistentVolume (PV)

Definition: A piece of storage in the cluster provisioned by an admin or dynamically.

💡 Think of it as: An available parking spot — exists independently of who parks there.

🔗 Related: PVC, StorageClass, Volume, CSI

PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

Definition: A request for storage by a user — binds to a PV that satisfies the request.

💡 Think of it as: A parking reservation — "I need 10GB, please find me a spot".

🔗 Related: PV, StorageClass, Volume Mount

Pod

Definition: The smallest deployable unit — one or more containers sharing network and storage.

💡 Think of it as: A pea pod — multiple peas (containers) sharing the same shell.

🔗 Related: Container, Deployment, ReplicaSet, Sidecar

PodDisruptionBudget (PDB)

Definition: Limits simultaneous disruptions during voluntary operations (min/max available).

💡 Think of it as: A staffing rule — "at least 2 cashiers must be on duty at all times".

🔗 Related: Drain, Eviction, Rolling Update, HA

Probe

Definition: A diagnostic check performed by kubelet to assess container health.

💡 Think of it as: A doctor's checkup — are you alive? are you ready for visitors?

🔗 Related: Liveness Probe, Readiness Probe, Startup Probe

PriorityClass

Definition: Defines Pod scheduling priority — higher priority Pods can preempt others.

💡 Think of it as: VIP status — important pods get seated first, even if others are waiting.

🔗 Related: Preemption, Scheduler, QoS Class

🆀 Q

QoS Class (Quality of Service)

Definition: A classification (Guaranteed/Burstable/BestEffort) based on resource requests/limits.

💡 Think of it as: Priority boarding — first class (Guaranteed), business (Burstable), economy (BestEffort).

🔗 Related: Resource Requests, Limits, Eviction, OOMKilled

📊 QoS Classes Comparison

QoS Class Condition Eviction Priority Use Case
Guaranteed requests == limits (all containers) Last to be evicted Critical workloads
Burstable requests < limits Middle priority General workloads
BestEffort No requests or limits set First to be evicted Batch jobs, dev pods

🆁 R

RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)

Definition: Authorization mechanism using Roles and RoleBindings to control API access.

💡 Think of it as: Office key cards — different badges open different doors.

🔗 Related: Role, ClusterRole, ServiceAccount, RoleBinding

Readiness Probe

Definition: A check that determines if a Pod is ready to receive traffic.

💡 Think of it as: "Open" sign on a store — only send customers when the sign is lit.

🔗 Related: Liveness Probe, Startup Probe, Service, Endpoint

ReplicaSet

Definition: Ensures a specified number of Pod replicas are running at any time.

💡 Think of it as: A manager ensuring 3 cashiers are always on duty — replaces anyone who leaves.

🔗 Related: Deployment, Pod, Scaling, Selector

ResourceQuota

Definition: Limits aggregate resource consumption per Namespace.

💡 Think of it as: A department budget cap — the whole team can't exceed $100k total.

🔗 Related: LimitRange, Namespace, Multi-tenancy

Rolling Update

Definition: Update strategy that gradually replaces old Pods with new ones (zero downtime).

💡 Think of it as: Changing tires one at a time while the car is moving — no stopping.

🔗 Related: Deployment, maxSurge, maxUnavailable, Rollback

Rollback

Definition: Reverting a Deployment to a previous revision after a bad update.

💡 Think of it as: Ctrl+Z for deployments — undo the last change.

🔗 Related: Deployment, Revision, kubectl rollout undo

🆂 S

Scheduler (kube-scheduler)

Definition: Control plane component that assigns Pods to Nodes based on constraints.

💡 Think of it as: A wedding planner assigning guests to tables — considers relationships, preferences.

🔗 Related: Node, Affinity, Taint, Toleration, PriorityClass

Secret

Definition: A Kubernetes object that stores sensitive data (passwords, tokens, keys) — base64 encoded.

💡 Think of it as: A locked safe — keeps sensitive values separate from code.

🔗 Related: ConfigMap, Environment Variable, Volume Mount, External Secrets

Selector

Definition: A query that selects objects based on their labels (app=django).

💡 Think of it as: A search filter — "show me all red items in size M".

🔗 Related: Label, Service, ReplicaSet, Deployment

Service

Definition: An abstraction that provides stable networking (DNS + IP) for a set of Pods.

💡 Think of it as: A phone number that never changes — even if people behind it change shifts.

🔗 Related: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, Endpoint, DNS

ServiceAccount

Definition: An identity for Pods to authenticate against the Kubernetes API.

💡 Think of it as: An employee badge for applications — proves who they are when accessing resources.

🔗 Related: RBAC, Token, Secret, Pod Identity

Sidecar

Definition: A helper container that runs alongside the main container in a Pod.

💡 Think of it as: A sidecar on a motorcycle — rides along providing extra functionality.

🔗 Related: Pod, Multi-container, Logging Agent, Service Mesh

StatefulSet

Definition: Workload for stateful applications with stable network identity and persistent storage.

💡 Think of it as: Reserved parking spots with names — db-0, db-1, db-2 always get the same spots.

🔗 Related: PVC, Headless Service, Deployment, Databases

StorageClass

Definition: Defines storage "classes" with different performance/cost characteristics.

💡 Think of it as: Amazon shipping options — standard, express, same-day (different speed/cost).

🔗 Related: PV, PVC, Dynamic Provisioning, CSI

🆃 T

Taint

Definition: A Node property that repels Pods unless they have matching Tolerations.

💡 Think of it as: A "staff only" sign — only authorized personnel (with toleration) can enter.

🔗 Related: Toleration, Node Selector, Affinity, GPU Nodes

Toleration

Definition: A Pod property that allows scheduling on Nodes with matching Taints.

💡 Think of it as: A VIP badge — overrides the "staff only" restriction.

🔗 Related: Taint, Scheduling, DaemonSet, Special Nodes

TopologySpreadConstraint

Definition: Distributes Pods evenly across failure domains (zones, nodes, racks).

💡 Think of it as: Don't put all your eggs in one basket — spread across multiple zones.

🔗 Related: Pod Anti-Affinity, HA, Multi-zone

🆅 V

Validating Webhook

Definition: An admission controller that validates objects (can reject but not modify).

💡 Think of it as: A proofreader who rejects bad grammar but doesn't fix it for you.

🔗 Related: Mutating Webhook, Admission Controller, OPA/Gatekeeper

Velero

Definition: A backup and disaster recovery tool for Kubernetes clusters.

💡 Think of it as: A cloud backup service for your entire cluster — restore anytime.

🔗 Related: Backup, Disaster Recovery, PV Snapshots, Migration

Volume

Definition: A directory accessible to containers in a Pod — various backend types supported.

💡 Think of it as: A USB drive you can plug into your container — stores data that persists.

🔗 Related: PV, PVC, ConfigMap Volume, Secret Volume, emptyDir

VPA (Vertical Pod Autoscaler)

Definition: Automatically adjusts CPU/memory requests and limits based on usage.

💡 Think of it as: A tailor who adjusts your clothes as you gain/lose weight — right-sizes resources.

🔗 Related: HPA, Metrics Server, Resource Optimization

🆆 W

Watch

Definition: A mechanism to receive streaming updates about object changes.

💡 Think of it as: A live news feed — get notified the moment something changes.

🔗 Related: Controller, Reconciliation, kubectl get --watch

Workload

Definition: An umbrella term for Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob.

💡 Think of it as: Different types of workers — some are permanent, some temporary, some on every floor.

🔗 Related: Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob

📚 Glossary Quick Reference Card

🏗️ Core Objects

Pod, Deployment, Service, ConfigMap, Secret, Namespace

📦 Workloads

Deployment, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob, ReplicaSet

🌐 Networking

Service, Ingress, NetworkPolicy, DNS, Endpoint, kube-proxy

💾 Storage

PV, PVC, StorageClass, Volume, CSI

🔐 Security

RBAC, ServiceAccount, Secret, NetworkPolicy, Admission Controller

📊 Scaling

HPA, VPA, Metrics Server, ReplicaSet

⚠️ Common Confusions — Don't Mix These Up!

Beginners often confuse these related concepts. Here's how to tell them apart:

Concept A vs Concept B Key Difference
Label ⚔️ Annotation Labels are for selection (queries); Annotations are for metadata (tooling info)
Deployment ⚔️ StatefulSet Deployment = stateless apps; StatefulSet = stateful apps needing stable identity
ConfigMap ⚔️ Secret ConfigMap = non-sensitive config; Secret = sensitive data (base64 encoded)
Service ⚔️ Ingress Service = L4 (TCP/UDP) load balancing; Ingress = L7 (HTTP) routing with path/host rules
PV ⚔️ PVC PV = the actual storage resource; PVC = a request/claim for that storage
Role ⚔️ ClusterRole Role = namespace-scoped permissions; ClusterRole = cluster-wide permissions
Node ⚔️ Pod Node = physical/virtual machine; Pod = container wrapper running ON a node
Taint ⚔️ Toleration Taint = "Keep away!" on Node; Toleration = "I can handle it" on Pod
Liveness ⚔️ Readiness Liveness = "Is it alive?" (restart if not); Readiness = "Can it serve?" (exclude from LB)
kubectl apply ⚔️ kubectl create apply = declarative (idempotent); create = imperative (fails if exists)
🗺️

Kubernetes Concept Map

                              ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                              │         KUBERNETES CLUSTER          │
                              └─────────────────┬───────────────────┘
                                                │
               ┌────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────────────┐
               │                                │                                │
               ▼                                ▼                                ▼
    ┌─────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────┐
    │   CONTROL PLANE     │         │   WORKER NODES      │         │   STORAGE           │
    │   ═══════════════   │         │   ════════════      │         │   ═══════           │
    │   • API Server      │         │   • kubelet         │         │   • PV              │
    │   • etcd            │────────▶│   • kube-proxy      │◀───────▶│   • PVC             │
    │   • Scheduler       │         │   • Container RT    │         │   • StorageClass    │
    │   • Controller Mgr  │         │                     │         │   • CSI             │
    └─────────────────────┘         └──────────┬──────────┘         └─────────────────────┘
                                               │
                    ┌──────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────┐
                    │                          │                          │
                    ▼                          ▼                          ▼
         ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────┐   ┌─────────────────────┐
         │   WORKLOADS         │   │   NETWORKING        │   │   CONFIG/SECURITY   │
         │   ═════════         │   │   ══════════        │   │   ═══════════════   │
         │   • Deployment      │   │   • Service         │   │   • ConfigMap       │
         │   • StatefulSet     │   │   • Ingress         │   │   • Secret          │
         │   • DaemonSet       │   │   • NetworkPolicy   │   │   • RBAC            │
         │   • Job/CronJob     │   │   • DNS             │   │   • ServiceAccount  │
         │   • ReplicaSet      │   │   • Endpoints       │   │   • PSP/PSA         │
         │        │            │   └─────────────────────┘   └─────────────────────┘
         │        │            │
         │        ▼            │
         │   ┌─────────┐       │
         │   │   POD   │       │
         │   │  ─────  │       │
         │   │• Container(s)│  │
         │   │• Volumes    │   │
         │   │• Network    │   │
         │   └─────────┘       │
         └─────────────────────┘

E Complete anihpj Kubernetes Manifests

Production-ready YAML configurations for deploying the anihpj Django project with PostgreSQL on Kubernetes. All manifests include detailed inline comments explaining every field.

📦 13 Manifests 🐍 Django + PostgreSQL ✅ Production-Ready
📄 13 YAML Files
🐍 Django Framework
🗄️ PostgreSQL Database
☸️ 100% Commented

Appendix E Complete anihpj Kubernetes Manifests

REFERENCE PRODUCTION

Copy-paste-ready manifests for the anihpj/jobpost Django application — from Namespace to Kustomize

📑 In This Appendix

E.1Namespace E.2ConfigMap E.3Secret E.4PersistentVolumeClaim E.5PostgreSQL E.6Django Deployment E.7Django Service E.8Ingress E.9HPA E.10NetworkPolicy E.11RBAC E.12PodDisruptionBudget E.13Kustomize Structure
🏗️

anihpj Full Architecture in Kubernetes

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        NAMESPACE: anihpj-prod                                    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                                                  │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                      │
│  │  🌐 INGRESS (E.8)                                      │                      │
│  │  ├─ Host: anihpj.example.com                           │                      │
│  │  ├─ TLS: cert-manager (Let's Encrypt)                  │                      │
│  │  └─ Path: / → django-service:8000                      │                      │
│  └────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                           │                                                      │
│                           ▼                                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐                      │
│  │  🔌 SERVICE: django-service (E.7)                       │                      │
│  │  Type: ClusterIP | Port: 8000                           │                      │
│  │  Selector: app=django, component=web                    │                      │
│  └────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘                      │
│                           │                                                      │
│                           ▼                                                      │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  📦 DEPLOYMENT: django-deployment (E.6)       Replicas: 3 (HPA: 3-10)      │  │
│  │  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐          │  │
│  │  │   🐍 POD-0        │  │   🐍 POD-1        │  │   🐍 POD-2        │          │  │
│  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   │          │  │
│  │  │ Init Containers:  │  │ Init Containers:  │  │ Init Containers:  │          │  │
│  │  │ 1. wait-for-db    │  │ 1. wait-for-db    │  │ 1. wait-for-db    │          │  │
│  │  │ 2. migrate        │  │ 2. migrate        │  │ 2. migrate        │          │  │
│  │  │ 3. collectstatic  │  │ 3. collectstatic  │  │ 3. collectstatic  │          │  │
│  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   │          │  │
│  │  │ Main Container:   │  │ Main Container:   │  │ Main Container:   │          │  │
│  │  │ gunicorn          │  │ gunicorn          │  │ gunicorn          │          │  │
│  │  │ Port: 8000        │  │ Port: 8000        │  │ Port: 8000        │          │  │
│  │  │                   │  │                   │  │                   │          │  │
│  │  │ Probes: ✅         │  │ Probes: ✅         │  │ Probes: ✅         │          │  │
│  │  │ • liveness        │  │ • liveness        │  │ • liveness        │          │  │
│  │  │ • readiness       │  │ • readiness       │  │ • readiness       │          │  │
│  │  └─────────┬─────────┘  └─────────┬─────────┘  └─────────┬─────────┘          │  │
│  │            │                      │                      │                    │  │
│  │            │  envFrom: ConfigMap (E.2) + Secret (E.3)    │                    │  │
│  │            └──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┘                    │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                      │                                              │
│                                      │ port 5432                                    │
│                                      ▼                                              │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐    │
│  │  🗄️ POSTGRESQL (StatefulSet) (E.5)                                          │    │
│  │  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐   │    │
│  │  │   postgres-0                                                          │   │    │
│  │  │   Image: postgres:15                                                  │   │    │
│  │  │   Port: 5432                                                          │   │    │
│  │  │                                                                        │   │    │
│  │  │   Mounted: PVC (E.4) → /var/lib/postgresql/data                       │   │    │
│  │  │   Size: 10Gi (gp3-ssd)                                                 │   │    │
│  │  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘   │    │
│  │  Service: postgres-service (ClusterIP:5432)                                   │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                                      │

│  │  🛡️ ADDITIONAL RESOURCES                                                     │    │
│  │  ├─ HPA (E.9): Scale Django 3→10 replicas @ 70% CPU                          │    │
│  │  ├─ NetworkPolicy (E.10): Allow Django↔PostgreSQL, Ingress→Django            │    │
│  │  ├─ RBAC (E.11): ServiceAccount + Role + RoleBinding                          │    │
│  │  └─ PDB (E.12): minAvailable: 2 for Django pods                               │    │
│  └────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘    │
│                                                                                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

📁 KUSTOMIZE STRUCTURE (E.13)
├── base/
│   ├── kustomization.yaml
│   ├── namespace.yaml
│   ├── configmap.yaml
│   ├── secret.yaml
│   ├── pvc.yaml
│   ├── postgres.yaml
│   ├── django-deployment.yaml
│   ├── django-service.yaml
│   ├── ingress.yaml
│   ├── hpa.yaml
│   ├── networkpolicy.yaml
│   ├── rbac.yaml
│   └── pdb.yaml
└── overlays/
    ├── dev/
    │   └── kustomization.yaml (replicas: 1, DEBUG=True)
    ├── staging/
    │   └── kustomization.yaml (replicas: 2)
    └── prod/
        └── kustomization.yaml (replicas: 3, HPA, TLS)
📋 Manifest File Index
Section Resource File Purpose
E.1 Namespace namespace.yaml Isolate anihpj resources
E.2 ConfigMap configmap.yaml Django settings (non-secret)
E.3 Secret secret.yaml Passwords and keys
E.4 PVC pvc.yaml PostgreSQL + media storage
E.5 PostgreSQL postgres.yaml Database deployment + service
E.6 Django Deployment django-deployment.yaml Main application (3 replicas)
E.7 Django Service django-service.yaml Internal ClusterIP service
E.8 Ingress ingress.yaml External access + TLS
E.9 HPA hpa.yaml Autoscaling (3-10 pods)
E.10 NetworkPolicy networkpolicy.yaml Traffic control
E.11 RBAC rbac.yaml ServiceAccount + permissions
E.12 PDB pdb.yaml Disruption budget
E.13 Kustomize kustomization.yaml Environment overlays
🗂️

E.1 Namespace

🗂️ Resource Isolation

Namespaces provide logical isolation for anihpj resources across environments (dev, staging, prod).

namespace.yaml 
# ============================================================================
# NAMESPACE MANIFEST FOR ANIHPJ
# ============================================================================
# This creates isolated environments for the anihpj Django application.
# Each namespace acts like a "folder" that groups related resources together.
#
# Best Practice: Use separate namespaces for dev/staging/prod environments
# ============================================================================

---
# Development namespace - for local testing and development
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-dev                    # Namespace name (referenced by all resources)
  labels:
    app: anihpj                        # Standard app label for identification
    environment: development           # Environment label for filtering
    team: backend                      # Team ownership label
  annotations:
    description: "anihpj development environment"
    
---
# Staging namespace - for pre-production testing
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-staging
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    environment: staging
    team: backend
  annotations:
    description: "anihpj staging environment"
    
---
# Production namespace - for live traffic
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-prod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    environment: production
    team: backend
  annotations:
    description: "anihpj production environment"
    
# ============================================================================
# USAGE EXAMPLES
# ============================================================================
# Create namespaces:
#   kubectl apply -f namespace.yaml
#
# List all namespaces:
#   kubectl get namespaces -l app=anihpj
#
# Switch context to a namespace:
#   kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=anihpj-prod
#
# Delete a namespace (CAUTION: deletes ALL resources within):
#   kubectl delete namespace anihpj-dev
# ============================================================================

💡 Namespace Tips

🔑 Key Points
  • Resources within a namespace are isolated
  • Services can communicate cross-namespace via FQDN
  • Deleting namespace deletes ALL its resources
🔗 Cross-Namespace DNS 
# Same namespace:
postgres-service:5432

# Different namespace:
postgres-service.anihpj-prod.svc.cluster.local:5432
⚙️

E.2 ConfigMap

⚙️ Non-Secret Configuration

ConfigMaps store Django settings like ALLOWED_HOSTS, DATABASE_URL, and DEBUG flag — decoupled from code.

configmap.yaml 
# ============================================================================
# CONFIGMAP FOR DJANGO SETTINGS
# ============================================================================
# ConfigMaps store non-sensitive configuration data as key-value pairs.
# Django reads these as environment variables via envFrom or env.
#
# NOTE: Never store passwords here — use Secrets (E.3) for sensitive data!
# ============================================================================

apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: django-config                  # Referenced by Deployment's envFrom
  namespace: anihpj-prod               # Must match your target namespace
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: config
data:
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # DJANGO CORE SETTINGS
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  
  DEBUG: "False"                       # NEVER True in production!
  DJANGO_SETTINGS_MODULE: "anihpj.settings"  # Python path to settings
  
  # Comma-separated list of allowed hosts
  ALLOWED_HOSTS: "anihpj.example.com,www.anihpj.example.com,localhost"
  
  # Trusted origins for CSRF protection (Django 4.0+)
  CSRF_TRUSTED_ORIGINS: "https://anihpj.example.com,https://www.anihpj.example.com"
  
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # DATABASE SETTINGS
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  
  # Database connection (password comes from Secret)
  DATABASE_HOST: "postgres-service"    # K8s Service name (same namespace)
  DATABASE_PORT: "5432"
  DATABASE_NAME: "anihpj_db"
  DATABASE_USER: "anihpj_user"
  
  # Connection pool settings
  DATABASE_CONN_MAX_AGE: "60"          # Reuse connections for 60 seconds
  
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # APPLICATION SETTINGS
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  
  # Gunicorn settings
  GUNICORN_WORKERS: "3"                # Workers per pod (2*CPU + 1)
  GUNICORN_TIMEOUT: "30"               # Request timeout in seconds
  
  # Static/Media files (for collectstatic)
  STATIC_URL: "/static/"
  MEDIA_URL: "/media/"
  
  # Logging level
  LOG_LEVEL: "INFO"                    # DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
  
  # Time zone
  TZ: "UTC"
  
# ============================================================================
# USAGE IN DEPLOYMENT
# ============================================================================
# Option 1: Load ALL keys as environment variables (recommended)
# 
#   envFrom:
#     - configMapRef:
#         name: django-config
#
# Option 2: Load specific keys only
#
#   env:
#     - name: DEBUG
#       valueFrom:
#         configMapKeyRef:
#           name: django-config
#           key: DEBUG
#
# ============================================================================
# USAGE EXAMPLES
# ============================================================================
# Create/Update ConfigMap:
#   kubectl apply -f configmap.yaml
#
# View ConfigMap contents:
#   kubectl get configmap django-config -n anihpj-prod -o yaml
#
# Edit ConfigMap live (triggers pod restart if configured):
#   kubectl edit configmap django-config -n anihpj-prod
#
# Delete ConfigMap:
#   kubectl delete configmap django-config -n anihpj-prod
# ============================================================================
⚠️ ConfigMap vs Secret
Store In... Examples
ConfigMap DEBUG, ALLOWED_HOSTS, DATABASE_HOST, LOG_LEVEL, TZ
Secret SECRET_KEY, DATABASE_PASSWORD, API_KEYS, AWS_CREDENTIALS
🔐

E.3 Secret

🔐 Sensitive Configuration

Secrets store sensitive data like passwords, tokens, and keys — base64 encoded (not encrypted by default!).

secret.yaml 
# ============================================================================
# SECRET FOR DJANGO SENSITIVE DATA
# ============================================================================
# Secrets store sensitive information like passwords and API keys.
# Values are base64-encoded (NOT encrypted!) — use sealed-secrets or 
# external-secrets for production encryption.
#
# SECURITY NOTE: Never commit real secrets to Git! Use placeholders.
# ============================================================================

apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: django-secrets                 # Referenced by Deployment's envFrom
  namespace: anihpj-prod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: secrets
type: Opaque                           # Generic secret type (most common)
stringData:                            # Plain text (K8s auto-encodes to base64)
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # DJANGO SECRET KEY
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Generate with: python -c 'from django.core.management.utils import get_random_secret_key; print(get_random_secret_key())'
  # Or: openssl rand -base64 50
  
  SECRET_KEY: "your-super-secret-django-key-change-this-in-production-abc123xyz"
  
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # DATABASE CREDENTIALS
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  
  DATABASE_PASSWORD: "super-secret-postgres-password-change-me"
  
  # Full DATABASE_URL (alternative to separate host/port/name/user/pass)
  # DATABASE_URL: "postgres://anihpj_user:super-secret-postgres-password@postgres-service:5432/anihpj_db"
  
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # POSTGRESQL INIT CREDENTIALS (used by PostgreSQL container)
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  
  POSTGRES_USER: "anihpj_user"
  POSTGRES_PASSWORD: "super-secret-postgres-password-change-me"
  POSTGRES_DB: "anihpj_db"
  
# ============================================================================
# ALTERNATIVE: Using data (pre-encoded base64)
# ============================================================================
# If you prefer base64-encoding yourself:
#
# data:
#   SECRET_KEY: eW91ci1zdXBlci1zZWNyZXQtZGphbmdvLWtleQ==
#   DATABASE_PASSWORD: c3VwZXItc2VjcmV0LXBhc3N3b3Jk
#
# Encode: echo -n "your-value" | base64
# Decode: echo "eW91ci12YWx1ZQ==" | base64 -d
#
# ============================================================================
# USAGE IN DEPLOYMENT
# ============================================================================
# Option 1: Load ALL keys as environment variables
# 
#   envFrom:
#     - secretRef:
#         name: django-secrets
#
# Option 2: Load specific keys only
#
#   env:
#     - name: SECRET_KEY
#       valueFrom:
#         secretKeyRef:
#           name: django-secrets
#           key: SECRET_KEY
#
# ============================================================================
# SECURITY BEST PRACTICES
# ============================================================================
# 1. NEVER commit real secrets to Git
# 2. Use sealed-secrets, external-secrets, or Vault for production
# 3. Enable encryption at rest in etcd (EncryptionConfiguration)
# 4. Limit Secret access with RBAC
# 5. Rotate secrets regularly
#
# Create Secret from literal values:
#   kubectl create secret generic django-secrets \
#     --from-literal=SECRET_KEY='your-key' \
#     --from-literal=DATABASE_PASSWORD='your-pass' \
#     -n anihpj-prod
#
# Create Secret from file:
#   kubectl create secret generic django-secrets \
#     --from-env-file=.env.prod \
#     -n anihpj-prod
# ============================================================================
🚨 Production Secret Management

Don't store real secrets in YAML files! Use one of these approaches:

🔒 Sealed Secrets

Encrypts secrets so they can be safely stored in Git.

kubeseal < secret.yaml > sealed-secret.yaml
🔑 External Secrets

Syncs secrets from AWS Secrets Manager, Vault, etc.

kind: ExternalSecret
🏛️ HashiCorp Vault

Centralized secrets management with rotation.

vault-injector sidecar
💾

E.4 PersistentVolumeClaim

💾 Persistent Storage

PVCs request storage for PostgreSQL data and Django media files — data survives pod restarts.

pvc.yaml 
# ============================================================================
# PERSISTENTVOLUMECLAIMS FOR ANIHPJ
# ============================================================================
# PVCs request storage from the cluster's StorageClass.
# Storage persists across pod restarts and rescheduling.
#
# StorageClass determines the underlying provider (AWS EBS, GCP PD, Azure Disk)
# ============================================================================

---
# PostgreSQL Data Volume - stores database files
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: postgres-data-pvc              # Referenced by PostgreSQL deployment
  namespace: anihpj-prod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: database
spec:
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # ACCESS MODE
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # ReadWriteOnce (RWO): Mounted by ONE pod at a time (default for DBs)
  # ReadOnlyMany (ROX):  Mounted by MANY pods, read-only
  # ReadWriteMany (RWX): Mounted by MANY pods, read-write (NFS, EFS)
  
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce                    # Single pod access (correct for Postgres)
    
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # STORAGE SIZE
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 10Gi                    # Request 10 GB for database
      
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # STORAGE CLASS
  # ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  # Common options:
  # - standard:     HDD (cheap, slow)
  # - gp2/gp3:      AWS SSD
  # - pd-ssd:       GCP SSD
  # - managed-csi:  Azure SSD
  # - local-path:   Rancher/k3s local storage
  
  storageClassName: gp3                # AWS gp3 SSD (or use "standard" for testing)

---
# Django Media Volume - stores user uploads (optional if using S3)
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: django-media-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-prod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: media
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany                    # RWX if multiple Django pods need access
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi                     # 5 GB for media files
  storageClassName: efs-sc             # AWS EFS for shared storage (RWX)
  # NOTE: For single-pod or S3 media storage, this PVC may not be needed

---
# Django Static Volume (Optional) - for collectstatic output
# Most setups serve static from nginx/S3 instead of PVC
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: django-static-pvc
  namespace: anihpj-prod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: static
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteMany
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
  storageClassName: efs-sc

# ============================================================================
# STORAGE CLASS REFERENCE
# ============================================================================
#
# ┌─────────────────┬───────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────────────┐
# │ Provider        │ StorageClass  │ Access Modes │ Use Case              │
# ├─────────────────┼───────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────────────┤
# │ AWS EBS         │ gp2, gp3      │ RWO          │ Databases, single-pod │
# │ AWS EFS         │ efs-sc        │ RWX          │ Shared files, media   │
# │ GCP PD          │ pd-ssd        │ RWO          │ Databases             │
# │ Azure Disk      │ managed-csi   │ RWO          │ Databases             │
# │ Azure Files     │ azurefile-csi │ RWX          │ Shared files          │
# │ Minikube        │ standard      │ RWO          │ Local testing         │
# │ k3s/Rancher     │ local-path    │ RWO          │ Local testing         │
# └─────────────────┴───────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────────────┘
#
