F.5 Recommended Resources

📚 Curated Learning Resources

The best books, courses, labs, and tools for your Kubernetes journey.

📖 Official Documentation

Resource URL Best For
Kubernetes Docs kubernetes.io/docs Reference, concepts, tasks
kubectl Cheat Sheet kubectl cheatsheet Quick command reference
API Reference kubernetes-api Deep YAML field reference
Kubernetes Blog kubernetes.io/blog Release notes, features

📕 Recommended Books

🌱 Beginner
  • Kubernetes Up & Running — Burns, Beda, Hightower
  • The Kubernetes Book — Nigel Poulton
  • Kubernetes in Action — Marko Lukša
📈 Intermediate
  • Kubernetes Patterns — Ibryam, Huss
  • Programming Kubernetes — Hausenblas, Schimanski
  • Kubernetes Best Practices — Burns et al.
🚀 Advanced
  • Production Kubernetes — Rosso et al.
  • Cloud Native DevOps with Kubernetes — Arundel, Domingus
  • Kubernetes Security — Rice, CNCF

🎓 Online Courses

Course Platform Level Focus
Introduction to Kubernetes (LFS158) edX (Linux Foundation) Beginner Free, official intro
Kubernetes for Developers (LFD259) Linux Foundation Intermediate CKAD preparation
Kubernetes Administrator (LFS258) Linux Foundation Intermediate CKA preparation
Kubernetes CKA with Practice Tests KodeKloud / Udemy Intermediate Hands-on CKA prep
Kubernetes Mastery Udemy (Bret Fisher) Intermediate Deep dive with Docker

🧪 Practice Labs

Lab URL Description
killer.sh killer.sh Official CKA/CKAD/CKS exam simulator (included with exam purchase)
KodeKloud kodekloud.com Interactive labs with scenarios
Play with Kubernetes play-with-k8s.com Free browser-based cluster (4 hours)
Katacoda K8s killercoda.com Free interactive tutorials
GitHub: kubernetes exercises CKAD-exercises Comprehensive CKAD practice questions

🛠️ Essential Tools

📦 Local Development
  • minikube — Local single-node cluster
  • kind — Kubernetes in Docker
  • k3d — Lightweight K3s in Docker
  • Docker Desktop — Built-in K8s
🔧 CLI Enhancements
  • kubectx/kubens — Context/namespace switching
  • k9s — Terminal UI for K8s
  • stern — Multi-pod log tailing
  • krew — kubectl plugin manager
📋 IDE Extensions
  • Kubernetes (VS Code) — Cluster explorer
  • YAML — K8s schema validation
  • Lens — K8s desktop IDE
  • Helm Intellisense — Helm chart support
🔍 Debugging
  • kubectl-debug — Debug running pods
  • kail — Log streaming
  • kube-capacity — Resource reports
  • kubepug — Deprecation checker

👥 Communities

Community Link Description
Kubernetes Slack slack.k8s.io Official community slack (~170k members)
CNCF Community community.cncf.io Local meetups, events, webinars
r/kubernetes reddit.com/r/kubernetes Reddit community discussions
KubeCon KubeCon Major annual conference

📋 Exam Day Checklist — CKA/CKAD/CKS

The performance-based exams are challenging. Here's your pre-exam and during-exam checklist:

🗓️ Before Exam Day
  • ✅ Complete killer.sh 2 times
  • ✅ Practice with 2-hour mock sessions
  • ✅ Memorize key bookmark locations
  • ✅ Set aliases: k=kubectl, do='--dry-run=client -o yaml'
  • ✅ Test webcam, microphone, ID
  • ✅ Clear desk completely
  • ✅ Use Chrome/Chromium browser
⏰ During Exam
  • ✅ Read each question TWICE
  • ✅ Check namespace FIRST (-n xxx)
  • ✅ Skip hard questions, return later
  • ✅ Use kubectl explain liberally
  • ✅ Copy names from question, don't type
  • ✅ Verify after each task
  • ✅ Don't panic — 66% is passing!
EXAM TIME MANAGEMENT (2 hours)
══════════════════════════════

 First Pass (90 min)              Second Pass (30 min)
 ═══════════════════              ════════════════════
┌─────────────────────────┐      ┌─────────────────────────┐
│                         │      │                         │
│  🟢 Easy (1-3 pts)      │      │  🟡 Medium questions    │
│  Do ALL of these first  │──────│  Return with fresh eyes │
│  ~15-18 questions       │      │  ~5-7 questions         │
│                         │      │                         │
├─────────────────────────┤      ├─────────────────────────┤
│                         │      │                         │
│  🟡 Medium (4-6 pts)    │      │  🔴 Hard questions      │
│  Quick attempts only    │      │  Only if time permits   │
│  Flag and move on       │      │  ~2-3 questions         │
│                         │      │                         │
├─────────────────────────┤      └─────────────────────────┘
│                         │
│  🔴 Hard (7+ pts)       │
│  SKIP on first pass!    │
│                         │
└─────────────────────────┘

SCORING STRATEGY:
─────────────────
• Finish all easy = ~50-60%
• Add medium = ~70-80%
• Hard = bonus points

TARGET: 70%+ (passing is 66%)
⚡ Essential Exam Aliases (Set these first!)
~/.bashrc additions 
# Add these at exam start (paste into terminal):

# Shortcut for kubectl
alias k=kubectl

# Auto-completion
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -F __start_kubectl k

# Dry run shortcut
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"

# Force delete (careful!)
export now="--force --grace-period=0"

# Set namespace for session
alias kn='kubectl config set-context --current --namespace'

# Quick pod creation
alias krp='kubectl run test-pod --image=nginx --restart=Never'

# USAGE EXAMPLES:
# k run nginx --image=nginx $do > pod.yaml
# k delete pod test $now
# kn kube-system
Vim Configuration (~/.vimrc) 
" Paste at exam start for better YAML editing:
set expandtab       " Use spaces instead of tabs
set tabstop=2       " Tab = 2 spaces
set shiftwidth=2    " Indent = 2 spaces
set autoindent      " Keep indentation
set number          " Show line numbers
🎉 You're Ready!

With this guide and the resources above, you have everything needed for your Kubernetes journey. Remember:

💪 Practice Daily

Even 30 minutes of hands-on practice beats hours of reading.

🔄 Build & Break

Deploy apps, break them intentionally, learn to fix them.

🤝 Join Communities

The K8s community is welcoming — ask questions, help others.

🚀 Keep Learning

Kubernetes evolves rapidly — stay curious and keep exploring.

🎯 Appendix G: CKAD Exam Simulator

APPENDIX 🎓 CKAD EXAM 🔴 CRITICAL

A full mock CKAD exam with 15 performance-based tasks covering all 5 domains. Complete under 2 hours to simulate real exam conditions. Each question is weighted by its domain percentage.

⚠️ CKAD Exam Rules & Tips
  • Duration: 2 hours (120 minutes) for 15-20 questions
  • Environment: Linux terminal with kubectl, browser-based
  • Resources allowed: kubernetes.io/docs, kubernetes.io/blog, github.com/kubernetes only
  • Scoring: Partial credit is given — attempt EVERY question
  • Strategy: Do easy questions first (flag hard ones), use imperative commands for speed
  • Aliases: Set up alias k=kubectl and enable autocompletion FIRST

First 2 Minutes: Exam Environment Setup

exam-setup.sh 
# RUN THESE IMMEDIATELY when exam starts
alias k=kubectl
alias kgp='kubectl get pods'
alias kgs='kubectl get svc'
alias kgd='kubectl get deploy'
alias kgn='kubectl get nodes'
export do="--dry-run=client -o yaml"
export now="--force --grace-period=0"

# Enable autocompletion
source <(kubectl completion bash)
complete -o default -F __start_kubectl k

# Verify
k version --short

📊 Domain Weight Distribution in This Mock Exam

  Domain 1: Application Design & Build (20%)    ████████░░░░░░░░░░  Q1, Q2, Q3
  Domain 2: Application Deployment (20%)         ████████░░░░░░░░░░  Q4, Q5, Q6
  Domain 3: Observability & Maintenance (15%)    ██████░░░░░░░░░░░░  Q7, Q8
  Domain 4: Config & Security (25%)              ██████████░░░░░░░░  Q9, Q10, Q11, Q12
  Domain 5: Services & Networking (20%)          ████████░░░░░░░░░░  Q13, Q14, Q15

🎯 Domain 1: Application Design & Build (20%)

Question 1: Multi-Container Pod with Init Container (8%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes
Context: kubectl config use-context cluster1

Create a pod named web-init in namespace exam-ns that:

  1. Has an init container named init-svc using image busybox:1.36 that waits for a service named backend to be available: until nslookup backend.exam-ns.svc.cluster.local; do echo waiting; sleep 2; done
  2. Has a main container named web using image nginx:1.25
  3. Both containers share an emptyDir volume named shared-data. The init container mounts it at /init-data, the main container mounts it at /usr/share/nginx/html
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q1 
kubectl create ns exam-ns

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: web-init
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  initContainers:
  - name: init-svc
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'until nslookup backend.exam-ns.svc.cluster.local; do echo waiting; sleep 2; done']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-data
      mountPath: /init-data
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx:1.25
    volumeMounts:
    - name: shared-data
      mountPath: /usr/share/nginx/html
  volumes:
  - name: shared-data
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

Question 2: Sidecar Container with Shared Volume (6%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 7 minutes

Create a pod named sidecar-log in namespace exam-ns with:

  1. Container app (image: busybox:1.36) that writes the current date to /var/log/app.log every 5 seconds: while true; do date >> /var/log/app.log; sleep 5; done
  2. Container log-reader (image: busybox:1.36) that tails /var/log/app.log continuously
  3. An emptyDir volume named log-volume mounted at /var/log in both containers
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q2 
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: sidecar-log
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'while true; do date >> /var/log/app.log; sleep 5; done']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log
  - name: log-reader
    image: busybox:1.36
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'tail -f /var/log/app.log']
    volumeMounts:
    - name: log-volume
      mountPath: /var/log
  volumes:
  - name: log-volume
    emptyDir: {}
EOF

# Verify logs flowing
kubectl logs sidecar-log -c log-reader -n exam-ns

Question 3: Job with Completions and Parallelism (6%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 5 minutes

Create a Job named batch-process in namespace exam-ns that:

  1. Uses image busybox:1.36 and runs the command echo "Processing batch item" && sleep 3
  2. Must complete 6 times total
  3. Runs 3 pods in parallel
  4. Has a backoffLimit of 4
  5. Uses restartPolicy: Never
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q3 
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: batch-process
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  completions: 6
  parallelism: 3
  backoffLimit: 4
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: batch
        image: busybox:1.36
        command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo "Processing batch item" && sleep 3']
      restartPolicy: Never
EOF

# Verify
kubectl get jobs batch-process -n exam-ns -w

🚀 Domain 2: Application Deployment (20%)

Question 4: Rolling Update with Strategy Configuration (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 7 minutes

In namespace exam-ns:

  1. Create a deployment web-deploy with image nginx:1.24 and 4 replicas
  2. Configure the rolling update strategy with maxSurge: 1 and maxUnavailable: 1
  3. Update the image to nginx:1.25 and record the change
  4. Verify the rollout succeeded
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q4 
# Create deployment
kubectl create deployment web-deploy --image=nginx:1.24 --replicas=4 -n exam-ns

# Patch strategy
kubectl patch deployment web-deploy -n exam-ns -p '{"spec":{"strategy":{"type":"RollingUpdate","rollingUpdate":{"maxSurge":1,"maxUnavailable":1}}}}'

# Update image
kubectl set image deployment/web-deploy nginx=nginx:1.25 -n exam-ns

# Verify
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-deploy -n exam-ns

Question 5: Rollback a Broken Deployment (6%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 5 minutes

Using the deployment web-deploy from Q4:

  1. Update the image to a broken version: nginx:nonexistent-tag
  2. Verify the rollout is stuck (pods in ImagePullBackOff)
  3. Check the rollout history
  4. Rollback to the previous working version
  5. Confirm all pods are running
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q5 
# 1. Break it
kubectl set image deployment/web-deploy nginx=nginx:nonexistent-tag -n exam-ns

# 2. Verify stuck
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-deploy -n exam-ns  # hangs
kubectl get pods -n exam-ns  # ImagePullBackOff

# 3. History
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-deploy -n exam-ns

# 4. Rollback
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-deploy -n exam-ns

# 5. Verify
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-deploy -n exam-ns
kubectl get pods -n exam-ns

Question 6: Helm Release Management (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 6 minutes
  1. Add the Bitnami Helm repo if not already added
  2. Install a chart bitnami/nginx as release exam-web in namespace helm-exam
  3. Upgrade the release to set replicaCount=3
  4. View the release history to confirm 2 revisions
  5. Rollback to revision 1
  6. Confirm the rollback with helm list
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q6 
# 1. Add repo
helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami
helm repo update

# 2. Install
kubectl create ns helm-exam
helm install exam-web bitnami/nginx -n helm-exam

# 3. Upgrade
helm upgrade exam-web bitnami/nginx -n helm-exam --set replicaCount=3

# 4. History
helm history exam-web -n helm-exam

# 5. Rollback
helm rollback exam-web 1 -n helm-exam

# 6. Verify
helm list -n helm-exam

🔍 Domain 3: Observability & Maintenance (15%)

Question 7: Debugging a Failing Pod (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes

A pod named broken-app exists in namespace debug-ns but is not running. Diagnose and fix it.

Setup (run first):

setup.sh 
kubectl create ns debug-ns
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: broken-app
  namespace: debug-ns
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    command: ["sh", "-c", "exit 1"]
    resources:
      requests: { cpu: "50m", memory: "64Mi" }
EOF
  1. Identify why the pod is failing
  2. View the pod's logs (including previous crash)
  3. Fix the pod by removing the failing command so nginx starts normally
  4. Verify the pod is Running
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q7 
# 1. Identify failure
kubectl get pod broken-app -n debug-ns
# STATUS: CrashLoopBackOff

kubectl describe pod broken-app -n debug-ns
# Events show: Back-off restarting failed container

# 2. View logs
kubectl logs broken-app -n debug-ns --previous

# 3. Fix — remove the command override
kubectl get pod broken-app -n debug-ns -o yaml > /tmp/fix.yaml
# Edit /tmp/fix.yaml — remove the "command" line
# OR delete and recreate:
kubectl delete pod broken-app -n debug-ns $now

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: broken-app
  namespace: debug-ns
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    resources:
      requests: { cpu: "50m", memory: "64Mi" }
EOF

# 4. Verify
kubectl get pod broken-app -n debug-ns

Question 8: API Deprecation Awareness (8%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 6 minutes

Check for deprecated API usage and update resources to current API versions.

  1. List all API resources available in the cluster
  2. Check which API version CronJob uses (it was moved from batch/v1beta1 to batch/v1 in K8s 1.21)
  3. Check which API version Ingress uses (moved from extensions/v1beta1 / networking.k8s.io/v1beta1 to networking.k8s.io/v1 in 1.22)
  4. Create a CronJob using the correct current API version
  5. Use kubectl explain to verify the correct fields for the Ingress spec
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q8 
# 1. List all API resources
kubectl api-resources

# 2. CronJob API version
kubectl api-resources | grep -i cronjob
# batch/v1 — NOT batch/v1beta1

# 3. Ingress API version
kubectl api-resources | grep -i ingress
# networking.k8s.io/v1 — NOT extensions/v1beta1

# 4. CronJob with correct API
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: batch/v1     # NOT batch/v1beta1!
kind: CronJob
metadata:
  name: api-check
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  schedule: "*/5 * * * *"
  jobTemplate:
    spec:
      template:
        spec:
          containers:
          - name: check
            image: busybox:1.36
            command: ["echo", "API version correct"]
          restartPolicy: OnFailure
EOF

# 5. Explain Ingress
kubectl explain ingress.spec
kubectl explain ingress.spec.rules

API Deprecation Quick Reference (Domain 3)

Know these API version changes — the CKAD may give you YAML with old versions:

Resource Old API (Removed) Current API Since
Ingressextensions/v1beta1networking.k8s.io/v11.22
CronJobbatch/v1beta1batch/v11.25
HPAautoscaling/v2beta1autoscaling/v21.26
PodDisruptionBudgetpolicy/v1beta1policy/v11.25
PodSecurityPolicypolicy/v1beta1Removed entirely1.25
FlowSchemaflowcontrol.../v1beta2flowcontrol.../v11.29

Quick check command: kubectl api-resources | grep <resource> to find the correct API version.

🔒 Domain 4: Application Environment, Configuration & Security (25%)

Question 9: ConfigMaps and Secrets (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes

In namespace exam-ns:

  1. Create a ConfigMap app-config with: DB_HOST=postgres, DB_PORT=5432, LOG_LEVEL=info
  2. Create a Secret app-secret with: DB_PASSWORD=supersecret123, API_KEY=abc-xyz-789
  3. Create a pod config-pod (nginx:1.25) that:
    • Loads ALL ConfigMap keys as environment variables using envFrom
    • Loads the Secret DB_PASSWORD as env var DATABASE_PASSWORD
    • Mounts the entire Secret as files at /etc/secrets
  4. Verify the env vars are set correctly
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q9 
# 1. ConfigMap
kubectl create cm app-config -n exam-ns \
  --from-literal=DB_HOST=postgres \
  --from-literal=DB_PORT=5432 \
  --from-literal=LOG_LEVEL=info

# 2. Secret
kubectl create secret generic app-secret -n exam-ns \
  --from-literal=DB_PASSWORD=supersecret123 \
  --from-literal=API_KEY=abc-xyz-789

# 3. Pod
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: config-pod
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    envFrom:
    - configMapRef:
        name: app-config
    env:
    - name: DATABASE_PASSWORD
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: app-secret
          key: DB_PASSWORD
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /etc/secrets
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: app-secret
EOF

# 4. Verify
kubectl exec config-pod -n exam-ns -- env | grep -E 'DB_|LOG_|DATABASE_'
kubectl exec config-pod -n exam-ns -- ls /etc/secrets
kubectl exec config-pod -n exam-ns -- cat /etc/secrets/API_KEY

Question 10: RBAC — ServiceAccount and Role (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes

In namespace rbac-ns:

  1. Create namespace rbac-ns
  2. Create a ServiceAccount pod-reader
  3. Create a Role pod-read-role that allows get, list, watch on pods
  4. Create a RoleBinding pod-read-binding that binds pod-read-role to the pod-reader ServiceAccount
  5. Verify the ServiceAccount can list pods: kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:rbac-ns:pod-reader -n rbac-ns
  6. Verify the ServiceAccount CANNOT delete pods
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q10 
# 1. Namespace
kubectl create ns rbac-ns

# 2. ServiceAccount
kubectl create sa pod-reader -n rbac-ns

# 3. Role
kubectl create role pod-read-role -n rbac-ns \
  --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods

# 4. RoleBinding
kubectl create rolebinding pod-read-binding -n rbac-ns \
  --role=pod-read-role --serviceaccount=rbac-ns:pod-reader

# 5. Verify CAN list
kubectl auth can-i list pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:rbac-ns:pod-reader -n rbac-ns
# yes

# 6. Verify CANNOT delete
kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --as=system:serviceaccount:rbac-ns:pod-reader -n rbac-ns
# no

Question 11: SecurityContext (6%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 6 minutes

Create a pod secure-pod in namespace exam-ns with:

  1. Image nginx:1.25
  2. Runs as user ID 1000
  3. Runs as group ID 3000
  4. runAsNonRoot: true
  5. Read-only root filesystem
  6. allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  7. Drops ALL Linux capabilities
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q11 
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: exam-ns
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsUser: 1000
    runAsGroup: 3000
    runAsNonRoot: true
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.25
    securityContext:
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
EOF

# Verify
kubectl get pod secure-pod -n exam-ns
kubectl exec secure-pod -n exam-ns -- id
# uid=1000 gid=3000

Question 12: Resource Requests, Limits, and LimitRange (5%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 6 minutes

In namespace limits-ns:

  1. Create namespace limits-ns
  2. Create a LimitRange that sets default CPU request to 100m, default CPU limit to 500m, default memory request to 128Mi, default memory limit to 256Mi
  3. Create a pod auto-limits (nginx:1.25) without specifying any resources
  4. Verify the LimitRange defaults were automatically applied
  5. Create a ResourceQuota limiting the namespace to 4 CPUs and 4Gi memory total
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q12 
# 1. Namespace
kubectl create ns limits-ns

# 2. LimitRange
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: default-limits
  namespace: limits-ns
spec:
  limits:
  - default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    type: Container
EOF

# 3. Pod without resources
kubectl run auto-limits --image=nginx:1.25 -n limits-ns

# 4. Verify defaults applied
kubectl get pod auto-limits -n limits-ns -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}'
# Shows requests and limits from LimitRange

# 5. ResourceQuota
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: compute-quota
  namespace: limits-ns
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.cpu: "4"
    limits.memory: 4Gi
EOF

kubectl describe quota compute-quota -n limits-ns

🌐 Domain 5: Services & Networking (20%)

Question 13: Services — ClusterIP, NodePort, and Expose (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 7 minutes

In namespace exam-ns:

  1. Create a deployment backend with image nginx:1.25 and 2 replicas
  2. Expose it as a ClusterIP service on port 80
  3. Create a temporary pod and verify connectivity: kubectl run test --rm -it --image=busybox -- wget -qO- backend.exam-ns:80
  4. Change the service type to NodePort
  5. Find the assigned NodePort number
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q13 
# 1. Deployment
kubectl create deployment backend --image=nginx:1.25 --replicas=2 -n exam-ns

# 2. ClusterIP service
kubectl expose deployment backend --port=80 --target-port=80 -n exam-ns

# 3. Test connectivity
kubectl run test --rm -it -n exam-ns --image=busybox --restart=Never \
  -- wget -qO- backend.exam-ns:80

# 4. Change to NodePort
kubectl patch svc backend -n exam-ns -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'
# OR delete and recreate:
# kubectl expose deployment backend --port=80 --type=NodePort -n exam-ns

# 5. Find NodePort
kubectl get svc backend -n exam-ns -o jsonpath='{.spec.ports[0].nodePort}'

Question 14: Ingress (7%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes

In namespace exam-ns, assuming an Ingress controller is installed:

  1. Create an Ingress web-ingress that:
    • Routes /app to service backend on port 80 (pathType: Prefix)
    • Routes /api to service api-svc on port 8080 (pathType: Prefix)
  2. Add the annotation nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
  3. Verify the Ingress was created and shows the rules
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q14 
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: web-ingress
  namespace: exam-ns
  annotations:
    nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: /
spec:
  rules:
  - http:
      paths:
      - path: /app
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: backend
            port:
              number: 80
      - path: /api
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: api-svc
            port:
              number: 8080
EOF

# Verify
kubectl get ingress web-ingress -n exam-ns
kubectl describe ingress web-ingress -n exam-ns

Question 15: NetworkPolicy (6%)

⏱️ Suggested Time: 8 minutes

In namespace netpol-ns:

  1. Create namespace netpol-ns
  2. Create a deployment web (nginx, 1 replica) with label app=web
  3. Create a deployment db (postgres:15, 1 replica) with label app=db
  4. Create a NetworkPolicy db-policy that:
    • Applies to pods with label app=db
    • Allows ingress only from pods with label app=web
    • Allows only TCP port 5432
    • Denies all other ingress traffic
  5. Verify: a pod with label app=web can reach db:5432, but a random pod cannot
📖 View Solution
Solution — Q15 
# 1. Namespace
kubectl create ns netpol-ns

# 2-3. Deployments
kubectl create deployment web --image=nginx -n netpol-ns
kubectl create deployment db --image=postgres:15 -n netpol-ns
kubectl set env deployment/db POSTGRES_PASSWORD=test123 -n netpol-ns

# 4. NetworkPolicy
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: db-policy
  namespace: netpol-ns
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: db
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: web
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 5432
EOF

# 5. Verify — allowed (from web pod)
kubectl run test-web --rm -it -n netpol-ns --image=busybox \
  --labels="app=web" --restart=Never \
  -- nc -zv -w3 db.netpol-ns 5432
# Should succeed

# Verify — denied (random pod, no matching label)
kubectl run test-random --rm -it -n netpol-ns --image=busybox \
  --restart=Never \
  -- nc -zv -w3 db.netpol-ns 5432
# Should timeout/fail

📚 About This Guide

A comprehensive beginner-to-advanced Kubernetes learning resource using the anihpj Django project with jobpost app as a real-world example.

Models covered: Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost
